facts: Kia will invest more than $200 million to expand EV9 production at its West Point plant.
The investment will create nearly 200 new jobs at the facility.
The expansion accommodates assembly of the all-electric 2024 EV9 three-row SUV.
EV9 assembly is expected to begin in the second quarter of 2024.
The EV9 will become the fifth model assembled at Kia Georgia, joining the Telluride, Sorento, Sportage SUVs, and K5 mid-size sedan.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the investment on July 12, 2023.
The project aims to help propel Georgia toward its goal of becoming the e-mobility capital.
Currently, more than 40 percent of Kia vehicles sold in the U.S. are manufactured at the West Point plant.
The EV9 is Kia’s first three-row EV SUV and the first Kia EV assembled in North America.
The EV9 serves as the flagship vehicle for Kia’s global Plan S strategy focused on sustainable mobility leadership.
Sean Yoon, President & CEO of Kia Motors North America and Kia Motors America, stated the EV9 will be the most innovative vehicle Kia has ever built.
Kia Georgia utilizes Georgia Quick Start workforce development program, which operates the Kia Georgia Training Center.
Georgia Quick Start provides comprehensive, customized training services to prepare new employees for the new assembly line.
Stuart Countess, President & CEO of Kia Georgia, highlighted partnerships with the Governor’s Office, Department of Economic Development, Georgia QuickStart, City of West Point, and Troup County.
City of West Point Mayor Steve Tramell noted Kia has been an outstanding corporate citizen for seventeen years, providing employment opportunities and fostering economic growth in West Point.
Amanda Fields, Senior Regional Project Manager, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on the project.
The project involved partnerships with the West Point Development Authority, City of West Point, Troup County, Georgia Center of Innovation’s Manufacturing and Energy teams, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Kia has exceeded expectations since being recruited in 2006.
To date, Kia has invested more than $1.9 billion in the state of Georgia.
Kia Georgia is responsible for more than 14,000 plant and supplier jobs in the region.
Governor Kemp and state lawmakers recognized January 31 as “Kia Day” earlier in 2023 to commend Kia’s manufacturing impact.
The 2006 announcement to establish a vehicle assembly facility in West Point was one of the biggest single foreign investments in Georgia’s history.
Kia ships vehicles from Georgia to global sales markets including the United States, Canada, Mexico, the Caribbean, Middle East, and Pacific nations.
Since 2020, Georgia has attracted more than $22.7 billion in investment and created over 28,400 jobs along the entire EV supply chain.
Kia Georgia has an annual production capacity of 340,000 units.
The Kia Georgia facility is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Georgia, and began mass production on November 16, 2009.
Kia America is headquartered in Irvine, California, and operates a U.S. dealer network of over 750 locations.
The K5, Sportage, Sorento, and Telluride are assembled in the United States from U.S. and globally sourced parts.
The EV9 is expected to be assembled in the United States from U.S. and globally sourced parts.
source_title: Kia to Invest Over $200 Million in EV9 Production Expansion | Georgia

facts: Absolics, Inc., a subsidiary of SKC Co. Ltd., broke ground on Nov 1, 2022, on a planned $600 million manufacturing facility in Covington, Ga.
The investment represents an increase from the $473 million originally announced in October 2021 due to the need for more advanced manufacturing equipment to meet anticipated demand.
The facility will create more than 400 high-skilled jobs in Georgia.
Absolics plans to build the facility in two phases at SKC's existing campus in Covington.
Phase one is a $240 million investment expected to be completed by the end of 2023, creating 140 jobs and focusing on small-volume manufacturing.
Mass production at the facility is expected to start in the second quarter of 2024.
Phase two is an additional $360 million investment that will create another 270 jobs and ramp up to high-volume manufacturing, anticipated to be completed over the next three to five years.
The facility will produce a glass substrate, a thin layer of glass approximately 3-by-3 inches used to package different semiconductor components together.
Processing, memory, and logic chips can be mounted next to each other on the glass to create the core of a computing system.
The new glass material replaces both silicon imposer and plastic substrate layers, simplifying manufacturing and reducing package thickness by almost half.
Multi-layer ceramic capacitors (MLCC) can be embedded in the glass substrate, freeing more surface space for chips.
Absolics' product allows larger processors and up to four times more chips to be mounted in the same space compared to standard packaging.
The energy efficiency of the glass substrate reduces overall power consumption by up to 50 percent.
Phase one is expected to have an annual production capacity of 12,000 square meters.
Phase two will ramp up to an annual production capacity of 72,000 square meters.
The glass substrate technology was originally developed by SKC and Absolics as part of a research consortium with the Georgia Institute of Technology.
Absolics is expected to be the first company in the world to mass produce this material.
Groundbreaking ceremony attendees included Senator Jon Ossoff and Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson.

Dr. Woncheol Park, President and Chief Executive Officer of SKC, stated that Absolics will be an integral part of the U.S. semiconductor ecosystem and praised the factory's job creation impact on Covington and Georgia.

Senator Jon Ossoff stated he is working to bring jobs and business to Georgia, noting the plant will create Georgia jobs, support U.S. national security, and bring advanced manufacturing to the state.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted the development as an opportunity to grow the semiconductor industry in Georgia and the U.S., linking it to planning for an increasingly electrified future.
Absolics was established last year by SKC, which remains a majority owner of the company.
SKC is an operating company of SK Group, South Korea's second-largest conglomerate with global companies in clean energy, semiconductors and life sciences.
SKC was founded in 1976 and established a presence in Georgia in 1996 that grew into one of North America's largest manufacturers of polyester films.

SKC's Semiconductor Material Business develops and manufactures semiconductors, specialty materials, chemical mechanical polishing pads and slurry, and copper foil for electric vehicle battery anodes.
SK Group plans to increase its total investments and assets in the U.S. to more than $50 billion by the end of 2025, focusing on clean energy, semiconductors, and life sciences.

Earlier this year, SK Siltron CSS opened a new facility in Michigan as part of a $300 million investment to increase manufacturing capacity for semiconductor wafers used in electric vehicle components.
SK On is investing $2.6 billion in a site in Commerce, Ga., to manufacture batteries for electric vehicles.
SK Group employs more than 100,000 people worldwide and has $139 billion in global annual revenue.

SK companies are investing billions of dollars in expanding their U.S. presence with business operations or partnerships in hydrogen energy and fuel cells, EV battery manufacturing and technology, energy storage solutions, pharmaceutical manufacturing and development, semiconductors, and advanced materials.
source_title: Absolics Breaks Ground on Planned $600 Million Manufacturing Site in Georgia for Breakthrough Semiconductor Material

facts: Hyundai Motor Group opened its new metaplant just outside Savannah in March.
Georgia celebrated the 40th anniversary of its investment office in Seoul in 2025, which was one of the first such offices established by any U.S. state in Korea.
Korea opened a consular presence in Georgia in 1971.
KOTRA, Korea’s trade and investment organization, maintains an active office in Georgia to support bilateral investment and trade.
Georgia was honored with the James A. Van Fleet Award from The Korea Society in 2023, which was accepted by Gov. Brian Kemp.
SK Group subsidiary SKC opened a plant in Georgia in 1996, marking the first major Korean-owned manufacturing facility in the state.
Since 2019, SK Battery America has invested $2.6 billion in an electric vehicle battery facility in the state.
Hyundai Motor Group’s investment of $12.6 billion includes the new metaplant and two battery joint ventures with LG Energy System and SK On.
Hyundai's investment will generate up to 40,000 direct and indirect jobs.
The metaplant alone will produce 500,000 electric and hybrid vehicles annually at its state-of-the-art facility near Georgia’s coast and deepwater ports.
Kia, also part of Hyundai Motor Group, has invested $2.8 billion into its West Point plant since 2009.
Kia's West Point plant now produces 350,000 vehicles per year.
Kia's facility has expanded to include EV manufacturing lines alongside traditional models.
In 2018, Hanwha Qcells announced its solar panel manufacturing plant in Dalton, which opened as the largest in the Western Hemisphere.
Hanwha Qcells has expanded three times since 2019.
A second Hanwha Qcells plant in Cartersville is now ramping up and is expected to support up to 4,000 jobs in northwest Georgia.
CJ Foodville will open a $47 million bakery and food processing facility in Hall County this year, supplying Tous les Jours cafés with over 19,000 tons of baked goods each year.

Over the past decade, Korean companies supported directly by the State of Georgia are slated to contribute more than $24 billion in investment and create more than 33,600 new jobs across automotive, logistics, food production, and advanced technology sectors.
Around 100 Korean-owned facilities operate in Georgia, employing more than 17,000 people as of 2024.
Georgia is home to one of the largest Korean communities in the U.S.
Georgia-based companies such as Coca-Cola, Novelis and UPS operate in Korea.
Delta Air Lines connects Atlanta to Seoul with direct flights, making business and cultural exchange even more seamless.
In 2024, Korea ranked as Georgia’s third-largest total trade partner, with more than $17.5 billion in goods traded.
Pat Wilson is the commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
source_title: Op-Ed: Atlanta Journal-Constitution: Hyundai metaplant investment

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. will invest more than $30 million in a new manufacturing facility in Metter.
The investment will create 200 new jobs in Candler County.
This marks the second automotive supplier announced in Candler County in less than six months.
In fiscal year 2023 alone, 82 percent of new jobs created and more than $20 billion of investments went to communities outside the metro Atlanta area.
Doowon Group was established in Korea in 1974 and specializes in designing and manufacturing automotive climate control systems.
The company will be a key supplier for Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (Kia Georgia) and Hyundai Motor Group.
Ki Chun Sung is the CEO of Doowon Climate Control America, Inc.
Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. is establishing itself as Doowon Group’s inaugural automotive air conditioning system production facility in the United States.
The new manufacturing facility will be located in the Metter-Candler County I-16 Industrial Park.
The site is a “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development” (GRAD) certified site.
The facility will primarily produce climate control systems for use in traditional and electric vehicles.
Operations are projected to begin in 2026.
Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. plans to hire injection molding technicians, assembly operators, quality materials and production specialists, engineers, and management personnel.
Metter Mayor Ed Boyd welcomed Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. as the newest corporate citizen in Metter and Candler County.
Glyn Thrift is Chairman of the Candler County Board of Commissioners.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was completed in partnership with the Candler County Industrial Authority, Georgia Power, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated the City of Metter and Candler County’s intentional investment in site preparation positioned them for success.
Since 2018, Georgia has pursued the entire EV supply chain, creating more than $25.7 billion in investments and 30,200 jobs.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
Doowon Group is the ultimate parent entity of Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. and is headquartered in South Korea.
Doowon Group manages four domestic production plants, a technology research institute, and thirteen overseas production facilities, including operations in the United States.
Doowon Group sustains a workforce exceeding 3,125 employees and achieved sales revenue surpassing $1.9 billion in fiscal year 2023.
The company develops and produces air conditioning systems for all types of vehicles, including electric cars, hybrids, and internal combustion engine vehicles.
source_title: Second Automotive Supplier Creating 200 New Jobs in Candler County | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that TEKLAS, a Turkish advanced research and development, manufacturing company and supplier of electric vehicle (EV) parts, will invest $6.5 million in opening their first North American facility and headquarters in Gordon County.
The project will deliver 120 jobs to Calhoun.
Governor Kemp cited Georgia's highly skilled workforce, top-notch logistics network, and pro-business environment as reasons TEKLAS joined the state's growing electric vehicle parts industry.
TEKLAS was founded in Turkey in 1971.

TEKLAS designs, develops, and manufactures fluid circulation systems for cooling and heating applications, which includes hoses and pipes from rubber, plastic, and metal for the global automotive industry.
TEKLAS uses highly innovative and automated manufacturing processes using advanced water molding technology.
TEKLAS USA will include assembly lines to serve the most prominent original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) in the industry.
Start of operations is scheduled for the spring of 2021.
TEKLAS is headquartered in Turkey and has locations in Bulgaria, Serbia, Mexico, and China, along with sales offices in Germany and France.
Regional Director Arcan Ergur stated that the Calhoun, Georgia location will help them be closer to their main customers.
The 200,000-square-foot facility and headquarters will be located at 320 South Industrial Boulevard in Calhoun, Georgia.
TEKLAS will be hiring for positions at a variety of skill levels, including positions in advanced manufacturing, management, and supervision.
Larry Roye, chairman of the Development Authority of Gordon County, applauded the efforts of state and local partners who helped encourage the company to come to this decision.

Kathy Johnson, Gordon County Chamber of Commerce President and CEO, commended TEKLAS for their research and advancements in electric vehicle manufacturing and for their contribution to the expansion of the automotive sector of our manufacturing base.
GDEcD Project Manager Fernanda Kirchner represented the Global Commerce division on this competitive project.
The project was executed in partnership with the Economic Development Authority of Gordon County and Georgia Power.
The Center of Innovation for Logistics, a division of GDEcD, provided freight analysis to support the project and company planning.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the State of Georgia is focused on attracting and growing the jobs of the future, and TEKLAS choosing to establish their North American headquarters and build their very first U.S. advanced manufacturing facility in Calhoun is another great example of these efforts producing results.

As the automotive industry continues to transform and move toward electric vehicles and e-mobility, GDEcD will work to foster an ecosystem of manufacturers and suppliers on the leading edge of the industry's transition.
Commissioner Wilson welcomed TEKLAS to the diverse family of EV suppliers that call Georgia home.
TEKLAS is a global automotive fluid circulation system supplier for major car original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
With more than 45 years of experience, TEKLAS serves its customers with design, development, and production capabilities.
TEKLAS provides innovative solutions for thermoplastics, rubber, and metal products to its customers.
source_title: Electric Vehicle Manufacturer TEKLAS to Open North American Headquarters in Georgia, Create 120 Jobs in Calhoun | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that DAS Corp. will establish a new manufacturing facility in Metter.
The facility will create 300 new jobs and generate more than $35 million in investment in Candler County.
The initiative will involve collaborative indirect investments.
Suppliers for the Hyundai Metaplant resulted in over $2 billion in investment last fiscal year, alone, helping Georgia achieve a third straight year of record-breaking economic growth.
Georgia's logistics assets, including a reliable network of rail lines and highways, connect companies like DAS to key business partners in all corners of the state and to markets across the world.
DAS was established in Korea in 1987 and specializes in designing and manufacturing seating systems, safety seating components, and seating structures.
DAS is a key supplier for Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG) and Hyundai Motor Group.
The strategic location near prominent cities like Savannah and Macon facilitated convenient access and played a pivotal role in the decision-making process.
Metter was deemed an ideal location in anticipation of its role as a global electric vehicle production hub thanks to its close proximity to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Future expansion prospects were considered in the site selection.
The new DAS manufacturing facility will be located in the Candler County Industrial Park in Metter.
The facility will primarily produce automotive seat structures.
Operations are projected to begin in the latter half of 2024.
Over the course of the next five years, DAS will be recruiting for assembly technicians.
DAS intends to establish collaborations with local employment services as part of its hiring process.
The City of Metter and Candler County welcome DAS, anticipating transformative impact on housing investment, other business opportunities, and socioeconomic wellbeing.
Glyn Thrift, Chairman of the Candler County Board of Commissioners, stated DAS will provide a boost to spur more economic development.
Griff Lynch, President and CEO of the Georgia Ports Authority, expressed pride in supporting the growing automotive manufacturing presence in the state.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Candler County Industrial Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Electric Cities of Georgia, and Georgia Quick Start.
Georgia has pursued the entire EV supply chain, creating more than $25 billion in investments and 30,000 jobs since 2018.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
DAS Corp. garnered a total revenue of approximately $900 million USD during the fiscal year of 2022.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: DAS Corp. to Bring 300 Automotive Jobs to Metter | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Dongwon Autopart Technology plans to invest $30 million in a new production facility in Emanuel County.
The automotive project is anticipated to create 200 new jobs over the next several years.
Dongwon Autopart Technology was established in 1971.
The company supplies components such as doorframes, seat frames, and battery frames to automotive original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
Dongwon Autopart Technology currently supports more than 500 direct jobs in the Southeast.
Existing facilities in Hogansville, Georgia will remain operational.
Dongwon Autopart Technology has been operating in the United States since 2003.
Construction on the new manufacturing facility will be located at the Hwy 297 Industrial Park near Swainsboro.
The company will be hiring for positions such as management, system leads, and production personnel.
Hiring is expected to begin in 2026.
Representative Butch Parrish, Chairman of the House Rules Committee, stated the Georgia House of Representatives is committed to bringing high-quality jobs to rural communities.
Chairman Will Donaldson of the Emanuel County Development Authority welcomed Dongwon Autopart Technology to Emanuel County and noted their economic development investments paid off for the community.
Ken Warnock, CEO of the Emanuel County Development Authority, called the project the single largest new economic development project for Emanuel County in over 30 years.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Emanuel County Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated the automotive industry is expanding outward along the I-16 corridor as suppliers choose strategic locations with access to OEMs and Georgia’s ports.
Dongwon Autopart Technology’s new facility will be its second location in Georgia.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: 200 New Jobs Headed to Emanuel County | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: On March 01, 2023, Governor Brian P. Kemp, First Lady Marty Kemp, Rivian founder and CEO RJ Scaringe, Lt. Governor Burt Jones, Speaker Jon Burns, Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson, legislative leaders, local elected and economic officials, and company representatives celebrated the first-ever Rivian Day at the State Capitol in Atlanta, GA.
The event included a legislative luncheon for members of the General Assembly to engage with Rivian executives regarding planned operations in Georgia.
Governor Brian Kemp officially recognized Wednesday, March 1, as Rivian Day in Georgia.
Governor Kemp stated that Georgia is fast becoming the EV capital of the nation and thanked RJ Scaringe and his team for their ongoing commitment to making Georgia the No. 1 state for business.
Governor Kemp noted that Rivian chose Georgia because it has all that is needed for continued success and mentioned preparations for a facility groundbreaking later in 2023.

In December of 2021, Governor Kemp and Rivian executives announced the company would invest $5 billion in a manufacturing facility at the 2,000-acre East Atlanta Megasite represented by the Joint Development Authority of Jasper, Morgan, Newton, and Walton Counties.
The plant will create 7,500 jobs and be capable of producing up to 400,000 vehicles per year.

Rivian CEO and founder RJ Scaringe thanked state legislative leaders and local elected officials, expressing excitement to build the next plant in Georgia and citing the growing EV ecosystem as a perfect environment to scale the business and launch new products grounded in a mission to preserve the world for generations.
Site development for the facility began in 2022 with production expected to begin in 2026.
Rivian’s technology centers at the new campus will support the company’s research and development programs.
The company is actively working to ensure that both the construction process and future facility meet high standards of conservation and sustainability.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and 27,400 jobs in Georgia.

Senator Reverend Raphael Warnock called Rivian one of the leaders of Georgia’s electric vehicle revolution and stated that the multi-billion dollar investment will uplift Georgia workers and their families while keeping the state economy moving forward.

Senator Jon Ossoff expressed appreciation for Rivian’s continued investment in Georgia jobs and prosperity, stating the state will continue working to make Georgia the national leader in advanced energy production and manufacturing.
Rivian’s strategic location on the I-20 corridor will enable access to resources and speed products to market.
Georgia offers 1,200 miles of highway and 5,000 miles of rail to move products like Rivian’s vehicles quickly and efficiently.
The Mason Mega Rail Terminal at the Port of Savannah is the largest on-terminal rail facility in North America, which will allow Rivian to efficiently tap into supply chain needs.
Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Rivian’s choice of Georgia is a historic win that has put the state at the forefront of the electric mobility ecosystem.

Commissioner Wilson noted that the generational investment delivering well-paying jobs in Jasper, Morgan, Newton, and Walton Counties will benefit families, schools, and public services beyond the region.
source_title: Gov. Kemp, Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe and State Leaders Celebrate Rivian Day | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: The announcement was made on January 22, 2020.
Korean manufacturer Enchem Ltd. will build two new manufacturing plants in Jackson County, Georgia.
The project will create more than 300 jobs.
Enchem is investing $61.35 million in the project.
Governor Brian P. Kemp welcomed Enchem to Georgia, noting the project builds on the success of SK Innovation in Jackson County.
The project aligns with goals established during Governor Kemp's first international trip to Korea last June.
The market for electric vehicles and batteries continues its rapid growth, with the industry building a hub in Georgia.
Strong international partnerships between South Korea and Georgia will benefit hardworking Georgians and create opportunities throughout the state.
Enchem specializes in the development and manufacturing of electrolytes for rechargeable batteries and electrostatic double-layer capacitors (ELDCs).
Enchem is the first announced supplier for SK Innovation, which broke ground in Jackson County in March 2019 on its $1.67 billion battery manufacturing plant.
Since its founding in 2012, Enchem has developed multiple new electrolytes and functional additives to improve and extend battery life.
The company continues to increase its sales to major rechargeable battery and EDLC manufacturers worldwide.
EnChem CEO Oh Jung Kang stated the company will benefit from Georgia's infrastructure, transportation, and excellent weather.
Jackson County Board of Commissioners Chairman Tom Crow noted the county's thriving business climate continues to attract top companies from around the world.
GDEcD Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Global Commerce Division on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Jackson County Development Authority, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted the department's strategic representation in Korea since 1985 and its ongoing commitment to the Korean market.
Commissioner Wilson noted reliance on partnerships and programs like Georgia Quick Start to ensure workforce and company success.
In fiscal year 2019, South Korea was a leader in both foreign direct investment and jobs in Georgia, investing $1.87 billion and creating 2,507 jobs.
Enchem Ltd electrolytes are recognized for guaranteeing long battery life, high power, large capacity, high safety, and stability in rechargeable batteries and EDLCs.
Enchem's electrolytes have been recognized for performance in high-voltage, high-capacity, and high-power rechargeable batteries used in leading edge IT and electrical vehicle applications.

The rapid transition from small mobile technology rechargeable batteries to mid- and large-size hybrid and electric vehicle batteries (xES) and energy storage system (ESS) batteries has increased electrolyte consumption and customer requests.
Increased demand for electrolytes prompted the creation of two new plants.
source_title: Enchem to Build Two Facilities in Jackson County, Create 300 Jobs | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Hyundai Mobis is the largest tier-one supplier for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
Hyundai Mobis manufactures front and rear end chassis modules.
Hyundai Mobis manufactures cockpit modules.
Hyundai Mobis manufactures key parts to build batteries for electric vehicles.
Training for Mobis includes company-specific assembly courses, mechatronics, and electrical safety awareness.
Trainees are provided with safety and organizational development courses, such as Lean.
Trainees are provided with leadership training and conflict resolution courses.
Quick Start is using its main Advanced Manufacturing Training Center in Pooler, Ga., to provide the training.
Quick Start is using an interim facility for the training while a new center is under construction.
The new Hyundai Mobility Training Center of Georgia is under construction adjacent to the Metaplant.
The Metaplant, where Mobis’ operations in the Savannah area will be located, is slated to start production on vehicles by early 2025.
Quick Start’s Eastern Operations around Savannah, Georgia, are busier than ever as opening day approaches for HMGMA.
source_title: Mobis - Georgia Quick Start

facts: At Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, employees don’t work in a factory; Meta Pros fulfill a vision within the spaces of the Metaplant.
HMGMA is deploying its custom-designed vocabulary for a reason: what they will be achieving in Georgia will be more than a consumer product.
The leadership behind the conceptual vision at HMGMA sees that a smart factory in the 21st century must define value in more terms than just an accounting ledger.
The facility is adapting practices that emphasize flexibility, humanity, community, wellness, nature and a concept of mobility that means a journey is measured in more than just mileage.
Something more than just a car is created at a Metaplant.
To actualize the concept of “meta” calls for being aware of relationships and connections that aren’t just on the surface.

These connections are extra-linguistic, in language and beyond language, communicating a shared imaginary through the common language of space, color, shape, materials and finishes, behaviors and practices.
Add history, geography, climate and spirit, and a new world takes shape.
The facility is located in Bryan County.
The term “Meta” originates from the Greek meaning with, across or after, functioning as an adjective, combining form prefix, or meta-.
Savannah Technical College is associated with the project.
Ogeechee Technical College is associated with the project.
Oconee Fall Line Technical College is associated with the project.
Euisun Chung is identified as the Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair.
source_title: Understanding “Meta” - Georgia Quick Start

facts: Hyundai Motor Group celebrated the grand opening of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Ellabell, Georgia, on March 26.
HMGMA is the Group’s third production hub in the United States, following Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Autoland Georgia.
HMGMA operates as a Software Defined Factory (SDF) that integrates AI and robotics-powered automation.
The Group's investment in Georgia related to HMGMA and joint battery ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK On is $12.6 billion.
This $12.6 billion investment represents the largest single investment in Georgia's history.
The investment includes a total of 8,500 jobs at HMGMA by 2031.
Group’s total investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs.
These investments are expected to generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year, according to the Center for Automotive Research.
As of the end of 2024, more than $2.5 billion in capital investment has been announced by HMGMA suppliers across 12 counties in Georgia.
Approximately 6,900 jobs have been announced by HMGMA suppliers in those 12 counties as of the end of 2024.
The $2.5 billion supplier investment more than doubles the additional $1 billion investment originally anticipated.
Prior to its grand opening, HMGMA began production of the IONIQ 5 in October of last year.
In March, the plant commenced mass production of Hyundai Motor Company’s electrified flagship SUV, the IONIQ 9.
Kia models are slated to join the HMGMA production line next year.
Genesis vehicles are expected to join production at HMGMA in the near future.
The plant leverages an integrated mixed-model production system and is equipped to manufacture hybrid vehicles.

Grand opening attendees included Executive Chair Euisun Chung, Vice Chair Jaehoon Chang, Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO José Muñoz, Kia President and CEO Ho Sung Song, Governor Brian P. Kemp, U.S. Representative Buddy Carter, Georgia Institute of Technology President Ángel Cabrera, and Korean Ambassador Hyundong Cho.
Executive Chair Euisun Chung declared: “With HMGMA, we are building the future of mobility together with America, in America.”

Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that HMGMA is a once-in-a-generation opportunity made possible by partnerships with job creators and highlighted collaborations with Georgia’s universities, technical colleges, and Georgia Quick Start’s on-site training facility.
Governor Kemp noted that Georgia's award-winning infrastructure, such as its ports, connects companies to markets around the nation and globe.
Executive Chair Chung and Governor Kemp signed the hood of the first IONIQ 5 to roll off the HMGMA production line.
HMGMA has an annual production capacity of 300,000 units.
The plant scaled up advanced manufacturing technologies originally developed and validated at the Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center Singapore (HMGICS).
HMGMA features an automated production system connecting order collection, procurement, logistics, and assembly through AI, IT, robotics, and data technologies.
The plant includes a 41-acre ecological park with walking trails, sports fields, and picnic areas.
The plant will pilot Boston Dynamics’ humanoid robot, ‘All-New Atlas,’ to support its workforce.
Hyundai Mobis established its largest global production facility at HMGMA to manufacture 300,000 battery systems annually along with cockpit, front-end, chassis, and PE component modules.
Hyundai Glovis operates a Consolidation Center (CC) and Vehicle Processing Center (VPC) within HMGMA, using autonomous drones with AI-powered forecasting for just-in-time parts delivery.
Hyundai Steel processes ultra-high-strength automotive steel plates at the Georgia Steel Service Center (SSC) within HMGMA, marking its first use of aluminum-steel hybrid processing equipment.
Hyundai Transys established a seat production facility at HMGMA with an annual output of 420,000 units using advanced automation technologies.
Hyundai WIA deploys autonomous robots for logistics automation at HMGMA, while Hyundai Rotem supplied equipment for the press shop.
Construction is nearing completion on a joint battery cell plant between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution targeting an annual capacity of 30 GWh.
The LG Energy Solution joint venture is scheduled to open next year and will produce enough batteries for approximately 360,000 vehicles per year based on the IONIQ 5's 84 kWh pack.
Battery cells from the joint venture will be processed at Hyundai Mobis before being supplied to HMGMA, Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama, and Kia Autoland Georgia.
Seventeen key suppliers are either expanding or establishing new operations near HMGMA.
HMGMA's completion brings Hyundai Motor Group's total U.S. production network capacity to 1 million units annually.
The network includes Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (completed in 2005 with a capacity of 360,000 units) and Kia Autoland Georgia (completed in 2010 with a capacity of 340,000 units).
HMG is expected to surpass 30 million cumulative vehicle sales in the U.S. this year, nearly 40 years after launching the Hyundai Excel in 1986.
The Group plans to expand HMGMA’s annual production capacity to 500,000 units and establish an integrated U.S. production system capable of manufacturing 1.2 million vehicles annually.
Executive Chair Euisun Chung announced a commitment to invest $21 billion in the U.S. over the next four years during a White House visit on March 24.
In Louisiana, HMG is preparing to establish an Electric Arc Furnace-based integrated steel mill with an annual production capacity of 2.7 million tons.

HMG plans to accelerate commercialization of U.S.-based affiliates Boston Dynamics, Supernal, and Motional, while strengthening collaboration in autonomous driving, robotics, AI, and advanced air mobility (AAM).
Additional investments will support nuclear and renewable energy, as well as the expansion of EV charging infrastructure.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America Ushers in the Era of Future Mobility | Hyundai Motor Group

facts: The Economic Development Agreement was signed on May 2, 2022, between Rivian Horizon, LLC, the State of Georgia, the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), and the Joint Development Authority (JDA) of Jasper, Morgan, Newton, and Walton Counties.
Rivian Automotive, LLC executed the agreement to agree to provisions applicable to it.
The project aims to establish an electric vehicle manufacturing plant in Morgan County, Newton County, and the City of Social Circle, Georgia.
The company committed to a capital investment totaling $5,000,000,000, which may be increased at its option under Section 3.6(b).
The project will create 7,500 new full-time jobs directly employed by the company or an affiliate.
Average annual wages for the new jobs are $56,000.
The investment and job creation commitments must be met by December 31, 2028.
Performance standards require a minimum of 7,500 jobs and $5,000,000,000 investment to avoid incentive repayment.
Reaching an eighty percent combined performance level satisfies basic performance standards but must be maintained through the specified performance period.
Failure to meet performance standards triggers direct repayment of incentives to the applicable public party.
The project site consists of forty-four parcels totaling approximately 1,978 acres near Interstate Highway I-20 and US Highway 278 in Walton and Morgan Counties.
The JDA currently owns approximately 665.17 acres across three parcels designated as the JDA Property.
The JDA holds options to purchase an additional approximately 1,312.83 acres designated as the Option Property.
A trailing tract of approximately 272.2 acres will be acquired by the JDA no later than July 15, 2022.
The facility site will be provided to the company on a long-term basis via a rental agreement and is offered as-is with all faults.
GDEcD oversees state activities for the project and will appoint a dedicated government liaison to monitor performance.
The JDA acts as an instrumentality of Jasper, Morgan, Newton, and Walton Counties under the Development Authorities Law.
GDEcD confirmed REBA budget funding is available for the Project Development Grant.
The Georgia legislature allocated $111,307,760 to the Georgia Department of Community Affairs via the Amended Year 2022 Budget to fund JDA Grant costs.
A commitment letter from the DOT was provided for funding public roadway improvements.
The JDA authorized issuance of Project Bonds (Series 2022) in an aggregate principal amount up to $15,000,000,000.
Public parties will provide site infrastructure work, permit assistance, and workforce training facilities and programs.
Due diligence materials provided by public parties include Phase I and II environmental assessments, wetlands delineation, geotechnical reports, traffic studies, and title work.
The JDA is responsible for conducting additional due diligence per Exhibit A-4 and will share final reports with the company within ten days of completion.
Confidentiality of due diligence reports is subject to the Georgia Open Records Act, Open Meetings Act, and Trade Secrets Act.
Incentives include a Project Development Grant, state tax incentives, real and personal property tax incentives, PILOT payments, Freeport Tax Exemption, and Mega-Projects Job Tax Credit.
A Revenue Sharing Agreement for Stanton Springs dated November 16, 2021, governs intergovernmental contracts among the counties, school districts, and taxing authorities.
The project requires a §404 Wetlands Permit from the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers for stream and wetland impacts.
Workforce training commitments reference the Quick Start program (Exhibit C) and a manufacturing training center with defined construction funds and option prices.
Early access to the project site may be granted via an early access agreement prior to or after conveyance to the state, excluding the trailing tract until acquired.
LG Energy Solution Vertech and Qcells announced a multi-year commitment to install reliable, affordable, American-made storage products for Qcells' development projects across the country.
The agreement provides American-made energy storage projects across a portfolio of utility-scale sites scheduled from 2028-2030.
All projects will meet U.S. domestic content requirements.
The agreement includes 5GWh of lithium-ion energy storage projects for utility-scale projects.
The agreement covers lifecycle services from LG Energy Solution Vertech.
In May 2024, Qcells entered into battery purchase agreements with LG Energy Solution Vertech totaling 4.8 GWh of BESS projects developed across the U.S.
LG Energy Solution's battery energy storage products are made in Michigan.
Qcells' solar panels are made in Georgia.
Leveraging made-in-America products supports U.S. manufacturing jobs, strengthens the nation's clean-energy supply chains, enhances grid reliability, and reduces exposure to trade risks and tariffs.
Chris Hodrick is CEO of Qcells EPC.
Qcells operates the largest solar panel manufacturing facility of its kind in the United States, based in Dalton, Georgia.
The company is building a second factory in Cartersville, Georgia, where it will manufacture ingots, wafers, cells and finished panels.
The Cartersville factory represents the largest investment in U.S. solar history to create an integrated, sustainable domestic supply chain.
Once fully operational, Qcells' annual solar panel production capacity in the US will reach 8.4 gigawatts.
This capacity equals nearly 46,000 panels every day.
The production is enough to power more than 1.3 million American homes each year.
Jaehong Park is CEO and President at LG Energy Solution Vertech.
The energy storage systems included in the commitment are built using the latest LG Energy Solution containerized lithium-ion battery solutions with LFP cells manufactured at U.S. facilities.
LG Energy Solution is manufacturing up to 16.5 GWh of battery energy storage cells in Holland, MI.
Production at LG Energy Solution's sites across North America is set to ramp up to around 50GWh by the end of 2026.
LG Energy Solution Vertech is an energy storage systems integrator backed by LG Energy Solution.
The company delivers integrated, reliable, and bankable grid-scale energy storage systems supported by in-house services and software.
In 2022, LG Energy Solution purchased NEC Energy Solutions and established LG Energy Solution Vertech Inc.
Via a single contract, the company provides systems, warranties, services, and software.

Qcells EPC provides complete utility-scale turnkey solutions through the entire project lifecycle from modules, solar and energy storage project development, and Engineering Procurement and Construction (EPC) services to the US utility-scale market.
Qcells USA has a track record that includes 8.4 GW of sustainably produced module manufacturing capabilities in Georgia.
Qcells USA has nearly 2 GW of developed, constructed, and operating projects.
Qcells EPC is a flagship company of Hanwha Group, a FORTUNE Global 500 firm and a Top 7 business enterprise in South Korea.
source_title: LG Energy Solution Vertech and Qcells Partner to Deliver 5GWh of U.S. Energy Storage Projects

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group-LG Energy Solution EV Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced an EV battery cell manufacturing joint venture in the U.S. on May 26, 2023.
The two companies signed a memorandum of understanding to produce EV batteries in the U.S. and accelerate Hyundai Motor Group’s electrification efforts in North America.
The signing ceremony took place at LG Energy Solution’s headquarters in Seoul on May 26, 2023.
Jaehoon Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, attended the signing ceremony.
Youngsoo Kwon, CEO of LG Energy Solution, attended the signing ceremony.
Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution will each hold a 50 percent stake in the joint venture.
The joint venture will involve an investment of over USD 4.3 billion (KRW 5.7 trillion).
Jaehoon Chang stated that Hyundai Motor Group is focusing on electrification efforts to secure a leadership position in the global auto industry.
Jaehoon Chang stated the group will create a strong foundation to lead the global EV transition through establishing a new EV battery cell plant with LG Energy Solution.
Youngsoo Kwon stated that two strong leaders in the auto and battery industries have joined hands to drive the EV transition in America.
Youngsoo Kwon stated LG Energy Solution will commit its best efforts to offering ultimate sustainable energy solutions by advancing product competitiveness and global operational expertise.
The annual production capacity of the new joint venture will be 30 GWh.
The facility will be able to support the production of 300,000 units of EVs annually.
The new battery cell manufacturing facility will be located in Bryan County, Savannah, Georgia.
The facility site is adjacent to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, which was under construction at the time of the announcement.
Construction on the facility is scheduled to begin in the second half of 2023.
The joint venture plans to start battery production at the end of 2025 at the earliest.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs using cells from the new plant.
The assembled battery packs will be supplied to Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. manufacturing facilities for the production of Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
The new facility aims to create a stable supply of batteries in the region and enable the group to respond quickly to soaring EV demand in the U.S. market.
With this joint venture, LG Energy Solution will have seven battery plants operating or under construction in the U.S.
LG Energy Solution is concentrating most of its resources on expanding production capacity in the U.S.
LG Energy Solution aims to expedite the clean energy transition in the U.S. by ramping up local production to provide innovative products at scale and with speed.

Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution have a long-standing partnership in electrification, having previously supplied EV batteries for vehicles including the Elantra Hybrid, Kona Electric, and IONIQ 6.
The Hyundai Elantra Hybrid, an LPi hybrid vehicle introduced in 2009, was Hyundai Motor Group’s first electrified model.
In 2021, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution began construction of a battery cell joint venture in Indonesia, which is set to start production in the first half of 2024.
Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution aim to further strengthen their ties through ongoing EV battery cooperation.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution to Establish Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture in the U.S. | Hyundai Motor Group

facts: Rivian is increasing its initial production capacity to 300,000 vehicles annually for its future manufacturing facility in Stanton Springs North, Georgia.
The updated capacity represents a 50 percent increase over the initially planned capacity of 200,000 units.
The capacity increase will facilitate a lower cost per unit while providing significant room for future expansion in later phases.
The expanded capacity aims to support thousands of U.S. manufacturing jobs and bolster American tech leadership on the global stage.
Rivian Founder and CEO RJ Scaringe stated the company is excited to partner with the U.S. Department of Energy to grow its manufacturing footprint in Georgia.
RJ Scaringe noted that the R2 vehicle model dramatically expands Rivian's market opportunity and that thousands of dedicated workers will be instrumental to scaling American manufacturing.
Rivian worked with the U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Energy Dominance Financing (EDF) to update its DOE loan aimed at bolstering American manufacturing and construction jobs.

The total DOE loan is aligned with the updated facility design and roadmap at up to $4.5 billion, comprising $4,006 million in principal and $494 million in capitalized interest for the initial phase.
Rivian expects to first draw on the DOE loan by early 2027.
Vertical construction at the Georgia plant is expected to commence in spring 2026.

Preparations are underway for the development of the stamping press area, which is one of the most capital-intensive and technically demanding projects within the plant from a construction perspective.
Construction progress will accelerate this summer as the facility’s primary buildings begin to take shape.
Vehicle production at the Georgia plant is expected to begin in late 2028.
Rivian recently announced a partnership with Uber.
Rivian is expected to build up to 50,000 robotaxis at its Georgia plant beginning in late 2028.
Rivian (NASDAQ: RIVN) is an American automotive technology company that develops and manufactures electric vehicles as well as vertically integrated technologies and services.
The company utilizes innovation across electrical architecture, end-to-end software, autonomous driving platform, artificial intelligence, and propulsion to create its vehicles.
Rivian vehicles are manufactured in the United States and sold directly to consumer and commercial customers.
source_title: Rivian Announces Optimized Capacity Plan for Georgia Plant

facts: Strong economic ties between Georgia and Korea are founded on personal relationships between leaders, policy makers, and representatives crucial to building long-lasting global partnerships.
The State of Georgia recently welcomed a delegation of high-ranking officials from the Korean province of Gyeong sang Buk-Do to discuss future business opportunities.
The State of Georgia and Korea have a long history of mutually beneficial partnerships, investments, and cultural exchange.
In March, GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson and Mr. Soo Deuk Sohn (Senior Executive Vice President of KOTRA) celebrated the opening of the new KOTRA overseas office in Atlanta.
The new KOTRA office is the eighth in the United States and the tenth in North America.
KOTRA’s overseas offices host teams dedicated to facilitating trade and investment opportunities between host states and Korea.

The automotive industry has been a cornerstone for the economic relationship between Georgia and Korea, with environmentally friendly and sustainable technology companies from Korea finding a home in the state.

Relationships developed by Georgia’s international office in Seoul led to the Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG) project announcement just six months after Kia requested a special meeting with GDEcD officials.
Since beginning operations in 2009, Kia Georgia has attracted numerous suppliers including HYUNDAI TRANSYS, Sewon Precision, DAEHAN Solution, and Powertech America between 2009 and 2014.

Kia’s relationships in Georgia span more than a dozen local entities, with state partners including the Departments of Labor, Transportation, and Community Affairs, the Georgia Ports Authority, the Attorney General’s office, and the state’s technical college system.

The Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program partnered with Kia to construct and operate the 70,000-square-foot Kia Georgia Training Center for customized advanced industrial training.

Quick Start developed a maintenance certification program at West Georgia Technical College to help students fine-tune industrial maintenance knowledge and open employment opportunities with large manufacturers like Kia Georgia.
Kia Georgia sponsors local school system STEM programs as well as Troup County’s THINC College and Career Academy, a model facility for exploring automotive and manufacturing career opportunities.
The Ray, a section of West Georgia’s I-85 serving as a proving ground for evolving transportation technology, is a community project supported by Kia Georgia.
As part of the corridor, Kia Georgia sponsors a free solar panel charging center and tire inspection center at the state-operated West Point Visitor Information Center.

Kia Georgia is a proud inaugural partner in Georgia Made™, a program requiring 50% of the final product to be manufactured in Georgia that provides logistics, manufacturing, and marketing support to qualifying companies.
In 2018, the announcement that SK On would manufacture EV batteries in Jackson County attracted global electric mobility suppliers like Duckyang, Enchem Ltd, TEKLAS, GEDIA, and others.
SKC, a subsidiary of SK Group, located in Georgia in 1996 as the first South Korean company to establish a manufacturing plant in the state.

SKC’s original Newton County facility became one of the major polyester films (PET film) producers in North America, providing high-quality, environmentally friendly service as a base-film market leader.

In 2021, SKC and several business partners announced plans to manufacture glass-based substrates for semiconductor chips in Covington with an investment of more than $473 million to create more than 400 new jobs.
Dr. Sung Jin Kim, SKC Director of New Business Development, stated that Georgia will be a basecamp for SKC’s AI and high-speed data center semiconductor applications.

Georgia Tech played a key role in the venture through research collaboration with SKC over the last decade and a half on advanced circuitry research and methods for packaging fragile technology as part of its Packaging Research Center, launched in 1993 by Georgia Research Alliance Eminent Scholar Rao Tummala.
In addition to the Covington location, SK On is investing nearly $2.6 billion in two battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County to supply electric vehicles including the Ford F-150 Lightning.
Founded in 1976, SK Group is one of the largest conglomerates in South Korea, and its new facility represents the third major investment by SK Group in the State of Georgia.
Qcells was attracted to Georgia’s emerging EV ecosystem after SK On's 2018 announcement and played a role in securing new investments in the state.
During its nationwide site-selection process, Meta Platforms Inc. announced it would require 100% new renewable energy sources to power its planned $1 billion state-of-the-art facility.
In 2018, Meta announced its choice of Stanton Springs, Georgia for the facility.

A large part of Meta’s decision to locate in Newton County was that Georgia’s utility providers had found a way to meet the renewable energy requirement using solar panels manufactured at Qcells plant in Dalton just up I-20 and I-75, which is the largest solar panel manufacturing facility in the western hemisphere.
The solar panels were installed and maintained in south Georgia’s Early County.
Qcells’ 300,000-square-foot facility in Dalton employs more than 700 local workers and produces 1.7 GW of solar module capacity annually.
The Qcells facility opened in 2019 and produces 12,000 panels a day.
Leadership from the State of Georgia and Qcells agreed that Georgia was positioned to continue growing as a leader in the solar and renewable power industries.

Creating a local supply chain that can power an industry ecosystem is important to attracting opportunities, and Qcells has become a key source of renewable energy options for new or expanding businesses.
The State of Georgia hosts a Korean consular presence since 1971 and maintains continuous representation in Seoul since 1985.
The state has a dedicated Director of Korean Investment based in the State of Georgia.
In 2019, Governor Kemp made an economic development mission to Korea his very first as Governor.
source_title: Korea Connections: Successful Business Built on Strong Relationships | Georgia

facts: Georgia has attracted record investment over the last several years to build an ecosystem across the electric vehicle supply chain, battery production, eVTOL aircraft, and other advanced solutions.
Georgia has aligned its workforce programs with industry to secure long-term talent for the electric mobility sector.
Georgia began its transition toward attracting electric mobility and battery technologies well before the Inflation Reduction Act was signed in August of 2022.
Hanwha Qcells opened its first Georgia facility in 2019.
Hyundai selected Georgia for its operations before the Inflation Reduction Act became law.
In 2018, SK Battery America announced a historic investment in Jackson County.
Groundbreaking for the SK Battery America project followed in 2019, bringing 2,000 jobs and $1.67 billion in capital investment.
The SK Battery America announcement attracted investment from global e-mobility suppliers including Duckyang, Enchem Ltd, TEKLAS, and GEDIA.
In 2021, Rivian announced what was then the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history, comprising 7,500 jobs and $5 billion in capital investment.
Vertical construction on the Rivian site is set to begin this year, with vehicles expected to roll off the line as early as 2028.
Rivian has established its east coast headquarters in Atlanta.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America selected Georgia in October of 2021 and formally signed its agreement in May of 2022.
The initial Hyundai announcement included 8,100 jobs and $5.5 billion in investment.
With the addition of the LG Energy Solution joint venture on site, the combined total for the Hyundai project rose to 8,500 jobs and more than $7.5 billion in capital investment.
Twenty suppliers have announced plans to locate in Georgia, bringing more than 7,000 additional jobs and $4.5 billion more in investment.
By the time the Inflation Reduction Act was signed, at least 34 electric mobility projects were already active or in discussion in Georgia.
Between 2018 and 2023, e-mobility and battery projects represented more than $27 billion in planned investment.
In 2015, Georgia attempted to recruit a major automotive OEM to Bryan County but ultimately lost the project.
Following that loss, state and local leaders formed a joint development authority to ensure regional alignment.
Leaders preserved land for a future mega-auto project and planned infrastructure years in advance, including power, water, gas, sewer, and rail.
Georgia studied wetlands early to reduce risk during its seven-year commitment that laid the groundwork to win Hyundai.
Electric mobility in Georgia includes air mobility via eVTOL solutions like those from Archer Aviation, which are being manufactured in the state.
Georgia Power and Southern Company are investing in reliable, scalable power to support large-scale manufacturing.
Georgia Power and Southern Company are expanding EV charging infrastructure across urban and rural communities to alleviate range anxiety.
Georgia Power and Southern Company are piloting managed charging programs that reduce peak demand and enhance grid stability.
Georgia Power has extended both its EV infrastructure and charger rebate programs through 2028.
Georgia Power began a vehicle-to-grid (V2G) pilot using electric school buses this year, marking the first of its kind across the Southern Company system.
Hyundai and Kia can scale internal combustion or electric production as market demand dictates.
Rivian is adjusting its product mix in response to consumer preferences.

Georgia’s approach allows organic adoption by bringing public and private partners together to build infrastructure that lets consumers drive the pace of change rather than mandating market manipulation.
The current electric mobility wave mirrors the economic impact Georgia experienced following Kia’s decision to locate in West Georgia nearly two decades ago.
Competition for automotive OEMs is currently intense in Georgia.
Georgia’s success in electric mobility stems from starting early, staying disciplined, and aligning policy, infrastructure, workforce, and energy leadership around a shared vision.
source_title: Powering the Next Generation of Mobility | Select Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Archer Aviation Inc. will create 1,000 jobs and invest $118 million over 10 years in a new state-of-the-art electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft (eVTOL) manufacturing facility.
The facility will be located alongside the Covington Municipal Airport in Newton County.
Georgia has made fostering innovation a priority to bring the jobs of the future to the No. 1 state for business.
Georgia's established leadership in the aerospace industry was essential to landing Archer Aviation in Newton County.
Archer is based in Santa Clara, CA and is currently developing and manufacturing eVTOL aircraft for use in Urban Air Mobility networks.
Adam Goldstein, Archer’s Founder and CEO, stated that Archer decided to locate its first manufacturing facility in Covington as it progresses towards commercialization.
Archer's goal is creating Urban Air Mobility networks across major cities nationwide, which requires ramping production of its aircraft.
Glen Burks, Archer’s Vice President of Manufacturing, noted the new manufacturing facility is a crucial next step in bringing their vision for eVTOL travel to life.
Archer plans to break ground on the facility in the coming months.
Archer plans to construct the facility adjacent to the Covington Municipal Airport in Newton County, which was established in 1963 as part of a long-term airport master plan.
The site is strategically accessible by Georgia’s robust interstate system via I-20, and large adjacent parcels of land were set aside for future development that the new facility will use.
Archer’s new facility will consist of three buildings across more than 96 acres.
Construction is expected to begin in December 2022.
The buildout will follow a phased approach for eVTOL aircraft production, with eventual capacity able to produce up to 2,300 aircraft per year.
Hiring is expected to begin in late 2023.
The facility is slated to open in the first half of 2024.
Mayor Steve Horton expressed excitement for Archer Aviation to join Covington's business community and noted that Covington has always focused on quality jobs and new technology.

Lanier Sims, Chairman of the Newton County Industrial Development Authority, stated that Archer fits perfectly with Newton County's current industry mix and will be an asset to the airport in Covington.
The Newton County Industrial Development Authority is committed to ensuring the workforce is prepared for the community's future and ready for jobs from Archer Aviation.

Metro Atlanta Chamber President and CEO Katie Kirkpatrick welcomed Archer Aviation to metro Atlanta, citing the region's aviation industry expertise and support from academic, business, civic, and government partners.
The Global Commerce Industrial Projects team represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) on this competitive project.

Partners supporting the project include the Newton County Industrial Development Authority, Metro Atlanta Chamber of Commerce, the Georgia Center of Innovation’s Aerospace industry team, Georgia EMC, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Tech.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted that Archer Aviation is on the forefront of a technological evolution in aviation using an innovative eVTOL aircraft design to sustainably tackle mobility issues in urban centers.
The GDEcD Center of Innovation’s specialized Aerospace team has been involved in fostering Georgia’s aerospace and defense industries.

Home to more than 800 aerospace companies, Georgia maintains a robust aerospace education pipeline including a dozen high schools with training programs, five technical colleges with aviation programs, and several universities offering degrees in aerospace engineering.
The Georgia Center of Innovation for Aerospace is a strategic arm of the GDEcD that has been cultivating an ecosystem for innovative companies like Archer Aviation for more than 10 years.
The Georgia Center of Innovation for Aerospace builds and connects Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS) networks for the commercialization of Advanced Air Mobility and UAS Systems.

Archer (NYSE: ACHR) is designing and developing electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft for use in urban air mobility networks, with a mission to unlock the skies and reimagine how people move.
Archer's team is based in Santa Clara, CA.

links.context: Press Secretary email for Governor Kemp's office
Deputy Press Secretary email for Governor Kemp's office

source_title: Archer to Create 1,000 Jobs in Covington | Georgia

facts: President Biden is pushing hard to get more Americans to buy electric vehicles to rein in global warming and spur domestic manufacturing.
The $2 trillion infrastructure plan includes about $174 billion to boost electric vehicle sales and production — more than the president proposes spending on roads and bridges.

Biden has been pulled into a global trade dispute over the fate of lithium ion battery factories in Georgia that threatens his electric vehicle goals for the United States and the Democratic Party's fragile success in the key swing state.
South Korean corporation SK Innovation has built a large battery factory about 70 miles northeast of Atlanta, in the city of Commerce, population 7,000.
Another factory alongside it is under construction, and a third is in the works.
All told, they are expected to represent 2 million square feet of manufacturing space.

Earlier this year, SK said its Georgia operation may shut down because of a ruling against the company by the U.S. International Trade Commission in a dispute with LG Chem, a competitor also based in South Korea.
Even with the deep political divisions in the state, Georgia Democrats and Republicans have found common cause in trying to save the factories.
Democratic Sen. Raphael Warnock said the ITC decision "threatens seriously" the future of the project in Commerce and Biden's own goals.
Warnock stated during a Senate Commerce Committee meeting earlier this year: "We would lose 2,600 clean energy jobs in the short term, and 10,000 in the long term."
In a letter to the Biden administration, Georgia Republican Gov. Brian Kemp requested that the president overrule the trade commission, which he has the power to do.
Kemp said: "The livelihoods of thousands of Georgians are now in your hands." and "This critical opportunity requires us to work together for the good of my state and our country."
Biden has until this Sunday to decide whether he will reject the trade commission's ruling.
Letting the ruling stand could damage the Democratic Party's credibility in Georgia and hobble a key supplier in a domestic EV market already hungry for batteries.
Vetoing the decision would call into question the administration's global efforts to advance intellectual property protections, a major priority of American business.
The White House did not respond to a request for comment.

The dispute at the heart of Biden's choice stems from allegations by LG that as demand for electric vehicle batteries grew worldwide, SK developed a "catch-up plan" to expand its share of the U.S. electric vehicle market.
As part of the plan, SK allegedly sought out and hired LG employees to "mine them for LG's know-how."
In 2019, LG filed a complaint with the ITC, arguing that SK became a viable competitor in the U.S., winning contracts with Ford and Volkswagen, only because it "misappropriated" trade secrets.

As the case moved forward, SK destroyed some internal documents, a move that an administrative law judge wrote "was done with a culpable state of mind and with intent to hide evidence of trade secret misappropriation."
SK argued that the destruction was "routine" and that it preserved documents relevant to the case.

In an order that did not rule on whether SK stole trade secrets, the ITC slammed the company, writing that it was eager to destroy the documents and that it demonstrated a "callous disregard" for the scale of the "extraordinary" act.
The ITC barred SK from importing the materials it needs to make batteries in Commerce, with a carveout for existing deals it has with Ford and Volkswagen.
In response, SK has suggested it may pull out of the U.S.
About a century ago, leaders in the small city of Harmony Grove, Ga., decided they wanted to make sure everyone knew their home was a center for business and capitalism, said Mayor Clark Hill.
The textile industry reigned at the time, and they changed the name to Commerce.
Hill was first elected mayor in 2011, and he's tried to make sure the city lives up to its name.
Perhaps his biggest achievement is helping to persuade SK to build massive battery factories in Commerce.

"It's surreal," said Hill, as he looked at one towering gray factory building and the steel skeleton of another under construction. "I mean, honestly, it seems like yesterday that we met with the scouts from SK."
SK is expected to spend more than $2 billion on factories in Commerce, and the state of Georgia and local authorities have provided a total of at least $300 million in tax breaks for the factories.
Hill says SK has donated money for libraries in Commerce in addition to the $400,000 it donated to aid the state's response to the coronavirus pandemic.

"This plant is going to serve basically the Western Hemisphere," Hill said. "So they have an operation that they're expanding right now in Hungary that takes care of Europe, and then they have a facility in South Korea that takes care of Asia, and we will take care of Mexico and the Americas and Canada."

Hill worries that if Biden doesn't reject the trade commission's ruling, it could harm or even halt the massive development he has facilitated, one that he hopes will give hope to the community and keep young people from moving out of town.

"There's a lot at stake here," Hill said, directing a message to the president. "Not just for our community, but for the Southeast, and also for, you know, redefining who we are as America, you know. If we want to do this clean energy thing and decrease our dependency on fossil fuels, I mean, this is the way to do it."

There is a worry that if Biden rejects the trade commission's ruling and keeps SK's operations going in Georgia, it could actually hamper the administration's long-term goal of getting more Americans to buy electric vehicles.

"If Biden overturns the ITC ruling, then that is basically saying, 'Yeah, it's OK to misappropriate trade secrets," said Sam Abuelsamid, an analyst with Guidehouse Insights, a market intelligence firm.
Even though that's the signal some may receive, the ITC decision did not say that SK was guilty of stealing trade secrets; it ruled that it destroyed potential evidence.

Abuelsamid worries that overturning the trade commission's ruling would stifle innovation in the U.S. And that's just what it will take to develop cheaper batteries that last longer so more people will buy electric vehicles, like Biden wants.

They "spend billions of dollars every year on R&D to try to improve this technology," he said. But if there's no guarantee they can sell what they've developed, "you're gonna be probably less inclined to make that investment. And you're just gonna sit on what you have instead."

What's more, Abuelsamid said, there's so much demand in the U.S. for batteries right now, as more auto manufacturers plan to roll out electric vehicles, that even if SK leaves the U.S., it's unlikely those factories in Commerce will sit empty.
In fact, he expects more factories to come in.

"This is something where not only is this plant going to be operating," Abuelsamid said, "but there is probably going to be one built in the state, you know, the next state over, and the state to your north, and to the south, and in many other places that are going to be required to supply this industry over the next 15 years."
source_title: Betting Big On Electric Vehicles, Biden Faces Fraught Decision On Ga. Battery Plant : NPR

facts: Aspen Aerogels Inc., a Massachusetts-based electric vehicle supplier, has abandoned plans to build a manufacturing facility in Bulloch County near Statesboro, Georgia.
The company announced on Thursday during an investor call that it will instead increase production capacity at its East Providence, R.I., plant.
Governor Brian Kemp originally announced the Statesboro project in 2022.
At the time of the 2022 announcement, the company was planning to invest $325 million in the plant and create more than 250 jobs.
Production had been expected to start late in 2023.
Aspen Aerogels manufactures thermal barriers that insulate electric car batteries from fires.

CFO and Treasurer Ricardo Rodriguez wrote that in early 2023, pre-empting a reset in EV demand expectations, the company decided to right-time the construction of its planned second aerogel manufacturing facility in Statesboro, Georgia, and subsequently ramped up external manufacturing capacity.

Rodriguez stated that the external manufacturing model has fully demonstrated its ability to efficiently increase aerogel supply, making a capital-light and modular capacity plan the most efficient path to creating value.
Demand for electric vehicles has not grown as much in recent years as EV manufacturers expected.
Factors contributing to slower EV demand include high sticker prices, an insufficient number of charging stations, and government policies that have discouraged car buyers from purchasing EVs.
The Trump administration recently froze a $5 billion program aimed at funding the construction of EV charging stations.

links.context: Capitol Beat nonprofit news service information

source_title: EV Supplier Cancels Plan to Build Plant Near Statesboro - Columbus CEO

facts: On September 4, 2025, an immigration raid occurred at the Hyundai-LG battery plant under construction near Savannah, Georgia.
The raid resulted in the detention of 475 workers, including more than 300 South Korean nationals.
LG Energy Solution announced that construction at the plant will remain on hold until the first half of 2026.
The facility's opening is delayed by at least two to three months to early 2026.
The plant is a cornerstone of Hyundai's $12.6 billion investment in Georgia.
Plans for the facility include producing batteries for up to 500,000 hybrid and electric vehicles across Hyundai's brand portfolio.
Hyundai Motor CEO José Muñoz confirmed that specialized South Korean workers deported in the raid were essential to the joint venture battery plant.
The company had to move workers from other plants to make up for the lost labor.
Approximately 400 state and federal law enforcement personnel participated in the operation.
The initial search warrant targeted just four Mexican nationals as 'targeted persons.'
ICE agents arrested South Korean workers after determining they entered on B-1 visas or through ESTA.
Immigration attorney Charles Kuck, representing 11 individuals, noted that ICE agents did not bring Korean language interpreters to the raid.
One detained South Korean worker had just arrived the night before and was in a business suit attending a meeting when arrested.
Multiple sources indicate a White House-imposed arrest quota drove the mass detention.
Stephen Miller, White House deputy chief of staff and homeland security adviser, publicly stated a goal of 3,000 immigrant arrests per day.
Charles Kuck told Forbes that ICE considered the operation successful because it met Miller's quota, despite arresting people eligible for their work type.
NBC News confirmed the raid was part of a broader Trump administration deportation drive to fulfill campaign promises.
Top US diplomat Christopher Landau expressed regret over the raid during meetings with South Korean counterparts.
Washington promised that South Korean workers would face no disadvantages in reentering the US and that similar incidents would be prevented.
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung called the incident 'bewildering' and an 'unjust infringement on the activities of our people.'
President Lee warned the incident could discourage future foreign investment in the United States.
A South Korean editorial, cited by Paul Krugman, compared the sight of detained workers being led out in chains to images of African slaves in the 18th and 19th centuries.
Charles Kuck reported that companies in Asia and Europe are considering holding off on big US investments for at least three years due to the raid.
South Korean tech giants Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix have been investing billions into US facilities as part of reshoring efforts.
On September 19, Hyundai confirmed it is moving forward with expanding its Georgia plant.
The expansion commitment includes $2.7 billion and 3,000 new jobs for phase two of the complex.
The US H-1B visa program allows temporary hiring of foreign workers in 'specialty occupations' but is highly selective due to an annual cap and lottery system.
On Friday, Sept. 19, President Trump signed a proclamation imposing a $100,000 fee for H-1B visas granted to skilled foreign workers.
The administration announced on Saturday that the new fee would only apply to new H-1B applicants.
South Korean President Lee urged the US to normalize the visa process related to investment by securing sufficient quotas or creating a new visa category.

LGES North America President and Chief Strategy Officer Bob Lee noted that difficulty obtaining visas has long been a challenge for battery companies, but increased attention on the issue will be beneficial.
