Georgia Governor Brian Kemp suggested the raid may ultimately lead to improvements in the visa system, smoothing the way for South Korean employees to more easily obtain legal permission to work in the US.
source_title: Georgia EV Battery Plant Raid Fallout Impacts Industry

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy announced it will make a $6.6 billion loan to Rivian Automotive to build a factory in Georgia that had stalled as the startup struggled to become profitable.

It is unclear whether the administration can complete the loan before Donald Trump becomes president again in less than two months, or whether the Trump administration might try to claw the money back.
Donald Trump previously vowed to end federal electric vehicle tax credits, which are worth up to $7,500 for new zero-emission vehicles and $4,000 for used ones.
Rivian began producing large electric R1 SUVs, pickup trucks, and delivery vans at a former Mitsubishi factory in Normal, Illinois, in 2021.
Months after going public, the California-based company announced it would build a second, larger, $5 billion plant about 40 miles (64 kilometers) east of Atlanta, near the town of Social Circle.
The R1 vehicles cost $70,000 or more.
The company plans to produce R2 vehicles, a smaller SUV, in Georgia with lower price tags aimed at a mass market.
The first phase of Rivian's Georgia factory is projected to make 200,000 vehicles a year.
A second phase of the Georgia factory will be capable of producing another 200,000 vehicles a year.
Eventually, the plant is projected to employ 7,500 workers.
Rivian was unable to meet production and sales targets and rapidly burned through cash.
In March, the company said it would pause construction of the Georgia plant.
The company said it would begin assembling its R2 SUV in Illinois instead.
CEO RJ Scaringe said pausing construction would allow Rivian to start selling the R2 sooner and save $2.25 billion in capital spending.
In June, German automaker Volkswagen AG said it would invest $5 billion in Rivian in a joint venture in which Rivian would share software and electrical technology with Volkswagen.
The investment from Volkswagen eased Rivian's cash crunch.
Rivian announced that its plans to make the R2 and the smaller R3 in Georgia are back on following the loan announcement.
Production of the R2 and R3 vehicles in Georgia will begin in 2028.

CEO RJ Scaringe stated, "This loan would enable Rivian to more aggressively scale our U.S. manufacturing footprint for our competitively priced R2 and R3 vehicles that emphasize both capability and affordability."

The Energy Department said the loan would substantially boost electric vehicles made in the United States and support Biden's goal of having zero-emission vehicles make up half of all new U.S. sales by 2030.

The department stated, "As one of a few American EV startups with light duty vehicles already on the road, Rivian's Georgia facility will allow the company to reach production volumes that make its products more cost competitive and accelerate access to international markets."
The loan includes $6 billion, plus $600 million in interest that will be rolled into the principal.
The money comes from the Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing Loan Program, which provides low-interest loans to make fuel-efficient vehicles and components.
The program has focused mostly on loans to new battery factories for electric vehicles under Biden, but earlier helped finance initial production of the Tesla Model S and Nissan Leaf.
The loan program was created in 2007 and requires a "reasonable prospect of repayment" of the loan.
Under Biden, the program has announced deals totaling $33.3 billion, including $9.2 billion for massive battery plants in Tennessee and Kentucky for Ford's electric vehicles.

Democratic U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff, a vocal supporter of electric vehicle and solar manufacturing in Georgia, hailed the announcement as "yet another historic federal investment in Georgia electric vehicle manufacturing."
Ossoff had asked Energy Secretary Jennifer Granholm to support the loan in July.
Ossoff stated, "Our federal manufacturing incentives are driving economic development across the state of Georgia."
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp says his goal is to make Georgia a center of the electric vehicle industry.

Kemp has had a strained relationship with the Biden administration over its industrial policy, even as some studies have found Georgia has netted more electric vehicle investment than any other state.
Kemp has long claimed that manufacturers were picking Georgia before Biden's signature climate law, the Inflation Reduction Act, was passed.

Efforts to bring Rivian to Georgia predated the Biden administration and "our shared vision to bring opportunity to Georgia will remain no matter who resides in the White House or what party controls Congress," said Kemp spokesperson Garrison Douglas.
The loan to Rivian could rescue one of the Kemp administration's signature economic development projects even as Biden leaves office.
That situation could put Rivian and Kemp in the position of defending the loan if Trump tries to quash it.
State and local governments offered Rivian an incentive package worth an estimated $1.5 billion in 2022.
Neighbors opposed to development of the Georgia site mounted legal challenges.
State and local governments spent around $125 million to buy and prepare the nearly 2,000-acre (810-hectare) site.
The state has completed most of $50 million in roadwork that it pledged.
Rivian's construction pause contrasts with rapid construction at Hyundai Motor Group's $7.6 billion electric vehicle and battery complex near Savannah.
Hyundai said in October that it had begun production in Ellabell, where it plans to eventually employ 8,500 workers.
source_title: Biden administration to loan $6.6B to EV maker Rivian to build Georgia factory that automaker paused

canonical_name: Senate Bill 26 (Georgia Electric Vehicle Future Act)
facts: During the latest state legislative session, the Georgia House passed a measure that proponents say will “advance” the state’s electric vehicle industry.
State officials have given millions of dollars in taxpayer-funded incentives to various EV projects.
The state provided $1.5 billion in incentives for a Rivian Automotive electric vehicle assembly plant in Morgan and Newton counties.
Senate Bill 26, the Georgia Electric Vehicle Future Act, permits the Georgia Department of Economic Development to launch a statewide electric vehicle manufacturing program.
Under the program, the agency would focus on developing, marketing and promoting investments and job creation for Georgia’s EV industry.

State Rep. Chuck Martin (R-Alpharetta) stated in a constituent newsletter that the state economic development agency would work with state agencies, including the Georgia Department of Transportation, to deploy “the appropriate infrastructure,” including EV charging stations.
The legislation would establish the Georgia Electric Vehicle Manufacturing Commission, according to Rep. Martin.
The commission would be comprised of private sector and public officials to serve as an oversight body for state-led EV initiatives.
The commission would work to develop policy recommendations for the General Assembly to consider.
The commission would coordinate with higher educational systems to determine workforce needs and help train future workers for this industry.
The Georgia Electric Vehicle Future Act would promote public-private partnerships to develop forward-looking EV solutions, according to Rep. Martin.
The act aims to support the job-producing industry and strengthen Georgia’s position in EV manufacturing and innovation.
Last year, the Federal Highway Administration signed off on Georgia’s National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Plan, opening the door to millions in federal taxpayer dollars for the state.
With this approval, the Georgia Department of Transportation began planning how the state will spend roughly $130 million to pay for electric vehicle charging infrastructure over the next five years.
The state House and state Senate passed different versions of the bill.
The state Senate’s version would have allowed development authorities and community improvement districts to hold meetings and public hearings via teleconference.
The Senate did not agree to the House’s substituted version before the end of the session.
The bill will be pending when the state Senate resumes its session next year.
source_title: Georgia Could Develop Statewide Electric Vehicle Manufacturing Program - Tennessee Star

facts: The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant is located in Ellabell, Georgia, on land that was bare fields less than three years ago.
The facility represents a major investment in the future of EV and automobile manufacturing in America.
The 2026 Hyundai Ioniq9, a three-row electric SUV tested at the plant, offers up to 530 kilometers (335 miles) of driving range.
The Ioniq9 features a native NACS connector that allows owners to use Tesla Superchargers with Plug and Charge ease.
Hyundai invested US $7.6 billion in the Metaplant.
The inaugural 2025 Ioniq5 rolled off the plant's assembly line in October.
Ioniq models are fully eligible for $7,500 consumer tax credits for U.S.-built EVs with North American batteries.
The tax credits are currently on the Trump administration’s chopping block.
The factory provides Hyundai a bulwark against potential tariffs and positions the automaker ahead of many rivals.
The plant consists of 11 cavernous buildings spanning 697,000 square meters (7.5 million square feet).
It is set to become America’s largest dedicated plant for EVs and hybrids with a capacity for 500,000 Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis models per year.
Tesla’s Texas Gigafactory has a production capacity of 375,000 vehicles.
Company executives claim the Metaplant is North America’s most heavily automated factory and a showcase for AI and robotic tech.
Workers, called "Meta Pros," can park in nearly 1,900 spaces beneath solar roofs that provide up to 5 percent of the plant’s electricity.
Hyundai has a target of obtaining 100 percent of its energy from renewable sources.
The plant uses hydrogen trucks from Hyundai-owned Xcient, the world’s first commercialized hydrogen fuel-cell semis.
A fleet of 21 hydrogen trucks hauls parts from area suppliers using 400-kilometer driving ranges with zero tailpipe emissions.
Most finished vehicles are shipped by rail rather than truck to reduce fossil-fuel emissions and the carbon footprint.
Approximately 850 robots at the plant unload parts from the hydrogen trucks at the docks.
About 300 automated guided vehicles (AGVs) move around the factory without tracks, smartly avoiding human workers.
An AI-based procurement and logistics system uses AGVs to automatically allocate and ferry parts for just-in-time delivery, saving space, time, and money otherwise used for stockpiling.
Jerry Roach, senior manager of general assembly, states that the automated system delivers the right parts to the right station at the right time without relying on people to make decisions.
"Bodies in white," the building blocks of modern unibody car chassis, are welded by 475 robots in the welding shop.
Sled-like AGVs slide beneath electric Hyundais rolling off the line to autonomously grab wheels and ferry finished cars to parking areas without human drivers.
Spots, robotic quadrupeds designed by Hyundai-owned Boston Dynamics, use 360-degree vision and "athletic intelligence" to detect potential defects on car welds.
Hyundai plans to deploy Atlas humanoid robots from Boston Dynamics throughout its global factories for non-union roles.
Atlas robots are being trained to sense environments, avoid collisions, and manipulate/move parts in complex sequences.
Hyundai executives state that robots will not replace autoworkers despite their advanced capabilities.
The plant houses about 850 robots in total, including 475 in the welding shop alone.
An onsite steel stamping plant is so quiet that no ear protection is required for workers.
Robots at the stamping plant automatically stamp out roofs, fenders, and hoods and store them in soaring racks overhead.
Jerry Roach notes the Metaplant allowed Hyundai to design an electrified car plant from the ground up, unlike Tesla and Rivian who had to retrofit existing factories in California and Illinois.
Automation is acknowledged as inevitable for replacing some human workers, but it also frees humans from heavy lifting and repetitive tasks.
Collaborative robots install bulky doors on the assembly line without physical safety separation, performing the task perfectly and consistently.
Jerry Roach emphasizes that automation frees people to focus on precision tasks requiring dexterity, vision, intelligence, and skill, stating "I want my people doing craftsmanship."
The plant currently employs 1,340 Meta Pros with an annual average pay of $58,100.
This average pay is 25 percent higher than the average in Bryan County, Georgia.
Hyundai's annual local payroll has already reached $497 million.
The company foresees eventually creating 8,500 jobs on site and another 7,000 indirect jobs for local suppliers and businesses.
Hyundai currently sources battery cells from Georgia and SK On, with some Ioniq5 batteries imported from Hungary.
The Metaplant campus includes the HL-GA battery company, a $4 billion joint operation with LG Energy Solutions.
HL-GA plans to produce nickel-cobalt-magnesium cells beginning next year, with packs assembled on site by Hyundai's Mobis subsidiary.
Hyundai is on track to open a second $5 billion battery plant in Georgia as a joint operation with SK On.
These projects are part of Hyundai's planned $21 billion in U.S. investment between now and 2028.
This planned investment exceeds the $20 billion Hyundai has invested since entering the U.S. market in 1986.
source_title: Hyundai Metaplant: Cutting-Edge EV Factory - IEEE Spectrum

facts: Rivian selected Clayco to construct its Georgia plant.
Rivian is setting out to create a 1,800-acre complex.
The complex aims to advance sustainable transportation.
Efficiency and sustainability are key tenants of the project.
source_title: Rivian selects Clayco to construct its Georgia plant - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: Kia Corp. will invest $200 million in its Georgia factory to begin producing an electric-powered SUV.
The company said it would hire an additional 200 workers to begin producing its large EV9 vehicle in West Point by early 2024.
The company said it would expand overall production at the plant near the Alabama state line southwest of Atlanta.
Kia says the plant currently produces 340,000 vehicles a year, including Telluride, Sorrento and Sportage SUVs and K5 sedans.
Kia has 3,000 employees at the plant.
Spokesperson Patrick Sands said the EV9 would be built on the same assembly line as the other vehicles, and Kia was investing to assemble electric powertrains.
Hyundai Motor Group, Kia’s parent, is pushing hard into electric vehicles.
Hyundai is sprinting to complete a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
The company also invested $300 million in its Montgomery, Alabama plant to begin making electric vehicles there, starting with the Genesis Electrified GV70 SUV.
Hyundai hired additional workers for that Alabama investment.
Kia leaders said they hoped the EV9, with three rows of seats, would do for company sales what the large Telluride did for the brand once it began rolling off the assembly line in West Point in 2019.
Kia began delivering EV9s to customers in South Korea last month.
Sean Yoon, President and CEO of Kia Motors America, said in a statement: “Like Telluride, EV9 has the potential to be another change catalyst for Kia.”
Sean Yoon stated: “This will be the most innovative vehicle that we have ever built and will be a standout in the EV market and on the road.”
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp and Hyundai complain that federal electric vehicle tax credits are unfair to the South Korean manufacturer because its vehicles are only eligible if they are leased.

The domestic content standards of the Inflation Reduction Act make tax incentives on electric vehicle purchases available only when the vehicle, the battery, and key raw materials in the battery are all made in the United States.
Hyundai has announced it will partner in a battery factory in Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.

Georgia has been a top beneficiary of a nationwide electric vehicle investment boom, with more than 40 electric vehicle-related projects since 2020 pledging $22.9 billion of investment and 28,600 jobs in the state.
Kemp has said he and other Georgia officials should get credit for the boom, while Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff has argued President Joe Biden’s policies are driving it.
Georgia will pay to train workers for the Kia expansion.
Production workers at the plant start with a salary of $38,000 a year, with pay raises and extra pay for working evening shifts.
Kia could qualify for $1.75 million in state income tax credits, at $1,750 per job over five years, as long as workers make at least $31,300 a year.
The company could qualify for other incentives, including property tax breaks from West Point and Troup County.
Kia got more than $450 million in incentives for its plant in West Point, which opened in 2010.
West Point’s financial statements show that in 2019, Kia, its affiliates and suppliers were receiving $2.8 million in property tax breaks from the city alone.
source_title: Kia to invest $200M in Georgia plant to begin building electric SUV there, hiring 200 | AP News

facts: Georgia can keep spending $135 million in federal funds allocated by the Biden administration for electric vehicle charging infrastructure.
The funded infrastructure includes sections of Interstates 16 and 95 in Coastal Georgia.
An edited plan for the funding was approved this month.
The project to install EV charging points on unserved Georgia interstate and highway corridors has survived largely intact.
State officials deleted language that referred to equity and safety from the plan.
President Donald Trump’s Department of Transportation halted funding nationwide for the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure program earlier this year.
The federal agency demanded new state plans that omit any emphasis on serving rural, underserved or disadvantaged communities.
The federal agency also removed requirements that states address emergency evacuation needs, small business participation and labor and safety standards.
Sixteen states and Washington D.C. sued, arguing a president can’t legally freeze funding approved by Congress.
A federal judge dealt Trump a setback regarding the lawsuit, though the case is ongoing.
Georgia did not join the suit against the federal government.
Georgia was among the states that submitted revised plans to the federal agency.
GDOT did not respond to numerous requests for comment on this program.
In 2021, the U.S. Congress passed a $5 billion program with bipartisan support for the goal of easing long-distance trips in electric vehicles.

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp, a Republican, praised the program in 2022, saying the state’s Department of Transportation would play a critical role in building infrastructure to match rapidly developing EV manufacturing capacity around the state.
GDOT’s revised plan leaves out former statements on equity and workforce considerations.

The deleted equity statement read: “GDOT recognizes the impacts new transportation infrastructure and related energy systems may have on rural and historically disadvantaged communities and is committed to implementing an EV program that is convenient, reliable, affordable, and equitable for all users.”
The new plan also leaves out chapters of technical detail about existing road conditions.
What remains emphasizes the state’s second round of federal project spending on Interstate 95 in Liberty or McIntosh counties and on Interstate 16 in Bryan and Chatham counties.
Georgia awarded its first funds last year to help pay for new EV charging stations in Brunswick, Dublin, Byron, Metter and Tifton.
Love’s Travel Center in Brunswick is getting $650,000 to help pay for an EV charging station.
The Love's funding is matched by $435,000 in company money.
Contracting and design for the Love's station are underway.
The four-charger station at Love's is likely to open in 2026.
source_title: Feds OK updated Georgia plan for electric vehicle charging stations - The Current

facts: Ascend Elements is a Massachusetts-based company.
Ascend Elements has opened a new electric vehicle battery recycling facility in East Georgia.
The facility measures 150,000 square feet.
The facility can process 30,000 tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap per year.
The processing capacity equates to roughly 70,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
The facility is the largest of its type in the nation.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O’Kronley stated the facility is critical infrastructure needed in the United States to help support the energy transition.
Mike O’Kronley noted that millions of EVs will be on the road soon and more facilities like this will be needed.
The new facility will open 185 new jobs for the area.
source_title: Nation’s Largest EV Battery Recycling Plant Opens in Georgia

facts: Georgia Tech researchers developed a new cathode chemistry for solid-state batteries called FeCl3 (iron trichloride).
The FeCl3 cathode uses abundant materials iron (Fe) and chlorine (Cl), avoiding the use of nickel and cobalt.
Initial tests showed the FeCl3 cathode performed as well or better than other more expensive cathodes.
The new cathode demonstrated a higher operational voltage compared to LFP batteries currently flooding the market.
Lead researcher and associate professor Chen stated the technology could make EVs much cheaper than internal combustion cars.
Chen noted the cathode provides a promising form of large-scale energy storage that enhances electrical grid resilience.
The research group claims their cathode would greatly improve the sustainability and supply chain stability of the EV market.
Researchers estimate the technology may need less than five years before reaching commercial viability in electric vehicles.
Chen stated the team's immediate goal is to make the materials as perfect as possible in the lab and understand the underlying functioning mechanisms.
The team expressed openness to opportunities to scale up the technology and push it toward commercial applications.
The research group's findings were published in the paper “Low-cost iron trichloride cathode for all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries” in Nature Sustainability.
source_title: Georgia Tech reveals iron-chloride cathode for solid-state batteries - electrive.com

facts: Anovion Technologies selected Decatur County in Southwest Georgia as the location of its first large-scale expansion of manufacturing capacity for production of its premium synthetic graphite anode materials.
The new facility will be 1,500,000 square feet.
The facility is expected to initially produce 40,000 metric tonnes per annum of synthetic graphite anode material for lithium-ion batteries once fully operational.
Anovion announced an $800 million initial investment for the new Georgia manufacturing facility.
The project will create over 400 high-paying, quality jobs for the surrounding region.

In October 2022, Anovion was awarded a grant totaling $117 million from the U.S. Department of Energy under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law to help secure the domestic battery manufacturing supply chain.

Anovion chose Decatur County based on its proximity to existing and planned low- and carbon-free energy sources for power, short supply chains, access to existing rail infrastructure, a highly valuable skilled workforce, and a business-friendly environment.
Eric Stopka, Chief Executive Officer of Anovion, stated the company conducted a thorough search to identify a location that addressed its energy, transportation, logistics, and human capital needs.
Eric Stopka noted that proximity to other battery and EV manufacturing plants in Southwest Georgia will allow Anovion to further reduce its carbon footprint.
Governor Brian Kemp welcomed Anovion to Georgia, calling it the No. 1 state for business that is fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that job creators are recognizing that every corner of the state has the resources and workforce needed for success.
Bainbridge Mayor Edward Reynolds welcomed Anovion to Bainbridge, noting the $800 million investment and creation of over 400 high-paying, quality jobs as a huge win for the community.
Chip Dunn, Executive Chairman of Anovion, stated the company's long-term strategic vision is to focus on growth and innovation while positioning Anovion as a leader in the U.S. energy transition.
The new facility will rely on the region's rapidly decarbonizing energy mix to power its manufacturing process.

Anovion will introduce proprietary furnace technologies at the Georgia facility that substantially reduce greenhouse gas emissions and lay out a clear roadmap for achieving a carbon-neutral footprint over time.

Chip Dunn emphasized continued research and development in electrochemistry, technology improvements in production processes, and ambitious climate tech initiatives to mitigate greenhouse gas emissions.
Anovion is expanding its Anovion Center of Excellence and its hydro-powered sister manufacturing facility near Niagara Falls, NY.
The Niagara Falls facility is one of the only qualified U.S. sources of battery-grade synthetic graphite that is commercially shipping product today.
Anovion Technologies is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois, and is a U.S.-owned and U.S.-based advanced materials business.
Anovion currently operates an existing footprint near Niagara Falls, NY and Clarksburg, WV.
Anovion began commercial production of premium, cleaner anode-grade synthetic graphite in early 2021.
Anovion is among a limited number of graphite anode producers in America to have successfully gained qualification for EV applications.

In 2022, Anovion was awarded the $117 million DOE grant to rapidly scale production capacity with the construction of a large-scale factory in the southeastern U.S. and investment in its relocated Anovion Center of Excellence in Sanborn, NY.
Anovion is commencing a multi-year expansion program of 150,000 metric tonnes of annual capacity for anode-grade synthetic graphite.
source_title: Anovion Technologies Announces Plans for New Manufacturing Facility in Georgia

facts: Solid-state batteries are less flammable and can hold more energy than lithium-ion batteries but often require intense pressure to function.
Georgia Tech researchers have designed a new metal for solid-state batteries that enables operation at lower pressures.

Matthew McDowell is a professor and Carter N. Paden Jr. Distinguished Chair in the George W. Woodruff School of Mechanical Engineering and the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Georgia Tech.
Combining lithium with softer sodium metal results in improved performance and novel behavior for solid-state batteries.
Research findings were published in the paper “Interface Morphogenesis with a Deformable Secondary Phase in Solid-State Lithium Batteries” in Science on June 5, 2025.
Lithium-ion batteries use a flammable liquid electrolyte, while solid-state batteries utilize a less flammable solid material.
During operation, lithium metal changes shape and can lose contact with the solid electrolyte, which degrades battery performance.
High pressure applied via metal plates is commonly used to maintain contact but makes batteries heavy and bulky, with plates sometimes larger than the battery itself.
Georgia Tech research scientist Sun Geun Yoon led the team that developed a solution requiring less operating pressure.
Pairing lithium with sodium reduces required pressure because sodium is very soft.
In a controlled environment, sodium metal is soft enough that a gloved finger can leave an imprint in it.
Researchers applied the biological concept of morphogenesis to understand how the lithium and sodium combination behaves under low pressures.

McDowell’s research group has been applying morphogenesis to battery materials as part of a project funded by the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) in collaboration with several other universities.
The battery represents among the first viable demonstrations of using morphogenesis, with sodium deforming readily at the low pressures needed for solid-state batteries to function.
Potential applications include longer-lasting phone batteries and electric vehicles capable of driving 500 miles between charges.
McDowell and his team have filed for a patent for this battery system.
Solid-state batteries still require further development before commercial use but could eventually compete with lithium-ion technology.
McDowell’s lab continues to experiment with other materials to further improve solid-state battery performance.
The research project received funding from the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA).
The publication is dated June 5, 2025, and originates from Atlanta, GA.
source_title: A New Metal Design for Solid-State Batteries | Research

facts: Industrial SalesLeads announced the July 2022 results for new planned capital project spending report for the Industrial Manufacturing industry.
The firm tracks North American planned industrial capital project activity, including facility expansions, new plant construction and significant equipment modernization projects.
Research confirms 156 new projects with Texas leading the top locations for July.
Manufacturing/Production Facilities accounted for 139 New Projects.
Distribution and Industrial Warehouse accounted for 71 New Projects.
New Construction accounted for 51 New Projects.
Expansion accounted for 58 New Projects.
Renovations/Equipment Upgrades accounted for 59 New Projects.
Plant Closings accounted for 11 New Projects.
Texas led the top 10 states by project location with 13 projects.
North Carolina ranked second with 11 projects.
Florida, Georgia, and Indiana each had 9 projects.
New York and Ohio each had 8 projects.
California had 6 projects.
Illinois and Michigan each had 5 projects.
During the month of July, the research team identified 18 new Industrial Manufacturing facility construction projects with an estimated value of $100 million or more.

The largest project is owned by Samsung Electronics America, Inc., who is planning to invest $192 billion for the construction of 11 semiconductor manufacturing facilities in the Austin, TX and Taylor, TX area.
The Samsung project is currently in the early design phase.
A semiconductor company is planning to invest $5 billion for the construction of a manufacturing facility in Chatham County, NC, and is currently seeking approval for the project.
An electronics mfr. is planning to invest $4 billion for the construction of an EV battery manufacturing facility in De Soto, KS, and is currently seeking approval for the project.
A battery mfr. is planning to invest $1.5 billion for the construction of a manufacturing facility in Kingston, ON.
Construction for the Kingston, ON facility is expected to start in 2023, with completion slated for late 2025.
A pharmaceutical company is planning to invest $470 million for a 260,000 sf expansion, renovation, and equipment upgrades at their processing and laboratory facility in Pearl River, NY.
The Pearl River, NY project is currently seeking approval, with completion slated for early 2026.
A specialty copper foil mfr. is planning to invest $430 million for the construction of a manufacturing and office facility in Augusta, GA.
Construction for the Augusta, GA facility will occur in three phases starting in early Fall 2022.
Completion for the Augusta, GA facility is slated for late 2024.
A steel company is planning to invest $265 million for the construction of a manufacturing facility in Sulphur Springs, TX, and is currently seeking approval for the project.
A steel company is planning to invest $150 million for the expansion of their manufacturing facilities in Keetac, MN or Minntac, MN, and is currently seeking approval for the project.
A steel tube mfr. is planning to invest $110 million for the construction of a manufacturing, warehouse, and office facility at 1923 Airport Road in Columbus, MS.
Construction for the Columbus, MS facility is expected to start in Fall 2022, with completion for Summer 2023.

A specialty roofing mfr. is planning to invest $100 million for the construction of a 450,000 sf manufacturing, warehouse, research, and office facility in Georgetown, TX, and is currently seeking approval for the project.
A medical equipment mfr. is planning to invest $90 million for the construction of a 270,000 sf manufacturing facility in North Charleston, SC, and is currently seeking approval for the project.
Completion for the North Charleston, SC facility is slated for 2024.
SalesLeads, Inc. has been based in Jacksonville, FL since 1959.
The company delivers industrial capital project intelligence and prospecting services for sales and marketing teams to ensure a predictable and scalable pipeline.

The company's Industrial Market Intelligence (IMI) identifies timely insights on companies planning significant capital investments such as new construction, expansion, relocation, equipment modernization and plant closings in industrial facilities.

facts: Gov. Brian Kemp and Hyundai Motor Group announced plans to build the first electric vehicle and battery manufacturing plant in Georgia, located in Bryan County.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that this project represents the largest economic deal in Georgia's history.
Hyundai Executive Chairman Euisun Chung confirmed the company will invest $5.54 billion to open the facility at the Bryan County Megasite.
The 2,923-acre Bryan County Megasite was purchased by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority and the state of Georgia less than a year prior to the announcement.
The project is expected to create 8,100 new jobs in Coastal Georgia.
Gov. Brian Kemp noted the economic benefits will extend beyond Bryan County to include Effingham, Bulloch, and Chatham counties, as well as the broader region and state.
Pat Wilson, commissioner of Georgia’s Department of Economic Development, described Hyundai as the fastest-growing automotive company in the world based on market share.
Wilson stated that partnering with Hyundai puts Georgia at the forefront of a significant industry transformation.
Hyundai selected Georgia due to its existing network of subsidiaries and suppliers, as well as site resources designed to increase shipping speeds.
Hyundai expects construction on the new facility to begin in January 2023.
The plant is scheduled to become operational in the first half of 2025.
source_title: Hyundai bringing $5 billion electric vehicle and battery manufacturing plant to Bryan Co.

entity_name: Hyundai-LG EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture Facility
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution stated they will construct a USD 4.3 billion electric vehicle battery plant in the United States as part of a move to take benefit of tax credits.
Building of the factory in Bryan County, Georgia will commence in the second half of 2023.
Battery manufacturing at the facility will begin at the end of 2025 at the earliest.
The plant will have a yearly production capacity of 30 gigawatt-hours.
The 30 GWh capacity is enough for 300,000 EVs.
Hyundai Motor Group's corporate target is to attain EV sales of 2 million a year by 2030.
Hyundai Motor Group is the world’s third-greatest auto manufacturer by vehicle sales.
The new facility will assist in making a stable supply of batteries and permit Hyundai to respond quickly to the rising EV demand in the U.S. market.
LGES and Hyundai Motor Group will each own 50% of the joint venture.
Hyundai Motor Group includes Hyundai Motor, Kia, and auto parts manufacturer Hyundai Mobis.
Youngsoo Kwon is the CEO of LG Energy Solution.
Youngsoo Kwon stated that two strong leaders in the auto and battery industries have joined hands to drive the EV transition in America.
Hyundai Motor Group is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea.
LG Energy Solution Ltd. is a battery company based in Seoul, South Korea.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy to Construct USD 4.3 Billion EV Battery Plant in USA - America | Energetica India Magazine

facts: A man died Tuesday morning at an electric vehicle battery plant under construction when a load fell off a forklift.
The fatal accident occurred at the HL-GA Battery Co. site located at the Bryan County MegaSite in Ellabell, approximately 25 miles from downtown Savannah.
The plant is scheduled to start producing EV batteries for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis vehicles in early 2026.
Deputies responded to the site around 11 a.m. on Tuesday.
As the truck was being unloaded, the load came loose from the forklift and fell.
The victim was an employee of a subcontractor at the plant site who was struck and killed because he could not get out of the way in time.
Law enforcement officials had not yet identified the victim as of Tuesday, as authorities were contacting his family.
Bryan County officials are continuing to investigate the fatal accident, with no foul play suspected.
The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) has opened an investigation into the Tuesday death.
OSHA is also investigating another fatal forklift accident at the same site that occurred on March 21.
HL-GA Battery Co. issued a statement expressing that it is deeply saddened by the death of a subcontractor employee at its Bryan County construction site on May 20, 2025.
The company extended condolences to the subcontractor’s family, friends, and colleagues.
HL-GA Battery Co. stated that the health and safety of its larger team is its highest priority and that it is committed to following all Occupational Industrial Safety and Health Policies.

The company said it is fully supporting a thorough investigation with its subcontractor and relevant authorities to determine the root cause of the incident and help prevent such accidents in the future.
HL-GA Battery Co. had no further comment as the investigation remains ongoing.
Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution have committed more than $4.3 billion to build the EV battery plant.
source_title: Bryan County HL-GA Battery Company worker killed in forklift accident - WSB-TV Channel 2 - Atlanta

facts: The new Hyundai Mobility Training Center is located in Ellabelle, GA.
The facility will support the automaker’s assembly plant.
The 89,000-square-foot facility will teach workers how to build next-generation electric vehicles.
The state-of-the-art center is operated by the state’s workforce development program and is part of the Technical College System of Georgia.
At full capacity, it will be able to train up to 824 individuals at a time.
source_title: EV Training Center Supports New Hyundai Plant | ASSEMBLY

facts: Georgia has emerged as a burgeoning hub for electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing, fueled by major investments from industry giants like Hyundai and LG Energy Solution.
Recent events involving immigration authorities and worker detentions at a key battery plant construction site have brought this vision under scrutiny.
The state has attracted substantial foreign direct investment, including a $7.6 billion electric battery complex planned by HL-GA Battery Co., a joint venture between Hyundai and LG.
The HL-GA Battery Co. facility is located near Savannah in Ellabell, GA.
The Ellabell facility is expected to be operational next year.
The Ellabell facility aims to employ up to 8,500 people eventually.
Hyundai's footprint in Georgia includes a $5 billion battery joint venture in Cartersville.
Hyundai also operates a steel mill in Donaldsonville, Louisiana.
Hyundai is expanding assembly plants in Alabama and Georgia alongside robotics hubs.
These ventures are projected to fuel thousands of jobs across the region.
Immigration authorities detained around 475 workers at the Ellabell battery plant site during a recent raid.
The detention included more than 300 South Korean nationals.
The governor of Georgia has historically taken a firm stance on immigration enforcement, including past statements about personally rounding up unauthorized immigrants.
Balancing tough immigration policy with attracting and retaining global business investment has proven tricky for the state.
South Korean officials and public opinion are questioning the ripple effects of the enforcement action on the relationship between Hyundai, its partners, and the United States.
Hyundai is preparing to invest $26 billion in the US between 2025 and 2028.
These investments drive local economies and contribute to key supply chains for electric vehicles, batteries, and related technologies.
The governor's office reinforced that all businesses must comply with both state and federal laws.
Responses to the raid have been measured, reflecting a delicate balance between immigration control and economic development.
Georgia’s Department of Economic Development emphasized the expectation that every company operates lawfully, with no direct state incentives benefiting subcontractors employing the detained workers.

Governor Kemp is focused on branding Georgia as the “electric mobility capital of America” while facing bipartisan debates on green energy incentives and economic benefits derived from large-scale investments.
Georgia has seen a surge in green technology projects valued over $20 billion, promising 25,000+ jobs by the end of 2024.
Federal advantages, such as the Inflation Reduction Act, have helped turbocharge this growth despite shifting political landscapes around EV incentives.
Multiple billion-dollar investments from Hyundai, LG, and other companies are expanding into battery manufacturing, vehicle assembly, and robotics, creating thousands of jobs in multiple states.
Hyundai is also expanding plants and manufacturing EVs in Montgomery, AL and West Point, GA, though investment and employment figures are undisclosed.
Efficient logistics are critical for keeping inbound raw materials to outbound EVs and batteries on track as the automotive and battery sectors expand.
Managing freight flows, shipments of bulky and heavy goods, and just-in-time delivery schedules is crucial to maintaining competitiveness.
Growing volumes of industry-grade cargo are moving through ports like Savannah and inland rail and road networks.
Integrated multimodal transport networks are required to address complex inbound supply chains for raw materials, ensuring timely material arrivals and reducing production delays.
Specialized freight forwarding and containerization are needed to maintain safety and efficiency in transporting heavy and bulky battery shipments.
Flexible cargo and relocation services are necessary to support workforce mobility and local shipments of staff and equipment.
Technology-enabled customs and clearance support is required for regulatory compliance with cross-border freight to minimize shipment bottlenecks.

CTA to book rides and get offers
CTA to start planning deliveries and secure cargo

facts: Porter’s Savannah 3PL warehouse services the EV industry, taking both new and used batteries across a wide range of specifications.
Georgia is the #1 destination for new AI data centers.
Porter can stage batteries for data centers until they are ready for installation.
Porter Logistics has HAZMAT capabilities perfect for storing raw materials such as lithium carbonate, black mass, cobalt, and more, whether recycled or new.
Savannah Battery warehousing requires the utmost attention to detail, compliance, and safety.
Porter Logistics has containment for spill mitigation, PPE, and a fully trained staff on chemical regulations and safe handling procedures.
Porter Logistics is registered with US DOT & the EPA as a chemical handler.
Porter Logistics is audited by the Alliance for Chemical Distribution (ACD) bi-yearly to ensure compliant chemical warehouse standards.
The team takes fire suppression, spill mitigation, and PPE standards extremely seriously.
The Savannah battery warehouse facility has a mix of racked and bulk space.
The facility is fully networked with high performance networking technology.
Heavy duty forklifts, clamp trucks, and narrow aisle equipment are provided to clients.
Porter maintains numerous certifications/affiliations and opens itself to governmental & 3rd party auditors to ensure compliance with the highest standards in their areas of work.
Clients can efficiently track inventory using the Porter Logistics dashboard and WMS system.
Clients may bring their own Savannah fulfillment technology if desired.
Porter manages storage and logistics for batteries used in electric vehicles (EVs), data centers, and various industrial applications.
The company services manufacturers, retailers, brands, and direct sellers from a diverse set of industries.

Beyond storage, Porter provides transloading, cross-docking, B2B order fulfillment, B2C eCommerce fulfillment, import and export services, climate-controlled warehousing, and specialized HAZMAT warehousing.
Advanced Warehouse Management Systems (WMS) integrate seamlessly with leading Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) management providers.
The WMS offers pre-built integrations with over 50 shopping carts and marketplaces.
This integration ensures real-time inventory tracking, compliance documentation management, and efficient order processing.
Pricing for Savannah battery storage varies based on contract length and volume.

facts: Southeastern Georgia has landed another supplier for the state’s fast-growing electric mobility industry.

South Korea-based Hanon Systems, a global provider of automotive thermal and energy management solutions for conventional and electric vehicles, will invest more than $40 million in a manufacturing plant in Bulloch County.
The project is expected to create 160 jobs.
Gov. Brian said Tuesday from Israel, where he is leading a trade mission: “Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow as we proudly welcome Hanon Systems to Georgia.”
Gov. Brian stated: “As our state furthers its position as the EV capital of America, new jobs and opportunities like these are coming.”

Billy Allen, chairman of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, said Hanon will work closely with Hyundai Motor Group, which is building a huge EV manufacturing plant in Bryan County west of Savannah.
The Hyundai Motor Group investment is valued at $5.5 billion.
The Hanon Systems plant is expected to begin operations in May of next year.
Min Sung, Hanon’s president and CEO, said: “We are pleased to be contributing to the local economy of Bulloch County through job creation.”
Min Sung stated: “Today’s announcement aligns with our strategy to support the industry shift toward electrification while growing our North America footprint.”
The Hyundai plant has been attracting EV suppliers to Bulloch County and other areas near Savannah since breaking ground on the project last October.
Ecoplastic Corp announced plans in January to build an automotive parts plant in Bulloch that will create 456 jobs.
Auto parts manufacturer Joon Georgia unveiled plans last November for a new manufacturing plant in Bulloch that will create 630 jobs.

Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, said: “Bulloch County, as part of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, has been a part of this growing success story from the beginning.”

The state agency’s Global Commerce team worked on the Hanon Systems project with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Power, the Georgia Ports Authority and the Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program.
source_title: Bulloch County draws second EV supplier |

entity_name: Hyundai-SK On Georgia EV Battery Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai-SK On Georgia EV Battery Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and battery maker SK On finalized plans for a $5 billion EV battery manufacturing facility in Bartow County, Georgia.
The boards of Hyundai Motor Company, Kia Corporation and Hyundai Mobis approved the joint venture.
Hyundai and its battery partner will each take a 50% stake in the investment to supply the automaker’s U.S. production facilities.
The facility is expected to start making battery cells by the second half of 2025.
The battery cells will support production of 300,000 all-electric Hyundai, Kia and Genesis vehicles yearly.
Hyundai is working to secure a stable supply of batteries as it builds out its EV production capabilities in the U.S.
The automaker signed a memorandum of understanding for an EV production facility with SK On in November.
This signing occurred the month after Hyundai broke ground in Savannah, Georgia on its first dedicated EV campus in the U.S.
Heung-soo Kim, the automaker’s head of corporate future growth planning and EV divisions, said in November that the MOU will allow Hyundai to accelerate production in the North American EV market.

Heung-soo Kim, who is also executive vice president, stated that the stable supply of EV batteries from SK On will enable Hyundai to contribute to emissions reduction and meet climate goals in the market.
The Bartow County facility is strategically located within a couple hundred miles of the Hyundai operations it is intended to supply.
The supplied Hyundai operations include Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama, Kia Georgia and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp called Hyundai Motor Group and SK On "valued partners and key players in our state’s ever-growing automotive industry" in a Dec. 8 statement.
The partners selected Bartow County for the facility on Dec. 8.
SK On subsidiary SK Battery America recently exceeded its hiring goal of 2,600 workers for two Georgia facilities.
These two Georgia facilities will manufacture batteries for Ford and Volkswagen’s EVs.
source_title: Hyundai and SK On plan $5B Georgia EV battery plant | Manufacturing Dive

facts: Aspen Aerogels would have been one of several EV parts suppliers near the Hyundai Metaplant in Bryan County.
President & CEO Donald Young stated on Wednesday's earnings call: "We are taking decisive actions to navigate an evolving environment."
Donald Young explained that the approach allows them to create capacity in a modular fashion that can more closely anticipate the demand curve, and to do so with minimal capital.
Instead of focusing on a plant in Southeast Georgia, Aspen Aerogels is targeting upgrades on its already existing plant in East Providence, Rhode Island.
The Bulloch County Development Authority says they are disappointed with the news.

Bulloch County Development Authority CEO Benjy Thompson stated: "Businesses often reevaluated their strategies. The Development Authority will continue to pursue long-term projects that bring good jobs, smart investment, and economic opportunities to the citizens of Bulloch County."
Hyundai told WJCL 22 that Aspen Aerogels is not a supplier of HMGMA, and will not affect production.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp is in Munich for an economic development mission, and is unable to provide a statement at this time.
In 2022, Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced that an electric vehicle parts supplier was coming to Bulloch County and, once operational, would employ more than 250 people.
Nearly three years later to the day, those plans have changed.

Aspen Aerogels, recognized as a technology leader in aerogel-based sustainability and electrification solutions, intended to invest at least $325 million at its site at the Southern Gateway Commerce Park near Statesboro.
Aspen Aerogels announced Wednesday that it is halting construction on the 500,000-square-foot manufacturing facility on a 90-acre parcel.
The company will be assessing options to derive value from the assets in Statesboro, including relocating some equipment to upgrade and expand its existing plant (in Rhode Island).
Aspen Aerogels previously announced back in 2015 plans to bring 100 jobs and a $70 million investment to the same area.
source_title: Georgia: EV parts supplier halts $325M project, 250 jobs lost - WJCL

facts: Bulloch County Board of Commissioners contracted to buy two acres of land on Old Highway 46 as the proposed site for one of four large wells intended to supply water to Hyundai Motor Group’s Meta Plant America.
Bulloch County is expected to own two wells and Bryan County will own the other two wells to furnish water to the $5.54 billion electric vehicle and battery manufacturing complex.
The Hyundai plant is projected to open in the first half of 2025 and eventually employ 8,100 people.
The plant is under construction in northern Bryan County a little over five miles from the Bulloch County line.
Bulloch is expected to host all four wells, including the two that Bryan County will own.

Bulloch County Manager Tom Couch stated this is still an “unwritten agreement in principle,” part of arrangements by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, its four member counties (Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham and Effingham), and Georgia’s state government to lure Hyundai.

Bulloch commissioners voted 6-0 on July 5 to approve a purchase contract offering current owners Randall T. Neal, Janet N. Reid and Connie N. Robinson exactly $60,000 for the two-acre site in southeastern Bulloch County.
The sale is not final, but the contract predicts that the county and current owners will close on or before Sept. 1 if conditions are met.
Tom Couch confirmed in an email that the land is intended for one of the well sites serving Hyundai and noted the two acres would not allow co-locating other facilities.

County officials have suggested at least one well site might host a Bulloch County Fire Department, EMS, or Sheriff’s Office substation, or a park, but Couch said 3-5 acres or more would be ideal for that purpose.
This is the first identified site for one of the two wells Bulloch will own to provide water across the county line to the Hyundai plant.

Couch and other staff proposed these wells will supply the beginnings of Bulloch’s first county-operated water system, serving future residential subdivisions, commercial businesses, and Hyundai suppliers in southeastern Bulloch County.

A development strategy using county water and sewer systems to concentrate development was codified in an amendment to the Smart Bulloch 2040 Comprehensive Plan and Future Development Map approved by commissioners in early June.
The plan stakes out a new “suburban neighborhood” character area and narrower “suburban corridors” in the southern end of Southeast Bulloch.
A rewrite of zoning regulations created a new R-8 single-family residential classification allowed only for subdivisions served by public water and sewer systems in these areas.

In June, commissioners approved rezoning to “HI” heavy industrial for a Hyundai supplier to build an injection-molded plastic parts factory on a 22-acre parcel on Georgia Highway 119 South in southeastern Bulloch.
The project summary stated the supplier plant could use 50,000 gallons of water per day and that the county anticipates supplying it eventually with its water system.

Before closing, the county must pay for a boundary survey and sellers must give access for an environmental audit if chosen by the county, with a 30-day notice to correct problems; similar conditions apply to title encumbrances.
Bryan County communications manager Matthew Kent said he also understands Bryan will own two wells within Bulloch County, with Bulloch owning the other two.
Couch commented that Bryan County officials may be prospecting for their own well sites.

The regulatory reason for Bryan’s wells in Bulloch is coastal saltwater intrusions into the Floridan aquifer, which has led the Georgia Environmental Protection Division to include Bryan County in a “yellow zone” with restrictions on new wells and goals to reduce groundwater withdrawals.
Unrelated to Hyundai, the city of Pembroke in northern Bryan County already owns a well in southern Bulloch under an intergovernmental agreement first signed in 2017.
The four wells owned by Bulloch and Bryan counties are expected to supply 1.5 million gallons of water per day to the Hyundai plant.
Last summer, Couch and staff projected $22.6 million in infrastructure spending to provide water and sewer service to future homes in southeastern Bulloch as well as the Hyundai plant.
County officials earmarked federal American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) money for about half of these costs.
Hyundai and other customers will pay for the water they use, and Couch suggested Bryan County would pay a “host fee” for its wells in Bulloch.
source_title: Bulloch County deal will supply water to Hyundai plant - Statesboro Herald

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hanon Systems will create 160 new jobs and invest more than $40 million in a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
Hanon Systems is a global provider of automotive thermal and energy management solutions.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow as the state welcomes Hanon Systems.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that Georgia furthers its position as the EV capital of America, with new jobs and opportunities coming to Georgians statewide.
Min Sung is President and Chief Executive Officer of Hanon Systems.
Min Sung stated that Hanon Systems is pleased to contribute to the local economy of Bulloch County through job creation.
Min Sung thanked the State of Georgia and the cooperation between its agencies for guiding the project.
Min Sung said the announcement aligns with Hanon Systems' strategy to support the industry shift toward electrification while growing its North America footprint.
Hanon Systems will build a new facility at Gateway Regional Industrial Park in Bulloch County.
The new facility will assemble automotive thermal solutions.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in May 2024.
Billy Allen is Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County.
Billy Allen stated that Statesboro-Bulloch County is honored by Hanon Systems' decision to locate its new facility there.
Billy Allen expressed excitement to welcome another automotive supplier to the community, particularly one working closely with the Hyundai Metaplant and the electric vehicle industry.
Billy Allen cited the decision as an example of the resources offered by the community and region to help companies succeed.
Griff Lynch is Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority (GPA).
Griff Lynch stated that the announcement further solidifies Georgia’s position as a growing hub for auto manufacturing.
Griff Lynch noted that GPA supports the operations of Hyundai Motor Group and its suppliers to deliver jobs and opportunity for Georgia residents.
Alyce Thornhill is Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).
Alyce Thornhill represented the GDEcD Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was executed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
Pat Wilson is GDEcD Commissioner.

Pat Wilson stated that suppliers like Hanon Systems successfully support larger industry ecosystems through partnership-driven solutions ranging from workforce training to logistics systems efficiencies.
Pat Wilson noted that Bulloch County is part of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority and has been part of its growing success story from the beginning.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, over 40 EV-related projects have announced more than $22.7 billion in investment and 28,400 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Hanon Systems was formed in 1986.
Hanon Systems is a leading supplier of automotive thermal and energy management solutions for electrified and conventional vehicles.
Hanon Systems' offerings include heating ventilation and air conditioning, powertrain cooling, compressor, fluid transport, and electronics and fluid pressure solutions.
source_title: Automotive Supplier Hanon Systems to Create 160 New Jobs in Bulloch County - AllOnGeorgia

facts: SK Battery America (SKBA) will open a regional IT hub facility in Roswell.
The new facility will create 200 high-tech jobs.
SK Battery America is investing approximately $19 million over the next few years for the Roswell IT center.
Governor Brian Kemp stated Georgia is the No. 1 state for business.
Governor Brian Kemp noted the company brings jobs of the future that benefit hardworking Georgians in manufacturing and tap into tech talent from top-ranking universities.
SK Battery America is investing nearly $2.6 billion in two lithium-ion battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County.
The Jackson County facilities will supply electric vehicles such as the Ford F-150 Lightning and Volkswagen ID.4.
The Roswell IT facility is one of SK Group’s three major investments in the State of Georgia.
SK Group was founded in 1976 and is the second-largest conglomerate in South Korea.
Jason Choi, Head of Information Technology at SK Battery America, stated the decision to open the IT regional center in Roswell aims to strengthen SK’s position as a leader in the battery industry.
The IT center will help build and operate an advanced manufacturing system in accordance with further expansion of battery production bases in the United States.
Jason Choi expressed excitement to expand SK Battery America's presence and engage with the community and surrounding region.
The new IT center will serve as an integrated IT management center for SKBA’s battery manufacturing facilities across the United States, including its Georgia facilities.
The Roswell facility is located at 1110 Sanctuary Parkway in Roswell.
Operations at the new IT center are expected to begin in January.
The company will be hiring IT experts for battery manufacturing execution system development and operations as well as data analysts.
City of Roswell Mayor Kurt Wilson stated he looks forward to SK Battery America becoming part of the community and bringing opportunities to Roswell.

Mayor Kurt Wilson noted that since taking office a year ago, his priority has been recruiting businesses like SK Battery America that provide high-quality job opportunities for Roswell’s workforce and enhance economic strength.
Mayor Kurt Wilson called Roswell the perfect location for the IT Regional Center and expressed pride in the choice.
Fulton County Board of Commissioners Chairman Robb Pitts welcomed SK Battery America to Fulton County and noted the new job opportunities they will create for residents.
Robb Pitts described Fulton County as a destination for the best and brightest, reflecting the announcement.
Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was in partnership with the City of Roswell, Fulton County, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated SK On established SK Battery America as its U.S. production base in 2019.
Pat Wilson noted the new IT regional center is a natural result of the company’s commitment to Georgia.
Pat Wilson discussed the ripple effects of economic development, noting suppliers, support centers, and expansions create opportunities beyond the original scope, location, and industry.
Pat Wilson expressed looking forward to future opportunities to celebrate SK Battery America and SK On’s success in Georgia, and congratulated Roswell and Fulton County.
Electric mobility related projects have contributed more than $21 billion in investments to Georgia since 2020.
Electric mobility related projects have contributed more than 26,700 new jobs to Georgia since 2020.
SK Battery America’s new IT regional center will support the company’s battery production operations.
The center will build on Georgia’s existing automotive assets and developing workforce.
The center will help ensure a domestic supply chain for the electric mobility industry.
SK On is the parent company of SK Battery America.
SK On is a global leading electric vehicle battery developer, manufacturer, and solutions provider.
As SK On’s U.S. production base, SK Battery America produces safe, fast charging, and long-lasting batteries currently installed in the Ford F-150 Lightning and Volkswagen ID.4.
The company will have a combined capacity to make 22 GWh of battery cells a year.
This capacity is enough to power the equivalent of about 300,000 EVs annually.
Since 2019, SK Battery America has invested a total of $2.6 billion to build EV battery plants in Commerce, Georgia.
These investments created 2,600 high-skilled jobs.
source_title: SK Battery America to Open U.S. IT Center, Create 200 High-Tech Jobs in Roswell - AllOnGeorgia

facts: VinFast announced in March 2022 that it would open an electric vehicle assembly plant in North Carolina.
The battery manufacturing plant will be built in Chatham County.
The plant is expected to eventually create 7,500 jobs.
It is the largest economic development announcement in the state’s history.
On July 28, VinFast will launch formal construction of its planned $4 billion electric vehicle production and assembly plant.
The plant is located near the small Chatham County town of Moncure, about 30 miles southwest of Raleigh.
VinFast anticipates the plant will initially produce 150,000 vehicles a year.
The target opening for the plant is in 2025.

VinFast Auto CEO Le Thi Thu Thuy stated that when operations begin, the factory will be VinFast’s primary supplier of electric vehicles to the North American market, allowing them to optimize production and business activities.
VinFast was founded in 2017.
VinFast is a subsidiary of the Vietnamese conglomerate Vingroup.
Vingroup is owned by the wealthiest man in Southeast Asia.
Since pivoting to a fully electric car lineup in 2021, VinFast has spent billions pursuing global ambitions.
The plant is expected to cover approximately 1,800 acres.
A zoning permit application filed last week to the Chatham County Planning Department projects the site’s initial phase will include almost 3 million square feet of space.
VinFast represents a milestone for North Carolina officials who have repeatedly tried to recruit a major automaker to the state.
In recent decades, North Carolina attempted to land Volkswagen, BMW, Volvo, Mercedes, and a joint Toyota-Mazda plant, but each selected nearby southeastern states.
In March 2022, North Carolina and VinFast officials announced their partnership.
The automaker has experienced setbacks, including poor reviews for its first U.S. car and a delay in its Chatham plant timeline.
Around 350 VinFast cars have been delivered to U.S. customers, according to a company spokesperson.
By 2028, VinFast has committed to create 7,500 area jobs.
Between the state and Chatham County, the company is set up to receive incentives worth $1.25 billion if it meets its hiring and investment goals.
Construction will be divided into two phases, with Phase 1 focused on car production and Phase 2 on manufacturing electric vehicle batteries.
The project has received basic permits to begin Phase 1 construction.
On Feb. 9, the North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality issued VinFast an air quality permit.
The state has also approved multiple permits for erosion and sediment control plans.
DEQ is still reviewing VinFast’s applications related to water quality and buffers.
The company is in the process of applying for a dam safety permit for its stormwater ponds.
source_title: VinFast to break ground on NC plant, first in North America | Raleigh News & Observer

facts: Seoyon E-HWA plans to open a manufacturing facility in the Savannah area.
The facility will be located in Chatham County.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced on Wednesday that Seoyon E-HWA will invest nearly $76 million into the project.
The investment funds a 700-plus employee factory at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center near Bloomingdale.
Seoyon E-HWA specializes in interior and exterior components such as headlinings and bumpers.
The company manufactures these components for Hyundai vehicles.
Operations are scheduled to begin in October 2024.
source_title: Hyundai supplier Seoyon E-HWA to build facility in chatham county

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at its manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday amid automakers’ changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.
Friday marked the last working day for exactly 958 plant employees.
The workforce reduction was reported via a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
SK Battery America opened its $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The Commerce plant notably supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning in December.
SK Battery America and Ford had together previously invested $11.4 billion in joint battery plants in the U.S.
SK Battery America ended its joint venture with Ford in December.
SK Battery America has invested significantly in Jackson County, Georgia, in recent years.
In June 2020, SK Battery America announced plans to pour $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
At the time, Gov. Brian Kemp’s office said the Atlanta expansion would create 600 jobs.
SK Battery America and Hyundai are still jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
The state of Georgia has attracted other massive EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s own $7.6 billion factory complex.
EVs accounted for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025, much the same as a year earlier.

Automakers including Ford, General Motors, Stellantis and others across the EV supply chain have reneged on factory, investment and product plans, laid off workers, and pivoted efforts to hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles.
Under President Donald Trump, Congress has eliminated tax credits of up to $7,500 for consumers’ purchases of new or used EVs.

The administration has announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules for automakers, essentially eliminating any federal incentive for auto companies to make their vehicle fleets cleaner.
source_title: Nearly 1,000 workers laid off at Georgia battery plant as companies cancel EVs and lose incentives | Fortune

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at a manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday.
The layoffs occurred amid automakers’ changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.
Friday marked the last working day for 958 plant employees, representing about 37% of its workforce.
The layoff was reported via a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
The plant will continue to employ about 1,600 workers after the layoffs.
SK opened the $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The Korean company notably supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning in December.

The U.S. electric vehicle market is currently at a standstill amid the Trump administration steering federal support away from electrification toward laxer automotive emissions policies and broader support for oil and gas industries.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated the workforce reduction was made to align operations to market conditions.
Collier said: “SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and to building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.”
Collier added that the company is pursuing future customers, including in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
Ford scrapped the fully electric version of its pickup truck in December, opting instead for an extended-range version.
A Ford spokesperson declined to comment on supplier personnel actions regarding the layoffs.
SK and Ford previously invested $11.4 billion together in joint battery plants in the U.S.
The joint venture between SK and Ford ended in December.
SK is also a supplier to Volkswagen.
Georgia U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff (Democrat) stated: “Let’s be clear: these were battery manufacturing jobs and now they’re gone.”
Ossoff claimed: “As predicted, Trump’s war on electric vehicles is hurting Georgia’s economy. We were booming and building new plants. Now Georgians are losing their jobs.”
SK has invested significantly in Jackson County, Georgia, in recent years as automakers developed EV plans with support from the Biden administration's domestic supply chain efforts.
In June 2020, SK announced plans to invest $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
At the time of the 2020 announcement, Gov. Brian Kemp’s office said the expansion would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has attracted other massive EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s own $7.6 billion factory complex.
Few states benefited more than Georgia from Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act, which accelerated a rush of green energy projects.
By the end of 2024, Georgia announced 33 additional EV/green energy projects, the most nationwide according to environmental business group E2.
Projects in Georgia topped $20 billion in pledges, promising more than 25,000 jobs.

Qcells, a unit of South Korea’s Hanwha Solutions, resumed normal production on Friday after temporarily reducing hours and pay last year due to U.S. customs detaining imported components for solar panels.
A spokesperson for Gov. Kemp’s office expressed gratitude for the remaining workers at SK’s factory.
The statement noted Georgia’s job market remains robust with over 219,700 new jobs announced since 2019.
EV demand has not met automakers’ ambitious expectations recently, though it is still growing.
EVs accounted for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025, similar to the prior year.
Automakers are reevaluating multibillion-dollar electrification plans as financial losses mount and demand shifts.

Manufacturers including Ford, General Motors, Stellantis, and others have reneged on factory/investment/product plans, laid off workers, and pivoted some efforts to hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles.
Hybrids and more efficient gasoline-powered vehicles are becoming more palatable for mainstream buyers concerned about EV driving range and charging infrastructure availability.
Under President Donald Trump, Congress eliminated tax credits of up to $7,500 for consumers’ purchases of new or used EVs.
source_title: SK lays off nearly 1K workers at Ga. plant amid cooling automaker EV plans – The North State Journal

The facility operates on a 24/7 schedule.
The site location is less than 1 mile off I-85 at exit 147.
SK Battery America produces the world's safest, fastest charging, and longest lasting batteries for electric vehicles.

The Total Number of Charging Stations is 28.
