facts: Volkswagen AG called itself an “unintended victim” in a battle between two battery suppliers and urged the U.S. government to extend a reprieve to buy batteries key to its planned U.S. electric vehicle production.

The U.S. International Trade Commission (ITC) sided with LG Chem in a trade secrets case but permitted SK Innovation to import components for domestic production of lithium ion batteries for Ford Motor Co’s EV F-150 program for four years, and for Volkswagen of America’s electric vehicle line for two years.
VW requested its carve out be “extended to at least four years to give an adequate transition period” and expressed hope that the two suppliers will settle this dispute outside of court.
The German automaker has invested $800 million to build electric vehicles in Tennessee and is hiring hundreds of employees.
VW stated it will take all necessary steps to allow fulfillment of its commitment to provide skilled jobs to proud Tennessee workers.
Ford Chief Executive Jim Farley publicly encouraged LG Chem and SK Innovation to reach a settlement.
VW and Ford previously warned the dispute could disrupt supplies of key EV parts and cost U.S. jobs during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp called on President Joe Biden to overturn the ITC ruling, warning the long-term prospects for the $2.6 billion SK Innovation battery plant in Jackson County, Georgia would be “harmed significantly.”
The SK Innovation plant in Jackson County, Georgia will eventually build batteries for Volkswagen and Ford.
The White House and SK Innovation declined comment on the matter.
LG Energy Solution counsel Dave Callahan said the company “remains committed to negotiating a fair settlement for the theft of its trade secrets” and stated the Georgia plant will be able to operate.
Dave Callahan added that the only obstacle to resolving the matter and securing the plant’s long-term future is SK Innovation’s refusal to acknowledge its wrongdoing and make amends.
source_title: VW 'unintended victim' in battery supplier dispute, seeks four-year reprieve - The Fiji Times

facts: Korean company SK Innovation announced an investment of US$1.67 billion to build the first electric vehicle battery plant in the United States.
SK Innovation is considering plowing an additional $5 billion into the project planned for Jackson County, Georgia.
The yet-to-be-built plant would have an initial annual capacity of 9.8 gigawatt-hours.
The plant plans to eventually ramp up to 105 GWh capacity.
The eventual 105 GWh capacity will be five times the 20GWh capacity of Tesla’s Gigafactory 1 in Nevada.
Production at the SK Innovation facility is targeted for 2022.
The Georgia facility would focus solely on batteries, unlike Tesla's factory which also makes drivetrains.
It would become the largest EV battery plant in the United States.
SK Innovation currently supplies batteries to several global automakers, including Daimler and Hyundai Motor.
Two of SK Innovation's customers, Mercedes-Benz and Hyundai-Kia Motors, will need battery cells in the US, prompting the local investment.
Locating the EV battery plant in Georgia is strategic because Hyundai, Mercedes, and Honda operate assembly plants in nearby Alabama.
Mazda and Toyota have broken ground on a factory in Alabama.
Volkswagen has an assembly plant in Chattanooga, Tennessee.
SK Innovation aims to be the first mover in establishing a mine-to-battery supply chain in the United States.
There is currently no mine-to-battery supply chain in North America, including the United States and Canada.
At Albemarle’s Silver Peak Mine in Nevada, lithium is shipped to Kings Mountain in North Carolina to produce lithium hydroxide for lithium-ion batteries.
Lithium hydroxide from North Carolina is loaded on ships and sent to Asian battery manufacturers that sell to auto-makers.
Tesla has shown little interest in making deals with current (Albemarle) and future US lithium miners.
Tesla inked a deal last May with Kidman Resources, an Australian company developing a lithium processing plant in Western Australia with SQM.
Tesla plans to buy lithium hydroxide from Kidman and manufacture batteries in China for its Gigafactory.
Canadian lithium miner Nemaska Lithium will send its lithium hydroxide to Swedish battery maker Northvolt AV, which will take between 3,500 and 5,000 tonnes per year.
Nemaska accepted an investment of $78 million from Japan-based Softbank in return for a 9.9% stake.
Nemaska agreed last May to supply spodumene concentrate to Hanwa Co., Ltd. of Japan and General Lithium Corp from China.

Battery companies are securing long-term prices and supply through offtake agreements due to climbing prices of EV battery ingredients (copper, lithium, cobalt, graphite) and falling prices of finished batteries.
SK Innovation's Georgia plant requires a US lithium supplier to complete the mine-to-battery supply chain.
Cypress Development Corp has a lithium claystone deposit in very close proximity to Silver Peak.
Cypress released a preliminary economic assessment (PEA) last fall and plans to release a prefeasibility study (PFS) soon.
Cypress’ mine has a 40-year minelife in the initial pit.
Cypress holds an Indicated Resource of 3.835 million tonnes LCE and an Inferred Resource of 5.126 million tonnes LCE.
Cypress’ claystone lithium operation is pegged at around 24,000 LCE tonnes a year according to the PEA.
Cypress could provide battery-grade lithium carbonate and hydroxide to Silver Peak at low cost.
Cypress’ mine has no water issues, is lower cost than brine or hard-rock lithium operations, and includes rare earths and other by-product credits.
Richard Mills authored the article for Ahead of the Herd.
Cypress Development Corp (TSX.V:CYP) is an advertiser on the site, and Richard Mills owns shares in CYP.
source_title: SK Innovation mulling another $5B for America’s first EV battery plant – Ahead of the Herd

facts: Georgia’s leadership position in the electric vehicle supply chain was enhanced by Anovion Technologies locating to Bainbridge.
Anovion plans to create 400 new jobs as the result of an $800 Million investment to produce components for EV batteries.
At the Savannah area Hyundai complex that will build new EV’s beginning about 18 months from now, a partner has been announced for their on-site battery manufacturer.
LG will be Hyundai’s joint venture partner in the battery plant, representing $4.3 billion in investment and represent 3,000 of the 8,100 employees expected in Bryan County.
Hyundai also has a joint venture with SK On to build a $5 Billion battery plant in Bartow County.
SK Battery America has a separate plant in Jackson County Georgia that builds EV batteries, currently supplying Ford and Volkswagen.
The current infrastructure to charge these future vehicles remains woefully inadequate.
Auto manufacturers are aware of this issue, as are the policy makers that are incentivizing a transition to EV’s.
Policy makers like to quote stats of total charging plugs available, but most are the slowest Level 1 and Level 2 chargers, and are concentrated in urban areas.
Americans outside of big cities rely heavily on their cars for longer trips, and road trips are part of the automobile’s allure – even for those who rarely take them.
Finding the fastest Level 3 chargers in sufficient numbers to provide motorists the confidence to take a long drive is a real barrier to EV adoption at the moment.

The federal government is working to address this shortage over the next five years with up to $7.5 Billion investments in Level 3 EV charging stations, with a focus on long distance travel corridors.
Georgia has already been awarded approximately $135 million for the first tranche of this National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) funding.
The goal is to have at least four high-speed chargers within 1 mile of an interstate or major highway corridor every 50 miles by 2030.
Tesla’s company-owned charging network mostly meets these criteria but, other than a handful of locations, currently serves Tesla exclusively.
Rivian plans a similar network that will be open to other makes.
Most other companies are relying on outside charging companies.
The largest non-Tesla network currently belongs to Electrify America, which is a company formed and funded by Volkswagen’s settlement related to their diesel engine emissions.
Electrify America plans on partnering with TravelCenters of America to add 1,000 chargers at 200 of their truck stop/travel centers.
General Motors has partnered with charging network EvGo and gas retailer Pilot/Flying J for 2,000 chargers at 500 stations.
Consumers and manufacturers have grown impatient, not only with the relatively slow progress of this buildout, but of the reliability of the non-Tesla charging networks.
Frustration is routinely expressed by reviews in car magazines and on YouTube with enough real world anecdotes that match the available data.
A recent post by InsideEVs.com noted that in 2022 almost two out of every five visits to superchargers were unsuccessful last year.
Tesla has a charging success rate above 90%.
The rest of the field – who combined have less superchargers than Tesla has – has to have a failure rate that has now ingrained real doubt among early adopters.
Last week, Ford Motor Company changed its charging strategy, and announced its new EV’s would be designed to work on Tesla’s network.
Current Ford EV’s will soon be able to access Tesla’s network using adapters.
The assessment of this Ford-Tesla tie up from Car & Driver is that there is currently no incentive for the EV charging companies to focus on reliability.
EV charging companies are losing money on charging and focused on the “land grab” to lock up sites for future charging customers when demand finally arrives.
The Tesla deal changes that, letting the charging companies know that manufacturers will form alliances with companies that enable their customers to use their cars to their full potential.
It remains to be seen if other manufacturers blink and join the Tesla network, or if the charging companies are willing to step up their game and quickly improve their reputation.
Georgians in all corners of the state now have billions riding on the answer to this question.
source_title: Georgia is building batteries and cars. Who is going to charge them? – Houston Home Journal

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced on Aug. 29 that Daesol Ausys Georgia will establish a new manufacturing facility in West Point.
The facility will create more than 140 new jobs.
The facility represents $72 million in investment in Harris County.
Harris County is located south of Atlanta near Columbus, GA.
Operations are expected to begin in December 2024.
Gov. Kemp stated that West Point has become an automotive capital in its own right.
Georgia’s automotive industry employs over 50,000 hardworking Georgians and will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers choose all corners of the state for future jobs.
Daesol Ausys was established in Korea in 2017.
The company specializes in designing and manufacturing motor and electric vehicle interior components and accessories.
It is a key supplier for Kia Georgia, Hyundai Motor Group, and General Motors.
Min Ho Kwon serves as CEO of Daesol Ausys.
The project will be located in the Northwest Harris Business Park.
Daesol Ausys’ new facility will produce luggage boards and luggage covers.
The company will be hiring for positions in production, purchasing, quality control, maintenance, human resources, and management.
Robert Grant serves as Chairman of the Harris County Commission.
West Point and Harris County have a wonderful relationship with Daesol Ausys’ sister companies Daehan Solutions and Daesol Materials.
Craig Greenhaw serves as Chairman of the Harris County Development Authority.
Alyce Thornhill serves as Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy.
Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Harris County Commission, Harris County Development Authority, City of West Point, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
Pat Wilson serves as GDEcD Commissioner.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
As the market transitions to electric vehicles, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain, creating more than $25 billion in investments and 30,000 jobs since 2018.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
source_title: Daesol Ausys automotive supplier to create 140 jobs in Harris County, GA - On Common Ground News - 24/7 local news

facts: Gov. Brian Kemp announced that Daesol Ausys Georgia will build a new manufacturing facility in West Point, Georgia.
Daesol Ausys Georgia will invest $72 million in Harris County.
The project will create more than 140 jobs.
West Point is also home to Kia and Hyundai assembly sites.
Daesol Ausys is a key supplier for Kia, Hyundai, and General Motors.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that West Point has become an automotive capital in its own right and expressed excitement that Daesol Ausys will join other innovators in Harris County.
Gov. Brian Kemp noted that Georgia’s automotive industry employs over 50,000 hardworking Georgians and will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers choose all corners of the state for future jobs.
The newly announced West Point plant will focus on producing luggage boards and covers.
Daesol Ausys specializes in designing and manufacturing interior components and accessories for electric vehicles.
Automotive industry job creation in Georgia rose 324% in the 2023 fiscal year compared to 2021, attributed to the state’s business climate and pursuit of the entire EV supply chain.
Min Ho Kwon, CEO of Daesol Ausys, stated the company is committed to delivering world-class services that exceed client requirements and expectations.
Min Ho Kwon expressed excitement about the project in the Northwest Harris Business Park and look forward to continuing the partnership with the State of Georgia and Harris County.
The Daesol facility in West Point is expected to begin operations in December 2024.
The company will hire for job openings in production, purchasing, quality control, maintenance, human resources, and management.

In recent months, multiple Korean manufacturing companies with ties to the automotive industry have announced developments in Georgia, including EV parts manufacturer NVH Korea and automotive supplier Hanon Systems.
Woory Industrial announced the opening of an EV parts manufacturing and research facility in Georgia in June.
source_title: Another Korean auto parts manufacturer to open facility in Georgia, governor announces – WSB-TV Channel 2 - Atlanta

facts: The Ray, Donovan Energy, and ABB partnered to upgrade the solar-powered electric vehicle charging station located at the visitor information center off Interstate 85 in West Point, Georgia.
The upgraded charging station was commissioned on June 3, 2020.
Charging at this location is free.
The Ray is a nonprofit organization and an 18-mile living transportation lab located along the I-85 corridor between LaGrange, Georgia, and the Georgia/Alabama state line.
The original station was a 50 kW unit installed in 2015 by The Ray in partnership with Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia.

That original station was the Southeast’s first solar-powered EV charging station, and prior to its installation, travel between the state capitols of Georgia and Alabama was not possible for the majority of electric vehicles.
The new charger is an ABB 175 kW Terra HP, a modular, high-powered charging system designed to minimize charging time.

John Picard, Vice President of The Ray, stated the initiative aims to demonstrate what’s next and showcase the electrification of transportation by showing how easy it is to store energy and rapidly recharge.

Georgia DOT Commissioner Russell R. McMurry, P.E., noted pride in collaborating with The Ray on drive-over tire pressure checks, visitor center solar panels, the upgraded EV charging station, and testing emerging technologies like all-weather striping for machine-operated vehicles along I-85 to bring sustainable innovations to transportation.

Donovan Energy served as the technology partner for this electrification project, recommended and procured the ABB modular system, and is a clean energy project development and finance company based in Cincinnati that has delivered over 400 energy efficiency and building retrofit projects since 2016.
The ABB Terra HP is future-proofed as a modular system, allowing a second 175 kW power unit to be added to scale up to 350 kW for the next generation of EVs.
Jeff Martin, Chief Operating Officer of Donovan Energy, stated the flexible architecture of the ABB Terra HP high power solution was an ideal fit for The Ray's upgrade goals.

Bob Stojanovic, Director of ABB’s EV Infrastructure business in North America, stated ABB is committed to deploying technology for societal advancement efficiently and sustainably, and is privileged to deploy DC fast charging technology fitting The Ray's vision.

Harriet Langford, President of The Ray, stated the debut of high-powered, 175kW fast charging on the interstate highlights dedication to transportation electrification and demonstrates how The Ray can evolve and upgrade previously piloted technologies.

Tim Echols of the state's Public Service Commission stated that having EV chargers in convenient locations like rest stops is the next frontier for electric vehicle charging, with The Ray, Georgia DOT, and City of West Point leading the way from theory to reality.
The solar-powered EV (PV4EV) charging station is grid-connected to ensure operability day or night and all year long.

Clean energy generated by twelve-panel Solar LIFT adds renewable resources to the local grid that supplies power to the ABB Terra HP, ensuring clean cars are fueled by clean, renewable energy on The Ray on I-85.

The Ray is a proving ground for evolving transportation ideas and technologies, named after Ray C. Anderson (1934-2011), a Georgia native and industry captain recognized as a leader in green business who challenged Atlanta-based Interface, Inc. to pursue zero environmental footprint.
The Ray is chaired by Ray’s daughter Harriet Langford.

Donovan Energy approaches energy in new ways to help customers reduce use and save money, offering solutions for procurement, PACE finance, LED/HVAC/envelope solutions, and renewable energy production across education, commercial real estate, healthcare, municipal, manufacturing, nonprofit, and parking sectors.

Georgia DOT plans, constructs, and maintains state and federal highways, is involved in bridge, waterway, public transit, rail, aviation, bike and pedestrian programs, helps local governments maintain roads, has nearly 4,000 employees, and focuses on innovation, safety, sustainability, and mobility.

ABB (NYSE: ABB) is a global engineering company energizing society and industry transformation, connecting software to electrification, robotics, automation, and motion portfolios, with over 130 years of history and 144,000 employees in over 100 countries.

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On EV Battery Manufacturing Facility
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On EV Battery Manufacturing Facility
facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and SK On have selected a site in Bartow County for a new electric vehicle (EV) battery manufacturing facility that will supply Hyundai Motor Group’s plants in the U.S.
Stakeholders estimate the project will create more than 3,500 new jobs through approximately $4-5 billion of investment in Bartow County.

Hyundai Motor Group and SK On recently signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) regarding the partnership for a new EV battery facility in the U.S., with details of the partnership still in development.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in Georgia’s ever-growing automotive industry, and his administration has focused on bringing jobs to overlooked communities while sharing their goal to invest further in the No. 1 state for business.
SK On was established in 2021 as the lithium-ion battery subsidiary branch of SK Innovation.
SK On currently employs more than 2,000 Georgians at its SK Battery America facility in Commerce.
In July, SK Battery America announced it would partner with the Work for Warriors Georgia program to hire veterans, servicemembers, and their families at its Georgia operations.
The new facility is subject to execution of relevant agreements and Hyundai Motor Group and SK On’s final board decisions.
The new facility will be located at Bartow Centre, a zoned manufacturing and industrial site located on Highway 411.
The project is aiming to begin operations in 2025.

Bartow County Sole Commissioner Steve Taylor expressed pleasure with the decision of Hyundai Motor Group and SK On and thanked the owners of the former Bartow Centre Industrial property for pursuing this extraordinary economic development project together.

Commissioner Taylor stated the project will have regional benefit for job seekers, establish Bartow in Georgia’s EV ecosystem significantly, and thrive due to Bartow’s strong history and talent base built upon advanced manufacturing, the automotive industry, and innovation.
Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The GDEcD partnered with Georgia EMC, Development Authority of Bartow County, the Cartersville-Bartow County Department of Economic Development, and Georgia Quick Start on the project.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Hyundai Motor Group and SK On have been pioneering partners for Georgia for decades as major drivers for Georgia’s automotive renaissance and the first Korean manufacturer to locate in the state, respectively.

Commissioner Wilson noted that supporting cooperation across Georgia’s growing EV ecosystem creates a fully integrated supply chain for automotive OEMs while connecting battery manufacturers with recyclers to close the loop on battery manufacturing.
Commissioner Wilson expressed excitement for the jobs of the future this will create for Bartow County and northwest Georgia, and gratitude for community and utility partner support.

Georgia’s prime location, major ports system, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and pro-business climate have made it an attractive location for diverse rapidly developing industries focused on creating a sustainable future.
Building on assets that make the automotive industry successful, Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric transportation industry.
Excluding this announcement, EV-related projects announced in the state since 2020 total approximately $17 billion in investment and more than 22,800 new jobs in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group separately announced in May its plans to invest $5.54 billion in Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, an EV manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on the Metaplant America facility in October with Governor Kemp, other state leaders, and local and federal officials.
source_title: Governor Announces Plans For EV Battery Manufacturing Facility In Bartow County | AM 1180 Radio

facts: International investment accounted for two-thirds of the $24 billion in capital pledged toward 426 projects in Georgia during the fiscal year ended June 30.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development reported that 47 percent of the 38,400 announced jobs came from foreign subsidiaries.
Approximately $15.6 billion of the foreign capital investments came from the automotive sector, driven by the transition to electric vehicles and South Korean OEMs and parts suppliers.
Late in 2022, Hyundai broke ground on a $5.54 billion plant near Savannah.
In May, Hyundai announced a partnership with LG Energy Solution for an on-site battery plant.
Hyundai's Bryan County site, called the “Metaplant,” has attracted nearly $2 billion in additional supplier investments.
These supplier investments are located either co-located on the 2,900-acre property or in nearby communities.
Hyundai revealed that total investment planned for a separate Cartersville EVbattery plant built in partnership SK with would top $5 billion.
Hyundai-affiliated projects, including suppliers, accounted for about half of the investment total in fiscal year 2023.
Hyundai announced a multi-decade partnership with Georgia Tech that ESPN reported will include a $55 million deal for naming rights at what will now be called Bobby Dodd Stadium at Hyundai Field.
The deal includes a hydrogen research initiative.
The deal also includes a previously announced $120,000 scholarship fund.
California-based Rivian accounted for another $5 billion commitment with plans to put a factory in Covington.
Job creation in the auto sector has grown four-fold in two years.
The state pointed to an electric-mobility initiative launched under Gov. Brian Kemp.
Even removing Hyundai and Rivian's generational investments, the state still eclipsed last year’s record job-creation figure by 2,800 positions.
Norway’s Freyr Battery is set to put a $2.4 billion energy storage plant in Coweta County.
Finland’s ADMARES said it would bulid a $750 million modular housing plant in Waycross.
Norway and Finland are among the top-five investors in the state this year, behind Korea and in front of perennial powerhouses Switzerland, Japan and the Netherlands.
The $2.5 billion Q CELLS expansion in Cartersville from Korea’s Hanhwa was announced in January.
Executives credited the solar production incentives in the Inflation Reduction Act for providing an attractive regulatory framework.
Most announced expansion projects are multi-year projects that don’t commence production until years into the future.
As market conditions and company plans change, the actual number of jobs created and capital deployed may not necessarily match up with initial pledges.
States can use “clawback” provisions to recoup some tax incentives calculated on the baseline level of jobs and capital investment.
source_title: Georgia’s Record Year Driven by International Investment  - Global Atlanta

facts: the $5 billion electric vehicle battery plant planned between cartersville and rome took a big step forward tuesday with the filing of the development plan with the state.
source_title: Margaret Sanborn Blog

facts: Ascend Elements has raised $542 million to take its plans to recycle lithium-ion batteries to the next level.
The investment round announced Wednesday will support building a factory in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.

At the Kentucky facility, Ascend will process the “black mass” derived from slicing and sorting old lithium-ion batteries into precursor cathode active materials (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).
Ascend plans to spend about $1 billion to build the Kentucky facility.
Last year it won $480 million in grant funding from the Department of Energy to go toward its construction.
CEO Mike O’Kronley said, “This $542 million we announced today will be coupled with, and really unlock, the DOE grants.”
Ascend already processes black mass into lithium carbonate, a key mineral for lithium-ion batteries, at its Covington, Georgia facility that opened in March.
The pCAM and CAM it will make from black mass at its Kentucky facility will be precisely engineered at the molecular level to serve as a direct input into lithium-ion battery manufacturing.
When it’s up and running, the factory will have enough capacity to provide materials for batteries for up to 750,000 electric vehicles per year.
CEO Mike O’Kronley said, “There’s a lot of activity, a lot of investment that’s going to support the energy transition.”
CEO Mike O’Kronley said, “We are providing a key part of that overall transition, with respect to batteries. We’re helping to make batteries more sustainable.”
Ascend’s new funding round is led by Decarbonization Partners, Singapore-based investment firm Temasek, and Qatar Investment Authority.
Investors joined the round include Agave Partners, Alliance Resource Partners, Alumni Ventures, BHP Ventures, Fifth Wall, Hitachi Ventures and Tenaska.
The funding round is one of the largest in the cleantech space this year.

The funding is in line with larger funding rounds going to U.S. startups targeting demand from the fast-growing U.S. battery and electric-vehicle manufacturing sector, including $1 billion raised by battery-recycling startup Redwood Materials earlier this month.
The Biden administration has made battery recycling a priority.
The DOE earlier this year offered a $2 billion conditional loan commitment to Redwood Materials for facilities in Nevada and South Carolina to produce anode and cathode components.
The DOE also offered a $375 million conditional loan commitment to Canada-based Li-Cycle for a New York facility to process lithium carbonate.
Recycled battery materials could be a vital source of supply for the fast-growing U.S. lithium-ion battery and EV industries.
Over the past year, more than $53 billion has been invested in U.S. EV battery and supply-chain manufacturing capacity.
This investment is largely to meet demand from automakers planning to invest more than $200 billion in domestic production of EVs over the coming decade.
DOE forecasts that U.S. battery production capacity could hit 1,000 gigawatt-hours a year by 2030.
Today, China supplies the vast majority of the world’s lithium-ion battery pCAM and CAM from mined metals.
Ascend and other battery recyclers are targeting the growing supply of depleted batteries to provide much of that material at far lower cost and environmental impact.

Ascend’s patented “Hydro-to-Cathode” process, originally developed by researchers at the Worcester Polytechnic Institute more than a decade ago, skips several intermediary steps of more traditional methods for making pCAM and CAM.
The company is already producing pCAM for nickel manganese cobalt (NMC) lithium-ion batteries at its facility in Westborough, Massachusetts.
Peer-reviewed research indicates that cathode materials made from recycled batteries perform just as well as those made from primary metals.
Deriving them from recycled batteries rather than raw materials that have to be mined, shipped and refined reduces the associated carbon emissions by 93 percent, Ascend Elements says.
CEO Mike O’Kronley noted, “We just process it, purify it, and make these materials directly,” largely because they’re starting with a highly concentrated source of minerals already in batteries.
Recycling won’t be able to eliminate mining for new lithium-ion battery materials.
The booming demand sparked by EV manufacturing growth will far outpace the scale of available used batteries for years to come.
But recycling can make a dent in the need for new mining, and produce battery materials at lower cost.
EV batteries need to be recycled anyway, to keep their toxic materials out of landfills.
The amount of lithium-ion battery waste from EVs is expected to grow from roughly 600,000 metric tons in 2021 to as high as 11 million metric tons worldwide by 2030.
Ascend is now feeding its Georgia recycling facility with battery scrap from a nearby SK Battery America plant as well as from other battery factories in the Southeast’s growing “battery belt.”
It is also lining up customers for its pCAM and CAM products.

In June it announced a $1 billion contract with an unnamed U.S.-based company to supply sustainable pCAM starting in Q4 2024 with options to expand the multiyear contract to a larger quantity with a value of up to $5 billion.
CEO Mike O’Kronley said, “Any battery manufacturer would prefer to have more sustainable materials that are produced sustainably rather than mined out of the ground.”
source_title: Another US EV-battery recycler just raised a massive… | Canary Media

facts: The Joint Development Authority (JDA) of Jasper, Morgan, Newton and Walton counties officially closed on bonds issued to finance the Rivian electric vehicle plant in Stanton Springs.
A special called meeting was held on Nov. 6 where the JDA voted to approve the closing in a near unanimous decision.
Only one JDA member, Ben Riden from Morgan County, opposed the closing approval.
Rivian executed an exclusive rental agreement which will give the electric vehicle company long-term rights for the Stanton Springs property.
The JDA stated that closing and issuing bonds for the Rivian project is a great day in Georgia.
Renting the site to Rivian is the next step in delivering a generational opportunity for Georgians in Jasper, Morgan, Newton, and Walton counties and beyond.
The project will bring $5 billion in investment to the region.
The project will create 7,500 good-paying jobs.
Grading work at the site will be completed by both the JDA and the state of Georgia in the weeks to come.
Construction on the electric vehicle manufacturing facility will begin by early 2024.
Rivian will continue efforts to grow its local presence by opening a new office in Covington.
An apprenticeship program has been announced for the Stanton Springs location.
Tuition for the apprenticeship will be covered by the state of Georgia’s HOPE grants.
All other expenses for the apprenticeship will be paid for by Rivian.
The goal of the apprenticeship is to assemble an initial group to support future industrial maintenance needs.
source_title: JDA, Rivian to close on Stanton Springs location - The Covington News

facts: ABB unveiled its latest DC-DC converter technology in the GENBETA prototype car, as the ABB FIA Formula E World Championship returned to Tempelhof Circuit in Berlin.
The GENBETA is a high performance modified version of the Formula E's GEN3 racecar, developed to push EV performance to its limit.
The GENBETA is well known for winning the Guinness World Record for fastest indoor land speed in 2023.
US President Donald Trump has signed H.J. Res. 140 into law, overturning a Biden administration mining ban in northern Minnesota.
The resolution reopens 225,504 acres of land in the Superior National Forest to mineral exploration and development.
The Duluth Complex in northern Minnesota contains nearly 7.3 billion tonnes of minerals.
If developed, this region could constitute 95% of America's nickel reserves, 88% of its cobalt reserves, one third of its copper reserves and 75% of US platinum resources.
Nickel and cobalt are core materials in lithium-ion batteries used by most EV manufacturers.
EV manufacturer Rivian announced it is increasing the initial production capacity of its future manufacturing facility in Stanton Springs North, Georgia to 300,000 vehicles per year.
The company has invested US$5bn in its new facility where it will manufacture the R2 SUV.
Progress on the facility will accelerate this summer as the facility’s primary buildings begin to take shape.
Rivian also operates a plant in Normal, Illinois where it works on commercial vans and the Rivian R1.
Mercedes-Benz Trucks has launched the reECONIC project with Hydro supplying recycled aluminium components.
The initiative builds on the eECONIC battery electric waste collection truck platform.
This involves Hydro providing low-carbon, recycled aluminium made from Hydro CIRCAL to Mercedes-Benz Trucks for its reECONIC project.
According to Hydro, CIRCAL has one of the world's lowest carbon footprints for recycled aluminium.
The material is produced using end-of-life automotive parts and building materials.
More customers have turned to EVs since the start of the US and Israel's war in Iran, which has triggered rising fuel costs.
Bloomberg data shows that in March 2026, consumers in France, Germany and the UK drove off 206,200 EVs, a 44% increase over 2025.
Recent data from Bloomberg shows that in South Korea, electric car transactions more than doubled.
In Italy, 16,000 battery-powered vehicles were sold last month, a 67% increase over the previous year.
source_title: Formula E, Rivian & US Mining: Top EV News This Week | EV Magazine

facts: Rivian held a kickoff ceremony for its upcoming Georgia manufacturing plant near Social Circle, Georgia.
The facility is projected to generate approximately 7,500 direct jobs by 2030.
Thousands more indirect roles across the supply chain will be created in surrounding counties including Newton County.
Construction at the site is expected to begin in 2026.
Production at the plant is slated for 2028.
The event underscores regional momentum in advanced manufacturing, workforce development, and long-term economic vitality for communities in east-central Georgia.
The plant offers access to a growing pipeline of skilled manufacturing and engineering roles for Newton County residents and regional employers.
It creates opportunities for local educational institutions and training programs to align curricula with the future job market that Rivian will help stimulate.
It enhances the prospective viability of local suppliers, vendors, and service businesses serving the plant’s ecosystem.
Local education and workforce training partners should begin mapping programs tailored to the types of manufacturing jobs Rivian will bring.
Economic development teams should continue attracting supplier firms and supporting infrastructure enhancements to complement the Rivian investment.
The project aligns with the Newton County Industrial Development Authority's (NCIDA) mission to attract and support high-value industry in east-central Georgia.
NCIDA views the facility as a catalyst for community transformation, complementing its strategic vision for economic impact, advanced industrial assets, and workforce capacity.
source_title: Rivian Kickoff Ceremony for Georgia Plant Ushers in Local Opportunity

facts: Rivian breaks ground on a long-delayed $5 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia east of Atlanta.
Construction begins despite President Donald Trump's successful push to roll back electric vehicle tax credits.
Starting Sept 30, buyers will no longer qualify for savings of up to $7,500 per car.
Rivian is a money-losing California-based company.
The Georgia plant was first announced in 2021 and is key to Rivian reaching profitability.
Rivian currently manufactures the high-end R1T pickup truck and R1S SUV in Normal, Illinois, as well as delivery vans for Amazon and others.
R1T prices start at $71,000.
The Illinois plant will begin producing smaller R2 SUVs next year with prices starting at $45,000.
An expanded Illinois plant will be able to assemble 215,000 vehicles yearly.
Rivian's Georgia operation will be able to make 200,000 vehicles yearly starting in 2028.
The company plans another 200,000 in capacity in phase two to spread fixed costs over more vehicles.
These projections represent a leap from the 40,000 to 46,000 vehicles Rivian expects to deliver this year, down from 52,000 last year.
The company is limiting production now in part to launch 2026 models.
Alex Oyler, North American director of auto research firm SBD Automotive, stated that for Rivian it is do-or-die time and scale is key to profitability.
EV sales growth in the United States rose only 1.5% in 2025's first half according to Cox Automotive.
Tesla accounted for almost 45% of U.S. electric vehicle sales in that period, while General Motors climbed to 13%.
Rivian held a 3% share in the first half of the year, behind Tesla and six traditional automakers.
Excluding Tesla, Rivian is the most successful startup automaker.
Rivian initially targeted demand for electric pickups and SUVs but now faces competition from Ford’s F-150 Lightning and the electric Chevrolet Silverado.
After an initial public offering in 2021, Rivian shares have fallen by more than 80%.
Rivian lost $1.66 billion in 2025's first half.

Some automakers are cooling on EVs, including Stellantis canceling Ram’s electric truck program, Ford delaying production at a new Tennessee plant, and GM abandoning plans for a suburban Detroit plant.
Sam Fiorani, vice president at AutoForecast Solutions, noted that slowing EV growth and competition do not play in Rivian's favor but affirmed an EV market still exists.
Georgia has pledged $1.5 billion in incentives to Rivian in exchange for 7,500 jobs paying at least $56,000 a year on average.
Rivian cannot benefit from most incentives unless it meets employment goals.
The state is already spending $175 million to buy and grade land and improve roads for the project.
Georgia Republican Gov. Brian Kemp wants to make Georgia the electric mobility capital of America and remains confident Rivian can fulfill its promises despite facing bumps.
Two separate EV makers hoping to assemble vehicles in a former GM plant in Lordstown, Ohio, went bankrupt.
Georgia’s Hyundai complex near Savannah is producing vehicles, though a battery plant there was delayed by ICE arresting 475 people on site, including over 300 South Koreans.
Rivian was supposed to be making trucks by now at the 2,000-acre site near Social Circle, about 45 miles east of Atlanta.
The company paused construction in 2024 as it burned through cash.
German automaker Volkswagen agreed to invest $5.8 billion in Rivian in exchange for software and electrical technology.
Then-President Joe Biden’s administration in November agreed to loan Rivian $6.6 billion to build the Georgia plant.
Rivian hopes the U.S. Department of Energy will distribute the loan money, arguing it will boost domestic manufacturing.
Some neighbors oppose the plant, citing concerns about pollution and inappropriate proximity to farms.
Resident Eddie Clay, who lives less than a mile away, reported his well water turned mud-choked after excavation at the site.
Rivian faces additional challenges including tariffs costing $2,000 per vehicle.
The Trump administration ending a tax-credit program will cost the company $140 million in revenue this year.
Long-term threats include low-priced, cutting-edge Chinese EVs.
Chief Policy Officer Alan Hoffman stated Rivian is in it for the long haul and believes it can compete with anyone and excel when given the opportunity.
source_title: It’s ‘do or die’ as Rivian breaks ground on a $5B Ga. plant

facts: Electric vehicle maker Rivian confirmed it plans to build a $5 billion assembly plant and battery factory in Georgia.
The confirmation was announced on Thursday, December 17, 2021.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp called the project “the largest single economic development project ever in this state’s history.”
A Rivian executive outlined the employment plans at a press conference with Gov. Kemp at the State Capitol.
The factory is planned to initially employ 7,500 workers.
State officials have said the jobs tally could grow to 10,000.
The plant will be built on a sprawling swath of farmland and pine forest about an hour’s drive east of Atlanta.
Rivian had previously pitched the new Bryan County megasite earlier in the year.
source_title: GA Gov. Kemp announces $5 billion EV maker Rivian plant east of Atlanta

facts: CGE Renewables serves Social Circle, GA businesses with demand response programs, peak charge reduction, and energy load management incentives.
Utilities in Social Circle charge commercial accounts for peak demand, defined as the highest 15-minute power draw recorded in a billing period.
A single 15-minute power spike from equipment like compressors or HVAC units can set the entire month's demand charge even if average load is low.
CGE Renewables uses load monitoring, automated controls, and strategic scheduling to flatten peak demand without disrupting operations.
Demand response programs in Social Circle vary by utility, ISO, and region; CGE identifies qualifying programs and manages enrollment across all of them.
Utilities typically call on enrolled customers to reduce load during grid emergencies 10–20 times per year, with each event lasting 1–4 hours.
Participants receive bill credits or direct payments in exchange for load reduction during events.
ISO capacity markets like PJM, NYISO, and ISONE pay businesses to commit load reduction capacity in advance for predictable annual revenue.
Utility tariffs often charge significantly higher rates during peak hours, typically between 2–8 pm on weekdays.
CGE shifts flexible loads, including EV charging in Social Circle, pre-cooling, and batch processing, to off-peak windows to reduce energy and demand costs automatically.
Customers can achieve a 15–30% rate cost reduction in Social Circle through load shifting.

Interruptible rates offer a permanent discount on demand charges in exchange for allowing utilities to curtail part of the load during system emergencies, best suited for facilities with flexible production schedules or backup generation.
Proactive load management strategies include staggering equipment startups, pre-cooling buildings, and deferring non-critical loads to reduce peak 15-minute demand monthly.
These proactive strategies can achieve a 20–40% demand reduction in Social Circle, GA.

Pairing battery storage with demand response allows batteries to charge during off-peak hours and discharge during peak events, reducing demand charges while earning DR incentive payments simultaneously for a dual revenue stream.
CGE handles enrollment and load management from initial bill analysis to automated real-time controls on event days.
The company pulls 24 months of interval data from utility accounts to map load profiles against tariff structures, identifying peak windows and demand spikes.
CGE screens facilities against every available DR program in the utility territory and ISO region to model payment and savings potential.

CGE works with operations teams to identify loads that can be shifted, curtailed, or pre-conditioned without disrupting production, such as HVAC pre-cooling, lighting dimming, EV charge scheduling, and equipment stagger starts.
Automated controls are configured to respond to demand events in real time, automatically flattening the demand peak without manual intervention.
Monthly reporting is provided on demand charge savings, DR event performance, and incentive payments earned, with continuous tuning of the load management strategy as operations and tariffs evolve.
Demand response delivers the most value to facilities with variable loads, high peak-to-average ratios, or operations that can flex without disruption.

Manufacturers with production scheduling flexibility, large motor loads, and compressed air systems have significant curtailment capacity, often worth $30–$100K per year in combined demand savings and DR payments in Social Circle.

Non-critical HVAC loads, parking facilities, and administrative areas can participate in DR without affecting critical systems like patient care, unlocking incentive revenue from already managed loads.
Lighting, HVAC, and EV charging loads across multiple locations can be aggregated into significant demand response capacity using pre-cooling strategies to capture peak savings.
Hotel HVAC systems are ideal DR candidates because guest comfort is maintained through pre-cooling while the building's thermal mass absorbs the load shift during event windows.
Refrigerated warehouses, EV fleet charging, and large lighting loads make logistics facilities natural DR participants, especially when paired with battery storage for dual revenue.
Campus HVAC, lighting, and EV charging loads across school buildings can participate in DR programs to earn incentive payments that offset operating budgets and utility expenses.
One facility earned $31K in DR payments over 12 months with zero operational impact using a pre-cooling strategy that kept guests unaware of load reduction events.
Another facility aggregated loads across 14 stores into a DR program and shifted HVAC pre-cooling schedules, saving $48K per year across the portfolio without changing store operations.
CGE offers free demand analysis and enrollment in DR programs to help facilities see savings on their next bill in Social Circle.
source_title: Demand Response & Load Management in Social Circle, GA | Peak Reduction Programs | CGE Renewables

facts: Ateios Systems has closed $7.25 million in Series A funding to scale production of its next-generation lithium-ion battery electrodes and expand supply to global battery manufacturers.

The funding round was led by TitletownTech, with participation from existing investors Elevate Ventures, VisionTech Partners, Good Growth Capital, Lateral Capital, Techstars, and Keshif Ventures, as well as new investors JHH Ventures, Stonecast Ventures, Zaka Ventures, Carat Venture Partners, and E8 Angels.
Ateios’ high-speed electrode manufacturing lines are powered by the company’s solvent-free RaiCure™ electron-curing platform.
The technology enables battery manufacturers to modify existing assembly lines to qualify RaiCore™ electrodes toward gigawatt-hour production.
The new capital will support the expansion of Ateios’ commercial engagements with battery and advanced materials partners, including ongoing work with companies such as Eastman Kodak and Vianode.
The funding will enable Ateios to increase production capacity and further advance its manufacturing capabilities as it moves toward supplying battery manufacturers at larger commercial scale.
Dr. Rajan Kumar is the Founder and CEO of Ateios Systems.
In September, Ateios and Eastman Kodak demonstrated a top speed for a solvent-free electrode production process.
The system achieved coating speeds of 80 meters per minute, nearly three times faster than the industry standard.
The process produces PFAS-free, solvent-free electrodes compatible with lithium-ion battery chemistries, including LCO, LFP, NMC, and synthetic graphite.
Craig Dickman is a Managing Partner at TitletownTech and an Ateios board member.
Ateios Systems was founded by Dr. Rajan Kumar, whose background in nanoscale science and nanoengineering led to the development of printable energy composites.
The company advanced its technology through the Department of Energy’s Innovation Crossroads program at Oak Ridge National Laboratory and incubation at the Battery Innovation Center.
Ateios earned industry recognition including Battery Manufacturer of the Year at The Battery Show North America in 2025.

The company recently showcased production-grade RaiCore™ electrodes, including third-party verification of PFAS-free battery materials, alongside its manufacturing partners at the 43rd International Battery Seminar & Exhibit in Orlando.

links.context: Request samples for qualification testing and pilot production

source_title: Ateios Systems Closes $7.25M Series A to Scale Next-Generation Battery Electrode Manufacturing - Athens Banner-Herald

facts: In June, Toyota warned buyers of its bZ4X electric SUV not to drive their vehicles for fear the wheels could fall off.
Toyota has not yet found a solution to the problem and is offering to buy back the SUV from its owners.
Toyota stated it appreciates customer loyalty and is supporting them through the recall, but will offer to repurchase the bZ4X if customers do not want other options.
Owners who keep their vehicles get free use of another Toyota vehicle until a fix is found.
Toyota will pay $5,000 toward an owner’s car payments or as a partial refund for those keeping the vehicle.
Toyota will extend the factory warranty on the bZ4X by the length of time before the owner gets their vehicle back.
The offer includes free EV charging once owners get the vehicle back and covers gasoline costs for loaner vehicles.
It was unclear how owners would handle dealer markups, as the vehicle's price starts at $43,215.
In June, Toyota announced that all 2,700 of its new electric bZ4X SUVs were a danger to drivers, with only 260 delivered in the United States to date.
Toyota stated that after low-mileage use, hub bolts on the wheel can loosen enough for the wheel to detach, potentially causing loss of control and crashes.
The cause of the issue and driving patterns are under investigation, and no one should drive these vehicles until a remedy is performed.
No remedy was available at the time of the June release.
The bZ4X debuted in Japan in June.
Toyota president Akio Toyoda said in December that the company planned to roll out 30 BEV models by 2030.
David Leggett, automotive editor at GlobalData, noted Toyota has been under pressure to up its game in EVs and will be disappointed by the recall on its first mass-market electric cars.
Anjani Trivedi of Bloomberg wrote that if traditional auto giants commit to this level of quality and safety, investors and regulators should increase scrutiny.
source_title: Toyota Offering to Buy Back Electric Vehicles After Issuing Startling Warning to Stop Driving SUV Immediately – Long Island, NY

facts: Norwegian battery maker Freyr Battery announced it will move its global headquarters to Central Texas after canceling plans for a $2.6 billion Georgia factory.
Austin will be the home of Freyr Battery's new global headquarters, according to a Monday, February 10 news release.
The company chose Austin "to geographically align the company’s workforce with its operations and strategy to provide solar modules made in the United States."

This announcement comes just days after Freyr agreed to sell its 368-acre site in Coweta County, Georgia previously designated for a large-scale "Giga America" factory that would have supplied electric batteries for clean energy functions.
The relocation coincides with the company's acquisition of Trina Solar’s 5-GW solar module factory in Wilmer, which already employs more than 1,000 people across the Wilmer and Dallas communities.
Freyr Battery develops and manufactures clean batteries to power energy storage, electric vehicles, and marine applications.

Daniel Barcelo, FREYR’s Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer, stated: "Today marks another important step for FREYR as we execute our strategic plan to establish an integrated U.S. solar + battery storage manufacturing enterprise built on advanced technology."

Barcelo added that the company's global headquarters will be in Austin, Texas, describing it as a vibrant and fast-growing hub of renewable energy and advanced technology development strategically located close to their operations.
Austin is located about three hours south of Freyr's manufacturing facility in Wilmer, a suburb of Dallas.
Barcelo stated the company intends to "bring more than 1,000 new American jobs to the Texas economy" and build its teams in Austin and Wilmer.
Last week, Freyr confirmed plans to abandon its Georgia lithium-ion battery plant in a letter to the Coweta County Development Authority during a Thursday, February 6 meeting.

Jason Peace, Frey's senior vice president of business development, cited rising interest rates, falling battery prices, and a change in leadership as contributors to the decision to abandon the Georgia plant.
In a letter to the Coweta County Development Authority, Freyr stated it would work with the Georgia Department of Community Affairs to repay grants and incentives tied to the project.
In conjunction with its relocation to Texas, Freyr will sell its Coweta County site to an unnamed buyer for $50 million.
The sale of the Coweta County site is expected to close on February 15.
Freyr's estimated net profits from the Coweta County land transaction are expected to be $22.5 million.
Freyr’s new global headquarters will be located at 1211 E 4th St., Austin, TX 78746.
source_title: Freyr Battery moves global headquarters to Austin, Texas

facts: Aspen Aerogels is abandoning a $325 million plant to make thermal barriers in Statesboro.
It becomes the latest battery or component supplier to vacate a major Georgia project amid uneven demand for electric vehicles.
Chief Financial Officer Ricardo Rodriguez stated the company is meeting long-term demands by investing to maximize capacity at its aerogel facility in Rhode Island.
The company will supplement capacity by leveraging an external manufacturing model that worked well in 2024 for its energy industrial products.
Aerogel sheets are used between battery cells to prevent what Aspen calls 'thermal runaway,' when lithium-ion batteries reach a critically high temperature and potentially burst into flames.
Aspen is working with outside manufacturing partners, initially in China in the near term.
The company enacted job and spending cuts designed to save $35 million per year.
These moves are intended to shore up its balance sheet and accelerate its path to profitability.
The 250 jobs promised when the Massachusetts-based company announced its location in Bulloch County in 2022 will never materialize.
The company will assess options to derive value from assets in Statesboro, including relocating some equipment to upgrade and expand its existing plant, a path requiring minimal capital.
This news comes a week after Freyr Battery, a Norwegian investor, also pulled the plug on a $2.6 billion Georgia plant in Coweta County.
The Development Authority of Bulloch County will look to activate clawback measures to recoup taxpayer funds spent on the 90-acre site.
The plant was set to be located in what is now Bruce Yawn Commerce Park, the former Southern Gateway Commerce Park.
This park is now home to two Korean suppliers to the Hyundai Meta Plant in nearby Bryan County: Ajin Georgia and SECO Ecoplastic.
Combined, these two investments total more than $500 million.
Aspen was tentatively approved in October for a massive loan by the Biden administration’s Department of Energy to support the Georgia plant.
Aspen opted to forgo the debt to reduce external obligations even as it scrambled to ramp up production overall.
CFO Ricardo Rodriguez noted it feels great not to take on an incremental $671 million of debt on the balance sheet while still fulfilling expected demand.
In October, Aspen executives viewed low-cost capital as vital for a plant poised to receive an outpouring of $1 billion over multiple phases reaching 500,000 square feet.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp has decried the picking of 'winners and losers' by the government in the EV space, even as federal support has proven critical for investors in the state.
Kemp's office previously incorporated Aspen into roundups about record investment hauls.
In the coming quarter, Aspen projects a possible 'low point' for its EV thermal barrier business.
General Motors, whose Ultium platform counts Aspen as an exclusive supplier, is clearing out finished-vehicle inventories built up in 2024.
Longer-term, the company is optimistic, having landed deals with six OEMs including GM, Toyota and Valmet, a Porsche supplier.
It was set to supply Audi and Scania through a deal with Northvolt, the now-bankrupt Swedish battery producer.
Aspen is looking into 'plan B' options to fulfill that demand.

President, CEO and Chairman Don Young stated they are confident electric vehicles will be the choice of a growing number of car buyers and that energy and power generation will be important macroeconomic drivers where they have an important role to play.
Aspen swung to a narrow profit on $452.7 million in annual revenue, a 90 percent increase from the previous year.
As early as 2023, the company had decided to delay construction on the plant in Register, 60 miles west of Savannah, 'pre-empting a reset in EV demand expectations.'
The company was experimenting with contract manufacturers during the delay.
When outside suppliers proved sufficient enough to support bids for new contracts, it was a 'no brainer' to avoid the large capital outlay.
CFO Ricardo Rodriguez said they can more modularly build up the supply source and would not have gotten awards last year and this year if customers were not OK with that model.
Aspen’s PyroThin thermal barrier recently won a Volvo Truck contract that the company expects to add $45 million in revenue per year when it becomes active.
source_title: EV Thermal Barrier Firm to Halt $325M Plant in Statesboro - Global Atlanta

facts: FREYR Battery is a Europe-based producer of next-generation battery cells.
FREYR Battery will build a manufacturing plant in Coweta County, Georgia.
The project represents a $2.57 billion investment.
The project will create 723 jobs during the next seven years.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the FREYR plant project on Friday.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that job creators and innovators from around the world choose Georgia for success.
Gov. Brian Kemp welcomed FREYR to Georgia as a company bringing transformational investments and opportunity to local communities.
FREYR is dedicated to supporting a domestic supply chain for renewable power sources.
FREYR's battery cells can be used for stationary energy storage, electric mobility, and additional applications.
FREYR co-founder and CEO Tom Einar Jensen stated the company is committed to decarbonizing the transportation and energy sectors alongside U.S. partners.
FREYR expects its U.S. expansion plan to spur job creation and develop localized, decarbonized supply chains to enhance energy security and economic activity.
Georgia is ramping up to become a leading producer of electric vehicles and EV batteries.
During the past year, Rivian announced plans for a large EV manufacturing plant near Covington.
During the past year, Hyundai announced plans for a large EV manufacturing plant near Savannah.
The Rivian and Hyundai projects are the two largest economic development projects in Georgia's history.
SK Innovation has built two EV battery manufacturing facilities in Northeast Georgia.
FREYR's new facility will be named Giga America.
Giga America will have a planned first-phase production capacity of about 34 gigawatt hours.
The company is evaluating clean power supply solutions for the new plant, including potential renewable energy from solar installations.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team partnered with the Coweta County Development Authority, the Metro Atlanta Chamber, Georgia EMC, and the state Department of Labor’s Quick Start program to land the project.
source_title: Battery manufacturer to build plant in Coweta County |

facts: Lisa Nash serves as general manager of the Qcells solar panel factory in Dalton, Georgia.
Dalton is a town of 34,000 located 50 miles up I-75 from Cartersville, Georgia.
The Qcells factory opened in January 2019 after the Trump administration imposed tariffs on Chinese-made panels.
The Korean conglomerate Hanwha owns Qcells.
Qcells initially planned to hire several hundred people at the Dalton site.
By the end of 2019, the Dalton factory employed more than 800 people.
Georgia elected President Joe Biden and Senators Jon Ossoff and Raphael Warnock in 2020, leading to policies promoting domestic clean energy manufacturing.
The Inflation Reduction Act passed in August 2022, creating comprehensive policies to support domestic clean energy manufacturing.
Qcells broke ground on a second facility in Dalton in February 2023.
The Dalton expansion was completed in August 2023.
The expansion added two football fields' worth of manufacturing space with four new production lines.
The new lines produce 1.5 times more solar panels than the original three lines due to technological advances.
The entire Qcells Dalton complex now employs 2,000 full-time people and produces 5.1 gigawatts of solar panels a year, more than any other site in the U.S.
Since the IRA passed, Georgia has received $23 billion in clean energy factory investment, much flowing to northwest Georgia.
Both CSX and Norfolk Southern run Class I rail lines through Dalton.
Local factories consolidated into global carpeting giants Shaw Industries and Mohawk Industries.
80 percent of America’s tufted carpet production happens within 100 miles of Dalton.
The largest Shaw site covers 650,000 square feet, and the new Engineered Floors facility covers 2.8 million square feet.
Dalton employment peaked at 80,200 in 2006 before the Great Recession cratered demand for carpeting products.
From June 2011 to June 2012, Dalton recorded the most jobs lost of all 372 metro areas surveyed by the Bureau of Labor Statistics.
One-quarter of Dalton’s pre-recession jobs vanished, and unemployment surged to 12.3 percent.
Engineered Floors, Mohawk, and Shaw still dominate local employment with some 14,000 jobs among them.
Local leaders acquired an undeveloped lot south of town and cleared/leveled it to be shovel-ready for a future tenant.
Georgia’s Department of Economic Development sent Qcells to the Greater Dalton Chamber of Commerce in February 2018.
Qcells originally promised to invest $130 million and hire 525 people within five years, fulfilling the promise in three months.
Qcells brought in helicopters to install air-conditioning units on the factory roof.
Qcells hourly wages for non-experienced hires start at $17.50 to $22, amounting to $36,400 to $45,760 a year for full-time work.
Workers with experience in robotics and manufacturing can earn much more through on-the-job training handling the in-house fleet of robots.
Qcells relied on Quick Start, a Georgia state program that funds worker training for new factories before they open.
Some 56 percent of Dalton-area students enroll in postsecondary education within 16 months of graduating high school.
The Greater Dalton Chamber of Commerce runs Project Purpose, a crash course for living-wage careers.
Last year, seven young adults completed Project Purpose and Qcells hired one; this time, 18 finished and Qcells hired 12 to start on July 1.
Nash hopes to double the number of Project Purpose hires next year or more.
900 apartment units are slated to come online from last August through November to address a long-running housing deficit.
The plush Carpentry Hotel opened this spring across from the Oakwood Cafe in downtown Dalton.
Senator Jon Ossoff authored the IRA manufacturing incentives that Qcells is tapping into.
Qcells is building an even bigger factory compound down in Cartersville, which won a conditional $1.45 billion loan guarantee from the Department of Energy on August 8.
The Cartersville facility will use Inflation Reduction Act tax credits to onshore slicing silicon wafers, carving them into solar cells, and assembling finished modules with newer robots.
Workers in Dalton currently complete just the last step of solar panel production: assembling modules.
97 percent of the glass that encloses solar panels comes from China.
Solarcycle is breaking ground on a combination solar-panel recycling facility and solar-glass factory in Cedartown, some 70 miles southwest of Dalton.
Solarcycle founder and CEO Suvi Sharma expects to invest $344 million in the community and hire 600 full-time employees.
Solarcycle will use its rail spur to ship in low-iron silica from a Georgia mine, plus soda ash and limestone.
Over time, Solarcycle will supplement raw ingredients with increasing amounts of glass pulled from decommissioned solar panels, including those made by Qcells.
The goal is to produce enough glass for 5 gigawatts of panels per year and ship it to nearby customers.
Hanwha recently became the largest shareholder in REC Silicon to secure access to domestic polysilicon from the Pacific Northwest.
Hyundai is dropping nearly $1 billion on its “Metaplant” near the deepwater port of Savannah.
LG is building an adjacent $4.3 billion battery plant with Hyundai.
Kia erected a new EV9 SUV manufacturing line at its plant in West Point, about halfway down Georgia’s border with Alabama.
The first EV9 rolled off the line in June, less than two years after the IRA was signed into law.
source_title: How Dalton, Georgia, went from Carpet Capital to… | Canary Media

facts: Duracell Manufacturing officials announced last week the company will invest $25 million to expand its existing battery component manufacturing operations in Cleveland, Tennessee.

Duracell’s $25 million investment in Tennessee represents the company’s continued focus on providing the highest quality alkaline batteries.

facts: LaGrange is home to Kia, Interface, Jindal Films, Caterpillar, and more than a dozen other companies, widely recognized as a forerunner in industry and manufacturing.

Duracell’s LaGrange location has produced every nine-volt battery sold in North America since 1981.

The Duracell expansion will consolidate global engineering, purchasing, and customer service on a 58-acre campus and add approximately 100 jobs in the business center.

Jindal Films is continuing a $205-million project that includes expanding an existing plant and relocating its North American headquarters and research and development facilities from Rochester, N.Y., to LaGrange.
The Jindal Films relocation will bring 243 very high-end, high-paying jobs to the headquarter facilities.
Caterpillar’s global headquarters for its forestry division has operated in LaGrange for 20 years, manufacturing every piece of forestry equipment the company sells.

Duracell, Caterpillar, and Jindal have all been in LaGrange for over 20 years and collectively invested $325 million in expansions in the past year alone.
Scott Malone serves as president of the Development Authority of LaGrange.

A Korean engineer described experiencing confusion, anger, and relief after spending a week in U.S. ICE detention.
Anger mounted in South Korea as the release of workers detained in Georgia was delayed.
Latino individuals recounted that agents stormed the facility and detained everyone during a huge ICE raid at the Hyundai plant.

Although Trump offered to let detained Korean workers stay in the U.S., nearly all are heading home.

facts: The Trump administration announced a massive 25-percent tariff on all vehicles assembled outside the United States.
Hyundai Motor Group celebrated the grand opening of its new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) production facility in Georgia.
The plant will eventually produce up to 500,000 hybrid and electric vehicles per year.
Initial plans called for 300,000 vehicles per year to be built at HMGMA.
HMGMA will produce hybrid and electric vehicles for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.
Hyundai has already announced that its Ioniq 5 crossover will be produced at HMGMA.
Hyundai has already announced that its upcoming Ioniq 9 three-row crossover will be produced at HMGMA for the 2026 model year.
Kia has not confirmed a vehicle for the facility yet, but Kia will start building at the HMGMA facility in 2026.
Hyundai Group announced the deal to build the plant in Georgia in May 2022.
The announcement came months before the Biden administration's Inflation Reduction Act passed that incentivized domestic production of EVs.
Kia's KMMG plant in Georgia already manufactures the brand's three best-selling SUVs (Telluride, Sorento, Sportage) as well as the K5 sedan and the EV9 electric three-row crossover.
Hyundai builds its best-selling crossovers, the Tucson and the Santa Fe, along with the Santa Cruz and Genesis Electrified GV70 at its HMMA plant in Alabama.
Hyundai Motor Company was founded in 1967 and is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea.
Hyundai emphasizes investment in eco-friendly technologies, such as hydrogen fuel cell and electric vehicles.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) accelerated its official start of production to the fourth quarter of 2024, with a grand opening celebration targeted for the first quarter of 2025.
The start of production date was previously announced as the first quarter of 2025.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty A. Kemp celebrated Hyundai Motor Group's positive impact on the state alongside company leaders during an event marking Feb. 26, 2024 as Hyundai Day.
President and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America José Muñoz participated in the celebration.
The Georgia General Assembly passed a Resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group's dedication to the state and officially recognizing Feb. 26, 2024, as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles on display outside the State Capitol included the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, and Electrified GV70.
A hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck was displayed for use in logistics between Hyundai's manufacturing facilities and suppliers.
José Muñoz stated that Hyundai's investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great paying American jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
Governor Brian Kemp noted Hyundai helps recruit businesses, gives back to communities, and works with world-class colleges and universities to connect Georgians with opportunities.

Oscar Kwon, president and CEO of HMGMA, called the project the largest economic development project in Georgia's history and credited the "Meta Pros" and state/local partners including the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Joint Development Authority.

Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones, House Speaker Jon Burns, legislative leaders, Department of Economic Development Director Pat Wilson, and local officials from the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority attended the celebration.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $7.59 billion to develop HMGMA, which will create 8,500 direct jobs.
Hyundai and SK On are building a $5 billion EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia that will employ 3,500 workers.
A Center for Automotive Research study projects Hyundai's investments in Georgia will create nearly 40,000 jobs and generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings annually.
HMGMA continues hiring efforts and has developed educational partnerships with area colleges to create an employment pipeline.
The Electric Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate (EVP TCC) is being offered at four area technical colleges as part of this collaboration.
Students who complete the EVP TCC program receive preferred status when applying for positions at HMGMA.
Hyundai is actively developing a hydrogen mobility value chain at HMGMA, first announced at CES 2024.
Through the Clean Logistics Project in Georgia, the Metaplant will utilize XCIENT Fuel Cell tractors to manage logistics between the plant and its suppliers.
The XCIENT is Hyundai's commercialized class 8 6x4 fuel cell electric model with a proven record of real-world application and over 6.25 million accumulated miles globally.
Hyundai is developing hydrogen refueling infrastructure needed to support the fuel cell activity at HMGMA.
This hydrogen ecosystem collaboration is anticipated to stimulate economic growth and create job opportunities within Georgia.
HMGMA is Hyundai Motor Group's first dedicated electric vehicle mass-production plant, located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The facility will feature a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem.
The Georgia facility will operate as an intelligent manufacturing plant optimizing order collection, procurement, logistics, and production using AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system aims to create a human-centered work environment where robots assist human workers.

Hyundai Motor America supports Hyundai's Progress for Humanity vision with significant U.S. operations including its North American headquarters in California, the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama assembly plant, HMGMA in development in Georgia, and several R&D facilities.
Hyundai's combined U.S. operations and 835 independent dealers contribute $20.1 billion annually and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy.

facts: The Hyundai Ioniq 5 SUV will be the first electric vehicle to roll off the production line of the company’s new $7.5 billion manufacturing campus in Georgia.
The Ioniq series has been a hit in the US and globally, with the Ioniq 5 SUV pulling the rug out from under EV market naysayers despite some glitches in the US market last year.
EV sales began to pick up in January 2024 and momentum has continued globally, with the Ioniq 5 serving as Exhibit A in the rebound.

According to a Hyundai press release dated June 18, the IONIQ 5’s sales numbers prove its popularity, making it Hyundai’s highest volume EV with sales up 43% so far in 2024 and an increase of 82% in May alone.
The new campus is named Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) and is located in Ellabell, Georgia.
HMGMA will host the production of the 2025 Ioniq 5, which has yet to be officially unveiled for North America.
HMGMA is currently training and preparing to begin production in the fourth quarter of this year (2024).
The new plant will produce all trim lines except IONIQ 5 N and will eventually be the sole facility building IONIQ 5 models for the U.S. market.
Hyundai is investing $7.59 billion to develop the campus, with the expectation of creating 8,500 new direct jobs.
Hyundai and SK On are building a $5 billion EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia that will employ 3,500 workers.

A study from the Center for Automotive Research calculated that Hyundai’s electric vehicle investments in Georgia will create almost 40,000 jobs, with an annual figure for individual earnings of an estimated $4.6 billion.

The state of Georgia and local governments are on track to give $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai Motor Group after the automaker announced an additional $2 billion investment at its EV complex last month (September).

Associated Press calculations show projected incentives will rise by more than $290 million from the $1.8 billion deal originally announced last year, with only $2.75 million representing additional cash from the state and the rest coming from increases in tax breaks.
Georgia has a longstanding school-to-factory worker training and hiring program that attracts manufacturers.
Georgia is a “right to work” state under regulations passed in 1947, making it difficult for workers to unionize and allowing employers to enjoy a more compliant workforce.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) states that building on the assets that make the state’s automotive industry successful, Georgia is rapidly developing as the capital of EV manufacturing for the nation.
In addition to Hyundai’s venture, GDEcD notes that the Kia facility in West Point will begin making the company’s new EV9 model this year (2024).

Rivian announced plans for a $5 billion factory in Georgia back in 2021, but the project got well into the site preparation phase before switching gears to focus on its EV production facility in Illinois, though characterized as temporary.

GDEcD notes that Georgia is rapidly expanding across multiple EV supply chain segments: cathode and anode manufacturing, chemical and mineral solutions, battery manufacturing, metals and aluminum manufacturing, and battery recycling.

Energy storage startup Freyr Battery Inc. announced earlier this year that it is relocating its home base back to the US from Europe, citing global tax developments and U.S. incentive programs for battery manufacturers as a key advantage.
Freyr’s new Giga America factory site selection was announced in 2022 in Coweta County, Georgia.
