The initial phase of Giga America is planned to be a cell production module of approximately 34 GWh based on the next-generation of 24M’s U.S.-based SemiSolidTM production platform at an initial projected capital cost of $1.7 billion.

Freyr name-checked the Inflation Reduction Act as a key element in support of the project, stating that with its passage, they expect U.S. demand for ESS, passenger EV and other electric mobility applications to grow rapidly over the next decade.

The total package of financial incentives for Giga America includes robust assistance collectively provided by The State of Georgia and Coweta County in addition to expected production tax credits associated with the recently passed Inflation Reduction Act.
A corporate update earlier this year indicated that plans for the Giga America battery factory are moving forward.

Georgia state legislators have been working to pass bills aimed at blocking financial firms that ascribe to ESG investing principles, even while their own economic development agencies invest millions of public dollars to attract new clean tech businesses.
source_title: Money Talks: The Electric Vehicle Revolution Trumps Red State Politics - CleanTechnica

facts: LG Energy Solution announced on Nov. 21 that the name of the joint venture for building the Georgia joint plant with Hyundai Motor is "HLBMA LLC."
Until now, the joint venture had been called "LGES-HMG Battery JV."
The two companies signed a contract in 2023 regarding the establishment of a battery joint venture in North America to build the "HL-GA Battery Company," a joint battery plant in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group's U.S. unit "HMG Global LLC" and LG Energy Solution's Michigan branch will jointly invest $4.3 billion (about 6.3275 trillion won).
Each partner will hold 50% equity in the joint venture.
The joint plant is being built to a scale capable of mass-producing about 30 gigawatt-hours (GWh) annually.
The annual capacity is enough for battery cells for about 300,000 electric vehicles.
The joint plant sits at one end of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) site.
The HMGMA site spans about 3 million pyeong in Ellabell, Georgia.
EV battery cells produced at the joint plant will be moved to Hyundai Mobis within the HMGMA site.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble the cells into battery packs.
The assembled battery packs will be supplied in full to electric vehicles produced at Hyundai Motor's Alabama plant.
The battery packs will also be supplied to Kia's Georgia plant and other U.S. plants of Hyundai Motor Group.
Initially, the two companies aimed to start production at the end of 2025.

The plant construction plan is facing setbacks due to a Sept. 4 sweep by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) for undocumented immigrants at the construction site.
During the Sept. 4 sweep, U.S. immigration authorities arrested a total of 475 people and detained them for seven days.
Of the arrested personnel, 317 were from Korea.
The Korean detainees included 47 from LG Energy Solution, 210 employees of LG Energy Solution partners, and 60 from Hyundai Engineering.
The remainder of the arrested personnel were foreign nationals.
source_title: Hyundai Motor and LG Energy Solution name Georgia battery joint venture HLBMA LLC - CHOSUNBIZ

facts: Construction of Hyundai’s newest electric vehicle manufacturing site began in January 2023 on 3,000 acres of clear-cut land just south of I-16 in Ellabell, Georgia.

Hyundai officials aim to bring their first electric vehicle off the assembly line by the end of 2024 to make vehicles eligible for buyer incentives under the Biden Administration’s 2024 Inflation Reduction Act.
HMGMA is a newly formed entity contracted with Georgia to receive $2.1 billion in tax deferments, subsidized construction costs, and other perks.
HMGMA has promised to create 8,500 jobs together with other Hyundai affiliates on the site.
Seven Hyundai factories are rising ahead of schedule from the Ellabell site.
At least 20 traumatic injuries occurred at the site since construction began in January 2023 through May 2024, according to emergency services and fire department records.
Thirteen of these injuries occurred in the first six months of 2024, including two falls, two head strikes from equipment, and four vehicle accidents.
Many of the traumatic injuries required emergency airlifts off the site, and one resulted in a worker’s death.
OSHA lists only 10 reports regarding accidents on the site between January 2023 and May 2024, creating a discrepancy with emergency services records.
On April 29, 2023, construction worker Victor Javier Gamboa fell roughly 60 feet from a third-story steel beam at the HMGMA paintshop site.
Gamboa was a subcontractor employed by Eastern Constructors who supported five children and a fiancee in Statesboro.
His arrest lanyard was severed by a steel I-beam during the fall, and EMS was unsuccessful in saving him; he was pronounced dead 1 hour and 42 minutes after EMS arrived.
OSHA ruled that Eastern Constructors committed both a serious and willful violation of labor law for failing to inspect lifesaving equipment and allowing use of a defective body harness.
OSHA fined Eastern Constructors over $160,000 and added the firm to its Severe Violator Enforcement Program.
Eastern Constructors contested the fine in November; the case remains open.
HMGMA ended work with Eastern Constructors following the OSHA ruling and confirmed on Aug. 8 that the company was removed from the site.
On Feb. 16, 2024, EMS airlifted a worker who fell 15 to 20 feet from a platform after experiencing significant delays in locating him on the sprawling site.
The injured worker was found in a stupor-like state with blood leaking from his head and left ear, with no safety harness visible.
Site workers encircled EMS personnel, shouting to hurry and trying to touch the patient, which EMS reported posed a significant delay in care.
The worker was transferred via LifeStar helicopter to Memorial Health University Medical Center in Savannah.
OSHA opened an inspection into a fall incident at a HMGMA assembly building on Feb. 21, but it remains unclear if it relates to the Feb. 16 incident.
Two other workers fell and were injured on Sept. 23, 2023, and March 21, 2024; one fell from a crane and the other was flung from a boom lift 30 feet in the air.
Neither of these fall incidents appears in OSHA inspection data.

Five other traumatic injuries involved workers being struck by heavy objects, including a steel beam cutting a shin in October 2023, a mangled leg in December 2023, a metal pipe strike to the eyes in January, a forklift leg injury in December 2023, and a scissor lift foot crush in February 2024.
On May 31, a 40-year-old Korean-speaking technician employed by SFA Engineering Corp. was trapped in a newly installed conveyor belt at the Hyundai GLOVIS logistics facility.
The technician suffered uncontrollable bleeding from his left thigh, a deformed right hand with stripped skin, and a crushed chest possibly with a collapsed lung.

First responders arrived 15 minutes after a manager called 911, placed two tourniquets, and performed a decompression needle procedure before airlifting him to Memorial Hospital via LifeStar helicopter.
On June 3, HMGMA stated the man was treated for severe internal and external injuries and was in stable condition.
OSHA classified the May 31 injury as an “amputation” and ended its onsite inspection on June 5, but had not issued violations or fines two months later.
HMGMA confirmed the technician was still receiving medical treatment.
Former and current site safety managers requested anonymity, stating workplace safety standards are among the worst they have experienced in their careers due to organizational chaos.
A former safety manager witnessed workers not wearing basic safety equipment like hard hats or appropriate footwear on multiple days and noted safety orientation lasted only a few minutes.
The pace of work created “an atmosphere of fear and frustration,” according to the former manager.
HMGMA spokesperson Bianca Johnson stated that each of the seven Hyundai factories is independently responsible for reporting construction worker injuries to OSHA.

Johnson wrote that HMGMA provides standard site orientation covering PPE and accident reporting, holds weekly safety team meetings with affiliates, and maintains an on-site Emergency Response Team trained in Medical, Fire, and Spill Response.
OSHA levied fines against two subcontractors: Eastern Constructors Incorporated (over $160,000) and Sungwon Georgia Corp. ($22,000), both of which are contesting the fines.
OSHA fined Sungwon Georgia Corp. $22,000 for exposing workers to 15-foot fall hazards without an anchored fall arrest system; Sungwon is appealing the fine.
The Current reached out to all seven Hyundai companies regarding safety and only received a response from HMGMA.
OSHA’s Savannah office did not return multiple phone calls seeking comment about safety standards or oversight of contractors at the site.
source_title: Worker injuries mount as giant Hyundai project nears completion - The Current

facts: Rivian Automotive leaders are confident as the company starts long-delayed work on a $5 billion facility in Georgia.
The California-based money-losing company breaks ground Tuesday east of Atlanta despite President Donald Trump’s successful push to roll back electric vehicle tax credits.
Starting Sept. 30, buyers will no longer qualify for savings of up to $7,500 per car.
Rivian Chief Policy Officer Alan Hoffman said the company believes it can sell electric vehicles not for environmental or tax incentive reasons, but because they're superior.
Hoffman stated: “We did not build this company based upon federal tax incentives,” and “And we’re going to prove that we’re going to be successful in the future.”
The Georgia plant was first announced in 2021 and is Rivian’s key to reaching profitability.
Currently, Rivian makes the high-end R1T pickup truck and the R1S sport utility vehicle in Normal, Illinois, as well as delivery vans for Amazon and others.
Rivian's truck prices start at $71,000.
The Illinois plant will begin making smaller R2 SUVs next year, with prices starting at $45,000.
An expanded Illinois plant will be able to assemble 215,000 vehicles yearly.
If the R2 is a hit and Rivian successfully produces an even smaller R3, it will need more capacity.
The company has said the Georgia operation will be able to make 200,000 vehicles yearly starting in 2028.
Rivian plans another 200,000 in capacity in phase two, volume that would spread fixed costs over many more vehicles.
These projections would be a big leap from the 40,000 to 46,000 vehicles Rivian expects to deliver this year, down from 52,000 last year.
The company says it’s limiting production now in part to launch 2026 models.

Alex Oyler, North American director of auto research firm SBD Automotive, said: “For Rivian, it’s do-or-die time,” and noted that Tesla showed the key to profitability is scale, and you can’t scale if your cheapest vehicle is $70,000.
Sales growth for electric vehicles in the United States is slowing, rising only 1.5% in 2025's first half, according to Cox Automotive.
Tesla accounted for almost 45% of U.S. electric vehicle sales in that period, according to Cox.
General Motors' slice of American EV sales has climbed to 13%.
Rivian had a 3% share in the first half of the year, behind Tesla and six traditional automakers.
Excluding Tesla, Rivian is the most successful of the startup automakers.
The company initially tapped a largely unfilled niche: demand for electric pickups and SUVs.
Competition now includes Ford’s F-150 Lightning and the electric Chevrolet Silverado.
After an initial public offering in 2021, Rivian shares have fallen by more than 80%, while automaker shares overall have outpaced the broader stock market.
Rivian lost $1.66 billion in 2025’s first half.
Stellantis last week canceled Ram’s electric truck program.
Ford has delayed production at a new Tennessee plant.
General Motors abandoned plans to build electric vehicles at a suburban Detroit plant.

Sam Fiorani, a vice president at AutoForecast Solutions, said: “With all the competition out there in this market and the slowing growth of EVs, it does not play in Rivian’s favor,” but added that “there still is an EV market out there.”
Georgia has pledged $1.5 billion of incentives to Rivian in exchange for 7,500 company jobs paying at least $56,000 a year on average.
Rivian can’t benefit from most incentives unless it meets employment goals, but the state is already spending $175 million to buy and grade land and improve roads.

Georgia Republican Gov. Brian Kemp, who has said he wants to make Georgia “the electric mobility capital of America,” acknowledges Rivian faces bumps but remains confident the company can fulfill its promises.
Rivian was supposed to be making trucks by now at the 2,000-acre (800-hectare) site near Social Circle, about 45 miles (70 kilometers) east of Atlanta.
As the company burned through cash in 2024, it paused construction.
German automaker Volkswagen agreed to invest $5.8 billion in Rivian in exchange for software and electrical technology.
Then-President Joe Biden's administration in November agreed to loan Rivian $6.6 billion to build the Georgia plant.
Despite the Trump administration's hostility toward EVs, Hoffman said Rivian hopes the U.S. Department of Energy will distribute the loan money, arguing it will boost domestic manufacturing.
Some neighbors oppose the plant, saying it is an inappropriate neighbor to farms and will pollute the groundwater.

Eddie Clay, who lives less than a mile away, asked: “I planned on dying and retiring on the front porch and the biggest project in Georgia has to go next door to me, of all places in the country?” and says his well water turned mud-choked after excavation at the Rivian site.

Other challenges for Rivian include tariffs costing $2,000 per vehicle, the Trump administration ending a tax-credit program that will cost the company $140 million in revenue this year, and long-term threats from low-priced, cutting-edge Chinese EVs.
Hoffman says Rivian is “in this for the long haul” and believes they can compete with anyone out there and excel once given the opportunity.
source_title: It's 'do or die' for electric vehicle maker Rivian as it breaks ground on a $5 billion plant

facts: Rivian broke ground on its Georgia plant, which had been paused in 2024.
The company held a groundbreaking ceremony for the facility in Social Circle.
Actual construction is not expected to begin until 2026.
Vehicle production at the facility is scheduled to start in 2028.
The plant will manufacture the R2 and R3 vehicle models.
The facility aims for a combined annual production capacity of 400,000 units.
Production will be split across two phases, with each phase providing 200,000 units of annual capacity.
Vehicles produced at the Georgia plant will be sold in both domestic and international markets.
The first vehicles from the Georgia facility are expected to appear in 2028, approximately two years after R2 manufacturing begins at Rivian's Normal, Illinois plant.
The facility is expected to span approximately nine million square feet on nearly 2,000 acres of land.
Rivian envisions the site will include recreational trails for employees and customers, as well as a dedicated "Rivian experience trail."
Rivian was granted a $6.6 billion loan from the Department of Energy during the waning days of the Biden Administration.
Stakeholders, including Georgia Republicans, have promised thousands of new jobs for the project.
The project is expected to create 2,000 construction jobs and 7,500 plant jobs by 2030.
Nearly 8,000 indirect jobs are also projected to be generated by the facility.
Rivian stated that the new job openings could generate over $1 billion in annual labor income.
This economic impact is expected to support suppliers, vendors, and small businesses in Jasper, Morgan, Newton and Walton Counties and the surrounding region.
Georgia's electric vehicle landscape also includes Hyundai’s Metaplant, which builds the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9.
Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe stated that the Georgia plant will cement the company's future there and help ensure America maintains its technology leadership and excellence in automobile manufacturing.

Scaringe added that the facility will support Rivian's global expansion and provide the scale necessary to get millions of future drivers in their all-electric vehicles, both in the United States and overseas.
source_title: Rivian Breaks Ground On Its EV Plant Again Without Actually Breaking Ground | Carscoops

facts: Hyundai Motor America’s newest “metaplant” in Georgia, a highly automated, smart manufacturing facility, started production of its first Kia America vehicle on June 2.
Kia, which is owned by South Korea-based Hyundai Motor Group along with Hyundai and Genesis, is assembling its new Sportage Hybrid line at the manufacturing facility.
The Sportage marks the metaplant’s first time building a Kia model and a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) powertrain.
Dubbed the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, the factory also makes Hyundai’s IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9 EV models.
The Hyundai group plans to expand the plant’s capacity to include vehicles from all three automotive brands.
Kia looks to expand the number of SUVs produced for and sold within the US market and further accelerate electrification of their entire line-up, according to a statement from the Irvine automaker.
source_title: Kia Starts Production at Hyundai Metaplant in Georgia - Orange County Business Journal

facts: Rivian will host two special events next month to mark the restart of construction of its $5 billion Georgia electric vehicle factory.

A community event is set for Sept. 14, which will be followed by a formal kickoff ceremony on Sept. 16 “in celebration of our strong partnership with Georgia and the planned resumption of construction in 2026.”
The kickoff will be attended by stakeholders and government officials including Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp.
The plant near Social Circle is expected to start production in 2028 with an annual capacity of about 200,000 units.

In March 2024, Rivian announced it was stopping construction on the Georgia factory to save money and decided to move production of its upcoming R2 SUV to its plant in Normal, Illinois, to expedite sales.
Rivian won final approval for a $6.6 billion Department of Energy loan in January.
The DOE loan funds are available through September 2028 but cannot be accessed until Rivian resumes work on the Georgia factory.

In July, Rivian announced it was opening a new East Coast headquarters in Atlanta later this year that will initially employ 100 people, but will ultimately house 500 workers to support its operations in the state.
source_title: Rivian moving forward on $5B Georgia EV plant | ESG Dive

facts: The Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG) held an electric vehicle listening session at West Georgia Technical College’s (WGTC) Callaway Conference Center in LaGrange.

Leaders from Georgia’s emerging electrification industry along with technical college administrators and faculty attended the session to discuss the future of EV manufacturing and skilled workforce demand.
Dr. Ray Perren serves as Deputy Commissioner for Technical Education at TCSG.
Perren stated this was the third of four regional listening sessions across Georgia.
Perren emphasized TCSG’s commitment to building a well-educated, globally competitive workforce through technical education.

Perren said they are there to listen to business partners regarding the future of e-vehicles and ensure they provide the workforce EV partners need so Georgia can continue to be the best place to do business.
Industry representatives from Kia Georgia, FREYR Battery, Hyundai-Transys, and Georgia Power spoke at the event.
Latesha Bailey serves as Senior Manager of Kia Georgia.
Bailey discussed labor market challenges in Georgia for attracting and retaining talent for the mobility industry.
Bailey stated upskilling the current workforce is front and center of Kia's operation.
Bailey noted the mobility industry faces additional challenges converting or diversifying existing skill sets to meet EV production needs.
Bailey emphasized that today’s vehicle technology advances quickly, making upskilling the current workforce important for EV technology success.
Ensuring existing and new workers are continually educated on fast-paced, changing technology is required.
Kia discussed developing a certification course in EV structure components, manufacturing processes, high voltage safety, charge and repair, problem-solving, and troubleshooting.
Creating a badge and database for employers of potential candidates would assist in building an EV workforce pipeline.
Kia Georgia is preparing to launch its plant’s first electric vehicle.
Bailey emphasized the importance of TCSG and WGTC on the front lines of building Georgia’s future workforce of technicians and skilled professionals for the auto industry's era of EVs.
Jason Peace serves as Senior Vice President of Business Development and Supply Chain for FREYER Battery.
Peace explained that FREYER focuses more on grid electrification and broader global electrification than specifically on EVs.
Peace stated that when discussing electrifying the U.S., they are directly competing with China.
Peace noted China has 95% of the battery production in the world and 95% production of the raw materials required for batteries.
Peace said China got a 10- to 15-year head start, and catching up requires talent from the high school system to support future plants.
FREYR Battery is a developer of clean, next-generation battery cell production capacity.
FREYR recently announced the selection and purchase of a site in Coweta County for its planned Giga America battery plant.
Peace challenged TCSG administrators and faculty to help educate high school students on future possibilities beyond traditional trades like welding or CDL.

Peace stated painting a picture of where the world is headed is impactful, as being part of future industry opportunities is a rare chance that requires getting in front of high schoolers to share the story and passion.
source_title: WGTC Hosts TCSG, Industry Partners for Electric Vehicle Listening Session – Winters Media

facts: Kia Georgia presented a $100,000 donation to the Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG) Foundation at an event in Atlanta celebrating TCSG’s top students and instructors.
Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess presented the check at TCSG’s annual Georgia Occupational Award of Leadership (GOAL) and Rick Perkins Awards banquet in Buckhead.

TCSG Commissioner Greg Dozier stated that Kia is a valued partner and remarkable corporate citizen in Georgia, and the funding will help technical college students remain on track to graduate and join Georgia’s workforce.
Since 2016, Kia Georgia has provided funding to award the state GOAL winner with a brand-new Kia as the grand prize.
The 2023 GOAL winner will receive a 2023 Kia K5, which is built at the Kia facility in West Point.
Remaining funding from the donation supports The Last Mile Fund to provide gap funding for more than 131,000 technical education students served within TCSG.

Stuart Countess remarked that TCSG undergirds workforce development by adapting to evolving technical needs of state industries and businesses, contributing to economic growth in manufacturing, logistics, and healthcare.
In 2006, Kia established a state-of-the-art production facility in West Point, Georgia, representing one of the largest single foreign investments in the history of Georgia.
The West Point facility has an annual production capacity of more than 340,000 vehicles.
Kia Georgia produces the Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV, Sportage SUV, and K5 midsize sedan at the West Point facility.
Kia recently announced plans to produce the Kia EV9 at the West Point facility, launching its first battery-electric vehicle to be produced in the U.S.
The West Point advanced manufacturing facility represents a $1.9 billion investment on a 2,200-acre site and operates 24 hours a day.

Kia Georgia’s partnership with TCSG produced a Maintenance Certification Program through collaboration with Quick Start to upskill production team members for maintenance tasks on highly automated production equipment.
The Maintenance Certification Program was expanded to West Georgia Technical College and integrated with the campus's standard offerings.
The program provides a steady stream of qualified candidates to fill maintenance roles at Kia Georgia and other local manufacturers.
source_title: Kia Georgia Donates $100,000 to Technical College System of Georgia Foundation • The Georgia Virtue

facts: Gov. Brian Kemp announced two more Korean suppliers for the Hyundai electric-vehicle plant in Bryan County, bringing the tally of supplier-announced jobs to about 4,000.
The two most recent arrivals represent the prospect of 1,140 new positions for the state.
On March 6, PHA said it would invest $67 million to put a new factory in Chatham County, creating more than 400 jobs.
PHA is a tier-one supplier whose door modules, latches, locks and hinges go directly from its factories into the original-equipment manufacturer’s assembly line.
The OEM assembly line is the Hyundai “Metaplant” that has committed $5.54 billion to coastal Georgia.
PHA operates a U.S. headquarters in Auburn Hills, Mich., and serves Kia as well as other brands beyond Hyundai.
Founded in 1985, PHA is based in Daegu, the Atlanta sister city located at the center of a South Korean auto parts manufacturing belt in southern South Korea.
PHA also has facilities in Vietnam, China, Slovakia, the Czech Republic, India and elsewhere.
PHA's new location will be one of multiple sites in the United States, located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center site is less than two miles off Interstate 16 and has been vetted through the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) process.
GRAD certification includes due diligence on zoning, environmental issues and endangered species investigation, utility services, wetlands delineation.
More than 8,000 jobs have been created on GRAD sites throughout the state.
A few weeks before the PHA announcement, longtime Kia Georgia supplier Sewon America announced it would invest $300 million for a stamped body components plant in Rincon.
The Sewon America investment will create 740 jobs and bring its total employment in the state to 1,600.
Sewon America is a subsidiary of South Korea-based Sewon Precision Industry Co.
Sewon America opened its first Georgia plant in LaGrange in 2008 with a $170 million investment and expanded twice to meet growing demand from the nearby Kia plant.
The most recent investment in the LaGrange facility was a $160 million infusion in 2018, less than a year before Gov. Brian Kemp headed to Korea on his first economic development mission abroad.

Gov. Brian Kemp said in a news release that on his very first international trip as governor to Korea, he congratulated Chairman Moon-ki Kim on the company’s then-recent expansion and discussed how they could continue to work together.
Gov. Brian Kemp expressed pride that those discussions led to the announcement as Sewon America continues to grow its Georgia operations.
With Sewon’s Effingham County factory, all four counties that banded together to create the 2,900-acre site that wooed Hyundai have now received supplier investments.
The $1.8 billion in commitments announced is already $800 million more than the $1 billion Hyundai initially said its suppliers would bring.
Rincon Mayor Ken Lee said in the governor’s release that the Sewon project will be the largest in the city’s history.
The Sewon project will be located in the Grande View Industrial Park.
Sewon is also based in Daegu and was founded in 1989.
source_title: Two Hyundai Suppliers From Atlanta’s Korean Sister City Adding 1,140 More Jobs in Georgia - Global Atlanta

facts: Kia Georgia, Inc. has re-opened its online application process for hourly production positions for the first time since completing a third wave of hiring at the West Point automotive facility in 2011.
This marks the first time hourly production positions have been available for direct hires at the plant since then.
Chris Miller, Kia Georgia's chief administrative officer, stated that the team has become synonymous with success since launching the Sorento in November 2009.
The company is adding to a team that has set new standards for safety, quality, and efficiency.
Hiring coincides with the recent launch of Plan S and initiatives following a dramatic brand shift during the next decade.
Kia Georgia produces the award-winning and top-selling Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV, and K5 mid-size sedan.
To date, Kia Georgia has produced almost 3.5 million vehicles.
The facility offers pay, medical coverage, a 401K matching program, and paid holidays as part of its benefits package.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Seoul-based Kia Corporation.
The plant has an annual production capacity of 340,000 units.
The facility is located on 2,200 acres in West Point.
Kia Georgia began mass production on November 16, 2009.

links.context: Application portal for hourly production positions

source_title: Kia Georgia reopens direct hiring process for all hourly pro...

facts: SK is laying off nearly 1,000 workers at its Georgia plant.
The layoffs are occurring amid cooling automaker EV plans.
Supply chain layoffs are spreading across warehouses, factories, and rail terminals.
The report on SK's layoffs was dated March 6.
A related report on industry-wide supply chain layoffs was dated March 12.
source_title: Google News - Overview

facts: Hyundai’s huge EV manufacturing facility in Georgia became the latest target of the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown last week, with federal agents detaining 475 workers, most of them from South Korea.
The raid has delayed the opening of the complex’s battery factory, which the automaker is building with LG Energy Solution in the Southeast’s growing “battery belt.”

Experts, including South Korea’s president, have warned the move could have a much broader chilling effect on foreign investment in U.S. factories — much of which has flowed to clean energy projects in recent years.
Hyundai broke ground on its Georgia complex three years ago after securing $2.1 billion in subsidies from the state and nearby counties, with strong support from Republican Gov. Brian Kemp.
That investment came with conditions, namely that Hyundai and its suppliers would hire at least 8,500 long-term workers by 2031.
Immigration and Customs Enforcement alleges those arrested were working illegally.
An attorney for several detained South Koreans says they have valid visas and were only working for a short time to get the facility’s battery operations up and running.
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung defended the workers in a Thursday statement.

South Korean President Lee Jae Myung stated: “When you build a factory or install equipment at a factory, you need technicians. But the United States doesn’t have that workforce, and yet they won’t issue visas to let our people stay and do the work.”

South Korean President Lee Jae Myung added: “If that’s not possible, then establishing a local factory in the United States will either come with severe disadvantages or become very difficult for our companies. They will wonder whether they should even do it.”
Georgia is home to about 100 Korean-owned facilities employing 17,000 people.
These include an SK Battery America EV battery factory, Hanwha Qcells’ solar panel plant, and a Kia EV manufacturing facility.
Last week’s raid is already having tangible ripple effects on U.S. manufacturing.
Reuters reports that South Korean workers at other LG Energy Solution production sites and an LG/General Motors plant are preparing to depart due to visa worries — or already have.
source_title: Trump’s immigration crackdown hits clean energy | Canary Media

facts: U.S. Senator Reverend Raphael Warnock released a statement regarding the announcement that SK Battery America is laying off over 900 workers at its Commerce, Georgia plant.
Warnock expressed sympathy for affected workers and emphasized the importance of these jobs in making Georgia a leader in advanced manufacturing.
In his statement, Warnock criticized the current administration for prioritizing politics over people and urged for the protection of clean energy investments that create jobs.
He referred to these layoffs as resulting from increasing manufacturing costs due to harmful tariffs.
Warnock highlighted his ongoing efforts to support clean energy policies and domestic manufacturing.
He mentioned that historic policies he helped pass have led to job growth in Georgia's clean energy sector, including significant electrification initiatives and tax credits for electric vehicles.
source_title: Press Release: Senator Raphael Warnock Responds to Over 900 Layoffs at SK Battery Plant in Georgia | Quiver Quantitative

facts: Nearly 500 workers were taken in a raid at Hyundai’s battery plant.
An attorney stated that detained Korean Hyundai workers had special skills for short-term jobs.
South Korean workers returned home to cheers a week after the U.S. immigration raid.
Seoul stated that Trump offered to allow South Korean detainees to stay and train U.S. workers.
South Korea confirmed a charter plane carrying detained workers would leave Atlanta on Thursday at noon.
South Koreans detained in the U.S. immigration raid arrived back home to applause and protests.
Sources reported that South Korean workers departed from additional LGES U.S. battery sites following the raid.
A detained Korean engineer described experiencing confusion, anger, and relief during his week of U.S. ICE detention.
Anger mounted in South Korea as the release of workers detained in Georgia was delayed.
Latinos recounted a huge ICE raid at the Hyundai plant where officers stormed the facility and detained everyone.
The immigration raid exposed tensions spanning Seoul, Washington, and rural Georgia.
Workers stated that Korean companies were warned about questionable U.S. visas before the Hyundai raid.
The raid on the Georgia EV battery plant raises new risks for a shaken industry.
The Hyundai raid will delay construction of the Georgia battery plant by months.
After the ICE raid, Trump expressed a desire for detained Korean workers to stay in the U.S.
Although Trump offered to let detained Korean workers stay in the U.S., almost all are heading home.
South Korea’s president stated the Georgia ICE raid could have a ‘considerable impact’ on direct U.S. investment from his country.
Detained South Korean workers were delayed but ultimately departed on their way home.
source_title: Google News - News about South Korea • US - Overview

facts: U.S. President Donald Trump warned foreign companies to hire and train American workers following the Immigration Enforcement Operation on South Korean carmaker Hyundai Motor Company’s battery plant in Georgia.
Trump said in a post on Truth Social that he is calling on all Foreign Companies investing in the United States to please respect the Nation’s Immigration Laws.

Trump encouraged foreign companies to LEGALLY bring very smart people with great technical talent to build World Class products, and stated that what is asked in return is that they hire and train American Workers.
Last week, U.S. federal agents detained 475 people including about 300 from South Korea at the battery plant construction site in Georgia.
U.S. authorities said these people were “illegally” working at the battery plant in Georgia.

In a statement on September 5, 2025, Hyundai Motor Company said it is aware of the immigration enforcement action that took place at the construction site of its supplier, the HL-GA Battery Company in Bryan County, Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Company stated it is closely monitoring the situation while working to understand the specific circumstances.
Based on current understanding, none of those detained is directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.
Hyundai Motor India said it is committed to full compliance with all laws and regulations in every market where it operates.
This includes employment verification requirements and immigration laws, and Hyundai expects the same commitment from all partners, suppliers, contractors, and subcontractors.
The safety and well-being of everyone working at sites, whether directly employed or working for suppliers, is a top priority.
Incidents like this remind us of the importance of robust oversight throughout the entire supply chain and contractor network.
Hyundai said it is reviewing its processes to ensure that all parties working on its projects maintain high standards of legal compliance.
This includes thorough vetting of employment practices by contractors and subcontractors.
The South Korean auto major said it will conduct an investigation to ensure all suppliers and their subcontractors comply with all laws and regulations.

As the company continues to invest in American manufacturing and create thousands of jobs, it will do so in full accordance with U.S. law and in a manner that reflects values of treating all people with dignity and respect.
source_title: Trump warns foreign firms to hire and train U.S. workers after Hyundai battery plant raid

facts: Hyundai Mobis unveiled a new 160-kilowatt power electric (PE) system designed for mass-market electric vehicles.
The PE system marks a significant expansion of the company's electrification portfolio and drivetrain technology capabilities.
Hyundai Mobis is focusing on modular and standardized EV drive systems that can be applied across multiple vehicle models to improve scalability, manufacturing efficiency, and overall profitability.
The platform-based approach is intended to support faster production and broader application across different EV segments.

The development strengthens the company’s ambitions to become a larger global supplier of integrated EV powertrain solutions as demand for advanced electrification technologies continues to rise worldwide.
The new PE system is designed for scalable, modular EV production and includes a motor, inverter, and reduction gear.
Specific power improved by around 16% and the system volume was reduced by nearly 20%.
Plans are underway for a 120-kW compact EV system.
Hyundai Mobis introduced the new 160-kW PE system as another step to strengthen its position within the global EV supply chain amid intensifying automotive industry competition.
The development reflects a broader strategy of expanding beyond battery systems into integrated electric powertrain technologies.
The PE system functions as a core component of an electric vehicle, performing a role similar to the powertrain in internal combustion engine vehicles.
It combines several essential drivetrain technologies, including a motor, inverter, and reduction gear, into a unified system that powers EV movement and energy conversion.

While previously focusing largely on manufacturing PE systems based on customer orders, Hyundai Mobis has now expanded its in-house research and development capabilities to independently design complete drive systems.
This transition represents a strategic shift from contract-based manufacturing toward greater technological ownership and product differentiation.
The company expects the move to strengthen profitability by combining proprietary design capabilities with mass-production capacity.
This allows Hyundai Mobis to capture greater value across the EV supply chain while improving its competitiveness as automakers increasingly seek integrated electrification solutions from suppliers.
A major focus during development was the standardization and modularization of core components, including shared architectures for the motor stator, inverter, and semiconductor-based power modules.

This modular approach enables the system to function as a scalable platform that can be applied across multiple vehicle models rather than requiring entirely new powertrain designs for each EV launch.

The platform-style structure differs from the more traditional approach used by many global automakers, where component technologies are often developed separately with multiple suppliers before being integrated into a complete system.
Hyundai Mobis’ strategy instead emphasizes a more unified development process that combines system design and manufacturing under a single framework.

As EV model counts continue to rise globally, modular and scalable drive systems are expected to become increasingly valuable for manufacturers seeking faster production timelines, lower engineering costs, and improved flexibility across vehicle segments.
Despite being designed as a more universal platform, the new PE system delivers notable performance improvements.
Specific power, measured as output relative to system weight, improved by approximately 16%, while the system’s overall volume was reduced by nearly 20%.
These gains were achieved through a combination of modular engineering, standardized components, and new cooling technologies applied to the motor structure.
The company also developed a power module utilizing advanced power semiconductors designed to maximize energy efficiency and improve thermal management.

Reducing size and weight while increasing output is considered particularly important in EV development, as lighter and more compact systems can contribute to improved vehicle efficiency, driving range, and packaging flexibility.
The 160-kW PE system delivers output equivalent to approximately 215 horsepower in internal combustion engine terms, making it suitable for most electric vehicles currently in mass production.
Hyundai Mobis noted that installing dual PE systems on both front and rear axles could effectively double output, enabling application in higher-performance vehicles.
The latest announcement builds on Hyundai Mobis’ broader electrification strategy and follows the company’s earlier development of a high-performance 250-kW PE system last year.

That previous system was designed for more powerful EV applications and focused on achieving advantages in areas such as motor torque, maximum output, and cooling performance, targeting higher-performance segments within the electric vehicle market.
At the same time, the company is developing a smaller 120-kW PE system tailored for compact vehicles and emerging market applications, with completion expected within the first half of the year.

Compared with the larger models, the compact version emphasizes reduced size, lower weight, and stronger price competitiveness, making it better suited for smaller EV platforms and cost-sensitive markets.

By developing multiple PE systems across different power ranges, Hyundai Mobis is creating a more comprehensive electrification portfolio capable of serving a wider variety of vehicle categories and customer needs.
This approach allows the company to target everything from compact urban mobility solutions to mainstream passenger EVs and high-performance electric vehicles.
Once completed, Hyundai Mobis will effectively have a full lineup of EV drive systems covering a broad range of applications.

The expanded portfolio is expected to improve the company’s ability to meet varying customer requirements while supporting more differentiated product strategies across different global markets and vehicle segments.
Hyundai Mobis plans to proactively market the newly developed PE systems to global automotive customers, with some overseas manufacturers already reportedly expressing interest in the technology.
The expansion into integrated drive systems significantly broadens Hyundai Mobis’ electrification portfolio beyond battery systems, where it has already secured international contracts.

By adding proprietary PE systems to its offerings, the company is strengthening its position as a supplier capable of delivering more complete electrification solutions rather than individual standalone components.

The move comes as competition among EV suppliers intensifies globally, with automakers increasingly seeking strategic partnerships capable of providing integrated electrification technologies at scale.
Suppliers that can deliver complete systems—including motors, inverters, semiconductors, and drive units—may gain advantages in manufacturing coordination, cost efficiency, and development speed.

By strengthening both design and production capabilities internally, Hyundai Mobis is positioning itself as a more comprehensive EV solutions provider capable of supporting multiple vehicle platforms and customer requirements across international markets.
This broader capability could help the company expand relationships with global automakers as the transition toward electric mobility continues to accelerate.
The PE system expansion aligns with Hyundai Mobis’ broader investment strategy in electrification infrastructure and advanced EV technologies.

The company previously secured approximately $940 million in green-loan funding tied to North American battery system and PE system production facilities as part of a wider $1.3 billion electrification investment plan running through 2030.

These investments highlight the scale of capital being directed toward EV supply chain development as suppliers compete to strengthen their positions within the rapidly evolving electric mobility market.

As global automakers accelerate EV production and expand model lineups, demand for integrated drivetrain systems, battery technologies, and advanced electrification platforms is expected to continue growing.
For Hyundai Mobis, expanding into scalable drive systems strengthens its position not only as a traditional parts supplier but also as a developer of core EV technologies.

By combining design, engineering, and manufacturing capabilities internally, the company is aiming to expand its role within the global electrification ecosystem while improving long-term competitiveness and profitability.
Hyundai Mobis’ latest 160-kW PE system highlights the growing importance of modular and integrated powertrain technologies within the global EV market.

As automakers continue expanding electric vehicle production across multiple segments, scalable drive systems capable of supporting diverse platforms are becoming increasingly important for improving manufacturing efficiency, reducing development costs, and speeding up production timelines.

By combining modular engineering, standardized components, and improved energy efficiency, Hyundai Mobis is positioning itself to compete more aggressively within the global electrification supply chain.

The company’s expansion beyond battery systems into complete EV drive systems reflects a broader trend among automotive suppliers seeking greater control over high-value electrification technologies and integrated vehicle systems.

Looking ahead, Hyundai Mobis’ ability to secure additional international customers and successfully scale production across its expanding PE system lineup will likely play a key role in strengthening its long-term competitiveness within the rapidly evolving electric vehicle market, where demand for efficient and scalable EV technologies is expected to continue rising.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis Expands EV Powertrain Push With New PE System

facts: Hyundai Mobis Co., South Korea's leading auto parts maker, plans to build a 90 billion-won (US$65.8 million) electric vehicle (EV) module plant in the country's southeastern industrial city of Ulsan.
The company signed a memorandum of understanding with the city government of Ulsan for the factory.
The factory is envisioned to be built near a dedicated EV plant by Hyundai Motor Co. currently under construction in the city.
Hyundai Mobis plans to supply chassis modules, driver seat modules and other components for Genesis EV models produced by Hyundai through this factory.
The company aims to complete construction of the plant by the second half of 2025.
Domestically, Hyundai Mobis operates battery system factories in Ulsan, Daegu, Cheongju and Pyeongtaek.
Domestically, Hyundai Mobis operates power electric system factories in Ulsan, Daegu, Cheongju and Pyeongtaek.
Overseas, Hyundai Mobis runs battery system plants in China for supply to Hyundai Motor Group.
Overseas, Hyundai Mobis runs battery system plants in the Czech Republic for supply to Hyundai Motor Group.
Hyundai Mobis is currently constructing a new battery system factory in Indonesia.
Hyundai Mobis is establishing production bases to supply parts to Hyundai Motor Group's new EV plant under construction in the U.S. state of Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis to build EV module plant in Ulsan | Yonhap News Agency

facts: Hyundai Mobis plans to invest $926 million in an Electric Vehicle (EV) Power Electric system plant in Bryan County that will eventually employ at least 1,500 people.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that celebrating the groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County would unleash transformational job creation and investment in that region of the state.
The announcement marks the second supplier to locate in the area within two weeks.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing facility with almost 1,200 employees in West Point since 2009.
The West Point plant is the largest Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia and supplies automotive manufacturers with complete cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies.

H.S. Oh, Vice President of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit, said the Bryan County investment reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia’s auto industry.
Hyundai Mobis aims to be a major production player in the EV market, which will trigger more growth within the sector and provide high-quality work opportunities to the growing local workforce.
Once it reaches full production, the 1,200,000-square-foot facility will supply over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems annually.

The facility will also supply 450,000 Integrated Charging Control Units annually to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Bryan County, Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA) in Montgomery, and the Kia Georgia plant.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin as early as January 2023.
Production at the new facility is expected to begin in 2024.
The facility will be located at the GRAD Certified Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.

Development Authority of Bryan County Chairman Jon Seagraves noted the site's close proximity to available housing in nearby Heartwood, a top-performing public school system, interstate access, and rail.
The site for the new facility was purchased about two years ago.
Jon Seagraves expressed gratitude to Bryan County, the City of Richmond Hill, and Raydient Places for their support and partnership on the project.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The GDEcD partnered with the Development Authority of Bryan County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power for this project.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that suppliers like Hyundai Mobis are transitioning to the jobs of the future and becoming part of Georgia’s growing EV ecosystem.
In May, officials predicted that non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia.
That prediction was blown past in about two weeks.
GDEcD is working with partners like Georgia Quick Start and sister agencies to prepare Georgia’s workforce of tomorrow for the jobs being created now.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground on October 25 in Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers connected to the HMGMA project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: Second Global Automotive Supplier for Hyundai Metaplant To Create 1,500 Jobs - Expansion Solutions

facts: Presented an expansion strategy in the North American market with its highly value-added technologies at the NAIAS 2022 Press Conference.
Plans to expand production facilities worldwide, including a new EV components plant for prompt supply to North American automakers.
Aims to reach about 30-40% CAGR in the North American market by 2030 with future mobility technologies.

Hyundai Mobis (KRX 012330), the sixth largest global automotive supplier, introduced its expansion strategy in the North American market during a press conference at the North American International Auto Show (NAIAS).
According to Axel Maschka, Executive Vice President and head of Hyundai Mobis Global OE Sales, the two keywords of the company's growth strategy are Plant Expansion and Value Expansion.
Plans to expand its production facilities across the globe for prompt and timely supply to global automakers.
Plans to construct more EV dedicated sites in the region and invest in EV components and battery system assemblies to allow rapid and environmentally optimized local production.
Currently operates 44 production bases, with North American facilities located in Alabama, Georgia, Ohio, and Michigan.
Considers North America leading the electric vehicle shift to achieve carbon neutrality and green mobility as integral to its electrification production expansion plan.
Believes having a strong production capacity will be key to winning sales orders as the EV market continues to grow.
Plans to leverage a new manufacturing site, combined with over ten years of expertise in producing high-quality electrification components, for continued growth in the market.
Revealed a value expansion plan to increase its sales orders in the North American market with its value-added technologies, optimized specifically for electric and autonomous vehicles.
Presented future mobility technologies, including the electric Complete Chassis Platform Module (eCCPM) and next-generation AR HUD.
States that Hyundai Mobis has a wide range of innovations in its portfolio, is ready for the future of mobility, and aims to bring the future to the present and make it more valuable.
Points to its successful mass-production of key electrification components, including the battery system for the e-GMP platform used in Hyundai and Kia electric vehicles, and the PE module.
Shows confidence in the paradigm shift for electric vehicle platforms.

Notes that value-added products including the eCCPM module, electrification platform, AR HUD, and lighting grilles are drawing attention from clients in North America for their versatility and innovation.
Announced a goal of driving a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 36% in the North American market through the year 2030.
Reported steady growth in total order volumes over the last few years, with $660 million USD in 2020, $1.4 billion USD in 2021, and $1.7 billion USD in the first half of 2022.
Is the global no. 6 automotive supplier, headquartered in Seoul, Korea.
Has outstanding expertise in sensors, sensor fusion in ECUs and software development for safety control.
Products include various components for electrification, brakes, chassis and suspension, steering, airbags, lighting and automotive electronics.
Operates R&D headquarters in Korea, with four technology centers in the United States, Germany, China and India.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis Announces Expansion Plan in North America for Future Mobility

facts: Hyundai Glovis is pursuing emerging ventures in waste electric vehicle battery handling.
The company operates a subsidiary named Hyundai Glovis Georgia.
Its rail network efforts include adaptations for EV battery and component handling.

In December 2024, Glovis America supported the deployment of 21 Hyundai XCIENT heavy-duty hydrogen fuel-cell trucks at the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Georgia for inbound logistics.
These trucks transported vehicle parts from regional suppliers to the on-site consolidation center and eventually the assembly line.
In 2019, the company's PCTC MV Golden Ray capsized off Georgia, United States.
The incident incurred extensive salvage efforts, environmental remediation, and regulatory fines exceeding $3 million due to improper stability calculations and cargo handling errors.
The MV Golden Ray capsized on September 8, 2019, in St. Simons Sound near Brunswick, Georgia, USA.
The vessel was a 200-meter-long roll-on/roll-off car carrier that departed from Colonel's Island Terminal carrying approximately 4,200 vehicles primarily from Hyundai and Kia.
At the Glovis America facility in West Point, Georgia, 33-year-old worker Seth Dylan Holmes suffered a catastrophic fall on November 22, 2024.
Holmes succumbed to his injuries days later on Thanksgiving.
In early 2025, two workers died in separate preventable accidents at the Hyundai megasite in Ellabell, Georgia, within a span of less than three months.

GLOVIS EV Logistics America LLC, a Hyundai Glovis subsidiary, was implicated as the employer in at least one case involving a conveyor installation mishap that severely injured a worker via crushing and internal trauma.
The fatalities were linked to inadequate safeguards during high-pressure construction phases for the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America project.
OSHA reports cited 12 injuries at the site since January 2023, some involving foreign workers.
On September 4, 2025, an ICE raid at the Georgia Hyundai facility detained over 300 Korean workers, including those affiliated with Glovis operations.
The detention occurred amid allegations of visa fraud, forced labor, and exposure to hazardous conditions that contributed to fatalities.
Federal claims highlighted systemic safety lapses, with at least three companies tied to the project—including Glovis-linked entities—under scrutiny for a worker death probe.
This raised questions about oversight in subcontracted logistics roles.
source_title: Hyundai Glovis — Grokipedia

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced two years ago that South Korea’s Hyundai Motor Group was going to build an electric vehicle plant near Savannah.
The deal promised nearly $8 billion in investment.
The deal promised to bring more than 8,000 well-paying jobs for Georgians.
A federal raid occurred last week at the Hyundai Metaplant and the EV battery facility, which is a joint venture between Hyundai and Korean company LG Energy Solution.
Federal officials detained approximately 475 foreign workers, approximately 300 of whom were Korean, during an ongoing investigation into illegal labor practices.
The Current GA has published numerous stories about misleading wage claims and workplace safety problems at the sprawling site.
The plant sits on a 2,300-acre site where Hyundai has assembled a complete supply chain of companies.

These supply chain companies are separate legal entities but all have a role in meeting the employment and wage targets that Georgia received in exchange for $2 billion in state tax credits and abatements agreed to between the Kemp administration and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).

Prior to last week’s raid, HMGMA said it was not responsible for the actions of its suppliers and subcontractors that The Current’s reporting showed were allegedly in violation of federal workplace safety laws.
On Friday, Hyundai announced that it was bringing a senior executive to supervise the firms amid the ongoing federal investigation at the battery plant.
As of Monday, no arrests have been made, and it is still unclear who employed those detained.
The search warrant authorizing the raid cited the battery plant as a target, along with five other companies that appear to have served as subcontractors for the plant.
Operations at the Hyundai site are intertwined, meaning a worker in one company may have been recruited by a different company or subcontractor.

To build EVs, Hyundai has organized its supply chain at the site, including Hyundai Mobis (power electric systems), Ecoplastic Corporation (plastic components), Seoyon E-HWA (door trim, seats, bumpers), and Sewon America (body components).
Other companies at the site include Hanon Systems, PHA, and Daechang Seat Corp. USA.
Hyundai’s own affiliates present at the site include Hyundai Glovis, Hyundai Steel, and Hyundai Transys.
The joint venture battery factory with LG Energy Solution is also located at the site.

Hyundai, the battery plant, and five affiliated Hyundai suppliers must together invest $5.545 billion and hire at least 8,500 workers by 2031 (making an average of $58,105) to receive the tax credits and tax deferments offered by the state.
As of mid-March, Hyundai told The Current that approximately 1,200 people were employed across the site, not counting Koreans.
This story comes to GPB through a reporting partnership with The Current.
source_title: Explainer: What Hyundai companies are part of the Savannah-area car plant? | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: The federal workplace safety agency fined three companies approximately $27,000 for violations that contributed to the death of a Korean worker in March.
The fines conclude one of the most gruesome chapters in the construction of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The fine amount is less than the retail price of a new electric vehicle made at the facility.
The facility has developed a checkered reputation for safety.
OSHA launched an investigation after Korean worker Sunbok You was killed by a forklift at the battery plant in March.
A Bryan County Sheriff’s report described the incident as leaving a long bloody trail on the factory floor.
The fatality occurred just days before the metaplant’s grand opening, where the chief executive and other senior Hyundai Motor Group officials extolled the company’s safety culture.
Fines were assessed against Beyond Iron Construction LLC., which was helping construct the battery plant.
Fines were assessed against SBY America, which employed Sunbok You.
Fines were assessed against HL-GA Battery, a joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Battery Solutions.
The battery plant was targeted in a September raid by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and other federal agencies.
OSHA inspectors concluded Beyond Iron and SBY allowed forklift drivers to exceed speed limits and failed to ensure enough safety measures.
HL-GA Battery was cited for failing to submit required reports after injuries.
The original fines exceeded $160,000 but were reduced on appeal.
Safety managers and documents suggest a culture of lax workplace safety at the site.
A 2024 investigation by The Current GA found approximately 10 serious accidents had not been reported by Korean suppliers at the Ellabell facility between January 2023 and May 2024.
One unreported incident involved a Korean engineer being crushed in a conveyor belt he was helping to install at the Hyundai Glovis facility in 2024.
First responders flew the injured engineer to the hospital while he was still alive.
It is unknown whether the engineer survived his injuries, which included a crushed torso.
Three men have died on site during the construction of the metaplant in Ellabell.
The first death was construction worker Victor Gamboa in 2023 after a fall due to faulty safety equipment.
Gamboa's employer, Eastern Constructors Inc., was originally fined approximately $160,000 for allowing him to use a defective body harness with notable indicators of damage and deterioration.
The fine against Eastern Constructors Inc. was reduced on appeal to approximately $20,000.
HMGMA later severed ties with Eastern Constructors Inc.
Another death occurred in May 2025 when an American worker was crushed by a load that fell from a forklift.
In September, Hyundai announced it was installing a new official at the site to coordinate safety measures across all suppliers at the metaplant.
This announcement came days after the OSHA fine against HL-GA Battery and the federal raid that resulted in more than 400 workers being detained and deported, including approximately 300 Koreans.
At the time of the federal raid, the United Automobile Workers union issued a statement criticizing Hyundai for dangerous work conditions at the metaplant.

Earlier this month, a nonprofit labor organization in California filed a lawsuit against Hyundai and Kia, its Montgomery, Ala. manufacturing facility, and its west Georgia supplier plant for alleged human trafficking and child labor abuses.
The lawsuit claims these practices give Hyundai and its subsidiaries an unfair pricing advantage when bidding for state contracts.
Last week, Hyundai Chief Executive José Muñoz said the White House phoned him to apologize for September’s immigration raid.
Speaking at a business leaders conference in Singapore, Muñoz said Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp also called him.
Muñoz quoted Kemp as saying, “I don’t know what happened. This is not state jurisdiction.”
Citing the need to “bring in talent” to the U.S., President Donald Trump appeared earlier this month to condemn the raid.
Trump told Fox News host Laura Ingraham there are not enough American workers to perform the “very complicated” and “dangerous” task of building car batteries.
source_title: Hyundai suppliers fined by OSHA following worker's fatal forklift accident - The Current

facts: South Korean battery maker LG Energy Solution suggested that it could build a factory in the US state of Georgia to manufacture batteries for electric vehicles.
An official at LG Energy Solution made the suggestion on Saturday.
LG Energy Solution CEO Kim Jong-hyun sent a letter to US Senator Raphael Warnock.
In the letter, Kim Jong-hyun said that "the company is prepared to do whatever we can help the people and workers of Georgia."
The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported on the contents of the letter.
LG Chem's wholly owned battery division, LG Energy Solution, confirmed it recently sent the letter to the senator but declined to confirm details.
Kim Jong-hyun said in the letter that if an outside investor acquires a separate SK Innovation plant in Georgia, LG could partner with it to run the facility.
The Atlanta Journal-Constitution also reported on the potential partnership with the SK Innovation plant.

An official at LG Energy Solution told Reuters that the letter was intended to clearly address the current situation caused by SK's misappropriation of trade secrets and to alleviate concerns about jobs in Georgia.
An official at SK Innovation rejected the idea of LG taking over its plant.
The SK Innovation official stated that automakers do not allow contracts and suppliers to change separately, making it unreasonable for LG to say it could take over the plant and replace them.
Georgia's governor Brian Kemp repeated his request to US President Joe Biden on Friday to overturn the US International Trade Commission (ITC)'s ruling against SK Innovation.
Governor Kemp's request aims to save thousands of jobs in the state directly tied to SK's battery factory.
LG Energy Solution has been in a legal feud with SK Innovation over allegations that SK stole trade secrets.
The ITC sided with LG Chem in February and issued a 10-year order prohibiting most US imports of SK lithium-ion batteries.
SK Innovation has lobbied the White House to overturn the decision.
The ITC ruling could also be negated if SK and LG reach an independent settlement.
On Thursday, LG announced its plans to invest more than $4.5 billion in its US battery production business over the next four years.
LG's investment announcement includes plans to build at least two new plants.
source_title: LG Energy Solution suggests building EV battery factory in Georgia | HT Auto

entity_name: LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group Joint Battery Cell Plant
canonical_name: LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group
facts: LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group plan to invest an additional $2 billion to expand their joint battery cell manufacturing plant in Georgia.
The investment adds to the $4.3 billion announced for the project in May.
The expansion will add 400 jobs, according to Gov. Brian Kemp.
The factory will be adjacent to Hyundai’s upcoming $5.5 billion manufacturing campus in Ellabell, Georgia.
Production at the facility is slated to begin at the end of 2025.
The plant will have enough capacity to produce 300,000 EVs annually.

Dong-Myung Kim, president and head of the advanced automotive battery division of LG Energy Solution, stated that the investment underscores their dedication to driving America’s EV transition while bolstering the local economy through the creation of quality jobs.
The two South Korea-based companies will each take a 50% stake in the venture and facility.
Hyundai will assemble battery packs using cells from the plant.
The battery packs will be sent to the automaker’s U.S. manufacturing sites to produce Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution have been working together in the EV battery sector since 2009, when Hyundai introduced its Elantra Hybrid EV.
LG Energy Solution provided batteries for the Elantra, along with Hyundai’s Kona Electric and IONIQ 6 EVs.
This investment marks the second joint venture between the two companies.
The two companies broke ground on a battery cell manufacturing plant in Karawang, Indonesia, in September 2021.
The $1.1 billion Indonesian plant is set to start production in the first half of 2024.
Hyundai’s upcoming EV campus has drawn a host of suppliers to the area.
On Tuesday, Daesol Ausys Georgia unveiled plans to invest $72 million in a manufacturing facility in West Point.
The auto part maker will produce luggage boards and covers at its new facility.
Daesol Ausys Georgia is also a key supplier for Kia and General Motors.
source_title: LG Energy, Hyundai to invest additional $2B in Georgia battery cell plant | Manufacturing Dive

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
facts: The new battery cell joint venture will be located in Bryan County, Savannah, Georgia, adjacent to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, which is currently under construction.
Total investment for the joint venture will be over USD 4.3 billion (KRW 5.7 trillion), with Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution each taking a 50 percent stake.
Construction on the joint venture facility is scheduled to start in the second half of 2023.
Battery production at the joint venture is aimed to start at the end of 2025 at the earliest.
The facility will have an annual production capacity of 30 GWh, capable of supporting the production of 300,000 EVs annually.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs using cells from the plant and supply them to the Group’s U.S. manufacturing facilities for production of Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
The new facility aims to create a stable regional supply of batteries and allow the Group to respond quickly to soaring EV demand in the U.S. market.

With this joint venture, LG Energy Solution now has seven battery plants currently operating or under construction in the U.S., where it is concentrating most of its resources to expand production capacity.
Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution signed a memorandum of understanding to produce EV batteries in the U.S. during a signing ceremony at LGES’s headquarters in Seoul on May 26.
The May 26 signing ceremony was attended by Jaehoon Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, and Youngsoo Kwon, CEO of LG Energy Solution.
Hyundai Motor Group is focusing on electrification efforts to secure a leadership position in the global auto industry through establishing this new EV battery cell plant with LG Energy Solution.
LG Energy Solution commits to advancing product competitiveness and global operational expertise to drive the EV transition in America and expedite the clean energy transition in the U.S.

Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution have a long-standing partnership in electrification, having previously supplied EV batteries for vehicles including the Elantra Hybrid, Kona Electric, and IONIQ 6 dedicated EV.
The Hyundai Elantra Hybrid, an LPi hybrid vehicle introduced in 2009, was the Group’s first electrified model.
In 2021, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution started construction of a battery cell joint venture in Indonesia, which is set to start production in the first half of 2024.
This facility marks the second battery manufacturing joint venture for Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution as they aim to continue strengthening their ties in electrification efforts.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution to Establish Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture in the U.S.

entity_name: Georgia EV & Clean Energy Supply Chain & Workforce Initiatives
canonical_name: Georgia EV & Clean Energy Supply Chain & Workforce Initiatives
facts: Students in the Richmond County School System will benefit from a $1 billion federal investment in clean energy buses.
Goodwill prepares to train the workforce needed to support the electric vehicle industry.
Senator Raphael Warnock (D-Ga) and EPA Administrator Michael Regan announced the funding through Warnock’s Clean Commute for Kids Act, which was included in the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
Nearly $60 million will fund 25 buses for Richmond County School System, along with buses for DeKalb, Bibb, Clayton, and Glynn County Schools, as well as Marietta and Carrollton City Schools.

Senator Warnock stated: “This is about converting fleets of diesel-powered school buses into clean energy vehicles for the future. This is a ride to the future, and this investment demonstrates the power of bipartisan cooperation to deliver tangible results for our communities.”
Electric buses can cost more than twice that of a diesel bus, but the investment balances when factoring in future costs for fuel, parts, and maintenance.
Electric energy to refuel the buses costs less than diesel, and with fewer moving parts, the cost to maintain electric buses is lower.
Finding a trained workforce to service and maintain EVs is a challenge, particularly in Georgia, which has seen a shift toward facilities that manufacture EVs along with supporting industries.
Goodwill Industries International partnered with Accenture to launch “The Goodwill Clean Tech Accelerator.”
The workforce training program addresses the most in-demand, entry-level, clean energy jobs.
Participants will gain skills for jobs in electric vehicle charging and other industries, including solar.

Steve Preston, President and CEO of Goodwill Industries International, stated: “As our labor market transitions, we see important opportunities for people to move into more promising roles with better pay. It is essential that we provide the training and other support needed to ensure people capture these opportunities.”
Preston added: “The Goodwill Clean Tech Accelerator will open doors for people in an expanding industry and provide support to employers who are helping us transition to a more sustainable world.”
The accelerator will provide skills and certifications for job opportunities with higher wages and better career advancement.
The program will target under and unemployed individuals.
Nearly 25 million children ride more than 500,000 predominantly diesel buses to school in the U.S., exposing children to polluted air.

Warnock noted: “Exposure to diesel exhaust from these school buses negatively affects the health and well-being of children, especially those with asthma or other pulmonary ailments. This federal funding will help support and grow jobs at Georgia companies like Fort Valley’s Blue Bird Corporation, which has been an industry leader in green commute technology.”
Augusta is jumping into green energy technology with the construction of Aurubis Richmond, LLC, currently under construction in the Augusta Corporate Park off Mike Padget Highway.
The Aurubis plant will recycle copper, precious metals, and non-ferrous materials nationwide once operational.
Operation is expected to be early this year.

Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated during a recent visit: “The world needs metal which can be recycled without loss of quality or performance for cars, mobile phones, batteries, cables, and computers.”
The plant will have the capacity to process up to 90,000 tons of complex metals, including printed circuit boards.
Aurubis will be joined by Denkai America, which will locate its new manufacturing facility and North American headquarters in Richmond County.
Denkai America’s initial investment will be $150 million to establish an ED copper foil production facility, with a focus on electric vehicle (EV) battery foil.
The facility will be located on a 115-acre site.
Denkai America aims to invest a total of $430 million and create 250 new jobs over the next five years.
source_title: Major investment in electric buses and training - Augusta Business Daily

facts: Industrial Info Resources is tracking nearly 500 active and proposed metal-recycling projects worldwide, totaling more than US$45 billion of investment.
The U.S. accounts for nearly US$5 billion of the global metal-recycling investment.
China accounts for about 80% of investment at metal-recycling facilities worldwide, with the U.S. accounting for nearly 11%.

China accounts for more than 94% of US$14.6 billion worth of projects deemed to have a high likelihood (81% or more) of beginning construction within the next 12 months, while the U.S. makes up only 3.1%.
Strong demand from data-center and automotive markets is keeping global copper prices at elevated levels.
Aurubis Richmond LLC, a subsidiary of German firm Aurubis AG, expects to finish construction later this year on an expansion of its copper scrap recycling and processing plant in Augusta, Georgia.
The Augusta plant expansion has been under construction since 2022.
The additional capacity from the Augusta plant expansion is expected to double the plant's output to 70,000 tons per year.
Demand for the Augusta plant's output is fueled by data centers and EVs.
Aurubis stated in a September 2025 press release that American industry currently needs about 1.8 million tons of copper per year.
Experts estimate copper demand will rise nearly 30% in the next five years, according to Aurubis.
As of the September 2025 announcement, the U.S. imports about half of the copper it processes.
Multi-metal recycling can close the domestic copper supply gap quickly, according to Aurubis.
China's copper-recycling market is experiencing a regional shortage of copper scrap raw materials, fueling sustained rallies in copper prices.
Growing adoption of EVs is driving a boom in U.S.-based lithium-ion battery recycling, which accounts for most investment in U.S.-based metal-recycling projects under construction.
