Cirba Solutions, a subsidiary of environmental-services and heavy-construction firm The Heritage Group, has two lithium-ion battery-recycling projects set to be completed this year: a plant in Columbia, South Carolina, and an expansion in Lancaster, Ohio.
The new South Carolina plant is expected to produce up to 500,000 EV batteries per year.
The Ohio expansion is expected to boost output by more than 250,000 EV batteries per year.
Ara Partners is proposing an expansion of its lithium-ion battery-recycling plant in Bartlesville, Oklahoma.
The first phase of the Ara Partners facility completed construction in December and can process 14,000 tons per year of battery materials.
The Ara Partners expansion is designed to elevate processing capacity to 50,000 tons per year.
source_title: Metal Recycling Offers Answer to Rising Copper, Battery Needs

facts: Accelerated expansion of the Aurubis Richmond plant in Georgia is expected to double production by 2026.
Strategic investments in multimetal recycling will secure critical raw materials such as copper for the U.S. and Europe.
Aurubis Richmond to increase U.S. hiring by around 80 additional employees.
Aurubis’ metals are crucial contributors to renewable energy, electric vehicles, batteries, and other essential industries.

The Supervisory Board of Aurubis AG, a European-based global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide, announced a significant and accelerated investment in its U.S. operations.
The company plans to spend an additional € 250 million to fast-track the expansion of its Richmond plant, which is currently under construction in Augusta, Georgia.
This investment is projected to double throughput volume from the previously planned 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials to 180,000 tons per year.
Once both phases are complete, Aurubis anticipates about 200 employees at the facility.
Aurubis broke ground on their Richmond plant in June 2022.

Together with the expansion stage announced today and taking into account a capital expenditure adjustment for infrastructure requirements and inflation of € 90 million for the current construction project, Aurubis plans to invest a total of € 640 million in the U.S. state of Georgia.
Aurubis expects to generate operative earnings (EBITDA) of around € 170 million starting in fiscal year 2026/27.

Roland Harings, CEO of Aurubis commented that the world needs metals such as copper – which can be recycled without loss of quality or performance – for cars, mobile phones, batteries, cables, and computers.
Roland Harings noted that today’s high recycling demand cannot be met by existing facilities.
Roland Harings stated that Aurubis’ increased commitment to the U.S. will be a game-changer for the environment, manufacturers, and the larger transition to a renewable economy.
Aurubis Richmond’s first phase is expected to come online in mid-2024 with the completed expansion scheduled for 2026.
Aurubis received the necessary approvals for the operation of both facilities earlier in the year (2022).
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp welcomed Aurubis to the state last year and celebrated the start of its partnership with the Augusta community.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that with this latest announcement coming well ahead of schedule, they are thankful to see the benefits of this international brand already coming to hardworking Georgians.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed looking forward to many successful years ahead as Aurubis plays a key role in meeting the needs of tomorrow’s economy.

Consistent U.S. market growth of complex recycling creates excellent conditions for Aurubis’ rapid expansion by efficiently repurposing hundreds of thousands of tons of recyclable materials that otherwise would be shipped out the U.S. or sent to local landfills.

Following a meeting in Hamburg, Germany, CEO Harings said they have doubled down on commitments to circular economy, the environment and to safeguarding the long-term health of investors by balancing profit with meaningful sustainability goals.
source_title: Aurubis doubles U.S. investment in multimetal recycling facility

facts: Korean supplier Duckyang Industrial is set to invest $10 million to open a manufacturing facility in Braselton, Ga., the company’s first such facility in the U.S.
The planned 230,000-square-foot plant will add an estimated 285 jobs in production, maintenance, logistics and warehouse management to the region’s economy.
It will manufacture battery modules and energy storage systems for SK Battery America, a Commerce, Ga.-based developer of lithium ion batteries for electric vehicles.
SK Battery America is a subsidiary of the Seoul-based conglomerate SK Innovation.

Duckyang CEO Dong-in Son stated that entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market with SKBA will provide an opportunity to cooperate with other automotive companies, including Hyundai and Kia Motors, that already have a manufacturing base in the U.S.
A Duckyang official did not confirm when production would start.
Duckyang is headquartered in Ulsan, South Korea.
Duckyang supplies driver’s seat cockpit modules to clients such as Hyundai, Kia, Renault, Samsung Motors, Genesis and Mobis.
In 1999, Duckyang entered a joint venture with Visteon Corp., a suburban Detroit-based supplier that spun off from Ford Motor Co. in 2000.
Visteon fully divested its Duckyang stake in 2014.
source_title: Supplier Duckyang to open $10 million plant in Georgia

facts: A joint venture to manufacture battery energy storage systems (BESS) for industrial facilities is set to begin production in Jackson County this summer.
NeoVolta, a California-based firm previously focused solely on home BESS systems, is branching into the commercial space with a new entity in Pendergrass, Ga.

The joint venture will build an initial run of 2 GWh of BESS systems at a logistics center just off Interstate 85 in partnership with PotisEdge, a Canadian company recently acquired by Longi Green Energy Technology Co. Ltd.

Longi Green Energy Technology Co. Ltd. is headquartered in Xi’an, China, has annual revenues exceeding $10 billion, and acquired a majority interest in PotisEdge as part of a global effort to offer an integrated “one-stop” solution from generation to storage.
The joint venture was announced in a January news release that teased a mid-2026 Georgia production launch and the creation of 89 jobs in the first phase.

The plant will initially focus on prismatic-cell battery pack assembly and DC container integration, with plans to quickly double production to 4 GWh after the initial ramp-up, according to NeoVolta CEO Ardes Johnson.

NeoVolta owns 80 percent of the venture, while PotisEdge takes a 20 percent stake, creating a majority U.S.-controlled entity required to qualify for investment tax credits that can offset 30-40 percent of construction costs.

Energy storage systems are allowed until 2032 before investment tax credits are phased out, whereas electric vehicle tax credits from the Inflation Reduction Act were eliminated soon after the One Big Beautiful Bill Act of 2025 passed.

Battery storage projects breaking ground in 2026 require 55 percent of overall expenditures on a qualifying facility to go toward domestically sourced content, with the local-content requirement rising each year.
PotisEdge manufactures many of its components in Suzhou, China, but does not manufacture battery cells, which constitute the majority of any system's cost.
NeoVolta plans to import Chinese batteries this year, then move sourcing to other international locations, and ultimately shift to on-shore purchases of cells as domestic capacity grows.

PotisEdge currently sources from Chinese cell suppliers for assembly of energy storage systems, and the Georgia plant will replicate much of that production with more automation, according to reports citing PotisEdge CEO Minjie Shi.
NeoVolta is traded on the NASDAQ and collaborates with established players like PotisEdge and Longi on the technology side.

The arrangement allows NeoVolta’s executives to focus on finance and compliance while leveraging the hardware strengths of their Chinese partners, who have given NeoVolta “more support than we have even asked for.”
Longi has a track record with FEOC compliance through its partnership with Invenergy in Ohio, giving the Georgia venture “bankability from day one,” according to Johnson.
PotisEdge signed the initial lease in Pendergrass to get the project moving.

NeoVolta will see a major revenue step change by tackling the utility-scale storage market first, followed by the commercial and industrial sector, which CEO Ardes Johnson calls the “missing middle” of storage.
NeoVolta’s revenues are projected to grow to $20 million in the short term from just a few million dollars last year, while still focusing on home-energy systems.
Full production at the Georgia plant is calculated to represent approximately $400 million in potential BESS sales at current market rates.
The joint venture needs to raise some $20 million beyond the $23 million it has already netted to power the Georgia plant’s initial phase.

In late 2025, NeoVolta received a $13 million private placement to fund the joint venture, led by a $10 million infusion from Infinite Grid Capital, a private equity fund focused on energy storage with executives experienced in China.

Jackson County Chamber of Commerce President and CEO Steve Wittry stated that while he was unaware of the project, the nearby SK plant has drawn attention to the area and put Jackson County “on the map” for companies producing materials SK is interested in.
Early on, the SK investment attracted South Korean suppliers like Enchem and later Duckyang.
NeoVolta Power is bringing established Chinese technology to Georgia with partners who understand long-term partnership approaches needed to succeed in the U.S. market.

CEO Ardes Johnson, a former Tesla sales executive, visited PotisEdge’s processes and assembly lines designed for Georgia during trips to China and believes the partnership will successfully marry both sides’ strengths.
source_title: Battery Storage Factory Involving Major Chinese Energy Player Planned for Jackson County  - Global Atlanta

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Seoyon E-HWA will create 740 new direct and indirect jobs.
The company will invest almost $76 million in a new manufacturing facility in Chatham County.
Seoyon E-Hwa is a global auto parts supplier that manufactures both interior and exterior components.
Established in 1972, Seoyon E-Hwa operates 32 corporations in North America, South America, Europe, and Asia.
The company supplies door trim, headlining, seats, C/PAD, bumpers, and other parts to Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, Mercedes-Benz, Ford, Volkswagen, and more.
Seoyon’s LaGrange location will continue to support 630 full time jobs.
The new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center, a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site.
The new manufacturing facility will produce automotive interior parts such as door trims, headlining, and tailgate trims.
Production is expected to begin in October 2024.
More than two-thirds, or approximately 500, of the announced jobs will be permanent roles in human resources, development, quality, and production personnel.

Savannah Economic Development Authority President and CEO Trip Tollison stated the location ideally positions Seoyon E-HWA in close proximity to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, the Port of Savannah, Interstates I-16 and I-95, and more.
Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director Griff Lynch noted that Seoyon is a valued customer using the Port of Savannah to supply its manufacturing facility in West Georgia.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Savannah Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that with its second Georgia facility, Seoyon is committing to nearly doubling the number of jobs it supports in the state.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced over $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers have already exceeded the $1 billion in investment predicted from other locations connected to the project around the state by more than $600 million.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia aims to become the e-mobility capital of the nation as projects continue to choose the state for business.

Chairman Mr. Ryu Yang-seok stated that Seoyon E-Hwa is an automobile manufacturer in various countries such as Korea, Europe, and India, and it is an honor to supply electric vehicle parts in Chatham County, Georgia.
source_title: Seoyon E-HWA, a Hyundai supplier, to locate in Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center - SEDA

facts: Hyundai Motor Group’s Georgia supplier list expanded with three more auto part makers announcing investments to establish manufacturing facilities in the state.
Hyundai Industrial Co., a subsidiary of Hyundai Motor Group, invested over $24 million to acquire a building in Newnan, creating 100 jobs.
Hyundai Industrial Co. plans to retrofit its Newnan facility to produce armrests and headrests for Hyundai’s upcoming $5.5 billion EV metaplant in Ellabell as well as other OEMs.
South Korea-based tier-1 supplier Hwashin plans to establish a $176 million plant in Dublin, creating over 460 jobs.
Hwashin specializes in crafting chassis and body parts for Hyundai, its subsidiary Kia, and other OEMs.
Production at Hwashin’s Dublin plant is expected to begin in late 2025.
Daechang Seat Corp. USA announced it is investing $72.5 million to build a manufacturing facility in Savannah, creating over 500 jobs.
Daechang Seat Corp. USA specializes in automotive seat frames and will join previously announced suppliers Seoyon E-HWA and PHA at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center is located near Hyundai’s EV metaplant, which is still under construction.
The latest investments bring an estimated $2.5 billion from a total of 14 suppliers to Georgia.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated in a Nov. 1 news release that the growing EV network is creating over 6,000 jobs.
In September, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution invested an additional $2 billion toward their joint battery cell manufacturing plant.
The total investment for the Hyundai-LG Energy Solution joint battery cell manufacturing plant is $4.3 billion.
Production at the joint battery cell plant is slated to begin at the end of 2025.
The joint battery cell plant will be located next to Hyundai’s manufacturing campus.
The joint battery cell plant will have enough capacity to produce 300,000 EVs annually.
Hyundai broke ground on its manufacturing campus over a year ago.
Construction has recently begun on Hyundai’s production-related buildings, with foundation work nearly complete, according to the company last month.
The nearly 3,000-acre Hyundai manufacturing megasite will use renewable resources to power its mass production.
Hyundai aims to reach carbon neutrality by 2045, supported by the campus's use of renewable resources.
source_title: Hyundai’s EV manufacturing campus draws 3 more suppliers to Georgia | Supply Chain Dive

facts: Seo Yeon Ewha is a manufacturer and supplier of auto parts including door trim, bumper, seat, covering self and package tray.
The CEO is Yongseok Kang.
The company is located in Montgomery, Alabama.
The company operates as a private entity in the Automobiles & Parts sector.
Estimated annual revenue is $100-500M.
Estimated number of employees is 100-250.
On Oct 28, 2024, news reported that Seoyon e-Hwa, a South Korean supplier to the automotive sector, is setting up shop in Hutto with the aim of attracting more industry players.
On Feb 10, 2023, news reported that Georgia welcomes automotive part manufacturing growth as a new factory serves as an OEM supplier.
On Mar 13, 2020, news reported that Seoyon E-Hwa Interior Systems expands operations in Selma, Alabama.
On Mar 12, 2020, news reported that the auto supplier plans expansion, creating 60 jobs in Selma.
On Jun 11, 2014, news reported that Hanil E Hwa is expanding in LaGrange.
source_title: Seoyon e-Hwa’s Competitors, Revenue, Number of Employees, Funding, Acquisitions & News - Owler Company Profile

facts: Automotive parts supplier Sewon America is a subsidiary of South Korea-based Sewon Precision Industry Co. Ltd.
Sewon America is investing more than $300 million in a new manufacturing plant for EV body parts.
The new manufacturing plant will be located in Rincon, Ga.
source_title: $300M investment by South Korean auto supplier Sewon in Georgia - Plastics News

facts: Hyundai's 8,100-employee automobile assembly plant and EV battery factory is set to open soon in the Savannah region.
The Savannah region's training centers and school systems are creating workforce pipelines for Hyundai and its suppliers.
Talent needs to be flowing through labor conduits early, according to Hyundai officials and school administrators.
Workforce programs emphasize skills ranging from technical certificates to higher-level engineering and computer science knowledge to boost the local workforce pool.
School administrators from Chatham, Effingham, Bulloch, and Bryan counties are part of the Savannah Joint Development Authority that brokered the Hyundai deal.

These counties utilize CTAE (Career Technical, Agricultural Education) career pathway programs designed to prepare students for the workforce through targeted classes, certificates, or articulated college credit.
Each county's school district provides different CTAE pathway offerings across their K-12 schools.
The Savannah-Chatham County Public School System offers 17 different subjects or clusters for students, including collision repair, computer science, engineering and technology, and manufacturing.
These pathways are geared toward the EV manufacturing industry, according to Angie Lewis, executive director of the district's Office of College and Career Readiness.
Computer science programs are offered at each high school in the Savannah-Chatham County Public School System.
Computer science pathways can focus on general computer science, cybersecurity, computer networking, or gaming to prepare students for the Hyundai plant.
Chatham County public school students can apply for a choice school to attend programs outside their attendance zone.
Savannah elementary, middle, and high schools have programs centered around STEM education.
Jenkins High School offers an engineering program that provides dual college credit and real-world experience for students pursuing degrees or certificates in the field.
Administrators have a strong partnership with technical colleges and dual enrollment offerings at Armstrong State University and Georgia Southern University.
Administrators plan to meet with Hyundai and workforce development officials to create more specific pathways serving the Hyundai plant.
Georgia Speaker of the House Jon Burns stated that the General Assembly has a study committee actively looking into aligning workforce needs with school curriculums.
Aligning workforce needs could mean creating a new pathway specifically geared toward EVs, according to some school officials.

Todd Wall, director of CTAE at the Effingham County School District and CEO of the Effingham College and Career Academy, said discussions are already taking place on creating a dual enrollment program between high schools and Savannah Tech's Electric Vehicle Professional certificate.
The potential partnership is hoped to be rolled out this fall, allowing high school seniors to graduate with the certificate in hand.
Savannah Tech serves Chatham, Effingham, Bryan, and Liberty schools.
Ogeechee Tech is Bulloch County's regional technical college.
Hyundai plans to expand EV certificate programs to technical colleges across south Georgia.

Effingham County's College and Career Academy opened in 2010 and offers programs in logistics, engineering, automotive, and computer science among other subjects specifically prepared to serve Hyundai's needs.
The Bulloch County School District has a Career-Workforce Steering Committee working on a three-year career workforce development plan.
The plan aims to increase employability, enlistment, or enrollment by bolstering CTAE pathways, dual enrollment opportunities, and work-based learning.

Charles Wilson, Bulloch County Schools' superintendent, wrote that leadership has been collaboratively developing a workforce development plan for the past 10 years to meet industry expansion needs in the region.
The steering committee hopes to engage with Hyundai and other industry representatives as the plant ramps up towards opening.
Hyundai representatives are focused on creating strategies to grow long-term talent in technical skills, high-level engineering, and computing fields.
Savannah Tech announced an Electric Vehicle Professional certificate program and a training partnership with Hyundai supplier Sewon America.
Savannah Tech also announced a certificate for EV repair technicians.
Brent Stubbs is the senior manager of learning and development for the Hyundai Metaplant.
More partnerships with area technical colleges are in the works, according to Stubbs.
Hyundai's most immediate priority is filling gaps in the local high-level engineering pool.
The automaker is in discussions with Savannah State, Georgia Tech, and Georgia Southern about adding an EV component to their curriculum.
Stubbs described the facility as a first-in-the-world smart factory with automation, requiring computer scientists and software engineers.
Hyundai is looking at building relationships for that talent pool.
Georgia Southern University currently offers six different types of undergraduate engineering degrees and six graduate-level engineering degrees.

Statesboro-based Georgia Southern will add two engineering doctorates this fall: a Ph.D. in applied computing and a Ph.D. in engineering with concentrations in civil, electrical, advanced manufacturing, and mechanical engineering.
Mohammad Davoud is the dean of GSU's Allen E. Paulson College of Engineering and Computing.
Davoud said the university has been planning these programs for about 10 years in anticipation of Georgia's growth.

STEM programs such as engineering and applied computing are extremely attractive to industries, directly providing an employee pool of highly trained engineers, computing professionals, and problem solvers with B.S., M.S., and Ph.D. degrees, according to Davoud.
Adding advanced degrees will result in high-quality post-secondary educators to support the next generation, bolstering the ecosystem of engineers and computer scientists, according to Davoud.

Stubbs described a symbiotic relationship between the metaplant and area universities, noting that previously young talent left for college at UGA or Georgia Tech and then moved away, but now they can stay for great jobs at a company that shares their values.
Quick Start is a division of the Technical College System of Georgia that provides customized workforce training for companies.
To support the Hyundai workforce, the Technical College System is building a $62.5 million Quick Start Training Center on the metaplant site.
The center will prepare Hyundai employees as well as those of relevant suppliers.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development will request more than $10 million in funds from the Georgia Legislature to expand the existing Quick Start Advanced Manufacturing Center in Pooler.

Stubbs described Quick Start's two major goals: training new employees (called "metapros" by Hyundai) to be ready on Day One, and building community relationships by providing training for incoming technical college programs and building interest in EV manufacturing.
The training center will feature a scaled-down version of everything experienced in the plant, including welding, stamp, paint, general assembly, and quality control, with some proprietary elements.
Quick Start is working with Hyundai's subject matter experts to customize the curriculum.
The training focuses on teaching about automotive electric vehicles and safety rather than how to build the car, which will be done after hiring.
Hyundai aims to create a new talent pipeline excited about electric cars.
Hyundai plans to hire 500 employees this year and another 700 by the end of 2024, with the majority coming from the local labor pool.
The pipelines being created will feed a gradual workforce buildup, with Hyundai projecting the full workforce of 8,100 to be on the job by 2031.
Nancy Guan is the general assignment reporter covering Chatham County municipalities for the Savannah Morning News.
Latrice Williams co-authored the series examining the local workforce landscape from labor pools and training programs perspectives.
The five-part series explores how Savannah's economy is evolving, where officials expect newcomers to move from, and the scale of workforce development programs being put in place.
source_title: How to build a robust labor pipeline? Hyundai partners with K-12 schools, colleges and state - AOL

facts: Sewon America is a subsidiary of Korean-based Sewon Precision Industry Co.
Sewon America will create 740 new jobs in Effingham County, Georgia.
Sewon America will invest more than $300 million in a new manufacturing facility.
The project is the largest known private investment in the City of Rincon to date.
The project fulfills the commitment to create jobs and investment in all four Savannah Harbor Interstate-16 Joint Development Authority (JDA) counties.
This JDA commitment results from Hyundai’s Metaplant location in Bryan County, which is the largest economic development project in state history.
Suppliers for the Metaplant have now committed over $1.8 billion in investment, surpassing initial estimates by at least $800 million.
Sewon America operates one of the largest automobile body parts manufacturing plants in North America.
The company opened a manufacturing facility in LaGrange in 2008, which has since doubled in size.
Following the completion of the Rincon facility, Sewon is expected to support 1,600 jobs in Georgia.
Vice Chairman Kim stated that Sewon America was founded 15 years ago in west Georgia and expressed excitement about building another facility in east Georgia as one family in two locations.
The growth is attributed to the Sewon team, Governor Kemp, the Georgia Department of Economic Development, and Effingham County.
Sewon’s new facility will be located at the Grande View industrial park in Rincon.
The new manufacturing location will produce electric vehicle (EV) body parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Operations are expected to begin in 2025.

Governor Brian P. Kemp noted that his first international trip as governor to Korea included congratulating Chairman Kim on a recent expansion and discussing continued collaboration, which led to this announcement.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) worked with the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC to support the project.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Sewon has integrated into Georgia’s automotive community through work with local colleges, Georgia Quick Start, and statewide initiatives like Georgia Made.
The project won the 2022 Deal Of The Year: Bronze Award.
Chairman of the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority (IDA) Troy Smith stated that Sewon selected Grande View industrial park for significant future growth investment.
The IDA and Sewon’s investment will allow current and future generations to have fulfilling, well-paying careers without leaving Effingham County.
The project was made possible with help from the City of Rincon and the Effingham County Board of Commissioners.

Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority Griff Lynch stated that the announcement is terrific news for Effingham County and the State of Georgia, and that the Hyundai announcement continues to bring new economic opportunities to the Peach State.
On October 25, 2022, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia.
source_title: Sewon America Will Create 740 New Jobs In Georgia - Automotive - Business Facilities Magazine

facts: Hanon Systems, a South Korean company, plans to build a $40 million factory in coastal Georgia to manufacture heating and cooling systems for automobiles.
The company announced it will construct the facility in Statesboro, Georgia.
Hanon Systems plans to hire at least 160 new employees for the new plant.

Hanon Systems is the eighth major supplier to locate in the region following Hyundai Motor Group's 2022 announcement of a $5.5 billion electric vehicle and battery assembly plant in Ellabell, Georgia.
The Hyundai site in Ellabell could grow to 8,100 employees and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Hanon Systems and seven other suppliers have collectively pledged to invest more than $2 billion and hire 4,800 people in the region.
Hanon Systems CEO Ming Sun stated that the announcement aligns with the company's strategy to support the industry shift toward electrification while growing its North America footprint.
Hanon Systems currently supplies heating and cooling systems to a Hyundai assembly plant in Montgomery, Alabama, and a Kia plant in West Point, Georgia.

Headquartered in Daejon, South Korea, the company manufactures heating and air conditioning systems at an existing facility in Shorter, Alabama, which opened in 2003 and has expanded at least three times.
Production at the new Statesboro facility is planned to begin in May 2024.
The new plant will be located in a business park on the southern outskirts of Statesboro.
A majority of Hanon Systems is owned by a South Korean private equity firm, while the rest is publicly traded.
Hanon Systems operates plants worldwide and was created by combining Halla Climate Control with related businesses from Visteon Corp., Cooper Standard Automotive, and Magna International.
The company could qualify for $3.2 million in state income tax credits, at $4,000 per job over five years, as long as workers make at least $31,300 a year.
The state will pay to train the new employees.
Hanon Systems could qualify for other incentives, including property tax breaks from Bulloch County.
source_title: South Korean supplier plans $40 million auto parts plant in Georgia near new Hyundai complex | AP News

facts: Hanon Systems announced that it is supplying its highly integrated cooling module for BMW’s fully electric SUV, iX3.

The system integrates several components — including the innovative eCompressor, electronic expansion valve block, a combined water-cooled condenser, and internal heat exchanger, chiller, A/C lines, an
source_title: News - Hanon Systems - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: Metaplant America is a massive electric vehicle and battery manufacturing plant rising about 30 minutes from Savannah.
The project represents the scope of Georgia's largest economic deal ever.
More details regarding the scope of this economic deal emerged as of June 2, 2023.
Related page content highlights that the state will amend Hyundai's economic development agreement.
Related page content notes that Hyundai and LG are building a $4.3 billion battery plant.
Related page content mentions Georgia securing an EV supply chain piece with an Anvion Technologies factory in Bainbridge.
Related page content reports that Hyundai suppliers have announced over 4,500 jobs.
source_title: Hyundai's Georgia plant: What's the latest update? - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: On July 18, 2024, AJIN Georgia in Bulloch County’s Bruce Yawn Commerce Park celebrated the grand opening of their new automotive metal structural parts facility.
The facility is the first Tier 1 supplier for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bulloch County and will manufacture interior metal and structural parts.
About 285 people attended the grand opening ceremony, including AJIN executives, local and state leaders, and prominent guests.

The gathering featured Jamie Calloway (HR General Manager at AJIN Georgia), CEO Onchung Gwon of HMGMA, delegates from MOBIS, officials from state and local governments, the Korean consulate, utility service providers, regional press, and delegates from Ajin USA.
KBD Group served as the design-builder for the project.
The facility spans 853,000 square feet.
Over a hundred employees are currently inside making parts for an all-electric Hyundai Motor Group crossover SUV.
The manufactured parts include the main metal portions of a vehicle’s doors, dash, fenders, and floorboard.
The project represents a $317-million corporate investment in Statesboro Georgia.
When production begins, tractor-trailer trucks will haul parts from the AJIN Georgia plant to Hyundai Motor Group’s Metaplant America in northern Bryan County about 29 miles down Interstate 16.
The tour featured hundreds of one-armed robots and large stamping presses that exert up to 1,500 tons of force along with rolls of steel that feed them.
AJIN Georgia is located 30 miles west of the under-construction Hyundai EV plant in Bryan County.
According to a news release from Gov. Brian Kemp’s office, the company plans to hire 630 workers for the future 7 million parts factory.

Jung Ho Sea, CEO of Ajin USA, stated: “We are excited to be one of the first Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America automotive suppliers to build an automotive parts manufacturing facility in Bulloch County and for our first plant in the great State of Georgia.”
Jung Ho Sea added: “Joon Georgia, Inc. will create approximately 630 new jobs with competitive benefits in Bulloch County and the State of Georgia.”

Jung Ho Sea stated their commitment is to be the best parts supplier for automotive and EV companies like Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, as well as the best employer and corporate citizen giving back to the community of Bulloch County and the City of Statesboro.
KBD Group completed the AJIN Georgia project on time and within budget without any injuries.

links.context: Reference to Gov. Kemp's office press release about first Hyundai supplier in Bulloch County

source_title: AJIN Georgia Grand Opening

facts: An auto parts manufacturer plans to hire 630 workers at a new factory in southeast Georgia to supply Hyundai Motor Group's first U.S. electric vehicle plant that is under construction nearby, according to state officials.
Joon Georgia will invest $317 million to produce parts in Bulloch County, according to Gov. Brian Kemp's office.
The supplier facility will be located roughly 30 miles (50 kilometers) west of the southeast Georgia site where Hyundai executives broke ground on the new EV plant two weeks ago.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated the company is "the first of many" expected to come to Georgia to supply the $5.5 billion Hyundai plant in Bryan County.
Hyundai plans to open its Georgia plant in 2025, producing up to 300,000 electric vehicles per year.
Joon Georgia is a subsidiary of Ajin USA, which supplies parts to other Hyundai plants.

Joon Georgia already operates a facility in Cusseta, Alabama, near the Georgia line that makes parts for Hyundai's plant in Montgomery, Alabama, as well as for Kia's auto plant in West Point, Georgia.
The Joon Georgia factory is expected to open near Statesboro in mid-2024, according to Kemp's office.
Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, called the announcement a "landmark moment as we drive Georgia's automotive industry into the future."
State and local officials in Georgia lured Hyundai with tax breaks and incentives worth $1.8 billion, making it the state's largest economic development deal.
Wilson and other Georgia officials have insisted the Hyundai investment is worthwhile.
In addition to Hyundai hiring 8,100 workers, suppliers are expected to create thousands of additional jobs in the state.
source_title: Georgia: Supplier to hire 630 near Hyundai's EV plant in Georgia - Times of India

facts: Ecoplastic America Corporation specializes in the manufacture of plastic automobile parts for equipment manufacturers, most notably Hyundai Motors and KIA.
Products include bumpers, consoles, trims, and plastic molds.
Products are used in Hyundai/Kia vehicles including the Genesis G90, Ionic, Palisade, GV80, Santa Fe, Tucson, Sonata, and Venue.
The company is following Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) to the Georgia region as a tier-1 supplier.
Ecoplastic America Corporation is building a new facility in Bulloch County, Georgia.
The new Bulloch County facility is currently under construction at 4800 Highway 301 South.
The project will create more than 500 jobs.
The company has invested over $205 million in the new Bulloch County facility.
Corporate headquarters currently employs approximately 520 employees, and the scale of the Georgia operations will rival that size.
CEO Chulhwan (Charlie) Kim stated the company intends to work closely with local businesses and residents to integrate into the community.
The company is considering partnerships with local educational institutions to provide career opportunities for students.
source_title: Ecoplastic America is proud to build new home in Bulloch County  - Grice Connect

facts: Ascend Elements celebrated the grand opening of its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility at the end of March.
The $50 million Base 1 facility is located in Covington, GA.
It is North America's largest electric vehicle battery recycling facility.
Lithium-ion battery recycling is becoming an increasingly important part of the EV battery materials supply chain as demand for EVs increases.
The facility began partial operations in August 2022.
The facility now has an annual capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
This processing capacity is equal to 70,000 EV batteries per year.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O'Kronley stated the facility is an important part of America's EV battery infrastructure and just the start.
O'Kronley emphasized that the industry needs to continue building a closed-loop battery materials supply chain to make electric vehicle batteries cleaner and more sustainable.
Ascend Elements recovers up to 98% of critical battery metals in used EV batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap.
The company uses its patented Hydro-to-Cathode™ process to recycle used lithium-ion batteries and scrap.
This recycling process can reduce the carbon footprint of new EV battery cathode materials by up to 90%.
The facility is powered by renewable energy.
The facility features onsite wastewater recycling and treatment systems.
The facility currently employs about 100 team members.
Ascend Elements plans to increase the facility's headcount to 185 by 2024.
source_title: Ascend Elements Opens EV Battery Recycling Facility in Georgia - Autobody News

facts: Ascend Elements is a Massachusetts-based recycling startup.
Ascend Elements opened a recycling plant in Covington, Georgia in late March.
The company says its Covington facility is the largest electric-vehicle battery recycling facility in North America.
The Covington plant can process 30,000 metric tons of input each year.
The plant breaks down old batteries and preps the most valuable materials inside to be processed and turned into new batteries.
This processing capacity equates to breaking down the battery packs from 70,000 electric vehicles annually, according to Ascend CEO Mike O’Kronley.
Redwood Materials told Canary Media its Nevada facility is already processing 40,000 metric tons of input annually.
The Redwood Nevada facility capacity is equivalent to around 100,000 battery packs.
The Covington plant opening is an early example of a nationwide movement to cost-effectively recycle and repurpose EV batteries as more drivers go electric.
In previous decades, companies had not invested much in lithium-ion recycling.
Investment in lithium-ion recycling soared in the last few years to match spiking demand for battery materials.
Recycling can deliver new battery materials without the expense and environmental impact of new mining.
It is extremely hard to develop new mines in the U.S.
The federal government is lavishing funds on new battery recycling plants.
The revamped EV tax credits call for increasing shares of domestically sourced batteries and battery materials.
Market and policy shifts made recycling sufficiently desirable that Ascend is paying other companies for their old batteries.
At the moment, those deals are mostly with EV or battery makers that have high volumes to get rid of.
Ascend CEO Mike O’Kronley stated that paying for spent batteries keeps them from going into the landfill and is better than throwing them away.
Ascend also accepts used consumer electronics from battery-collection programs, such as Call2Recycle.
Currently, 80 to 90 percent of what goes into Ascend’s Covington facility is scrap materials from battery factories.
This includes scrap materials from SK Battery America’s plant in Commerce, Georgia.
That relationship influenced Ascend’s choice of location, as Covington sits in the emerging “Battery Belt.”
The Battery Belt is a swath of new battery factories and electric-vehicle plants opening up across the Midwest and the Carolinas, Georgia, Tennessee and Kentucky.
Fellow battery-recycling startup Redwood Materials also chose South Carolina for a forthcoming $3.5 billion recycling facility.

Ascend CEO Mike O’Kronley stated there will need to be a recycling plant within about an hour’s drive of every single new battery gigafactory because EV batteries are classified as Class 9 hazardous materials.
Nearby battery factories send their scrap down the road to Ascend for “pre-processing.”
The scrap and used batteries go through mechanical shredding and sieving, which produces “black mass.”
Ascend extracts lithium carbonate from the black mass.
The remaining mass contains materials such as graphite, nickel, cobalt and manganese.
Currently, Ascend sells most of these substances to the market.
Ascend also converts some black mass into cathode precursor and cathode active materials at its Massachusetts R&D center.
The company is building a second commercial-scale facility in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
The Kentucky facility will take outputs from Covington and convert them into cathode precursor and cathode active material so they are ready for new battery manufacturing.
That $1 billion plant received $480 million in grant funding from the Department of Energy as part of the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law’s investment in domestic supply for critical materials.
The Covington plant operates similarly to existing battery recycling plants.
The Kentucky location will introduce a brand-new technique for efficiently extracting cathode materials from black mass, which Ascend has dubbed “hydro to cathode.”

Ascend CEO Mike O’Kronley stated those two facilities represent the investment being made in key infrastructure to recover batteries, retain critical elements in the United States, and return them into the supply.
The surging popularity of EVs will produce far more worn-out batteries than the country’s recyclers can process.
Ascend is already out raising money to build more plants, according to O’Kronley.
Redwood Materials was founded by Tesla co-founder JB Straubel.
Redwood Materials won a $2 billion conditional loan from the DOE for a Nevada plant to make new batteries from recycled materials.
Canada-based Li-Cycle received a $375 million conditional DOE loan for its own facility to process lithium carbonate from a network of recycling plants.
Cirba Solutions is expanding a battery-recycling plant in Ohio.
All of these facilities tie into the Biden administration’s goal to make the U.S. more capable of supplying itself with batteries pivotal to electrifying transportation and decarbonizing the grid.
source_title: One of the biggest battery recycling plants in the US… | Canary Media

facts: Metaplant America is located in Bryan County, GA.
Production at the Metaplant America started in late-2024.
The Metaplant America investment totals $7.6B.
An LG-Hyundai battery joint venture is located in Cartersville, GA.
The LG-Hyundai battery JV has a capacity of 30 GWh and is scheduled for commissioning in 2026.
Hyundai Metaplant Georgia produces the EV6 and EV9 models.
Projected revenue at full ramp for Hyundai Metaplant Georgia EV6/EV9 is ~$4.5–5.5B.
Hyundai adopted the NACS charging standard for the Ioniq 5 in 2025 and the Ioniq 9 in 2026.
source_title: Global Electric Vehicle Market Size, Share, Forecast & Strategic Outlook | 2025–2032

facts: Ascend Elements has opened the biggest EV battery recycling center in Georgia, making it the largest in North America.
Ascend Elements celebrated the grand opening of its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility this week.
The $50 million Base 1 facility is located in Covington, Georgia.
The facility began partial operations in August 2022.
The facility now has an annual capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
The processing capacity is equal to 70,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O’Kronley stated that the facility is an important part of America’s EV battery infrastructure and just the start.
Lithium-ion battery recycling is becoming an increasingly important part of the EV battery materials supply chain as demand for electric vehicles increases.
Ascend Elements recovers up to 98% of the critical battery metals in used electric vehicle batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap.
The company uses its patented Hydro-to-Cathode™ process to recycle used lithium-ion batteries and scrap.
The Hydro-to-Cathode™ process can reduce the carbon footprint of new electric vehicle battery cathode materials by up to 90%.
The facility is powered by renewable energy.
The facility features onsite wastewater recycling and treatment systems.
The facility currently employs about 100 team members.
Ascend Elements plans to increase the facility's headcount to 185 by 2024.
Ascend Elements is based in Westborough, Massachusetts.
Ascend Elements produces lithium-ion battery precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).
The company's Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis technology produces new CAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods.
The technology results in improved economics and lowered GHG emissions.
source_title: Ascend Elements Launches Largest EV Battery Recycling Facility in North America

entity_name: Development Authority of Bainbridge and Decatur County
canonical_name: Development Authority of Bainbridge and Decatur County
facts: Executive Director Rick McCaskill reported that Anovion Technologies has a site in the Down Range Industrial Park and is currently in a "fundraising mode."
President Donald Trump’s changes to electric vehicle, battery, and tariff policies have affected Anovion Technologies’ ability to find investors.
Anovion Technologies is rebuilding its investor package and still expects to start construction this year.
Southern Electric Motorworks is in the process of engineering its property in the Commodore Industrial Park.
CSX and Georgia Southwestern Railroad are actively sending business prospects to the Bainbridge-Decatur County region.
Decatur County participates in Locate South Georgia, a group of 23 counties that markets the region for business and industry.
Executive Director Rick McCaskill found new prospects through trade shows and the February Georgia Quail Hunt.
The Development Authority of Bainbridge and Decatur County board approved its fourth-quarter financials, with McCaskill noting the authority "paid a lot of debt off" in 2025.
A bond resolution to provide millions of dollars for Spring Creek Charter Academy’s campus renovation is expected to be finalized within the next three or four weeks.
Several prospects are seeking space for a spec building in the Commodore Industrial Park.
The Development Authority of Bainbridge and Decatur County’s next meeting is scheduled for Thursday, May 21, at noon at Bainbridge City Council Chambers.
source_title: Development Authority gets Anovion, Chamber updates | The Post-Searchlight

facts: Eric Stopka is the president of Anovion Technologies.
Stopka evaluated 15 states while searching for a battery materials factory site.
Three of the five most important site selection criteria were power-related.
The plant required a vast amount of electricity, fast ramp-up capability, and clean power.
Stopka stated he does not want to be the company dragging down its parent company's net-zero goals.
Anovion rejected a potential site because its grid was largely fired by fossil fuels and the 10-year plan was outside their comfort range.
In May, Anovion selected Bainbridge, Georgia (a town of fewer than 15,000 in the southwest corner) as its factory location.
The Bainbridge site allows the factory to build a turnout on the local train line and ship by barge on the nearby Flint River.
To staff the facility, Anovion is in talks with the local technical college for 400 employees.
Georgia Power has promised to deliver more zero-carbon energy over time to support the site.
Georgia has two large nuclear power plants, and regulators approved a plan last year for Georgia Power to add 2,300 MW of renewables by 2025.
Anovion's site will require a new substation and transformers before production begins at scale.
The company plans to build a second large factory and then a third facility in the future.
Stopka noted that utilities willing to start and move quickly scored higher, emphasizing that breaking ground starts the clock ticking.
Anovion makes synthetic graphite, one of the most voluminous ingredients of lithium-ion batteries.
The company received a $117 million grant last fall from the Department of Energy funded by the 2021 bipartisan infrastructure law.
Domestic synthetic graphite production aims to ease fears that availability could vanish during U.S.-China tensions, as China currently overwhelmingly controls graphite production.
Anovion's manufacturing technique starts with needle coke, a petroleum waste material described as "the last 15 percent of the barrel."
The needle coke is poured into metal and ceramic crucibles a meter or two long.
A monster electric current heats the petroleum to more than 5,400 degrees Fahrenheit for nearly a month.
The process yields synthetic graphite, a gray substance with the consistency of baking powder ready for EV battery integration.
Anovion is one of dozens of EV-related companies flocking to the southeastern U.S., where local officials aggressively court them and a cultural aversion to labor unions may trim employment costs.
Stopka said people do not typically think of southwest Georgia as a clean energy region but were pleasantly surprised by the site's attributes.
The battery supply chain anticipates pressure from downstream customers, especially automakers, to make products with immaculate environmental standards.
Europe passed new rules to track the energy footprint of battery materials back to the mine.
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission is drafting requirements for public companies to report carbon emissions.
Venkat Srinivasan is a chemical engineer at Argonne National Laboratory and director of the Argonne Collaborative Center for Energy Storage Science.
Srinivasan said in 10-20 years they want a different world and need to ensure batteries use clean energy as the linchpin to a clean energy future.
Battery supply chain executives say electricity cleanliness is top of mind for factory siting.
Stopka thinks the new battery and vehicle supply chain will transcend any growth seen in the U.S. in the last 150 years.
The search for zero-carbon electricity acts as an invisible hand guiding where factories get built.
Li-Bridge, a battery industry consortium, reported that shovel-ready sites for large electricity demands are in short supply and need access to large amounts of clean, reliable energy.
Big wind and solar energy projects are constrained by a lack of transmission and clogged grid interconnection queues.
Making 1 kilowatt-hour of battery requires an input of 47 kWh of energy, according to Argonne National Laboratory estimates.
Seven years ago, Argonne researchers visited Chinese battery plants that used mostly natural gas.
Ford Motor Co.’s F-150 Lightning has a battery of 131 kWh in its largest format.
Synthesizing one F-150 Lightning battery would use more than 6 megawatts of energy per Argonne estimates.
Celina Mikolajczak is a manufacturing veteran and chief battery technology officer at Lyten, a California maker of lithium-sulfur batteries.
Anovion's synthetic graphite production process highlights the battery industry's extreme energy needs driven by temperature control.
Downstream battery-making mixes powders and liquid into slurry, which is pasted onto metal sheets.

Ovens remove water and chemicals from the slurry; almost 47 percent of the energy needed to make a battery cell comes from this drying process per a 2021 Worcester Polytechnic Institute and A123 Systems LLC study.
Some materials like nickel are irreversibly changed by moisture, requiring entire assembly shops to be enclosed in dry rooms with constant air conditioning.
The Worcester Polytechnic study said another 29 percent of battery manufacturing energy use is from air conditioners.
More than three-quarters of a battery's energy use is controlling temperature.
Heating produces huge energy bills and contributes substantially to the battery's finished cost.
Srinivasan noted that "temperature is money."
Automakers are seeking "dry cell" technology to fabricate electrodes without slurry, potentially slashing energy use nearly in half.
Tesla considers dry cells key to continuing its EV dominance and acquired Maxwell Technologies in 2019 for this technology.
Tesla cited dry-cell tech as a key innovation for its new 4680 battery cell to reduce the energy and footprint of making cells tenfold.
Musk tweeted in 2021 that dry electrode is key to lowering lithium battery costs but required immense engineering to reach volume production.
Tesla did not reply to a query about current plans to deploy dry cells.
Volkswagen AG announced last month it is scaling up dry-cell technology, which could save hundreds of millions of euros a year.
Battery makers in the U.S. do not have to use renewables or carbon-free power but feel urgency due to climate goals and business reasons.
Gene Berdichevsky is co-founder and CEO of Sila Nanotechnologies, which is building a battery materials factory in Washington state.
Berdichevsky said limits will be set on the CO2 footprint per kilowatt-hour of battery production over time.
Carmakers needing to stay below emission caps will pay more for products that help them stay under limits.
Sila's principal investor and customer is Mercedes-Benz AG.
Recent EU regulations vow to rigorously track the carbon footprint of batteries used in Europe's EVs.
Sila builds silicon-based anode powder by cooking materials in electric furnaces and sited its factory on a power grid in eastern Washington with abundant hydropower for a low carbon footprint.
Mikolajczak said any executive looks at the carbon footprint of their factory because factories run for 30 years and must be cost-effective.
Energy requirements drive battery startups' conversations with electric utilities and sway where factories get built.
Aqua Metals Inc. is a battery recycler recovering high-purity critical minerals from spent batteries.
Steve Cotton is president of Aqua Metals.
Old batteries are chopped into black mass, immersed in proprietary liquid, and electroplated to deposit metals on a sheet.

Aqua Metals' pilot plant in Reno, Nev., intends to soak 3,000 metric tons a year of black mass and produce $60 million worth of critical minerals including cobalt, manganese dioxide, lithium hydroxide, nickel, and copper.
Electroplating uses tens of megawatts of electricity, giving the plant an energy footprint similar to a data center.
Power for Aqua Metals comes from NV Energy, which promised 40 percent renewable power; the remaining 60 percent will come from renewable energy certificates.

Cotton said by the end of the decade, net zero requires every electron from a clean source, so the plant will have massive solar (about a megawatt across roofs and parking canopies on a 5-acre campus).
Amprius Technologies Inc. is a California battery cell maker that announced a new 1.3 million-square-foot factory outside Denver in March, located in a former Costco distribution warehouse.
Amprius CEO Kang Sun said Denver's low humidity will lower energy bills and with 300 days of sunlight, they can take advantage of solar and wind energy.
source_title: Why EV battery makers are so hungry for clean energy - E&E News by POLITICO

facts: Anovion Technologies announces plans for an $800 million initial investment in a new manufacturing facility in Southwest Georgia.
The facility will produce 40k metric tpa of synthetic graphite anode material for lithium-ion batteries.
Anovion selected Decatur County in Southwest Georgia as the location for its first large-scale expansion of manufacturing capacity for premium synthetic graphite anode materials.
The initial facility will be 1,500,000 square feet.

The location was chosen based on proximity to existing and planned low- and carbon-free energy sources, short supply chains, access to existing rail infrastructure, a highly valuable skilled workforce, and a business-friendly environment.
Eric Stopka is the Chief Executive Officer of Anovion.

Anovion conducted a thorough search to identify a location addressing energy, transportation, logistics, and human capital needs for producing high-quality sustainable synthetic graphite anode material.
Existing infrastructure makes Southwest Georgia attractive, and proximity to other battery and EV manufacturing plants will help reduce carbon footprint.
Governor Brian Kemp welcomed Anovion to Georgia, calling it the No. 1 state for business and fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Kemp noted that job creators recognize every corner of the state has resources and workforce needed for success.
Governor Kemp expressed looking forward to celebrating their partnership with Georgians of Decatur County after meeting their team personally.
In October 2022, Anovion was awarded a grant totaling $117 million from the U.S. Department of Energy under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
The grant aims to help secure the domestic battery manufacturing supply chain for the country's electrified future.
The new expansion facility will be the first of this scale for Anovion and in North America.
It is a crucial first step to reshoring synthetic graphite anode production.
Once fully operational, the facility is expected to initially produce 40,000 metric tonnes per annum.
The facility will create hundreds of high-quality local clean energy jobs for the surrounding region.
Edward Reynolds is the Bainbridge Mayor.
He welcomed Anovion to Bainbridge.
The investment involves $800 million and the creation of over 400 high-paying, quality jobs.
Mayor Reynolds stated the workforce is ready to embrace the opportunity and that the community will grow and prosper together.
Anovion will use an innovative, sustainable, and technologically advanced manufacturing process at the new facility.
The process relies on the region's rapidly decarbonizing energy mix to power manufacturing.
It introduces proprietary furnace technologies that substantially reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
This lays out a clear roadmap for Anovion to achieve a carbon-neutral footprint over time.
Chip Dunn is the Executive Chairman of Anovion.
The company's long-term strategic vision focuses on growth and innovation, positioning Anovion as a leader in the U.S. energy transition.
A key goal includes securing a domestic source for critical materials required for U.S. lithium-ion battery production.
Focus areas include continued R&D in electrochemistry, technology improvements in production processes, and ambitious climate tech initiatives.
The ultimate goal is to become carbon-neutral over time, mitigate greenhouse gas emissions, and position Anovion as a climate tech leader.
Anovion is excited to bring its full arsenal of innovation to the Georgia facility to execute its long-term business strategy and combat the climate crisis.
In addition to efforts in Georgia, Anovion is expanding its Anovion Center of Excellence.
The company is also expanding a hydro-powered sister manufacturing facility near Niagara Falls, NY.
The Niagara Falls facility is notably one of the only qualified U.S. sources of battery-grade synthetic graphite that is commercially shipping product today.
Anovion Technologies is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois.
It is a U.S.-owned and U.S.-based advanced materials business.
It is currently one of America's first commercial-scale manufacturers of premium, cleaner anode-grade synthetic graphite.
The company's existing footprint includes facilities near Niagara Falls, NY and Clarksburg, WV.
Anovion began commercial production in early 2021.
It is among a limited number of graphite anode producers in America to have successfully gained qualification for EV applications.
In 2022, Anovion was awarded the $117 million DOE grant to rapidly scale production capacity to meet growing demand.
Plans include construction of a large-scale factory in the southeastern U.S. and investment in its relocated Anovion Center of Excellence (ACE) in Sanborn, NY.
Anovion is commencing a multi-year expansion program of 150,000 metric tonnes of annual capacity for anode-grade synthetic graphite.
The announcement was made on May 15, 2023.
source_title: Anovion Technologies Announces Plans For $800 Million Initial Investment in New Manufacturing Facility in Southwest Georgia - 40k Metric Tpa of Synthetic Graphite Anode Material for Lithium-ion Batteries - Batteries News

facts: Construction has started on a new, battery-grade manufacturing facility in Augusta.
Syensqo is building an EV manufacturing facility beside their existing plant, formerly known as Solvay Polymers.
Cal Wray, President of the Augusta Economic Development Authority, stated that EV is one component but the project adds manufacturing and advanced manufacturing while supplying the EV sector.
The material produced will be used by 456 other industries.
The project represents growth of the local advanced manufacturing hub, providing value-added or higher manufacturing that pays higher wages.
The facility is expected to create 100 full-time local manufacturing jobs that will require skilled labor.
The facility’s products will support Georgia’s growing electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing ecosystem by providing material for more than five million EV batteries per year at full capacity.
The project will create hundreds of jobs throughout the value chain.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is developing an e-mobility supply chain that will position the state as a leader in this industry for years to come.
Gov. Brian Kemp expressed excitement for Syensqo to become a valued part of the ecosystem and the Augusta-Richmond County community.
The total investment for the project is $850 million.

The employment and financial impact will be felt across the seven-county region of Richmond, Columbia, Lincoln, Burke, and McDuffie counties in Georgia, and Aiken and Edgefield counties in South Carolina.
Augusta Mayor Garnett Johnson attended the groundbreaking event.
The City of Augusta and the Augusta Economic Development Authority have worked to invest millions of dollars in tax savings on this project.
The facility will supply polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF).
PVDF is used as a lithium-ion binder and separator coating in electric vehicle batteries.
The Augusta plant will be the largest PVDF producer in North America.

Mike Finelli, Chief Technology & Innovation Officer and Chief North America Officer at Syensqo, thanked the Georgia Department of Economic Development for their support in expanding the company's footprint in Augusta.

The expansion enables the creation of new jobs, reinforces the local economy, and underscores the strategic importance of Augusta as a key player in the EV supply chain along the burgeoning Battery Belt.
The project further enhances the Augusta area’s notoriety on the world stage as an attractive place to build a facility.
Syensqo, formerly known as Solvay, is a worldwide known name.
This will be the largest facility of its type in the U.S.
Key suppliers in the EV industry from major producers of autos and batteries will know where Augusta is because they are likely getting the product from Syensqo.
Syensqo is based in Belgium.
The company’s U.S. headquarters is in Alpharetta.
Along with the manufacturing facility in Augusta, the company supports 800 jobs in Georgia.
source_title: $850 million investment for manufacturing plant announced - Augusta Business Daily

facts: Hyundai celebrated the groundbreaking for its 3,000-acre, $5.54 billion, 8,100-job EV manufacturing complex in Bryan County on Oct. 25.
The ceremony eased fears that a tiff over federal tax credits would slam the brakes on the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America project.
South Korea’s ambassador to the United States used the event to lobby for Congress to delay a domestic assembly requirement for the $7,500 credit.
The Savannah-area plant is due to go live in 2025.
Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson said it already is producing “opportunities in the EV revolution for Georgians.”
On Oct. 19, a $178.2 million grant from the U.S. Energy Department was awarded for a proposed Augusta plant.
Solvay Specialty Polymers, a European conglomerate with an EV-related innovation center in Alpharetta, was one of 21 winners of grants totaling $2.8 billion to support domestic EV battery production.
Solvay would have to match the grant and has yet to announce that it will move forward.
The Augusta plant would employ 100 people to produce polyvinylidene fluoride, known as PVDF, a coating material that extends battery life and driving range.

Solvay and Boston-based Orbia, which supplies the raw materials needed to produce PVDF, announced a joint venture Nov. 3 to invest about $670 million on top of the federal grant to build the Augusta plant.
Operations at the Augusta plant should begin in 2026.
The Energy Department says the plant could annually produce enough PVDF for more than 5 million batteries.
For perspective, the Hyundai Metaplant has a target of 300,000 vehicles a year.
Atlanta-based Delta Air Lines is investing $60 million in California’s Joby Aviation so premium customers can fly from home to the airport as well as between airports.
Joby’s zero-emission electric aircraft takes off and lands vertically, can carry four passengers up to 150 miles at 200 mph.
Delta’s initial investment, announced Oct. 11, could grow to $200 million and buys five years of U.S. and British partnership exclusivity from the time the joint service starts.
The plan is to begin in New York and Los Angeles at a date uncertain, although Joby aims to launch an independent air taxi service as early as 2024.
The Russell Innovation Center for Entrepreneurs in Atlanta is partnering with Sandy Springs–based UPS to provide data, services, and market access to Black-owned small businesses.
The UPS Logistics Launchpad opened at RICE on Oct. 11, providing a UPS Store and warehouse and fulfillment services run by UPS-owned Ware2Go.
UPS supply chain and logistics experts are on site at the launchpad.
RICE CEO Jay Bailey said in a press release that Atlanta’s corporations can have powerful inspiration on entrepreneurs.
source_title: Pucks, Pluck, and EV Power - Atlanta Magazine

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at its manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday.
The last working day for 958 plant employees was Friday, representing about 37% of the company's workforce.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
The Commerce, Georgia plant will continue to employ about 1,600 workers.
Human resources chief Chuck Moore filed a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice regarding the layoffs.
SK opened the $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The plant notably supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck before Ford announced plans to cancel its fully electric version in December.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated the workforce reduction was made to align operations with market conditions.
Collier said: “SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and to building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.”
Collier added that the company is pursuing future customers, including in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
The City of Commerce and Jackson County commission chair did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Georgia U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff stated: “Let’s be clear: these were battery manufacturing jobs and now they’re gone.”
Ossoff added that Trump’s policies are hurting Georgia’s economy and that Georgians are losing jobs after a period of booming plant construction.
SK had announced in June 2020 plans to invest $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
At the time, Gov. Brian Kemp’s office stated the expansion would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are still jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has attracted other massive EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s own $7.6 billion factory complex.
Georgia benefited significantly from Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act, with 33 additional green energy projects announced by the end of 2024, the most nationwide according to E2.
Projects in Georgia topped $20 billion in value, pledging more than 25,000 jobs.
Qcells, a unit of South Korea’s Hanwha Solutions, resumed normal production on Friday after temporarily reducing hours and pay due to U.S. customs detaining imported solar panel components.
A spokesperson for Kemp's office stated that Georgia’s job market remains robust, with over 219,700 new jobs announced since 2019.
EVs accounted for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025, remaining similar to a year earlier.

Automakers including Ford, General Motors, and Stellantis have reneged on factory, investment, and product plans, laid off workers, and pivoted some efforts to hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles.
Under President Donald Trump, Congress eliminated tax credits of up to $7,500 for consumers' purchases of new or used EVs.
The Trump administration announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules for automakers, eliminating federal incentives for cleaner vehicle fleets.
source_title: SK lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Georgia plant amid cooling automaker EV plans - Newsday

entity_name: Georgia Solar and Storage Success Stories
canonical_name: Solar Energy Industries Association (SEIA) Georgia Success Stories
facts: At the federal, state, and local levels, SEIA works to advocate for an equitable transition to a clean energy economy.
SEIA produces variety of research and supporting resources for the solar industry, ranging from full reports to short factsheets.

The Energy Storage Market Outlook (ESMO) is a quarterly publication produced by the Solar Energy Industries Association and Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, drawing on Benchmark’s proprietary grid and behind the meter data.
The Major Solar Projects List is a database of all ground-mounted solar projects, 1 MW and above, that are either operating, under construction or under development.
SEIA is building a resource for member companies to identify areas that are likely to qualify as Energy Communities (ECs) as defined in the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
SEIA members can access an interactive version of this map, as well as additional details on the methodology and justification for including the areas shown.

The Solar Energy Industries Association® (SEIA) is leading the transformation to a clean energy economy, working with its 1,200 member companies and other strategic partners to fight for policies that create jobs in every community and shape fair market rules that promote competition and the growth of reliable, low-cost solar power.
Founded in 1974, SEIA is the national trade association for the solar and solar + storage industries, building a comprehensive vision for the Solar+ Decade through research, education and advocacy.
The U.S. energy storage industry installed 9.7 gigawatt-hours (GWh) of new capacity in the first quarter of 2026, the strongest first quarter in the sector’s history.
A rooftop solar array helped a couple in Savannah keep the lights on after Hurricane Helene, installed thanks to a grant helping make solar more affordable.
The couple used their array to provide solar for them and their neighbors in the aftermath of Hurricane Helene.
Qcells announced a $2.5 billion investment for a manufacturing facility in Cartersville.
The Qcells facility will manufacture 3.3 GW of modules, cells, wafers, and ingots, and will be the first vertically integrated c-Si solar manufacturer in the United States.
The SAE School in Mableton became the first school in Georgia to become 100% solar powered.
This allows the school to save $15,000 a year in electricity costs, allowing the school to spend more money on the children.
The Savannah City Council voted to add a cumulative 1.26 MW of rooftop solar to 14 city buildings.
The solar arrays will provide over 1/3 of the city's electricity needs and save the city -- and taxpayers-- a total of $1.4 million over the project's lifespan.
Shaw Industries installed an innovative 300 kW solar array at its carpet tile manufacturing and recycling facility.
This solar array will produce enough electricity to power 45 homes and will help the manufacturer greatly reduce its energy needs.
Suniva recently restarted solar cell manufacturing at its Norcross facility, producing the first cells made in America since 2019.
This 1 GW facility will provide cells for many module producers around the country, helping contribute to the American solar manufacturing surge.
The Northwest Georgia Medical Clinic reported a large reduction of its energy footprint, just 11 months after it installed a rooftop solar array.
This array offset more than half of the hospital's energy usage, leading to significant savings and protection from power rate increases.
Becker Robotic Equipment announced a new $30 million manufacturing facility that will create 137 new jobs in Canton.
This advanced manufacturing facility will include solar panels on all three of its buildings.
Yonah Mountain Vineyards, a popular tourist attraction and wedding venue in White County, has a 106 kW solar array onsite.
This is coupled with solar shingles on the barn roof that provides over 60% of the vineyard's annual energy usage.
Hyundai Motor announced it signed a 15 year power purchase agreement to power its EV plant in Bryan County.
The manufacturing plant will receive 147 MW of power from a solar project in Texas once the solar project is operational in 2025.
Atlanta Motorsports Park in Dawsonville added a 402 kW solar array that includes modules from Qcells' Georgia manufacturing facility.
The panels were installed thanks to the Rural Energy for America Program, which allowed the motorsports facility to recoup 50% of its total costs.
K-Machine partnered with Georgia based Velo Solar to install a rooftop solar array to its industrial equipment maintenance and warehouse in Richmond Hill.
This solar project will save the company nearly $50,000 per year and $2.2 million over the project's lifespan.
Georgia Power recently projected that power demand is expected to triple in Georgia by the end of the decade, fueled in part by data centers.
Many of these data centers are turning with solar to provide power, making solar a key in meeting Georgia's expected load growth.
Piana Technology expanded its rooftop solar array at its textile manufacturing site in Cartersville.
The solar array will provide up to 70% of the manufacturer's electricity needs, and any excess is sold back to the city to contribute to the city's electricity supply.
Vanderlande moved to a new building in Marietta that includes a rooftop solar array.
This will help the company that employs nearly 1,000 people to reduce electricity costs by more than 25%.
Hanwha Advanced Materials from South Korea announced a $147 million manufacturing investment to supply EVA backsheets to Qcells.
This will bring 160 manufacturing jobs to the district and help build out the American solar supply chain.
Canton hosted a ribbon cutting for its downtown parking deck by city hall.
This includes an 80 kW solar carport that will provide electricity for EV charging and offset the city's electricity costs.
The 60+ year old Bibb Distributing Company is concerned about giving back to the community and being a responsible Georgian company.
To do so, the distributor installed rooftop solar panels in 2021.
Five students from Georgia Tech worked with the Lifeline Animal Project to install rooftop solar.
This will help the Atlanta animal shelter to save $1.6 million over the next 20 years.
A 25 MW solar project in Appling County began generating solar power for Meta's Newton Data Center.
This is helping meet the power demand increase created by data centers.
The project used 150 craft workers to complete, many of whom were from the local community.
Tybee Island Marine Science Center is an aquarium that helps nurse sea turtles, alligators, fish, and corn snakes back to health and helps teach Georgians about these animals.
Since aquarium tanks are expensive, the aquarium installed rooftop solar that has already led to energy savings in just 6 months.
American Peanut Growers Group uses a roof mounted solar array to generate 600,000 kWh of electricity at its drying shelters.
This significantly lowers energy costs for the farmers, which can get passed on as savings for consumers.
The South Fulton Fire Department installed a solar array that will save the fire department money while also providing emergency power during emergencies.
A 139 MW solar array next to Robins Air Force Base supplies electricity to the base.
This provides cost-effective electricity that can help the military meet its energy security and resilience needs.
A trailer from the United Methodist Church brought a solar microgrid to Valdosta to help with recovery after Hurricane Helene.
This provided much needed electricity to Georgians and came from the United Methodist Church.
