SOLARCYCLE, a solar recycling company, announced a state-of-the-art recycling facility in Cedartown.
This facility will recycle solar panels and use that to make glass for new modules.
The facility will create more than 600 full time manufacturing jobs and an estimated $344 million investment.
Two churches in Statesboro, the Trinity Episcopal Church and the Unitarian Universalist Fellowship, added solar installations with Georgia BRIGHT.
This will save the Unitarian Universalist Fellowship $9,000 and Trinity Episcopal Church $62,000.
The Oakhurst Presbyterian Church in Decatur completed a rooftop solar array that will save the church $146,000 over the project's life span.
A spokesperson highlighted new federal regulations that allow the congregation to access funds through the direct pay mechanism.

source_title: Georgia Solar and Storage Success Stories – SEIA

facts: The U.S. has installed more solar capacity than any other type of power generation for five years running.
Solar cells, which convert sunlight into electricity, have been manufactured almost entirely overseas during the U.S. solar boom.
American factories could assemble finished panels, but the critical cell component came from elsewhere.
QCells operates a sprawling, L-shaped factory in Cartersville, Georgia.
The facility is closing a long-standing gap in domestic solar cell manufacturing.
By the time the Cartersville factory came online, U.S. panel assembly capacity could produce nearly 70 gigawatts of finished panels annually.
This 70 GW panel assembly capacity far exceeds what the country installs in a single year, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association.
Before QCells began production, only three U.S. companies made solar cells: Suniva in Georgia, and ES Foundry and Silfab in South Carolina.
The combined operational capacity of those three pre-QCells U.S. cell makers was roughly 3 gigawatts.
Cell manufacturing remained the weak link in an otherwise expanding domestic supply chain.
The Biden administration recognized this gap and used the Inflation Reduction Act to introduce manufacturing tax credits for domestic cell producers.
The Inflation Reduction Act also introduced domestic-content bonuses for developers who buy American-made components.
These policies were explicitly designed to pull cell production back to the U.S. after ceding ground to overseas competitors, particularly China.
QCells is a subsidiary of South Korean industrial giant Hanwha Group.
QCells first announced the Cartersville project in early 2023.
The company pledged to colocate all four major steps of the solar supply chain under one roof: silicon ingots, wafers, cells, and finished modules.
The facility’s module lines went live in 2024.
Cell production lines took longer to calibrate and have only recently begun commercial output.
Full 3.3-gigawatt production is expected by the third quarter of this year.
When full capacity is reached, QCells alone will more than double current U.S. operational solar cell capacity.
Polysilicon enters at one end of the facility and moves sequentially through each production stage.
Finished panels roll off the line at the other end of the facility.
Combined with a module assembly plant roughly 30 miles north in Dalton, Georgia, the broader operation is expected to employ 3,800 people.
These roles are described as high-tech, robot-assisted manufacturing positions.
Cell manufacturing is more complex than panel assembly due to the use of potent chemicals like hydrofluoric acid and potassium hydroxide.
Creating functional solar cells requires precise, multi-step industrial processes.
An industry figure noted that leveraging complicated science to create a solar cell involves more intensive steps than panel assembly.
In March, Silfab temporarily shut down its South Carolina cell factory after accidentally releasing both potassium hydroxide and hydrofluoric acid in rapid succession.
State authorities found no impact to the surrounding community from the Silfab chemical release incident.
The incident illustrated the risks when chemical processes go wrong in cell manufacturing.
Another 22 gigawatts of solar cell capacity is currently under construction nationally, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association.
Toyo recently announced a $357 million investment to add 1.5 gigawatts of cell production at its Houston facility.
ES Foundry is working to expand its South Carolina factory to 3 gigawatts by year’s end.
T1 Energy is building a cell fabrication plant outside Austin.
The Trump administration phased out the installation tax credit for solar projects completed after July 4, 2025.
A four-year safe harbor provision still applies to projects already in the pipeline.
Once those projects are built or lapse, developers may lose their primary financial incentive to choose domestically produced cells over cheaper imports.
Domestic production offers resilience against foreign supply chain schedule risks highlighted by Covid-era disruptions and rising shipping costs.
American-made components delivered by truck from a nearby factory sidestep that uncertainty entirely.
Electricity consumption in the U.S. is at its highest level in a generation.
This demand is driven significantly by the rapid buildout of AI data centers.
AI companies are focused on “speed to power,” the ability to bring new generation online quickly.
Solar is well positioned to meet that need due to its relatively short installation timelines.
Whether U.S. solar cell manufacturing can sustain momentum beyond the safe harbor window remains an open question.
The convergence of industrial policy, AI-driven electricity demand, and supply-chain localization creates conditions that could support domestic production even as some incentives fade.
The Cartersville factory is cited as the most visible sign yet that the U.S. is serious about owning more of this supply chain.
source_title: For years America built solar farms with foreign parts, but a giant Georgia megafactory just changed that calculation

facts: Qcells plans to bring nearly 500 additional jobs to Whitfield County as part of its long-haul commitment to the U.S. solar market.
The investment marks Qcells’ latest efforts to rebuild the U.S. solar supply chain and help meet growing demand for decarbonized energy sources.
Qcells announced that its new solar panel manufacturing facility will be located in Dalton, Georgia.
A $171 million investment will boost production of advanced photovoltaic modules to help the U.S. achieve goals of decarbonizing the electric grid and creating good-paying manufacturing jobs.
Qcells CEO Justin Lee stated that the additional investment in Dalton will help Qcells better serve the needs of U.S. customers with increased local manufacturing capacity.
Justin Lee noted that Georgia has become the clean energy manufacturing heart of America, and Qcells is proud to contribute to the state’s advanced manufacturing economy.
The new facility will produce 1.4 gigawatts of solar modules per year made with Qcells’ next generation photovoltaic cells using high efficiency tunnel oxide passivated contact technology (TOPCon).

Located near the company’s existing 1.7-gigawatt factory in Dalton, the expansion will bring Qcells’ total U.S. capacity to 3.1 gigawatts, equivalent to one-third of the country’s solar module manufacturing capacity.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp expressed excitement over Qcells' continued expansion in Georgia, highlighting decades of outstanding partnership between the state and Korea focused on bringing jobs and opportunities to Georgians.
Qcells’ Dalton expansion is expected to bring 470 new jobs to Whitfield County.
With more than 750 staff currently employed at the existing factory, total local employees will exceed 1,000 when the expansion is complete.
Groundbreaking for the facility is planned for fall 2022, with operations expected to commence within the first half of 2023.

In March, Qcells’ parent company Hanwha Solutions became the largest shareholder of U.S. polysilicon manufacturer REC Silicon to secure reliable supply of raw material crucial to solar panel manufacturing.

Senator Jon Ossoff appreciated Qcells’ sustained and growing investment in Georgia and the working relationship built to create jobs, pledging to continue making Georgia a world leader in renewable energy innovation and manufacturing.
Qcells retains the largest market share in the U.S. commercial and residential markets.
According to the Wood Mackenzie Q1 2022 U.S. PV Leaderboard report, Qcells obtained 24.1 percent market share in the residential sector in 2021.
According to the Wood Mackenzie Q1 2022 U.S. PV Leaderboard report, Qcells obtained 20.6 percent market share in the commercial sector in 2021.
Qcells is also a leading supplier to the utility solar sector.

Senator Reverend Raphael Warnock commended Qcells’ smart investments in Georgia and pledged to work closely with the company to incentivize solar production and encourage additional economic investments creating good-paying jobs across North Georgia and the state.
Qcells aspires to realize its vision of being a “completely clean energy solutions provider” by working with partners in the U.S. and elsewhere to accelerate the energy transition.
Qcells provides low-carbon solar products, smart energy solutions, renewable electricity retail services, and packages to end customers worldwide.
Qcells is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea (Global Executive HQ).
Qcells maintains a Technology & Innovation HQ in Thalheim, Germany.
Qcells operates diverse international manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Malaysia, China, and South Korea.

Through a global business network spanning Europe, North America, Asia, South America, Africa, and the Middle East, Qcells provides services and long-term partnerships to customers in the utility, commercial, governmental, and residential markets.
source_title: Qcells Expands Manufacturing in Dalton, GA - Qcells North America

facts: Qcells aims to have 8.4-gigawatt of solar panel production capacity by 2024 and hire 2,500 people in the clean-energy sector.
The investment is the largest in U.S. solar history to build the nation’s only complete and sustainable solar supply chain.
Qcells announced it will invest more than $2.5 billion to build a complete solar supply chain in the United States.
The commitment will create nearly 2,500 direct jobs in Georgia.

The announcement marks the largest investment in the history of American solar and sets Qcells up as the only company in the U.S. to establish a fully-integrated, silicon-based solar supply chain from raw material to finished panel.
In the first quarter of 2023, Qcells will break ground on a new facility in Bartow County, Georgia.
The new facility will manufacture 3.3 gigawatts (GW) of solar ingots, wafers, cells, and finished panels.
The company will assemble an additional 2 GW of solar panels at its Dalton, Georgia facility.
The Dalton facility is the largest solar panel manufacturing plant in the Western Hemisphere.
The investment is expected to bring Qcells’ total solar panel production capacity in Georgia to 8.4-gigawatt by 2024.
The announcement was made by Qcells’ parent company Hanwha Solutions, headquartered in Seoul.
It represents the parent company’s largest clean energy investment to date in the U.S. and beyond.
The move aims to accelerate the United States’ renewable energy transition and domestic manufacturing expansion.
Justin Lee serves as CEO of Qcells.
Qcells opened its first factory in Georgia in 2019 and hired 750 people to manufacture 1.7 GW of solar.
Last year, Qcells announced a plan to add 1.4 GW to its Dalton manufacturing output and hire 535 more people.
Today’s announcement means Qcells will hire an additional 510 people in Dalton.
This brings Qcells’ total employment in Georgia to 4,000 and accumulated investment in the state to $2.8 billion.
Governor Brian Kemp commented on the growth of Qcells in Georgia for a second time in less than a year.
Qcells investments across the full solar value chain follow the passage of the Solar Energy Manufacturing for America Act within the Inflation Reduction Act.
U.S. Secretary of Energy Jennifer M. Granholm stated that building a strong solar supply chain will tackle the climate crisis while creating good-paying manufacturing jobs.
Senator Jon Ossoff wrote and passed major legislation to bring more solar manufacturing jobs to Georgia and called this the largest clean energy manufacturing project in American history.
Senator Reverend Warnock helped lead the fight to make Georgia a top state for clean energy jobs and collaborated with Qcells and Senate leadership on the Solar Energy Manufacturing for America Act.
According to the Q4 2022 U.S. PV Leaderboard report from Wood Mackenzie as of the third quarter 2022, Qcells topped the U.S. residential solar module market for five years running from 2018 to 2022.
Qcells has been the leading module provider for the U.S. commercial sector since 2019.
The investment will pave the way for Qcells to continue its leadership as a major supplier and developer of utility solar projects.
Investments will occur at two sites with a greenfield expansion encompassing ingots, wafers, cells, and more modules.
The new site will have 3.3 GW annual manufacturing capacity per value chain and be the largest fully integrated solar manufacturing site in the U.S.
Qcells aims to begin commercial production at the new site in late 2024.
The new site will be located in Cartersville, Georgia, about 55 miles outside of Atlanta.
The build-out follows an expansion of annual solar panel production to 5.1 GW in Dalton, Georgia by 2023.
The Dalton expansion comes from building a third module manufacturing facility producing an additional 2-GW of solar panels per year.
Qcells is currently operating a 1.7 GW module factory at Dalton and began construction earlier in 2022 to increase the facility to 3.1 GW.
Hanwha recently became the leading shareholder in polysilicon producer REC Silicon.

Efforts are underway to manufacture low-carbon polysilicon at REC Silicon’s factory in Moses Lake, Washington, which is powered by emission-free hydroelectricity and will restart production in the second half of 2023.
Qcells is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea (Global Executive HQ) and Thalheim, Germany (Technology & Innovation HQ), with manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Malaysia, China, and South Korea.
source_title: Qcells Invests $2.5 Billion in U.S. Solar Supply Chain - Qcells North America

facts: Freyr Battery abandoned plans to build a giant electric battery factory in Atlanta’s suburbs after it shifted to buy a solar panel plant in Texas.
Freyr Battery told officials in Newnan that it wouldn’t build a $2.6 billion plant that was supposed to hire more than 700 people.
The factory would have built batteries to store electricity produced by renewable sources and release it later.
It would have been the second-largest battery factory worldwide when it was announced in 2023.
Freyr, a startup founded in 2018, never began construction on the 368-acre (149-hectare) site.
Freyr moved its corporate headquarters from Norway to Newnan in part to maximize its eligibility for the U.S. tax benefits of President Joe Biden’s climate law.
The company shifted its focus to a newly opened solar panel factory that it bought last year for $340 million from top Chinese solar panel maker Trina Solar.

Freyr spokesperson Amy Jaick wrote in a statement that the company is grateful for support and partnership in Coweta County and throughout Georgia, but is focusing at the moment on the solar module manufacturing facility in Texas.
Freyr senior vice president of business development Jason Peace met Thursday with the Coweta County Development Authority.
Jason Peace blamed rising interest rates, falling battery prices and a change in leadership at the company for the decision not to build the factory.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development said the state conveyed a $7 million grant to buy a site for Freyr in Newnan, about 35 miles (55 kilometers) southwest of Atlanta.
Department spokesperson Jessica Atwell said the state and company are working together to ensure the money is repaid expeditiously.
Jessica Atwell stated that Georgia’s incentives process protects the Georgia taxpayer, and when a company’s plans change, that process ensures discretionary incentives are repaid.
The company had said it planned to build battery factories in Norway and Finland but said in November that it will try to sell its European business.
The company also said it was terminating its license for technology to make batteries, paying $3 million to the company it was licensed from.

Tom Einar Jensen, then the company’s CEO, told investors in August that it had grown difficult to raise money to make batteries because of a surplus of Chinese batteries being produced at lower costs.
The company said it was switching its strategy into businesses that would allow it to raise cash, including solar panel manufacturing.
The company saw its cash on hand fall from $253 million at the end of 2023 to $182 million on Sept. 30.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp has targeted recruitment of the electric vehicle industry.
Korean firm SK Innovation built a $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, northeast of Atlanta and hired 3,000 workers, but later laid off or furloughed some workers.
Hyundai Motor Group has started production at a $7.6 billion electric vehicle and battery plant near Savannah, with plans to hire 8,500 workers.
Electric truck maker Rivian revived its plans to build a plant east of Atlanta after a $6.6 billion loan from the Biden administration.
source_title: Clean-energy company Freyr Battery says it's abandoning plans to build a $2.6 billion factory in Georgia, which would've created 700 new jobs | Fortune

facts: FREYR Battery announced the cancellation of its planned $2.6 billion battery manufacturing facility in Coweta County, Georgia.
The decision reflects a strategic pivot towards solar panel production following the company’s recent acquisition of a solar module manufacturing plant in Texas.

In a letter dated January 21 to the Coweta County Development Authority, FREYR cited rising interest rates, declining battery prices, and changes in company leadership as factors influencing the decision.
The January 21 letter stated, “This decision was made reluctantly, as the Company has realigned its near-term strategic goals.”
The Georgia project was originally unveiled in 2022.
The canceled Georgia project was expected to create over 700 jobs and significantly contribute to the state’s growing clean energy sector.
Construction on the Georgia facility had not commenced prior to the cancellation announcement.
FREYR has entered into an agreement to sell the 368-acre Georgia site for $50 million.
The sale transaction is anticipated to close on February 15.
Net proceeds from the site sale are expected to be approximately $22.5 million after repaying state and local grants.
FREYR emphasized that the cancellation decision does not reflect any concerns with the support provided by Georgia or local partners.
The company acknowledged the “extraordinary assistance and support” provided by state and local authorities regarding the project.
This move aligns with FREYR’s broader strategy to establish a vertically integrated U.S. manufacturing presence, beginning with its Texas facility.
FREYR plans to expand its solar manufacturing capabilities, including the development of a 5 GW solar cell facility.
Construction for the planned 5 GW solar cell facility is slated to start in the second quarter of 2025.
source_title: FREYR Battery Cancels $2.6 Billion Georgia Factory Amid Strategic Shift

facts: Freyr Battery abandoned its $2.6 billion factory project in Georgia.
The company cited high interest rates, declining battery prices, and a leadership shakeup as contributing factors in discontinuing the Georgia plant.
Lithium-ion battery pack prices plummeted 20% last year to a record-low $115 per kWh, marking the sharpest annual drop since 2017 per BloombergNEF data.
Freyr Battery pivoted from battery manufacturing to solar and energy storage, rebranding to T1 Energy.
Solar and battery storage resources accounted for about 81% of U.S. electric-generating capacity additions in 2024.
T1 Energy aims to develop solar and storage infrastructure to meet rising electricity demand from domestic manufacturing, artificial intelligence, and data centers.
Lyten secured agreements with California Sulphur Company at the Port of Los Angeles and another supplier at the Port of Stockton to provide industrial-grade sulfur for its U.S. sites.
Lyten's U.S. production facilities are located in San Leandro (acquired from Northvolt subsidiary Cuberg), San Jose, and a planned lithium-sulfur gigafactory in Nevada.
Lyten’s lithium-sulfur batteries offer a high-performance alternative to conventional lithium-ion batteries that rely on nickel, cobalt, and manganese, materials China currently controls.
Unlike lithium-ion battery materials requiring 30,000 to 50,000 miles of transport during sourcing and production, Lyten’s sulfur can be processed within 100 miles of its U.S. plants.
Lyten’s sulfur-based cathodes are 40% lighter than those in lithium-ion batteries and 70% lighter than lithium-iron phosphate components, boosting energy density and efficiency.
Lyten recently received a $650 million letter of interest from the Export-Import Bank of the United States to support its lithium-sulfur battery production.
In 2024, Lyten delivered pouch batteries to Stellantis and other manufacturers for testing and evaluation, with additional customers like Chrysler joining the lineup.

Phase 1 of Lyten’s Reno-area gigafactory will launch in 2027, focusing on producing cathode active material and lithium-metal anodes and assembling lithium-sulfur cells in pouch and cylindrical formats.
Once fully completed, the plant will have up to 10 GWh of annual capacity.
Elevated Materials, backed by Applied Materials and TPG Rise Climate, launched to develop ultra-thin lithium metal films to enhance battery performance.

The company uses a roll-to-roll vapor deposition process to manufacture lithium films with improved cycle life and up to 70% higher energy density for electric vehicles, eVTOL aircraft, and grid storage.
Elevated Materials produces ultra-thin films (<1 to 20 µm) on substrates like copper, graphite, and silicon using this deposition method.
Elevated Materials enters a competitive field where companies like Sion Power and Li-Metal Corp are also developing lithium thin films and lithium-metal anodes.
Arrow Electronics partnered with Scotland-based Dukosi to distribute contactless cell monitoring technology for high-performance batteries used in EVs and energy storage systems.

Dukosi’s decentralized battery management system replaces traditional wired architectures with a secure deterministic communication protocol, enhancing system reliability, thermal management, and cell-level analytics.
The product is scalable for hundreds of cell monitors in single or multiple networks.
source_title: Energy Storage Insights: Batteries, Solar, Lithium Film and More - News

facts: Archer Aviation is nearing completion of its brand-new electric vehicle takeoff and landing (eVTOL) manufacturing facility in Covington, Georgia.
Once up and running, Archer Aviation plans to build up to 650 Midnight eVTOL aircraft each year at the facility in partnership with long term collaborators Stellantis.
In an update on July 31, 2024, Archer said work on the first build phase was completed earlier this year.
Phase two of construction, which includes erection of the exterior walls and roof, is nearing its end as of July 31, 2024.
The third phase of construction will focus on the building’s interior.
The final phase involves installing equipment and power on the facility.
All remaining construction phases should be completed in the coming months, with completion targeted by the end of 2024.
The 400,000 square foot complex will become one of the largest manufacturing facilities by volume in the aviation industry.
Archer is continuing to carry out test flight of its Midnight eVTOL aircraft, having already completed 233 flight tests this year.
More than 70 flights have been conducted since the Midnight's transition flight.
Each flight produces critical data that teams use to evaluate aircraft loads, vibrations, performance and handling qualities.
Archer aims to carry out over 400 test flights by the end of 2024.

Adam Goldstein, CEO and founder of Archer, stated that thanks to the hard work of the Archer and Stellantis teams, they are tracking towards completing the manufacturing facility by the end of the year.
Once complete, Archer will focus on ramping production up to 650 aircraft a year, with the goal of being the first in the eVTOL industry to achieve scaled manufacturing.

links.context: Tweet image link regarding flight test data

source_title: Archer Aviation nears completion of eVTOL mega-facility

facts: California-based Archer Aviation may soon have electric air taxis operating in cities across California, Florida, Texas, New York, and Georgia.
The company has proposed an exclusive slot with Huntington Beach for Los Angeles-area operations ahead of the 2028 Olympic Games.
Archer has filed multiple applications to pilot its Midnight electric air taxis under the federal eVTOL Integration Pilot Program (eIPP).

The eIPP is a public-private initiative that will include at least five projects involving piloted and uncrewed short-range air taxis, longer-range fixed-wing aircraft, cargo, logistics, and safety systems.
Selected cities and operators for the eIPP are expected to be named by mid-2026, with first flights following later in the year.
Archer's Midnight aircraft has been tested at altitudes reaching 10,000 feet, exceeding the typical 1,500 to 5,000 feet operational range of many eVTOLs.
The Midnight is designed for high-frequency, short-range urban routes typically spanning 20 to 50 miles and flown back-to-back throughout the day.

The aircraft uses a 12-rotor lift-plus-cruise configuration with six tilting propellers on the front wing for vertical lift and forward thrust, and six fixed rotors on the rear wing for takeoff and landing.
This rotor architecture provides redundancy in case of motor or rotor failure and enables significantly lower noise levels than helicopters during forward flight.
The Midnight carries a pilot and four passengers, travels up to 150 miles per hour, and is designed for rapid turnaround charging between flights.
The electric engine is powered by six battery packs containing cylindrical lithium-ion cells.
Archer states the Midnight can recharge from 20% to 80% in about 10 minutes with minimal downtime.
Archer is currently assembling its first Midnight fleet for early commercial deployments or certification testing.
The company plans to increase annual production up to 50 aircraft across 700,000 square feet of manufacturing and testing sites located in California and Georgia.

Archer acquired the Hawthorne Airport in Los Angeles as a strategic air taxi network hub and plans to upgrade the 80-acre site with charging, maintenance, and storage facilities alongside redeveloped hangar space.

The Hawthorne Airport will serve as a testbed for AI-powered airspace integration tools including smart sensor-equipped runways, autonomous ground vehicles, advanced security systems, and AI-assisted air traffic and surface management.
At CES 2026, Archer and Nvidia unveiled a partnership to integrate Nvidia’s IGX Thor modules into future onboard computing systems for AI-assisted flight planning and safety awareness.
The partnership will also focus on merging IGX Thor with Archer's control software to support future autonomous or semi-autonomous aircraft.
If the eIPP pilot projects proceed on schedule, 2026 could mark the first time U.S. cities see electric air taxis operating across an early network.
Establishing long-term urban service in the U.S. will require local operations teams, new or upgraded infrastructure, and coordination with local emergency response and safety agencies.

Securing municipal partnerships could provide a competitive advantage as the Federal Aviation Administration and Department of Transportation (DOT) consider new airspace rules, vertiport standards, grid connections, and emergency planning needs.

The DOT’s Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) National Strategy aims to integrate eVTOL into national airspace and existing air traffic control workflows, laying out 40 recommendations covering vertiport infrastructure, security vetting requirements, and workforce needs.

By 2030, the DOT aims to enable short VTOL and powered-lift flights with accessible vertiport infrastructure and a new AAM-ready Air Traffic Control system designed to manage low-altitude traffic for both piloted aircraft and uncrewed drones.
source_title: Future Flight: Archer Plans eVTOL Taxis for U.S. Cities by 2027 - News

facts: California’s eVTOL startup Archer has chosen a site in Georgia for its first manufacturing facility in Covington, close to an airfield.
Archer targets making 650 flying cabs a year from 2024.
Production capability is expected to rise considerably within ten years.
Archer plans to initially construct out a 350,000-square-foot manufacturing facility (about 32,516 sqm).
The facility can be increased by an extra 550,000 sq. ft.
The expanded capacity will support the manufacturing of up to 2,300 planes yearly.
Archer expects to complete the primary stage in 2024.
Archer intends to invest $118 million over the next ten years.
Archer will receive an incentive bundle totaling 33% of its capital investment dedication from the state of Georgia.
Georgia is ruled by Republican Brian Kemp.
Georgia has attracted multiple investments correlated with Biden’s infrastructure funding and Inflation Reduction Act, including local incentives.
Archer listed the expert local labor market, utility availability, costs, and logistics as “key issues” for its site selection.
Archer also cited the power to conduct seamless flight test operations as a key factor.
Adam Goldstein, Archer’s CEO, stated they are excited to pick Georgia after many months of debate and analysis.
Governor Kemp said they were thrilled to welcome Archer to the state.
Georgia's Fast Start workforce training program will help fill over 1,000 jobs in Covington long-term.
Georgia is home to more than 800 aerospace firms.
Georgia has a dozen high schools that offer training in the subject.
Georgia has five technical schools with aviation programs.
Several universities in Georgia offer degrees in aerospace engineering.
Despite Georgia awaiting multiple battery firms like SK Innovation or Freyr to open cell factories, Archer opted for an abroad provider.
Molicel of Taiwan will manufacture and provide the batteries for Archer’s eVTOL aircraft, Midnight.
Molicel plans to establish its first Gigafactory in Taiwan.
Molicel's Gigafactory is scheduled to ramp up manufacturing in 2023.
The facility will have the capability to supply 1.8 gigawatt-hours (GWh) of battery capacity yearly.
Archer’s CEO pointed to Molicel’s 40+ years of high-volume manufacturing expertise in the battery business.
Archer claims it has selected more than 64% of its suppliers for its bill-of-materials for the eVTOL.
Plans for such an eVTOL surfaced in May 2020.
The Midnight, formerly known as ‘Maker’ in the prototype stage, is a four-seater aircraft.
The Midnight initially targeted intra-city travel in the form of an air taxi of around 60 miles (slightly below 100 kilometres).
The current announcement mentions back-to-back short-distance flights of about 20 miles with roughly 10-minute charging cycles in between.
Archer disclosed no information regarding battery size this time.
Early information had specified the usable battery capacity at 143 kWh.
80 kWh was meant for flight (“cruise”).
26 kWh was meant for hovering.
37 kWh was meant as a reserve.
Archer updated its cooperation with United Airways.
In February last year, Archer mentioned it needed to run 200 Archer eVTOLs finally.
Archer revealed the first route: Downtown Manhattan to Newark Liberty International Airport.
They estimate the ultra-short flight will take ten minutes using present infrastructure like the Downtown Manhattan Heliport.
Michael Leskinen, President of United Airways Ventures, stated electrical air taxis will change how United customers experience comfort, convenience, and efficiency during airport commutes.
The New York to Newark route marks a benchmark toward providing a carbon-neutral and safe commute that drastically reduces journey time and produces a fraction of the sound emitted by helicopters.
Archer refers to new transport methods between the airport to the city centre as “trunk” routes.
Once trunk routes are established, Archer will begin building out “branch” routes to connect to surrounding communities.
The first part of this UAM network is targeted for launch in 2025.
source_title: Archer will manufacture eVTOL ‘Midnight’ in Georgia - Online EV Marketplace

facts: Archer Aviation will invest $118 million to construct a plant near Atlanta, Georgia.
The aircraft plant will be built adjoining an airport in Covington, Georgia.
The company plans to eventually hire up to 1,000 people at the facility.
Archer Aviation is building small electric aircraft, specifically battery-powered vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) crafts with six propellers that hold four passengers and a pilot.
The eVTOL craft could be used for short flights, especially in urban areas.
United Airlines said it would fly the craft from downtown Manhattan to Newark Liberty International Airport in New Jersey.
The planned United Airlines route would cut a trip that can take an hour by car in congested traffic to 10 minutes.
The U.S. Air Force is evaluating Archer's aircraft for possible use.
Archer is seeking Federal Aviation Administration certification, with the company saying it could be certified by 2024.
Archer aims to produce 650 aircraft annually at the Georgia site.
Production capacity at the Georgia site has the potential to rise to 2,300 aircraft per year.
Archer expects to receive an incentive package worth some $40 million from Georgia and local governments.
The incentive package includes the donation of 96 acres of land at Covington Municipal Airport.
The incentive package includes a cash grant from the state.
The incentive package includes other tax incentives.
Archer chose Georgia because of the availability of local workers.
Archer chose Georgia for the ability to conduct test flights.
Archer chose Georgia for affordable construction costs.
Archer chose Georgia for good highway, rail, and air connections.
Archer CEO Adam Goldstein stated that their eVTOL technology can transform how urban and rural communities live and commute.
Goldstein added that the factory can create pathways to highly skilled manufacturing hubs and other ladders of social and economic opportunity.
Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson said economic recruiters specializing in aerospace had worked with Archer.
Wilson stated that "technology and innovation continue to drive change across strategic industries."
source_title: Archer Aviation will build major factory in Georgia

facts: Archer Aviation Inc. provided an update on the construction progress of its high-volume manufacturing facility in Covington, GA.
The company reaffirmed an expected completion date for the facility in 2024.
Last week, Archer showcased its Midnight aircraft at Atlantic Aviation’s new terminal at DeKalb–Peachtree Municipal Airport in Atlanta.

Guests at the showcase included government officials from Georgia’s Office of the Governor, Georgia’s Congressional Offices, the City of Atlanta, Georgia’s State Legislature, the Federal Aviation Administration, and other local governments.
Archer completed the first build phase earlier this year, which included grading and foundation work.
The company is nearing completion of phase two, which involves the erection of the exterior walls and roof.
Phase three (interior fit out) and phase four (equipment load in and power on) are on track to be completed in the coming months.
Once complete, the facility will be nearly 400,000 square feet.
The facility will support Archer’s planned production of up to 650 Midnight aircraft annually.
This production volume would make it one of the largest manufacturing facilities by volume in the aviation industry.
Archer’s buildout is taking place in close partnership with Stellantis.
Archer and Stellantis are continuing to progress their plans to mass-produce Archer’s Midnight aircraft together.
Adam Goldstein, CEO and founder of Archer, stated that the team is tracking towards completing the manufacturing facility by the end of the year.
Upon completion, Archer will focus on ramping production up to 650 aircraft a year.
Archer aims to be the first in the eVTOL industry to achieve scaled manufacturing.
Midnight is an electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft designed around Archer’s proprietary electric powertrain.
The Midnight aircraft is designed to offer passengers a sustainable, low-noise, and safe alternative to ground transportation.
The aircraft travels at speeds up to 150 mph, turning hour-long ground commutes into minutes in the air.
It is designed for back-to-back flights of 20-50 miles with minimal charge time in between.
The Midnight aircraft is 100x quieter than a helicopter at cruising altitudes and nearly inaudible from the streets below.
It features redundant systems across the aircraft, allowing Archer to target similar levels of safety as commercial airliners.
The aircraft is designed to carry a pilot, up to four passengers, and carry-on luggage.
Archer is designing and developing key enabling technologies and aircraft to power the next great transportation revolution.
Archer's goal is for its proprietary technology to deliver unprecedented connectivity to people and places across the most congested cities in the world.

source_title: Archer Aviation Showcases Midnight Aircraft In Atlanta As It Nears Completion Of Its High-Volume Manufacturing Facility In Covington, Georgia - EIN Presswire

facts: A remarkable and deliberate shift occurred in Georgia’s automotive industry over the past five years.
The shift accelerated in the last 24 months.
An entire electric vehicle (EV) ecosystem began to flourish seemingly almost out of nowhere.
source_title: Duckyang Archives - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Duckyang, a South Korean-based company, will open its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Georgia.
The company will invest $10 million to open a manufacturing plant in Jackson County.
The project is expected to create an estimated 285 jobs.
The new facility will be 230,000 square feet and located at 984 Broadway Avenue in Braselton.
Duckyang is seeking to hire for production, maintenance, logistics, and warehouse management roles.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the project will create high-paying, advanced automotive manufacturing jobs for hardworking Georgians in Jackson County.
Georgia has remained the number one state for business for the past eight years according to Governor Kemp.
Duckyang currently supplies cockpit modules to customers worldwide including Hyundai, Kia, Renault Samsung Motors, Genesis, and Mobis.
Cockpit modules are auto parts made of more than 130 subcomponents found near the driver’s seat of a vehicle.
The new Georgia facility will enable Duckyang to better serve automotive customers within the region.

Dong-in Son, CEO of Duckyang Ind. Co. Ltd., stated that entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market with SKBA will allow cooperation with other automotive companies like Hyundai and Kia Motors that already have a U.S. manufacturing base.

The company expects business expansion opportunities to supply electric vehicle parts, interior parts including cockpit modules, door trim panel modules, and other flagship parts to global automakers based in the United States.
source_title: S. Korean-based company to open first-of-its kind manufacturing plant | KRDO

facts: Duckyang Ind. Co., Ltd. was established in 1977 with its headquarters and main plant in Ulsan, Korea, and additional plants in Gyeongju and Yesan.
The company reports annual sales of approximately $11 billion.
Duckyang initially supplied crash pads for Hyundai Motor Company's Pony car, which later evolved into the IONIQ 5 electric vehicle.
The company currently manufactures cockpit modules, door trims, and crash pads as automotive interior components.

Cockpit modules are the company's best-selling product, integrating approximately 130 items such as audio, air conditioning, and airbag systems around the driver's seat in a process called 'Modulization.'
Duckyang supplies these interior components to most Hyundai Motor models produced at its Ulsan Plant in Korea.
Responding to the industry shift from internal combustion engine vehicles to electric vehicles, Duckyang developed Battery Module Assembly (BMA) and Energy Storage System (ESS) components.
The company was awarded as an R&D national organization hosted by the Korea Institute for Advancement of Technology (KIAT) for its BMA development efforts.
Duckyang operates a separate R&D department with facilities in Suwon and Gyeongju, focusing on reducing the weight of cockpit modules while maintaining quality.
The company received the 2021 National Quality Competitiveness Excellent Corporation award from the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy for eight consecutive years.
Duckyang is establishing Duckyang America in Georgia, USA, in partnership with SK Innovation to pursue Battery Module Assembly (BMA) and Energy Storage System (ESS) business.
source_title: Duckyang Ind. – Korean-Machinery.com

facts: Rockefeller Group recently completed two distribution buildings totaling 427,655 square feet on 39 acres in Braselton, Ga.
Braselton 85- Phase II is located on Jesse Cronic Road and Highway 124 along the I-85 North corridor in the North Atlanta submarket.
The project broke ground in 2023.
It includes two rear-load distribution buildings totaling 207,009 and 220,646 square feet respectively.
Both buildings feature 32’ clear ceiling heights, 52’ x 50’ column spacing, and ample auto and trailer parking.
Braselton’s location along I-85 provides potential tenants with a distribution channel from Atlanta to Charlotte, bypassing metro-Atlanta traffic.
The company recently sold the smaller of the two buildings with the 220,646 square foot distribution center available for sale or lease.
The business park is the company’s second development in Braselton.
The company completed and sold Braselton 85 in 2021, a 222,618-square-foot distribution center located one mile from the new project.
Braselton 85 was leased to Duckyang America Inc., a Korean automotive equipment supplier, and sold to Cabot Properties.
source_title: The REview

entity_name: New Businesses in Right to Work Georgia
facts: Three new businesses are coming soon to Right to Work Georgia: Brightmark, Duckyang, and IC Biomedical.
Each one will be adding a new location somewhere within the state of Georgia.
Brightmark will be locating in Macon-Bibb with a $680 million investment.
Duckyang will be creating 285 new jobs with their new location in Braselton.
IC Biomedical is adding a new facility in Cartersville.
Altogether, these businesses will create an economic boost.
Brightmark is investing $680 million soon in Right to Work Macon-Bibb County, Georgia.
Brightmark’s advanced recycling facility will be the world’s largest and will utilize proven plastics renewal technology.
The project aims to advance a mission of reimagining waste while supporting economic development in the region.
Georgia’s pro-business environment and support for private-sector innovation continues to pave the way toward a more sustainable future.
A quote expresses thanks to Brightmark for investing in Georgia and the hardworking Georgians of Macon.
Duckyang is investing $10 million soon toward its first US manufacturing location in Right to Work Braselton, Georgia.
Duckyang’s investment will create 285 new jobs.
In Georgia, the focus is on growing the entire electric mobility supply chain.
Strong relationships with SK Battery America and other Korean companies, such as Duckyang, are helping grow the electric mobility supply chain.
The project helps bring suppliers to the state in order to secure a local supply of needed parts.
Partners in Jackson County supported these projects.
The automotive industry continues to move toward electric.
Duckyang is helping Jackson County and the State of Georgia secure a place in the industry’s future.
IC Biomedical is investing millions toward a new manufacturing facility in Right to Work Cartersville, Georgia.
IC Biomedical’s investment will create over 80 new jobs.
Georgia was chosen for IC Biomedical due to a highly trained workforce, transport links, and state infrastructure committed to the life science sector.

IC Biomedical joins metro Atlanta’s growing life sciences community with assets like Emory School of Medicine, Morehouse School of Medicine, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, Georgia Tech, and many businesses engaged in research, development, manufacturing, and distribution of life science related products and supplies.
source_title: What Businesses are Coming to Right to Work Georgia? - NRTWC

facts: GDEcD's mission includes attracting new business investment.
GDEcD supports the expansion of existing industry and small businesses.
GDEcD locates new markets for Georgia products.
GDEcD promotes the state as a desirable location for entertainment businesses and projects.
GDEcD pursues and expands the State of Georgia’s international relationships.
GDEcD drives innovation of strategic ecosystems through partnerships and resource connections.
GDEcD offers an internship program that provides hands-on experience and valuable insights into the workplace for students and emerging professionals.
GDEcD's Trade team works to bolster Georgia exports and brand the state as a competitive source of quality products and services.
GDEcD's Tourism Division, Georgia, Explore, operates nine Visitor Information Centers across the state.

facts: East Central Georgia is home to the University of Georgia in Athens, chartered in 1785 as the nation’s first public institution.
Today, the region blends vibrant communities with active research and talent development, supporting innovation, industry, and opportunity across a growing region.
Area Counties include Barrow, Clarke, Elbert, Greene, Jackson, Jasper, Madison, Morgan, Newton, Oconee, Oglethorpe, and Walton.
Across the region, a diverse economic base supports established and growing industries like advanced manufacturing and logistics.
This mix is reflected in communities like Elberton, Monroe, and Winder, where a range of employers continue to grow.

Weyman Hunt, Owner & CEO, Godfrey’s Feed stated: “Madison in Morgan County is a great place to be. There’s a lot of support in the local community for our products and our industry. It’s a rural county, so it has been a great place to foster what we do. For many years, Morgan County has been the No. 1 county in the state for beef cattle, and I’d like to think we have helped make it that way.”

facts: Georgia stands out as the nation’s premier business destination.
The state is home to 16 Fortune 500 company headquarters.
Proven workforce programs like Georgia Quick Start and a robust, 88-campus technical college system bolster local industries.
The world’s busiest and most efficient airport, fastest-growing port, and largest rail network in the Southeast are proof that Georgia’s infrastructure excels across air, sea, and rail.
Georgia’s government promotes favorable environments for businesses to thrive, including reducing the corporate tax rate to 4.99%.

From the mountains to the coast, 159 counties present unique advantages and vibrant communities ripe with opportunity, each offering access to qualified labor, workforce development resources, and logistical advantages.
source_title: Why Georgia | Georgia

entity_name: DOE List of EVs Assembled in North America
canonical_name: List of Electric Vehicles Assembled in North America
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) publishes a list of Electric Vehicles (EVs) with Final Assembly in North America.
The list is based on Fuel Economy label data submitted to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
The list also relies on Part 583 vehicle identification reports submitted to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA).
Manufacturers can use a website contact form to request changes to the vehicle list, such as adding new vehicles or modifying existing ones.
Requests to change the list must include the vehicle make/manufacturer, model name, model year, electric vehicle status, and country of final assembly.
Manufacturers must submit or update their Fuel Economy label data to the EPA through the EV-CIS portal.
Manufacturers must send new or revised Part 583 reports to NHTSA reflecting current electric vehicle makes, models, and applicable final assembly points.
Submitting updated data is important for DOE to confirm reported information when evaluating requests for the list of EVs assembled in North America.
The Engines and Vehicles Compliance Information System (EV-CIS) is EPA's database where manufacturers submit certification and Fuel Economy labeling information.
Under 40 C.F.R. § 600.313-08(a), EPA requires manufacturers to submit Fuel Economy labeling information no later than five working days before the date the model type is initially offered for sale.
Manufacturers must follow EPA's Fuel Economy labeling requirements under 40 C.F.R. Part 600 to ensure qualified vehicles are included on the list of EVs assembled in North America in a timely manner.

Under the American Automobile Labeling Act, implemented by NHTSA in 49 C.F.R. Part 583, manufacturers must report information about each model year and carline of new passenger motor vehicles they produce.
This reporting to NHTSA must be submitted no later than the date the first vehicle of the carline is offered for sale to the ultimate purchaser (per 49 C.F.R. § 583.17).
Required information for Part 583 reports includes the location of the final assembly point, which encompasses the city, state (for U.S.-assembled vehicles), and country.
NHTSA defines a vehicle's final assembly point further in 49 C.F.R. § 583.4(b)–4) - (b)(5).

To expedite review for the list of EVs assembled in North America, manufacturers should include common electric and hybrid vehicle descriptors (EV or PHEV) at the end of each carline name when resubmitting Part 583 reports.
Adding EV/PHEV descriptors helps NHTSA identify whether final assembly information reported for an entire carline applies to electric or hybrid versions of those models.
The public can use NHTSA's VIN decoder to confirm the general build location for a vehicle model.
The VIN decoder's final assembly information relies on build plant data submitted by manufacturers through NHTSA's Product Information Catalog and Vehicle Listing (vPIC) system.
Manufacturers should ensure build plant data is up to date, especially for carlines spanning multiple points of final assembly.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Details for Manufacturers Submitting Information on Electric Vehicles Assembled in North America

entity_name: Advanced Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Technologies to Drive National Decarbonization NOFO
canonical_name: Advanced Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Technologies to Drive National Decarbonization
facts: The Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy (EERE) intends to issue, on behalf of the Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Technologies Office (HFTO), a Notice of Funding Opportunity (NOFO).
The NOFO is entitled “Advanced Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Technologies to Drive National Decarbonization.”
Clean hydrogen and fuel cell technologies are important elements for hard-to-decarbonize sectors such as heavy-duty transportation and industrial applications.
These technologies enable long duration energy storage for a clean electric grid.
Advancing clean hydrogen and fuel cell technologies supports the Biden-Harris Administration’s goals of achieving carbon pollution-free electricity by 2035.
The administration's goal includes net-zero emissions economy-wide by no later than 2050.
The NOFO supports the U.S. National Clean Hydrogen Strategy and Roadmap vision of affordable clean hydrogen for a net-zero carbon future.
The roadmap envisions a sustainable, resilient, and equitable economy.
The NOFO will target research, development, and demonstration (RD&D) topics critical to scaling hydrogen infrastructure.
It targets enabling increased adoption of clean hydrogen across sectors, particularly in heavy-duty vehicles and other heavy-duty transportation applications.
Increased adoption of hydrogen technologies supports the Department of Energy’s Regional Clean Hydrogen Hubs (H2Hubs) Program.
The H2Hubs Program is an $8 billion federal investment to create networks of hydrogen producers, consumers, and local connective infrastructure.
The program aims to accelerate the use of hydrogen as a clean energy carrier and a carbon-free input to chemical and industrial processes.
Activities funded under this NOFO will align with the H2@Scale Initiative.
The H2@Scale Initiative aims to advance affordable hydrogen production, transport, storage, and utilization.
The initiative targets enabling decarbonization and revenue opportunities across multiple sectors.
The NOFO’s objectives support DOE’s Hydrogen Shot goal.
The Hydrogen Shot goal targets affordable clean hydrogen production at $1/kg within a decade.
Projects funded under this NOFO will be expected to advance diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility (DEIA).
Projects must contribute to the Justice40 Initiative and other considerations linked with energy and/or environmental justice.
Projects must invest in America’s workforce.
Applications must include an RDD&D Community Benefits Plan that addresses DEIA, Justice40, and workforce investment objectives.
EERE plans to issue the NOFO before or in October 2024 via the EERE eXCHANGE website.
Applicants wishing to receive official notifications and information regarding this NOFO should register in EERE eXCHANGE.
When the FOA is released, applications will be accepted only through EERE eXCHANGE.
Interested parties can navigate to TPL-0000058 in EERE Exchange to access the Teaming Partner List for this solicitation.
This notice of intent (NOI) informs interested parties that EERE intends to issue the NOFO in the near term.
All information contained in this NOI is subject to change.

links.context: EERE eXCHANGE website for NOFO issuance and applications
Teaming Partner List in EERE Exchange

source_title: DOE: CMEI eXCHANGE: Funding Opportunities

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Pratt & Whitney, an RTX business, is investing $200 million to expand its operations in Columbus, Georgia.
The new investment project will initially create 15 new jobs, with additional jobs to follow.
Pratt & Whitney President Shane Eddy, Columbus Mayor Skip Henderson, and other public and company leaders celebrated the announcement alongside the completion of a prior expansion announced in 2023.
The previously announced 2023 project added nearly 400 new jobs and 81,000 square feet to Pratt & Whitney’s Columbus Engine Center.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that Pratt & Whitney's decision to invest immediately after completing another major expansion demonstrates the reliable, ongoing, and long-term success companies find in Georgia.
Governor Kemp noted that throughout its more than forty years in Columbus, Pratt & Whitney has been a valued partner and driver of impact for the state's aerospace industry.
The Pratt & Whitney Columbus facility opened in 1984 and includes the Columbus Engine Center and Columbus Forge operating on the same campus.
The Columbus Engine Center maintains Pratt & Whitney GTF™ engines for the Airbus A320neo aircraft family, Airbus A220, and Embraer E-Jets E2.
The facility also supports the F117 engine for the Boeing C-17 Globemaster III and the F100 engine for the McDonnell Douglas F-15 Eagle and Lockheed Martin F-16 Fighting Falcon.
The Columbus Forge produces compressor airfoils, and compressor and turbine disks for Pratt & Whitney’s commercial and military engines.
These components include parts for the F135 engine, which powers all variants of the Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II fighter.

Pratt & Whitney President Shane Eddy stated that over four decades with community and state support, the Georgia presence grew from a small manufacturing facility to a state-of-the-art manufacturing and overhaul center employing 2,600 dedicated people.
Since 2008, Pratt & Whitney has invested more than $1 billion to continue expanding the footprint and capabilities of the Columbus facility.
The latest investment will increase output of critical parts for growing military and commercial engine programs and ramp industrial capacity to support customers.
The Columbus Engine Center is located at 8987 Macon Road, and the Columbus Forge is located at 8801 Macon Road.
The expansion project is expected to be completed by 2028.

Columbus Mayor Skip Henderson thanked Pratt & Whitney for its continued faith in the Columbus community and Valley region, and appreciated Governor Kemp, the Georgia Department of Economic Development, and Choose Columbus's assistance.

Chairman Selvin Hollingsworth of the Development Authority of Columbus, Georgia, stated that the project underscores the strength of the region’s advanced manufacturing sector and the power of partnership in driving economic growth.

Regional Project Manager Christy Bozeman represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Development Authority of Columbus, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Pratt & Whitney’s continued growth proves investing in workforce initiatives is the most important factor for the long-term success of companies in Georgia.
Pratt & Whitney was an early beneficiary of Georgia Quick Start and has engaged with the program for nearly every expansion or major investment over its decades-long relationship.
GDEcD collaborates with education partners such as Columbus Technical College and Columbus-Muscogee County to support workforce initiatives and company milestones.
Pratt & Whitney is a world leader in the design, manufacture, and service of aircraft engines and auxiliary power units for military, commercial, and civil aviation customers.
Since 1925, Pratt & Whitney engineers have pioneered the development of revolutionary aircraft propulsion technologies.
Pratt & Whitney currently supports more than 90,000 in-service engines through its global network of maintenance, repair, and overhaul facilities.
source_title: RTX’s Pratt & Whitney Announces New $200M Columbus Expansion | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Underwood Ammo will invest $41 million in building a new manufacturing facility.
The investment will create 120 new jobs.
The facility is located in Effingham County.
Georgia is described as the nation’s premier destination for manufacturers, including the defense and sporting sectors.
Underwood Ammo produces handgun and rifle ammunition for hunting and self-defense.
Underwood Ammo is based in the United States.
Kevin Underwood serves as President and Founder of Underwood Ammo.
The new manufacturing and assembly facility will be located at the Savannah Portside International Park.
Construction on the facility is expected to begin in 2026.
Hiring for positions in operations, maintenance, and assembly is expected to begin in 2027.
Forrest Floyd serves as District One Commissioner on the Effingham County Board of Commissioners.
Matt Saxon serves as Chairman of the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority.
The project received support from the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
The project received support from Electric Cities of Georgia.
Connor Hall represents the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team as Project Manager for this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with Effingham County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Electric Cities of Georgia.
Pat Wilson serves as Commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
Underwood Ammo is a U.S.-based manufacturer focused on the design, production, and distribution of precision-engineered products.
The company continues to expand its U.S. operations footprint with the addition of its Georgia-based facility.
Governor Brian Kemp stated, “Georgia is the nation’s premier destination for manufacturers, including the defense and sporting sectors.”
Kevin Underwood stated that today marks an important milestone in Underwood Ammo's history and that the investment positions the company for long-term success.
Forrest Floyd stated that Underwood Ammo's investment will create quality jobs and contribute to Effingham County's continued growth.
Matt Saxon stated that Underwood’s decision adds to the strength of the industrial community and creates diversity in available jobs.
Pat Wilson stated that the investment underscores confidence in Georgia’s skilled workforce, dependable infrastructure, and supportive business environment.
source_title: Underwood Ammo to Create 120 New Manufacturing Jobs | Georgia

facts: Global biopharmaceutical giant UCB, Inc. is planning a significant investment of $2 billion in Georgia to establish its first U.S. pharmaceutical biologics manufacturing facility.
The investment will generate 330 new jobs over the next several years at the Rowen Foundation's state-of-the-art, 2,000-acre science and learning campus in Gwinnett County.
Governor Brian Kemp met with UCB leadership earlier this year in Belgium to discuss how Georgia would be the right partner for their visionary plans in the U.S.
The announcement represents one of the largest investments in state history and establishes both the Rowen facility and Georgia as a true hub of innovation in the life sciences field.
UCB is a global biopharmaceutical company based in Brussels, Belgium, with North American headquarters located in Smyrna that currently support more than 400 jobs.
UCB's expertise spans neurology and immunology.
Jean-Christophe Tellier, CEO of UCB, stated the decision reflects confidence in UCB's long-term growth and deep-rooted commitment to the United States.
By investing in Georgia, where its U.S. headquarters have been based for more than three decades, UCB is strengthening its biologics manufacturing capabilities and supporting its innovation pipeline.
The project is expected to generate approximately $5 billion in total economic impact, reflecting the broader value it will create for the region and its communities.

UCB's new manufacturing footprint will be located at Rowen, serving as an anchor tenant for this 2,000-acre planned community in metro Atlanta designed to foster collaboration, knowledge sharing, and innovation.
The cutting-edge campus will use a digital-first approach by leveraging AI, robotics, and automation while prioritizing efficiency in the use of any natural resources.

Nicole Love Hendrickson, Chairwoman of the Gwinnett County Board of Commissioners, stated an investment of this magnitude was exactly what they envisioned when committing to establish Rowen as a hub for collaboration and discovery.
Gwinnett County connects UCB to a highly skilled, globally connected talent pool and is recognized as a partner in progress committed to world-class infrastructure and premier services.
Katie Kirkpatrick, President and CEO of the Metro Atlanta Chamber, stated metro Atlanta is not just a hub for life sciences today but a place where breakthroughs of tomorrow are taking shape.

Project Director EJane Caraway represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development's (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with Partnership Gwinnett, Metro Atlanta Chamber, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted that Georgia's growing life sciences ecosystem and collaborative approach to economic development connect companies with world-class partners in research, education, logistics, and infrastructure.

UCB has been a leader in biopharmaceutical innovation for more than a century and is focused on the discovery and development of innovative medicines for severe diseases of the immune system or central nervous system.
The company employs approximately 9,000 people across approximately 40 countries and generated revenue of €7.7 billion in 2025.
UCB is listed on Euronext Brussels under the symbol UCB.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: UCB to Invest $2 Billion in Georgia, Establish First U.S. Manufacturing Facility | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Hitachi Astemo Americas Inc. announced an expansion of its operation in Madison County with a $153 million investment.
The investment will create 167 new full-time jobs for Kentuckians.
Funds will support site improvements and renovations, including additional production lines and equipment to address increased market demand and growth within the EV industry.
Expansion brings company's total Kentucky employment across Harrodsburg and Berea facilities to over 2,100 workers.
Tim Clark serves as president of Hitachi Astemo Americas.
In January 2021, Hitachi Automotive Systems, Keihin, Showa, and Nissin Kogyo completed a business merger to form the global mega-supplier Hitachi Astemo.
Company provides advanced mobility solutions including electric powertrains for automobiles, advanced chassis, autonomous driving/advanced driver assistance systems, and motorcycle systems.
Hitachi Astemo is headquartered in Tokyo, Japan, entered the Americas market in 1972 with a Brazil subsidiary, and expanded into the U.S. in the mid-1980s with its first Kentucky factory.
Company operates more than 20 manufacturing sites across the United States, Canada, Mexico, and Brazil, including two locations in Kentucky.
Madison County Judge/Executive Reagan Taylor expressed support for the expansion, citing positive community relationships and quality of life.
Berea Mayor Bruce Fraley noted Hitachi Astemo is the city's largest employer and highlighted local workforce talent and economic climate.
Donna Angel, business development director for the Berea Business and Economic Development Office, stated the company develops newest technology in parts for automotive and other EV engines.
Kentucky has seen nearly 600 private-sector new-location or expansion projects commit to invest over $22.9 billion and create more than 30,000 jobs administration-to-date.
Gov. Andy Beshear announced Kentucky has filled more than 2 million jobs, the most in state history, with 57,000 new jobs filled since the beginning of his administration.
On May 18, Gov. Beshear announced Kentucky set a historic low unemployment rate of 3.7%.
Since the beginning of his administration, Gov. Beshear has announced more than 860 private-sector new-location and expansion projects totaling $26.8 billion in announced investments.
The average incentivized hourly wage in Kentucky was $26.78 before benefits in 2022, representing an 11.5% increase over the previous year.
In September 2021, Gov. Beshear and leaders from Ford Motor Co. and SK On celebrated a $5.8 billion investment to create 5,000 jobs in Hardin County.
In April 2022, leadership at AESC announced a $2 billion investment to create 2,000 jobs in Warren County.
In May 2023, Gov. Beshear and Toyota leaders announced a $591 million investment across multiple projects for Georgetown's first battery electric vehicle production.
In June 2023, Gov. Beshear joined INFAC North America to break ground on a $53 million investment for EV-related production in Campbellsville.
On May 12, Gov. Beshear announced Kentucky received its first state-level credit rating upgrade in 13 years from Fitch Ratings.
On June 29, the commonwealth received its first-ever rating increase from S&P Global Ratings.
Site Selection magazine ranked Kentucky first in the South Central region and top-5 nationally for its 2023 Prosperity Cup ranking.
Site Selection placed Kentucky second nationwide and first in the South Central region for its 2022 Governor’s Cup ranking.
Gov. Beshear recently announced a new “Supply Kentucky” initiative to boost job growth, reduce costs, and secure supply chains for Kentucky companies.
In March 2023, the Kentucky Economic Development Finance Authority (KEDFA) preliminarily approved a 10-year incentive agreement with Hitachi Astemo under the Kentucky Business Investment program.
The performance-based agreement can provide up to $2.4 million in tax incentives based on annual investment targets.

KEDFA also approved Hitachi Astemo for up to $200,000 in tax incentives through the Kentucky Enterprise Initiative Act (KEIA) to recoup sales and use tax on construction costs, building fixtures, equipment used in R&D, and electronic processing.
Eligible incentives can be claimed against income tax liability and/or wage assessments.

Hitachi Astemo is eligible for resources from Kentucky’s workforce service providers, including no-cost recruitment and job placement services, reduced-cost customized training, and job-training incentives.

entity_name: U.S. Department of Energy Industrial Technologies Office (DOE ITO)
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy’s Industrial Technologies Office (ITO) announced the selection of 20 projects to develop technologies designed to enhance the competitiveness of the U.S. industrial sector and support the onshoring of American manufacturing.
Award and cost share amounts for the selected projects are subject to change pending negotiations.

A project led by Oregon State University with partners Albemarle Corporation and Amrize aims to replace a substantial portion of cement with delithiated aluminosilicate (DLAS), a by-product from lithium battery production, as a supplementary cementitious material.
The DLAS project leverages optimized grinding techniques and thermodynamic simulation to improve reactivity, maximizing performance in concrete blends and turning waste into a valuable feedstock.

By lowering the amount of clinker in cement, this approach can increase the use of readily-available domestic resources to meet domestic cement demand, reducing reliance on imports and better positioning the U.S. against overseas competitors.

Georgia Tech Research Corporation is leading a $2,126,875 project to scale up a membrane-based nanofiltration system for harsh conditions, with the goal of onshoring production in American facilities to create global supply chain advantages and reduce direct heating costs for industrial users.
source_title: ITO FY23 & FY24 High-Priority Selections | Department of Energy

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp welcomed the announcement that Yamaha Motor Co., Ltd. has officially decided to move its U.S. headquarters to Kennesaw, Georgia.
Yamaha is relocating out of California following roughly 50 years of operation there.
The relocation decision follows a meeting between Governor Brian Kemp and Yamaha executives in October of last year at the Southeast U.S./Japan (SEUS-Japan) annual gathering in Tokyo.
Yamaha Motor Co., Ltd. is a manufacturer of ATVs, boat engines, personal watercraft, and other motorized products.
Yamaha already employs over 2,300 Georgians across its facilities in the state.
The relocation of the Yamaha Motor Corp. U.S. headquarters will begin this year and is expected to continue through 2028.
Governor Brian Kemp stated: “After many years of great partnership, we are honored and proud to welcome Yamaha's American headquarters to the No. 1 state for business.”

Governor Brian Kemp added: “This is another loud and clear testament to what we offer job creators from around the world. To any other California-based companies looking for a better home, we'll give you plenty of reasons to keep Georgia on your mind.”
The Yamaha Motor Company currently employs thousands of people across various facilities in Georgia.
The 1.3-million-square-foot Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America factory in Newnan opened in 1986 and currently employs roughly 2,000 Georgians.
Yamaha relocated its marine division to Kennesaw in 1999.
Yamaha expanded its Kennesaw facility with a 75,000-square-foot Marine Innovation Center in 2023.
In 2019, the company moved its motorsports operations to Georgia to benefit from close proximity to the existing facilities.
Yamaha Motor Co., Ltd. is a world-leading producer of motorcycles, marine products, power products, and robotics.
The company was founded in 1955 and is headquartered in Iwata, Shizuoka, Japan.
Yamaha operates under the corporate mission of being a "Kando Creating Company," aiming to offer new excitement and a more fulfilling life for people all over the world.
source_title: Yamaha to Move HQ to Georgia | Georgia

canonical_name: 2022 VTO Annual Merit Review Plenary and Awards
facts: The 2022 VTO Annual Merit Review plenary session was held on Tuesday, June 21, 2022.

VTO presents awards annually to individuals from partner institutions for contributions to program efforts and to recognize research, development, demonstration, and deployment achievements in specific areas.

Amgad Elgowainy and Jarod Kelly from Argonne National Laboratory received a Distinguished Achievement Award for leading a Cradle-to-Grave analysis supporting the U.S. DRIVE partnership between DOE and industry partners.
Jiguang (Jason) Zhang from Pacific Northwest National Laboratory received a Distinguished Achievement Award for outstanding technical contributions to VTO’s battery R&D activities.
Yan (Joann) Zhou and Jim Kuiper from Argonne National Laboratory received a Distinguished Achievement Award for distinguished achievements supporting electric vehicle charging.

Matthew Kramer from Ames Laboratory received a Distinguished Achievement Award for years of outstanding skill and leadership in magnetic materials research, planning, and collaboration for electric drive technologies.

Cummins, Inc. received a Team Award for demonstrating 55% brake thermal efficiency in a heavy-duty diesel engine to contribute to the SuperTruck 2 goal of more than doubling freight efficiency compared to trucks produced a decade earlier.
Navistar, Inc. received a Team Award for demonstrating 55% brake thermal efficiency in a heavy-duty diesel engine in a test cell to contribute to the SuperTruck 2 goal.

Clemson University, Honda Development & Manufacturing of America, LLC, University of Delaware, Lanxess, and Proper Group International received a Team Award for Dr. Srikanth Pilla’s expertise in leading carbon fiber thermoplastic composites door R&D to achieve weight savings, performance, and sustainability.

Brett Aristegui, Trev Hall, Darren Stevenson, David Kirschner, Neil Kirschner, Dan Nardozzi, and Erin Russell-Story from the National Energy Technology Laboratory received a Team Award for dedication to VTO and commitment to the national network of Clean Cities coalitions as Clean Cities Regional Managers.

Dennis Dees from Argonne National Laboratory received a Lifetime Distinguished Achievement Award for decades of research in continuum modeling of high-energy, high power, and long-lasting batteries to support the DOE advanced battery portfolio.

David Anderson from the U.S. Department of Energy received a Lifetime Distinguished Achievement Award for serving as inaugural Program Manager for Energy Efficient Mobility Systems and creating an enduring program.

Ira Bloom from Argonne National Laboratory received a Lifetime Distinguished Achievement Award for support of multiple DOE programs on advanced batteries in areas of accelerated testing, life estimation, and elucidating physical and chemical changes that limit battery life.

Steve Zimmer from the United States Council for Automotive Research (USCAR) received a Lifetime Distinguished Achievement Award for leadership as executive director of USCAR and contributions to U.S. DRIVE partnerships.
source_title: 2022 VTO AMR Plenary and Awards | Department of Energy

facts: Between July 1, 2023, and June 30, 2024, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 429 facility expansions and new locations resulting in more than $20.3 billion in investment and the commitment of 26,900 new, private sector jobs statewide.
83% of expansions and new locations are on their way to communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region, representing more than 18,300 new jobs and $12.5 billion in investment.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the state is committed to continuing momentum by investing in key areas like education, workforce development, and infrastructure.
Metro Atlanta attracted nearly $7.8 billion in investments as a logistics and business hub.
Statewide, new locations account for $12.8 billion in investments and expansions are responsible for 9,800 new jobs.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones emphasized the need to ensure businesses have the necessary workforce, citing the Technical College System of Georgia, University System of Georgia, College and Career Academies, and private-public partnerships as building the workforce pipeline.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns highlighted historic investments in the education system, workforce development, and infrastructure as part of sustained economic development success.
Governor and First Lady Kemp led economic development missions to Korea and Switzerland in FY2024 focusing on strengthening relationships with Hyundai Motor Group and its supplier network.

Georgia maintained its position as a global gateway for business, landing more than 8,100 new jobs and $5.9 billion in investments from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S. market this past fiscal year.

The State of Georgia celebrated more than 50 years of continuous representation in Europe and Japan, which remain among the top sources of foreign direct investment.
The coming fiscal year will mark 40 years of continuous representation in Korea, a partnership that earned GDEcD the James A. Van Fleet Award from the Korea Society.
