facts: Kia recently invested over $200 million to expand its West Point, Georgia plant to build electric vehicles like the new EV9 SUV.
The Kia EV9 began rolling off the Georgia assembly line in 2024.
The EV9 is the first-ever Kia EV assembled in North America.
Kia announced that its award-winning EV6 crossover will also be built in West Point starting in early 2025.
The EV6 production follows a $217 million plant expansion that created around 200 new jobs.
These moves are part of a broader push to localize EV production to qualify for federal EV tax credits.
Kia’s Georgia plant, officially named Kia Georgia, Inc., has undergone a major transformation to accommodate EV production alongside traditional models.
The factory already produces the Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage SUVs before adding EV assembly.
Kia added approximately 200 employees during the expansion.
Facilities were upgraded so that EVs and gasoline models can be built on the same line.
Total employment at the plant was brought to over 3,200 workers.
The first Georgia-built EV9 rolled out just months after the expansion.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that over $22.7 billion has been invested in Georgia’s EV supply chain since 2020.
This investment wave has created more than 28,000 jobs in Georgia’s EV supply chain.
As of 2025, Kia has both the EV6 and EV9 in full production in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group is opening a massive new “Metaplant” and battery facilities in Georgia.
The Hyundai Metaplant and battery facilities will supply local battery packs to boost output.
Kia joined other automakers in July 2023 to announce a joint venture that will build thousands of high-speed chargers across North America.
Under its “Plan S” electrification strategy, Kia plans to launch 14-15 new EV models globally by 2027.
The current Kia EV6 and EV9 use an 800-volt electrical architecture that allows ultra-fast charging and improved efficiency.
The EV9 is the first Kia to use a fourth-generation battery technology that boosts energy density for more range in the same space.
Every Kia EV battery and powertrain comes with a 10-year/100,000-mile limited warranty.
The bumper-to-bumper warranty is 5 years/60,000 miles.
Kia earned the title of top mass-market brand in J.D. Power’s 2023 Vehicle Dependability Study.
The 2023 Kia EV6 earned the IIHS Top Safety Pick+ rating for its safety and build quality.
Kia partnered with Electrify America to provide EV6 and Niro EV owners with free fast-charging sessions totaling up to 500 kWh in the first three years.
Starting with new models in 2024, Kia EVs will come with a NACS charging port, giving access to 12,000+ Tesla Supercharger stalls in the U.S. and Canada.
Kia’s EVs feature Vehicle-to-Load (V2L) capability, which turns the car into a mobile power source.
The EV9 is designed to eventually offer Level 3 autonomous driving capabilities using lidar-based sensors for hands-free driving in certain conditions.
source_title: Kia’s EV Revolution: From Georgia to the Pacific Northwest | Dick Hannah Kia

canonical_name: Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) Internship Program
facts: The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) Internship Program gives students and recent graduates hands-on experience working with professionals who promote Georgia’s economy, tourism, and creative industries.
Interns gain real-world exposure to research, marketing, communications, and event planning while building valuable professional connections.
Internships are unpaid, with academic credit available when required, and are designed for learning and career exploration.
Eligible candidates must be undergraduate or graduate-level students, or recent graduates within one quarter or semester following graduation.
Candidates must complete a minimum of one year of higher education (two semesters or three quarters) before starting the internship.
The internship must begin no later than the quarter or semester immediately following completion of one year of higher education.
Candidates must have a GPA of 3.0 or higher.
Candidates must be residents of Georgia or attend a Georgia institution of higher learning.
Interns must agree to the terms and conditions specified by GDEcD.
Interns seeking academic credit must provide program requirements no later than at the time an internship is accepted.
Interns must commit to a regular schedule of 20 to 40 hours per week for a period of 2 to 8 months.
Specific terms will be discussed and agreed upon in advance by the intern and their assigned supervisor.
Parking expenses will be reimbursed, but no additional personal expenses such as housing, food, or mass transit are reimbursed.
Interested applicants must submit an application and cover letter to apply.
The cover letter must include dates of availability for the internship, with a minimum duration of two months and a maximum of eight months.
The cover letter must include academic credit requirements for the applicant's institution, if any.
Only candidates selected for an interview will be contacted due to the large volume of applications received.
The Arts Intern gains hands-on experience in grant administration, communications, and project coordination while learning how the arts support Georgia’s economic development.

Arts Intern responsibilities include assisting with grant applications and reviews, coordinating communications with artists and arts organizations, supporting research, and helping with special initiatives such as Capitol exhibits.
Strong writing and organizational skills are preferred for the Arts Intern role, and an arts-related major is a plus.
The Southeast Asia Initiatives Intern supports outreach to Southeast Asian investors and government delegations.

Southeast Asia Initiatives Intern duties include assisting with trade missions and visits, developing promotional materials, and supporting special events and trade shows focused on the Southeast Asia region.
The International Trade Intern supports efforts to help Georgia companies expand exports.
International Trade Intern responsibilities include market and country research, analyzing trade data, responding to client requests, and assisting with event recruitment.
An interest in global trade, current affairs, and strong research and communication skills are preferred for the International Trade Intern role.
The Legal Intern provides support to the General Counsel’s office, gaining experience in public-sector legal work.
Legal Intern responsibilities include legal research, contract review, open records requests, and assisting with rules, regulations, and incentive agreements.
Candidates for the Legal Intern role must be current or recently graduated law students.
source_title: GDEcD Internship Program | Georgia

Had direct management over a majority of the Department’s national labs and their world-leading user facilities.

Is the lead for technology commercialization activities for the Department and its 17 national labs.

Is a member of the Council on Foreign Relations.

facts: On Tuesday, Oct. 8, 2024, the U.S. Department of Energy’s (DOE’s) Industrial Technologies Office (ITO) announced the selection of 16 projects as part of a $38 million funding opportunity on cross-sector technologies.
The total federal funding allocated for these selected projects is $38,515,323.
These projects will advance research, development, and pilot-scale demonstrations of cross-sector process and equipment technologies with wide applicability.
DOE’s investments in cross-sector technologies address common energy challenges across the U.S. industrial sector and can be tailored to meet the unique needs of specific subsectors.
Selected projects will advance DOE’s Industrial Heat Shot™ initiative, which aims to develop cost-competitive industrial heat technologies.
Topics selected for funding include Electrification of Industrial Heat, Efficient Energy Use in Industrial Systems, and Optimizing Organic Wastewater and Wet Organic Waste Treatment.

Topic Area 1 (Electrification of Industrial Heat) includes projects in Ames, Iowa ($2,600,000 lead by Ames National Laboratory); Worcester, Massachusetts ($2,750,000 lead by Worcester Polytechnic Institute); North Las Vegas, Nevada ($3,000,000 lead by Blue Mountain Energy); Alameda, California ($2,999,376 lead by Rondo Energy, Inc.); and Southfield, Michigan ($1,203,971 lead by Lawrence Technological University).

The Ames, Iowa project aims to develop a solid-state elastocaloric heat pump targeting ethanol distillation at 2 kW scale to reduce energy consumption by a factor of 3 or more and decrease carbon intensity of corn ethanol by 40%–80%.

The Worcester, Massachusetts project aims to demonstrate process heating electrification using laser technology to enhance energy efficiency by up to 40% in food applications and 20% in paper applications.
The North Las Vegas, Nevada project aims to develop a high-lift air-source oil-free closed-cycle mechanical vapor compression heat pump using low-global warming potential refrigerants.

The Alameda, California project aims to develop an advanced heat battery for temperatures >1300°C with 25-40% increased storage capacity and >85% greenhouse gas emissions reduction, scalable up to 1100°C.

The Southfield, Michigan project aims to validate a laser-based powder coat curing technology for industrial coatings to replace natural gas ovens, targeting lower temperature (<250°C) and higher temperature (>400°C) systems.

Topic Area 2 (Efficient Energy Use in Industrial Systems) includes projects in University Park, Pennsylvania ($2,491,443 lead by Penn State University); College Park, Maryland ($1,468,298 lead by University of Maryland: College Park); Atlanta, Georgia ($2,126,875 lead by Georgia Tech Research Corporation); Des Plaines, Illinois ($2,280,000 lead by GTI Energy); Amherst, New York ($3,000,000 lead by SUNY at Buffalo); and Austin, Texas ($2,371,167 lead by The University of Texas at Austin).

The Penn State project aims to develop a ceramic siliconized silicon carbide (SiSiC) heat exchanger for heat recovery at temperatures over 800°C, doubling heat transfer rates using additive manufacturing.
The College Park, Maryland project aims to develop a polymer-based heat exchanger for low- to medium-temperature waste-heat recovery tailored for data centers, power plants, and HVAC systems.

The Atlanta, Georgia project aims to develop and scale-up a continuous roll-to-roll fabrication process for reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and rGO-X nanofiltration membrane technology, with applications in pyrolyzed bio-oils, crude biofuels, and waste plastics.
The Des Plaines, Illinois project aims to demonstrate a high-temperature heat exchanger (RESE) capable of preheating combustion air up to 500°C from exhaust gases up to 900°C.

The Amherst, New York project aims to scale up carbon-doped titanium oxide (CDTO) membranes for organic solvent separation, demonstrating a prototype system processing 20 kilograms of crude soybean oil per day for >500 hours.
The Austin, Texas project aims to improve silver ion based facilitated transport membranes (FTMs) for petrochemical waste streams and fruit/vegetable shipping refrigeration.

Topic Area 3 (Optimizing Organic Wastewater and Wet Organic Waste Treatment) includes projects in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania ($2,497,554 lead by Philadelphia Water Department); New York, New York ($2,494,408 lead by Columbia University); Champaign, Illinois ($2,495,585 lead by University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign); Knoxville, Tennessee ($2,500,000 lead by University of Tennessee, Knoxville); and Baton Rouge, Louisiana ($2,237,383 lead by Louisiana State University).
The Philadelphia project aims to optimize autothermal pyrolysis for converting wastewater biosolids into biochar and gas, potentially reducing sludge volumes by at least 85%.
The New York project aims to develop control strategies to minimize N2O emissions from biological nitrogen removal while increasing process efficiency.
The Champaign project aims to minimize N2O emissions from ammonia removal processes using ion-exchange and electrolysis as alternatives to traditional nitrification/denitrification.
The Knoxville project aims to use nanobubbles in water resource recovery facility aeration processes to improve oxygen transfer and decrease energy requirements.
The Baton Rouge project aims to reduce or eliminate N2O emissions from wastewater disinfection by combining TiO2 catalysts with UVC LEDs to replace chlorine.
source_title: Industrial Funding Selections: 2024 Industrial Cross-Sector Technologies Funding Opportunity | Department of Energy

facts: ABC Technologies completed its acquisition of TI Fluid Systems plc on April 15, 2025.
The acquisition was for an enterprise value of over £1.8 billion.
The combined business will be rebranded as TI Automotive.

The transaction brings together two highly complementary businesses to create a stronger, more diversified Tier 1 manufacturer supplying market-leading safety and performance critical products and highly engineered components.
TI Automotive will have a propulsion agnostic portfolio positioned to support a diverse range of customers.
Combined pro forma revenue is $5.4 billion for 2024.
The combined company has 34,600 employees in 26 countries.
TI Automotive will be headquartered in Auburn Hills, Michigan.
Terry Campbell, President & CEO of ABC Technologies, will lead TI Automotive as its President & CEO.
An Executive Leadership Team combining the extensive skills and expertise of both ABC and TIFS will support Campbell.
TI Automotive will be managed through a Regional Operating Model to ensure customer proximity and agility to respond rapidly to local market dynamics.
TI Automotive is a Tier 1 supplier of market-leading safety and performance critical products and highly engineered components to the global automotive industry.
The company has more than 100 years' experience solving customers' safety, efficiency and environmental challenges.
TI Automotive's powertrain agnostic product portfolio features vertical integration and a global footprint.
TI Automotive was created through ABC Technologies' acquisition of TI Fluid Systems in April 2025.
TI Automotive is majority owned by certain of the affiliated funds of Apollo Global Management, Inc. and its subsidiaries.
Funds managed by Oaktree Capital Management, L.P. own a minority equity interest in TI Automotive.
source_title: ABC Technologies Completes Acquisition of TI Fluid Systems; Combined Business Will Rebrand as TI Automotive

facts: On July 2, 2026, Lucid Group, Inc. issued a press release announcing its production and delivery totals for the quarter ended June 30, 2026, alongside several organizational and leadership changes.
On July 1, 2026, the Company’s Board of Directors appointed Alexander De Bock as its incoming Chief Financial Officer.
Mr. De Bock is expected to join the Company in the coming weeks and will report to Silvio Napoli, the Company’s Chief Executive Officer.

Taoufiq Boussaid, the Company’s current Chief Financial Officer, will leave after a transition period following Q2 2026 earnings; his departure is not related to any disagreements on operations, policies, or financial disclosures.
Mr. De Bock, age 49, has served as CFO of Metalsa, a leading manufacturer of automotive components specializing in chassis structures for light and commercial vehicles, since June 2026.
Previously, Mr. De Bock served as CFO of TI Automotive, a global automotive Tier 1 supplier of safety and performance critical products, from April 2025 to February 2026.
Prior to TI Automotive's formation following ABC Technologies' acquisition of TI Fluid Systems, he served as CFO and Executive Director of TI Fluid Systems from April 2023 to April 2025.
From June 2020 to March 2023, Mr. De Bock served as CFO of the Commercial Vehicle Division of ZF Group and as a board member of ZF Commercial Vehicle Control System India Ltd.

Prior to ZF Friedrichshafen AG's acquisition of WABCO Holdings Inc. in May 2020, Mr. De Bock held various finance leadership positions at WABCO from July 2007 to May 2020, including Corporate Treasurer and Vice President, Financial Planning & Analysis.
Earlier in his career, Mr. De Bock held internal audit positions with American Standard and Audit & Assurance roles with Arthur Andersen & Co.
Mr. De Bock holds an M.A. in Applied Economics from the University of Antwerp, Belgium.
In connection with his appointment, Mr. De Bock will receive an annual base salary of $750,000.

He will receive a target incentive bonus opportunity of 150% of his base salary for calendar year 2026, paid out based on the greater of 150% of base salary and actual achievement, prorated based on his start date.
He will receive a cash signing bonus of $1,100,000 within thirty days of his start date and another $600,000 in January 2027, subject to continued employment.

If terminated without Cause or experiencing a Constructive Termination, he is eligible under the Executive Severance Benefit Plan for 75% of his annual target bonus and immediate vesting of one-third of his then-unvested stock awards, with unvested PSUs deemed service-vested based on actual performance achievement.
He will receive reimbursement of up to $15,000 per month for six months for temporary housing expenses, grossed up for taxes.
He will receive reimbursement for flights between the Bay Area and the Detroit metropolitan area between his start date and February 1, 2027.
He will also receive a Company vehicle, tax and financial planning, and relocation support.

Mr. De Bock will receive initial equity grants with an aggregate target grant value of $7,500,000, allocated as $2,500,000 in RSUs vesting over four years (25% on a one-year cliff, remainder quarterly) and $5,000,000 in PSUs subject to the Company’s 2026 PSU program.
He will be eligible for future annual long-term incentive awards beginning in 2027 with a target grant value of $2,000,000, subject to Board discretion.
He is eligible for a performance-based cash bonus segmented into five equal tranches of $500,000, with a maximum payout of $2,500,000 if achieved between his start date and the fourth anniversary.

Each tranche's performance vesting condition requires achievement of a Market Capitalization Performance Hurdle of $5.0 billion, $7.5 billion, $10.0 billion, $12.5 billion, and $17.5 billion for tranches 1 through 5, respectively.
The Market Capitalization Performance Hurdle is defined as the volume-weighted average of the Company’s market capitalization for 60 consecutive trading days per his offer letter.

Each tranche vests upon the later of its time-based vesting date (second anniversary for tranche 1, third for tranches 2 and 3, fourth for tranches 4 and 5) and certification of the market cap hurdle, subject to continued employment.

There is no arrangement or understanding with any person pursuant to which Mr. De Bock was appointed as CFO, and there are no family relationships between him and any director or executive officer of the Company.
There are no reportable transactions between Mr. De Bock and the Company or its subsidiaries under Item 404(a) of Regulation S-K.

The full details of Mr. De Bock's compensation arrangements are qualified by his offer letter, a copy of which will be filed as an exhibit to the Company’s Quarterly Report on Form 10-Q for the fiscal quarter ending September 30, 2026.
For Fiscal Year 2023 (July 1, 2022, to June 30, 2023), total investments in facility expansions and new locations totaled more than $24 billion.
FY23 resulted in 38,400 new jobs through 426 projects supported by GDEcD’s Global Commerce team.

When excluding the two largest projects in state history – Rivian and Hyundai Motor Group – from the previous year’s job creation figures, total jobs as a result of FY23 projects exceeded the previous record by more than 2,800 new positions.
Annual investment totals have increased by 131 percent over the last three years.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that the FY23 numbers confirm the state and local partnership approach with community and economic development leaders is working, bringing unprecedented opportunity to communities across Georgia.
Eighty-two percent of new jobs created and more than $20 billion in investments are going to communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region.
Metro Atlanta attracted $3.6 billion in investment last fiscal year.
Expansions account for 71 percent of total projects, and new locations account for 65 percent of new jobs announced across the state.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones stated that economic development is critical to remain the No. 1 state to do business and best to live, work, and raise a family, noting Georgia has secured its role as an economic and business hub of the Southeast.

Speaker of the House Jon Burns stated that Georgia's success results from ongoing public-private partnerships and commitment to expanding opportunity, highlighting workforce development and tax reform to maintain a pro-business environment.
Between July 2018 and July 2023, at least 40 e-mobility projects have been announced, committing more than 30,000 jobs and over $25 billion in investment to the state.
Job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent in FY23 when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
Mobility and cleantech projects alone delivered three investments of $1 billion or more and six of the largest projects by job creation.
This mobility success is attributed to fostering innovation with support from organizations like the Georgia Center of Innovation, which connects public and private partners in strategic ecosystems.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that innovation drives a sustainable economy for existing industries like aerospace and agriculture while building an emerging ecosystem to support cleantech and mobility of the future.
Investments in aerospace-related projects increased by more than 1,000 percent since FY21.
The aerospace industry ecosystem overlaps with e-mobility through companies like Archer.
Top regional aerospace employers include Gulfstream.
Georgia is home to the fifth-largest population of active and retired service members in the country, offering opportunities for defense contractors.

Partnerships on speed-to-market and workforce solutions like the “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development” (GRAD) certification and Georgia Quick Start programs spurred a 112 percent growth in advanced manufacturing investments since FY21.

GDEcD Deputy Commissioner for Global Commerce Kristi Brigman stated that working alongside local communities fulfills their long-term strategies and highlighted the state's superior logistics, infrastructure, education opportunities, and high quality of life.
Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport is described as the busiest and most efficient airport in the world.
The Port of Savannah is the single largest and fastest-growing container terminal in America.
The Port of Brunswick is one of the most utilized port terminals in the U.S. for roll-on, roll-off cargo.
Georgia welcomed household name brands including Walmart-subsidiary Sam’s Club, work clothing retailer Duluth Trading Company, and an expansion by professional food service supplier WebstaurantStore.
Georgia benefits from a reliable logistics industry extending throughout the state along a robust network of top-ranked highways and accessible railways.

Ample cold storage space allows key sectors like agriculture – the state’s number one industry – and food processing to safely transport products to local groceries and markets across the U.S. and abroad.
Agribusiness-related job creation grew by 29 percent compared to FY21.
The top international sources of job creation in FY23 were South Korea, Finland, Norway, Japan, Switzerland, and the Netherlands.
FY23 marked the return of important international economic development missions led by Governor and First Lady Kemp following a pause due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

Governor and First Lady Kemp traveled to Switzerland, Germany, Israel, the country of Georgia, and France for meetings with major companies, official diplomatic visits, and participation in the Paris Air Show.
International investments were responsible for 47 percent of expected jobs and 65 percent of the total investment in FY23.

Additional state missions focused on strengthening relationships were led by GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, Deputy Commissioner for International Trade Mary Waters, and Deputy Commissioner Kristi Brigman.

facts: The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (Public Law 117-169) amended the Qualified Plug-in Electric Drive Motor Vehicle Credit (IRC 30D), now known as the Clean Vehicle Credit.
A new requirement for final assembly in North America took effect on August 17, 2022.
The final assembly requirement applies to new electric, fuel cell electric, and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles acquired, delivered, and placed in service after August 16, 2022.
For vehicles placed in service on or after January 1, 2023, Clean Vehicle Credit provisions are subject to updated guidance from the IRS and the U.S. Department of the Treasury.
Beginning January 1, 2024, buyers can reduce a vehicle's upfront purchase price by the amount of their Clean Vehicle Credit by transferring the credit to the dealer.
Before 2024, eligible vehicle buyers could only receive the credit amount after filing their tax return.
Starting January 1, 2024, dealers must submit information to the IRS through IRS Energy Credits Online to determine vehicle eligibility and credit amount at the point of sale.
Without dealer submission to the IRS, buyers cannot claim a tax credit on their return nor transfer it to a dealer.
A dealer must provide the buyer a copy of the IRS's approval of the dealer's submission.
The list of EVs with final assembly in North America applies only to vehicles purchased and delivered between August 17 and December 31, 2022.
Model Year 2022 and 2023 vehicle eligibility is based on Fuel Economy labeling information and Part 583 data submitted by manufacturers to the EPA and NHTSA as of December 31, 2022.
Verification of final assembly for vehicles purchased beginning January 1, 2023, should use the VIN decoder.
Manufacturer sales caps apply to vehicles placed in service before January 1, 2023.
The Chevrolet Bolt EUV met its manufacturer sales cap.
The Chevrolet Bolt EV met its manufacturer sales cap.
The GMC Hummer EV Pickup met its manufacturer sales cap.
The GMC Hummer EV SUV met its manufacturer sales cap.
The Tesla Model 3 met its manufacturer sales cap.
The Tesla Model S met its manufacturer sales cap.
The Tesla Model X met its manufacturer sales cap.
The Tesla Model Y met its manufacturer sales cap.
The Cadillac Lyriq met its manufacturer sales cap.
The 2023 Chevrolet Bolt EV met its manufacturer sales cap.
The 2023 Tesla Model 3 met its manufacturer sales cap.
The 2023 Tesla Model S met its manufacturer sales cap.
The 2023 Tesla Model X met its manufacturer sales cap.
The 2023 Tesla Model Y met its manufacturer sales cap.
The vehicle eligibility list will be updated as manufacturers submit Fuel Economy labeling information and Part 583 vehicle identification information to relevant government agencies.
Final assembly in North America is not required for tax credit eligibility for vehicles purchased before August 17, 2022.
The build location of a particular vehicle should be confirmed by the VIN or an information label affixed to the vehicle.
The U.S. Department of Transportation's NHTSA provides a VIN decoder to identify a vehicle's build plant, country of manufacture, and other details.

Verification of North American final assembly is an initial step toward identifying vehicles eligible for the Clean Vehicle Credit, but further eligibility conditions may apply depending on the purchase date.
For eligibility questions, consumers should contact the manufacturer or reference information from the IRS.
For some manufacturers, the build location may vary based on the specific vehicle, trim, or production date because some models are produced in multiple locations.
To be eligible for the Clean Vehicle Credit, a vehicle must have undergone final assembly in North America.
In general, North America includes the United States and Puerto Rico, Canada, and Mexico for purposes of determining the location of final assembly.
The information on the page should not be viewed as an official or legally binding document.

facts: Companies have invested over $11.5 billion in Georgia since 2018 to lower emissions and reduce carbon footprint for themselves and their customers.
Georgia’s competitive advantages and responsiveness to rapidly evolving business needs have attracted a range of manufacturers, recyclers, and innovators leading the way to a greener future.
Ascend Elements, formerly known as Battery Resourcers, invested $43 million to open a commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling plant in Georgia.
The Ascend Elements facility will extract critically scarce materials like lithium, cobalt, and nickel from batteries.
The Ascend Elements facility will become the largest of its kind in North America when fully operational later this year.
Roger Lin is Ascend Elements’ Vice President of Marketing and Government Relations.
Roger Lin stated Georgia was selected primarily because of the growing electric vehicle and battery ecosystem in the state.
Proximity to partners and customers is essential in the sustainable battery materials industry, according to Lin.
The Southeast is a mecca for the electric vehicle and battery industry, with Georgia close to factories like SK Battery America and others, minimizing transportation costs.
Ascend Elements benefits from proximity to top talent from nearby universities and access to renewable energy.
Korea’s SK Battery America is investing nearly $2.6 billion in two facilities near Atlanta.
SK Battery America will supply batteries to Ford, Volkswagen, and other electric vehicle makers.
SK Battery America made its first announcement regarding the Georgia investment in 2018.
Since the company’s first announcement in 2018, more businesses focused on EV sustainability have invested in the state.
Plug Power recently announced a new $84 million, zero-emission hydrogen fuel production facility near I-95 and Georgia’s deepwater ports.
Plug Power provides fuel cell systems for e-mobility that deliver efficiency, fast fueling, and low cost to customers.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp launched the Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance.

The Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance is a statewide initiative between government, industries, electric utilities, nonprofits, and other stakeholders focused on growing the electric mobility ecosystem in the state.
The initiative aims to strengthen Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.
In 2021, EV manufacturer Rivian announced an investment of $5 billion to build a carbon-conscious facility.
The Rivian facility will make vertically connected electric platforms for trucks, sport/utility vehicles, and delivery vans.
Hyundai Motor Group will open its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in the State of Georgia.
Hyundai announced a $5.54 billion investment with operations slated to begin in the first half of 2025.
The Hyundai facility will have an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Many other Georgia companies are already making parts for EVs.
Companies from Korea, Germany, Turkey, and the Netherlands have announced plans to build EV-related facilities in the state.
Site Selection Magazine recently ranked Georgia No. 3 for sustainability in the South Atlantic region.
The Atlanta metro ranked in the Top 10 nationally for sustainability.
Since 2009, Georgia has increased net electricity generation from renewable sources by more than 93%.
Georgia’s electricity mix currently consists of nearly 12% renewables.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that renewable energy capacity installation in Georgia was driven organically by companies and consumers, accelerated through economic attractiveness rather than mandate.
Smart policies have allowed Georgia to promote renewable power development and offer a comprehensive portfolio of clean energy options that give the state a competitive edge.
Solar PV is Georgia’s fastest-growing source of renewable energy.
NanoPV will manufacture solar panels and equipment in south Georgia’s Sumter County.
Qcells is investing more than $320 million in its solar panel assembly plant in northern Georgia.
The Qcells plant is the largest in the Western hemisphere.
German copper recycler Aurubis announced plans to locate in Georgia.
Electronic waste recycler Igneo announced plans for Georgia.
Danimer Scientific, a manufacturer and developer of biodegradable materials, announced plans for Georgia.
Recycled furniture maker SoPoly announced plans for Georgia.
In Atlanta, EF Block turns everyday recycled Styrofoam into earth-friendly, stronger, sustainable walls that replace traditional building materials.
With support from GDEcD’s Center of Innovation and the City of Atlanta, EF Block launched a city-wide Styrofoam recycling initiative.
NANTRenewables is opening a plant on Georgia’s coast to manufacture biodegradable products like plates, straws, cutlery, trays, and cups.
NANTRenewables provides sustainable products to companies within the food, agriculture, and pharmaceutical industries.
Harold Meherg is a NANT Renewables senior executive.

Harold Meherg stated SeaPoint Industrial Terminal Complex in Savannah was selected for their new biogenic fillers and bioplastics manufacturing facility due to shared sustainability values and development-ready infrastructure including roads, rail access, sea access, and nearby utility connections.
Companies leading the charge in mobility research & development, battery manufacturing and recycling, and electric vehicle production all call Georgia home.
Ascend Elements’ Lin stated they are pleased to be in a strategic location with the critical mass of factories that are locating or already operating in the area.
Lin described Georgia as a very friendly, welcoming place to open and grow a business with large facilities like theirs.
source_title: Georgia Emerges as Hub for Sustainability | Georgia

facts: On May 15, 2026, the OneGeorgia Authority, housed within the Georgia Department of Community Affairs (DCA), released a Notice of Funding Availability for the Rural Site Development Initiative.
The initiative will provide approximately twenty million dollars ($20,000,000) in state funding to assist local communities with developing sites crucial to economic development across Georgia.
Funding will be awarded at regular semi-annual intervals through an ongoing, competitive grant cycle, subject to funding availability.
The Authority reserves the right to fund any, all, or none of the applications submitted and may waive minimum and maximum funding limits at its discretion.
Applications for less than fifty thousand dollars ($50,000) will not be accepted.
Only one application will be considered per applicant, per cycle.
Projects must demonstrate the ability to address a lack of available industrial sites for recruitment and retention of employment opportunities.
Projects must demonstrate the ability to address the difficulty of satisfying project sites in communities or regions with high unemployment.
Projects must demonstrate the ability to address a community or regional commitment to expand and improve existing sites.
Studies for due diligence on site development are limited to no more than two hundred fifty thousand dollars ($250,000).
Site Improvement grants are limited to no more than two million dollars ($2,000,000) per application and development.
Applicants located in an OneGeorgia Eligible community should generally contribute at least a ten percent (10%) match to the amount of grant funds requested.
Applicants located in an OneGeorgia Conditionally Eligible community should contribute at a minimum twenty-five percent (25%) match to the amount of grant funds requested.
Communities located in an OneGeorgia Non-Eligible county are not allowed to apply or receive grant funds.
Applications are expected to leverage other sources of funding such as private debt or equity, philanthropic support, or other public funds.
Eligible applicants include units of general-purpose local government (e.g., city, county, consolidated government), as well as local, regional, and state authorities.
Prospective applicants must be located in OneGeorgia Eligible and Conditionally Eligible counties to submit applications for activities within those areas.
Local government and authority applicants must comply with audit report and grant certification form submission requirements contained in O.C.G.A. §§ 36-81-7 and 86-81-8.1.
Applicants must be current and compliant with all reporting, audit, and survey requirements as required by statute.
Prior recipients of OneGeorgia funding must resolve any outstanding audit and/or compliance monitoring findings or deficiencies prior to submitting an application.

Eligible infrastructure and construction costs include grading, clearing, grubbing, infrastructure development, road improvements, accel/decel lanes, engineering, purchase of adjacent or expansion property, easements, and due diligence studies.
Sites must be publicly controlled at the time of application submission and must be a minimum of fifty (50) contiguous acres.
If a site is not publicly owned, the development authority must hold an option to purchase extending for a period of no less than three (3) years for an application to be considered.
Applicants seeking Site Improvement Grants must be GRAD certified or GRAD select at the time of application.
Applicants seeking Due Diligence grant funding must commit to applying for GRAD certification within one year.
If a site is not GRAD certified, completion of due diligence activities and receiving GRAD certification are both prerequisites to the Authority considering the site for Site Development funding.
Should an applicant receive a Due Diligence award and achieve GRAD certification on said property, the applicant may pursue Site Improvement grant funding in any subsequent funding cycle.

Funding for a second location will not be considered by the Authority prior to an original funded site receiving GRAD certification (if not already achieved), all infrastructure installed within the industrial park, and marketing of such property being undertaken.
Sale or lease activities not compliant with the NOFA parameters are subject to penalties addressed in award documentation.
Restrictions on OneGeorgia-improved property prohibit use for solar farm activities, data centers, or mini-storage facilities.
Developments must comply with state environmental and historic preservation requirements.
A pre-application is due thirty (30) days prior to the full application deadline and should be submitted via email.
Each completed application will first be reviewed to reconfirm eligibility and determine completeness, then thoroughly vetted and competitively scored.
The full application should include a preliminary engineering report and commitment letters for all local funding match.
For Fiscal Year 2027, the submission deadline is October 9, 2026, with a December/January award announcement.
For Fiscal Year 2027, the second submission deadline is April 9, 2027, with a June/July award announcement.
Successful applicants will receive a written notice of award including grant or loan documents, requirements, timelines, and other details.
OneGeorgia funds must generally be expended within two years from the date of the award.
Completion is defined as all activities for which funds were received being complete, all awarded funding having been expended, and all award close-out procedures being finalized.
