CEO Josh Ayer said the company chose Virginia for this expansion because of its positive business environment and strong support from the Commonwealth of Virginia and Floyd County.
Joe Turman, Chairman of the Floyd County Board of Supervisors, stated that the new investment is over three times bigger than the largest private investment in the county’s 191-year history.
Delegate Marie March welcomed the investment, noting it will provide crucial jobs for local families and keep them working at home.
H&V was incorporated in 1892 and is headquartered in East Walpole, Massachusetts.
The company has been family-owned for seven generations.
H&V established its first Virginia facility in Floyd County in 1976.
The Floyd County facility currently employs over 200 workers.
The Virginia Economic Development Partnership worked with Floyd County to secure the project for Virginia.
Governor Youngkin approved a $558,700 grant from the Commonwealth’s Opportunity Fund to assist Floyd County with the project.
source_title: Hollingsworth & Vose to Expand in Virginia | Virginia Economic Development Partnership

facts: Mativ Holdings, Inc. (formerly Schweitzer-Mauduit International, Inc.) is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, United States of America.

The company operates 47 production locations worldwide across the United States, United Kingdom, China, Germany, France, Belgium, Poland, India, Brazil, Canada, Spain, Italy, Mexico, Netherlands, Malaysia, and Luxembourg.
Mativ holds a 50% equity interest in two joint ventures located in China.
The company's technology portfolio combines polymers, fibers, and resins to optimize customer product performance across multiple stages of the value chain.
Mativ targets premium applications across diversified and growing end-markets.
On July 6, 2022, Schweitzer-Mauduit International, Inc. consummated a merger with Neenah, Inc., after which SWM changed its name to Mativ Holdings, Inc.
The company reports under two segments: Advanced Technical Materials (ATM) and Fiber-Based Solutions (FBS).
source_title: Annual Report (10-k)

facts: Hollingsworth & Vose (H&V) is a global company that specializes in advanced materials for filtration, battery separator, and industrial applications.
H&V was founded in 1843.
H&V has become a leader in the filtration industry through continual innovation.
Hollingsworth & Vose operates in the Nanotechnology category.
Hollingsworth & Vose has between 1,000 and 5,000 employees.
source_title: Employment Verification for Hollingsworth & Vose | Truv

facts: Honda is one of America’s most fuel-efficient full-line automakers.
Honda celebrates 50 years of Accord.
Honda reveals a plan for 15 new Honda and Acura hybrid models by 2030.
The 2026 CR-V now includes a new hybrid trim.
The 2026 Ridgeline is built for rugged terrain.
The 2026 Odyssey features versatile design for family adventures.
Honda envisions a future where collisions are a thing of the past, starting with model year 2040 vehicles.
Honda is committed to continually reducing its environmental footprint at every step to help preserve the planet for future generations.
Honda offers career opportunities ranging from automotive to robotics.
source_title: American Honda Motor Co., Inc. - Official Site

facts: Honda associates Jessika Laudermilk and Tim Leopold were named 2024 Rising Stars by Automotive News.
Laudermilk was acknowledged for her role in directing marketing operations for both the Honda and Acura brands.
In June 2024, Laudermilk assumed a new role as assistant vice president of Honda National Sales.
Laudermilk is the company’s youngest-ever executive to lead U.S. sales activities for the Honda automobile brand and the first woman to serve in this position.
Tim Leopold is an associate chief engineer at Honda Development & Manufacturing of America (HDMA) in Ohio.
Leopold serves as project leader for the Marysville Auto Plant (MAP) Battery EV Infrastructure.
In this role, Leopold is responsible for the introduction of EV production at MAP.
The Marysville Auto Plant was established in 1982 as the first Honda auto plant in North America and will become the first Honda facility to begin EV production.

The Automotive News Rising Star awards annually honor automaker and supplier leaders based in the U.S. and under age 45 who have the talent and dedication necessary to become industry leaders of tomorrow.
Automotive News has published its list of up-and-comers annually since 2014.
Leopold and Laudermilk were two of 21 honorees from disciplines including executive management, purchasing, engineering, mobility, and design.
Laudermilk joined Honda in 2018 within the Honda Auto Sales Division.
After leading Honda Marketing, she became assistant vice president of the Marketing Division in 2022, where she led marketing for both the Honda and Acura brands.
Under Laudermilk’s leadership, Honda ranked as the number one auto brand in America in the 2024 Axios Harris Poll.
Leopold joined Honda in 2002 and has worked to innovate production capabilities in North America.
Leopold was instrumental in the planning, development, and global introduction of the first 10-speed automatic transmission at the Honda Transmission Plant Georgia.
The work at the Honda Transmission Plant Georgia demonstrated the ability of a U.S. facility to lead global production of a brand-new powertrain.
This 10-speed automatic transmission is now featured in a number of Honda and Acura vehicles.
Leopold took a senior role within the new Honda EV Hub in 2022 to prepare Honda for EV production at MAP.
source_title: Two Honda Leaders Named 2024 Rising Stars by Automotive News

entity_name: Honda Transmission Plant in Tallapoosa, Georgia
canonical_name: Honda Development & Manufacturing of America, LLC (HDMA)
facts: Honda celebrated 15 years of U.S. automobile transmission production at its Tallapoosa, Georgia plant on May 12, 2021.
Transmission production in Georgia began in 2006 with 179 associates.
The Georgia operation has a total investment of more than $465 million.
The facility currently employs more than 500 associates.
The plant produces 375,000 transmissions annually for the Honda Odyssey, Accord, Acura MDX, RDX, and TLX.
The facility was formerly known as Honda Precision Parts of Georgia (HPPG).
Production initially began with 5-speed automatic transmissions.
The plant added 6-speed automatic transmissions in 2012.
A second assembly line was added to the Georgia plant in 2017.
The Georgia facility became the first Honda plant globally to produce Honda's advanced 10-speed automatic transmission (10AT).
In early 2021, the plant began replacing its original production line to increase 10AT production.

Gerald Bruch, vice president of Honda Development and Manufacturing of America, LLC (HDMA), stated that the Georgia and Ohio plants form a transmission powerhouse creating advanced technology components for Honda and Acura products.
Honda's first dedicated U.S. transmission plant in Russells Point, Ohio, will mark its 25th anniversary in July 2021.
Honda has manufactured over 21 million transmissions in America since starting automatic transmission production in Ohio in 1989.
The Ohio transmission plant has a total investment of more than $1 billion and spans 1.1 million square feet.
The Ohio facility produces over 850,000 transmissions per year, along with gear sets, transfer cases, and differentials for four-wheel drive vehicles.
Both the Georgia and Ohio plants are taking on new responsibilities as Honda begins electrifying its vehicle lineup.
Production will include Honda's innovative two-motor hybrid system used in the Accord Hybrid, CR-V Hybrid, and Insight.
Honda began U.S. transmission production at its Anna Engine Plant in Ohio in 1989.
In 1996, transmission manufacturing lines were relocated from the Anna Engine Plant to Bellemar Parts Industries in Russells Point, Ohio.
Bellemar was incorporated as Honda Transmission of America in 1997.
In spring 2021, both Honda transmission plant locations were combined under a new company named Honda Development & Manufacturing of America, LLC (HDMA).
HDMA encompasses all Honda automobile manufacturing facilities in the U.S. related to frame, engine, transmission, and related engineering and purchasing operations.
source_title: Honda Transmission Plant in Georgia Celebrates Milestone, Steps Toward Electrified Future – Gradick Communications

facts: Honda debuted two "0 Series" EV concepts (Saloon and Space-Hub) following a CES stage appearance with Sony regarding their joint-venture Afeela EV.
The Honda 0 concept Saloon is positioned as the flagship model with a wide, low and angular silhouette of a sports coupe.
Behind its gull wing doors is seating for four passengers tucked low for a sporty feel.
Ahead of the driver is a steering yoke and a full-width dashboard display that stretches from door to door.
Honda states that the core concept behind the Honda 0 models is "man maximum/machine minimum," indicating its commitment to driver-first, engaging performance and lightweight efficiency.
The 0 Series will include steer-by-wire and robotics-based automated driving technologies.
Honda promises that the production car based on the Honda 0 Saloon will arrive in 2026 with "the joy of driving" intact.
The EVs will feature e-axles, which tuck the electric motors in-line with the wheels rather than in an engine bay.
The EVs will use compact, high-density battery packs that help maximize interior space.
Honda promises "stress-free charging" with a production expectation of 10 to 15 minutes for a 15% to 80% charge.
Honda targets less than 10% capacity loss after 10 years of driving to minimize battery degradation over time.
The Honda 0 Space-Hub concept is more upright and van-shaped compared to its conceptual sibling.
The Space-Hub features a generous transparent canopy, an airy living room on wheels design, and center-facing bench seats that encourage passengers to engage with one another on their journey.
Both concepts feature single-frame front fascia with small, rectangular headlamps.
Rather than a grille, each EV features an illuminated panel that creates the perception of depth while displaying a blue particle animation.
The 3D design is also echoed at the rear end of both concepts.
Both concepts feature a new frameless "H mark" badge designed for Honda's next-generation EVs to distinguish production Honda 0 models from the rest of the family.
source_title: Honda Debuts Two '0 Series' EV Concepts Aimed at 2026 Production - CNET

facts: Reallocation and retooling at Honda’s Greensburg assembly plant to produce next‑generation Civic hybrid models for the US market starting in 2028.
The program replaces previously planned production in Mexico.
Operator and developer is Honda Motor Co.
Location is Greensburg, Indiana.
Approximate budget is publicly reported but investment not fully disclosed; it is part of a US electrification shift.
Construction status is design phase.
Target completion is 2028.
Peak construction headcount is in the 1,000-3,000+ trades range during structural + MEP overlap.
Crew shuttle service provides multi-shift dispatch aligned to the GC’s construction schedule with 6 AM / 2 PM / 10 PM rotations on 24-hour ramps.
Chauffeurs are DOT-compliant and pre-credentialed through the operator’s contractor onboarding, requiring background checks, NDAs, and vehicle inspections.
Vehicle right-sizing covers crew vans through motor coaches plus 4×4 units for unimproved approaches to match the project ramp curve.
Branded vehicles are available for high-visibility GC, operator, or specialty-trade programs.
Regional lodging-cluster coverage consolidates the Indianapolis IN metro into a single dispatch network.
Real-time tracking and reporting are provided to the operator’s and GC’s project-management teams.
Substitution coverage includes backup vehicles staged for single-point-of-failure routes.
Multi-prime cost allocation enables shared shuttle programs across GCs and specialty subs.
Typical workforce ramp for a project of this scale peaks at 1,000-3,000+ trades during structural + MEP + interior overlap.
Multi-shift coverage (6 AM / 2 PM / 10 PM) is standard during peak ramp windows.
Contractors should ideally engage shuttle providers during precon or early site enablement, 60-90 days before crew mobilization.
Standard vehicle types include 14-passenger crew vans, 22/35-passenger mini coaches, 56-passenger motor coaches, plus 4×4 / AWD units.
Primary lodging staging is in the Indianapolis IN metro, with most crews having 15-60 minute drives to the project gate.
Auto assembly plant construction sites typically use controlled gate entry, badge-checked vehicle screening, and pre-approved-personnel manifests.

Fleet size and shift count scale with the build, tripling or quadrupling crew transport needs over 60-120 day windows as the project moves from civil + early MEP into structural + interior + commissioning peaks.
source_title: Honda Greensburg Civic Hybrid Assembly Program — Crew Shuttles

facts: Kia’s parent firm is planning a second automobile factory in Georgia.
The new plant plans include hiring 8,500 people.
South Korea-based Hyundai Motor Group is negotiating for a site along I-16 in Bryan County, near Savannah.
Details of the project, such as projected costs, were not immediately known at the time of reporting.
Hyundai Motor Group stated it is excited to announce a new EV plant plan in the United States soon but has no details to share at this stage.
Reuters first reported Hyundai Motor’s plans to build a U.S. plant.
The Atlanta Journal-Constitution (AJC) first reported details about the location and number of jobs for the new plant.
Kia Motors is investing $6.2 billion in its EV business through 2030.
A 2022 rift occurred between Kia and the Georgia state government over the capacity of Kia's first Georgia plant.
The company requested an additional $65 million in incentives for its Georgia plant during the rift.
Kia's new second plant will be located on 2,200 acres near Ellabell, off I-16 in Bryan County.
The site for the new plant was previously used as a military base.
Kia Motors Georgia VE project is projected to create 8,500 jobs and is located in Bryan County.
Hyundai Motor Group's South Georgia project is projected to create 7,500 jobs and is located near Savannah.
Hyundai Motor Group's South Georgia project has an investment of $5.1 billion.
KS Battery America project is projected to create 3,000 jobs and is located in Jackson County.
KS Battery America project has an investment of $6.2 billion.
The total projected jobs from Kia & Hyundai projects combined is 15,000.
The Peach State is cementing its status as a hub for electric vehicles due to these investments.
People familiar with the deal were not authorized to comment on the negotiations.
source_title: Kia plans second Georgia auto factory that could dwarf its first

facts: A fully electric Acura RSX SUV will be the first model produced at American Honda Motor Co.’s retooled flexible vehicle manufacturing assembly lines in Ohio later this year, the automaker announced on Feb. 3.

Honda is investing over $1 billion in three Ohio plants to serve as its U.S. “EV hub,” which will also continue producing internal combustion engine vehicles and fully electric models for the North America market.
The move gives the automaker flexibility to adapt to changing market conditions, the company said.

“Beyond adding the capability to produce EVs, we completely reimagined our approach to manufacturing, transforming the Honda production environment with more human-friendly processes and sustainable manufacturing practices,” Bob Schwyn, SVP Honda development and manufacturing of America, said in the release.
Honda announced plans to establish its U.S. EV Hub in October 2022 as part of its goal for zero emissions vehicles to account for 100% of its sales by 2040.
The three Ohio locations include the automaker’s Marysville Auto Plant, East Liberty Auto Plant and Anna Engine Plant, which are collectively referred to as the “Honda EV Hub.”
The three plants will also serve as a blueprint for Honda’s other North American and global facilities on how to adapt to similar manufacturing methods, the company said.
Honda aims to reimagine vehicle manufacturing across its three Ohio factories in line with its “Triple Action to Zero” strategic initiative.
It includes three focus areas — carbon neutrality, clean energy and resource circulation — to help the automaker achieve zero environmental impact by 2050.
In addition to the all electric Acura RSX, the U.S. EV hub will also produce the futuristic Honda 0 Saloon and Honda 0 SUV prototypes unveiled at CES in early January.
Honda and LG Energy Solution have also committed to a $3.5 billion investment in a new joint venture battery facility in Jefferson, Ohio, to produce batteries for Honda and Acura EVs.
The overall investment in the plant is forecast to reach $4.4 billion, with production scheduled to begin later this year with an annual capacity of approximately 40 gigawatt hours.
The Marysville facility will become the first Honda plant globally to assemble EV battery packs.
Among the plant’s upgrades, the automaker reconfigured welding operations using robots and other technology to weld EV bodies on the same production line as Honda’s ICE models.
Honda also created a new dedicated area in Marysville for the installation of battery packs into EVs.
The process combines the modules produced by Honda’s joint venture facility with a case megacast at the Anna Engine Plant for use in mid-size and larger vehicles.
The Marysville plant will also assemble smaller battery packs for hybrid vehicles.

Honda placed the new hybrid battery assembly closer to the installation area on the vehicle assembly line to prepare for what it expects will be “record customer demand for hybrid-electric models,” the company said.
Employees at Marysville are being trained for new roles in EV production, battery pack assembly and performing quality checks of software-defined vehicles, Honda said.
Honda also installed six, 31-foot tall, 6,000-ton high-pressure die cast machines at its Anna Engine Plant to megacast EV battery case parts, a first for the automaker.
Honda says the battery case is much larger than any other part it has die-cast before and it will serve as the main frame structure for the floor of Honda and Acura EVs.
These massive press machines also offer the flexibility to produce other cast aluminum parts, including body and frame parts or engine components for hybrid and electric vehicles.

Honda’s East Liberty Auto Plant also features a new “battery pack mount mezzanine,” which is designed to allow EV models to “disengage” from the main assembly line for EV battery pack installation, the company said.
Once the battery is installed, the EVs return to the line for final completion alongside ICE and hybrid-electric models.

Honda also strengthened vehicle carriers in the East Liberty plant’s paint department to support the additional weight of EVs, along with other improvements in the paint process to enhance overall appearance.
Honda is also investing in Canada to build EVs.
Last April, Honda and its joint venture partners announced plans to invest up to CAD $15 billion ($11 billion) to build out an electric vehicle supply chain.
The plans include a new EV factory in Canada and a battery plant in Alliston, Ontario.
The EV factory is expected to begin production in 2028, with a production capacity of 240,000 EVs per year, while the battery plant will have a capacity of 36 GWh per year.
source_title: Honda prepares to start ‘flexible’ vehicle production at Ohio EV hub | WardsAuto

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced that Doowon Climate Control America, Inc., an auto parts manufacturer and supplier, will invest more than $30 million in a new manufacturing facility in Metter.
The investment will create 200 new jobs in Candler County.
This marks the second automotive supplier announced in Candler County in less than six months.
In fiscal year 2023, alone, 82 percent of new jobs created and more than $20 billion of investments went to communities outside the metro Atlanta area.
Established in Korea in 1974, Doowon Group specializes in designing and manufacturing automotive climate control systems.
The company will be a key supplier for Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (Kia Georgia) and Hyundai Motor Group.
Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. is making significant investments to establish itself as Doowon Group’s inaugural automotive air conditioning system production facility in the United States.
Doowon Climate Control America, Inc.’s new manufacturing facility will be located in the Metter-Candler County I-16 Industrial Park.
The site is a “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development” (GRAD) certified site.
The facility will primarily produce climate control systems for use in traditional and electric vehicles.
Operations are projected to begin in 2026.

Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. plans to hire employees in various fields such as injection molding technicians; assembly operators; quality, materials, and production specialists; engineers; and management.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was completed in partnership with the Candler County Industrial Authority, Georgia Power, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Quick Start.
Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. marks the second automotive supplier announced in the City of Metter and Candler County in less than six months.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
As the electric vehicle market continues to grow, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain, creating more than $25.7 billion in investments and 30,200 jobs since 2018.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
source_title: GA: Second Automotive Parts Manufacturer Creating 200 New Jobs in Candler County | Trade and Industry Development

facts: Honda announced key next steps in the establishment of its EV Hub in Ohio to lead its journey to an electrified future in North America.
In October 2022, Honda announced it would invest $700 million to re-tool several existing auto and powertrain plants to establish the new EV Hub in Ohio.
The investment aims to prepare for the production of battery electric vehicles in 2026.
The Marysville Auto Plant (MAP), where Honda began auto production in America in 1982, will be Honda’s first auto plant in the U.S. to transition to making EVs.
MAP will consolidate two assembly lines currently making internal combustion engine and hybrid-electric vehicles to retool for EV and EV component production.
Accord production will be transferred from MAP to the Indiana Auto Plant as MAP retools for EV production.
Honda Transmission Plant in Georgia (TMP-G) will dedicate one production line to e-axle production, an essential EV component combining the electric motor, gearing components, and power electronics.

TMP-G will partner with a Honda supplier to install a new e-axle line in space currently occupied by a non-operational transmission production line; the supplier will install, own, and operate the new line.
Anna Engine Plant (AEP) will shift production of some engine components to the Honda engine plant in Alabama to prepare for battery case production for EV models.
AEP will transfer component production for two different generations of engines to the Alabama Auto Plant (AAP).
Machining of the engine head for the existing V6 engine that powers many Honda light trucks, which ended at AEP in February, will be added to AAP operations beginning in March 2023.
Engine block casting for the all-new V6 engine introduced in the 2023 Honda Pilot will begin at AAP in August 2023.
These moves will allow AEP to re-allocate space for future IPU Case production, which will be combined with battery modules from a new joint venture facility in Ohio with LG Energy Solution (LGES).
EV batteries from the JV will power EVs produced at MAP and East Liberty Auto Plant (ELP).
Honda expects to maintain stable employment across all locations during these transition steps.
MAP will begin preparing for EV production as early as January 2024 by consolidating production to one line to build infrastructure necessary for EV production.
Every MAP associate will have a position as part of this transition.
Starting later in 2023, MAP associates will begin training programs designed to prepare them for skills required for EV production.
Production of the Honda Accord will transfer to the Indiana Auto Plant (IAP) in 2025 to maintain core model volume while enabling MAP's transition.

As part of the EV Hub, Honda will transform MAP, ELP, and AEP to start EV production in North America, developing knowledge and expertise to be shared across its entire North American auto production network.
Honda and LGES recently held an official groundbreaking ceremony for a new joint venture EV battery plant in Fayette County, near Jeffersonville, Ohio.
The two companies committed to invest $3.5 billion in the JV facility, which will be over 2 million square feet and scheduled for completion by the end of 2024.
The JV facility aims for approximately 40GWh of annual production capacity, with overall investment projected to reach $4.4 billion.

Honda aims to achieve carbon neutrality for all products and corporate activities by 2050, with a vision to make battery-electric and fuel cell electric vehicles represent 100% of its vehicle sales by 2040.
Honda plans to sustain current ICE and hybrid-electric vehicle production through 2030 and beyond to meet customer demand and support investment in its electrified future.
Honda has been producing automobiles in America for over 40 years, beginning in November 1982 at MAP.
In 2022, more than two-thirds of all Honda and Acura automobiles sold in the U.S. were made in America using domestic and globally sourced parts.
Honda’s cumulative auto production in America now exceeds 30 million vehicles.

Honda established manufacturing operations in America in 1979 with motorcycle production in Marysville, Ohio, and today has one of the largest and most diverse U.S. manufacturing footprints of any international company.

The company’s 12 plants employ more than 22,000 associates in America, supporting operations with an annual capacity of more than 1.27 million automobiles, 1.52 million engines, 500,000 power equipment products, and 300,000 powersports products, as well as the HondaJet advanced light jet and GE Honda HF120 turbofan engines.
Cumulatively, Honda has invested $19 billion in its U.S. manufacturing operations, including more than $3.2 billion over the past five years alone.
The company works with over 600 U.S. original equipment suppliers, with U.S.-sourced parts purchases of approximately $470 billion since 1979.
source_title: Honda Announces Next Steps in Preparation for U.S. EV Production | Honda Global Corporate Website

facts: Honda has officially announced that it will discontinue production of the current generation of fuel cell systems by FCSM before the end of 2026.
FCSM, which stands for ‘Fuel Cell System Manufacturing,’ was established in January 2017 as a joint venture between Honda and General Motors.
The partners only began their joint series production of fuel cell systems in Brownstown, Michigan, at the beginning of 2024.
The actual production phase was short-lived.
The fuel cell systems produced in Brownstown were used by both partners in various applications—not only in vehicles but also for data centres and power generation.
Honda describes FCSM as ‘first ever joint venture in the automotive industry that would produce advanced fuel cell system.’
Through this collaboration, both companies pooled their expertise and achieved numerous synergies.
After ‘extensive discussions’ with GM, both parties agreed to cease production at FCSM.
For General Motors, this decision marks a temporary departure from fuel cell technology.
As early as October 2025, the American company terminated the development of the next generation of hydrogen fuel cells under its Hydrotec brand.
Honda is regarded as a pioneer in hydrogen and hybrid technologies but has lagged behind in battery-electric vehicles.
Honda stated as recently as late 2024 that it aimed to expand its hydrogen business into one of its core operations.
It identified four lucrative application areas: hydrogen passenger cars, hydrogen commercial vehicles, stationary power plants, and construction machinery.
Honda currently offers the CR-V e:FCEV, a compact plug-in hybrid SUV with a hydrogen fuel cell electric powertrain, on the market.
Honda plans to focus on its self-developed next-generation systems.
It aims to further expand business opportunities in order to grow its hydrogen business as one of the new core businesses of Honda.
It positions hydrogen alongside electricity as ‘high-potential energy carriers.’
In mid-2025, Honda announced that it would scale back its plans to build a new production facility for fuel cell modules in Japan.
At the time, the company stated it would reduce the initial production capacity and postpone the start of series production, despite government subsidies.
Honda cited recent changes in the global hydrogen market as the reason.
In its latest announcement, the company adopts a defiant tone, emphasising its 30 years of R&D experience in the field.
The discontinuation of FCSM production in Michigan represents another setback for Honda’s hydrogen ambitions.
source_title: Honda and GM discontinue fuel cell production in the US - electrive.com

facts: The 5th annual Georgia Manufacturing Summit was held on Oct. 9, 2019, at the Cobb Galleria in Atlanta.
Almost 800 manufacturing insiders attended the summit.
Stuart Countess, COO of Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, spoke at the summit.
Werhner Washington, recently retired plant manager for the Procter and Gamble Albany plant, also spoke at the summit.
Werhner Washington’s career at Procter and Gamble spanned more than 35 years.
In 2019, Werhner Washington led the Albany plant to win the “Manufacturer of the Year” award for Georgia.
Washington stated that delivering total shareholder returns was always their goal rather than winning awards.
Washington added that having the right culture is the key to success.
source_title: Manufacturing Leaders Share Success Stories at Georgia Manufacturing Summit -  Georgia Manufacturing Alliance

facts: Hyundai is rapidly building its first US electric vehicle plant in Ellabelle, Georgia.
The construction site spans more than a half mile and covers more than 2,900 acres (1,170 hectares) parallel to Interstate 16 west of Savannah.
Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on the $7.6 billion vehicle and battery plant one year prior to the article's reporting.
More than 2,000 people are working each week on the construction project.
The site is dotted with cranes, bulldozers, and construction workers, resembling a small city.
Foundation work for the factory’s main production buildings is almost finished.
The framework of more than 27,000 tons (24,490 metric tons) of steel is more than 80% complete.
Some buildings have roofs and floors, and exterior wall panels have begun to go up.
Hyundai officials refer to the facility as its American “metaplant.”
The plant will stamp, weld, paint, and assemble electric vehicles.
Production is on track to start in early 2025, with possible acceleration to open before the end of next year.
Construction was accelerated to take advantage of federal incentives that reward domestic production of EVs.
Hyundai says it will build 300,000 EVs each year at the plant.
The site will manufacture batteries to power those vehicles in a partnership with LG Energy Solution.
Artist renderings show buildings with plentiful windows and skylights to maximize natural lighting indoors.
A large covered parking lot for employees will be topped with solar panels.
An elevated bridge with glass sides will allow visibility of unfinished cars moving by conveyor from the paint shop to the assembly plant.
The plant will employ 8,500 workers upon completion.
Suppliers opening shop in nine Georgia counties near the Hyundai plant are projected to create another 6,000 jobs.
It is the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
State officials and local governments offered a $2.1 billion tax break incentive package for the project.
Georgia’s economic development commissioner Pat Wilson stated Hyundai is projected to have a direct payroll of $4.7 billion over the next 10 years.
The company has promised to pay workers a yearly average of $58,105 plus benefits.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America CEO Oscar Kwon told reporters visiting the site Wednesday that the project is advancing daily.
Hyundai president and global chief operating officer Jose Munoz indicated production could open before the end of next year.
Savannah Area Economic Development Authority president and CEO Trip Tollison noted the rapid progress made in just one year.
source_title: Hyundai is rapidly building its first US electric vehicle plant, with production on track for 2025 | AP News

facts: Hyundai Motor Group has commenced production of the IONIQ 9 at its advanced Georgia manufacturing facility.
The three-row IONIQ 9 serves as Hyundai's new flagship electric SUV for North America.
It is the second model produced at the HMGMA plant, following the IONIQ 5.
The vehicle is built on Hyundai’s specialized Electric Global Modular Platform (E-GMP).
Standout features include a sweeping panoramic display and what Hyundai describes as its most advanced driver-assistance systems yet.
Early reports suggest the combination of long range and quick charging could make it particularly appealing to families making the switch to electric.
Hyundai is investing billions into the Georgia facility to make it one of its key EV manufacturing hubs equipped with the latest equipment.
Local officials have noted the plant’s progress represents one of the largest industrial developments in recent Georgia history.
The facility is providing good-paying factory jobs that require technical skills, delivering real economic benefits to the local area.
Job numbers are expected to grow as Hyundai continues to add more production lines at the site.
Manufacturing the IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9 in Georgia allows Hyundai to sidestep import tariffs that would otherwise drive up vehicle prices.
Buyers of these domestically produced vehicles may qualify for $7,500 federal EV credits and state-level incentives applicable only to U.S.-made vehicles.
The plant is gearing up for higher production volumes, with insiders expecting more EV models to roll off the lines in the coming years.
Local production helps Hyundai stay competitive on both price and availability as EV demand grows, albeit slower than some predicted.

links.context: Detailed reviews and insights on electric SUVs and the EV market

source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Commences IONIQ 9 Production at Georgia Facility | EV.com

facts: Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on Metaplant America, an electric vehicle and battery plant under construction in Bryan County.
The automaker is investing $5.5 billion in its first EV plant in the United States.
Dozens of Hyundai officials, including Executive Chair Euisun Chung, traveled to Savannah for the groundbreaking ceremony.
Site preparation began on the 11-building, 16 million-square-foot facility this summer, and much of the 3,000-acre site has already been cleared and graded.
Hyundai projects a 2025 opening for the plant.

According to Hyundai's North American Division President José Muñoz, the plant will be the "foundation of Hyundai Motor Group's future in North America" as the automaker aims to "lead the future of electrified mobility."
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp, U.S. Sens. Raphael Warnock and Jon Ossoff, and U.S. Rep. Buddy Carter attended the ceremony alongside dozens of other state and local dignitaries.
U.S. Rep. Buddy Carter is noted as a Republican not backing Trump's pledge to scrap the Inflation Reduction Act.

Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated, "At Hyundai Motor Group, our corporate mission is 'Progress for humanity.' Looking around I know we picked the right site, the right partners and the right state for progress."
Chung added, "Together we will make the new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America the envy of the industry."
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp said, "Even in Georgia, where we seem to make history every day, this project is monumental."
Kemp also noted, "We're always working toward the future, and this project is significant."
The groundbreaking occurred five months after Hyundai officials initially announced the facility.
Trip Tollison, president of the Savannah Economic Development Authority, called the deal "God's handiwork."
Tollison noted the agreement took just several months to complete, whereas such deals typically take one and a half to two years.
Tollison stated the event marked a new chapter of progress that made the plant's completion feel "more real than ever."
Tollison remarked, "It’s a ceremonial symbolic day that captures all of our hard work."
Tollison added, "We have a ton of work to do but I think local officials are happy with the progress."
In anticipation of the plant, neighboring Effingham County halted future developments to prepare for growth.
Hyundai has not revealed which specific vehicle models will be produced at the Savannah plant.
The automaker currently produces two EV models and has announced plans for eight additional models before the end of the decade.
Savannah-area economic development officials stated Hyundai is expected to pay wages "very competitive with others in the advanced manufacturing market."
The local average annual salary in the advanced manufacturing industry is $58,105, excluding benefits.
Gov. Brian Kemp emphasized Georgia's strong workforce and the state's commitment to bringing quality jobs.
Kemp stated, "The auto industry will see more change in the next ten years than in the last 100. Thanks to Hyundai, that future is going to be made right here."
Savannah Mayor Van Johnson expressed concern about where the plant's workforce will come from and wants to partner with local colleges to ensure positions are filled.
Johnson said, "I want to make sure we have the workforce to take advantage of these opportunities."
Johnson noted, "8,100 jobs could from inside or outside our communities. I’m asking what skills are needed now for these jobs because we can’t wait until 2025 to get started on that."
Jose Munoz, president and CEO of Hyundai North America, pledged Bryan County residents' full commitment to providing resources.
Munoz added, "Here we are five months later, ready to break ground and start construction. This is going to be a massive operation. We are excted to be your neighbor and partner."
Tae-yong Cho, ambassador of the Republic of Korea to the U.S., stated that both countries are a "perfect duo to do business together."
Cho said, "It’s great to be in Georgia."

Cho added, "This is a great occasion for the U.S. and Korea. Today the Korean auto industry embarks on a new journey of climate consciousness. Georgia is a state that embodies the spirit of economics."
source_title: 'This project is monumental': Hyundai breaks ground on EV battery plant in Bryan County

facts: In 1991, Hyundai introduced its first electric vehicle to the world—a Sonata prototype that showed the promise of zero-emissions mobility.
The 1991 Sonata prototype was never mass produced.
Over the next three decades, Hyundai has been bullish on its electrification roadmap.
According to Hyundai Motors—consisting of Hyundai and Kia—its revenue has doubled since 2019.
Hyundai now represents more than 1 in 10 new vehicles sold in the U.S.
A significant portion of the company’s gains has come from EVs, which accounted for nearly 8% of U.S. sales in 2023.
Hybrids accounted for 10% of Hyundai's U.S. sales.
In the first quarter of 2024, Hyundai Motors became America’s second-largest seller of EVs.
José Muñoz is president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America.
At an investor event in August, Hyundai revealed it was doubling the number of hybrid variants it offers from seven to 14.
Hyundai is developing extended range electric vehicle (EREV) technology to give customers an added bridge from internal combustion engines to pure EVs.
EREV technology combines the advantages of internal combustion engines and EVs to provide an all-electric driving range of over 500 miles, with the engine being used only for battery charging.
Hyundai will begin producing the new EREVs at the soon-to-open $7.6 billion Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America facility in Bryan County, Georgia.
The Bryan County, Georgia facility had been originally slated to exclusively manufacture battery EVs.
Production of the new EREVs is scheduled by the end of 2026, with sales starting in 2027.
Hyundai plans to introduce 21 new EVs by 2030, from affordable to luxury to high-performance Hyundai and Genesis models.
Globally, Hyundai is investing $28 billion over the next decade.
Hyundai is targeting 2 million EV sales by 2030.
Hyundai’s electrification strategy really started to take shape in 2016 with the introduction of the award-winning IONIQ brand.
The company launched IONIQ with a battery electric model, a hybrid model and a plug-in hybrid model.
Hyundai continues to sell hybrids across its lineup, including the popular Santa Fe, Tucson, Kona, Elantra and Sonata models.
Hyundai has spent the past few years perfecting Hyundai Home Marketplace, which offers complimentary home EV chargers for owners, among other electrification needs.

Hyundai has taken a leadership role in building out the EV infrastructure across America by helping to found IONNA, a joint venture among several of the world’s largest automakers for the purpose of expanding a universal charging network.
By 2030, the goal for IONNA is to have more than 30,000 chargers in urban areas, along highway corridors and on vacation routes.
Hyundai is committed to achieving company-wide carbon neutrality by 2045.
source_title: Paid Program: Inside Hyundai’s EV Strategy to Electrify America

facts: Hyundai Motor Group confirmed it is going forward with previously announced plans to expand its Georgia plant.
The expansion confirmation came just weeks after an immigration raid delayed the startup of an electric vehicle battery plant at the site.
Hyundai will spend $2.7 billion to increase production capacity at the Ellabell site by 200,000 over the next three years.
The expansion will bring total production capacity at the site to 500,000 vehicles a year.
The company first announced the expansion in March at the grand opening of the plant west of Savannah.
Hyundai had said in August that it would invest an additional $5 billion in the United States overall.
An immigration raid occurred at the site, which included arrests of more than 300 South Korean citizens.
The raid led to questions about the wisdom of South Korea investing in the U.S.
Hyundai plans to produce 10 models of electric and hybrid gas-electric vehicles in Georgia.
This is up from the current two vehicle models the plant has been assembling as it ramps up production.
Hyundai says it’s still on track to expand production worldwide to 5.6 million vehicles a year by 2030.
The automaker pledged that 60% of those vehicles will be electric or hybrid-powered.
The targeted sales markets include South Korea, North America, and Europe.
Hyundai plans to make more than 80% of vehicles sold in the United States domestically by 2030.
Total domestic content will increase from 60% to 80%.
For the first time, the Georgia plant's vehicle lineup will include a mid-sized pickup truck.
Hyundai already makes the Santa Cruz model, a four-door compact pickup, that it started selling in 2021.
Hyundai CEO José Muñoz has said the immigration raid will delay opening the battery plant by at least two to three months.
Spokesperson Michael Stewart said Thursday that the facility will open in the first half of 2026.
Hyundai executives and Georgia officials have been trying to calm the situation since the raid.
The raid mushroomed into a diplomatic dispute between South Korea and the United States.

Republican Gov. Brian Kemp told reporters Tuesday at a ceremony marking the groundbreaking of a Rivian Automotive electric vehicle plant that he remains confident Georgia’s business advantages would “win out” in terms of foreign investment.
Gov. Kemp said the raid may even wind up smoothing the way for South Korean employees to more easily obtain legal permission to help build and operate facilities in the United States.
Kemp said he has had good conversations with companies that are here doing business in Georgia, and companies that are looking to do business here.
Kemp said he has had good conversations with people in the White House about the visa issue.
Brent Stubbs, the chief administrative officer of the Ellabell site, wrote in an opinion piece published Wednesday in the Atlanta Journal-Constitution that the company is still committed to Georgia.
Stubbs wrote that the situation doesn’t change their plans to continue expanding and localizing in the United States.
Stubbs wrote that their investments in America are part of a long-term strategic plan.
The $2.7-billion investment confirmed Thursday will go to expanding capacity at the plant and group affiliates.
That would bring total investment in the auto plant to $10.3 billion.
This total does not count the $4.3 billion that Hyundai and its joint-venture partner, LG Energy Solution, are putting into the battery plant.
Hyundai and its onsite affiliates currently have 3,129 employees in Ellabell.
Hyundai pledges to hire at least 8,500 workers by the end of 2031.
State and local governments have promised $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives.
The boost at the Ellabell site is the biggest part of Hyundai’s plan to raise production by 1.2 million vehicles a year worldwide.
This includes an additional 250,000 vehicles out of Pune, India.
It also includes 200,000 more at Hyundai’s electric vehicle plant in Ulsan, South Korea.
The automaker said it would also deliver parts for assembly into an additional 250,000 vehicles at plants in Saudi Arabia, Vietnam, and North Africa.
Hyundai underlined a previous announcement to deepen its investment in robotics.
By 2027, Hyundai will launch extended-range electric vehicles with gasoline motors to extend the range of its electric batteries to more than 600 miles.
source_title: Hyundai confirms $2.7-billion expansion of Georgia EV plant despite ICE raid - Los Angeles Times

entity_name: Hyundai-LG EV Battery Joint Venture Construction Site
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution battery joint venture
facts: South Korea reached an agreement with the U.S. to repatriate hundreds of South Korean nationals arrested during a U.S. immigration raid at a Hyundai–LG electric vehicle battery construction site in Georgia on Thursday.
The detained workers were connected to one of the largest Korean investments in the U.S.—a Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution battery joint venture.
U.S. officials and state leaders promoted the joint venture as a major job-creation project.
The arrests paused construction at the site.
The arrests raised questions about how multinational investments will be staffed amid tighter visa rules and heightened immigration enforcement.
U.S. immigration agents arrested 475 people at the Ellabell, Georgia, construction site on Thursday.
At least 300 of those detained were South Korean nationals, according to South Korea's foreign ministry.
U.S. officials described the operation as the largest single-site enforcement action in Department of Homeland Security history.
U.S. officials said those detained were in the U.S. illegally or working without authorization.
Seoul pledged to review visa procedures for business trips tied to large investment projects.
Kang Hoon-sik, chief of staff to South Korean President Lee Jae Myung, said at a meeting of senior officials: "We will not let our guard down until we have our people safely back home."
The immigration operation followed a months-long investigation into alleged illegal hiring practices at the Hyundai site.
According to court records cited by The Associated Press, U.S. prosecutors said they have not yet determined which company or contractor hired "hundreds of illegal aliens."

Some detainees had entered the country unlawfully, while others arrived on temporary visas or through a waiver program that does not allow employment, according to Steven Schrank, lead Georgia agent of Homeland Security Investigations.
Attorney Charles Kuck, who represents two detained workers, told AP his clients arrived under the visa waiver program and were in the country legally for tourism or business.
One detained worker had been in the U.S. for only two weeks and another for 45 days, both planning to return home soon, according to attorney Charles Kuck.
Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) spokesperson Lindsay Williams told AP that some individuals were detained due to prior criminal records.
ICE spokesperson Lindsay Williams stated: "Once citizens have identified themselves, we have no authority" to detain them.
Hyundai stated Friday that it believed none of its direct employees were among those detained.
Hyundai said it was reviewing its practices to ensure legal compliance by contractors and subcontractors.

Steven Shrank, special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations for Georgia and Alabama, said: "This operation underscores our commitment to protecting jobs for Georgians and Americans…We are sending a clear and unequivocal message that those who exploit our workforce, undermine our economy and violate federal laws will be held accountable."

Hyundai stated in a statement shared with Newsweek: "We are closely monitoring the situation and working to understand the specific circumstances…it is our understanding that none of those detained is directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company."

The nonprofit legal advocacy organization Asian Americans Advancing Justice-Atlanta stated: "Our communities know the workers targeted at Hyundai are everyday people who are trying to feed their families, build stronger communities, and work toward a better future."
U.S. President Donald Trump said Friday: "They were illegal aliens and ICE was just doing its job."
South Korea promised to review business-visa procedures for investment-related trips to aim to limit a recurrence of similar incidents.
source_title: South Korea Issues Update on Georgia Hyundai Plant ICE Raid - Newsweek

facts: Governor Brian Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) announced sustained momentum for business recruitment and expansions in Fiscal Year 2024 (July 1, 2023 – June 30, 2024).
GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 429 facility expansions and new locations resulting in more than $20.3 billion in investment and the commitment of 26,900 new private sector jobs statewide.
83% of expansions and new locations are on their way to communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region, representing more than 18,300 new jobs and $12.5 billion in investment.
Governor Brian Kemp emphasized prioritizing growth in rural Georgia and investing in key areas like education, workforce development, and infrastructure despite national economic headwinds.
Metro Atlanta attracted nearly $7.8 billion in investments as a logistics and business hub.
Statewide, new locations account for $12.8 billion in investments and expansions are responsible for 9,800 new jobs.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones highlighted the need to ensure businesses have the necessary workforce, crediting the Technical College System of Georgia, University System of Georgia, College and Career Academies, and private-public partnerships for building the workforce pipeline through training programs.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns noted historic investments in education, workforce development, and infrastructure alongside tax cuts and regulatory relief.
Governor and First Lady Kemp led economic development missions to Korea and Switzerland during FY2024, focusing on strengthening relationships with Hyundai Motor Group and its supplier network.
In the prior year, Governor and First Lady Kemp led similar missions to Israel, France, and Switzerland.

Georgia maintained its position as a global gateway for business, landing more than 8,100 new jobs and $5.9 billion in investments from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S. market during the past fiscal year.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted the state's strong partnerships, diverse industry landscape, and range of locations as major advantages for businesses.
The State of Georgia celebrated more than 50 years of continuous representation in Europe and Japan, which remain among the top sources of foreign direct investment.
The coming fiscal year will mark 40 years of continuous representation in Korea, a partnership that earned GDEcD the James A. Van Fleet Award from the Korea Society.
All markets where Georgia maintains representation accounted for 29% of total new jobs and investments in FY2024.

GDEcD Commissioner Kristi Brigman highlighted the state's transition from agribusiness and manufacturing to mobility and technology, noting a decade of excellence and project milestones across the state.
Manufacturing, including aerospace, automotive, and e-mobility sectors, accounted for over 50% of expansions or new locations in FY2024.
Kia Georgia added the EV9 to its lineup in Troup County.
Toyota Industries Corporation established a campus in Jackson County.
StandardAero operates in Richmond County.
International life sciences company Gerresheimer announced a recent expansion in Fayette County.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America suppliers announced new locations in Bryan, Candler, Chatham, Coweta, Effingham, Harris, and Laurens County.
SOLARCYCLE, an innovator in solar panel recycling, announced plans for a new location in Polk County to support solar panel manufacturer Qcells’ operations.
Investments in food processing and agribusiness totaled more than $1.3 billion in FY2024.
Japanese probiotic beverage company Yakult located in Bartow County.
Korean bakery CJ Foodville located in Hall County.
Walmart will source milk from local dairy farmers at a future dairy processing facility in Lowndes County.
Technology and corporate hubs are creating at least 4,400 new jobs as a result of wins in FY2024.
Cargill Corporation is establishing a new office hub in the City of Atlanta.
U.S. Soccer’s future headquarters and national training center will be located in Fayette County.
Georgia earned the rank of No. 1 state for business from Area Development magazine for 10 consecutive years.
Georgia earned the No. 1 spot in Site Selection magazine’s annual Prosperity Cup rankings for successful investment recruitment in 2023.
Georgia was awarded a Gold Shovel by Area Development for achieving significant success in attracting new business investment and job creation in 2023.
Business Facilities magazine ranked Georgia as the No. 1 state for EV investments.
Business Facilities magazine ranked Georgia as a top 10 state for food processing, cybersecurity, aerospace and defense, and AI growth hubs.

links.context: View further details of state-announced economic development projects

source_title: Georgia Job Creation & Momentum Remains Strong - Tifton CEO

facts: Hyundai is injecting $2.7 billion to kick-start the second phase of its Metaplant expansion in Georgia.
The $2.7 billion investment will create another 3,000 local jobs to support made-in-America electric vehicles and hybrids.
Hyundai announced the investment at its annual CEO Investor Day event in New York on September 18, 2025.
The cash infusion will add an estimated production capacity of 200,000 units.
Total annual output from Georgia will reach half-million vehicles by 2028.
The facility will produce between 10 hybrid and full battery-electric models.
Hyundai's labor pledge represents a 11.5% increase in jobs.
The investment represents an additional 10% of the $27.3 billion pledged in Georgia's local economy since 2018 from EV manufacturers and their downstream suppliers.
Hyundai aims to manufacture 80% of the cars it sells in the State domestically.
Investments in local supply chains will increase locally-sourced parts from 60% to 80%.
The Metaplant serves as Hyundai's next-gen manufacturing hub in the U.S.
The company describes the Metaplant as its new "software-defined factory" featuring automation systems and robotics tech derived from investments in Boston Dynamics.
Hyundai plans to crank out an additional 1.2 million cars by the top of the decade, bringing its total electrified output to 3.3 million units per year.
The Metaplant will build 15% of those cars.
Georgia has become the unofficial EV capital of the Southeast.
Hyundai's cash promise is seen as a vote of confidence in the community and a corporate promise to build where it sells on U.S. soil.
Continued investment between the U.S. and South Korea may help patch up relations following a massive immigration raid.
Between Hyundai, Rivian, SK, and numerous other e-mobility companies operating out of the region, the EV industry makes up 26,000 jobs in the region.
source_title: Why Hyundai Is Pouring Another $2.7 Billion Into Georgia

facts: Hyundai Motor Group entered an agreement with the State of Georgia to build its first dedicated full electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S.
The new facilities represent an investment of approximately USD 5.54 billion.
Commercial production is expected to begin in the first half of 2025.
The facility will have an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Hyundai Motor Group plans to produce a wide range of innovative full electric vehicle models for U.S. customers at the new Georgia plant.
Details regarding specific production models will be shared at later dates.
The local EV production aims to increase U.S. consumer accessibility to the Group’s innovative EVs.
The battery manufacturing facility will be established through a strategic partnership, with details to be disclosed at a later stage.
Through the battery facility, the Group aims to establish a stable supply chain and build a healthy EV ecosystem in the U.S.
The EV and battery manufacturing plant will be located on a dedicated 2,923-acre site in Bryan County, Georgia.
The site has immediate access to I-95 and I-16 highways, providing easy access to 250 major metro areas.
The site is located less than 50 kilometers from the Port of Savannah.
The Port of Savannah is the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S., featuring two Class I rail facilities on-site provided by Georgia Central Railway.
West Point, Georgia is already home to the Group’s Kia manufacturing hub in the U.S.
Hyundai Motor Group selected Georgia due to favorable business conditions including speed-to-market, a talented workforce, and an existing network of affiliates and suppliers.
Georgia is the Southeastern leader for EV registration per 1,000 registered automobiles.
Georgia constantly develops access to publicly available EV charging stations and already offers more outlets per capita than anywhere else in the Southeast.
The Group’s new investment will help accelerate the state’s sustainable shift to electrification.
The project is expected to create about 8,100 new jobs.
The EV plant investment is part of the Group’s 2021 announcement to invest USD 7.4 billion by 2025 to foster future mobility in the U.S., including EV production and smart mobility solutions.
Hyundai Motor Group aims to become a leader in the U.S. auto market’s EV transition with the new facility.
The Group has a global target to sell 3.23 million full electric vehicles annually by 2030.
To realize this goal, the Group plans to establish a global EV production network to ensure a stable supply of EVs around the globe.
With additional U.S. EV and battery production capabilities, the Group aims to become one of the top three EV providers in the U.S. by 2026.
The Group plans to lead the EV market in sales, design, technologies, and mobility solutions optimized for the electric mobility era.
The investment aligns with the U.S. government’s roadmap to accelerate electrification as global OEMs expand local EV production in the U.S.
Last year, the U.S. government announced an initiative requiring zero-emission vehicle sales to account for at least half of OEM new vehicle sales by 2030.
The new plant will feature a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem.
The Group plans to implement advanced intelligent manufacturing technologies currently under test at the Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center in Singapore (HMGICS).
The Georgia facility will operate as an intelligent manufacturing plant where order collection, procurement, logistics, and production processes are optimized using AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system will create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
As part of its sustainability commitment, the plant will mainly rely on renewable energy sources and use emission-reduction technologies to meet RE100 requirements.
Official signing ceremony was held in Bryan County, attended by Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp and other Georgia officials.
Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO Jaehoon Chang and Hyundai Motor President and Chief Operating Officer José Muñoz attended the signing ceremony.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung joined the signing ceremony virtually.
Governor Brian Kemp stated the project is the largest in the state’s history, delivering high-quality jobs on the leading edge of mobility to Georgians.
Governor Brian Kemp noted it is incredible that within a year of Georgia’s investment in the Bryan County Megasite, they are locating a world-class project with a company with great Georgia history.

Euisun Chung stated the Group is proud to open its first dedicated full EV and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S. and is excited to partner with Georgia for electrified mobility and sustainability.

Jaehoon Chang stated they decided to build their first dedicated EV plant in the U.S. because America embraces change and drives innovation, and it will help meet growing demands for leading edge design, safe, zero-emissions vehicles.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group to Establish First Dedicated EV Plant and Battery Manufacturing Facility in the U.S. - HMGMA

facts: U.S. regulators concluded a monthslong review of their environmental permit for Hyundai's $7.6 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia, finding that no changes are needed.
The Army Corps of Engineers agreed to revisit the permit last August after a conservation group threatened to sue.

The Ogeechee Riverkeeper group said the agency failed to consider potential impacts to the region's main drinking water supply from the factory using up to 6.6 million gallons (25 million liters) daily.

No revisions to the site's federal permit are needed, according to a March 25 memo from the Army Corps to the Georgia Department of Economic Development and local economic developers that applied for the Hyundai project's 2022 permit.
The Army Corps' memo stated that "the impacts on the environment as a result of this permit have been evaluated and found to be insignificant."
The Army Corps determined the EV plant would have "a long-term minor effect" on the area's drinking water supply.
This conclusion differs from the wording federal regulators used when assessing the original permit in 2022, when they concluded water supply impacts would be "negligible."
Hyundai began producing EVs just six months ago at the sprawling manufacturing plant in Bryan County west of Savannah.
The plant currently has 1,200 employees working at the site.
Hyundai executives announced plans last month to expand its production capacity by two-thirds to a total of 500,000 vehicles per year.
The Army Corps permit authorized the filling or dredging of 221 acres (89 hectares) of wetlands at the plant site.
Hyundai announced plans to build its EV factory in May 2022.
The initial permit application made no mention of how much water the 2,900-acre (1,170-hectare) factory would use.
The Ogeechee Riverkeeper group expressed concern that the plant's consumption could divert water from wells used by area farmers, as well as streams and wetlands.

Ben Kirsch, legal director for the Ogeechee Riverkeeper, said the group has requested additional records from the Army Corps to better understand its conclusions and the wording changes regarding water impacts.
Ben Kirsch stated, "We do not yet know what, if any, ramifications will come from that."
Ben Kirsch added, "Until we review that decision document, we cannot offer substantive comments, but we continue to monitor actions around this permit closely."
Bianca Johnson, a spokesperson for Hyundai's Georgia plant, said the automaker had no comment.
State and local officials who recruited Hyundai to Georgia praised the Army Corps for the "thoroughness" of its permit review.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development and the Savannah area's Joint Development Authority stated they "remain dedicated to supporting industry and agriculture while protecting and preserving the environment."
source_title: U.S. Regulators Finish Review of Hyundai Plant's Georgia Water Permit; No Changes Needed | Industrial Equipment News

facts: Federal officials detained 475 people at a construction site on the Hyundai Metaplant campus near Savannah during a Thursday operation.
The operation was described as the largest single immigration raid in the history of Homeland Security Investigations (HSI).
Steven Schrank, special agent in charge of HSI Atlanta, stated the arrests stemmed from a monthslong investigation into alleged undocumented and illegal work at a battery plant under construction.
The battery factory is part of Hyundai Motor Group’s 3,000-acre Metaplant site, a $12.6 billion project.
Georgia leaders have touted the project as the largest economic development deal in state history.
HSI was founded in 2010 as a division within Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).
source_title: HSI says immigration raid at Georgia site was largest in agency history

facts: Auto manufacturer Hyundai and autonomous vehicle technology developer Motional have partnered to launch a new robotaxi.
The all-electric Ioniq 5-based robotaxi is Motional’s first commercial vehicle.
The robotaxi will be used in its fully driverless public ride-hail service starting in 2023.
The vehicle represents the product of “unparalleled expertise and partnership” between Motional and Hyundai Motor Group.
Motional and Hyundai say the new vehicle is a “next-generation robotaxi”.
Based on the all-electric Hyundai Ioniq 5, which launched to worldwide acclaim earlier this year, the Ioniq 5 robotaxi is an SAE Level 4 autonomous vehicle that can safely operate without a driver.
It’s a zero tailpipe-emissions robotaxi and represents the convergence of the two most transformative technologies in mobility: electrification and autonomy.
The fusion of these technologies can make transportation smarter, safer, and more sustainable.
The Ioniq 5 robotaxi is also Motional’s first commercial vehicle and signals a pivotal milestone in the company’s roadmap.
Motional will begin transporting public passengers in the Ioniq 5 robotaxi in 2023 through a landmark partnership with Lyft.
The Ioniq 5 robotaxi features a technology-driven design that celebrates the innovation behind the autonomous operation.
The vehicle’s sensor suite is prominently displayed across the exterior, easily distinguishing the robotaxi from human piloted vehicles.

The robotaxi has more than 30 sensors – a combination of cameras, radars, and lidar – that provide robust 360-degree perception, high-resolution images, and ultra-long range detection of objects for safe autonomous operation in diverse driving environments.

The robotaxi will be outfitted with Motional’s proven driverless technology, which includes advanced machine learning systems – trained on decades of real world data – that enables the vehicle to safely navigate challenging and complex driving situations.

Based on the Electric Global Modular Platform (E-GMP), Hyundai Motor Group’s dedicated battery electric vehicle platform, the Ioniq 5 robotaxi’s interior provides passengers with a spacious, comfortable place to work, relax, or socialize during their driverless ride.

The interior will also feature a suite of rider-focused interfaces to allow passengers to intuitively interact with the vehicle during their ride, such as directing the robotaxi to make an extra stop.
The resulting passenger experience will set a new standard for driverless ride-hailing.
source_title: Hyundai and Motional unveil new robotaxi | InTradeFairs

facts: Hyundai started production of electric small utility vehicles (SUVs) at its $7.6 billion plant in Georgia.
The automaker broke ground at the site in late October 2022, less than two years before production began.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) announced that the first EV for commercial sale rolled off the assembly line.
Spokesperson Bianca Johnson provided details to the Associated Press regarding the launch.
HMGMA validated its production processes to ensure vehicles meet Hyundai Motor Group's high quality standards before starting initial customer vehicle production ahead of schedule.
A grand opening celebration at the Georgia plant is expected in the first quarter of 2025.
The sprawling Georgia facility, located west of Savannah, received $2.1 billion in tax rebates and incentives from state and local governments.
This project is described as the biggest economic development Georgia has ever seen.
Once fully functional, Hyundai and battery partner LG Energy Solution plan to employ 8,500 total workers at the Bryan County site.
The Bryan County site is located about 50 miles west of Savannah.
Hyundai plans to produce up to 300,000 EVs per year in Georgia.
The plant will also produce the batteries that power these vehicles.
Following the recent launch of the IONIQ 5, the Georgia facility is facing safety issues involving 20 injuries and 12 traumatic occurrences.
The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) has issued fines and conducted investigations regarding the facility.
OSHA's actions highlight a conflict between worker safety and economic growth at the site.
The Ioniq 5 is the first electric SUV produced at the facility.
2025 variants of the Ioniq 5 are expected to hit U.S. showrooms by the end of 2024.
In the first half of 2024, the Ioniq 5 was the second-best-selling electric car in America, excluding Tesla.
The plant's vehicle production areas are currently completed and employ more than 1,000 workers.
The battery-making facilities at the plant remain under construction.
To be eligible for 2024 tax advantages, the U.S. government mandates that vehicles be fully constructed in North America.
The federal mandate requires at least 60% of battery components to be supplied from the North American region.
The goal of this directive is to decrease dependency on foreign suppliers and increase home production.
Hyundai benefited from the Inflation Reduction Act in the United States.
Despite early reservations, President Jose Munoz stated that Hyundai accelerated the construction of its Georgia factory due to the law's $7,500 EV tax credit.
source_title: Hyundai's $7.6B Georgia Plant Starts Electric SUV Production Amid Safety Concerns

facts: Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp joined President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America José Muñoz to celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s historic impact on Georgia.
An update was provided on the progress of Hyundai Motor Group's Metaplant America facility.
The facility will officially start vehicle production in the fourth quarter of this year.
Governor Brian Kemp stated Hyundai has been an incredible partner to the state for decades and highlighted the historic benefits a project like this brings to Georgians.
Hyundai helps recruit businesses, gives back to communities, and works with world-class colleges and universities to connect Georgians with opportunities.
Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles on display included the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, and Electrified GV70.
The hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck will be used for logistics needs between Hyundai’s manufacturing facilities and suppliers.
Governor Kemp officially declared February 26 as Hyundai Day throughout the state.
The General Assembly passed a Resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group’s dedication to Georgia and recognizing February 26 as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
José Muñoz stated that Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great paying American jobs.
Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America project was announced in May 2022.
LG Energy Solution was named as the joint venture partner in the manufacture of battery components.
An additional expansion of the battery facility was announced.
Hyundai and SK On are building an EV battery manufacturing facility in Bartow County to supply Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. plants.
Hyundai Motor Group’s planned investment in Georgia has reached more than $12.6 billion.
The planned investment brings the creation of 12,000 direct, long-term jobs.

Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones and House Speaker Jon Burns joined legislative leaders, GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, local elected officials from the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, and company representatives at the celebration.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones applauded Governor Kemp, state agencies, and the University System of Georgia for their partnership with Hyundai.
The commitment from all stakeholders has enabled Hyundai's positive impact and the creation of thousands of jobs.
These initiatives support Georgia's position as the number one state in which to do business.
Up to 300,000 Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia EVs are expected to be produced annually at the Metaplant.
17 HMGMA suppliers have announced projects across 10 Georgia counties spanning beyond southeast Georgia.
Further supplier announcements are expected.
Supplier facilities are expected to create more than 6,700 new jobs and more than $2.5 billion in investment.
Supplier facilities will be operational prior to the opening of HMGMA.
Speaker Jon Burns praised Governor Kemp and the General Assembly for policy direction supporting Hyundai's Southeast Georgia investment.
The Metaplant will have a generational impact as a force multiplier for additional investments in education, healthcare, housing, and transportation.
The nearly 3,000-acre Metaplant site has four building exteriors complete with outfitting for internal operations underway.
The Metaplant’s architecture incorporates technologically advanced elements, design features representing the Savannah area, Hyundai’s brand identity, nature, and clean energy.
"Meta Pros" are HMGMA’s full-time employees being hired rapidly for training and skills on machinery, office and clerical work, and numerous other employment roles.
HMGMA currently has 566 full-time employees.
Approximately 75 percent of the workforce is from the Greater Savannah area.
An additional 37 employees are from Georgia but outside the local area.
Georgia Quick Start broke ground on its customized training center at the Metaplant site on February 8.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgians will be trained in high-skill careers as Meta Pros at one of the most advanced vehicle assembly and battery plants in the world.
HMGMA partnered with local technical colleges to prepare students for entry-level employment at the Metaplant.

Electrical Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate of Credit (EVP TCC) training is available at Coastal Pines Technical College’s Baxley and Waycross campuses, Southeastern Technical College’s Vidalia campus, Savannah Technical College, and Ogeechee Technical College.
TCSG Commissioner Greg Dozier stated the collaboration demonstrates a shared commitment to innovation, excellence, and the future of the automotive industry.
The Technical College System of Georgia is providing comprehensive training programs tailored to the growing electric vehicle industry.
In September 2023, Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Company signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on research and applications for sustainable mobility.
Collaboration areas include developing a hydrogen economy, workforce development, and making cities smarter and more sustainable.
U.S. News & World Report ranked Georgia Tech as the top public university and No. 3 nationally in energy and fuel research.
Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera stated the partnership represents one of their most significant transformative corporate relationships.
Through the agreement, they are working together to advance science for key sectors and emerging technologies while producing the next-generation workforce.
Since 1998, Hyundai has directed over $6 million towards local Georgia communities through philanthropic programs and partnerships.
The company is promoting STEM programs in local K-12 schools and hosting educational programs on alternative energy sources.
Hyundai supports local organizations dedicated to affordable homeownership and focuses on environmental stewardship.
Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority’s Trip Tollison stated Hyundai has given more than $1 million to regional nonprofits.
Hyundai has committed to $7.59 billion in capital investment in Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The project will create 8,500 jobs.
More than 550 Meta Pros have already been hired, with 75 percent coming from the Savannah region.
Beginning with mass production, the electricity used to power the Metaplant will come from 100 percent clean power sources.
This aligns with Hyundai’s commitment to carbon neutrality by 2045.
A 41-acre park will be featured in front of the plant, including trails, running tracks, environmental art displays, sports fields, and other amenities for employee use.
