facts: Georgia Governor Brian Kemp will visit South Korea soon and has requested a meeting with Hyundai Motor Company officials.
The governor's office sent an email in Korean and English on September 8 emphasizing that Hyundai Motor Company is an important investor and partner in Georgia.

The email was sent four days after U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) conducted a raid on September 4 at the Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution battery joint venture construction site in Ellabell, Georgia.
The ICE raid arrested a total of 475 people, including 317 Koreans.
The detained employees returned to Korea on a chartered flight on September 12.
Interviews with the repatriated employees have been released, leading to deteriorating public sentiment in Korea.
President Donald Trump initially stated that ICE's actions were 'legally conducted.'
President Trump asserted the need for skilled foreign professionals via Truth Social on September 14.
Governor Kemp's office clarified that the visit to South Korea had been under discussion before the September 4 ICE arrests.
The stated purpose of the visit is to strengthen ties with Georgia's economic, educational, and cultural partners.
Trip Tollison, President and CEO of the Savannah Economic Development Authority, is part of the delegation to Korea.
Tollison stated that they have been working to strengthen the relationship between Georgia and Hyundai and that this visit will be no different.
The specific schedule for the visit has not been disclosed but is expected around October.
An international conference involving governors from other southern U.S. states is scheduled in Japan on October 28-29, and Governor Kemp is highly likely to attend.
On September 16, Governor Kemp stated that the incident is not just Hyundai's problem and that the U.S. visa system needs a thorough review.
This marked Governor Kemp's first public comment since the ICE raid.
Kemp added that Georgia is currently discussing the visa issue with the White House.
Kemp emphasized that the incident will not discourage foreign companies from investing in Georgia.
In an interview with the Savannah Morning News on September 17, Kemp said, 'We rely on Koreans, and we want them to return in time for the plant schedule.'
Philip Reiner, spokesperson for the Economic Development Authority, stated that detained LG employees were in the U.S. temporarily to install equipment, provide support, and train staff.
Reiner stressed that the detained LG employees are skilled technicians with professional expertise.
Hyundai Motor Group announced its intention to continue investing in the U.S. despite recent incidents.
On September 18, Hyundai Motor Company announced at an event in New York that it plans to invest 77.3 trillion won over the next five years.
The planned investment includes expanding production capacity at plants in Georgia and Alabama and increasing the number of hybrid models in the U.S. market.
Hyundai Motor Group expects construction of its electric vehicle battery plant in Georgia to be delayed by at least two to three months due to the ICE crackdown.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) plans to hold a public recruitment event at Savannah Technical College campus in Georgia on September 30.
This recruitment event is HMGMA's first public recruitment since the September 4 ICE raid.
A local American employee highlighted Hyundai Motor Company's contribution to local employment and the economy in an opinion piece published on September 18.
The employee explained that foreign professionals help with the initial production process through equipment installation and staff training.
Hyundai Motor Group Chairman Chung Euisun took a commemorative photo with attendees at the completion ceremony of HMGMA on March 26 in Ellabel, Georgia.
Attendees at the March 26 ceremony included former Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue, current Georgia Governor Brian Kemp, and Jose Munoz, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company.
source_title: Georgia Governor to Visit Korea, Requests Meeting with Hyundai Amid Detention Incident - The Asia Business Daily

facts: Georgia and local governments are on track to give $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai Motor Group.
Hyundai Motor Group and a partner announced last month that it will invest an additional $2 billion at an electric vehicle complex it's building in Georgia.
Projected incentives will rise by more than $290 million from the $1.8 billion deal originally announced last year.
Only $2.75 million of the incentive increase represents additional cash from the state.
The rest of the incentive increase will come from increases in tax breaks.
The deal calls for Hyundai and battery maker LG Energy Solution to invest $7.6 billion in the Georgia plant.
The deal calls for hiring 8,500 workers by the end of 2031.
This is up from the original job projection of 8,100 at the sprawling electric vehicle and battery complex being built in Ellabell, west of Savannah.
It’s the largest economic development deal in Georgia history.
It comes with the largest incentive package.
State leaders say benefits to Georgia outweigh the incentives.
Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson said Hyundai is projected to have a direct payroll of $4.7 billion over the next 10 years.
The company has promised to pay workers a yearly average of $58,105, plus benefits.
Pat Wilson stated that Hyundai Motor Group will give back to the region by investing in schools, families, and communities as they deliver a state-of-the-art facility in Bryan County.
Local officials released property tax projections to the AP on Tuesday.
The Hyundai package has been described as the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised a single automotive plant.
That description comes from Good Jobs First, a group skeptical of subsidies to private companies.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the assembly plant through 2048.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the battery plant through 2049.
During that time, Hyundai is projected to pay $523 million in taxes while saving $669 million.
The state projects it will waive an additional $81.8 million in sales taxes on construction materials and machinery.
This brings Hyundai's savings from those exemptions to more than $478 million.
The company is projected to receive an additional $10.5 million in state income tax credits.
The income tax credits are calculated at $5,250 per job over five years because of the increase in jobs.
This brings Hyundai's state income tax savings to $223 million.
If Hyundai didn't owe that much state corporate income tax, Georgia would instead give the company personal income taxes collected from Hyundai workers.
The state will spend an additional $2.75 million to help fund construction, machinery and equipment.
This boosts that total to nearly $53 million.
State and local governments will spend more than $112 million to buy and prepare 2,913 acres (1,179 hectares) for the plant.
The state will spend $175 million on water and sewer facilities.
The state will spend $210 million on road construction and improvements.
The state will spend more than $153 million to recruit and train workers.
The deal requires Hyundai to pay back a portion of the incentives if the company falls below 80% of promised investment or employment.
Hyundai plans to start EV production in 2025.
Initially, the plant will make 300,000 vehicles per year.
Production may possibly expand to 500,000 a year.
Since the company announced its first U.S. plant solely dedicated to electric vehicles, suppliers have pledged to invest $2.2 billion and to hire 5,300 people.
The announcements are part of an electric vehicle and battery land rush across the United States.
Under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be assembled in North America to qualify for a full $7,500 EV tax credit.
A certain percentage of battery parts and minerals must come from North America or a U.S. free trade partner to qualify for the full $7,500 EV tax credit.
source_title: Higher investment means Hyundai could get $2.1 billion in aid to make electric cars in Georgia | Taiwan News | Sep. 13, 2023 14:08

entity_name: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Multi-Decade Partnership
canonical_name: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Company Partnership
facts: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Company announced a multi-decade partnership as part of Hyundai’s investments in the state of Georgia.
The partnership vision includes research and applications to support sustainable mobility, hydrogen economy, workforce development, and smart cities.
More details about the partnership will be announced in the coming months.
Hyundai is investing $5.54 billion to develop the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The new plant is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The facility will be a dedicated electric vehicle and battery plant.
The plant will create more than 8,100 direct jobs.
Hyundai created a $120,000 STEM scholarship at Georgia Tech when it broke ground on the site in October 2022.
The partnership includes field-naming recognition for Bobby Dodd Stadium, which will now be known as Bobby Dodd Stadium at Hyundai Field.
Sponsorship elements feature enhancements to boost the gameday experience for fans in and around the stadium.

Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera stated that Hyundai is a global brand synonymous with quality, innovation, and advancing technology, and expressed gratitude for Hyundai's transformative investments in the state.

Cabrera noted he is proud the Hyundai brand will feature prominently on campus and looks forward to working with Hyundai leaders to develop exceptional leaders and produce ideas shaping the automotive industry and advancing mobility.

José Muñoz, president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, stated that proximity to institutions like Georgia Tech was one of the reasons Hyundai selected Georgia for its new EV manufacturing facility.
Muñoz added the partnership will include opportunities for student professional development, cooperative work programs, and athletic engagements.
J Batt, director of Athletics at Georgia Tech, stated the partnership will be truly transformative for Georgia Tech Athletics and expressed appreciation to Muñoz and his team.
The partnership provides a vital pathway for Georgia Tech to expand its stature as a leading research entity in automotive electrification through world-class hydrogen research.
The partnership offers an opportunity for Georgia Tech to contribute to the state of Georgia’s commitment as a hub for electric vehicle production.
The deal generates new revenue for Georgia Tech Athletics to provide resources for student-athletes and teams and continue delivering a world-class experience for students, alumni, and fans.
Bobby Dodd Stadium at Hyundai Field is the oldest on-campus stadium in NCAA Division I’s Football Bowl Subdivision and opened in 1913 as Grant Field before being renamed in 1988.
The historic Grant Field name will continue to be memorialized with a display at the stadium.
Legends, which has managed corporate partnerships and multimedia rights for Georgia Tech Athletics since 2021, helped facilitate the athletics partnership with Hyundai.

Georgia Tech Athletics fields 400-plus student-athletes across 17 varsity sports and competes at the highest level of intercollegiate athletics as a member of NCAA Division I and the Atlantic Coast Conference.
Hyundai Motor Company was established in 1967, is present in over 200 countries, and employs more than 120,000 people.
Hyundai is accelerating its transformation into a Smart Mobility Solution Provider and invests in advanced technologies such as robotics and Advanced Air Mobility (AAM).
Hyundai will continue introducing zero emission vehicles equipped with industry-leading hydrogen fuel cell and EV technologies.
Legends was founded in 2008 and operates six divisions worldwide to provide data-and-analytics-fueled service solutions for sports, entertainment, and leisure clients.
source_title: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Announce Multi-Decade Partnership | News Center

entity_name: Hyundai's Georgia EV and Battery Plant
facts: Three workers have died since Hyundai Motor started construction of its $7.6 billion auto plant in Georgia in 2022.
More than a dozen workers have suffered serious injuries, including from falling without wearing harnesses and getting crushed by forklifts.
Hyundai Motor North America conducted a comprehensive audit and strengthened safety oversight across the site.
Hyundai has enhanced contractor vetting, training, and accountability to ensure all partners meet Hyundai’s standards and legal requirements.
Dozens of current and former workers, many of them safety coordinators who helped oversee construction, reported that the work environment involved many inexperienced immigrant laborers.
Workers reported that the work environment often had lax safety standards and frequent accidents.
The plant is operated through a joint venture between Hyundai and South Korea's LG Energy Solution.
An immigration raid last month detained hundreds of South Korean workers in the largest single-site enforcement operation in the U.S. Department of Homeland Security's history.
Workers stated that Hyundai did not ensure that people were properly trained.
Safety regulators did little to prevent worksite violations.
Hyundai CEO Jose Munoz stated: 'We acted immediately and comprehensively to prevent anything like this from happening again.'
Hyundai CEO Jose Munoz traveled to Georgia to tell the team directly: 'Their safety comes before production schedules, before costs, before profits, before everything.'
Construction on the electric vehicle and battery plant is ongoing.
The plant is located in Bryan County about 30 miles west of Savannah.
source_title: Three workers died at Hyundai's Georgia plant since 2022, before US immigration raid, WSJ reports - NewsBreak

entity_name: Toyota Motor Corp. and Hyundai Motor Group
canonical_name: Toyota Motor Corp. and Hyundai Motor Group
facts: Toyota and Hyundai are the world's number one and number three automakers by sales respectively.
Both automakers are facing intensifying competition in electric vehicles from Tesla and Chinese carmakers such as BYD.

The chairmen of Asia's two largest automakers made a rare joint public appearance at an event to showcase their companies' high-performance vehicles, underscoring the trend of rivals joining hands in the face of electrification.
Toyota Motor Corp.'s chairman Akio Toyoda and Hyundai Motor Group's chairman Euisun Chung were at a racing festival in Yong-in, south of Seoul.
Samsung Electronics Co. chairman Jay Y. Lee and Hankook & Company Co.'s chairman Hyunbum Cho attended the event to meet Toyoda and Chung.
The event displayed rally cars from both automakers and several future-oriented models, including hydrogen-powered vehicles.

Toyota chairman Akio Toyoda stated that 'Toyota and Hyundai are joining hands to create a better society and shape the future of mobility,' adding that he and Chung discovered a shared passion for racing.
Sunday's festival marks the first time the two auto giants have teamed up to hold an event, raising the prospect of further collaboration.
Earlier this month, Toyota's research unit and Hyundai's Boston Dynamics Inc. announced a plan to jointly develop humanoid robots with artificial intelligence.
Global car companies are increasingly joining forces in production and research and development to lower costs and boost competitiveness.
Nissan Motor Co. and Honda Motor Co. announced a joint research agreement in August to develop a platform and technologies for next-generation software-defined vehicles.
Hyundai has also partnered with General Motors Co. around joint product development, manufacturing and future clean energy technologies.
Hyundai showcased rally cars from its World Rally Championship program, including the i20 N Rally 1 Hybrid and i20 N Rally 2.
Toyota's Gazoo racing unit presented its GR Yaris Rally 1 Hybrid, GR Yaris Rally 2, GR Supra, and GR86 among others.
source_title: Toyota and Hyundai chairmen team up to host a rare race event. Know more | HT Auto

facts: An automotive parts producer specializing in transmissions will expand its Georgia operations with a $240 million investment that includes adding hundreds of new jobs.
Hyundai TRANSYS will build a 620,000-square-foot facility in West Point, near the company's current location on the Kia campus.
The facility will produce eight-speed transmissions for Hyundai Motor Company.
Hyundai TRANSYS is the only automobile manufacturing company in the world that specializes in transmissions.
New jobs will include advanced manufacturing and management roles, as well as supplementary construction handling and inspection.
The expansion will bring Hyundai TRANSYS' total employment in Georgia to 1,741 jobs.

Sangkil Jung, president of Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Powertrain, stated that trust built through years of capital investment, employment, and training support, along with new opportunities unmatched by any other states, made Georgia a natural choice.
Sangkil Jung expressed excitement for future opportunities as the company expands its presence in the West Point community.
Senior Project Manager Amanda Fields represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development's Global Commerce division on securing the project.
The project was secured in partnership with the West Point Development Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
Georgia has seen more than $1 billion in investment and created 2,000 jobs from South Korean companies like Hyundai in 2020.
Earlier in 2020, TEKLAS announced it would invest $6.5 million to open its first North American facility and headquarters in Gordon County.
TEKLAS is a Turkish manufacturing company and supplier of electric vehicle parts.
Over the summer, GEDIA Automotive Group announced an $85 million investment to build a new 180,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Whitfield County.
SK Innovation announced a $940 million plan to expand its EV lithium-ion battery plant in Commerce.
Georgia is home to more than 200 automotive-related facilities.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group inked a 147-megawatt power purchase agreement with Matrix Renewables, a Spanish renewable energy company, to power its electric vehicle plant currently under construction in Georgia.
The deal marks the largest single power purchase agreement signed by a Korean company in the US.

Four Hyundai subsidiaries joined the contract: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (the EV plant), Hyundai Mobis North America Electric, Hyundai Steel Georgia, and Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain.
The deal establishes a renewable energy system for both the auto plant and its supply chains.
Hyundai Motor Group stated the deal signifies a pan-conglomerate step to secure renewable energy.
Overseas offices pushed for a joint purchase agreement instead of individual deals, saving on renewable energy purchasing costs.
North American offices of Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai Steel, and Hyundai Transys will utilize the secured solar power to supply automation parts, EV steel sheets, and seats to HMGMA.
The PPA delivers power from a new solar power plant rather than existing renewable facilities, resulting in better impacts on reducing global carbon emissions.
The solar power supply is expected to decrease carbon emissions by 140,000 tons per year.
This emission reduction equals absorbing the carbon emissions produced by about 84,000 mid-sized sedans annually.
Matrix Renewables is constructing a 210-megawatt solar power plant named Stillhouse Solar in Bell County, Texas.
The Stillhouse Solar plant aims to start operating in October 2025.
Hyundai Motor has secured about 70 percent of the generation capacity.
Hyundai Motor will obtain about 378 gigawatt-hours per year from the deal.
The secured power is enough to supply approximately 100,000 households for a year.
A Hyundai Motor Group official stated they will conduct investments and deals to transition all US operations to renewable energy starting with this PPA.
The company will continue pushing for cooperation between its companies to secure renewable energy efficiently.
Hyundai Motor's EV-dedicated plant in Georgia's Bryan County is expected to begin operations in October 2024.
The plant's start date is three months ahead of the initial planned timeline.
source_title: Hyundai Motor to power US EV plant with renewable energy - The Korea Herald

Hyundai TRANSYS President of Georgia Powertrain Sangkil Jung stated that years of capital investment, employment, and training support in Georgia made it a natural choice for expansion.
Sangkil Jung expressed excitement for new opportunities as the company expands its presence in the West Point community.
Hyundai TRANSYS will bring hundreds of advanced manufacturing jobs to the local community.
The company will also create management and office staff positions.
The company plans to create 165 supplementary construction handling and inspection jobs.
This expansion will increase Hyundai TRANSYS’ total employment in Georgia to 1,741 jobs.
The company directs individuals interested in career opportunities to its website for updated information.
West Point Mayor Steve Tramell expressed thrill over Hyundai TRANSYS's long-term investment in West Point and the creation of new jobs.
Mayor Steve Tramell thanked the West Point Development Authority, the state of Georgia, and Troup County for making the project a reality.
Hyundai TRANSYS has been a community partner with West Point and Troup County for over a decade.
Troup County Chairman Patrick Crews stated that Troup County was honored to be chosen as the location for the new manufacturing facility.
Chairman Patrick Crews noted that the significant capital investment will strengthen the local economy and create economic opportunity for residents through good jobs.
Chairman Patrick Crews described Georgia as having the best state in the country for business and a community with a history of great leadership.
Chairman Patrick Crews called the project a "team win for all involved."
Senior Project Manager Amanda Fields represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce division on the project.
The project was completed in partnership with the West Point Development Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that companies investing in Georgia become Georgia companies and emphasized commitment to supporting their long-term growth.
Commissioner Pat Wilson celebrated Hyundai TRANSYS choosing to invest in Georgia again and praised their commitment to the state.
Commissioner Pat Wilson thanked Governor Kemp and the General Assembly for their continued support of recruiting business and opening new markets for Georgia companies globally.
Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that communities across Georgia, large and small, urban and rural, continue to benefit from the state's international trade and investment.
In 2020 alone, Georgia’s international relationships facilitated more than $1 billion in investment and more than 2,000 jobs from South Korean companies.
Georgia is home to more than 200 automotive-related facilities.

Georgia remains a national leader in advanced manufacturing, outpacing the United States in 10-year GDP growth in the manufacture of products including machinery, electrical equipment and components, and fabricated metals.
source_title: Hyundai TRANSYS to create 678 new jobs in West Point expansion | Local News | times-herald.com

facts: IMMI celebrated the grand opening of its new manufacturing facility in Macon, Georgia on October 2, 2025.
The new plant spans 75,000 square feet and will support school bus manufacturer Blue Bird Corp.

IMMI will equip select Blue Bird model buses with industry-first safety upgrades, including standard three-point seat belts for all student passengers and a steering wheel-deployed airbag for drivers.
The facility opening adds 80 good-paying U.S. manufacturing jobs to middle Georgia.
Larry Gray, CEO of IMMI, announced the job creation and partnership with Blue Bird.
Norm Gould III, School Bus President & IMMI CFO, recognized Blue Bird’s commitment to advancing school bus safety.
IMMI’s 4Front airbag safety system will become standard equipment on Blue Bird’s Vision-model school buses beginning in October 2025.
Under the SafeGuard brand, the Macon facility manufactures state-of-the-art school bus seats designed to protect millions of children daily.
Blue Bird was the first school bus manufacturer to announce in 2024 that lap-shoulder belts would be standard safety equipment on its seats.
John Wyskiel, President and CEO of Blue Bird Corporation, stated that the partnership brings industry-first standard student and driver safety systems to school districts in North America.
Wyskiel noted that the joint commitment to safety drives investments, manufacturing expansion, and job growth in their home state and beyond.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) and the National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) have called for lap-shoulder belts for over 25 million children who ride school buses annually.
IMMI expects to add additional jobs to its Macon workforce as demand increases for SafeGuard lap-shoulder belt seats.
Illinois recently passed legislation mandating lap-shoulder belts on new school buses, joining eight other U.S. states in the requirement.

IMMI is an employee-owned global leader in safety solutions headquartered in Westfield, Indiana, with nine additional global facilities and nearly sixty years of experience developing innovative safety restraints and systems.
IMMI’s safety products are utilized on car seats, heavy trucks, school buses, recreational/off-road vehicles, military applications, fire/EMS, motor coaches, and construction vehicles.
Blue Bird Corporation (NASDAQ: BLBD), founded in 1927, is a technology leader in school bus design, engineering, and manufacturing with a focus on safety, reliability, and durability.
Blue Bird has sold more than 25,000 propane, natural gas, and electric powered buses and positions itself as the proven leader in low- and zero-emission school buses.

links.context: Blue Bird Corporation product and service portfolio information

source_title: IMMI and Blue Bird Celebrate Opening of New Manufacturing Plant in Macon, Georgia - Blue Bird Corporation

facts: Hyundai Motor Group announced it will proceed with its planned expansion of its Georgia manufacturing facility despite recent disruptions caused by an immigration enforcement action that temporarily stalled the launch of an electric vehicle battery plant on the site.
Hyundai said it will invest $2.7 billion to boost production at the Ellabell, Georgia, facility.
The investment will increase production by 200,000 vehicles over the next three years.
This raises the plant’s total annual capacity to 500,000 vehicles.
The $2.7 billion will be used to expand production capacity at the Georgia plant and related Hyundai affiliates.
Company spokesperson Michael Stewart confirmed this allocation.
Combined with earlier investments, total spending on the auto facility reaches $10.3 billion.
This excludes the $4.3 billion Hyundai and joint-venture partner LG Energy Solution are contributing to the battery plant.
Hyundai initially unveiled the Ellabell expansion in March during the plant’s grand opening west of Savannah.
In August, Hyundai announced plans to invest an additional $5 billion across the United States.
A recent immigration raid resulted in the arrest of more than 300 South Korean workers.
The raid sparked scrutiny over the company’s continued investment in the U.S.
Hyundai CEO José Muñoz acknowledged that the recent immigration raid will push back the opening of the battery plant by at least two to three months.
Stewart said on Thursday that the facility is now expected to begin operations in the first half of 2026.
Ellabell site chief administrative officer Brent Stubbs stated, 'this situation doesn’t change our plans to continue expanding and localizing in the United States.'
Stubbs wrote in an opinion piece in The Atlanta Journal-Constitution that the company is still committed to Georgia.
The opinion piece was published Wednesday, as per AP.
The South Korean automotive company revealed that the Georgia plant will eventually assemble 10 different electric and hybrid models.
This is up from the two currently in production as the facility scales up.
Hyundai remains on track to reach a global production target of 5.6 million vehicles annually by 2030.
Plans call for 60% of those global vehicles to be electric or hybrid.
These will be aimed at markets in South Korea, North America, and Europe.
Hyundai plans to produce more than 80% of the vehicles it sells in the United States domestically by 2030.
This increases domestic content from 60% to 80%.
For the first time, the lineup will include a mid-sized pickup truck.
The lineup will also include the Santa Cruz, a four-door compact pickup that the company began selling in 2021.
Stewart said Hyundai and its on-site affiliates currently employ 3,129 workers at the Ellabell facility.
The company has pledged to expand its workforce to at least 8,500 by the end of 2031.
State and local governments have offered $2.1 billion in tax incentives and other benefits in support of the project.
The expansion at the Ellabell plant represents the largest component of Hyundai’s plan to increase global production by 1.2 million vehicles annually.
The strategy includes adding 250,000 units from its Pune, India, facility.
The strategy includes adding 200,000 from its electric vehicle plant in Ulsan, South Korea.
Hyundai said it will supply parts for another 250,000 vehicles to be assembled at plants in Saudi Arabia, Vietnam, and North Africa.
Hyundai reaffirmed its commitment to advancing robotics technology.
The company announced plans to introduce extended-range electric vehicles by 2027.
These new models will combine electric batteries with gasoline engines.
They will enable a driving range of more than 600 miles (960 kilometers).
This reflects the company’s push to innovate in both efficiency and performance.
source_title: Hyundai moves ahead with Georgia plant expansion post immigration raid

facts: Celina Mikolajczak draws on her experience leading engineering at both Tesla and Panasonic to explain why the US can succeed in battery manufacturing.

During an October 7 panel at The Battery Show North America titled "The Commercial Compass: Navigating Advanced Technologies to Market Leadership," she stated that the US can lead in batteries but must decide to do it for real.
A 10 gigawatt hour (GWh) battery factory costs about $2 billion to build.
Research and development to commercialize battery manufacturing requires about a billion dollars.
Constructing a dry room for battery production costs between $1,000 and $1,500 per square foot.
A facility needs approximately 18 to 22 gigawatt hours of capacity to achieve profitability.
Traditional venture capital models fail in battery manufacturing because investors will not fund projects requiring $1 billion and a 10-year timeline.
Ordering equipment for a battery factory results in delivery after two and a half years.
Commissioning battery factory equipment takes about one year after delivery.
The total lead time from placing an equipment order to actually making product is three and a half years.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, Panasonic's Gigafactory faced a crisis that became a proving ground for American manufacturing capability.
The factory operated with approximately 300 Japanese advisors and expats working alongside about 4,000 local employees across all shifts.
Japanese advisors typically stayed for 90 days to support a specific area before returning to Japan.
A mentorship system running since 2016 had Japanese experts serve as teachers for American workers over a three-year period.
When almost all Japanese advisors returned to Japan during the pandemic, the American team was forced to shut down the factory for two weeks.
The cell factory had been running continuously for three years without shutdown and was designed to operate 24/7 indefinitely.
Shutting down the factory took about 20 days, involving stopping production, halting cathode and electrolyte coating, and drying out the remaining lines.
The American team successfully restarted the factory independently without Japanese advisors after being down for two weeks.
Production during the restart was lower because customer Tesla was making fewer packs at the time.
The team later completed a whole factory conversion for new technology on schedule and below budget.
US staff achieved better defect rates than originally planned by planners in Japan during the factory conversion.
Mikolajczak emphasized that becoming great cell makers in the US is entirely possible but requires doing the work and learning systematically.
She compared learning battery manufacturing to learning math, noting that while some can self-teach, most need structured classes and homework.
The US should invite teachers from China, Korea, or Japan to help with battery manufacturing, but American workers must still do the work.
Battery factory teams face severe psychological challenges when yield rates drop to 50% and coating issues cause inter-departmental conflict.
Mikolajczak advised that during grim periods with low yields, teams must keep working and cannot run away from the problem.
Success requires determination and patience with capital, as Panasonic's leadership initially noted regarding the American team's struggles.
Government demand acts as an industrial policy tool, and failing to deploy it has limited supply for national interests.
Guaranteed government purchases can create a market floor that makes private investment more attractive.
The path forward requires internalizing that battery manufacturing will be expensive and take time, with no shortcuts.
Mikolajczak noted that Tesla made EVs commercial and real, and she personally drives a Model 3 and a Model S while owning four Powerwalls.
When properly supported and given time to learn, American workers and engineers can match or exceed global standards in battery manufacturing.
The core lesson is that capability exists but requires commitment, investment, systematic learning, and persistence through difficulties.
source_title: Battery Veteran's Hard Truths About US Manufacturing

facts: During its 2024 legislative session ending March 28, 2024, the Georgia General Assembly passed several key tax bills impacting state policy.
HB 1015 lowers Georgia’s individual income tax rate from 5.49 percent to 5.39 percent for tax years beginning after Jan. 1, 2024.

HB 1023 matches the corporate income tax rate to the individual income tax rate set in HB 1015 at 5.39 percent, applicable to every domestic and foreign corporation paying taxable income in Georgia and pass-through entity owners electing entity-level taxation.

HB 1181 reduces the carryforward period for most income tax credits from 10 years to five years, specifically affecting the research and development credit, jobs tax credit, and investment tax credit.
HB 1181 reduces certain other credits, such as those for student scholarships and film production, from five years to three years, with changes applying only to credits earned after Jan. 1, 2025.
HB 1181 adds sunset dates to some tax incentives and exemptions in Georgia.
HB 1162 conforms Georgia’s revenue code to the federal Internal Revenue Code (IRC) with no additional decoupling from federal tax law.

HB 598 transitions the Georgia Tax Tribunal from an administrative court in the executive branch to a judicial branch court, with voters deciding on a ballot question in November 2024 whether to grant it superior court authority.
If the November 2024 referendum passes, HB 1267 will enable the Georgia Tax Court to begin operations on Jan. 1, 2026, and receive cases starting Aug. 1, 2026.
HB 1019 allows voters to decide in November 2024 whether to increase the homestead exemption from $2,000 to $10,000.
HB 1022 proposes a state constitutional change allowing local governments to offer property tax relief for homeowners by limiting how much the taxable value of a primary residence can increase.

HB 581 proposes capping annual increases in home taxable value, likely based on inflation, and allows counties to enact a new flexible local sales tax with a generally three percent cap, separate from existing tourism (HOST) and education (LOST) taxes.

HB 808 increases the ad valorem tax exemption for tangible personal property in a county from $7,500 to $20,000, exempting taxpayers if their total tangible personal property fair market value is under $20,000.
HB 1180, which would have revamped Georgia’s film tax credit program with stricter criteria and limits on sold or transferred credits, died in the Senate without a vote despite committee approval.
Legislation passed by the General Assembly requires action from Governor Brian Kemp within 40 days of the session's close to become law; otherwise, it lapses.
source_title: GA Tax Legislation Update | Individual Income Tax Deduction

facts: Approximately 475 people were detained during an immigration raid at a sprawling Georgia site where Hyundai manufactures electric vehicles.
Steven Schrank is the Special Agent in Charge for Homeland Security Investigations.
Schrank stated that the majority of the detained individuals were from South Korea.
Schrank said the operation underscores a commitment to jobs for Georgians and Americans.
Schrank described it as the largest single site enforcement operation in the history of Homeland Security Investigations.
The investigation has been ongoing for several months, with authorities receiving leads from community members and former workers.
South Korean Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lee Jaewoong described the number of detained South Koreans as large but did not provide an exact figure.
Lee stated the detained workers were part of a network of subcontractors working for various companies on the site.
The raid targeted one of Georgia's largest and most high-profile manufacturing sites, touted by Gov. Brian Kemp and other officials as the largest economic development project in the state's history.
Hyundai Motor Group began manufacturing EVs at its $7.6 billion plant a year ago.
The Hyundai plant employs about 1,200 people.
Hyundai has partnered with LG Energy Solution to build an adjacent battery plant slated to open next year.
LG Energy Solution stated it was closely monitoring the situation and gathering all relevant details.
LG could not immediately confirm how many of its employees or Hyundai workers had been detained.
LG emphasized ensuring the safety and well-being of employees and partners, pledging full cooperation with authorities.
Hyundai's South Korean office did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
ICE spokesman Lindsay Williams confirmed federal authorities conducted an enforcement operation at the 3,000-acre (1,214-hectare) site west of Savannah, Georgia.
Agents were focused on the construction site for the battery plant.
Lee announced the South Korean ministry is dispatching diplomats from its embassy in Washington and consulate in Atlanta to the site.
The South Korean ministry plans to form an on-site response team centered on the local mission.
Lee stated that business activities of investors and rights of nationals must not be unjustly infringed during U.S. law enforcement.

The Department of Homeland Security said agents executed a search warrant as part of an ongoing criminal investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices and other serious federal crimes.
President Donald Trump's administration has undertaken sweeping ICE operations as part of a mass deportation agenda, raiding farms, construction sites, restaurants, and auto repair shops.
The Pew Research Center cited preliminary Census Bureau data regarding U.S. labor force changes.
The U.S. labor force lost more than 1.2 million immigrants from January through July, including both undocumented individuals and legal residents.
Hyundai and LG's battery joint venture, HL-GA Battery Company, stated it is cooperating fully with authorities and paused construction of the battery site to assist.
Plant spokesperson Bianca Johnson confirmed operations at Hyundai's EV manufacturing plant were not interrupted.
The Georgia plant has served as a test ground for robots.
Hyundai-owned Boston Dynamics announced earlier this year it would start deploying its dog-like Spot robot to inspect exterior quality at the facility's weld shop.
Boston Dynamics also planned to deploy its humanoid robot, Atlas, at the facility in the future.
source_title: Federal authorities detain 475 people during immigration raid in Georgia, official says | PBS News

facts: Three workers died at Hyundai’s Georgia plant since construction began in 2022.
The Wall Street Journal reported the deaths, citing a review of federal records.
Dozens of current and former workers, many of them safety coordinators who helped oversee construction, told the WSJ that the work environment involved many inexperienced immigrant laborers.
Workers cited lax safety standards and frequent accidents at the site.
The plant is operated through a joint venture between Hyundai and South Korea’s LG Energy Solution.
The facility has been in the limelight since an immigration raid last month that detained hundreds of South Korean workers.
The immigration raid was the largest single-site enforcement operation in the U.S. Department of Homeland Security’s history.
Apart from the deaths, more than a dozen workers have suffered serious injuries.
Injuries included falling without wearing harnesses and getting crushed by forklifts.
Workers interviewed said Hyundai did not ensure that people were properly trained.
Safety regulators did little to prevent worksite violations, according to the report.
Reuters could not immediately confirm the report.
Hyundai did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
Hyundai stated it is committed to following immigration laws and does not compromise safety for the sake of speed.
Hyundai took steps to address safety issues in response to incidents during construction.
Hyundai CEO Jose Munoz told the newspaper: “We acted immediately and comprehensively to prevent anything like this from happening again.”
Jose Munoz traveled to Georgia to tell the team directly that their safety comes before production schedules, costs, profits, and everything else.
Construction on the electric vehicle and battery plant is ongoing.
The plant is located in Bryan County about 30 miles west of Savannah.
Hyundai started construction of its $7.6 billion auto plant in Georgia in 2022.
source_title: Three workers died at Hyundai’s Georgia plant since 2022, before US immigration raid, WSJ reports | Salem News Channel

facts: U.S. immigration authorities arrested 475 workers at a Hyundai facility in Georgia on Thursday.
A U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement official confirmed the arrests on Friday.
Many of the arrested workers held temporary visas for tourism and business travel that are not authorized for work.
The group of arrested workers included South Korean nationals.
source_title: U.S. immigration authorities arrested 475 workers at Hyundai facility in Georgia on Thursday, ICE official says - Yahoo News Canada

facts: IMMI and Blue Bird Corporation, which is based in Georgia, opened a new manufacturing plant in Macon.
The new facility will create 80 new jobs.
The facility enhances safety features in school buses.
John Wyskiel, President and CEO of Blue Bird Corporation, stated that Blue Bird has led the way in school bus safety for nearly 100 years.
The new facility spans 75,000 square feet.
The facility will produce school bus seats equipped with three-point seat belts.
Blue Bird is pioneering three-point seat belts as a standard feature on its buses.
Additional safety features include a steering wheel airbag for driver protection.
Larry Gray, CEO of IMMI, stated that the project adds 80 good-paying U.S. manufacturing jobs to middle Georgia.
The introduction of lap-shoulder belts as standard equipment aligns with recommendations from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration and the National Transportation Safety Board.
The Macon facility operates under the brand SafeGuard.
The facility will manufacture state-of-the-art seats contributing to the safety of millions of children who travel on school buses daily.
Demand for IMMI SafeGuard’s lap-shoulder belt seats is expected to rise, potentially leading to more job opportunities in the future.
Illinois recently passed legislation requiring lap-shoulder belts on new school buses.
Illinois joins eight other states with similar seat belt mandates for school buses.
Blue Bird and IMMI expect the trend of state seat belt mandates to continue as more states recognize the importance of seat belts in enhancing school bus safety.
The opening of the plant boosts local employment and sets a new standard for school bus safety across the United States.
As more states adopt seat belt requirements, IMMI and Blue Bird’s innovations are likely to play a crucial role in protecting student passengers nationwide.
source_title: Georgia-based bus company expands manufacturing in Macon – WSB-TV Channel 2 - Atlanta

facts: Kia Motors Corp. and State of Georgia officials in Seoul announced Kia's $1.2-billion investment in its first North American manufacturing complex a few days after DaimlerChrysler's March 8 announcement.
The plant will be located in West Point, Ga., hard by the Alabama state line.
The plant will have a capacity of 300,000 vehicles a year when it opens in 2009.

Euisun Chung, president and CEO of Kia Motors Corp., stated at the signing ceremony that Kia has entered an aggressive growth phase in the U.S. and the decision to build in West Point, Troup County, Georgia, is the latest example of the company's commitment to the marketplace.
Georgia Gov. Sonny Perdue accompanied Euisun Chung at the Seoul signing ceremony.
Kia's first priority in selecting a site was to maximize synergy with Hyundai Motor's Alabama plant near Montgomery.

Other site selection considerations included availability of qualified workforce, logistics and transportation infrastructure, risk of natural disasters, surrounding infrastructure, and incentive package.
The Southern region is the most strategically important market for Kia's U.S. sales strategy.
West Point was considered the most favorable site among all options.
Runners-up for the site included multiple locations in Mississippi: one near Kewanee on Alabama's western state line and one in Lowndes County near Columbus.
Mississippi offered an incentive package worth some $200 million plus Gulf Opportunity Zone federal incentives enabling faster depreciation on materials and machinery investments.
Another 2,000 jobs are expected from five suppliers that the state and company agreed would locate in Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis is the only one of the five suppliers officially confirmed to be on or near the chosen Kia site.
The West Point site covers 3,300 acres (1,336 hectares) shaped like a warm front moving in on I-85.
The plant complex itself will occupy 2,200 acres (890 hectares), with the rest given over to infrastructure and transportation improvements.
A total of 37 parcels belonging to 30 different owners were put under option by the state in approximately two months, concluding in February.
The state expects all options to be exercised within the next month in preparation for a late April groundbreaking ceremony.

Landowner sentiment shifted from negative when selling was first broached in late December to nearly universal support that selling is in the best interest of the community's future and leaves a legacy for future generations.
Groundwork for the project was laid in October 2003 when Gov. Perdue first visited South Korea.
Georgia projects from Korean firms LG Electronics and Samsung have come to fruition since that time.
A Kia consultant previously looked at the state's mega-site in Pooler near Savannah, a site in Morgan County, and several in middle Georgia under Project Pine Tree.
The site selection process for the West Point plant took place largely in the States rather than Korea.
Bert Brantley, communications manager for the Georgia Dept. of Economic Development, described the competitive battle with Mississippi as largely hooey.
Hyundai-Kia's negotiating style was described as very proper and respectful, illustrated by Mr. Ahn only calling Georgia project manager Chris Clark by his first name after the agreement was final.
West Point may become the third point of a bi-state opportunity zone within a zone alongside Auburn-Opelika, Ala., and Columbus, Ga.
AFLAC announced a $100-million, 2,000-employee expansion in Columbus, exclamation point on a year of multiple projects and BRAC news bringing ~10,000 military personnel to nearby Fort Benning.
Hyundai supplier Mando America invested $65 million in two separate projects in Opelika during 2004-2005 and relocated its small headquarters there from Detroit.
The Atlanta-Birmingham-Montgomery triangle formed by Interstates 85, 20, and 65 has a longest arm of only 160 miles (257 km).

Local organizations involved in cementing the Kia deal included the Development Authority of LaGrange, LaGrange-Troup County Chamber of Commerce, West Point Development Authority, cities of LaGrange and West Point, and Troup County.
Troup County's population was 58,779 in 2000.
Milliken and Co. remains the area's largest employer with around 2,000 employees despite a weakening textile base over 30 years.
Recent corporate investors in the area include Wal-Mart, Caterpillar, and German automotive supplier ae group, which located there in 2005.
Gulfstream Aerospace announced $300 million over seven years investment in Savannah operations a week before the Kia announcement, boosting employment by 1,100 to 5,400.
Toyota committed same day to building 100,000 Camrys a year at the Subaru plant in Lafayette, Indiana, transferring production from Japan.
Kia is taking Toyota's multifaceted facility development strategy as its model for North America, choosing to site production, design and headquarters where its target market resides.
Construction is under way on Kia's $87-million U.S. corporate campus in Irvine, Calif., expected to open in December 2006.
The Irvine campus will include a 65,000-sq.-ft. design center.
Kia Motors has invested more than $300 million in the United States over the last four years.
Kia's investments include a state-of-the-art research and development center in Ann Arbor, Mich., and a custom-built vehicle proving grounds in Mojave, Calif.
Hyundai-Kia's Ann Arbor center expanded beyond original plans, boosting capital investment there to $162 million.
The company has a plan to sell some 800,000 vehicles a year in North America by 2010.
For the two months ending in February, Kia's total global export sales were up by more than 18 percent vs. the same period in 2005, at 181,659.
Kia has enjoyed 12 years of consecutive U.S. sales records since coming to market, including 275,871 vehicles in 2005.
The deal coincides with U.S. and Korea embarking on free trade agreement (FTA) negotiations.

U.S. Sen. George V. Voinovich (R-OH) and Sen. Carl Levin (D-MI) wrote a February letter fearing the FTA will significantly benefit Korean automakers without giving U.S. automakers additional access to Korea's historically closed market.
One study indicates Korean exports to the U.S. would increase by as much as 17 percent with an FTA, boosting Korea's GDP by 2 percent.

Frank Vargo, vice president of international economic affairs for the National Association of Manufacturers, stated he would be surprised if trade negotiations affected the timing of Kia or Samsung Austin investments.
Assistant U.S. Trade Representative Wendy Cutler said Korea is currently the U.S.'s seventh largest trading partner and export market, and sixth largest agricultural market.
Two-way trade between the U.S. and Korea is valued at $73 billion and expected to grow under an FTA.
The Korea Institute for International Economic Policy estimates two-way trade will increase by almost $20 billion as a result of the agreement.
source_title: Georgia Finally Lands the Big One — Site Selection Online

facts: The event is scheduled for April 9, 2025 @ 1:00 pm – 3:15 pm EDT.
Registration for this event is open through COB, Wednesday, April 2nd in compliance with Kia Georgia security.
No refunds or substitutions are offered after that date.
The facility is located in West Point, GA.
It is the only Kia manufacturing plant in the United States and the only automaker in Georgia.
The plant covers 2200 acres with a total investment of over $2.8 billion.
The Kia Georgia team manufactures the Telluride, Sorento and Sportage SUVs (and soon the EV9).
Total production is 350,000 vehicles per year at this location.
Operations run on 3 shifts a day, 24 hours a day.
The Kia Georgia facility ships vehicles to sales markets around the globe, including the United States, Canada, Mexico, the Caribbean, Middle East and Pacific Nations.
Kia Georgia states that environmental protection and regulatory compliance are core values while producing high quality, affordable vehicles to enhance both economic value and sustainability.
Registration is open to executives, directors and senior managers of manufacturing companies.
source_title: Kia Georgia Facility Tour – Next Generation Manufacturing

facts: Kia Georgia announced a $100,000 donation to the Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG) Foundation to support workforce development and technical education across the state.
The contribution was presented by Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess at TCSG’s annual GOAL and Rick Perkins Awards banquet.

Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess stated that technical education plays a critical role in driving innovation and economic growth, and the investment ensures students have necessary resources.
The donation will expand access to critical support services for TCSG students, with a portion directed to the TCSG Foundation’s Last Mile Fund.
The Last Mile Fund provides financial assistance to students nearing graduation to help them overcome unexpected barriers and complete their credentials.
Since 2016, Kia Georgia has awarded a brand-new Kia vehicle annually to the state’s GOAL winner.
This year's GOAL winner will receive a new Kia Sportage, which is built at Kia’s advanced manufacturing facility in West Point, Georgia.
TCSG Commissioner Greg Dozier stated that Kia Georgia continues to be an outstanding partner in building a highly skilled, job-ready workforce.
Dozier noted that the investment directly supports students and strengthens the talent pipeline that drives Georgia’s economy.
Kia Georgia's impact in the state dates back to its initial $1 billion investment in 2006 to establish its West Point manufacturing facility.
Kia Georgia has invested more than $3.2 billion in its West Point operation, which spans 2,200 acres and has the capacity to produce more than 350,000 vehicles annually.
The West Point plant manufactures the Telluride, Sorento, Sportage, all-electric EV9, and EV6 models, serving as a major driver of job creation and innovation in Georgia.

More than 159,000 students are currently enrolled across TCSG’s 22 colleges, preparing for high-demand careers that support Georgia’s position as the No. 1 state for business and Top State for Talent.
source_title: Kia Georgia Invests $100,000 in TCSG Foundation to Support Georgia’s Workforce Pipeline - Metro Atlanta CEO

facts: Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc. (KMMG) extended its online application period by two weeks due to strong regional interest.
Since re-opening the application process in November, more than 42,000 individuals have applied for Production Team Member, Maintenance Team Member, or Die Maintenance Team Member positions.
The application window will remain open until May 7 to allow everyone a chance to apply.
Only online applications are being accepted at this time.
Applicants can submit their applications online, view videos of current team members, and get an inside look at the facility.
Randy Jackson, director of human resources for KMMG’s West Point, Ga., facility, expressed appreciation for the overwhelming response from interested individuals.
The success of the new Kia Sorento has led to increased vehicle demand, which accelerated the hiring schedule for a second wave of team members.
More than 1,300 team members are currently employed at KMMG.
The company anticipates hiring hundreds more in preparation for another production shift beginning in the fall.

Randy Jackson stated that consumer response to the quality and appeal of the new Kia Sorento is a testament to the dedication and quality of current team members and the effectiveness of support received from Georgia Quick Start.
KMMG’s online application process was first launched in 2008, marking an industry-first innovation developed for Kia by Georgia Quick Start.
Georgia Quick Start is the state’s acclaimed workforce training organization.
Jackson noted that the partnership with Quick Start has helped the company truly “select the best” from applicants.
KMMG anticipates additional future growth as it adds production of additional car models at the facility, at which point the online application process will be re-opened again.
Individuals can subscribe to a FirstAlert! email notification for future hiring opportunities.
Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc. (KMMG) is the first manufacturing site in North America for Seoul, Korea-based Kia Motors Corporation.
KMMG is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Georgia.
The facility began full mass production of the 2011 Kia Sorento on November 16, 2009.
At full capacity, the plant will have the ability to build 300,000 vehicles annually.
The 2011 Kia Sorento is assembled in the United States from U.S. and globally-sourced parts.

facts: Kia debuted the 2026 Kia EV4 electric sedan at the 2025 New York Auto Show.
The EV4 is positioned at the entry point of Kia's lineup as a more affordable option for North American shoppers.
The EV4 utilizes Hyundai Motor Group's Electric Global Modular Platform (E-GMP), which underpins nearly all HMG's dedicated EV models including the Ioniq 5 and EV9.
The EV4 is scheduled to arrive in early 2026.
The EV4 is powered by a single 150-kilowatt (201-horsepower) electric motor mounted on the front axle.
The EV4 will be offered with two 400-volt battery options.
The base EV4 Light trim includes a 58.3 kilowatt-hour battery pack expected to deliver up to 235 miles of range per charge.
The EV4 Wind and top-spec EV4 GT-Line trims feature an 81.4 kWh battery unit promising up to 330 miles of Kia-estimated range for the lighter Wind trim.
The EV4 can charge from 10% to 80% in 29 to 31 minutes depending on the battery via a built-in North American Charging System (NACS) port.
The EV4's charging port is relocated to the front passenger side to facilitate convenient nose-in parking at Tesla Supercharger stations.
The EV4 features version 3.0 of Kia's i-Pedal variable regenerative braking system, which automatically adjusts regeneration based on speed and distance to leading vehicles.
The i-Pedal v3.0 system now operates in reverse and retains the driver's preferred regeneration level when the vehicle is restarted.
Like other E-GMP vehicles, the EV4 is compatible with Kia's vehicle-to-load (V2L) adapter, converting the external charging port into a 120-volt/15-amp power outlet for running appliances and tools.
The interior features a dual 12.3-inch display digital dashboard paired with a dedicated 5-inch climate display.
The cabin tech suite includes the new Kia AI Assistant for natural language voice commands to minimize driver distraction.
Available technology includes Digital Key 2.0, wireless Android Auto and Apple CarPlay connectivity, and standard Highway Driving Assistant safety tech.
The EV4's software will be maintained via over-the-air updates and feature-on-demand purchases through the Kia Connect data connection, requiring a subscription after a three-year included trial.
Final EPA range and pricing for the South Korean-manufactured EV are still pending announcement.
More details on the EV4 are expected to be announced in the coming months.
Kia also debuted the 2026 K4 Hatchback variant of its combustion-powered 2026 K4 sedan at the same event.
The K4 Hatchback offers 22.2 cubic feet of cargo capacity behind the rear seats, expanding to 59.3 cubic feet when folded flat.
The K4 Hatchback is approximately 11 inches shorter bumper-to-bumper than the sedan while maintaining the same rear legroom and gaining about an inch of rear headroom.
The K4 Hatchback will be offered with a standard 2.0-liter four-cylinder engine producing 147 hp and 132 lb-ft of torque, or a turbocharged 1.6-liter engine producing 190 hp and 195 lb-ft of torque.
First examples of the K4 Hatchback are expected to reach dealerships in late 2025.
source_title: 2026 Kia EV4: Wild-Styled EV Sedan (Hopefully) Won't Break the Bank - CNET

facts: Kia is potentially partnering with Apple to produce self-driving electric vehicles.
CNBC reported that Apple is close to finalizing a contract with Hyundai Motor and Kia to manufacture an Apple-branded autonomous EV at Kia's plant in Georgia.
Kia has maintained that nothing has been decided on the deal yet.
Kia is set to hold an event for investors on Feb. 9, where attention is being drawn to whether the company will unveil specifics of the plan.
The CNBC report stated the "Apple Car" under development aims to be put into production in 2024, although the official launch could be delayed.
Analyst Kuo Ming-chi at Taiwan-based TF International Securities said Apple's first vehicle is expected to be built around Hyundai's E-GMP electric vehicle platform.
Kuo projected that Hyundai Mobis will be in charge of designing and producing some of the components.
Industry watchers believe Hyundai-Kia's EV supply chain makes the company a favorable partner for Apple.
Hyundai Motor has a production capacity of 7 million vehicles a year.
According to industry sources, Apple could request Hyundai to designate manufacturing lines to produce only Apple vehicles strictly based on designs it provides.
Hyundai's E-GMP platform is seen as an enticing factor for Apple because not many carmakers have original EV platforms.
source_title: Hyundai-Kia nearing deal with Apple? - The Korea Times

facts: The Georgia Department of Labor will assist Kia Georgia, Inc. to host a virtual recruitment on November 18, 2021.
The event will include general information sessions or scheduled interviews to fill approximately 550 Production Team Member positions.
The virtual recruitment is scheduled for November 18, 2021, from 10 am until 12 pm and again from 2 pm until 4 pm.
Applicants must be 18 years of age.
A criminal background check will be conducted on applicants.
Entry salary starts at $16.27 and will incrementally increase to $25.15 over a three-year period.
Applicants can apply in advance to be considered for a scheduled virtual interview.
Positions are full-time, permanent shifts with overtime required.
Benefits include vacation/PTO and medical leave.
Retirement benefits include 401K and profit sharing.
Insurance coverage includes dental, health, life, and vision.
Miscellaneous benefits include tuition, clothing, and uniforms.

facts: UGA and Kia Georgia have a partnership that continues to advance several of Georgia’s workforce development initiatives and overall economic growth.
The two-pronged collaboration supports university programs and prepares the next generation of leaders in industry and policy at UGA Public Service and Outreach.
Kia Georgia has contributed $75,000 since 2020 as a presenting sponsor of the Biennial Institute through UGA’s Carl Vinson Institute of Government.

The Biennial Institute provides legislative training for members of the Georgia General Assembly with valuable time for learning, networking and exploring state issues prior to the upcoming General Assembly session.
Kia Georgia sponsored Georgia’s Workforce Conference in 2023 and 2024.
Georgia’s Workforce Conference is hosted annually by the UGA Institute of Government.
Stuart Countess, President and CEO of Kia Georgia Inc., was a keynote speaker at the 2023 conference event.
Stuart Countess helped to moderate and organize the Strategies to Retain and Engage Employees plenary session in 2024.

Matt Bishop, interim vice president for Public Service and Outreach at UGA, stated that Kia Georgia’s continued partnership is expanding the impact of key university initiatives like Georgia’s Workforce Conference and the Biennial Institute for Georgia Legislators.
Matt Bishop noted that Kia Georgia’s commitment to innovation and community investment is helping to strengthen the state’s workforce and drive economic growth.
The partnership extends to UGA’s College of Engineering where Kia Georgia supports the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility (GNEM).
Kia Georgia also supports the Annual E-mobility Summit and a campus-wide e-mobility initiative at UGA.
Kia Georgia made a $1 million founding gift to GNEM.
GNEM is a public-private partnership network established by UGA in early 2023.
GNEM seeks to enhance research, education and outreach opportunities for students interested in a career in e-mobility across the state.
GNEM envisions Georgia as a world leading ecosystem for electric mobility.
GNEM accelerates electric and future mobility innovation through workforce and economic development, research and thought leadership, and key industry partnerships.
GNEM aims to further advance Georgia’s nation-leading momentum in EV manufacturing across the entire ecosystem, involving rural and underserved communities and small businesses.

Stephen Durham, interim dean for UGA’s College of Engineering, stated that Kia Georgia’s support as a founding partner has helped students become leaders in e-mobility and create new opportunities to strengthen Georgia’s economy.
Since opening its West Point facility in 2009, Kia Georgia has created over 14,000 jobs at the plant and nearby suppliers.
To date, Kia Georgia has produced more than 4.7 million vehicles.
Kia Georgia produced the state’s first all-electric vehicle, the EV9.
Stuart Countess stated that Kia Georgia is driven by a commitment to shape the future of mobility and is proud to work in a state that values innovation and forward-thinking partnerships.
Countess noted that UGA’s support of GNEM reflects a shared vision that ultimately helps position Georgia as a global leader in e-mobility.
As Georgia’s land-grant university, UGA is uniquely positioned to partner with Kia Georgia to provide resources and expertise for workforce development and economic growth.
source_title: UGA and Kia combine efforts to boost Georgia’s economy - Give to UGA

facts: Kia Georgia announced a $100,000 donation to the Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG) Foundation.
Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess presented the donation check at TCSG’s annual GOAL and Rick Perkins Awards banquet celebrating top students and instructors.
The donation will directly benefit students enrolled in technical colleges across Georgia by providing vital resources to enhance educational opportunities and career pathways.
Since 2016, Kia Georgia has provided funding to award the state GOAL winner a brand-new Kia as the grand prize.
This year’s GOAL winner will receive a brand-new Kia Sportage built at the Kia facility in West Point.
Remaining funding from this year's contribution will support The Last Mile Fund to provide gap funding for more than 131,000 technical education students served within TCSG.
TCSG Commissioner Greg Dozier stated the donation will help remove barriers to education and create pathways to success in high-demand fields.
In 2006, Kia made one of the largest single foreign investments in Georgia's history by establishing a state-of-the-art production facility in West Point.
Kia Georgia has an annual capacity of more than 350,000 vehicles.
Kia Georgia is home to the Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV, Sportage SUV, and all-electric EV9.
The advanced manufacturing facility represents a $2.8 billion investment on a 2,200-acre site.
The facility operates 24 hours a day.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
Kia Georgia began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
TCSG consists of 22 colleges offering training in more than 600 associate degrees, diploma, and certificate programs.
TCSG houses Georgia’s Office of Adult Education, which promotes adult literacy and education programs including the GED testing program throughout the state.
TCSG partners with companies through Quick Start, described as the nation’s top customized workforce training program.
The TCSG Foundation is a 501(c)(3) organization that supports TCSG through marketing, advocacy, and fundraising.

facts: Area Development magazine named Georgia the number one state for business for a ninth consecutive year.
No other state has earned this distinction for so many years.

Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the ranking alongside executives from Kia Georgia, Bridgestone Golf, and W.C. Bradley Company (parent of Char-Broil), plus members of the General Assembly, state and local economic developers, and local officials.
The announcement took place at the Kia Georgia Training Center in West Point.

Governor Kemp cited partnership approach, conservative governing, pandemic response, world-class infrastructure, award-winning workforce training programs, pro-business environment, and hardworking Georgians as reasons for the ranking.
Area Development's annual poll considers approximately 50 leading site consulting firms across the U.S. and evaluates 13 different factors.
Georgia earned a No. 1 ranking for its workforce development program, Georgia Quick Start, for the thirteenth consecutive year.

Georgia earned top rankings in six additional categories, including cost of doing business, cooperative and responsive state government, competitive labor environment, available real estate, and tied for first place in energy availability and costs.
Area Development's Editor Geraldine Gambale stated that state and local economic developers in Georgia consistently work together to guide companies through location and retention processes.

The 13 evaluation categories include overall cost of doing business, cooperative/responsive state government, favorable regulatory environment, corporate tax environment, business incentives, competitive labor environment, workforce development programs, logistics/infrastructure, access to capital/project funding, available real estate, energy availability/costs, speed of permitting, and site-readiness programs.
Georgia placed in the top 10 for all 13 categories.

Lt. Governor Geoff Duncan attributed the ranking to business/workforce centric policies, an influx of major investments from small and large businesses, and one of the lowest unemployment rates in state history.
Speaker David Ralston highlighted pro-business policies like cutting taxes and incentivizing investment to create private sector jobs across the state.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that hundreds of companies chose Georgia in the last year alone to create future jobs.
During fiscal year 2022 (July 1, 2021 – June 30, 2022), investments in expansions and new locations totaled more than $21.2 billion.
51,132 jobs were created in the state through 358 projects supported by GDEcD’s Global Commerce team during that period.
The two largest projects in state history are Rivian and Hyundai Motor Group, which totaled more than $10.5 billion in investments and 15,600 new jobs combined.
Area Development was founded in 1965 and is considered the leading executive magazine covering corporate site selection and relocation.

GDEcD serves as the state’s sales and marketing arm for attracting business investment, encouraging industry expansion, locating markets, promoting tourism/arts/film/music/digital entertainment, and mobilizing state resources for economic development.
source_title: Georgia named No. 1 state for business

facts: The 2025–2026 Kia EV9 and certain trims of the 2025 Kia EV6 stand out for full eligibility for the federal EV tax credit.
Both the EV9 and qualifying EV6 trims are assembled in West Point, Georgia.
Other Kia models like the Niro EV generally do not qualify due to overseas manufacturing in South Korea.
For leasing, even non-qualifying Kia models may offer the full $7,500 incentive under commercial fleet rules.
Federal tax credit eligibility now hinges on where the car and its battery components are made rather than brand origin.
The shift in policy opened the door for Korean automakers like Kia to qualify provided they meet domestic production thresholds.
The current federal tax credit offers up to $7,500 per vehicle: $3,750 for final assembly in North America and another $3,750 if critical minerals and battery components meet sourcing requirements.
As of early 2025, only the EV9 and specific EV6 trims satisfy both assembly and battery sourcing conditions.
Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S.-assembled EVs were previously ineligible despite strong specifications.
In 2025, the Georgia plant began producing the EV9 and adjusted EV6 output to meet federal criteria.
The EV9 became a top consideration among three-row electric SUVs under the $80,000 MSRP price cap for credit eligibility.
Kia provides a five-year/60,000-mile warranty on batteries and an eight-year/100,000-mile powertrain coverage.
All trims of the 2025–2026 EV9 (Light, Wind, Wave, and GT-Line) assembled in West Point, Georgia are eligible for the full $7,500 credit assuming buyer income and MSRP thresholds are met.
The EV9 features an 800V architecture enabling fast charging, high towing capacity up to 3,500 lbs, and standard advanced safety features.
The base range of the EV9 is approximately 230–280 miles.
Only certain 2025 EV6 trims qualify; the Light and Wind AWD models produced in Georgia are eligible while the high-performance GT trim imported from Korea is excluded.
The EV6 GT trim hits 0–60 mph in 3.4 seconds and features V2L (vehicle-to-load) capability but has limited rear headroom and a smaller trunk than competitors.
The Niro PHEV qualifies for a partial credit of $3,750 only if battery components meet sourcing rules, which is rarely the case.
Both the Sorento PHEV and Sportage PHEV are plug-in hybrids assembled outside North America and do not qualify for the full credit, with partial eligibility being uncommon due to battery sourcing.
At least 50% of battery components must originate in North America to qualify, increasing to 60% in 2026.
Critical minerals requirements mandate that 80% be sourced from U.S. free-trade partners or extracted/recycled domestically for the second $3,750 portion of the credit.
The MSRP cap for SUVs and vans is $80,000; the EV9 GT-Line with full options can approach this limit.
Household income caps apply at up to $300,000 for joint filers and $225,000 for single filers.
The EV9 starts around $56,000 and reaches $77,000 fully loaded.
The U.S.-built EV6 starts near $53,000.
Manufacturer-specific caps on tax credits have largely phased out, so Kia buyers are not limited by volume-based roll-offs.
High demand for Georgia-built EV9s has led to extended wait times of 3–6 months in high-demand areas.
The EV9 competes directly with the Ford Explorer EV (assembled in Oakville, Canada), Chevrolet Blazer EV (Hamtramck, MI), and Tesla Model Y (Austin, TX / Fremont, CA).
The Kia EV6 is assembled in West Point, Georgia and Hwaseong, South Korea.
Both the EV9 and EV6 earned Top Safety Pick+ from IIHS with standard features including forward collision warning, automatic emergency braking, lane keeping assist, and adaptive cruise control.
Battery health monitoring is built into the MyKia app for Kia EVs.
The tax credit is claimed when filing annual returns and requires documentation from the dealer, typically IRS Form 8936.
Tax credit rules may change in late 2025, requiring buyers to verify eligibility close to the purchase date.
When leasing, the tax credit typically goes to the leasing company but savings are passed to the buyer via lower monthly payments.
source_title: Which Kia Electric Cars Qualify for the EV Tax Credit – Guide

This marks the first time the compact crossover has been produced in the U.S.
