facts: A decisive shift into thermal management and e‑mobility fluid handling has repositioned TI Fluid Systems from legacy fuel systems to a critical enabler of electrified vehicles.
The company operates 100+ sites across ~28 countries and serves major OEMs with fluid storage, delivery and thermal solutions.

Primary customers include global OEMs across Europe, North America and China, plus tiered EV manufacturers and e-mobility platform integrators seeking thermal management modules and integrated fluid systems to support electrification and improved fuel-efficiency targets.

Localized plants in North America, Europe and China are synchronized to OEM platform shifts toward heat-pump and battery thermal circuits, reducing logistics lead times and improving responsiveness to SOP schedules.

Additional capacity in Mexico and Eastern Europe targets lower landed cost and tariff mitigation for European, US and Korean OEMs, supporting model launches from 2024 through 2026 with staged ramp plans.

Product mix is moving from fuel tanks and conventional lines toward coolant manifolds, battery chiller modules, sensorized integrated lines and refrigerant lines to capture higher margin e-mobility content.
Management targets high-teens CAGR for e-mobility thermal revenue through 2026–2027 as global EV penetration reaches about 16% of light-vehicle sales in 2024 (BEV+PHEV).
Platform awards and a growing lifetime order book are central to TI Fluid Systems growth strategy, with a focus on bundled thermal modules that expand content per vehicle.
Since 2022 new-business awards have accelerated on electrified platforms, emphasizing higher-margin modules and bundled line sets that drive outgrowth versus global LV production.
The company is targeting outgrowth of 300–500 bps versus global light-vehicle production through 2026 via Europe and China EV wins and North American hybrid refreshes.
Multiple thermal production lines are ramping to meet 2024–2026 model SOPs for German, US and Korean OEM platforms.
The lifetime order book is increasingly weighted to e-thermal components, improving revenue visibility and margin mix.
Operational SOPs and capacity ramps are synchronized to OEM start-ups to minimize launch disruption.
TI Fluid Systems pursues selective partnerships and acquisitions that are immediately accretive and deepen EV thermal content per vehicle while expanding regional footprint.
The company is pursuing refrigerant component and advanced polymer partnerships to shorten product development cycles.
It is developing sensorized line capability with strategic technology partners to add diagnostics and system-level value.
TI Fluid Systems is open to bolt-on acquisitions focused on thermal module capability or faster market access in Asia and North America.
The company prioritizes assets that raise content per vehicle and improve margin profile.
Customers increasingly demand lighter, lower-permeation thermal and fluid systems that enable EV range targets, stricter refrigerant compliance, and predictive maintenance.
Procurement prioritizes validated performance, low lifecycle emissions, and supply resilience from suppliers like TI Fluid Systems.
Investment concentrates on integrated thermal assemblies for battery, cabin and powertrain cooling to meet OEM packaging and efficiency targets.
Expanded testing for R1234yf and CO2 systems addresses leakage, NVH and thermal performance to satisfy stricter regulations and OEM specs.
Model-based systems engineering and virtual validation shorten development cycles and reduce physical prototyping needs.
Advanced extrusion, braiding and automation increase throughput and quality for complex multi-layer lines and manifolds.
Sensors and diagnostics-ready ports are being added to enable predictive maintenance and energy optimization across vehicle fleets.
Migration to lower-mass, lower-permeation composites and recycled-content polymers reduces refrigerant charge and pumping losses to support OEM carbon and range targets.
The company holds a growing patent estate focused on thermal interfaces and fluid conveyance, supporting IP-driven differentiation.
Multi-year awards for integrated thermal assemblies on high-volume EV platforms validate product-market fit and provide revenue visibility.
Design-for-manufacture and virtual validation efforts reduced development cycle times by up to 30% in select projects, improving time-to-production.
Material shifts and optimized architectures target up to 10–15% lower system mass and measurable reductions in refrigerant charge versus legacy designs.
TI Fluid Systems operates globally with manufacturing and engineering footprints across Europe, North America, Asia and South America, supplying OEMs and aftermarket channels.
Revenue rebounded to the mid‑€3bn range in 2023 as supply‑chain normalization and a favorable EV/hybrid mix supported demand.
Margin recovery was aided by commercial resets, cost pass‑throughs and higher utilization.
Shift toward thermal and e‑mobility content increased per‑vehicle content and drove operating leverage as factories filled.
Thermal/e-thermal systems now represent a growing share of revenue and margin contribution.

Management targets outgrowing global light‑vehicle (LV) production by 300–500 bps and lifting adjusted EBIT margin toward the high‑6% to ~8% range as thermal mix expands and productivity programs mature.
Capex is expected to normalize near 4–5% of sales, enabling conversion of EBITDA to free cash flow.
Consensus into 2025–2026 implies improving FCF, with steady‑state potential >€200m annually to support deleveraging.
Primary uses of cash are organic thermal capacity, selective bolt‑on acquisitions and maintaining balance‑sheet strength to target net leverage near 1–1.5x EBITDA in benign markets.
Pricing discipline, raw‑material indexing and footprint optimization are expected to preserve margins amid regional volume swings and support sustained cash generation.
Consensus expects low‑ to mid‑single‑digit top‑line CAGR into 2025–2026, with faster growth in e‑thermal systems as EV/hybrid mix rises and ICE exposure declines.
Improving FCF generation and disciplined M&A should allow progressive net‑debt reduction; target leverage reflects a conservative capital structure for cyclicality in auto markets.
Margin ambition is benchmarked to diversified auto suppliers with similar thermal/e‑mobility mix, aiming to close the gap toward peer medians as EV thermal content per vehicle increases.

Stronger margins, indexed pricing and capacity investments in EV cooling position TI Fluid Systems to capitalize on automotive electrification while supporting an investment thesis focused on revenue growth outlook and margin improvement.
Revenue baseline is mid‑€3bn in 2023.
Adjusted EBIT margin target is high‑6% to ~8% by 2026.
Capex intensity is ~4–5% of sales.
Steady‑state FCF potential is >€200m annually.

Potential risks and obstacles for TI Fluid Systems center on technology shifts in vehicle electrification, margin pressure from OEMs and competitors, supply‑chain volatility, regulatory change, China competition, and execution complexity.
Slower EV uptake or regional variability across Europe, the US and China could delay thermal growth and prolong reliance on price‑sensitive ICE components.
Rapid tech shifts such as heat‑pump adoption or new refrigerant architectures could advantage competitors with different IP.
OEM cost‑down cycles and consolidation increase sourcing leverage, raising warranty and margin risks.
Volatility in polymers, aluminum, copper and refrigerants, plus freight and labor bottlenecks, can pressure working capital and margins.
Management uses index‑linked contracts, dual sourcing, inventory discipline and regionalized manufacturing to mitigate exposure.
Shifts in refrigerant regulations (e.g., phasedown of high-GWP fluids), recyclability rules or emissions standards drive requalification, engineering cost and capex.
Robust compliance engineering and scenario planning reduce redesign cycle risk.
Local suppliers with lower cost bases intensify price competition for thermal management assemblies.
TI Fluid Systems counters through localization, partnerships and differentiated thermal assemblies to preserve content and margins.
Simultaneous SOPs across multiple EV platforms and new plant ramps increase operational complexity.
Phased launches, automation investment and strict supplier PPAP reduce start‑up defects and protect gross margin.
Key mitigants and close monitoring areas include supply-chain diversification, IP and product differentiation for EV cooling systems, and active OEM engagement.
Index‑linked raw material contracts and dual sourcing lower input volatility.
Regional manufacturing reduces freight lead times and working capital needs.
Proactive R&D and compliance engineering anticipate refrigerant and recyclability changes, limiting requalification cycles and unexpected capex.
Localization and differentiated thermal assemblies target share retention against low‑cost Chinese players while supporting TI Fluid Systems growth strategy 2025 and beyond.
Phased SOPs, automation and supplier PPAP rigor aim to limit ramp-related cost overruns and protect annual margin targets and cash flow metrics.
source_title: What is Growth Strategy and Future Prospects of TI Fluid Systems Company? – PortersFiveForce.com

facts: Georgia has been ranked the No. 1 state for business for a decade.
GDEcD Deputy Commissioner of Global Commerce Misti Martin highlighted the state's strong partnerships, infrastructure investments, and business-first mindset as key drivers of success.
Before joining GDEcD, Martin led economic development in Cherokee County, one of metro Atlanta’s fastest-growing areas.
The state's economic development strategy focuses on job creation, smart expansion, and transformative investments from global companies including Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, Q CELLS, and Rivian.
Martin identifies three pillars giving Georgia its competitive edge: a skilled workforce, well-planned infrastructure, and a consistent business-friendly climate.
The Georgia Quick Start program is ranked the No. 1 workforce training program in the nation.
The Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG) experienced a 10.6% enrollment boost in 2024.
State programs like GEORGIA MATCH and Be Pro Be Proud Georgia help students connect with in-state higher education and training to strengthen the talent pipeline for employers.
The state is currently focused on building out entire supply chains, ranging from logistics providers to advanced manufacturers, recyclers, and R&D firms.
Billions have been allocated for recent infrastructure investments covering highway, airport, and freight corridor improvements.
GDEcD collaborates with local economic developers, utility partners, technical colleges, and employers to ensure each project aligns with regional goals.
Helping Georgia-grown businesses expand and innovate is a top priority for the state.
Georgia possesses two deepwater ports, the world’s busiest airport, and an integrated highway and rail system.

Martin stated: “We’ll accomplish our goals by delivering on our consistent and proven methods. Georgia’s collaborative approach and strong, solutions-based partnerships with our industries allow us to support their growth trajectories.”
source_title: Why Georgia Stays on Top: GDEcD's Misti Martin on Growth, Talent, and What’s Next - Augusta CEO

facts: Global fuel systems supplier TI Automotive Ltd. has agreed to be acquired by private equity firm Bain Capital LLC.
Sources told Bloomberg the acquisition deal is valued at $2.4 billion.
Bain will pay about six times earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (EBITDA).
TI’s EBITDA is approximately $400 million, giving the company a sale price of around $2.4 billion.
Talks between Bain and TI have been ongoing for more than a year.
The two sides previously tried to finalize a deal last January valuing TI at more than $2 billion.
Hedge funds owning TI delayed a vote on the sale to Bain last year to weigh a rival bid of roughly $2 billion from Pamplona Capital Management LLP.
The hedge funds that own TI, led by Oaktree and Duquesne Capital Management LLC, voted against a sale last year because they wanted a higher price.
The existing management team, including CEO Bill Kozyra, will continue to lead the company with no operational changes.
Frank Buscemi serves as the global communications director for TI Automotive and is based in suburban Detroit.
A source told Crain’s Detroit Business that CEO Bill Kozyra had a troubled relationship with Oaktree and other owners on the board, and the deal is likely a sigh of relief for him.
The deal is subject to approval by TI Automotive shareholders.
Closing is expected in mid-2015.

CEO Bill Kozyra stated that Bain Capital will invest in TI Automotive to grow the business and enable the team to focus solely on manufacturing high quality, safety critical fluid systems for customers.
Kozyra noted that Bain Capital brings a deep understanding of the business and a well-known track record of global success in the automotive industry.
A substantial majority of investors have pledged to support the deal, which is still pending regulatory review and other customary closing conditions.
TI traces its roots to 1919 and once supplied fuel lines to Ford Motor Co.’s Model T.
The company is chartered in Britain with a headquarters in Auburn Hills, Mich., and was bought by Oaktree and Duquesne Capital in a 2007 debt restructuring.
TI generated about $3 billion in revenue in 2013, with more than one-fourth coming from North America.
Bain manages about $80 billion in assets.
Bain's investments include Texas-based specialty retailer Michaels Stores Inc. and German auto-parts maker FTE Automotive GmbH.
source_title: TI Automotive to be acquired by Bain for $2.4 billion, report says - Automotive News

facts: Elkhart-based Dexter Axle has acquired the axle, brake and actuation business assets of Atlanta-based Tie Down Engineering Inc.
Financial terms of the deal were not disclosed.
A Dexter spokesman says the acquisition will create additional job opportunities in both Atlanta and Elkhart.
Dexter says Tie Down will continue operations through its manufactured housing, roofing, marine and industrial laser divisions.
Fred Bentley, chief executive officer of Dexter parent DexKo Global Inc., says the acquisition broadens the company’s aftermarket offerings and allows for further growth in adjacent areas.
Tie Down Engineering President Dean Samuelson will continue to lead the business following the acquisition.
Adam Dexter, chief executive officer of Dexter, says they are excited to add the Tie Down axle, brake & actuation business to their marine product offering to better serve customers.
Adam Dexter states Tie Down’s product offering perfectly complements Dexter’s prior acquisitions, providing a comprehensive product portfolio meeting all customer needs.
The deal follows Dexter’s acquisition of Texas-based trailer product manufacturer Rockwell American, which was announced in late August.
source_title: Dexter Acquires Portion of Georgia Company – Inside INdiana Business

facts: Solar panel manufacturer Qcells is expected to announce an expansion in Dalton plus a massive new plant northwest of Atlanta.
The expansion could be the largest clean energy manufacturing investment in U.S. history.
Qcells plans to build a new solar factory in Bartow County.
Qcells plans to develop a second facility near its existing Dalton plant.
The two projects combined are expected to bring roughly 2,600 jobs to the state.
Gov. Brian Kemp is expected to announce the project during the Georgia Chamber of Commerce’s annual “Eggs and Issues” breakfast on Wednesday.
Some details of the company’s plans are fluid and could change ahead of Wednesday’s planned unveiling.
Qcells is owned by South Korea-based Hanwha Solutions.
Qcells' first Georgia plant opened in 2019 near Dalton and employs roughly 750 workers.
Last year, the company began building a new, $171 million facility nearby capable of producing 1.4 gigawatts of Qcells’ solar panels each year.
When that expansion opens, it’s expected to create 470 jobs.
With the expected Wednesday announcement, the company appears set to more than double its Georgia-based manufacturing workforce.
The announcement is part of a flurry of green energy projects heading to Georgia.
In the past 13 months, EV upstart Rivian (Social Circle) and Hyundai Motor Group (Bryan County) announced plans for sprawling factories to build plug-in vehicles in Georgia.
Rivian and Hyundai have pledged collectively to employ more than 15,000 workers.
In November, Freyr Battery announced plans for a $2.6 billion battery plant in Coweta County.
Freyr will build massive utility-scale storage systems that use batteries to hold electricity from renewable sources like solar and wind.
Last month, Hyundai and SK announced a joint venture that plans a $4 billion to $5 billion battery assembly plant in Bartow County.
The Hyundai-SK joint venture will employ 3,500 workers.
The wave of clean energy investments began in late 2021 with Rivian.
President Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), passed last August, has supercharged clean energy investments in the state.
Georgia’s Democratic U.S. senators Jon Ossoff and Raphael Warnock were critical votes to pass the IRA.
Ossoff sponsored significant new solar provisions within the IRA.
The IRA provides $10 billion in tax credits for building new solar manufacturing facilities.

The IRA provides $30 billion for U.S. manufacturers to make components for solar panels, batteries, wind turbines and to process minerals needed for renewable energy technologies and electric vehicles.
The law extended a 30% income tax credit for rooftop solar for 10 years.
The law offers a credit for battery systems that store excess energy when the sun isn’t shining.
Many electric utilities are turning to solar as they shutter uneconomical coal-fired power plants and attempt to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
Georgia Power plans to increase its solar capacity by around 200% by 2035.
The IRA legislation offers investment and production tax credits for solar to companies like Georgia Power.
Companies have the potential to unlock even more savings if they use U.S.-made components.
Supply chain issues and trade disputes with China have slowed solar deployment in the U.S.
Most raw materials and other components needed to build solar panels are still manufactured abroad, particularly in China.
China holds more than an 80% share in all of the various materials needed to produce panels.
Democrats promised the IRA will help end the country’s reliance on China and bring jobs to the U.S.
A Qcells expansion would further cement economic ties between Georgia and South Korea.
Georgia opened its Seoul trade office in 1985.
Multiple Georgia governors have visited Korea, and in 2019, Kemp made South Korea his first overseas visit as governor.
In 2006, Kia Motors announced it would build its first U.S. factory in West Point near the Alabama border.
The Kia project mushroomed into thousands of jobs across not only the Kia plant but the campuses of dozens of suppliers.
The Georgia-Korea partnership kicked into overdrive when SK announced its first Peach State battery factory in Jackson County in 2018.
The announcement of the $5.54 billion Hyundai EV plant near the coast followed last May.
Today, Korea is the 10th largest export market for Georgia.
Georgia’s imports from Korea totaled $8.83 billion in 2021.
source_title: Solar panel maker Qcells expected to announce huge Georgia expansion

facts: Rivian now expects to borrow $4.5 billion from the Department of Energy for its Georgia plant, down from the $6.6 billion originally set aside.
Rivian is raising the Georgia plant's initial capacity to 300,000 vehicles from 200,000.
The Georgia plant will support production of the R2 SUV.
The R2 SUV is also moving toward production at Rivian's Illinois factory in Normal.
Rivian broke ground on the Georgia site outside Atlanta late last year.
The Georgia plant is currently in early vertical construction.
Vehicle production at the Georgia plant is slated to begin by the end of 2028.
Some of the Georgia plant's capacity will be reserved for Uber-branded R2 robotaxis.
Uber has agreed to make an initial $300 million investment in Rivian.
Uber expects to buy 10,000 fully autonomous R2 robotaxis ahead of a planned 2028 rollout in San Francisco and Miami.
The first Uber payment is expected to close in the second quarter.
A second $250 million Uber investment is due later this year.
Uber has an option to buy up to 40,000 more autonomous R2 SUVs starting in 2030.
Uber could invest as much as $1.25 billion through 2031 if Rivian clears a set of milestones.
Rivian recently started R2 production at its Illinois factory in Normal despite tornado damage at the plant.
Rivian has made initial R2 deliveries to employees.
Customer deliveries for the R2 SUV are expected to begin in the coming weeks.
Rivian reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $1.38 billion, including $908 million from vehicle sales and $473 million from software and services.
Automotive revenue fell about 2% from a year earlier, partly due to a drop in regulatory credits.
Rivian's net loss narrowed to $416 million in the first quarter of 2026, down from $541 million in the same period last year.
source_title: Rivian trims DOE loan as Georgia plant gets bigger - forgeeks

facts: Georgia Institute of Technology received a $65 million grant from the U.S. Department of Commerce’s Economic Development Administration for the Georgia AI Manufacturing (GA-AIM) Technology Corridor.
The grant was announced by President Joe Biden on September 2, 2022.
The funding is part of the $1 billion Build Back Better Regional Challenge (BBB) competition under the Investing in America’s Communities initiative within the American Rescue Plan Act of 2021.
Georgia Tech was selected as one of 21 Phase II awardees in the BBB competition.
The largest of nine projects within the GA-AIM corridor will transform the Advanced Manufacturing Pilot Facility (AMPF) into the Artificial Intelligence Manufacturing Pilot Facility (AI-MPF).
The AMPF is located on 14th Street, about a mile north of Georgia Tech's campus off Hemphill Avenue.
The facility currently spans 24,000 square feet and will more than double in size following the grant.

AMPF serves as a flagship component of the Georgia Tech Manufacturing Institute (GTMI) and functions as a testbed for scaling basic research into implementable technologies coupled with education and workforce training.
Research fields at AMPF include additive/hybrid manufacturing, composites, digital manufacturing/Industry 4.0, and industrial robotics.

Aaron Stebner, an associate professor in the George W. Woodruff School of Mechanical Engineering, outlined a plan in his 2019 cover letter to establish Georgia Tech as a world leader in additive manufacturing of solid materials (ceramics & metals) R&D, fusing data sciences and AI.
Stebner predicted the goal would take 10-15 years but noted it is being realized two years after his arrival on campus.

Stebner stated that AMPF has been a shell waiting for a vision like Build Back Better to fill it out, and the transformation aims to create one of the nation's first manufacturing labs designed for autonomy to help the state and nation become world AI manufacturing leaders.

Prior to Georgia Tech, Stebner built an additive manufacturing technology center and a public-private innovation consortium at the Colorado School of Mines, working with Colorado companies to integrate machine learning.
Stebner identified that bottlenecks in using AI in additive manufacturing stem from inconsistent human processes in acquiring and processing data rather than AI technologies themselves.
He emphasized that computers and machines excel at consistent repetition without boredom or creativity-driven deviations, contrasting them with human operators.
Stebner argued the country greatly underutilizes machine learning in manufacturing, comparing current usage to using a supercomputer for handheld calculator tasks.

He advocated for a new mindset mimicking supercomputers making unfathomable calculations and stated that a facility autonomously creating and curating AI data is necessary for manufacturing innovation leaps.

Stebner noted it is too early and high-risk for the federal government to establish a national autonomous manufacturing lab, positioning Georgia and Georgia Tech as ideal for a first prototype with AI-MPF.
Manufacturing is Georgia's second-leading employer behind real estate, with diverse sectors including defense, food, timber, and energy spread throughout the state.
Georgia Tech produces approximately 4% of the nation's mechanical engineers.

Devesh Ranjan, Eugene C. Gwaltney, Jr. School Chair at the Woodruff School, stated the school prides itself on being an inclusive, innovative, and thriving educational and research environment, and the grant will enable researchers to collaborate for a better local, national, and global future.
Tom Kurfess serves as GA-AIM's regional economic competitiveness officer and GTMI's executive director.

Kurfess recently returned to campus after working on leave as chief manufacturing officer at Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) from 2019-2021, where he handled strategic planning for advanced manufacturing.
Kurfess previously led the advanced manufacturing team at the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy for the Obama administration from 2012-2013.
Kurfess holds the HUSCO/Ramirez Distinguished Chair in Fluid Power and Motion Control and is a professor in the Woodruff School.

He highlighted that industry is growing rapidly in Atlanta and the southeast, with Georgia Tech researchers studying machines, robots, manufacturing plants, and the entire supply chain to integrate technology, people, and privacy via GA-AIM.

Raheem Beyah, Dean of the College of Engineering and Southern Company Chair, noted that workforce development sometimes takes a backseat to research but GA-AIM provides funding to tie workforce development with AI research to create a new generation of engineers and technicians for Georgia.
GA-AIM aims to revolutionize the state's industrial economy by developing and deploying talent and innovation in AI across all manufacturing sectors, with transforming AMPF being just one component.
The initiative aligns with Georgia Tech's strategic focus on expanding access and empowering people of all backgrounds and life stages.
Women currently comprise 18% of Georgia's manufacturing workforce, with GA-AIM planning to improve this to 23% over the next five years.
Black workers currently make up 6% of the state's manufacturing workforce, with a target of 15% over the next five years.
Additional initiatives will reskill Georgia workers across all demographics, with special focus on rural and distressed counties.

Kurfess emphasized addressing the national need for a workforce with greater technology and manufacturing capability, stating it is Georgia Tech's responsibility to upskill mid-career workers facing technology disrupters.

Tim Brown, managing director of Georgia Tech’s Supply Chain and Logistics Institute (SCL), is tasked with developing executive education and youth- and minority-focused programs to upskill workers around Georgia using GA-AIM.
Brown will divide the Peach State into four regions to capitalize on each area's manufacturing strengths and needs.
Savannah's regional focus will be on shipping and distribution operations, along with reskilling veterans and military families from nearby bases.
Albany's priorities will center on training military families and community members to stimulate the local economy, leveraging its proximity to the Marine Corps Logistics Base.
Dalton's focus will target the flooring industry, while Gainesville area initiatives will cover poultry-related industries and the expanding advanced manufacturing community in northeast Georgia.
Over the next five years, Brown and his team will hold virtual sessions and travel to each region to help companies and employees test new processes and learn emerging technologies.

Brown described the initiative as creating change agents and exposing middle managers to technology to improve manufacturing and supply chain resilience, develop business cases for change, and sell executives on technology-enabled investments.
The team's role in the Build Back Better initiative includes examining logistical flows and planning approaches alongside manufacturing processes to improve overall supply chain resilience.

Brown will start with 25 students and four certificate courses per region, totaling 100 people per year, and will establish industry councils in each region to develop pilot projects for state manufacturers.

A second outreach initiative led by Woodruff School senior research engineer Roxanne Moore will focus on inventiveness and entrepreneurship in K-12 schools and expand partnerships with technical colleges and minority-serving institutions.

This project aims to teach up to 1,000 students and 100 teachers from underserved areas, focusing on rural communities via Georgia Tech programs, nonprofits, the Technical College System of Georgia, Southwest Georgia Regional Commission, local manufacturers, and K-12 school leaders.
Georgia Tech’s K-12 InVenture Prize education program and competition will expand offerings through partnerships with GoSTEM to better serve Latino populations.

GA-AIM will support an existing high school entrepreneurship program in Fitzgerald and work with Albany State University to host teacher workshops, support local schools, and host regional competitions focusing on inventiveness and entrepreneurial mindset.

Moore stated the goal is to illustrate relationships between innovation, entrepreneurship, and manufacturing so students see how ideas come to life and improve communities, hoping regional ecosystems become role models for expanding high-quality STEM access to diverse students.

The $65 million grant comes 21 years after the groundbreaking of Tech Square, which grew from 3 acres to 13 acres and now houses the highest density of startups, corporate innovators, and research centers in the Southeast.

Kurfess sees similarities between AMPF's location off 14th Street and Hemphill Avenue and Tech Square's expansion, noting AMPF is already surrounded by local businesses, student housing, and accessible via Georgia Tech’s Stinger bus system.
He predicted that in five or ten years, AMPF will become a societal juggernaut similar to Tech Square, prompting people to look back at the moment as a crazy time at Georgia Tech.
GA-AIM has the capability to expand Georgia Tech's impact on the state beyond its research institute role.
Stebner noted that rural companies often take years to fill machine vision jobs due to an inability to train local workers and reluctance of Atlanta residents to relocate.
He stated the state needs people in rural areas and rural communities need them, with GA-AIM creating programs that train the trainer where they live.

On campus, the focus on creating an autonomy-capable facility at AI-MPF gives Georgia Tech a national and world-leading resource to create breakthroughs in AI usage and manufacturing advancement over the next several decades or longer.

The College of Engineering GA-AIM Team includes Faisal Alamgir (MSE), Tim Brown (ISYE), Tequila Harris (ME), Surya Kalidindi (ME), Joshua Kacher (MSE), Tom Kurfess (ME), Kim Kurtis (CEE), Matthew McDowell (ME), Benoit Montreuil (ISYE), Cary Ogletree (ME), Chris Saldana (ME), Emily Sanders (ME), Steven Sheffield (ME), Ryan Sherman (CEE), Lauren Stewart (CEE), Manos Tentzeris (ECE), Chelsea White (ISYE), Saman Zonouz (ECE), and Chuck Zhang (ISYE).
source_title: Building Georgia with AI and Manufacturing | George W. Woodruff School of Mechanical Engineering

facts: Intercalation released a new documentary exploring why U.S. battery manufacturing lags behind China and what it will take to catch up.
Since 2020, roughly $100 billion has poured into the American battery industry sector.
The path to commercial-scale production at gigawatt-hour capacity remains blocked for most companies.
One company stands as the lone exception breaking through what the industry calls the "valley of death."
The industry landscape features promising laboratory breakthroughs that are shadowed by shuttered factories and failed ventures.
Chinese manufacturers have cemented their control over the global battery market.
U.S. policy support for the battery sector remains uncertain under the current administration.
A determined cohort of entrepreneurs, researchers, and investors continues developing innovations in battery chemistry and manufacturing processes to signal an American resurgence.
Support networks have formed to help these ventures navigate the path to commercialization.
Intercalation collaborated with Rahul Dhiman and Spiral Studios on a documentary examining California's battery innovation ecosystem.
The film poses the critical question of whether American ingenuity can translate into manufacturing competitiveness on the global stage.

Six industry leaders share their perspectives in the documentary: Kara Rodby (Investor, Volta Energy Technologies), Jonathan Tan (CEO, Coreshell), Brenna Teigler (Co-Founder and CEO, Electrochemistry Foundry), Richard Wang (CEO, Voya Energy; Co-Founder, Electrochemistry Foundry; former CEO, Cuberg), Vivas Kumar (CEO, Mitra Chem), and Asma Sharafi (CTO, Ensurge Micropower).
The documentary runs just over 20 minutes.
It examines how China achieved its lithium-ion battery dominance, analyzes American successes and setbacks, and explores emerging opportunities for the U.S. battery sector.
Several major U.S. battery gigafactories are scheduled to come online in 2025-2026.
source_title: America is Losing the Battery Race: Documentary Explores Path

facts: State Representative Mike Cameron (R-Rossville) commends Trenton Pressing on the completion of its $12 million expansion.
The expansion enhances manufacturing operations in Dade County by 100,000 square feet.
Rep. Mike Cameron stated: “The Southeast United States has become a magnet for automotive brands and suppliers in the last two decades, and as is the case in so many ways, Georgia leads by example.”

Rep. Mike Cameron stated: “By investing in our workforce and identifying ways to make doing business better here, we continue to see great companies like Trenton Pressing create high-quality jobs and invest in Georgia.”
The 100,000-square-foot expansion covers manufacturing and assembly operations at Trenton Pressing.
The growth enhances Trenton Pressing’s ability to supply high-quality components to its clients.
Clients include brands such as BMW, Club Car, and ZF.
Trenton Pressing is now one of the leading employers in Dade County.
The company has a workforce exceeding 200 employees.
Trenton Pressing specializes in metal stamping, welding processes, and complex assemblies.
The company provides comprehensive turnkey contract manufacturing solutions along with warehousing and shipping services.
Services cater to both automotive and non-automotive clients.
source_title: Rep. Mike Cameron commends Trenton Pressing's $12 million expansion in Dade County - Peach Tree Times

facts: Trenton Pressing is a manufacturer located in Georgia, USA.
The company was previously struggling with a high scrap rate and an on-time delivery rate of only 60%.
Trenton Pressing partnered with the Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership (GAMEP) to implement a series of process improvements and technology upgrades.
These interventions dramatically reduced Trenton Pressing's scrap rate by 60%.
The company improved its on-time delivery rate from 60% to over 90%.
These operational improvements set the company up for sustained growth.
source_title: Decoding Tomorrow: The Way Forward: #1 Manufacturing – “The most valuable part of a modern manufacturing operation is the part you can’t see: the algorithms that optimize flow, the data that predicts failure, and the agility that makes the entire process intelligent.”

facts: Trenton Pressing, a tier one manufacturing supplier located in Dade County, Ga., broke ground today on a $10 million, 105,000-square-foot facility.
The additional space will allow for expanded manufacturing and assembly services and significantly increases the organization’s manufacturing capacity.
Hans Pfunder, chief operating officer for Trenton Pressing, said the company is extremely grateful for Dade County’s support throughout this expansion process.

In just over two years, Trenton Pressing purchased a struggling manufacturing company and completely turned it around through a reorganization of management, technology and equipment upgrades and personnel training.
The expanded facility will allow the company to better service its growth and meet the manufacturing needs of its clients which include BMW, Club Car, ZF and more.

Pfunder noted that it was exciting to see the tremendous growth over the past two years after the reorganization process, stating that the achievement speaks to the dedication and commitment of employees.
Trenton Pressing is currently one of the top five employers in Dade County with 220 employees.
The company plans to add 20 more jobs with the facility expansion.
Trenton Pressing prides itself on building an empowering, transparent and safe working environment for its employees.

Dade County Executive Ted Rumley stated that Trenton Pressing has been a great community partner since it began operations in the county in 2021, providing jobs and supporting schools and the environment.
Construction on the new facility is expected to be completed by December of 2023.

Trenton Mayor Alex Case stated that Trenton Pressing has been a great asset to the city since arriving just over two years ago, providing needed jobs and helping improve the quality of life for citizens.
Trenton Pressing provides metal stamping processes, welding and assembly processes, warehousing and shipping.

Jeff Mullis, president and CEO of the Northwest Georgia Joint Development Agency and retired Georgia state senator, congratulated Trenton Pressing on its expansion efforts and commended it for adding jobs in the Northwest Georgia area.
source_title: Trenton Pressing breaks ground on new $10 million manufacturing facility | Local News | local3news.com

facts: The Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership (GaMEP) is a program of the Enterprise Innovation Institute at Georgia Tech.
GaMEP received recognition from Gov. Brian P. Kemp at the Georgia State Capitol for 65 years of service to the manufacturing industry.
GaMEP leverages expertise and resources to advance manufacturing and economic prosperity across Georgia.
The manufacturing industry adds $82 billion to the Georgia economy and employs 425,000 residents, according to the National Association of Manufacturers.
By 1960, more than 4,500 manufacturers had established operations across Georgia due to strategic efforts by state leaders with economic development assistance from Georgia Tech.
The Georgia General Assembly voted to establish the Georgia Tech Industrial Extension Service (now GaMEP) to provide technical support to manufacturers facing new challenges.
GaMEP created statewide field offices that link industry and innovation, delivering on-site technical expertise to help manufacturers thrive.
Tim Israel serves as GaMEP director and EI2 associate vice president for corporate engagement/firm-based programs.
In 2024, GaMEP generated an impressive $294 in return for every state dollar allocated to manufacturing projects.
Over the past decade, GaMEP provided assistance and education to more than 3,900 manufacturers across 144 counties.
During that same decade, GaMEP helped manufacturers create or retain 14,500 jobs.
Over the past decade, GaMEP helped manufacturers invest $1 billion in capital improvements.
Over the past decade, GaMEP helped manufacturers realize $3.5 billion in sales.
Over the past decade, GaMEP helped manufacturers save nearly $450 million in costs.
GaMEP primarily serves small- to medium-sized manufacturers, with 75% employing fewer than 250 workers.
GaMEP's top-served industries include fabricated metal products, food, machinery, and chemical and transportation equipment manufacturing.
David Bridges serves as EI2 vice president.
GaMEP's reach spans 10 regions across the state, each led by a dedicated region manager who lives and works locally.
Lloyd Avram is the CEO and president of the Georgia Association of Manufacturers.

In the Coastal Region (counties: Bryan, Bulloch, Camden, Chatham, Effingham, Glynn, Liberty, Long, McIntosh, Screven), GaMEP served 269 manufacturers, created/retained 283 jobs, facilitated $23,171,292 in capital improvements, saved $1,645,061 in costs, realized $13,965,000 in sales, and supported success story Roger Wood Foods.

In the East Region (counties: Burke, Columbia, Glascock, Hancock, Jefferson, Jenkins, Lincoln, McDuffie, Richmond, Taliaferro, Warren, Washington, Wilkes), GaMEP served 169 manufacturers, created/retained 3,899 jobs, facilitated $127,754,280 in capital improvements, saved $14,771,582 in costs, realized $975,465,000 in sales, and supported success story Nutritional Resources.

In the East Central Region (counties: Barrow, Clarke, Elbert, Greene, Jackson, Jasper, Madison, Morgan, Newton, Oconee, Oglethorpe, Walton), GaMEP served 209 manufacturers, created/retained 621 jobs, facilitated $19,703,035 in capital improvements, saved $2,535,494 in costs, realized $29,486,000 in sales, and supported success stories Creature Comforts Brewing Company and Poly Tech Industries.

In the Metro Atlanta Region (counties: Cherokee, Clayton, Cobb, DeKalb, Douglas, Fayette, Fulton, Gwinnett, Henry, Rockdale), GaMEP served 1,601 manufacturers, created/retained 2,928 jobs, facilitated $235,763,480 in capital improvements, saved $112,083,262 in costs, realized $844,679,890 in sales, and supported success stories Compass Technology Group, Construction Specialties, and Highland Forge.

In the Middle Region (counties: Baldwin, Bibb, Crawford, Houston, Jones, Monroe, Peach, Pulaski, Putnam, Twiggs, Wilkinson), GaMEP served 170 manufacturers, created/retained 972 jobs, facilitated $121,814,846 in capital improvements, saved $8,810,950 in costs, realized $300,213,400 in sales, and supported success story Unified Defense.

In the Northeast Region (counties: Banks, Dawson, Forsyth, Franklin, Habersham, Hall, Hart, Lumpkin, Rabun, Stephens, Towns, Union, White), GaMEP served 280 manufacturers, created/retained 1,029 jobs, facilitated $88,443,395 in capital improvements, saved $63,999,228 in costs, realized $259,453,900 in sales, and supported success story Freudenberg Sealing Technologies.

In the Northwest Region (counties: Bartow, Catoosa, Chattooga, Dade, Fannin, Floyd, Gilmer, Gordon, Haralson, Murray, Paulding, Pickens, Polk, Walker, Whitfield), GaMEP served 387 manufacturers, created/retained 1,090 jobs, facilitated $92,948,931 in capital improvements, saved $141,460,651 in costs, realized $326,366,408 in sales, and supported success story Trenton Pressing.

In the South Region (counties: Appling, Bleckley, Candler, Dodge, Emanuel, Evans, Jeff Davis, Johnson, Laurens, Montgomery, Tattnall, Telfair, Toombs, Treutlen, Wayne, Wheeler, Wilcox), GaMEP served 176 manufacturers, created/retained 969 jobs, facilitated $219,300,221 in capital improvements, saved $6,596,254 in costs, realized $39,632,275 in sales, and supported success story Healthy Pet.

In the Southeast Region (counties: Atkinson, Bacon, Ben Hill, Berrien, Brantley, Brooks, Charlton, Clinch, Coffee, Cook, Echols, Irwin, Lanier, Lowndes, Pierce, Tift, Turner, Ware), GaMEP served 166 manufacturers, created/retained 281 jobs, facilitated $20,684,800 in capital improvements, saved $3,098,700 in costs, realized $77,724,500 in sales, and supported success stories CJB Industries and South Georgia Pecan.

In the Southwest Region (counties: Baker, Calhoun, Colquitt, Decatur, Dougherty, Early, Grady, Lee, Miller, Mitchell, Seminole, Terrell, Thomas, Worth), GaMEP served 130 manufacturers, created/retained 130 jobs, facilitated $12,266,730 in capital improvements, saved $1,682,790 in costs, realized $5,002,020 in sales, and supported success story Sweet Grass Dairy.

In the West Region (counties: Chattahoochee, Clay, Crisp, Dooly, Harris, Macon, Marion, Muscogee, Quitman, Randolph, Schley, Stewart, Sumter, Talbot, Taylor, Webster), GaMEP served 111 manufacturers, created/retained 713 jobs, facilitated $37,948,131 in capital improvements, saved $3,197,600 in costs, realized $69,588,348 in sales, and supported success story Oneda Corporation.

In the West Central Region (counties: Butts, Carroll, Coweta, Heard, Lamar, Meriwether, Pike, Spalding, Troup, Upson), GaMEP served 234 manufacturers, created/retained 1,658 jobs, facilitated $90,750,763 in capital improvements, saved $89,931,074 in costs, realized $615,900,002 in sales, and supported success stories Complete Truck Bodies and Mountville Mills.
GaMEP is a member of the U.S. Department of Commerce National Institute of Standards and Technology Manufacturing Extension Partnership.
GaMEP has advanced manufacturing and economic prosperity in Georgia since 1960.
source_title: Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership at Georgia Tech Celebrates 65 Years of Service | News Center

facts: Plug Power Inc. is a global leader in the hydrogen economy and is building an end-to-end green hydrogen ecosystem from production, storage, and delivery to energy generation.
2024 marked a pivotal commercial inflection point for Plug as it advanced its hydrogen generation platform and scaled new product offerings such as electrolyzer solutions.

In response to evolving market conditions in 2024, Plug streamlined its workforce, consolidated facilities, increased pricing on certain offerings, reduced working capital, and reprioritized hydrogen and new product investments to improve margins and cash flows.

Plug initiated Project Quantum Leap, which includes additional workforce reductions, rooftop facility consolidations, discretionary spending cuts, inventory reduction, and limiting capital expenditures to near-term critical requirements.
Project Quantum Leap is targeted to reduce annual expenses in the range of $150 million to $200 million.
In the fourth quarter of 2024, Plug reported revenue of $191.5 million, reflecting electrolyzer deployment growth and hydrogen network expansion.
Customer warrant charges for the fourth quarter of 2024 totaled $22.7 million, driven by updated forecasts and customer program timing.
Operating cash flow improved by 25% quarter-over-quarter and 46% year-over-year in the fourth quarter of 2024, with full-year 2024 burn improving 34% compared to 2023.
Capital expenditures were down by 56% quarter-over-quarter and 52% for the full year of 2024 compared to 2023 due to limiting incremental investments.

Plug reported a gross margin loss of 122% in the fourth quarter of 2024, including $104.2 million in inventory valuation adjustments linked to a strategic slowdown in mobility, high-power stationary applications, and other products.
For the full year of 2024, results included an estimated reduced amount of labor, overhead, and outside services of approximately $42 million from targeted cost reductions.

In the fourth quarter of 2024, Plug recorded $971.3 million in non-cash charges for asset impairments and bad debt provision, which will reduce future depreciation and amortization by an estimated $55 million to $60 million in 2025.
Electrolyzer revenue increased 583% year-over-year in the fourth quarter of 2024, driven by 5-megawatt system sales and a large-scale order deployment.
Plug announced a purchase agreement with Allied Green Ammonia to supply three gigawatts of electrolyzer capacity for a green hydrogen-to-ammonia plant in Australia.
To date, Plug has secured more than 8 GW in global Basic Engineering and Design Package contracts.
A joint venture hydrogen plant with Olin Corporation in Louisiana is on track for full operation with final commissioning nearly complete.
The Louisiana plant expansion is expected to increase Plug's hydrogen nameplate network capacity to over 39 tons per day.
The expanded hydrogen network will provide vertically integrated hydrogen solutions to partners including Amazon and Walmart.

Plug anticipates its material handling business to grow approximately 10-20% year-over-year, with a major customer placing an approximate $10 million order in the fourth quarter of 2024 positioning over $200 million in equipment opportunities.
Plug closed 2024 with over $200 million in unrestricted cash on its balance sheet, with cash burn expected to continue significantly improving in 2025.
Plug completed the transfer of approximately $30 million in energy storage investment tax credits related to a hydrogen liquefier at its hydrogen generation plant in Woodbine, GA.
The company plans to leverage a similar ITC strategy for its Louisiana and Texas hydrogen plants and pursue sales of other asset ITC opportunities.
Plug closed the $1.66 billion Department of Energy Loan Guarantee program, having invested over $250 million to date with an estimated additional required investment of approximately $600 million.
The DOE loan is targeted to cover approximately $400 million of the additional investment, with the remainder to be covered through project finance and equity investors.
Construction of the DOE-funded project is expected to take approximately 18 months once the EPC contractor is mobilized.
CEO Andy Marsh stated that hydrogen plays a crucial role in energy diversification and contributes to economic growth and job creation, and Plug remains committed to scaling its solutions.
Plug has deployed more than 72,000 fuel cell systems and over 275 fueling stations globally and is the largest buyer of liquid hydrogen.
Plug plans to operate a green hydrogen highway across North America and Europe and built a state-of-the-art Gigafactory to produce electrolyzers and fuel cells.
The company delivers green hydrogen solutions directly to customers and through joint venture partners in material handling, e-mobility, power generation, and industrial applications.
source_title: Plug Power Inc. | Plug Power Announces Continued Cash Flow Improvement and Progress on Path to Profitability

facts: Rivian announced a major revision to its manufacturing plans for its future facility in Stanton Springs North, Georgia.
Initial annual production capacity increased by 50%, from 200,000 to 300,000 vehicles.
The move compresses what had previously been a two-phase build-out into a single, larger first phase.
The restructured approach will lower cost per unit while offering meaningful flexibility for further expansion in subsequent phases.
Announcement comes alongside a renegotiated loan with the US Department of Energy's Office (DOE) of Energy Dominance Financing.
Updated agreement is valued at up to $4.5 billion, comprising $4,006m in principal and $494m in capitalised interest.
Loan amount reduced from the $6.6 billion conditional commitment finalised in January 2025.
Revised loan is aligned with the updated single-phase facility design.
Company expects to make its first draw on the loan by early 2027, a full year ahead of the prior schedule.
Vertical construction at the Stanton Springs North site set to begin this spring.
Preparations already underway for development of the stamping press area.
Stamping press area described as one of the most capital-intensive and technically demanding elements of the plant from a construction perspective.
Pace of activity expected to accelerate through summer as facility's primary buildings take shape.
Vehicle production remains on track to commence in late 2028.
Georgia plant will anchor production of vehicles on Rivian's all-new midsize platform, with the R2 SUV leading output.
Platform offers three powertrain configurations: single-motor rear-wheel drive, dual-motor all-wheel drive, and tri-motor.
New 4695 cell will underpin both battery size options.
Larger pack estimated to deliver over 300 miles of range.
Vehicle features an 11-camera, five-radar autonomous perception stack.
R2 and its production line were designed in concert, not in sequence.
Rivian built a digital twin of the plant to develop and test the production system virtually while the vehicle was still being finalised.
Parallel development process allowed team to optimise entire vehicle lifecycle from initial assembly through to long-term serviceability.
Assembly process is leaner at every stage due to designing vehicle and factory together.
Rivian eliminated 2.3 miles of wiring from the R2’s electronics architecture.
Reduced part complexity through extensive use of large, high-pressure die castings.
Castings removed thousands of welds and fasteners from assembly process.
Cut build time and reduced overall vehicle mass by around 2,000 lbs compared with the R1.
Structural battery unit included, where top of pack serves as vehicle floor, further consolidating components and simplifying build.
Fully automated hang-on system in body shop ensures precise gap and flush alignment across all exterior panels.
AI-powered robot handles part scanning and placement without manual input.
Lineside wireless diagnostics identify and correct faults in real time.
Advanced vision robots carry out precision quality checks at each stage of assembly.
AI algorithms continuously optimise floor plan utilisation and deploy team members where needed most.
Production line design allows for 155,000 R2 units annually at existing Normal, Illinois facility.
Georgia plant will not be limited to consumer R2 production.
Rivian's recently announced partnership with Uber will see up to 50,000 R2-based robotaxis built at the facility.
Robotaxi production also beginning in late 2028.
Initial autonomous deployments planned for San Francisco and Miami.
Expansion to 25 cities expected by 2031.
Robotaxi programme layers high-volume autonomous vehicle manufacturing stream on top of consumer production line.
Any future expansion beyond restructured first phase will be funded by Rivian rather than through DOE loan facility.
Company secured larger plant for less financing than original deal required, with earlier access to capital.

Quote from company: "R2 dramatically expands our market opportunity. The thousands of dedicated people who will soon work in our Georgia plant will be instrumental to Rivian’s growth as we scale American manufacturing."
source_title: Rivian boosts Georgia EV plant capacity to 300,000 vehicles with optimised phase-one plan

facts: The news announcement was published on October 25, 2022.
Valeo showcased its latest automotive innovations at the 2022 AAPEX Show in Booth #A1642.
Highlights of Valeo's showcase included new product launches in Electrical Systems, Transmission Systems, and Wiping Systems.
Valeo displayed an award-winning Pendulum Dual Mass Flywheel Gen. 2 at the event.

By investing heavily in innovation, Valeo strives for its position at the epicenter of the three revolutions redefining the automotive industry: electrification, autonomous vehicles, and digital mobility.

Alongside innovating for future cars, Valeo continues to provide cutting-edge automotive technologies for replacement parts and the aftermarket for customer vehicles long after they leave the production floor.
During the three-day event, Valeo introduced visitors to its latest launches across Electrical Systems, Transmission Systems, and Wiping Systems product lines.
Valeo debuted its comprehensive range of hydraulic systems through its clutch expertise.
Valeo's hydraulic system capabilities were expanded by its acquisition of industry specialist FTE Automotive in October 2017.
Valeo presents its visibility excellence through an exclusive distribution partnership with global leader PIAA established in April 2021.

Valeo showcased at the launch of the newest addition to the “Joe’s Garage” experience at AAPEX 2022, a real-world shop setup where exhibiting brands provide demonstrations and real-time training across eight service bays.
In the Product Display area at Joe’s Garage, Valeo displayed an array of powertrain, wiping systems, thermal systems, and lighting products.
Valeo Group won a 2022 Automotive News PACE (Premier Automotive Suppliers' Contribution to Excellence) Award for its Pendulum Dual Mass Flywheel Gen. 2.
The Pendulum Dual Mass Flywheel Gen. 2 reduces noise, vibration, and harshness associated with the start and stop mechanism of hybrid vehicles.
This technology is essential for hybrid vehicle performance, providing a strong filtration system to maintain both longevity and comfort within the vehicle.
Valeo has won 13 total Automotive News PACE Awards.
In 2021, Valeo won a PACE Award for its 48V eAccess.
In 2020, Valeo won a PACE Award for its Valeo XtraVue™ Trailer.
In 2018, Valeo won a PACE Award for its Valeo SCALA®and R-744 HVAC system.
In 2016, Valeo won a PACE Award for its Electric Supercharger and Water-cooled Condenser.
In 2015, Valeo won a PACE Award for its EG Efficiency Alternator.
In 2014, Valeo won a PACE Award for its Back-Over Protection System.
In 2013, Valeo won a PACE Award for its Air Intake Module.
In 2012, Valeo won a PACE Award for its AquaBlade®/VisioBlade® wiper system.
In 2008, Valeo won a PACE Award for its Park4U® park assist system.
In 2007, Valeo won a PACE Award for its Multi-Beam Radar blind spot detection system.
In 2006, Valeo won a PACE Award for its StARS micro-hybrid system.
In 2005, Valeo won a PACE Award for its LaneVue™ lane departure warning system.
Valeo reaches beyond delivering quality products by focusing on enriching customers’ experience through a variety of supply chain solutions, technical support, and marketing specialties.
Valeo's booth was located on Level 2 at the Sands Expo Center.

facts: Valeo offers an Organosheet Battery Casing for PHEVs using thermoplastic composite for lightweighting and reinforced mechanical and thermal battery protection.
The casing is up to 20% lighter than a steel baseline, making it the lightest solution on the market.
The material used is 100% recyclable.
The product has a low carbon footprint of 1.4 to 3.1 CO2eq./kg.
It provides optimum thermal insulation.
Easy function integration is enabled by plastic overmolding.
The casing is constructed from glass-fiber resin pre-impregnated thermoplastic composite.
The design meets crash compliance standards and is corrosion resistant.
Thermal insulation performance ranges from 0.4 to 0.6 W/m.K.
It is fire resistant (UL94 V0), withstanding temperatures above 1000°C for more than 5 minutes.
Manufacturing utilizes a One Shot/NetShape process.
The product is validated for mass production at a capacity of 300,000 units per year.
It provides lightweight and strong properties with a strength of 400-500M Pa.
The casing can be up to 40% lighter than metal solutions.
Compatible vehicle types include passenger cars, buses, trucks, robot taxis, motorcycles, bikes, droids, and off-road vehicles.

facts: The report analyzes the automotive battery market with a specific focus on Valeo’s 48V electrification systems and high-tech thermal management solutions for the 2026 fiscal period.
Market scope covers the Tier-1 automotive supplier landscape, focusing on low-voltage (48V) electrification, ADAS-integrated power systems, and thermal management.
Geographic focus remains on Europe (44% of Valeo sales) and Asia, particularly China (32%), which is shifting toward technology expansion.
Current status is as of early 2026, with revenue projections and technology forecasts extending through 2027.
48V mild-hybrid systems are prioritized due to their role as a cost-effective CO2 reduction path as full EV adoption pace stabilized in 2025.
Analysis prioritizes segments where Valeo holds a dominant market share (~40% in 48V systems) and where high-margin technology integration (software/electronics) is replacing commodity hardware.
The 2026 automotive market is characterized by a "technology expansion" phase, moving away from pure volume scaling.
Themes in the battery and electrification sector include high-tech thermal systems to extend EV range and cost-efficient 48V mild-hybrid architectures.
48V Mild-Hybrid Systems target Global OEMs (Legacy & EV), showing high market performance with ~40% global share; demand increased as EV transition stabilized.
VoC themes for 48V systems include cost-effective CO2 reduction and ease of integration.
Thermal Management Solutions target EV Manufacturers, showing strong performance with 78% of 2025 cooling revenues from liquid-based systems.
VoC themes for thermal management include range improvement (up to 20%) and cold-weather performance.
ADAS Sensing Kits target Premium/Mid-market OEMs, showing dominant performance with ~33% global sensing penetration and high-margin electronics focus.
VoC themes for ADAS kits include safety compliance and Level 2+/3 autonomy support.
48V Mild-Hybrid Battery Systems are integrated low-voltage systems designed for OEMs to meet emissions standards without the cost of full high-voltage electrification.
Trend status for 48V systems is Mature/Growing, seeing a "second wind" in 2026 as a practical mid-term solution for mass-market vehicles.
Valeo leads with ~40% market share; 48V demand rose in 2025 as full EV adoption pace stabilized.
48V systems are positioned as a cost-effective alternative to high-voltage systems, usually sold via long-term Tier-1 supply contracts.
Competitors for 48V systems include Bosch (Deep ADAS and powertrain reach), Denso (Electrification dominance in Asia), and Continental (Strong sensor/ADAS portfolio).
Channel-fit for 48V systems is OEM direct sales; modular manufacturing allows scale advantages and lower unit costs.
48V systems meet Euro 7 and tightening CO2 targets in North America and China.
Risks include geopolitical supply-chain regionalization and margin exposure during EV market fluctuations.
High-Performance EV Thermal Management includes integrated battery, motor, and cabin thermal management systems.
Trend status for thermal management is Emerging/Growing with a rapid shift toward liquid-based cooling solutions.
Liquid-cooled systems projected to account for 78% of 2025 segment revenues; Valeo's system improves EV range by up to 20% in cold weather.
Thermal management is high-margin system integration, sold as complete platforms rather than individual components.
Competitors for thermal management include Denso (Global leader in thermal), ZF (Integrated chassis-electrification), and Chinese firms like CATL (moving into skateboard chassis).
Channel-fit involves strategic partnerships with EV leaders; showcased at major tech events like CES 2026.
Thermal management is critical for meeting range transparency requirements and safety standards (IP67 for liquid systems).
Risks include rapidly evolving battery chemistries requiring frequent R&D pivots.
LiFePO4 Low-Speed Vehicle Packs cover 48V-72V lithium iron phosphate battery packs for low-speed electric vehicles, golf carts, and forklifts.
Trend status is Growing in industrial and niche mobility sectors.
Evidence shows high volume of specialized industrial listings and a shift away from lead-acid to LiFePO4 for long cycle life.
Pricing ranges from $135 (small packs) to $3,500+ (traction batteries).
Competitors include specialized battery manufacturers like Lithmate and CTS, with increasing competition from Chinese battery firms.
Channel-fit is wholesale (Alibaba) and B2B industrial supply chains.
Compliance includes CE/PSE certifications and IP67 waterproofing for outdoor use.
Risks include commodity price fluctuations for lithium and high competition in the non-automotive niche.
Competitive landscape scenario analysis shows 48V Mild-Hybrid Systems as a "Blue Ocean" with Valeo holding 40% market share and few Tier-1 rivals matching modular scale.
Recommended action for 48V systems is to leverage first-mover advantage to lock in long-term contracts with mid-market OEMs, with low risk level.
Thermal Management scenario is a "Red Ocean" with high demand but intense competition from Denso and emerging Chinese firms.
Recommended action for thermal management is to focus on the "System Integration" moat and sell software-hardware bundles rather than components, with medium risk level.
Industrial LiFePO4 Packs scenario is a "Ghost Town" due to low differentiation and price-driven commodity market.
Recommended action for industrial packs is to target high-spec industrial niches like automated forklifts rather than general LSEVs, with high risk level.
Top pick recommendation is 48V Mild-Hybrid Electrification Systems due to Valeo’s dominant ~40% market share and EV market stabilization making them a critical, high-volume revenue driver for 2026.

Verdict states Valeo remains a global leader in low-voltage electrification and ADAS sensing, with a shift toward electronics and software (Move Up strategy) successfully improving margins, targeting 7% operating margin by late 2025/early 2026.

Next steps include monitoring Chinese technology expansion at Auto China 2026 to see if "China Speed" innovations can be exported, and evaluating software monetization growth in Q2 2026 reports to confirm the high-margin "Move Up" strategy.
Data gaps include specific details on solid-state LiDAR development timelines and direct margin comparisons for the 4R circular economy program in North America versus Europe.
source_title: Valeo Battery 2026: Tech & Market Trends

facts: Valeo Service UK has released a new free training module on its Valeo Tech Academy platform offering technicians an introduction to battery electric vehicle (BEV) air conditioning systems.
The session is presented by Valeo technical trainer Stewart Lynch.
The course highlights the critical function of EV air conditioning in battery thermal management, emphasizing that regulating battery temperature is essential for performance, safety, and longevity.

The module breaks down core differences in compressor technology, noting that BEVs rely on electrically driven compressors powered by the high-voltage battery rather than internal combustion engine belt-driven compressors lubricated with PAG oil.
These electric compressors offer greater precision and energy efficiency thanks to sensor-driven input and inverter-based control.
Temperature, solar, and humidity sensors feed data into an electronic control unit that works with the inverter to modulate compressor speed and protect against voltage spikes and overheating.
The new module forms part of a growing library of free and IMI-certified training available on Valeo Tech Academy.
The platform covers traditional maintenance and repair, hybrid and electric vehicle systems, as well as advanced driver assistance systems.
Courses feature interactive, gamified content designed to make learning more engaging and effective.
Pre- and post-assessments enable technicians to monitor their progress and measure skill development.
Technicians can find additional resources on the Valeo Tech Assist website, including fitting guides and technical bulletins available at no cost.
Technicians fitting Valeo products can scan barcodes to earn rewards through the Valeo Specialist Club, which are redeemable in the Valeo online shop.

links.context: Access to full range of Valeo Tech Academy training content

source_title: Valeo releases free EV air conditioning training - autotechnician - servicing the independent workshop

facts: Henri Trintignac, Valeo’s director of Electric Vehicle Activity, claimed that Valeo SA has developed an electrification solution for the powertrain called Hybrid4All.
Hybrid4All enables car manufacturers to turn a traditional diesel or gasoline engine into a hybrid engine at an affordable price by using simple and standardized components.
The Hybrid4All architecture is based on a compact motor generator that uses a low voltage electrical system (48V).
Using a low voltage system reduces costs, making the solution more acceptable for the mass market.

In the Hybrid4All architecture, the electric motor assisting the ICE can be installed in different positions: in front of the ICE (on the accessory drive belt), after the gear-box, or between the two.
The solution integrates Valeo’s enhanced Stop-Start, regenerative braking, and torque assist functions.
The system enables fuel savings of more than 15 percent on an average gasoline engine vehicle.
The original Hybrid4All electro-technical architecture provides a high level of power (up to 15kW) and takes up no more room than a conventional alternator.
Hybrid4All recovers energy during deceleration (regenerative braking) and re-uses it during acceleration to assist the combustion engine.
The system reduces CO2 emissions by 15 percent, according to Henri Trintignac.
A car driver currently spending 100 euros ($120) per month on fuel will see their fuel budget drop to 85 euros ($102).
Valeo claimed a 15.5-percent CO2 reduction with a prototype car, the Peugeot 207 1.6 L THP.
The system is designed for small cars such as the Renault Twingo, Clio, and Opel Corsa.
These car models could previously not be equipped with hybrid engines due to cost.
Cars equipped with the electrical system are expected to be available in 2017.
source_title: Valeo makes your car engine hybrid ...

facts: On Thursday, April 24, Vernay’s Milledgeville team celebrated the grand re-opening of its manufacturing facility.

More than 30 guests attended the celebration, including representatives from the Milledgeville-Baldwin County Chamber of Commerce and students from Baldwin High School.

facts: Vista Metals Georgia broke ground on a new $30 million casthouse expansion project at its Adairsville manufacturing facility in Georgia.
