facts: The state is investigating reports of dirt blowing and flowing off the site of the Rivian electric vehicle plant in Morgan and Walton counties.
Critics say the situation confirms their fears about environmental issues associated with the controversial and troubled project.

Before recent complaints, critics expressed concern that the state and a local development authority hired Plateau Excavation, which is among defendants in a federal lawsuit alleging dirt from a neighboring Facebook data center project polluted a local stream and ponds.

Site preparation is speeding ahead on a project whose $15 billion bond deal and hundreds of millions of dollars in tax breaks were just shot down in court and still lacks all permits for work near wetlands and waterways.
JoEllen Artz, leader of Morgan Land, Sky & Water Preservation’s “No to Rivian” campaign, stated she has tremendous concern about land disturbance work being performed at this time.

Following her group’s legal victory on the bonds and tax breaks, Artz questioned whether Rivian will actually build on the site or leave a 500-acre, muddy, Georgia red clay pad that becomes a source of runoff and silt to streams, ponds, and spring heads.
The co-developers on Rivian’s behalf are the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) and the Joint Development Authority of Jasper, Morgan, Newton and Walton Counties (JDA).

GDEcD and JDA issued a joint written statement not explaining exactly why they are proceeding with site clearing despite the bond and permitting status, only that they are legally and physically able to do so.
An Oct. 13 runoff of dirt or clay from the site into the Rawlings Branch stream was self-reported by Plateau.
JDA and Plateau stated they will continue following all regulations on this project while deploying best practices and taking precautions to minimize environmental impacts.
Austell-based Plateau did not respond to emails and a phone message seeking comment.
California-based Rivian also did not respond to questions.
The Georgia Environmental Protection Division (EPD) was investigating the dirt complaints that included an on-site visit on Oct. 17, according to spokesperson Sara Lips.
EPD staff were at the Rivian site on Oct. 17 to investigate and determine what corrective actions are necessary.
The plan for Rivian’s $5 billion, 2,000-acre plant was announced late last year by Gov. Brian Kemp.

The project generated enthusiasm as a green-economy economic development deal but stirred rare local rebellion over the state’s practice of secretly signed deals involving huge tax breaks and incentives, roughly $1.5 billion in this case.
A main point of contention is the industrialization of a rural area about 40 miles east of Atlanta at I-20 and U.S. 278 near Rutledge and Social Circle.

Outrage over the lack of public input intensified when the state took over the project to speed permitting and avoid local zoning and other reviews as Morgan County planners sought to subject the project to standards above and beyond the usual due to its scale.
The plant became an issue in the Republican gubernatorial primary, with unsuccessful Kemp challenger David Perdue siding with the local opposition.

Last month, Artz’s opposition group scored a major victory with a court ruling that the deal for tax-exempt bonds was unwarranted for an unproven startup company and that the proposed property tax breaks were unlawful.
GDEcD and JDA are considering an appeal of the court decision.
Rivian, which specializes in electric trucks, had an overall rocky year with production struggles and an announcement of more than 800 layoffs.

Just days before the court decision, GDEcD and JDA began site clearance, following through on an April announcement that such work would begin on an initial 500 acres by avoiding wetlands and waterways while awaiting permitting under the federal Clean Water Act and state statutes.

Plateau was selected by the JDA in August, about eight weeks after the company was among defendants sued by two residents for alleged Clean Water Act violations and other actions related to a neighboring data center project.

The Rivian site is part of a sprawling industrial park conceptualized by the JDA called Stanton Springs that already includes facilities for biopharmaceutical company Takeda and Facebook data centers.
The Rivian site is known internally as Stanton Springs North.
The billion-dollar data center project within a Newton County section of the industrial park began in 2018 and expanded in 2020.

Site clearance for the data center project produced local complaints of dust storms of dirt blown off the area and culminated in a federal lawsuit filed in July by Morgan County residents Julie G. Jenkins and James P. Gunn against Facebook parent company Meta Platforms, general contractor Holder Construction Company, Plateau, subcontractors Alif Transport and AM Grading & Hauling, CEO Adnan Mohamed, and local property owner Daniel Bruce.

The complaint alleges that starting in May or June of 2020, Alif dumped hundreds and perhaps in excess of one thousand dump truck loads of red clay waste taken from the data center’s site onto Bruce’s property without permits or proper erosion controls.
As a result, red clay flowed off the site into the Rawlings Branch, polluting it and two large ponds owned by Jenkins and Gunn.
The lawsuit claims negligence, trespass, creation of a nuisance and violations of the Clean Water Act and other statutes, and seeks damages and remediation.
Meta, Holder, Plateau and Bruce filed answers broadly claiming there is no pollution and that they are not responsible for it if it does exist.
Alif, AM Grading and Mohamed did not file answers by a deadline, which could open them to a default judgment in the plaintiffs’ favor.

In early October, Rev. Alan Jenkins circulated a press release about Plateau’s work on the Rivian site while the lawsuit was pending, warning of significant risk that similar erosion, sedimentation, and water pollution problems could occur.

The Jenkins family sold about 40 acres of land to the JDA for the plant for nearly $2.8 million, though they say they did so only because the authority threatened that the Georgia Department of Transportation would take it by eminent domain anyway.
Alan Jenkins told SaportaReport that his mother is aware of his press release but is not commenting on it.

Artz mentioned Plateau’s involvement in her comments to EPD opposing a variation GDEcD and JDA are seeking from a stream buffer rule related to erosion and sedimentation control, stating she did not trust they would perform any better on the Rivian site.

Artz and Alan Jenkins reported the grading several times to EPD because Plateau was not watering the dirt in order to keep dust and dirt under control, noting water trucks seemed to be used sporadically.
Artz alleged the site has a dirt dump with no silt fencing and provided an aerial photo illustrating it.
EPD confirmed receiving those complaints and said they are still under investigation.
Artz said Rivian runoff on Oct. 13 resulted in the stream teeming with muddy red water.
Alan Jenkins said the dirt did not reach the ponds, which he believes is due to upstream beaver dams that could be overwhelmed by larger rains.
GDEcD and JDA stated based on project requirements Plateau provided the strongest proposal at the lowest cost and are a reputable contractor with a strong track record.
Plateau has committed to considering local subcontractors for work on the project, which GDEcD and JDA said is an important priority.
GDEcD and JDA stated there is no off-site dumping for Rivian and all materials are contained on the site.

GDEcD and JDA blamed the runoff on a period of drought-like conditions followed by persistent rainfall, noting rainfall in the preceding 24 hours at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport was 1.11 inches.
Officials stated best practices were in place to prevent the runoff but the rainfall was significant enough to overwhelm the double-row silt fence that was installed.
Officials with Morgan and Walton counties did not respond to questions; Walton County reportedly is in charge of local-level environmental monitoring on the project.
Rivian touts itself as a green-focused company aiming to build on the rural site atop a local groundwater source.
GDEcD and JDA have said the project will have minimized but some unavoidable impacts on wetlands and waterways as it seeks a federal Clean Water Act Section 404 permit.

In an April press release announcing the Section 404 application, GDEcD and JDA noted the plant plan has features such as leaving more than half the site unpaved and collecting rainwater for reuse on-site.
Officials have said the finished plant would have less runoff in total than the current site due to stormwater infrastructure.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Rivian’s corporate ethos prioritizes the natural environment in every aspect of their planning process and that the State and JDA continue to work closely with the company to thoughtfully develop its environmentally conscious manufacturing site at Stanton Springs North.

Artz expressed doubts about environmental commitments, stating she does not think anybody at the JDA particularly cares about these issues and does not believe the Walton County person in charge of monitoring the site has enough authority to influence what is done because of downward pressure from Walton County management and the JDA.
Artz said many opponents believe the site grading is probably being done in a snit of vindictiveness and that the project should have been postponed until all permits and funding were received.
source_title: Dirt pollution complaints at controversial Rivian site underline critics’ environmental concerns - SaportaReport

facts: Site Selection Magazine named Georgia the country’s #1 State for Business due to lucrative financial incentives.
Turner County offers Georgia’s Top Tier 1 financial incentives, which are the highest the state offers.
Costs of living and doing business are attractively low in Turner County.
ROI is rapid and accrues benefits rapidly and for years to come for operations in Turner County.
Custom-tailored incentives, a custom-trained workforce, financing, and fast-track permitting are available to help organizations succeed.
The Tier 1 Job Tax Credit offers up to $4,000 per qualified job created, per year, for up to 5 years.
Creating a minimum of two jobs allows a company to offset up to 100% of their annual state corporate income tax liability using the “Payroll Withholding” option.
Any leftover Job Tax Credits can be carried forward for up to 10 years from the time the job was created.
The Freeport Exemption provides a 100% ad valorem tax exemption on inventories for manufacturers, distributors, wholesalers and warehouse operations as defined by Georgia law.

Three classes of exempt personal property include: (1) manufacturer’s raw materials and goods-in-process inventory; (2) manufacturer’s finished goods held by the original manufacturer; and (3) warehouse, distribution or manufacturing firms with finished goods destined to be shipped outside of Georgia.

Bond Financing is issued through the Adel Industrial Development Authority, providing tax-exempt and taxable revenue bonds for low-interest financing of real estate, construction and manufacturing equipment.
Land price from the Turner County Development Authority is determined by the total capital investment of the project and the number of jobs to be created.
An Ad valorem Tax Incentive/PILOT program may be available to qualifying companies as an incentive for job creation and capital investment.
Turner County is one of the few Constitutional Authorities in Georgia, giving it more flexibility with incentives than traditional authorities.
Fast Track Permitting provides a streamlined and simplified permitting process to accommodate developing and expanding businesses.
Projects may qualify for a Build to Suit/Lease Back Arrangement based on capital investment and jobs created.
Low-cost utilities feature competitive rates enhanced by “customer choice” of providers for users requiring 900 kw loads or larger.
Qualifying companies may be eligible for Infrastructure Financial Assistance.
Georgia’s corporate tax rate is a flat 6%.
Georgia’s state and local taxpayer burden ranks as the 16th lowest in the nation.
Municipally sourced utilities keep operational expenses significantly lower while maintaining high productivity.
Turner County maintains a right-to-work, ready-to-work labor force that keeps quality high and costs low.
Georgia’s cost of living is 91.5% of the U.S. average.
Turner County’s cost of living is 81.7% of the national average.
Turner County has superb public schools, keeping the ratio of public versus private school attendance higher than the state average.
Recreation options include a sprawling state park and a municipal golf course that offer highly pleasurable experiences without high prices.
Turner County’s convenient hub location places top vacation spots like Disney World and Atlantic and Gulf beaches within just a few hours’ drive.

facts: Murata Electronics North America Inc. is located in Smyrna, Ga.
The company is repositioning itself in the passives market by building stronger relationships with its OEM and distribution partners.
By getting involved early in its customers' product-design cycles, the company believes it can dominate the component count of a typical cellular phone or other wireless device.
Tom Kuhl, director of sales, stated the company is evolving from a supplier of commodity products to becoming a high-end design partner.
The Smyrna facility supplies ceramic capacitors, microwave components, and crystal oscillators.
Murata Electronics North America Inc. reported $425 million in sales in 1998.
Sales climbed to $590 million last year as a result of the wireless and telecom boom.
Parent company Murata Manufacturing Co. Ltd., based in Kyoto, Japan, had sales of $3 billion in 1998 and $4 billion in 1999.
The company is looking to increase sales by 35% this year.
Murata is increasingly meeting with OEMs, contract electronics manufacturers, and distributors during the initial stages of an end-product's design to identify requirements earlier.
Product development efforts are driven by a market push for increasingly smaller end-use products such as cookie-thin cell phones, PDAs, and pagers.
Component miniaturization is driving much of Murata's product-development efforts.
Murata is about to bring to market high-frequency chip inductors that have a 0201 footprint, which is about the size of pepper.
The company is currently using thin-film, wire-wound, and monolithic designs on its chip inductors.
A typical cell phone contains 25 to 30 inductors.
Murata is developing smaller, surface-mount crystal-oscillator products that offer a longer life span.
Crystal oscillator technology is moving away from through-hole packages to surface-mount devices that feature digital tuning as well as inverted crystals.
John Switalski, product manager of crystal oscillators, noted the shift in technology.
Sales for these crystal oscillator types jumped from nearly $4 million more than a year ago to almost $10 million this year.
Crystal oscillator sales are expected to climb to $20 million next year.
Nearly 7% of Murata's revenue has been earmarked for R&D.
The company plans to raise capacity by 40% over the next year or so.
Manufacturing of 0402-size components and high-capacitance MLCCs will take the lion's share of the investments.
source_title: Murata revs production to meet wireless boom - EE Times

facts: Japanese Murata Manufacturing is setting up a new corporate venture capital (CVC) arm, Wonderstone Ventures, in the United States as part of its long-term strategy to expand innovation and competitiveness.

Through the CVC arm company plans to invest USD 50 million over five years in technologies with potential impact in areas such as next-generation communications, 6G, optics/semiconductors, bioelectronics, environmental technologies, robotics, and space.
Wonderstone Ventures will focus on identifying startups globally and accelerating collaboration and agility in Murata’s innovation efforts.
The move formalises and expands Murata's existing startup investment activity, aiming to enhance its ability to respond to technological and societal shifts.
The venture initiative is aligned with Murata’s “Medium-Term Direction 2027” plan, which emphasises preparation for long-term technological transformation.

By establishing Wonderstone Ventures in the US, Murata aims to strengthen its position in international innovation ecosystems and more effectively support a pipeline of emerging technologies relevant to its future growth.
source_title: Murata establishes US-based venture arm to boost innovation investment

facts: Top passive component suppliers are investing in new-product development and information technology to automate communications with their supply chain partners.

Cost-reduction initiatives have translated into plant closings, factory consolidation, manufacturing shifts to low-cost-labor regions in China and Eastern Europe, layoffs, and reduced capital spending.
Glyndwr Smith serves as assistant to the chief executive and senior vice president at Vishay Intertechnology Inc., located in Malvern, Pa.
Sandy Beck is vice president of sales and marketing at Kemet Corp., located in Greenville, S.C.
Inventory levels remain higher than necessary to support current consumption, with excess stock persisting despite not reaching previous highs.
Lead times for standard products range from four to eight weeks, while delivery times for specialty products range from about eight to ten weeks.
Prices for capacitors and resistors have continued to drop steadily over the past 12 months, with some tags approaching manufacturing costs.
Pockets of growth are emerging in end markets such as automotive, game consoles, cell phones, and laptop computers.
AVX Corp., located in Myrtle Beach, S.C., posted net sales of $294.9 million in its fiscal first quarter ended June 30, with net income of $1.3 million.
AVX reported unit volume sales were higher than expected because price pressure slowed as production capacity utilization increased.
Kemet reported $124 million in net sales for the June quarter, a 5% sequential increase, with net earnings of $3.4 million.
Kemet's unit shipments have increased for three consecutive quarters, while average selling prices declined approximately 5% in the June quarter.
Kemet has moved ahead with plans to close a plant in Greenwood, S.C., and another in Matamoros, Mexico.
Kemet lowered raw materials inventories during the quarter by $11 million and finished goods stores by $7.9 million.
In the past two years, Kemet cut its workforce to 6,500 from a high of about 16,000.
Vishay expected aggregate unit shipments for its passives, semiconductors, and optoelectronic devices rose 3% to 5% compared with the March quarter.
Vishay will drop expenditures, including R&D, to approximately $100 million from $160 million last year.
Taiwan-based Yageo Corp. is expected to cut its capital budget to just $30 million, down from $160 million in 2001.
About 30% of the EMS industry's total production is manufactured in low-cost-labor centers, including China and elsewhere in Asia, Eastern Europe, and Mexico.
Many top-tier players want to migrate more than 70% of total production to low-cost regions by 2004.
The manufacturing shift has resulted in a decline in spending in North America and Europe, leaving the United States as a center for design and new-product development.
China's passives suppliers are expected to chalk up resistor and capacitor sales of $1.6 billion in 2003, giving the country an 8.9% share of the global market.
Key Chinese players to watch include Chengdu, Fenghua, Man Yue, Nanyang, and Shenzhen.
Global suppliers retain a competitive edge because China's passives vendors currently lack advanced products and manufacturing tools, global support services, and brand recognition.

Production costs of components in China are less than those in Western regions and Japan, but total cost of acquisition associated primarily with shipping costs gives China a significant advantage over Western-based suppliers.
Vishay has several factories and a strong distribution network in China.
Japan's Murata Manufacturing Co. Ltd. struck its first joint venture in the early 1990s and has since opened two additional factories with major sales organizations.
John Denslinger is senior vice president of marketing at Murata Electronics North America Inc., located in Smyrna, Ga.
Murata Electronics North America is well equipped to handle expansion in China due to its global structure for sales, marketing, and supply chain.
Rohm Co. Ltd. has shifted manufacturing to China and is making investments to increase production capacity.
AVX is establishing a facility in China that will come on line in the third quarter to support multinational companies moving into the region.
Willie King is division vice president of product marketing at AVX.
Kemet remains one of the few top-tier passives companies with no manufacturing presence in China but has distribution hubs in Asia, including China.
Kemet expects to have a manufacturing presence in Asia in the not-too-distant future to support existing customers moving to China and introduce itself to local customers.
Epcos A.G., based in Munich, Germany, is moving production to low-cost areas in both China and Eastern Europe, including Hungary and the Czech Republic.
Denny Salmang is president and chief executive of Epcos Inc., located in Iselin, N.J.
Vishay last year acquired two companies, Tansitor and Mallory (a North American capacitor maker), to build up its passives portfolio.
Vishay plans to roll out 200 new products this year, of which 60 will be passive devices.
Yageo in 2000 acquired two passive component divisions from Royal Philips Electronics N.V., now marketed under the brand names Ferroxcube and Phycomp.
Allan H. Cole is chief executive of Yageo America Inc., located in Addison, Texas.
KOA Corp. of Japan acquired specialty resistor manufacturer Tama Electric Co. Ltd. in 2001.
KOA now offers metal oxide varistors, resettable fuses, three-pole capacitors, inductors, and ferrite beads to tap into emerging gigahertz designs.
Jeff Rice is vice president of sales and marketing at subsidiary KOA Speer Electronics Inc., located in Bradford, Pa.
Kemet's differentiator includes a full complement of surface-mount capacitor technologies, including solid-state aluminum and ceramic and tantalum dielectrics.
Kemet has continued to expand its high-frequency tantalum and high-capacitance ceramic products.
New-product introductions have generated significant revenue for Kemet over the past year.
Kemet is in discussions to supply products to the medical sector by targeting nontraditional markets.
Yageo's new-product development centers on lead-free components, a new line of miniaturized solid-state antennas, and X2Y attenuators primarily used for filtering in high-frequency applications.
Suppliers are creating customer and supplier Web portals to make it easier to do business with them.
AVX, Kemet, and Vishay have the ability to check distributors' inventory via the Web.

Taiyo Yuden (U.S.A.), located in Schaumburg, Ill., began offering an inventory check function on its Website in July that allows users to retrieve franchised distributor inventory information updated daily.

Customers can view available quantity, see when inventory information was last updated, download distributor contact information, and send requests for quotes or place purchase orders through the platform.
Vishay's new MLCC modeling program provides data that engineers can use for complex development of capacitor/circuit behavior.
AVX and Kemet both support engineers on the Web with SPICE models and software.
Vishay supported its EMS customers with account managers at the senior management level to coordinate component requirements.
Murata Electronics North America is focusing on design issues and strengthening its engineering and product management as business shifts from North America and Eastern Europe to Asia.
Kemet endorses systems that allow communication with end users to provide visibility up and down the supply chain.
Kemet and passives distributor TTI Inc., located in Fort Worth, Texas, began using RosettaNet Partner Interface Process 3A1 earlier this year to conduct automated quoting.
The goal of the RosettaNet implementation is to reduce cycle time for quoting and increase accuracy of information exchanged between companies.
source_title: 'Passive' but not inert - EDN

facts: Murata Electronics North America Inc. and its parent company Murata Manufacturing Co. Ltd. announced that it has closed the deal to acquire RF Monolithics Inc. (RFM).
The cash transaction paid the holders of RFM common shares $1.78 per share.

RFM, headquartered in Dallas, TX, will continue to market its broad range of solutions-driven, technology-enabled wireless connectivity for wireless applications under the RFM brand as a wholly owned subsidiary of Murata Electronics North America Inc.
RFM provides individual standardized and custom components to modules for comprehensive industrial wireless sensor networks and machine-to-machine (M2M) technology.
David M. Kirk serves as President and CEO of Murata Electronics North America, Inc., which acts as the Regional Headquarters of Murata Americas.

Murata aims to leverage RFM's unique low-power technology and their expertise in design and development of production-ready RF modules, SAW-based and RFIC short-range radios, and stand-alone radio systems.
The markets in which RFM sells its products and solutions include healthcare, energy, industrial, and M2M, matching Murata's growth strategy.
Farlin A. Halsey serves as President and CEO of RFM.

With the combined resources of Murata, RFM will be able to take advantage of market channels globally and in the development of innovative wireless solutions for healthcare, energy, and industrial markets.

Murata Manufacturing Co. Ltd., established in 1944 with global headquarters in Kyoto, Japan, is a worldwide leader in research, design, manufacture, and sale of ceramic-based passive electronic components, power supply, and wireless module solutions.
Murata has annual revenues in excess of $7 billion.

Murata's products include monolithic ceramic capacitors, polymer aluminum electrolytic capacitors, electric double layer energy device, noise suppression products/EMI suppression filters, inductors, resistor products, resonators, filters, RF components, communication and wireless module solutions, sensors, thermistors, DC-DC converters, AC-DC power supplies, sound components, piezo actuators, micromechatronics products, ceramic applied products, and RFID solutions.

Murata's products are found in a wide range of applications including consumer, mobile communications, computers and networking equipment, automotive electronics, advanced metering equipment, digital home electronics, lighting and illumination, and healthcare devices.
Murata has employees and manufacturing facilities throughout the world.

The company has an estimated workforce of 13,190 employees.
In the United States, Novelis holds an estimated 17.0% market share of total revenue in the Aluminum Production industry.
Novelis is classified as an "All Star" due to displaying stronger market share, profit, and revenue growth compared to its peers.

The company's product portfolio includes brands such as B57S, B73A, Coloris, CU77A, Falzonal, FF2, FF3, G77Ac, HRC73A, J57S UP, J73A UP, Novaltube, Novelis, Novelis Advanz, Novelis Fusion, PROF2, SKY, SSL BF, Super Grondal, and SuperWindy.

facts: Novelis Inc. has resumed operations at its site in Greenboro, Georgia.
The Greenboro plant was the site of an explosion and fire on Sunday, March 1.
An explosion occurred in the plant, damaging a baghouse and cold-end equipment.
No one was injured during the incident.
The plant was shut down from Sunday evening until Wednesday, March 4.
During the shutdown, employees assessed the situation and cleaned the affected areas.
The plant restarted casting operations as of March 4.
Novelis is investigating the cause of the incident.
Customers are not expected to be significantly affected by the incident.
source_title: Novelis resumes operations in Greensboro, Georgia - Recycling Today

facts: Novelis is a Canadian corporation headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia.
Novelis offers flat-rolled aluminium products for automotive, beverage can, and specialty segments.
The US Department of Justice proposed that a federal court order Hindalco-owned Novelis to divest Aleris Corporation's entire aluminum automotive body sheet operations in North America.
Novelis is a wholly-owned subsidiary of India's Hindalco Industries.
Novelis acquired Aleris for USD 2.8 billion last month.
As per a mutually reached settlement, Novelis is required to divest the automotive body sheet business in North America, which is just one plant in Lewisport.

Assistant Attorney General Makan Delrahim of the Justice Department's Antitrust Division stated that the proposed divestiture preserves competition in the market for aluminum automotive body sheet and protects automakers and American consumers.
Prior to its acquisition by Novelis, Aleris was a Delaware corporation headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio.
Aleris offers flat-rolled aluminum products to the automotive, aerospace, building, and construction industries among others.
In 2018, Aleris' revenues were approximately USD 3.4 billion.
The proposed final judgment follows the US' March 9 arbitration win.

Prior to filing its civil antitrust lawsuit to block the merger, the Department's Antitrust Division reached an agreement with Novelis and Aleris to refer the matter to binding arbitration if they were unable to resolve the US' competitive concern.
Under the arbitration terms, Novelis agreed to divest Aleris' aluminum automotive body sheet operations in North America if the US prevailed in the arbitration.
The arbitrator ruled in favour of the United States, holding that aluminum automotive body sheet constitutes a relevant antitrust product market.
Hindalco cemented its position as the world's largest producer of value-added aluminium products with the completion of the USD 2.8 billion acquisition of Aleris by Novelis Inc last month.
The acquisition positions Hindalco as one of the world's largest aluminium companies, with a global footprint spanning 49 manufacturing facilities in North America, Europe and Asia.
source_title: Aleris Automotive Sheet Business: US proposes Novelis to divest automotive body sheet biz in North America, ETAuto

facts: Novelis Inc. is a leading sustainable aluminum solutions provider and world leader in aluminum rolling and recycling.

The press release features thought leadership by Jamie Zinser (Vice President, Global Automotive), Xavier Varone (Global Technical Director), and Don Whitacre (Director, Automotive Technical Development) of Novelis Inc.

Making vehicles lighter, greener, and simpler to manufacture is critical to improving the features, functionality, and sustainability of internal combustion engine models while driving growth in the battery electric vehicle market.
Aluminum is the fastest-growing automotive material according to the Ducker Carlisle 2023 North American Light Vehicle Aluminum Content and Outlook report.

Novelis has been a leading global supplier of aluminum sheet to the auto industry for more than 40 years, with automotive operations on all three auto-producing continents: Asia, Europe, and North America.
Novelis' automotive product portfolio includes high-performing alloys featured in more than 315 models on the road today, including the Ford F-150 series, Jeep Wrangler, and Land Rover Defender.
Transportation makes up roughly 16% of total global greenhouse emissions according to Climate Watch and the World Resources Institute (2020).
Passenger cars, trucks, buses, and other commercial and recreational vehicles constitute more than three quarters (77%) of all transportation emissions according to a 2023 Statista report.
Life cycle assessments show the greatest potential to reduce vehicle environmental impact is addressing the use phase.
An Oakridge National Lab study shows the use phase of an ICE vehicle accounts for up to 90% of carbon emissions generated over its total lifecycle.
The use phase of a BEV contributes to approximately 70% of its lifetime carbon emissions according to a recently published Alumobility Last Mile Delivery Vehicles study.
Aluminum has a material density that is one third of steel while delivering greater specific strength, energy absorbing capacity, and longevity.
Lightweighting via aluminum typically generates a body-in-white weight savings of more than 40% compared to a steel body-in-white.
Replacing 1 kilogram of steel with 0.6 kilograms of aluminum results in no reduction in performance.
Primary mass reduction from aluminum generates secondary mass reduction opportunities for motors, motor controllers, batteries, wheels, tires, and brakes.
Secondary weight reduction can often exceed 50% of the primary weight reduction.

Ducker Carlisle identified key growth areas for aluminum in BEVs from 2022 to 2030: battery housings (+37 pounds per vehicle), electric motor housings (+34 ppv), doors (+8 ppv), and sills/rockers (+4 ppv).
Novelis participated in an Alumobility consortium study modeling an aluminum body for a fully electric last mile delivery van.
The study showed a compounded 550kg weight savings with aluminum versus a steel baseline.
This weight savings triggered a battery size reduction from 69kWh to 63kWh, representing a net saving of $770 per vehicle.
Alumobility studies on converting existing steel bodies into aluminum showed potential to reduce part counts by 10% and the number of joints required by 20%.
Aluminum content in automobiles grew from less than 130kg per vehicle at the beginning of this century to close to 220kg per vehicle today.
Aluminum content is expected to grow at a compound annual growth rate of 6.4% from 2022 to 2026, driven by the rise of BEVs according to Ducker Carlisle.
Reducing an ICE vehicle's weight by 220 pounds (100kg) decreases fuel consumption by as much as 5% over its entire life.
The same 220-pound weight reduction for a BEV translates to a reduction in energy needed for the same range by approximately 3.2kWh according to NREL's FASTSim simulator.
A weight savings of 450kg in a full-body aluminum automobile saves owners more than 6,000kWh of energy over a typical 140,000-mile life compared to steel bodies.
These energy savings amount to 60% of the average annual electricity consumption for a U.S. residential utility customer.
Lighter vehicles create less wear and tear on sub-systems like brakes and tires, resulting in lower maintenance costs.
Carbon emission reductions and consumer maintenance benefits can be achieved by using aluminum for hoods, doors, and battery enclosures.

Energy savings and corresponding carbon emission reductions can reach up to 20% if aluminum use is expanded to the full vehicle body according to the EDAG Silverado Body Lightweighting Final LCA Report.
Novelis operates automotive Customer Solution Centers in Stuttgart, Germany; Shanghai, China; and Detroit, Michigan.
The centers are designed to speed development of innovative aluminum solutions by collaborating with OEM customers and Tier 1 suppliers from early vehicle concept and design stages.
The Metro Detroit CSC features a hot stamping press, computer-aided design software, and advanced visualization technologies for digitally modeling vehicle performance and safety scenarios.
The Detroit CSC contains a roll forming development line for exploring solutions for battery enclosure frames, rockers, front longitudinal members, and various cross members.
This capability enables Novelis researchers to work with auto OEMs to develop and test alloys and processes as an emerging alternative to extruded parts.
Since launching its first CSC in 2018, Novelis has advanced efficient door designs using high-forming aluminum alloys.
Novelis has developed critical strength-dominated structural components to compete with ultra-high-strength steel alloys.
Novelis demonstrated optimized tempers for energy absorbing crash members that save weight and cost versus current extruded aluminum crash rails.
Novelis designed and prototyped aluminum-intensive battery enclosure solutions for electric vehicles.
Novelis works hand-in-hand with automotive customers globally on innovative design, roll forming research, sustainable alloys, and BEV efficiency.
source_title: Novelis Inc. "Lighter, Greener, Simpler - Aluminum Drives New Automotive Design Dynamic" :: Novelis Inc.

facts: Novelis Inc. is an Atlanta-based aluminum company.
Novelis broke ground on a $36 million investment at its Greensboro, Georgia facility.
The investment is designed to expand and upgrade the facility's recycling capacity.
Novelis is adding equipment for aluminum scrap recycling at the Greensboro facility.
A new baghouse is being added for improved dust mitigation.
The facility's design is being enhanced to ensure safer and more efficient traffic flow.
Novelis expects the expansion to be completed by the fall of 2021.
The investment will expand Novelis' capabilities to recycle automotive stamping scrap in a closed loop.
The Greensboro facility continues to recycle used beverage cans (UBCs) for the beverage packaging industry.
Novelis currently has closed-loop recycling programs in place with Ford and other automotive customers.
Novelis is exploring new opportunities to establish closed-loop recycling partnerships.

Daniel Groce, lead of Communications and Government Affairs at Novelis, stated that closing the loop preserves the value of the alloy, reduces costs, minimizes environmental impact, and establishes a more secure supply chain.
Daniel Groce noted that the initial startup cost of establishing a closed-loop recycling program with an automaker can be challenging, but these investments are worthwhile.
Daniel Groce stated there will be a nominal increase in capacity, but the primary focus is enabling the Greensboro facility to recycle automotive scrap and modernizing the plant.
Daniel Groce noted that the availability of automotive aluminum scrap is increasing as more automakers turn to aluminum to lightweight vehicles.

Marco Palmieri, senior vice president and president of Novelis North America, stated that Novelis is investing in recycling capabilities to increase the amount of recycled content in new vehicles and reduce carbon emissions as automakers choose aluminum.
Marco Palmieri stated the investment aligns with Novelis' purpose of Shaping a Sustainable World Together and demonstrates a commitment to helping customers meet sustainability targets.
The facility upgrades will enhance safety systems, including a new design that more effectively separates pedestrian walkways from mobile equipment traffic.
Beatriz Landa is the Novelis Greensboro plant manager.
The Greensboro facility has a team of 145 employees.
Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp stated that any time a home-based company expands, it is good news for business in Georgia.
Gov. Kemp noted Novelis' 40-year history in Greensboro has created countless opportunities for hardworking Georgians in the region.
Congressman Jody Hice stated the expansion of the Greensboro plant is indicative of its sustained success and expressed a look forward to a flourishing partnership.
The article was updated on Oct. 31 to include information from Daniel Groce.
source_title: Updated: Novelis invests in aluminum recycling capacity - Recycling Today

facts: Novelis Inc. is an Atlanta-based leading aluminum products manufacturer under the umbrella of Hindalco Industries Ltd., owned by billionaire Kumar Mangalam Birla.
The company is gearing up to launch its much-anticipated US initial public offering (IPO) by early June.
Sources disclosed that Novelis is poised to formally kick off the marketing of the IPO within the next two weeks.
Novelis filed publicly with the US Securities and Exchange Commission on Monday, marking a significant step towards its listing aspirations.
Bloomberg News previously reported that Hindalco aims to raise approximately $1.2 billion through the IPO and is eyeing a valuation of around $18 billion.
Novelis has refrained from commenting on the matter, as indicated by a representative for the company.
According to the filing, Novelis has enlisted the services of Morgan Stanley, Bank of America Corp., and Citigroup Inc. to facilitate the potential listing on the New York Stock Exchange.
The shares will be offered by a subsidiary of Hindalco, with Novelis itself not set to receive any proceeds from the sale, as outlined in the filing.
Novelis is renowned as the world’s largest producer of flat-rolled aluminum products.
The company caters to a diverse range of industries, supplying materials utilized in everything from automobiles to beverage cans.
The company initially disclosed its confidential filing for the IPO with the US Securities and Exchange Commission back in February.
Hindalco’s acquisition of Novelis in 2007 was valued at approximately $6 billion, including debt.
Novelis derives around 42% of its global net sales from North America, according to details outlined in its IPO filing.
source_title: Novelis Inc. to Pursue $1.2 Billion US IPO by June | Machine Maker - Latest Manufacturing News | Indian Manufacturing News - Latest Manufacturing News | Indian Manufacturing News - Machine Maker

facts: On June 16, 2026, Novelis Inc. entered into a material definitive agreement.
The ABL Amendment provides additional flexibility to operate its business.
On May 19, 2026, Novelis Inc. reported fourth quarter and full fiscal year 2026 results.
Novelis Inc. is a leading sustainable aluminum solutions provider and the world leader in aluminum rolling and recycling.
Novelis is driven by its purpose of Shaping a Sustainable World Together.
Novelis experienced two significant fire events at its Oswego, New York plant in fiscal 2026.
source_title: Novelis Inc

facts: Novelis Inc., a world leader in the production of flat-rolled aluminum products, will relocate its North American headquarters from Cleveland, Ohio to Atlanta, Georgia.
The relocation will add 80 corporate jobs to Novelis' Atlanta staff.
Novelis' Atlanta staff is expected to include 220 people by the end of 2010 when combined with other expected staff additions.
Atlanta was already home to the company's world headquarters, which located to Georgia in 2005.
Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue announced the relocation and stated that the headquarters' consolidation is a testament to the benefits of Georgia's pro-business environment.
Novelis produces an estimated 19 percent of the world's flat-rolled aluminum products.
The company is also a global leader in aluminum can recycling.
Novelis currently has about 12,000 employees in 11 countries spanning four continents.

The North American headquarters relocation to Atlanta is part of a series of steps over the past year designed to realign global operations, consolidate corporate functions, enhance organizational effectiveness, increase efficiencies, and reduce costs.

Philip Martens, president and chief operating officer of Novelis, stated that moving the North American headquarters to Atlanta will streamline processes and better align the North American business group with the global corporate center.
Customers served by Novelis include major brands such as Coca-Cola, Anheuser-Busch, Ford, General Motors, Lotte Aluminum, Kodak, and ThyssenKrupp.
The company is the leading producer of aluminum rolled products in Europe and South America.
Novelis is the second leading producer of aluminum rolled products in both North America and Asia.

Hans Gant, senior vice of economic development for the Metro Atlanta Chamber, said the consolidation validates that Atlanta continues to be a great location for headquarters, particularly for multinational companies.

Gregg Simon, manager of business engagement for the Atlanta Development Authority (ADA), expressed delight at helping entice Novelis to expand its Atlanta headquarters and highlighted the positive business climate offered in the city of Atlanta.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) partnered with the Metro Atlanta Chamber of Commerce and the Atlanta Development Authority on this project.
GDEcD statewide project manager Blair Lewis assisted the company with the relocation.
source_title: Novelis Inc. relocates North American headquarters

facts: Novelis announced on May 11 it will build an aluminum recycling and rolling facility in Bay Minette, Ala.
The facility will utilize renewable energy and have an initial production capacity of 600,000 metric tons of finished aluminum goods per year.
The $2.5 billion plant will be the first fully integrated aluminum mill built in the U.S. in four decades.
Novelis is currently the largest U.S. recycler of used beverage cans (UBCs).
Novelis sees strong demand from customers for flat-rolled sheet for aluminum beverage cans based on the recyclability of the metal packaging.
Ball Corporation is a customer for Novelis’ flat-rolled can sheet.

Ron Lewis, Ball’s chief operating officer for global beverage packaging, stated that decarbonizing the value chain is fundamental to creating a circular economy in the aluminum industry and that Novelis’ new plant will add domestic production of sustainable aluminum while decreasing carbon footprint.
Globally, Novelis can currently recycle 82 billion aluminum cans a year.
The Bay Minette project is expected to begin coming on-line in mid-2025.
The project will expand Novelis’ global recycling capacity to about 97 billion cans a year, an increase of about 18%.

Novelis’ annual report filed May 11 notes the Atlanta-based company is experiencing greater demand for can sheet partly because beverage brands are moving from plastic and glass to aluminum packaging.
Reasons for the shift include recyclability, ease of drink filling, shipping efficiency, metal’s gas and light barrier properties and more.
Over half the capacity of the new facility will be used to produce aluminum beverage can sheet for North American markets.
Because of the alloys in them, UBCs are most often recycled into new cans, as opposed to automotive or construction materials.
The remainder of the plant’s capacity will produce aluminum sheet for automakers, which are increasingly using the material to reduce weight and improve fuel efficiency.

Novelis’ recycled content during the 2022 fiscal year averaged 57%, down from 61% during the prior fiscal year, largely due to reduced scrap inputs impacted by supply chain disruption and logistical challenges.
In terms of weight of recovered metal, Novelis purchased or tolled over 2.2 million tons of recycled metal during the 2022 fiscal year.

Novelis has been working to develop circular economies for aluminum through state and federal public policies, as well as through partnerships with customers and other stakeholders on new approaches that encourage and incentivize U.S. consumers to recycle more often.
In November, Novelis backed a Can Manufacturers Institute (CRI) announcement that the industry would fund lobbyists to push lawmakers to support container deposit laws.

Other stakeholders behind the campaign are aluminum sheet suppliers Constellium, Kaiser Aluminum and Tri-Arrows Aluminum, and beverage can manufacturers Ardagh Metal Packaging, Canpack, Crown Holdings and Envases.

Steve Fisher, president and CEO of Novelis, noted the greenfield development in Bay Minette, which is not far from the city of Mobile on the Gulf Coast, will be capable of future expansion beyond 600,000 metric tons of production capacity.
The plant aims to be carbon neutral for Scope 1 and Scope 2 greenhouse gas emissions.

Environmental features include being primarily powered by renewable energy, using recycled water, diverting waste from landfill, utilizing rail transportation, and employing technologies such as artificial intelligence, augmented reality and robotics.
The plant is expected to create up to 1,000 jobs.
Site work is now under way, with commissioning expected to start in mid-2025.

Novelis already has recycling operations at 15 of its plants globally and runs seven recycling operations in the U.S. that re-melt post-consumer aluminum and recycled process material, mostly derived from UBCs and automotive scrap.
The largest North American end market for Novelis’ products is beverage can sheet.
Novelis recently announced a $130 million project to boost recycling capabilities at its Oswego, N.Y. plant.
Novelis announced plans to build a $365 million recycling facility in Guthrie, Ky. focused on recycled aluminum scrap generated by the production of automotive components.
Novelis is a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Limited, a metals company owned by Mumbai, India-based conglomerate Aditya Birla Group.

The 2022 fiscal year report noted that Novelis shipped a total of 4.08 million metric tons of product and tallied net sales of $17.1 billion last year, up 6% and 40%, respectively, from the year before.
source_title: Novelis unveils plans for $2.5B aluminum recycling plant - Resource Recycling

facts: Novelis Corporation operates a facility in Kennesaw, GA.
The facility address is 1950 Vaughn Rd NW, Kennesaw, GA 30144.
Novelis Corporation has been in business for 21 years.
Business hours are 8:00 am to 5:00 pm.
Novelis Global Research & Tech is associated with the location.
Novelis Inc is associated with the location.
source_title: Novelis Corporation - Kennesaw, GA 30144

source_title: Greg Schlicht named executive VP and president at Novelis - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: Novelis Inc., a leading sustainable aluminum solutions provider and the world leader in aluminum rolling and recycling, announced that 10 of its manufacturing plants have achieved the first of four levels of certification in the Novelis Operating System (NOS) as of the end of fiscal year 2026.
Emilio Braghi, chief operating officer of Novelis Inc., stated that having world-class operations is fundamental to the company's business and essential to exceeding customer expectations.
Reaching the first level of NOS certification reflects Novelis' commitment to safe and consistent execution, operational reliability, and continuous improvement and innovation.

The company customized the Novelis Operating System (NOS) specifically for the manufacturing requirements of aluminum rolling and recycling plants based on the principles of leading manufacturing operating systems.
While Novelis has long had a continuous improvement focus, NOS standardizes this approach across its global manufacturing footprint.
The Berea, Kentucky, United States plant earned first-level NOS certification in Novelis' recently concluded 2026 fiscal year.
The Changzhou, China plant earned first-level NOS certification in Novelis' recently concluded 2026 fiscal year.
The Göttingen, Germany plant earned first-level NOS certification in Novelis' recently concluded 2026 fiscal year.
The Nachterstedt Recycling, Germany plant earned first-level NOS certification in Novelis' recently concluded 2026 fiscal year.
The Nachterstedt Rolling, Germany plant earned first-level NOS certification in Novelis' recently concluded 2026 fiscal year.
The Ohle, Germany plant earned first-level NOS certification in Novelis' recently concluded 2026 fiscal year.
The Pindamonhangaba, Brazil plant earned first-level NOS certification in Novelis' recently concluded 2026 fiscal year.
The Santo Andre, Brazil plant earned first-level NOS certification in Novelis' recently concluded 2026 fiscal year.
The Sierre, Switzerland plant earned first-level NOS certification in Novelis' recently concluded 2026 fiscal year.
The Yeongju, South Korea plant earned first-level NOS certification in Novelis' recently concluded 2026 fiscal year.

Novelis plans to certify all of its 29 manufacturing facilities in NOS and advance them through the four progressive levels of NOS certification to further strengthen performance, reliability and service for customers worldwide.
source_title: Ten Novelis Plants Achieve New Level of Manufacturing Maturity - Metro Atlanta CEO

facts: Novelis produces aluminum sheet, plate, and light gauge products for use in the automotive, transportation, aerospace, electronics, architectural, and industrial product markets.
Flat-rolled aluminum sheet is used in the production of battery enclosures for the growing electric vehicle market.

Novelis expects increased growth in the transportation end-use market driven by OEM customers' desire to lightweight vehicles to improve vehicle performance, safety, and sustainability profile, as well as by increased adoption of electric vehicles, which use higher amounts of aluminum.
During fiscal 2025, Novelis had total shipment volumes of 3,972 kt and net sales of $17.1 billion.
As of March 31, 2025, Novelis had manufacturing operations in nine countries on four continents: North America, South America, Europe, and Asia, with 31 operating facilities.
Novelis has recycling operations in 15 of its operating facilities to recycle both post-consumer and post-industrial aluminum.
Novelis is a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Ltd., the registrant's parent company, which acquired Novelis in May 2007.
As of September 30, 2024, there was no established public trading market for the common stock of Novelis Inc., and all outstanding shares were held indirectly by Hindalco Industries Ltd.
As of May 9, 2025, Novelis had 600,000,000 common shares outstanding.
Novelis is building a greenfield, fully-integrated rolling and recycling mill currently being constructed in Bay Minette, Alabama.
source_title: May 12, 2025 - 10-K: Annual report [Section 13 and 15(d), not S-K Item 405] | Novelis Inc. ()

facts: Novelis Inc. offered Cary Chenanda the role of EVP and President of Novelis North America.
The position is effective April 15, 2024, or later as mutually agreed, with a transition period not exceeding 90 days after the start date.
Cary Chenanda will report to Steve Fisher, Novelis Inc.’s President and Chief Executive Officer.
The role will be based in Atlanta, Georgia.
The annual base salary for the position is USD 500,000.
The next salary review is scheduled for July 1, 2025.
Cary Chenanda is eligible for the Novelis Annual Incentive Plan (AIP) with a target opportunity of 70% of his annual base salary.
For Fiscal Year 2025 (April 1, 2024 to March 31, 2025), the AIP target will be pro-rated based on the start date.
Cary Chenanda is eligible for Novelis’ Long Term Incentive Plan (LTIP) with a target opportunity of USD 700,000 for Fiscal Year 2025.
LTIP award details will be communicated no later than July 1, 2024.

Employee benefits include a Defined Contribution Supplemental Executive Retirement Plan (DC SERP), life insurance, medical, prescription drug, and health savings plans, dental coverage, short-term disability, long-term disability, business travel and accident insurance, and executive physicals.

The employee receives an annual Flex Perk stipend of USD 50,000 minus required deductions, paid over 12 months for personal use such as club memberships, tax preparation, car allowance, or financial services.
Novelis does not pay for or provide club dues, vehicles, or other services beyond the Flex Perk stipend.
Vacation entitlement is no less than 30 days annually, plus paid holidays per the Atlanta office schedule.
Relocation assistance is coordinated through third-party provider Cartus.
The employee must repay 100% of any relocation assistance if they separate from Novelis within the first 24 months of the position.

A sign-on bonus totaling USD 1,250,000 will be paid in installments: USD 325,000 within 30 days of joining, USD 400,000 within 30 days of completing one year, and USD 500,000 within 30 days of completing two years.
The sign-on bonus is conditioned on continued employment through applicable payment dates and subject to taxes and withholdings.
If the employee voluntarily terminates or is terminated for cause within 12 months of receiving the sign-on bonus, they must repay any amount paid in the last 12 months (less applicable taxes).
Severance pay upon termination other than for Cause or for Good Reason equals one times the annual base and short term incentive opportunity (USD 850,000), reduced by any other severance paid.
The agreement is governed by the internal laws of the State of Georgia, with disputes handled exclusively in state or federal courts in Atlanta, Georgia.
The offer was extended by HR Shashikant, EVP and Chief Human Resource Officer at Novelis Inc.
source_title: June 25, 2025 - EX-10.10 - 10-K/A: Annual report [Section 13 and 15(d), not S-K Item 405] | Novelis Inc. ()

facts: Governor Brian Kemp announced that automotive parts manufacturer NVH Korea will establish a new facility in Locust Grove during an economic development trip to the Paris Air Show.
The project will create over 160 new jobs.
The project will bring $72 million in investment to Henry County.
This is the second economic development project Governor Kemp has announced that week while promoting Georgia to international job creators abroad.
NVH Korea is an automotive parts manufacturer based in the City of Ulsan and was established in 1984.
The company specializes in automotive noise, vibration, and heat-control parts.
The company currently produces these parts in Korea, Europe, and India.
The company already operates a manufacturing facility in Georgia that produces floor mats, cargo mats, and cargo trays.
The new facility will supply battery parts for electric vehicles (EVs) in the United States.
Customers for the new facility include Hyundai Mobis, Kia Georgia, and Hyundai Motor Group.
Ja Kyum Koo is the Chairman and CEO of NVH Korea.
The State of Georgia will serve as the company’s first EV battery component manufacturing site.
Cooperation between state agencies and company staff helped guide the project.
This marks the first step in the company's effort to contribute to the electrification of the mobility industry.
NVH Korea’s new facility will be located at Gardner Logistics Park on Colvin Drive in Locust Grove.
The facility will produce EV battery system components that protect, connect, and sense the performance of battery cells.
Operations are expected to begin in the second quarter of 2024.
Robert Price is the Mayor of the City of Locust Grove.
Carlotta Harrell is the Chair of the Henry County Board of Commissioners.
Jacob Lee is the Project Manager representing the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was executed in partnership with the Henry County Development Authority, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
Pat Wilson is the GDEcD Commissioner.
From July 1 through December 31, 2022, the automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment in Georgia.
Since 2020, Georgia has pursued job creation along the entire EV supply chain, resulting in more than $22.7 billion in investments and the creation of over 28,400 jobs.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia's reliable infrastructure network and great workforce allow job creators to find success in every community.
Governor Brian Kemp cited the incredible growth of Georgia's e-mobility and battery industries as a testament to the state's ability to support large suppliers like NVH Korea.

Ja Kyum Koo stated that choosing Georgia was the first step in contributing to the electrification of the mobility industry and expressed excitement about joining the Locust Grove and Henry County community.
Mayor Robert Price stated that NVH's investment will increase the manufacturing jobs base and integrate the city into Georgia’s electric vehicle ecosystem.
Carlotta Harrell stated that the announcement will add quality manufacturing jobs for citizens and continue to diversify the local economy.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that an economic transformation is happening across Georgia as manufacturers create future jobs aligned with cutting-edge technologies, and welcomed innovators establishing their first U.S. or e-mobility presence in the state.

links.context: Company website for more information about NVH Korea

source_title: EV Parts Supplier NVH Korea to Create Over 160 Jobs, Invest $72M in Henry County • The Georgia Virtue

facts: Cushman & Wakefield has arranged a new long-term 234,200-square-foot lease for acoustic and thermal management company NVH Korea at Gardner Logistics Park in Locust Grove, GA.
NVH Korea’s lease makes them the sixth major tenant for the park.
The park is located at 381 Davis Lake Rd.
Building 2 will be fully occupied by NVH Korea.
Building 2 was built as part of Phase 2 of a six-building Class A development that will total roughly 4 million square feet when complete.
The park will have two points of access to Interstate 75, both along US Route 23 and Market Place Boulevard and US Highway 42.
Cushman & Wakefield’s James Phillpott, Ray Stache, Lisa Pittman, and Helen Cauthen represented the landlord, Scannell Properties, in the negotiations for NVH Korea’s new lease.
The Cushman & Wakefield team has been overseeing leasing efforts for all three phases of the park’s development.
source_title: NVH Korea Leases Building 2 at Gardner Logistics Park - Connect CRE

facts: A Korean auto parts supplier is making its debut in the U.S. electric vehicle supply chain by setting up a new factory in Henry County.
The company will hire 160 people for the new facility.
The company will invest $72 million in the new facility.
NVH Korea’s Locust Grove facility will make battery parts for Hyundai Mobis.
Hyundai Mobis is tasked with assembling modules of batteries powering electric vehicles produced at the Hyundai Meta Plant near Savannah.
NVH will sell directly to the Georgia plants of both Kia and Hyundai.
The company is based in Ulsan, South Korea.
The company was founded in 1984 to make components that manage heat and reduce noise and vibrations.
For more than a decade, the company has operated a factory making floor mats, cargo mats, and cargo trays in Columbus through its subsidiary, AFS America LLC.
According to a news release from Gov. Brian Kemp’s office, NVH has factories in Korea, Europe, and India.
Georgia will be the first location where NVH places emphasis on electric vehicles.
Ja Kyum Koo is the chairman and CEO of NVH Korea.
Ja Kyum Koo stated that cooperation between state agencies and company staff helped guide them throughout the project.
Ja Kyum Koo stated this is the first step of the company’s effort to contribute to the electrification of the mobility industry.
Ja Kyum Koo expressed looking forward to joining the community of Locust Grove and Henry County.
The new factory is located at Gardner Logistics Park on Colvin Drive.
The new factory is expected to begin operations in the second quarter of 2024.
The manufacturing win is a significant boost for Henry County, which has seen increased investment in warehouses and e-commerce fulfillment centers run by foreign companies in recent years.
Henry County straddles Interstate 75 near Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, making it a prime location for warehousing.
Korean mattress maker Zinus USA invested $108 million to put a new factory in Henry County in 2020 to produce and distribute foam mattresses sold through various online platforms.
NVH is listed on the KOSDAQ stock market.
source_title: Henry County Attracts $72M Investment From Korean Auto Parts Supplier  - Global Atlanta

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that automotive parts manufacturer NVH Korea will establish a new facility in Locust Grove while on an economic development trip to the Paris Air Show.
The project will create over 160 new jobs and bring $72 million in investment to Henry County.
This is the second economic development project Governor Kemp has announced this week alone.

Governor Brian Kemp stated: “Thanks to our reliable infrastructure network connecting partners all over the world and a great workforce, job creators can find success in every community in our state.”

Governor Brian Kemp noted: “The incredible growth of the e-mobility and battery industries in Georgia is a testament to our ability to support large suppliers like NVH Korea. We’re proud to welcome them as the latest partner in making Georgia the epicenter of the industrial revolution of our time.”
NVH Korea is an automotive parts manufacturer based in the City of Ulsan and established in 1984.
The company specializes in automotive noise, vibration, and heat-control parts, which it currently produces in Korea, Europe, and India.
The company operates a manufacturing facility that produces floor mats, cargo mats, and cargo trays in Georgia.
The new facility will supply battery parts for electric vehicles (EVs) in the United States for manufacturers such as Hyundai Mobis, Kia Georgia, and Hyundai Motor Group.
Ja Kyum Koo, Chairman and CEO of NVH Korea, stated: “We are pleased to announce that we have chosen the State of Georgia as our company’s first EV battery component manufacturing site.”
Ja Kyum Koo noted that cooperation between state agencies and company staff helped guide them throughout the project.

Ja Kyum Koo stated: “It is the first step of our company’s effort to contribute to the electrification of the mobility industry, and we look forward to joining the community of Locust Grove and Henry County.”
NVH Korea’s new facility will be located at Gardner Logistics Park on Colvin Drive in Locust Grove.
The facility will produce the EV battery system components that protect, connect, and sense the performance of battery cells.
Operations are expected to begin in the second quarter of 2024.
Mayor Robert Price, City of Locust Grove, stated: “NVH’s investment will increase our manufacturing jobs base and make us part of Georgia’s electric vehicle ecosystem.”
Mayor Robert Price added: “We look forward to a successful partnership with NVH and the opportunities this investment creates.”

Carlotta Harrell, Chair of the Henry County Board of Commissioners, stated: “Today’s announcement by NVH will add a great mix of quality manufacturing jobs for our citizens, continuing to diversify our economy.”
Carlotta Harrell added: “We are excited to welcome NVH and for our community to join Georgia’s growing electric vehicle manufacturing base.”
Project Manager Jacob Lee represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was executed in partnership with the Henry County Development Authority, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated: “There is an economic transformation happening in communities across Georgia as manufacturers create the jobs of the future in line with cutting-edge technologies and industry trends.”

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson welcomed innovators establishing their first U.S. or e-mobility manufacturing presence, noting they enrich the local industry mix, provide well-paying jobs for Georgians, and continue to build on the statewide mobility ecosystem.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
From July 1 through December 31, 2022, the automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment in Georgia.
Since 2020, Georgia has pursued job creation along the entire supply chain, resulting in more than $22.7 billion in investments and the creation of over 28,400 jobs.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: EV Parts Supplier NVH Korea to Create Over 160 Jobs, Invest $72M in Henry County | Henry County Development Authority

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated: “We welcome innovators, many of which are establishing their first U.S. or e-mobility manufacturing presence, like NVH Korea. These companies enrich the local industry mix, provide well-paying jobs for Georgians, and continue to build on our already robust statewide mobility ecosystem. Many thanks to NVH Korea for selecting Georgia and congratulations to our community partners in Henry County.”

Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
From July 1 through December 31, 2022, the automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment in Georgia.
Since 2020, Georgia has pursued job creation along the entire EV supply chain, resulting in more than $22.7 billion in investments and the creation of over 28,400 jobs.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: EV parts supplier NVH Korea to create 160 jobs, invest $72M in Henry County | News | henryherald.com

facts: Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp announced they are leading a delegation of state officials on an international mission to the Republic of Korea to reinforce strategic economic development partnerships.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the Republic of Korea was the very first country he visited when he became governor because of the incredible partners located there.
Governor Kemp noted that friendships with Korean partners are bringing historic investments and jobs to communities all across Georgia.
Georgia’s connections to Korea go back decades, and this trip will further strengthen relationships having a generational impact on Georgia being the No. 1 state for business.
The state delegation consists of state elected officials, including President Pro Tem John Kennedy, Representative Soo Hong, and Representative Lehman Franklin.
The delegation includes representatives from the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), Georgia Environmental Protection Division, and Georgia Ports Authority.
Governor and Mrs. Kemp and the delegation will conduct meetings with existing industries operating or under construction in Georgia.
Industries to be met include Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, CJ Foodville, LG Group, SK Group, and Hanwha Qcells.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that connectivity leads to business, and relationships build the foundations of mutual progress.
Meeting with the leadership of existing industries at their headquarters provides a unique opportunity to learn more about their perspective on an industry and where it is headed.
By keeping the lines of communication open, Georgia can stay ahead of the curve in industry shifts, positioning itself at the forefront of new opportunities.
The State of Georgia has maintained continuous representation in Korea since 1985.
Korea’s Consular Office with jurisdiction across the Southeast, Puerto Rico, and U.S. Virgin Islands has been located in Georgia since 1976.
Korea has been one of the top sources of Foreign Direct Investment and international job creation in Georgia for the last three years.
In fiscal year 2023 alone, Korean companies announced more than $10 billion in investments and the creation of 12,605 jobs across the state.
Total trade between the State of Georgia and Korea totaled over $15.8 billion in calendar year 2023.
Trade with Korea accounted for nearly 10 percent of the state’s total trade in calendar year 2023.
source_title: Economic Partnerships in Korea - The ATL Airport Chamber

facts: Oneda Corporation utilizes the latest technologies in precision metal stamping, assembly, tool and die designs.
The company handles prototyping of initial design to full mass production.
Oneda is a customer driven and process centered organization striving to ensure the highest standards of quality, customer satisfaction and timelines of all projects.

Working in partnership with customers, Oneda uses combined extensive experience in flexible manufacturing processes to significantly reduce tooling expenses, improved overall unit costs and increase production capacity.
The company's location type is listed as HQ.
Oneda Corporation opened in 1989.
Annual revenue estimate is $10 to $20 million.
Employee count is 50 to 99.
Michael Brooks serves as Marketing / CS Manager.
Hiro Oneda serves as President.
Gary Holloway serves as Chief Financial Officer.
Sue Hudson serves as Purchasing / PC Manager.
David Edwards serves as Administration Executive.
source_title: Oneda Corporation Columbus GA, 31907 – Manta.com

facts: Oneda Corporation is a family-owned, Georgia-based contract manufacturer specializing in precision metal stamping, assembly, and tool and die designs.
The company utilizes innovative robotic transfer and progressive stamping capabilities with press sizes ranging from 25 to 500 tons.
Oneda Corporation is located in Columbus, Georgia.
It operates as a subsidiary of Oneda Electric Corporation.
The company serves the automotive, medical, heating and air, transportation, refrigeration, and consumer electronics industries.
Oneda Corporation is an American Made, third-generation, family-owned manufacturer based in Georgia.
The company maintains an internal Die Shop Apprenticeship program to train the next generation of tool and die makers.
Oneda Corporation holds ISO 9001:2015, ISO 14001:2015, and IATF16949:2016 certifications.
The company was formed to support US OEMs for custom metal stampings, assemblies, and tool & die operations.
Operations include Stamping, Tool & Die, Electronic Assembly, Main Electronic Assembly Plant, Assembly Operations Plant, Secondary Stampings, Large Metal Stamping, and Metal/Plastic Assembly.
On average, Oneda's team members have worked at the manufacturing plant for more than 10 years.
The company states it is always looking for great people to join its team.
source_title: Oneda | Georgia Metal Stamping Company Focused on Complex Parts

facts: Oneda Corporation is a Columbus, Georgia-based, third-generation, family-owned, contract precision metal stamping manufacturer.
The company was founded in 1989 and operates as a subsidiary of Japan-based Oneda Electric Corp.
The Columbus location employs 67 people.
The company primarily serves the automotive and heating and air conditioning industries.
Oneda Corporation previously worked with the Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership (GaMEP) at Georgia Tech and the Southeastern Trade Adjustment Assistance Center (SETAAC).
GaMEP provided marketing services to Oneda, including developing a strategic marketing plan with goals, tactics, and a progress tracking system.
GaMEP assisted with implementation, email and social media strategizing, and content writing.
GaMEP redesigned Oneda's website and revamped its LinkedIn page.
The project included writing a tip sheet to generate leads and writing customer success stories.
GaMEP trained Michael Brooks on content development so he could manage it internally.
Michael Brooks serves as Oneda’s manager of marketing, customer service, and logistics and is also an engineer.
Katie Takacs serves as the group manager for marketing at GaMEP.
Following the successful launch of Oneda's website and revamped LinkedIn page, parent company Oneda Electric Corp. began working to upgrade its own site.
GaMEP identified 100 companies and purchasing managers across eight states as potential new customers for Oneda.
GaMEP created a three-step lead generation campaign targeting the identified potential customers.
Oneda engaged the GaMEP team on an energy assessment analysis that resulted in decreased energy consumption at the company.
source_title: Oneda Corporation Success Story | GaMEP at Georgia Tech

facts: Located in Columbus, Georgia.
Yoshihiro Oneda serves as Chief Executive Officer.

Oneda Corporation Company Profile | Columbus, Georgia |
