facts: Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., a Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT) company, announces its new line of Cushman Shuttle personnel carriers powered by the company’s exclusive 72-volt AC electric drivetrain.

The leader in AC electric drivetrains in the golf-car industry under its E-Z-GO brand, Textron Specialized Vehicles has incorporated this innovative technology, with its improved energy efficiency and increased range, into its Cushman Shuttle 4, Shuttle 6 and Shuttle 8 models.

Kevin Holleran, president and CEO of Textron Specialized Vehicles, stated that the newest Cushman Shuttles are the latest example of the company's commitment to developing vehicles that support the evolving needs and demands of its customers.
The company leverages the proven energy savings, power and range benefits of its 72-volt AC technology to move customers’ employees and guests more efficiently and cost-effectively.
AC drivetrain technology is superior to DC electric power due to its alternating flow of charge, allowing it to deliver more power and performance while using less energy.
The 72-volt AC drivetrain on the newest Shuttle models generates longer battery life, enabling vehicles to travel further between charges, and delivers remarkable hill-climbing capabilities.
Shuttle vehicles are commonly found hard at work in a range of applications, including golf courses, resorts, airports, educational facilities, and college and commercial campuses.

Cushman Shuttles feature standard headlights, brush guard, hour meter and USB port, and can be equipped with a range of options and accessories, including a limited-slip differential, windshields, canopies, and more.
The Cushman Shuttle 4 features seating for up to four people, with a stake-side cargo bed to carry equipment, luggage or personal belongings.
The Shuttle 6 features seating for up to six people.
The Shuttle 8 can carry up to eight.
Cushman vehicles are designed and manufactured in Augusta, Ga. by Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc.
Since 1901, Cushman vehicles have been recognized light-transportation solutions for turf, commercial and industrial applications.
Today, Cushman products include more than 15 different vehicle models.
The Hauler utility vehicles are ideal for tough hauling and towing tasks in outdoor environments.
The Titan is a fully electric material-handling vehicle for indoor environments with a load capacity of 3,000 pounds.

Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc. is a leading global manufacturer of golf cars, utility and personal transportation vehicles, snowmobiles, side-by-sides, all-terrain vehicles, professional turf-care equipment, and ground support equipment.
The company markets products under the E-Z-GO, Cushman, Arctic Cat, Textron Off Road, Jacobsen, Dixie Chopper, Ransomes, TUG, Douglas, Premier and Safeaero brands.
Textron Inc. is a multi-industry company that leverages its global network of aircraft, defense, industrial and finance businesses to provide customers with innovative solutions and services.

facts: Jinhua Suncart and three other defendants have settled a legal complaint filed by Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., a Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT) company, for infringement of patents related to TSV’s E-Z-GO® RXV® golf-car platform.

Under the terms of the agreement, Jinhua Suncart and defendants Vitacci Motorcycles Inc., Cougar International Inc., and TPSP LLC (doing business as Turbo Powersports) will stop sales of all products within the scope of TSV's patent enforcement complaint.
The defendants must also stop using E-Z-GO’s registered RXV® and TXT® trademarks and remove all online listings that promote the products named in the complaint.
The settlement resolves a complaint filed by TSV in U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Georgia.

TSV General Counsel Rocco Sica stated that TSV will vigorously defend its intellectual property, which is the direct result of investments to generate innovation for products in the golf-car industry and other served industries.
The defendants were investigated for copying technologies developed through a substantial investment of time and money by TSV.
TSV warned other parties copying innovations that it is watching them and will take any appropriate actions necessary to protect innovations created by its employees.
E-Z-GO was founded in Augusta, Ga. in 1954 as a globally renowned leader in the design and manufacture of golf cars and personal transport vehicles.
E-Z-GO is known for innovation in sustainable electric-vehicle and powertrain technology.
E-Z-GO models include RXV® and TXT® fleet golf cars; Freedom® and Valor personal golf cars; and E-Z-GO Express™ personal utility vehicles.
E-Z-GO's latest innovation is the Liberty™, the industry’s first vehicle to offer four forward-facing seats in a compact, golf-car-sized footprint.
Employees of Textron Specialized Vehicles are committed to a culture of compliance and reduction of emissions.
E-Z-GO became part of Textron Inc. in 1960 and currently operates as part of the company’s Textron Specialized Vehicles division.
Textron Inc. is a multi-industry company that leverages its global network of aircraft, defense, industrial, and finance businesses to provide customers with innovative solutions and services.
Textron Inc. owns brands including Bell, Cessna, Beechcraft, Pipistrel, Jacobsen, Kautex, Lycoming, E-Z-GO, Arctic Cat, and Textron Systems.
source_title: JINHUA SUNCART SETTLES WITH TEXTRON SPECIALIZED VEHICLES INC. AND ITS COMPLAINT FOR INFRINGEMENT OF INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RELATED TO E-Z-GO® RXV GOLF-CAR PLATFORM - Golf Car Advisor

facts: ALBAform designs, develops, and manufactures precision metal components, frames, and weldments.
The company serves the automotive, agricultural, and aerospace industries.
ALBAform has a seasoned international team of industry experts that leverage their knowledge and understanding of their respective fields.
source_title: ALBAform | The Org

facts: ALBAform Inc. is owned by its Czech parent company ALBA-METAL.
The company designs, develops, and manufactures precision metal components with a focus on the automotive industry.
ALBAform leverages over 20 years of ALBA-METAL’s metal production experience alongside its own industry expertise.
The company is located in Flowery Branch, Georgia.
ALBAform is growing quickly to service its partners and customers.
In addition to manufacturing capabilities, the company offers consignment stock systems using standardized packaging and transport units to minimize inventory and transportation costs.
The facility features the latest fully automated CNC bending technology.
The facility operates 16 welding robots.
source_title: ALBAform Plant Tour -  Georgia Manufacturing Alliance

facts: ALBAform is a woman-owned manufacturing company in the automotive industry.
ALBAform has operations in Flowery Branch, Georgia, Czechia, and Mexico.
ALBAform's clients include BMW, Ford Motor Co., and Volvo.
ALBAform is a client of the Enterprise Innovation Institute’s Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership.
The Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership assisted ALBAform with leadership and business strategy consulting, and workforce development.

Chasten McCrary, a strategy consultant with the Enterprise Innovation Institute’s chief of staff, serves as a student consultant for ALBAform through Georgia Tech’s Scheller College of Business International Practicum Course.
McCrary is a second-year Evening MBA student selected to partake in the program.

The Bergmark Family Study Abroad Scholarship awarded to McCrary is named for Dick Bergmark (IM ‘75, Hon. PhD ’22), one of the Scheller College of Business’ main philanthropists and immediate past chair of the school’s advisory board.
source_title: EI2’s Chasten McCrary Awarded Bergmark Family Study Abroad Scholarship for Scheller Project in Czechia - Enterprise Innovation Institute

facts: Anovion Technologies officially broke ground for its new Bainbridge facility just over two years ago.
There has been little visible construction at the industrial park site since groundbreaking, though developments have remained ongoing in the background.
Patrick Donnelly serves as Anovion’s Chief Commercial Officer and provided updates on the progress of the Bainbridge facility.
During a December 2024 Bainbridge city council meeting, Chairman Chip Dunn informed the council that Anovion planned to begin construction on a “mini-mill” by mid-2025, to be completed by 2026.
Full production for the facility was scheduled at 2028.
Donnelly stated that the company is still targeting 2026 to kick off the “mini-mill” as well as production in 2028 for the full facility.
Donnelly noted that exact start and completion dates are dependent on several variables and he could not provide precise dates.
In November of last year, Anovion was announced as being in talks with the Export-Import Bank of the United States for $400 million in project financing.
Donnelly stated that they are still working with the Ex-Im Bank to meet the criteria to secure the financing.
The financing process is dependent on factors, some of which are not completely under Anovion’s control.
Donnelly stated that Anovion remains confident that this financing source will be part of the final package to build out the Bainbridge facility.
Anovion’s main product is synthetic graphite, a key component in electric vehicles, and federal policy decisions and bills have impacted the company.
The most recent influencing factors include the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, as well as tariffs.
Donnelly stated that Anovion is still assessing the impacts of the OBBBA, with some positives and negatives for battery cell producers.
A positive impact identified was restrictions placed on the use of foreign entity of concern (FEOC) material assistance.
A negative result identified is the loss of EV tax credits that had been a part of the Biden administration’s Inflation Reduction Act.

Donnelly stated that as 95%+ of synthetic graphite used for battery cells currently comes from China, there should be tailwinds for Anovion’s value proposition to domestic cell makers coming out of the OBBBA.
Donnelly added that ongoing uncertainty around tariffs and trade policy plays a significant role in the timing of the project and contracts.

Donnelly concluded that Anovion wants to get into business at scale in Bainbridge as fast as prudently possible to address both the national security needs of the US and build out a critical part of the battery supply chain of the energy transition throughout North America.
source_title: Anovion still aiming for 2026 construction | The Post-Searchlight

facts: Anovion announced plans to invest $800 million to build a factory in southwest Georgia.
The new Georgia facility will hire 400 workers.
Anovion Technologies is headquartered in Chicago.
The company will manufacture synthetic graphite anodes at the Bainbridge plant.
Production of synthetic graphite anodes at the Georgia plant is scheduled to start in 2025.
The facility supports Georgia's broader initiative to attract EV manufacturers and their supply chains.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated the project advances his goal of establishing Georgia as the nation's electric mobility capital.
The majority of battery-grade graphite is currently manufactured outside the United States.
Since 2020, Georgia has announced over 40 EV-related projects.
These 40+ projects pledge a combined $22 billion in investment.
These 40+ projects pledge the creation of 28,000 jobs.
Anovion was formed last year by merging the graphite operations of Pyrotek and Amsted Graphite Materials.
The formation of Anovion included new investment from private equity firm Monomyth Group.
Anovion currently operates existing facilities in Sanborn, New York.
Anovion currently operates an existing facility in Clarksburg, West Virginia.
The company previously announced plans to build a graphite factory with a 35,000-ton annual capacity near Muscle Shoals, Alabama.
Anovion received a $117 million grant under the federal infrastructure law last year.
The $117 million grant will help finance the planned Alabama plant and upgrades to the existing New York facility.
Anovion CEO Eric Stopka cited Georgia's workforce, research universities, and growing solar energy production as key attraction factors.
CEO Eric Stopka stated the company aims to secure the nation's electrified future with support from Georgia partners and the federal government.
Anovion executives aim to scale production to 150,000 tons annually in the coming years.
Synthetic graphite is manufactured by heating carbon-based products like petroleum coke (an oil refining byproduct) at super-high temperatures for extended periods.
The high-temperature furnaces used to produce synthetic graphite require massive amounts of electricity, making power costs a primary location factor.
Anovion may qualify for a Georgia state income tax credit allowing an annual deduction of $3,000 per job for five years.
The state income tax credit requires employees to earn at least $31,300 annually.
The potential state income tax credits could total up to $6 million.
Anovion may also qualify for property tax breaks from the city of Bainbridge and Decatur County.
The announcement marks the second major industrial project secured by Bainbridge in two years.
Bainbridge is located in the far southwest corner of Georgia.
Danimer Scientific, a biodegradable plastic manufacturer, announced plans in 2021 to build a $700 million factory in Bainbridge.
The Danimer Scientific project will hire 400 employees.
Decatur County Commission Chairman Pete Stephens stated that diversifying the local industry mix is necessary for regional competitiveness and growth.
source_title: Anovion, maker of graphite for electric batteries, plans $800 million factory in southwest Georgia | Georgia Public Broadcasting

entity_name: Georgia Clean-Energy and EV Supply Chain
facts: Olivia Amyette is the founding owner of Infinite Energy Advisors and the Solar Knowledge Institute in Cleveland, Georgia.
Infinite Energy Advisors is a solar energy company located in Cleveland, Georgia, a small town about an hour and a half northeast of Atlanta.
The Solar Knowledge Institute (SKI) is a workforce development program that prepares workers for careers in the solar industry.
Amyette stated that the overall success of her company would not really be likely if the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) wasn't in place, noting it drives demand for solar.
QCells employs nearly 1,800 people in Dalton, Georgia, producing 30,000 solar panels a day.
QCells employs another 750 workers at a plant in Cartersville, Georgia (population 23,100).
A 2024 expansion at the QCells Cartersville plant was powered largely by IRA tax credits incentivizing domestic manufacturing of clean energy products.
The Cartersville facility manufactures finished solar panels and semiconductive wafers.
When fully operational, QCells expects to produce 8.4 gigawatts of solar in Georgia, equal to about 16 million solar panels.
The IRA expanded the Investment Tax Credit (ITC) for clean energy projects, providing tax credits from about a quarter to a third over the coming decade.
Georgia does not offer consumers one-to-one net metering for solar panels.
Businesses in Georgia rely heavily on tax incentives to grow solar investments; Amyette estimates 90% of her clients would not switch to solar without the tax credit.
Georgia ranked in the top five states nationally for total clean investment from early 2023 to early 2024, with more than half of that $12.4 million going to manufacturing facilities.
The state has created at least 17,421 clean energy jobs across Georgia, according to Climate Power.
Rural Georgia has seen 13 projects resulting in over $2.23 billion invested and 3,479 jobs created in the two years since the IRA was enacted.
SKI is the only apprenticeship program with a specific focus on solar energy construction registered with the Georgia Department of Labor.
SKI offers a two-year, 4,000-hour apprenticeship program combining classroom instruction and on-the-job learning, plus a-la-carte courses in topics like sales.
Since launching in 2022, SKI has trained about 24 individuals, half from the Cleveland area.
Cartersville is expected to see 5,750 new jobs between the QCells Plant and the Hyundai factory.
QCells hired 600 people within the first six months of opening their Dalton plant.
Georgia has attracted more than a dozen manufacturing projects for electric vehicles, school buses, and batteries since 2018.
Rivian proposed a plant on a 2,000-acre parcel where four counties meet, expected to create 7,500 jobs; production was initially expected by the end of 2024 but is currently on hold.
Kia in West Point, Georgia (population ~3,500) is expanding its automotive manufacturing plant to add a line for electric vehicles.
Gov. Brian Kemp has promoted Georgia's resources and workforce for projects like the 1.5 million-square-foot Anovion Technologies facility, which will make battery components in southeastern Georgia.
In 2023, the Georgia General Assembly began funneling state money to the Rural Workforce Housing Initiative to provide 0-3% interest construction loans.
The initiative has awarded $46 million to housing and infrastructure loans, including single-family homes, multi-family senior apartments, and water/sewer capacity projects.
Governor Brian Kemp stated these grants are the first step in meeting community needs amid incredible growth.
The Dalton-Whitfield County Joint Development Authority received $1.5 million for street and stormwater drainage construction for 39 new homes.

The Stephens County Development Authority received $478,400 to build a sanitary sewer lift station to allow constructing 318 housing units near an expanding regional industrial park; SungEl has announced plans to begin manufacturing batteries in Toccoa, contributing to the project.
Hedge funds are buying properties with reasonable mortgages around $850 per month and renting them back at $1,600 a month, squeezing the rental market.
Most housing for new workers is expected to be built closer to Atlanta and Augusta, each about an hour's drive away from rural sites.

Community leaders often aren't aware of constraints in rental housing supply or how lower-income residents might be affected, according to research by Kim Skobba at the University of Georgia’s Housing and Demographics Research Center.
A survey of 164 decision-makers in rural, small town Georgia found that many are unaware of rental housing constraints.
source_title: Georgia Clean-Energy Boom Producing Jobs, Housing Shortfalls | Climate Central

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Anovion Technologies, a supplier of premium synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries, will build a new manufacturing facility in Bainbridge.
The project will create more than 400 jobs.
The project will involve over $800 million in investment in Decatur County.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that Georgia is fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Brian P. Kemp noted that job creators are recognizing that every corner of the state has the resources and workforce needed for success.
Anovion is a climate tech-driven advanced materials company.
Anovion is one of the first qualified U.S. suppliers of synthetic graphite anode materials for use in e-mobility applications.
Graphite is the largest battery material used in electric vehicles by mass, more than copper, nickel, manganese, cobalt, and lithium.
Eric Stopka, Chief Executive Officer of Anovion, stated the company is opening its first large-scale expansion manufacturing facility in Georgia.

Eric Stopka noted that Georgia’s strong technical workforce and top-tier schools will help advance research partnerships and contribute to a highly skilled talent pipeline for Anovion’s innovation and climate technology jobs.
Eric Stopka mentioned encouragement by the region’s growing availability of clean energy, resulting in increased sustainability for Anovion’s manufacturing process.
Anovion’s new facility will be 1,500,000 square feet.
The facility will utilize proprietary furnace technologies.
The facility is located at Downrange Industrial Park in Bainbridge.
The facility will be the first of its size and scale in North America.
The facility will produce synthetic graphite for use in batteries that power EVs, electric storage systems, electronics, military applications, and other products.
Operations are expected to begin in late 2025.
The company will be hiring for a variety of technical, production, and engineering roles.
The company will also hire for administrative and management positions.
Bainbridge Mayor Edward Reynolds welcomed Anovion to Bainbridge.
Mayor Edward Reynolds noted the $800 million investment and creation of over 400 high-paying, quality jobs as a huge win for the community.
Pete Stephens, Chairman of the Decatur County Board of Commissioners, stated Decatur County leadership has focused for many years on attracting quality companies and jobs.
Pete Stephens noted that diversifying the industry mix is necessary to remain competitive and grow.
Project Manager Taylor Walden represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project is in partnership with the City of Bainbridge, Development Authority of Bainbridge, Decatur County, University System of Georgia, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated much of the critical supply chain for e-mobility and manufacturing is currently sourced outside the U.S.
Pat Wilson described Anovion as an innovative industry leader that fills that need and delivers another critical component to Georgia.

Pat Wilson noted a diverse mix of public-private partnerships are coming together to support the next revolution in the automotive industry, from working with utility partners and establishing infrastructure to attracting and retaining top-talent through the University System of Georgia.
Building on automotive industry assets, Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, 40 EV-related projects have announced more than $21.9 billion in investment and 28,000 jobs in Georgia.
More than 10 of these projects are battery manufacturers, recyclers, and suppliers.
These battery-related projects represent approximately $8.5 billion in investment and 6,000 jobs.
source_title: Anovion Technologies to Create Over 400 Jobs in Bainbridge, Invest $800M in Manufacturing Facility - Augusta CEO

facts: Georgia scored the top spot in Site Selection’s annual Prosperity Cup ranking of state-level successful investment recruiting in the previous year.
The headline on Site Selection regarding the award is “Georgia’s Long Game Secures Its Claim to the Cup.”
Georgia unseated previous #1 North Carolina, which had claimed the top spot in the ranking for the past three years.
Press releases celebrating the award were issued by the Governor’s Office and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that job creators choosing Georgia get access to global markets through logistics assets like Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport and some of the fastest-growing ports in the nation, along with state and local partners.
Georgia attracted 318 new & expanded facilities in 2023.
A top criterion for the Prosperity Cup is a state’s ability to attract new and expanded facilities in the previous year.
Georgia led all states with 318 new and expanded projects, including the expanded scope of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant is described as the largest project in the history of Georgia.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant was originally announced in 2022.
The South Korean automaker plans to produce a diverse range of innovative Hyundai, Genesis and Kia electric vehicles (EVs) at the facility.
Hyundai also plans a new battery manufacturing facility northwest of Atlanta in Bartow County.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $7.59 billion to develop HMGMA.
The Hyundai project will create 8,500 direct jobs for Georgians.
Georgia received a boost in the Prosperity Cup ranking when Hyundai expanded its commitment in 2023 by an additional $2 billion and 400 new jobs.
Anovion Technologies announced plans for an $800-million, 400-job synthetic graphite manufacturing facility in Decatur County.
Meissner Corporation plans to double its Georgia manufacturing footprint with a new $250 million facility in Athens scheduled to open in 2026.
GDEcD Commission Pat Wilson credited the state’s pro-business environment as a solid contributing factor to Georgia's top ranking.
Pat Wilson stated that taking the top spot reaffirms that the Georgia Way works, citing decades of partnerships, intentional economic development groundwork, and consistent pro-business policies.
Florida, North Carolina, and South Carolina earned spots behind Georgia in Site Selection’s ranking of “South Atlantic” states.
North Carolina earned the #2 spot in the “South Atlantic” ranking for attracting new and expanded facilities.
South Carolina earned the #3 spot in the “South Atlantic” ranking.
Florida earned the #4 spot in the “South Atlantic” ranking.

Other states earning the #1 spot for attracting new investment in different regions included Ohio (East North Central), Texas (South Central), Pennsylvania (Northeast), Kansas (West North Central), and Arizona (Mountain).
source_title: Georgia's Top Spot: Investment Recruitment Success in 2023

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) announced that Georgia earned the No. 1 spot in Site Selection’s annual Prosperity Cup rankings for successful investment recruitment in 2023.
The Prosperity Cup criteria include total values of new and expanded facilities, business-climate attractiveness, and workforce readiness.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that he proudly tells companies across the country and the world how great Georgia is for operating and growing a business.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that the Site Selection award adds to a decade of evidence that the way they do business in the Peach State is working.
Companies choosing Georgia gain access to global markets through logistics assets like Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport and some of the fastest-growing ports in the nation.
According to Site Selection, Georgia attracted 318 new and expanded facilities in 2023 to secure the No. 1 ranking.
The expanded scope for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is the largest project in state history.
HMGMA announced an additional 400 new jobs and $2 billion in investment last year, bringing its total investment to $7.59 billion and netting 8,500 new jobs for hardworking Georgians.
Anovion Technologies selected southwest Georgia for its $800 million, 400-job synthetic graphite manufacturing facility in May and broke ground at the site in June of last year.
In northeast Georgia, Meissner Corporation plans to create more than 1,700 new jobs at a new campus that will more than double the company’s manufacturing footprint in the U.S.
Site Selection’s Mark Arend stated that the Prosperity Cup recognizes state-level economic development competitiveness.
Georgia’s consistent delivery of location attributes required by capital investors demonstrates to readers that it competes to win, according to Mark Arend.
Following HMGMA’s groundbreaking in October 2022, Georgia welcomed more than 10 Hyundai and automotive suppliers in 2023 reaching middle Georgia and beyond.
These Hyundai and automotive suppliers will ultimately create over 4,300 new jobs and bring $1.2 billion in combined investment.
Kia’s facility in West Point and the Toyota campus in Pendergrass both announced expansions during 2023, creating a combined 440 new jobs and $286 million in investment.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia’s economic development team is truly collaborative, including sister state agencies, utility partners, local economic developers, and many others.
Taking the top spot in the 2024 Prosperity Cup reaffirms that the Georgia Way works, according to Commissioner Pat Wilson.
Decades of partnerships, intentional economic development from the community level to the state level, and consistent pro-business policies have led to these positive results.
Commissioner Pat Wilson thanked Georgia leaders throughout the state for supporting efforts that make a difference for Georgia families.

links.context: Full article on Georgia's selection and 2024 Prosperity Cup info

source_title: Georgia Scores Top Spot in Competitive Economic Rankings - LaGrange CEO

facts: Anovion Technologies held a groundbreaking event on Tuesday for its new facility.
The remarks at the groundbreaking were unusually partisan for a factory groundbreaking.
Anovion Technologies received more than $100 million in federal financing from the infrastructure law signed by President Joe Biden to refine graphite for electric batteries.
Anovion Technologies is making an $800 million investment in rural Bainbridge, Georgia, in the state's far southwest corner.
Anovion Technologies' facility will create 400 new jobs.
Anovion Technologies will produce synthetic graphite, a key ingredient for lithium batteries.
The Chicago-based firm Anovion Technologies won $117 million in federal financing to build and improve its factories.
Anovion Technologies may claim 10% federal tax credits on the costs of producing graphite under the Inflation Reduction Act.
Anovion Technologies may claim 30% federal tax credits on its factory investment under the Inflation Reduction Act.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp attacked President Joe Biden's electric vehicle policy at the Anovion Technologies groundbreaking.

Governor Kemp stated that Georgia’s electric mobility boom is happening because the state is second to none for companies looking to invest, relocate, expand, and innovate, not because the federal government favors a few companies.
Governor Kemp has always opposed the Inflation Reduction Act, which is pumping billions into electric vehicle subsidies.

Governor Kemp disagrees with the Inflation Reduction Act's domestic content standards, which make tax incentives available only when the vehicle, battery, and key raw materials are made in the United States.
Governor Kemp wants to make Georgia the “e-mobility capital of the nation” as his second-term legacy.
Governor Kemp stated that unlike top-down systems like China’s, Georgia is not dictating how growth happens or picking winners and losers, but letting the market drive innovation and expansion.
Governor Kemp attributed Anovion's choice of location to state and local officials who do not posture, showboat, or try to steal credit.

Governor Kemp said it is wrong to credit Biden for the boom, noting that Rivian Automotive announced a $5 billion plant east of Atlanta in December 2021, while Hyundai announced in May 2022, both before Biden signed the Inflation Reduction Act.
Democratic Senator Jon Ossoff shared the stage at the groundbreaking but was invited but not scheduled for a speaking role.
Senator Ossoff is Georgia’s most high-profile supporter of Biden’s electric vehicle policies and could be Kemp's rival for the Senate seat in 2026.
Senator Ossoff argued that Georgia's investment boom would not have happened without Democratic policies.

Senator Ossoff called it bizarre to attend a groundbreaking and launch a political attack on the policy that made it possible, calling the governor's actions a panicked political tantrum over federal manufacturing success.
Senator Ossoff has claimed credit for Biden and Democrats, including for solar panel plant expansions in northwest Georgia.
Fellow Democratic Senator Raphael Warnock proposed making electric vehicle tax credits more flexible.
Hyundai Motor Group is building a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
Hyundai Motor Group has said the domestic content tax credits are unfair because its electric vehicles are not currently eligible.
Governor Kemp referenced Hyundai's criticism of the tax credit approach, stating “that approach simply doesn’t work.”
Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced a $4.3 billion electric battery plant in May at Hyundai’s new complex.
Senator Ossoff assertively trumpeted the Hyundai-LG battery plant news while Kemp was in Israel, a move that chafed some Kemp administration officials.
Rivian Automotive announced a $5 billion plant east of Atlanta in December 2021.
Hyundai Motor Group announced its Ellabell plant investment in May 2022.

Georgia has been a top beneficiary of the nationwide electric vehicle investment boom, with more than 40 electric vehicle-related projects since 2020 pledging $22.7 billion of investment and 28,400 jobs in the state.
Former President Donald Trump told the Georgia Republican convention he would abolish Biden's electric vehicle policies on his first day in office.
Kemp did not attend the convention where Trump spoke out of dissatisfaction with the state party's leadership.
Kemp has tried to persuade Republicans to break off their love affair with Trump while opposing a Democratic president whose administration lavished EV makers with billions in incentives.
source_title: Georgia governor attacks Biden's electric vehicle policy at federally-backed battery material maker

facts: President Joe Biden spoke at the groundbreaking last September for Intel Corp.’s $20-billion semiconductor chip plant facility in New Albany, Ohio.

Biden touted the Intel project as one of many that would help transform the Rust Belt into a “Silicon Heartland,” thanks in part to funding provided by the CHIPS and Science Act he had recently signed into law.
Participants in the two-plant Intel project include Gilbane Building Co., which is leading early excavation work, and Bechtel as general contractor on Phase 1 of plant design and construction.
Intel says the Intel Ohio facility work is supporting 7,000 construction jobs.
Intel is set to benefit from a package of incentives and tax breaks worth about $2 billion, according to Ohio’s Dept. of Development.
The Intel project is part of what the U.S. Treasury Dept. calls “a striking surge” in manufacturing construction.

In an analysis released June 27, U.S. Treasury Dept. officials say real manufacturing construction spending has doubled since the end of 2021 as a result of supportive legislation like the CHIPS Act, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, and the Inflation Reduction Act.
U.S. Census Bureau data show the value of construction put in place totaled $7.4 billion in December 2021 and rose nearly every month since then to an estimated $15.3 billion in April.
Manufacturing construction starts increased 185% last year, according to the Dodge Construction Network.
Technology manufacturing has driven the boom in manufacturing construction spending, according to the Treasury Dept. analysis.

Construction spending for computer, electronics and electrical manufacturing has nearly quadrupled since the start of last year, even after accounting for material and labor cost increases by deflating spending using the Producer Price Index.
Construction for other manufacturing segments has remained mostly consistent with their respective long-term levels.

The CHIPS Act and other legislation have included a focus on technology manufacturing, providing billions of dollars to incentivize the production of items such as electric vehicle batteries, solar energy equipment and semiconductor chips.
According to the Semiconductor Industry Association, more than 50 new semiconductor manufacturing-related projects have been announced in the U.S. since lawmakers introduced the CHIPS Act in 2020.

Treasury Dept. analysts wrote that “the macroeconomic situation, policy implementation, and a range of other conditions will continue to shape construction manufacturing spending in the months and years to come.”
Treasury Dept. analysts also noted that “while today’s striking trend may cool, the CHIPS Act has fostered an ongoing opportunity for investment in this sector.”
The Treasury report's release comes as President Biden is preparing for the 2024 presidential election.
The White House has been pushing the highlights of “Bidenomics” aimed at growing the middle class with public investments.
President Biden is facing pushback from some Republicans over who deserves credit for the economic successes.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp (R) spoke June 27 at the groundbreaking of Anovion Technologies' battery plant project in Bainbridge, Ga.

Gov. Kemp said the growth of EV-related manufacturing in Georgia was a result of the state being friendly to companies, rather than the federal government putting its “thumb on the scale, favoring a few companies.”
Anovion is receiving $117 million for the $800 million project via the Inflation Reduction Act.
In May, Intel said crews had started placing concrete for the foundation of its Ohio facility.
The Intel Ohio plant is scheduled to open in 2025.
source_title: ENR logo

facts: Ascend Elements achieved a significant milestone in domestic critical minerals recovery last month by producing >99% pure, recycled lithium carbonate (Li2CO3) from black mass for the first time at commercial scale.
Black mass is a dark, powery material recovered from used lithium-ion batteries during the recycling process.

The company operated a commercial-scale (3 kiloton per year) lithium recovery line at its battery recycling facility in Covington, Georgia last month to demonstrate the commercial viability of lithium recovery and recycling.
Ascend Elements plans to produce >15,000 metric tons of recycled Li2CO3 per year in the United States and Europe by 2027.

In addition to the 3,000 metric-ton-per-year lithium recovery line in Covington, Georgia, Ascend Elements plans to operate a state-of-the-art battery materials facility and advanced lithium recovery line at a new location in Europe.
Linh Austin, President and CEO of Ascend Elements, stated that the company is at the center of three macro trends: Electrification, localization and critical minerals.
Austin noted that their production of recycled lithium carbonate is an important milestone across all three of these trends.

Austin added that by producing recycled lithium carbonate at commercial scale in the United States and Europe, the team will advance energy independence, critical minerals security and electrification for the Western world.
Lithium carbonate is a highly valuable critical mineral listed on the official U.S. federal government list of critical minerals published in August 2025.
Lithium carbonate is used to manufacture lithium-ion batteries, glass and ceramics.
According to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, 98% of U.S. lithium carbonate imports come from South America.
Europe also imports most of its lithium carbonate from South America and China.
The United States and Europe currently have limited capacity to mine and process lithium carbonate.
Austin thanked the team in Georgia for working tirelessly on the project, stating that recovering lithium from black mass is the most efficient way to source this critical mineral.
Austin noted that the concentration of lithium in black mass is 10 times higher than in Chilean brine pools.

A recently published life cycle assessment found Ascend Elements’ lithium recovery process is significantly cleaner than traditional mining processes, producing 86% lower carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2e) emissions and 97% lower particulate matter 2.5 (PM 2.5) emissions than spodumene mining.
The same assessment found the process produces 37% lower CO2e emissions and 81% lower PM 2.5 emissions than Chilean brine extraction.
By 2030, Ascend Elements aims to achieve 99% lower CO2e emissions and up to 99.7% lower PM 2.5 emissions through additional decarbonization efforts.
Founded in 2015, Ascend Elements is a leading provider of domestic critical minerals and engineered battery materials for the lithium-ion battery industry.
The company produces sustainable Li2CO3 and NMC pCAM made from recycled battery materials.

Ascend Elements’ patented Hydro-to-Cathode® direct cathode precursor synthesis technology produces new pCAM material from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods, resulting in improved economics and lowered carbon emissions.
source_title: Ascend Elements produces recycled lithium carbonate from used Li-ion batteries at commercial scale - Chemical Engineering | Page 1

facts: Ascend Elements announced plans to invest $310 million in Phase 1 — and up to $1 billion over several possible phases — to build a sustainable lithium-ion battery materials facility in Hopkinsville, Ky.
Ground-breaking for the facility is expected in Q4 2022.
The first-of-its-kind manufacturing facility, known as "Apex 1", will utilize the company's Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis process technology.

The facility will take in black mass from recycled electric vehicle batteries and produce enough lithium-ion battery precursor and sustainable cathode active material to equip up to 250,000 electric vehicles per year.
This closed-loop manufacturing system generates minimal waste and carbon emissions compared to traditional cathode manufacturing.
Apex 1 will feature onsite chemical recycling capabilities and a wastewater treatment plant.

Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear stated that Kentucky has taken the initiative to ensure the state will be a leader in the automotive industry of the future, calling Ascend Elements' addition to Hopkinsville a tremendous step toward building that foundation.
The facility is located on over 140 acres in Commerce Industrial Park II in Hopkinsville.
The 500,000-square-foot Apex 1 facility will begin operations in late 2023.
With potential expansion phases, the facility would employ up to 400 people in roles ranging from engineers and chemists to warehouse associates and manufacturing operators.
The project is expected to potentially generate $4.4 billion in economic impact to Kentucky over the construction period and first 10 years of operation.

Ascend Elements CEO Michael O'Kronley thanked Gov. Beshear, his staff, Christian County, and the City of Hopkinsville for their support, noting that clean energy and climate technology industries are bringing good jobs to communities across the country.
O'Kronley stated that Ascend Elements is building a domestic source of sustainable lithium-ion cathode material for EV batteries in Kentucky that does not exist anywhere else in the United States.
The company evaluated more than 50 locations for this facility, with Hopkinsville being the only site that met all requirements.

Key site selection factors included easy access to transportation, clean renewable power, a diverse workforce, and proximity to electric vehicle manufacturers and lithium-ion battery factories in the southeast United States.
Ascend Elements' Base 1 battery recycling facility in Covington, Ga. is a key source of recycled battery feedstock (black mass) for the Apex 1 facility.
source_title: Ascend Elements to invest $1 billion in Southwest Kentucky EV battery materials mfg. facility -

facts: Ascend Elements is a battery-recycling startup that has secured $800 million in fresh funding.
CEO Mike O'Kronley plans to spend the funding on beating out rivals like Redwood Materials.
The company was founded in 2015 in Westborough, Massachusetts.
Its technology enables car companies and battery-makers to obtain materials at a lower cost and more sustainably than earth-mined metals.
Investors from Fifth Wall and car-industry giants contributed $300 million to the company.
The Series C funding consists of $200 million in equity investments and $100 million in debt financing.
This funding accompanies two grants from the Department of Energy totaling $480 million.
The funding includes a $50 million strategic investment from a division of South Korean energy giant SK received in September, which is part of the $200 million equity portion.
Many recycling methods only scour used batteries for precious metals like lithium and nickel, requiring additional processing before reuse.
Ascend's "hydro-to-cathode" technology recycles materials and processes them so they are ready to go straight back into battery supply chains.

CEO Mike O'Kronley stated: "We are ideally suited to work with battery manufacturers to help them close the loop and produce sustainable battery material, taking their manufacturing scrap, processing it, and returning the material that they can use right back to them."
Recycling is critical as the auto industry faces a brutal battery-supply shortage and materials-sourcing requirements in this summer's climate bill for EV tax credit qualification.
Automakers and stakeholders are establishing plans to return spent battery materials back into manufacturing.
Estimates suggest very little lithium has been recovered from batteries to date, but 9% to 11% of lithium demand in 2035 could be met with properly recycled materials.
Properly recycled materials would eliminate mining-related issues such as time, expense, and environmental damage.
Competitor Redwood Materials secured a $700 million Series C last year.
Competitor Li-Cycle brought in $200 million earlier this year from a contract with Glencore.
Ascend's first commercial-scale plant is located in Covington, Georgia, near the new "battery belt."
The Covington plant began running a new battery-shredding line and will recycle 30,000 metric tons of batteries and manufacturing scrap annually once fully operational.
Ascend is investing up to $1 billion into a new factory in Kentucky.
Partnered business tied to Ford and Volkswagen EVs will reduce Ascend's limitation on battery feedstock volume.
CEO Mike O'Kronley stated: "It's a very different value proposition that we offer to the market."
An October 26, 2022 correction clarified that the $50 million strategic investment from SK is part of the $200 million equity funding, not an additional amount.
source_title: Why Investors Gave Battery Recycler Ascend a Fresh $800 Million - Business Insider

canonical_name: Commonwealth of Kentucky EV Manufacturing Ecosystem
facts: Kentucky has attracted over $13.1 billion in electric vehicle (EV) related investments since 2020.
Over the past five years, Kentucky has emerged as "the EV capital of the United States."
The effort to bring electric vehicle manufacturing, battery making and other parts of the EV supply chain to the commonwealth has been a cornerstone of Gov. Andy Beshear's two terms in office.
Since 2020, the state has seen more than $13.1 billion in EV-related investments, bringing a slew of promised jobs, as well, Beshear said Aug. 11.

Ford Motor Co. announced a $2 billion investment into the Louisville Assembly Plant to create a new Universal EV Platform for assembly, bring a new four-door midsize truck EV, and secure 2,200 hourly jobs at the factory.
Gov. Andy Beshear said: "Together, Kentucky and Ford are bringing the future of the EV industry to this world."
Ascend Elements is a battery material manufacturer that landed a multimillion-dollar investment from the federal government to go toward production of battery materials for electric vehicles.

Ascend Elements scaled back its plans for the Hopkinsville plant, canceling a $164 million federal grant for cathode active material manufacturing while retaining a larger grant for cathode precursor production.
The EV battery company has faced legal disputes from contractors on the project due to payment issues.
Ascend Elements expects to start operations in late 2026.

Ford's $2 billion August announcement marks the first time in Louisville Assembly Plant's history that the plant will produce an electric vehicle, a midsize four-door EV pickup truck with a $30,000 starting price coming to consumers in 2027.
Ford said the new-to-market truck will be "as fast as a Mustang EcoBoost" in acceleration of 0-60 miles per hour and have more passenger space than the competitor Toyota RAV4.
Like many EVs, the truck will also feature a "frunk" or front trunk and have a truck bed.
Ford announced the Louisville Assembly Plant will be home to its newest iteration of the assembly line, which the company has dubbed the "assembly tree."
Ford is creating a new Universal EV Platform that will debut at the Louisville Assembly Plant and allow a family of "affordable" vehicles to be produced and scaled to meet customer demand.
BlueOval SK is a nearly $6 billion joint venture EV battery production company bringing together the automotive history and innovation of Ford combined with the technology of South Korean SK On.
BlueOval SK announced full production started Aug. 19.
The facility is located in Hardin County and has promised to employ 5,000 workers.
As of May there were roughly 1,400 employees at the battery park, according to documents filed with the federal government.
The battery park is a twin plant facility, with Kentucky 1 currently employing people.
Kentucky 2 is currently delayed as Ford previously said consumer demand for EVs was not where the automaker anticipated it would be.

Ahead of starting full production in August, the plant faced a slew of health and safety concerns filed with state government agencies, including concerns over chemical usage, adequate PPE and a bat infestation.
In 2022, Bowling Green was named as the future home for a planned $2 billion EV-related project.
Envision AESC, a global battery technology company now known as just AESC, planned to produce battery cells and modules for multiple automakers.
Envision AESC was expected to start producing batteries to power up to 300,000 electric vehicles annually by 2027.
While the building shell has been constructed, the project has essentially been abandoned, with promised jobs and output not yet materialized in the commonwealth.
Rivian, an electric vehicle manufacturer that builds sport utility vehicles and trucks, invested $10 million in Bullitt County in 2023 to expand its parts operations and remanufacturing facility.
The Rivian facility is where employees reconstruct vehicle parts such as electrical hardware, drive units and battery systems.
State law previously blocked direct-to-consumer sales from automakers in Kentucky.
In late 2024, Tesla found a caveat to the state law and was able to obtain a dealer license.
As of this summer, Tesla has been selling vehicles and offering leasing options from its Louisville location.
source_title: Kentucky becomes EV capital of America after $13 billion auto overhaul

facts: Ascend Elements is a US company that specializes in sustainable battery materials.
Ascend Elements announced a major battery material order worth up to $5 billion.
The initial contract value is around $1 billion, with deliveries expected to start in the fourth quarter of 2024.
The multi-year contract will supply cathode precursor materials (high nickel NMC) for battery production to an unnamed major US company.
The customer has the option to extend the contract to a larger quantity worth up to $5 billion.
Ascend Elements is building a factory in Kentucky to produce sustainable battery materials.
The planned completion date for the Kentucky factory is the end of 2023.
The initial planned capacity of the Kentucky facility is enough cathode material and precursors for up to 250,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
Ascend Elements later cited an updated output capacity three times as high, sufficient for up to 750,000 electric vehicle batteries.
In October 2022, the US Department of Energy awarded Ascend Elements two grants totaling $480 million to accelerate construction of the Kentucky plant.
In March 2023, Ascend Elements opened its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling plant in Georgia.
Mike O’Kronley serves as the CEO of Ascend Elements.
CEO Mike O’Kronley stated that nearly 100% of the world’s pCAM is produced in Asia.

CEO Mike O’Kronley emphasized the need to manufacture critical battery materials in the United States to secure the North American supply chain, reduce carbon emissions, and ensure energy independence.
source_title: Ascend Elements announces major battery material order - electrive.com

facts: Ascend Elements announced plans to relocate its corporate headquarters and research & development center from Westborough, Massachusetts to Devens, Massachusetts.
The new facility will be a 101,000 square foot space located at 39 Jackson Road in the Pathway Devens campus.
The facility is owned and operated by King Street Properties in Boston.
Ascend Elements has experienced over 500% headcount growth in the last 18 months.
The company currently employs approximately 350 team members globally.
Ascend Elements employs 140 people in Massachusetts.
The relocation from Westborough to Devens is planned for September 2024.
CEO Mike O'Kronley stated the relocation will be a milestone for the company's rapid growth in the engineered battery materials industry.
Pathway Devens is a 45-acre, purpose-built development in Devens that includes an 8,300 square foot amenity center and taproom opening in Q1 2024.
The Pathway Devens amenity center features a fitness center, lounge area, game room, and café to serve workers and the community.
Other businesses located on the Pathway Devens campus include Electric Hydrogen, Commonwealth Fusion Systems, and Azzur Group.

Tyson Reynoso, Managing Director & Partner at King Street Properties, noted that Ascend Elements' mission aligns with King Street's goal of creating science campuses to solve health and climate issues.
Tyson Reynoso added that Ascend Elements' addition solidifies Devens as the top destination in Greater Boston for innovators engaged in advanced manufacturing.
Ascend Elements is a leader in the electric vehicle battery materials and battery recycling industry.
The company uses its patented Hydro-to-Cathode® direct precursor synthesis process to transform used lithium-ion batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap into engineered battery materials.
Engineered battery materials produced by Ascend Elements include cathode active materials (CAM) and cathode precursor (pCAM).
The patented process eliminates several intermediary steps in traditional cathode manufacturing, providing significant economic and carbon-reduction benefits.
Ascend Elements' customers include Honda, SK Battery America, Freudenberg e-Power Systems, and others.
Ascend Elements recently closed a $542 million funding round.
The company received $480 million in U.S. Department of Energy grant awards.
The DOE grants will accelerate construction of a $1 billion engineered battery materials manufacturing campus in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
Ascend Elements operates one of North America's largest electric vehicle battery recycling facilities in Covington, Georgia.
CEO Mike O'Kronley stated that central Massachusetts is home to the company, noting its technology was invented at Worcester Polytechnic Institute and its first location was in Worcester.
O'Kronley emphasized the company's strong ties to central Massachusetts as it expands in the southeast United States.
source_title: Ascend Elements to Relocate and Expand Corporate Headquarters and R&D Tech Center to Devens, Massachusetts

facts: Elemental Strategic Metals is based in Zawiercie.
Ascend Elements is based in Westborough, Massachusetts.
In April, Elemental Strategic Metals and Ascend Elements announced the formation of AE Elemental.
AE Elemental is a joint venture based in Zawiercie.
Each company will own 50% of the JV.
The companies will be the operators of a newly built electric vehicle (EV) battery recycling plant.
The plant handles dismantling, discharging, and recycling EV batteries.
The plant processes active cathode material (CAM) and cathode precursor (pCAM).
The plant has a capacity to recycle up to 12,000 tons of batteries per year.
The plant capacity is equivalent to approximately 28,000 EV batteries per year.
The co-owners of AE Elemental agreed on further joint investments in the field.
Construction of a lithium extraction facility will begin in the fall of 2024.
The lithium extraction facility will have a production capacity of up to 20,000 metric tons of black mass per year.
The lithium extraction facility is expected to become operational in 2026.
The companies plan to start construction of a modern EV battery recycling plant in central Germany.
The AE Elemental plant in Germany will have a recycling capacity of up to 25,000 tons of batteries per year.
The German plant capacity is equivalent to approximately 58,000 EVs per year.
Paweł Jarski serves as president of Elemental Holding.
Mike O’Kronley serves as CEO of Ascend Elements.
Paweł Jarski stated: “This is a step towards becoming a key global player in the industry of materials necessary for the production of new EV batteries.”
Mike O’Kronley stated: “The AE Elemental joint venture is a milestone for Ascend Elements, representing our first commercial-scale battery recycling facility in Europe.”
source_title: Elemental Strategic Metals & Ascend Elements e-battery JV kicks off in Zawiercie - PolandWeekly

facts: Jobs and the economy of Northwest Georgia are increasingly tied to the growth of clean energy innovations like electric vehicles.
In Gordon County, TEKLAS, a Turkish supplier of electric vehicle parts, invested $6.5 million in its first North American facility.
source_title: GE Appliances Archives - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Through the third quarter of fiscal year 2022 (July 1, 2021 - March 31, 2022), GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 251 project locations.
These supported projects resulted in more than 35,400 new jobs for the state.
These supported projects resulted in $12.9 billion in investment for the state.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the Q3 FY2022 economic development records on April 7, 2022.
The reported job creation represents a 48% increase compared to three-quarters through the previous fiscal year.
The reported job creation represents a 6% increase compared to the prior year’s overall total.
Total investment increased by 54% compared to the same time period last year.
Nearly 78% of both projects and investments were located outside the 10-county metro Atlanta area.
Over half of the jobs created were located outside the 10-county metro Atlanta area.
Key industry sectors driving growth include automotive, advanced manufacturing, logistics and distribution, software and technology, and food processing.
Georgia’s electric vehicle (EV) and sustainability ecosystems have grown tremendously over the past year.
The state is actively recruiting the entire EV supply chain to ensure an effective, efficient, and affordable environment for EV production.
Rivian plans to develop a $5 billion campus at Stanton Springs North.
Rivian plans an electric vehicle service center in Atlanta that will provide training on EVs.
SK Battery America partnered with Ascend Elements to recycle cell and module lithium-ion battery manufacturing scrap from SK’s Jackson County facility.
Ascend Elements is an American lithium-ion battery recycling and engineered materials startup.
Ascend Elements announced its first commercial-scale battery recycling plant in Covington in January.
Aurubis will invest $340 million in a ‘first-of-its-kind in the U.S.’ state-of-the-art recycling and secondary smelting facility for multi-metal recycling in Augusta.
Aurubis is one of the leading recyclers of copper, precious metals, and non-ferrous materials worldwide.
Aspen Aerogels announced plans to locate a new 500,000-square-foot advanced manufacturing facility in Statesboro.
Aspen Aerogels will produce PyroThin materials that provide thermal management, mechanical stability, and fire protection properties to EV battery systems at its new Statesboro facility.
Aspen Aerogels is an electrification solutions and sustainability leader.
The No. 1-ranking workforce training program, Georgia Quick Start, helped secure numerous projects across the state.
Georgia Quick Start has provided specialized training tailored specifically to business needs for more than 40 years.
Georgia Quick Start is administered through the Technical College System of Georgia.
Pepsi Co.’s expansion in DeKalb County was secured with help from Georgia Quick Start.
Remington Firearms’ world-class research and development center in LaGrange was secured with help from Georgia Quick Start.
Titan Steel Doors’ expansion in Hart County was secured with help from Georgia Quick Start.

facts: Aspen Aerogels, Inc. (NYSE: ASPN) announced a definitive loan agreement with General Motors Holdings LLC for a secured lending commitment of $100 million.

facts: Aspen Aerogels is a technology leader in aerogel-based sustainability and electrification solutions.

facts: Aspen Aerogels, Inc. designs, develops, manufactures, and sells aerogel materials primarily for use in the energy industrial, sustainable insulation materials, and electric vehicle (EV) markets in the United States, Canada, Asia, Europe, and Latin America.
The company offers PyroThin aerogel thermal barriers specifically for use in battery packs in EVs.
Aspen Aerogels operates its business across two segments: Energy Industrial and Thermal Barrier.

Additional product offerings include Pyrogel XTE for refineries and petrochemical facilities, Pyrogel HPS for high-temperature refining and chemical processing, Pyrogel XTF for fire protection, Cryogel Z for sub-ambient and cryogenic applications, Spaceloft Subsea for offshore oil pipe-in-pipe applications, and Cryogel X.
Aspen Aerogels generated $271.10 million in annual revenue (TTM), reflecting a 66.4% year-over-year decline.
The company holds a market capitalization of $270.26 million and trades on the NYQ under ticker ASPN at $3.27 per share as of early 2026.
Key financial metrics include a gross margin of 17.0%, operating margin of -114.7%, net margin of -143.7%, and free cash flow of $41.07 million.
Aspen Aerogels maintains $156.86 million in cash and equivalents against total debt of $143.71 million, resulting in a debt-to-equity ratio of 61.0.
Management's strategic priorities focus on sustainable revenue growth, market share expansion, profitability improvement, and returning capital to shareholders.
The company competes against Honeywell (HON), Caterpillar (CAT), 3M (MMM), Boeing (BA), General Electric (GE) and others in the Building Products & Equipment segment of the Industrials sector.
Aspen Aerogels' core strengths include an established market position in Building Products & Equipment and deep industry expertise that creates barriers to entry.
The company faces weaknesses related to financial leverage risk and a significant year-over-year revenue decline.

Growth opportunities exist within the $8.4 trillion global industrial market, potential international expansion into emerging markets like India, Southeast Asia, and Sub-Saharan Africa, and the ability to pursue strategic acquisitions using its cash reserves.

External threats include sensitivity to macroeconomic slowdowns and interest rates, regulatory and geopolitical risks such as data privacy laws and trade restrictions, and intense competition for skilled technology and engineering talent.

facts: Beaver Manufacturing Co. Inc. is a supplier of technical fiber reinforcements for hoses in North America.
The company plans to open a new plant in Mexico.
The new plant will not impact operations at its headquarters facility in Mansfield, Ga.
The new facility will be located in Tepeji, about 45 miles northwest of Mexico City.
Mike Dubin is Beaver's senior vice president and chief operating officer.
The site will expand the company's global presence.
The site will increase converting capacity of meta- and para-aramid, rayon and polyester products.
These products support automotive, oil and gas, and other industrial applications in Mexico and South America.
Aramid fibers are a class of heat-resistant and strong synthetic fibers.
Aramid fibers provide high strength-to-weight ratios with greater flexibility not available in steel wire.
Strong growth in Mexico prompted the need for the Mexican factory.
The share of Beaver's revenues derived from Mexico has increased substantially over the past five years.
The new plant will be in a prime location for expanding the company's aramid product market.
Major growth is projected in Mexico and South America for the automotive and oil and gas industries.
Dubin stated that business transferred from the U.S. to Mexico or from other parts of the world into Mexico has really high growth rates.
Mexico has become a large percentage of Beaver's business.
The company felt like localizing that business with all the growth they are working on was an important way to handle it.
Serving South America from Mexico is much better for the company.
Beaver's factory will be located at an industrial park in Tepeji.
The firm's section in the industrial park will be in excess of 50,000 square feet.
Equipment will begin arriving in January.
Dubin hopes to have the plant operational by the end of 2018's first quarter.
Initial employment is planned at between 30 and 40 workers.
Employment will expand from there.
Beaver did not disclose investment or capacity figures for the operation.
The new plant will be headed by General Manager Enrique Quirarte.
Quirarte has 13 years of experience in sales and marketing.
Quirarte has managed sales force teams in Mexico and South America.
Quirarte has specialized in working in the aramid market.
Quirarte is accustomed to selling in diverse markets such as automotive, electrical, energy, pharma and others.
Dubin stated they are excited about having an expert with sales and marketing in the meta- and para-aramid markets.

Quirarte's extensive knowledge of heat-resistant and strong synthetic fibers will be a key asset in supporting development into the automotive, oil, gas and other specialty industries in Mexico and South America.
Quirarte will oversee both the operations of the factory and business in the region.
Quirarte will have full responsibility for the facility.
Quirarte will have full bottom-line responsibility for everything.
To this point, Beaver has serviced Mexico from its main facility in Mansfield.
The Mansfield site will continue to operate at full capacity and employment.
The addition of the new Mexico plant will complement operations at the main facility without disruption.
The new plant will help the company efficiently handle projections for growth.
Dubin stated there are no plans to change anything being done in their current facility in Mansfield.
This is an expansion of the business.
There will be no change in employment at the Georgia facility.
No equipment will be moved from the Georgia facility.
Growth has been so strong that the new capacity cannot come on-stream soon enough, according to Dubin.
This is Beaver's second major move in the past two months.
In October, Beaver reached an agreement where Germany's Mehler Engineered Products Group will represent Beaver's product line in Europe beginning Jan. 1.
The agreement with Mehler will eventually lead to manufacturing of the goods in Europe.
source_title: Fiber reinforcement firm Beaver expanding into Mexico

facts: Unified Legacy will invest $125 million to construct a new manufacturing facility in Macon-Bibb County, Georgia.
The expansion is projected to create 500 new jobs over the next several years.
Executing this project will effectively double Unified Legacy's footprint and production output within the state of Georgia.
The development reinforces Georgia’s position as a critical supplier for the aerospace, defense, and rapidly expanding data center sectors.
The new facility will be located on Barnes Ferry Road in Macon, Bibb County.
Construction on the new facility is scheduled to begin in 2026.
Parrish Construction was selected as the general contractor for the build.
A Development of Regional Impact (DRI) filing with the Middle Georgia Regional Commission indicates the expansion is expected to generate up to $600,000 in annual tax revenue for the local area.
The successful bid for the expansion involved a collaborative effort by the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), the Macon-Bibb County Industrial Authority, and Georgia Power.
Unified Legacy plans to hire across manufacturing, skilled trades, engineering, logistics, quality control, and administrative disciplines.
Local leaders view the hiring initiative as a major step in creating fresh pathways into skilled trades for Middle Georgia residents.
Macon-Bibb County Mayor Lester Miller stated that 500 more families will have the chance at careers and better lives due to the expansion.
Unified Legacy is headquartered in Macon, Georgia, and serves as the parent company for Unified Defense and Prince Service & Manufacturing.
The company specializes in advanced machining, welding, and precision metal fabrication.
Unified Defense has operated a manufacturing facility in nearby Byron, Georgia, since 2022.
Product lines include custom solutions such as ground support equipment, welded assemblies, generator enclosures, fuel storage tanks, and precision-machined components.
These products are primarily targeted at the defense, aerospace, industrial, and data center infrastructure markets.
Eric Williams serves as the CEO of Unified Legacy.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the substantial economic development project on May 15, 2026.
The official press release highlights a national push to strengthen domestic manufacturing, particularly within national security and defense ecosystems.

Pat Wilson, Commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, noted that Georgia offers a competitive edge with a highly skilled workforce, world-class logistics, and strong local and state partnerships for strengthening domestic manufacturing critical to national security.

AirPro News analysis observes that the $125 million investment is a strong indicator of shifting dynamics in U.S. supply-chains and the materialization of large-scale capital expenditures for aerospace and defense localization.

The expansion specifically targets the physical hardware and enclosures required to house advanced computing systems, highlighting a critical bottleneck in the tech industry's data center infrastructure sector.

By positioning itself at the intersection of aerospace, defense, and data centers, Unified Legacy aims to insulate its growth against sector-specific downturns while capitalizing on Georgia’s robust industrial incentives.
source_title: Unified Legacy To Invest $125M In New Macon-Bibb Manufacturing Facility - AirPro News

facts: Blue Bird Corporation builds more than 10,000 buses every year.

facts: Blue Bird Corp. recently reported lower than expected fourth-quarter performance on both its revenue and earnings fronts amid supply chain disruptions.
BLBD is an American bus manufacturer headquartered in Fort Valley, Georgia, and is best known for manufacturing school buses.
The company’s Q4 revenue decreased 31.7% year-over-year to $192.2 million, missing analysts’ estimates by $50.3 million.
Earnings per share at $0.07 missed estimates by $0.11.
Supply chain constraints led to delays to fiscal 2022 in more than 2,000 bookings.
Management noted that despite a rebound in the school bus industry coupled with 9,700 new bus orders during the year, production was limited due to supply chain bottlenecks.
Consequently, the company has a substantial backlog of over 4,200 buses, including internal combustion engine buses as well as electric buses.
EV stocks remain a key attention area for retail investors, and the company is increasing its focus on the EV business.
BLBD has also upped its vehicle prices by 11% to mitigate higher commodity prices.
According to the TipRanks Risk Factors tool, Blue Bird’s top two risk categories are Production and Finance & Corporate, contributing 32% and 22% to the total 37 risks identified, respectively.
Compared to a sector average of 19%, BLBD’s Production risk factor is at 32%.
In its recent annual report, the company has changed three key risk factors.
BLBD noted that it has reserved its common shares for current and future issuance, which would result in ownership dilution for existing investors.
On December 15, the company raised $75 million through a private placement of about 4.69 million shares at $16 per share.
BLBD conceded that it does not intend to pay cash dividends on its common shares at present.
As a result, investors’ ability to generate returns on their investment in the company’s shares will hinge on appreciation in BLBD’s share price.
Under the Production risk category, BLBD highlighted that its defined benefit pension plan is currently underfunded.
Future funding requirements could increase substantially due to a reduction in funded status, and BLBD would have to contribute more funds, which could adversely impact its cash flows.
On December 16, Craig-Hallum analyst Eric Stine reiterated a Buy rating on the stock and increased the price target to $135 from $125.
Consensus on the Street is a Moderate Buy based on 1 Buy and Hold each.
The average Blue Bird Corp price target of $19 implies a potential upside of 28.9%.
Shares are down 17.5% so far this year.
source_title: Blue Bird Corp Updates 3 Key Risk Factors - TipRanks.com

facts: Blue Bird Corp BLBD is renowned for its specialization in the design, engineering, manufacture, and sale of school buses.
On February 4, 2026, the company filed its 10-Q report.
Net sales increased from $313.87 million to $333.08 million according to the 10-Q filing.
Gross profit rose from $60.32 million to $71.23 million.
Operating profit saw an uptick from $33.04 million to $37.68 million.
Net income increased from $28.72 million to $30.76 million.
Blue Bird Corp BLBD stands out for its pioneering role in alternative powered school buses, including propane, gasoline, and electric models.
The company's market leadership in this segment is backed by a strong dealer network and customer loyalty.
Blue Bird Corp BLBD faces challenges with supply chain disruptions, as highlighted in the 10-Q filing.
Global supply chain constraints, exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic and geopolitical tensions, have periodically resulted in shortages of critical components.
These disruptions have impacted manufacturing schedules and operational efficiencies.
The imposition of tariffs and ongoing inflationary pressures have led to higher procurement costs for raw materials.
The company has implemented price increases to mitigate these effects.
A significant portion of Blue Bird Corp's revenue is generated from customers in the United States, with a smaller contribution from Canada and other international markets.
Federal, state, and local government incentives for eco-friendly transportation solutions provide a favorable environment for Blue Bird Corp to increase sales of its alternative powered buses.
Changes in emissions regulations and trade policies can have a significant impact on Blue Bird Corp's operations and cost structure.
The school bus manufacturing industry is competitive, with several well-established players vying for market share.
Fluctuations in property tax revenues, which are a major source of funding for school districts, can impact the demand for new school buses.
Changes in student enrollment and delivery mechanisms for learning, such as increased remote learning, could reduce the need for school bus transportation.
Investments in research and development could lead to breakthroughs in electric and autonomous vehicle technologies.
Questions or comments about this story can be sent to editors@gurufocus.com.
