facts: Blue Bird Corporation designs, engineers, manufactures and sells school buses, including electric, propane, natural gas and conventional models, and also sells replacement bus parts and extended warranties.
Blue Bird successfully completed a $75 million securities purchase agreement with Coliseum Capital Management, issuing shares at $16.00 each.
The $75 million capital will be directed towards enhancing the company's electric vehicle strategy, particularly following new legislation allocating $5 billion for clean school buses.
Coliseum Capital Management now owns 15% of Blue Bird and has placed a representative on the board.
The funds will help with working capital, pay down debt, and invest in technology to bolster Blue Bird's leadership in the alternative-power bus market.

Blue Bird announced its fiscal 2021 fourth quarter and full year financial results on December 15, 2021, at 4:30PM ET via a public audio webcast featuring CEO Matthew Stevenson and CFO Razvan Radulescu.
Blue Bird is recognized as a leader in the design and manufacturing of school buses, with over 550,000 buses sold.
Blue Bird announced a fourth amendment to its credit facility, effective through the fiscal second quarter of 2023.

The credit amendment provides covenant relief by replacing the net leverage ratio with a minimum EBITDA covenant and increases the leverage ratio from 3.50:1 to 4.00:1 for the first two quarters of fiscal 2023.
The amendment introduces a new pricing grid based on the net debt to EBITDA ratio and allows for an additional $10 million of revolver availability.
CEO Matthew Stevenson emphasized the importance of the credit amendment for operational flexibility during the pandemic.
Blue Bird filed a universal shelf registration statement on Form S-3 with the SEC, allowing it to raise up to $200 million through various securities.
The shelf registration is described as a prudent approach to capital management to capitalize on growth opportunities from the Bipartisan Infrastructure Deal and the evolving electric vehicle market.
The shelf registration will remain in effect for three years once declared effective, providing flexibility for future offerings.
Blue Bird is optimistic about the inclusion of student transportation in the recently approved Bipartisan Infrastructure Deal.
The company has over 20,000 clean fuel buses and aims to enhance emissions-reducing school transportation across the U.S.
The $5 billion investment in electric bus production is expected to modernize school fleets and decrease maintenance costs, potentially saving districts over $6,000 annually.
Blue Bird has pioneered electric buses for over 25 years and views increased demand for alternative-powered vehicles as a positive outcome of the legislation.
Nuvve Holding Corp. announced plans to install 200 V2G DC fast charging stations at Blue Bird's facility in Fort Valley, Georgia.
The charging hub will optimize electric bus utilization and enable them to function as energy storage assets.
Utilizing Nuvve's V2G technology, the buses will charge and discharge energy to enhance battery health and reduce utility costs.
The hub aims to aggregate power from multiple buses, creating a virtual power plant with a capacity of 25 MW that could potentially supply power to over 10,000 homes during peak demand.
Blue Bird appointed Razvan Radulescu as its new Chief Financial Officer effective October 1, 2021.
Razvan Radulescu brings over 20 years of experience from Daimler Trucks, having held significant roles in Finance, Procurement, and Compliance.
He succeeds Phil Tighe, the interim CFO, who will assist in the transition until year-end.
Blue Bird reported third quarter fiscal 2021 results showing net income of $4.3 million, an increase of $3 million year-over-year.
Revenue reached $196.7 million, marking a 4% increase from the previous year.
Adjusted EBITDA improved to $13.2 million, representing a $0.7 million rise.
Supply chain disruptions led to 550 fewer bookings and caused year-to-date net sales to decline by $106 million, down 17.7%.
The order backlog remains robust at over 4,000 buses, indicating strong market demand for traditional and electric school buses.
Micro Bird Holdings, a joint venture of Blue Bird and Girardin, acquired a controlling interest in GranTuned Automobiles (Ecotuned), a supplier of EV drivetrains.
This acquisition enhances Micro Bird's position in the light and medium-duty EV market by leveraging Ecotuned’s advanced technologies and expertise.
The partnership holds over 80% market share in North America's Type A school bus segment and is expected to bolster revenue and profitability for both Micro Bird and Blue Bird Corporation.
Nuvve Holding Corp. and Blue Bird are expanding their partnership to integrate Nuvve's vehicle-to-grid technology into Blue Bird's electric school buses.
The collaboration aims to offer financing through Levo Mobility LLC, enabling schools to lease V2G electric buses and related infrastructure with no upfront costs.
Levo has committed up to $750 million, potentially electrifying 3,500 buses.
This initiative allows schools access to revenue-generating capabilities by utilizing buses to stabilize the grid, enhancing the appeal and affordability of sustainable school transportation.

facts: Bonnell Aluminum is a prominent North American manufacturer of custom-fabricated and finished aluminum extrusions.

The company serves diverse markets including automotive and transportation, building and construction (residential and commercial), consumer durables, machinery and equipment, electrical, and distribution.
Bonnell Aluminum provides comprehensive capabilities ranging from design and extrusion to fabrication, finishing, and assembly.
Bonnell Aluminum is a subsidiary of Tredegar Corporation (NYSE: TG), a global manufacturing company.
The Newnan, Georgia facility serves as the primary corporate office overseeing strategic direction, administrative functions, sales, and engineering support for North American operations.
The Newnan headquarters is central to Bonnell's operational coordination across its multiple manufacturing sites, ensuring unified strategy, product quality, and market responsiveness.
Bonnell Aluminum's primary manufacturing and operational footprint is concentrated in North America, with facilities strategically located across the United States and a sales presence in Canada.
The company's custom aluminum extrusions serve as integral components in finished goods distributed globally by customers in automotive, building products, and consumer durables.
Bonnell Aluminum's headquarters address is 25 Bonnell Street, Newnan, GA, USA.
A key production and distribution hub serving the Southeastern and Midwestern United States is located at 1000 Bonnell Drive, Carthage, TN 37030.
A facility supporting Midwest and Great Lakes customers with specialized finishing capabilities is located at 1600 Comfort Drive, Niles, MI 49120 (AACOA an Alumil Company, part of Bonnell).
A location catering to the transportation and recreational vehicle manufacturing cluster in Northern Indiana is at 2900 S. Nappanee St, Elkhart, IN 46517 (AACOA an Alumil Company, part of Bonnell).
A strategic location supplying Western U.S. customers is at 1100 E. Freeport Center Bldg. X-5, Clearfield, UT 84016.
A Canadian sales office managing customer relationships and business development is located at 1900 Minnesota Crt, Suite 210, Mississauga ON, L5N 3C9 (Tredegar Canada Inc. address).

As of April 2025, Bonnell Aluminum's leadership structure is tied to its parent company, with specific executive changes typically announced through Tredegar Corporation's investor relations rather than as distinct Bonnell events.
On February 22, 2023, Bonnell Aluminum announced plans for a significant expansion of its Clearfield, Utah manufacturing facility.

The Clearfield expansion project includes investment in a new state-of-the-art extrusion press and additional fabrication equipment to meet growing demand in the Western U.S. for aluminum products in building, construction, and automotive markets.
On August 2, 2022, Bonnell Aluminum announced an $18 million investment to expand its Niles, Michigan manufacturing facility.
The Niles expansion aims to increase capacity for specialized finishing and fabrication, supporting growth in key markets such as automotive and consumer durables.

During Tredegar Corporation's Q1 2024 earnings call on May 2, 2024, CEO John Steitz noted that Bonnell's net sales decreased versus the prior year primarily due to lower average selling prices and slightly lower sales volume.

The sales decline reflected softer demand in non-residential building and construction and industrial markets, while the company highlighted ongoing projects and efforts to manage costs and improve efficiency.

facts: Howard Industries is an electrical transformer and technology products manufacturer.
The company is investing $236.95 million in its Mississippi facilities across Clarke, Jones, and Simpson counties.
The project will create a total of 450 jobs.
The expansion will enable the company to meet U.S. Department of Energy’s new requirements for electrical transformers.
The expansion addresses growing demand for power from sectors such as data centers, electric vehicles, transportation, and industry.
The Mississippi Development Authority is providing assistance through the Mississippi Flexible Tax Incentive (MFLEX) program.
The Mississippi Development Authority is providing assistance for land acquisition, infrastructure improvements, and construction costs.
The Clarke County Board of Supervisors is assisting with the project.
The Simpson County Board of Supervisors is assisting with the project.
The Jones County Board of Supervisors is assisting with the project.
Howard Industries is a privately held, family-owned Mississippi business established in 1968.
The company is a global leader in distribution transformers with more than 14 locations throughout Mississippi.
Howard Industries employs more than 4,000 workers across its locations.
The company has produced over 10 million transformers that are in service throughout the U.S. and abroad.
Howard Industries plans to complete the project within five years.
Howard Industries plans to fill the new jobs within five years.
Governor Tate Reeves stated the $236 million investment highlights record-breaking private sector growth across Mississippi and proves the state is open for business.
Mississippi Development Authority Executive Director Bill Cork stated the investment validates Mississippi as an ideal location for companies seeking long-term growth and generational success.

Howard Industries CEO Michael Howard stated the company is deeply committed to Mississippi's growth and that the investment will enhance production capabilities and create new job opportunities in Clarke, Jones, and Simpson counties.

Economic Development Authority of Jones County President Ross Tucker noted Howard Industries has brought new jobs and investment to Jones County for decades, with its first transformer plant in Laurel and corporate headquarters in Ellisville.

Simpson County Development Foundation Executive Director Pat Brown stated the company's growth and retention plans are beneficial for the community and that elected leadership made a sound investment in the facility.

Newt Ishee, Executive Director of the Mid-Mississippi Development District, stated the Clarke County Board of Supervisors and the city of Quitman extend gratitude to Howard Industries for choosing Quitman for the significant investment.

facts: DTNA’s Q3 profits fell 65% and sales dropped 39%, showing ongoing pressure from tariffs and weaker North American demand.
North American truck sales likely won’t rebound until the second half of 2026, keeping fleets cautious on new orders.
Global performance shows Europe improving but Asia struggling, signaling fleet managers must navigate uneven regional demand.
Daimler Truck North America’s profits fell more than 60% in the third quarter compared to a year earlier and will slide further this quarter, according to CFO Eva Scherer on November 7.
The North American operations of Daimler Truck Holding AG comprise the Freightliner and Western Star truck brands as well as Thomas Built Buses.
DTNA sold 30,225 units in Q3 (ended September 30), down 39% from the same period in 2024.
Q3 revenues slid by a third to about $4.6 billion.
Q3 earnings before interest and taxes fell 65% to $295 million.
CFO Eva Scherer expects DTNA’s North American profitability to take another step down this quarter as the Trump administration’s tariffs on trucks and their parts work through the company’s business.
Rival International, whose parent is Traton SE, could post a Q4 operating loss as tariffs compound falling orders and rising input costs.
DTNA sales picked up in September and October relative to the two months prior, but remain far below desired numbers.
Scherer stated that while the company hopes the market will pick up, it will not happen in Q1 and will probably occur towards the second half of the year.
DTNA executives expect North American sales to finish 2025 closer to the bottom of their forecast of 135,000 to 155,000 than the top.
Based on nearly 108,000 vehicles sold through the end of September, Q4 sales will be generally in line with Q3’s.
DTNA sold about 191,000 trucks in 2024.
The company cut about 2,700 jobs since this time last year in response to the order downturn.
Daimler’s leaders have not yet decided whether to move more truck assembly work from Mexico to the U.S. due to an unclear tariff situation.
It is premature to discuss DTNA imposing more surcharges to offset the effects of new tariffs.
Globally, Daimler Truck posted a net profit of about $530 million on more than $13.2 billion in sales.
Scherer noted that parts of Europe are showing signs of improving demand, but key Asian markets like Japan and Indonesia continue to struggle.
The company sold a little more than 98,000 vehicles during Q3, a drop of 15% from the same period last year.
Shares of Daimler (Ticker: DTG) fell slightly after the earnings announcement, leaving the company’s market value at about $20 billion over the past six months.
source_title: Daimler Truck CFO says North American turnaround is second-half story | FleetOwner

facts: Daimler Truck North America LLC (DTNA) has announced plans to upgrade its manufacturing plant in Redford Township, Michigan.
The plant is part of Detroit Diesel Corporation, a DTNA subsidiary.
The company will invest up to $285 million into the project.
The investment will create 400 new jobs.
Funds will be invested in DTNA’s internal combustion engine (ICE) platform and research and development facilities.
DTNA hopes the investment will help maintain its current production capacity.
DTNA aims to increase innovation of ICE components through this project.
The investment will support growing demand for cleaner, more fuel-efficient engines and components.
Construction on the project is expected to begin this year.
The Michigan Economic Development Corporation (MEDC) has offered DTNA a $27.7 million performance-based Critical Industries Program grant to help aid the project.
DTNA has received Michigan Strategic Fund support for a State Essential Services Assessment abatement with an estimated value of up to $3.29 million.
Matt Pfaffenbach, vice president of powertrain operations, stated that the Detroit Manufacturing Plant has a legacy of over 85 years in Michigan and stands as a testament to innovation and progress.
Pfaffenbach noted the expansion is about creating job opportunities and upskilling the workforce to meet the demands of tomorrow’s technology.
Pfaffenbach said the project will help stabilize the heavy-duty supply chain, strengthen DTNA's role in the industry, and bring significant growth to the community.

Quentin Messer, Jr., CEO of the Michigan Economic Development Corporation and chair of the Michigan Strategic Fund Board, thanked Detroit Diesel for its commitment to Michigan’s hardworking mobility industry workers employed at its Redford Township facility.
Messer stated that Michigan’s workforce, supply chain, and R&D strength in the mobility sector are the best in North America.
Messer added that the project embodies the ‘Make It In Michigan’ economic development strategy focused on People, Places, and Projects.
Hemlock Semiconductor received $325 million from the CHIPS Act to increase production of hyper-pure polysilicon in Michigan.
The Hemlock Semiconductor project will generate 180 new jobs for the surrounding area.

Construction on the Saab facility will begin later this year, with production beginning in 2026.

The NanoGraf facility will produce 2,500 tons per year of proprietary silicon anode material.

facts: Daimler Truck North America is deploying its own electric semi trucks to transport auto parts.
The Portland, Oregon-based company launched its Freightliner eCascadia battery electric semi-trucks for customers in 2022.
Daimler Truck North America is deploying an initial four eCascadias to support its production and aftermarket operations across North America.
The four eCascadias will pick up parts from suppliers in the Pacific Northwest and deliver them to a consolidation center in Portland.
Parts transported by the eCascadias will be shipped to Daimler's North American factories and aftermarket parts distribution centers serving customers in the US and Canada.
The four eCascadias charge at Daimler’s “Electric Island,” a heavy-duty electric truck charging, development, and testing site that opened in 2021 at the company’s headquarters.
The Freightliner eCascadia comes in 315 or 475kWh battery configurations.
The eCascadia has up to 250 miles of range while carrying approximately a 65,000-pound gross vehicle weight.
In 2020, the Portland truck factory where the eCascadia is built achieved carbon-neutral production with reduced energy consumption and the offset of onsite emissions.
Daimler plans to incorporate carbon-neutral production at its remaining truck factories by 2025.

facts: Daimler Truck North America announced the appointment of Joanna Buttler as General Manager, product strategy and market development.
In her new role, Joanna Buttler will oversee the development of DTNA’s product portfolio, both short and long-term.
Buttler will spearhead all go-to-market activities for the company.
Her responsibilities include product marketing, brand communications, sales training, and digital sales tools.
Joanna Buttler’s team represents the Freightliner, Western Star, and Detroit brands.
She will report directly to David Carson, senior vice president, sales and marketing and member of DTNA’s Operating Committee.
Buttler brings nearly 20 years of experience with Daimler Truck to her new position.
She began her career in 2006 as a Marketing Analyst and Sales Support Specialist focused on Detroit powertrain.
Her journey within the company includes roles such as project controller, manager of IT Controlling, and support manager for Martin Daum, former president and CEO of DTNA.
With Martin Daum’s promotion to CEO of Daimler Truck and Buses in 2017, Buttler became chief of staff.
She was subsequently appointed into strategic executive roles within the Autonomous Technology Group.
Most recently, Buttler served as head of DTNA’s Global Autonomous Technology Group.
In that role, she set the global autonomous technology strategy for Daimler Truck.
She led the post-merger integration within Torc Robotics.
She led the autonomous-ready Cascadia program.
She led key strategic partnerships in the autonomous vehicle industry.
According to DTNA, her efforts have been instrumental in forming strong partnerships and leading innovation programs within the transportation industry.
David Carson stated that Buttler’s extensive experience and proven leadership make her the ideal leader to drive product strategy and market development forward.
Carson added that her innovative approach and dedication to excellence will continue to strengthen DTNA's position in the industry.
Daimler Truck will continue along its autonomous roadmap, with a succession plan to be announced in the near future.
source_title: DTNA Taps Joanna Buttler as General Manager, Product Strategy and Market Development | Heavy Duty Trucking

facts: The head of eMobility at Daimler discussed why the company is developing battery electric and hydrogen solutions at the same time.
Hydrogen combustion engine technology is described as a zero-emission solution with a lower barrier to entry for many fleets.
source_title: Daimler Truck North America’s Rakesh Aneja: Infrastructure challenges still a bottleneck in commercial trucking (Episode 225) - Automotive News

facts: Nearly 7,000 Daimler Truck North America workers voted by a resounding 96% on Friday, March 8th to authorize a strike if necessary.
The contract covers 7,000 parts and assembly workers in North Carolina, Georgia, and Tennessee, and expires on April 26th.
Workers build Freightliner trucks, Western Star trucks, and Thomas Built Buses.
Workers are facing declining real wages and job security against a backdrop of rising cost of living and massive profits and shareholder payouts by Daimler Truck.
Daimler amassed nearly $6 billion in 2023.
The company plans to spend billions of dollars on stock buybacks over the next two years while lavishing millions of dollars on its shareholders.
Daimler Truck CEO Martin Daum boasted that the company’s profits are “red hot” and claimed that any “leftover money certainly belongs” to the company’s stockholders.
CEO Martin Daum received over $7.5 million in total compensation last year alone.
Daimler workers are the latest UAW members standing up to corporate greed.
Thousands of UAW members have won record contracts in the last year, including autoworkers at the Big Three automakers, and Allison Transmission workers in Indianapolis, IN.
source_title: Nearly 7,000 UAW Members at Daimler Truck Vote to Authorize Strike, If Necessary - UAW | United Automobile, Aerospace and Agricultural Implement Workers of America

facts: Joanna Cooper was appointed General Manager of the Mount Holly, N.C. manufacturing plant.
The Mount Holly plant is home to Freightliner M2 and SD model production.

Jeff Allen, senior vice president of operations and specialty vehicles at DTNA, stated: “Joanna is an incredible leader who has routinely demonstrated a passion for improving efficiencies in our manufacturing operations, and a commitment to meeting the needs of our ultimate customers.”
Jeff Allen added that Cooper's dedication to those she leads and her ability to rally teams around a common vision make her a great fit to oversee the Mount Holly Truck Manufacturing Plant.
Joanna Cooper is a 14-year veteran of the company.
Cooper began her career as a production buyer at Detroit Diesel Corporation (DDC).
Cooper previously served as general purchasing agent in Stuttgart, Germany.
Cooper held successive roles including program manager and assembly business unit manager at DDC.
Most recently, Cooper served as director of production at the Mount Holly Truck Manufacturing Plant.
As director of production, Cooper led a 1,500-member team to achieve its production goals.
Cooper earned a Bachelor of Science degree in Finance and a Master’s in Business Administration from the University of Detroit Mercy.
Cooper worked as an adjunct professor at the University of Detroit Mercy and continues to serve on its alumni board.
Daimler Truck North America (DTNA) is headquartered in Portland, Oregon.
DTNA designs, engineers, manufactures, and markets medium- and heavy-duty trucks, school buses, vehicle chassis, and associated technologies and components.
DTNA operates under the Freightliner, Western Star, Thomas Built Buses, Freightliner Custom Chassis Corp, and Detroit brands.
DTNA is a subsidiary of Daimler Truck Holding AG (DTG), one of the world’s leading commercial vehicle manufacturers.
source_title: Joanna Cooper Named General Manager of Mount Holly Truck Plant | Daimler Truck North America

facts: Daimler Trucks North America (DTNA) marked the production of its 3,000,000th vehicle in North America on August 22, 2014.
The milestone was commemorated by the delivery of a Freightliner Cascadia® Evolution to representatives from Schneider at DTNA’s manufacturing facility in Cleveland, North Carolina.

Roger Nielsen, chief operating officer of Daimler Trucks North America, stated the company is committed to pacing the industry in terms of engineering and manufacturing excellence, as well as production of vehicles that provide a real total cost of ownership solution for customers.
The 3,000,000th vehicle is the first Freightliner Cascadia Evolution to be equipped with the new 6×2 Detroit™ integrated Powertrain package.

Featuring the Detroit DD15® engine, Detroit DT12™ transmission and Detroit axles, the integrated Powertrain provides state-of-the-art component communication to achieve the highest level of performance.
The new Detroit integrated Powertrain will be available in full production in the Freightliner Cascadia Evolution in January 2015.
Richard Shearing, vice president of national accounts for Daimler Trucks North America, stated that DTNA is the only OEM that designs, engineers and manufactures a completely integrated Powertrain.
Schneider, one of the nation’s largest truckload carriers, took possession of the landmark truck.
Rob Reich, senior vice president at Schneider, noted they have owned more than 25,000 Freightliner trucks over the years.
Freightliner Cascadia Evolution has received over 50,000 orders since its start of production in 2013.
The Cascadia Evolution delivers up to a 7 percent increase in fuel economy over its 2010 EPA-compliant predecessor.
The integrated Detroit Powertrain features the flagship DD15 engine, with new downsped rating of 400hp and 1750 lb/ft.
The downsped rating enables the engine to turn at lower RPMs regardless of road speed, ultimately reducing fuel consumption and friction.
The integrated DT12 continues to push the innovation envelope with the new Intelligent Powertrain Management (IPM).
This powerful technology uses pre-loaded terrain maps and GPS to know the route ahead and automatically adjusts transmission and engine functions, resulting in an even smoother ride.

Features such as eCoast, a technology specific to the DT12 that allows the engine to operate at idle of 500 RPM when road and driver inputs don't require engine power to move the truck down the road, and creep mode, which improves low-speed maneuverability, perform with the powertrain to benefit performance.

Detroit now offers a 6×2 configuration with a 2.28 ratio that is specifically designed to work with the downsped engine rating, making it the fastest ratio available while still providing the performance drivers demand.
The 6×2 Detroit axle configuration incorporates a non-driven tag axle on the tandem, reducing total weight by almost 400 lbs.
A new 2.41 ratio in its 6×4 axle configuration is also available for applications requiring superior traction.
The integrated Detroit Powertrain includes steer axles that use low-friction needle bearings for maximum maneuverability and minimal maintenance.
DTNA was founded in 1942 when Leland James began manufacturing trucks in Portland, Ore., as the Freightliner Corporation.

In 1981, Daimler-Benz AG (now Daimler AG), one of the world’s premier automotive companies and builder of heavy-duty commercial vehicles, purchased Freightliner Trucks from Consolidated Freightways, and in the following decade, vehicle sales more than doubled.
With its vast technological resources, Daimler-Benz helped Freightliner Trucks reach the top of the North American heavy-duty truck market by 1992.
Today, DTNA is North America’s leading manufacturer of commercial vehicles, selling more than 140,000 commercial vehicles annually through its manufacturing network in the United States and Mexico.

Nine North American manufacturing facilities produce the company’s vehicles, which include Freightliner trucks, Western Star trucks, Thomas Built buses; as well as Freightliner Custom Chassis, Detroit engines and components, and Alliance Truck Parts.

DTNA manufacturing facility locations include Redford, Mich., Cleveland, NC, Gastonia, NC, High Point, NC, Mt. Holly, NC, Portland, Ore., Gaffney, SC, Saltillo, Mexico, and Santiago Tianguistenco, Mexico.
Daimler Trucks North America LLC, headquartered in Portland, Oregon, is the leading heavy-duty truck manufacturer in North America.
Daimler Trucks North America produces and markets commercial vehicles under the Freightliner, Western Star and Thomas Built Buses nameplates.
Schneider has been the top choice of safety-minded drivers for nearly 80 years.
With over 75 percent of its drivers getting home weekly or better, Schneider provides professional opportunities that meet the work-life balance needs of today's drivers and owner-operators.
Schneider is a $3.6 billion company and ranked a top-paying carrier by National Transportation Institute while being nationally recognized for its support of current and former military personnel.
source_title: Daimler Trucks North America Celebrates the Production of its 3,000,000th Vehicle - Thomas Built Buses

facts: Daimler revealed a testing and development platform consisting of a day cab eCascadia fitted with Torc Robotics' Virtual Driver.
These testbed trucks will provide insights into motion control and energy consumption impacts of autonomous control of BEVs.
The company unveiled a battery-electric eCascadia daycab equipped with an integrated autonomous driving stack from Torc Robotics.
The 116-inch BBC day cab will serve as a technology demonstration platform.
The truck was revealed during a media conference on May 8, 2024.

While still a research and advanced engineering project, the autonomous vehicle has the potential to evolve into a scalable, propulsion agnostic platform for flexible use in different trucking applications.
The goal is to offer customers a choice of the right vehicles for their specific business and transportation needs.
Daimler plans to have SAE Level 4 trucks in commercial hub-to-hub service by 2027 using traditional diesel engines.
Development teams will soon begin exploring promising use cases for autonomous-electric technology.

John O'Leary, president and CEO of Daimler Truck North America, stated: “By combining zero-emission and autonomous technologies in one product, we are testing solutions for challenges our customers are likely to face in the future.”
O'Leary added: “We want to give them choices that allow them to do what they do best. This truck is a great example of the beginning of that development process.”
In the currently tested hub-to-hub application, the intent is to drive autonomously between freight centers along U.S. highway corridors.

By identifying synergies between zero emissions and autonomous infrastructure in a future scenario, the charging infrastructure and autonomous freight hubs could be combined to charge and load simultaneously.
Depending on the application, future autonomous trucks could also be powered by hydrogen-based propulsion technologies.
Daimler Truck said it will leverage the highly scalable and profitable market opportunity that autonomous driving is expected to offer.
The company sees autonomous trucking generating revenues of 3 billion Euro (US $3.22 billion) and EBIT of more than 1 billion Euro (US$1.07 billion) as early as 2030.
Daimler Truck began developing and testing autonomous truck technology in 2015 with the reveal of the Freightliner Inspiration Truck.
The Freightliner Inspiration Truck was the first licensed SAE Level 2 autonomous commercial truck to operate on open U.S. public highways.
Daimler Truck took a majority stake with autonomous technology developer Torc Robotics in 2019.

Since 2022, Daimler has been working with logistics companies such as Schneider and C.R. England testing autonomous-ready trucks in real-world applications moving customer freight autonomously on its test route between Phoenix and Oklahoma City.
The test route between Phoenix and Oklahoma City is a distance of about 960 miles, still far beyond the range of current battery electric technology.
Daimler partnered in 2020 with Waymo to bring the autonomous ready redundant platform to market, designed and built upon 1,400 unique requirements needed for safe and reliable operation.
In January, Daimler Truck and Torc Robotics selected Lidar developer Aeva as the supplier of long-range 4D Lidar technology.
Daimler Truck intends to integrate the Lidar sensors directly in its production process, eliminating the need to retrofit the sensors.
Joanna Buttler, head of Daimler Truck's global autonomous technology group, said: "We believe we now have all the ingredients to drive success in this market by 2027."
The next challenge beyond 2027 will be charging infrastructure.

Buttler said the company wants to avoid introducing any additional inefficiencies to the day-to-day operations and is looking at routes and locations for autonomous terminals that align with customers' needs.
Buttler stated: "We're thinking about those types of challenges today so we'll have a solution tomorrow when the time is right, the technology is ripe, and the customers are ready."
The truck revealed during a media conference call was a 116" BBC eCascadia daycab.
It's a full Class 8 chassis with a gross vehicle weight rating of 82,000 pounds, and a payload capacity of about 43,000 pounds.
This is the first time the full sensor suite and computer stack has been fitted to an all-electric daycab.
It has a 6x4 drivetrain featuring two Detroit e-axles with a combined power rating of 425 horsepower.
Battery capacity of 438 kw/h gives it a range of about 230 miles.
It can charge from zero to 80% in about 2.5 hours using a single-cable CCS 1 connector, or in about 90 minutes with a two-cable configuration.
The demonstration truck features three short range radars, six long range radars, long- and short-range Lidar and about 15 cameras -- some of which are stereoscopic.
This gives very rich perceptual information for the virtual driver to make the necessary driving decisions.
Some of the perception sensors are installed onto the bumper area, while the remaining perception sensors are integrated into the upper sensor bar, above the windshield.
The sensor bar is new and designed to protect the sensors from the elements while retaining all the aerodynamic advantages of an integrated assembly.

Suman Narayanan, director of engineering for Daimler Truck North America, said: "This truck is an extremely solid foundation upon which we can learn what it means to integrate the motion control elements of an autonomous vehicle system and about the integration of perception sensors."
Narayanan added: "This truck will also help us understand how to balance the power consumption by the autonomous computer with most important requirement of an electric vehicle; range."
Narayanan anticipates a "fractional hit in the range" based on today's configurations and experience with power consumption in a diesel configuration.

Back of the envelope calculations suggest that consumption, including powering the perception sensors, the compute stack, and other autonomous related technology is about 3.5 kw of addition load on the batteries.
Narayanan stated: "The most important element that the customer expects from a battery electric vehicle is the range."

He added: "If today we have a range of up to 230 miles to haul about 43,000 pounds, we want to make that our goal. How do we optimize the consumption? How do we leverage aerodynamic efficiencies that we are developing in this product that will help us address that?"
Narayanan and his team will address optimization questions with this demonstrator truck.
New to this truck configuration is the compute stack installation, packaged between the seats and the space behind the passenger seat.
It also has a prototype air cooling system to ensure the system stays at its optimum operating temperature.

The compute system also has its own dedicated power supply and a back-up system with four lead-acid batteries mounted behind the cab, so the computer stays powered even when the high voltage battery system is inactive.
Daimler and Torc have learned about managing diesel trucks with computers, but there is a learning curve ahead with electric propulsion.
The virtual driver will have to learn how to feather the accelerator pedal and manage regenerative braking opportunities to its best advantage.
Managing regenerative braking opportunities will be one of the priorities with the new demonstrator.
Narayanan asked: "First and foremost, what kind of learning we can have on motion control?"
He noted that robotic operation of a diesel propelled vehicle with an automated manual transmission is something they have been learning over the last five years.
This project provides a chance to understand what kind of motion control adaptations must be made in order to propel a battery electric vehicle.
The vehicle's evaluations capabilities will eventually be expanded to include a hydrogen fuel cell solution or hydrogen internal combustion solution.
Narayanan added: "This is our chance to understand what propulsion agnosticism means for autonomous driving."

By expanding its testing and development capabilities, Daimler Truck will be able to provide customers the optimal vehicle solutions, utilizing various propulsion technologies, to enable the most suitable and efficient transportation of their goods.
source_title: Daimler to Merge Autonomous Driving with Battery Electric Propulsion | Heavy Duty Trucking

facts: Daimler Truck delivers the latest iteration of the autonomous-ready truck platform based on the new Fifth Generation Freightliner Cascadia with redundant safety features to Torc.
Torc, a subsidiary of Daimler Truck, enters productization phase to prepare for commercial release.
In addition to existing routes in New Mexico, Texas, and Arizona, the trucks will also be tested in autonomous mode on a new lane in Texas between Laredo and Dallas.
Joanna Buttler is the Head of Autonomous Technology Group at Daimler Truck.

Joanna Buttler stated: “Delivering the latest iteration of our autonomous-ready vehicle platform, including production-intent autonomy hardware to Torc, marks a significant milestone for Daimler Truck towards series maturity and scaling.”
Peter Vaughan Schmidt is the CEO of Torc.
Peter Vaughan Schmidt says: “Our strong collaboration with Daimler Truck represents six years of success in advancing the future of freight.”
Fully integrating Torc’s autonomous driver with Daimler Truck’s Freightliner Cascadia platform creates an industry-first, scalable, physical-AI autonomous trucking solution.

This integration will unlock tremendous value for customers by addressing key industry pain points and presents a clear opportunity to generate revenue and drive meaningful transformation across the industry.

Stuttgart, Germany / Portland, USA – Daimler Truck North America has started delivering its latest flagship on-highway trucks to the autonomous testing fleet of Torc Robotics, a subsidiary of Daimler Truck.
These trucks are based on the recently unveiled Fifth Generation Freightliner Cascadia, which was introduced last year.
The autonomous-ready version of the new Freightliner flagship is equipped with redundant safety features like braking and steering and is intended for series production.
Daimler Truck North America was the first in the industry to develop a scalable, powertrain-agnostic, redundant autonomous vehicle platform.

To enable SAE Level 4 autonomous driving, the company has purposefully designed and built redundancy into the Freightliner Cascadia platform for safety-critical systems for safe, driverless operations.

With over 1,500 engineering requirements, all translated into features, and a second set of electronically controlled systems like an integrated power network, the autonomous-ready Cascadia sets an industry standard for autonomous systems integration.
The new generation of the industry’s best-selling Class 8 truck in the North American market redefines benchmarks in safety, efficiency, and profitability.

In addition to key features for increased aerodynamics and more business efficiency, the new truck offers expanded capabilities for the Detroit Assurance Suite of Safety Systems, an all-new Intelligent Braking Control System as well as Dual Stage Intelligent LED Headlights.
The newest version of the Freightliner’s on-highway truck also delivers a greater than 35 percent fuel economy improvement since the model’s first introduction in 2007.
Daimler Truck North America installs all essential compute and sensor kits during the vehicle production process.
This preparation allows Torc Robotics to seamlessly integrate their virtual driver upon delivery.
There is significant freight volume moving between Laredo and Dallas, connecting major cities like San Antonio and Austin.
Torc recently announced that it has signed a leasing agreement for an autonomous hub in the Dallas-Fort Worth area.
This hub will serve as the operational base for its autonomous testing efforts, customer freight pilots, and future commercialization.
Torc is driving toward productization, positioning itself to scale and commercialize safe, robust autonomous trucking solutions on this busy freight corridor under real-world conditions.
In 2024, Torc reached another milestone by successfully completing driver-out test runs on a multi-lane test track in Texas.
As a next step, Daimler Truck and Torc will further develop autonomous trucks to safely achieve the driver-out capability on public roads.
Daimler Truck and Torc aim to enter the U.S. market with SAE Level 4 autonomous trucks by 2027.
In this application, the autonomous driving system takes over the entire dynamic driving task between two freight hubs.
Autonomous trucks can enhance business operations and manage increasing freight volumes, especially during driver shortages.
They also have the potential to reduce accidents, as the system remains alert and never tires.
Daimler Truck has highlighted the highly scalable and profitable market opportunities that autonomous driving is expected to offer.
source_title: Autonomous Driving: Daimler Truck delivers latest iteration of autonomous-ready truck platform to Torc | Daimler Truck North America

facts: Magna International, Inc. operates a facility in Carrollton, Georgia known as Decostar Industries.

The Magna Exteriors portfolio includes access systems such as liftgates, exterior trim, modular systems, front-end modules including fascia, active aerodynamic systems, and lightweight structural components for automotive, commercial truck, and other industrial markets.
A Sr. Program Manager position was posted at the Carrollton, Georgia facility, but the job posting has expired.
The role requires developing program execution plans under the Advanced Product Quality and Planning (APQP) process.
Production readiness is defined as developing parts capable of being manufactured by each department at the quoted performance parameters for material, labor, and overhead utilization.
The position involves creating and maintaining a master timeline detailing internal and supplier execution deadlines required to meet customer milestones.
Responsibilities include maintaining tooling costs within budget and supporting requests for quote packages to potential suppliers.
A competitive analysis must be completed prior to awarding business, using the Decostar Tooling Manual, engineering drawings, and math data as guidelines.
The role requires utilizing APQP and PPAP processes for automotive part submission as required for QS-9000/TS 16949 certification.
Candidates must have a minimum of five years experience in the design or engineering of automotive exterior decorative trim.

Candidates must have a minimum of two years of experience managing technical personnel and complex activities in research and development, product engineering, design engineering, or technical management.
A Bachelor's Degree in Engineering is required for the position.
The facility requires compliance with OSHA Act, EEOC, and FLSA regulations, ensuring employees understand their rights under these acts.
Safety protocols mandate immediately reporting accidents, incidents, or near misses to the Safety Department and completing required Accident Reporting Forms within 24 hours.
All equipment and tooling must be transferred on time, with parts properly manufactured, painted, CMM measured, and shipped to meet customer delivery dates and PPAP submission requirements.

Magna International, Inc. is recognized globally as an innovator in vehicle exteriors, providing materials development, design, manufacturing, and assembly to help automakers create vehicles worldwide.
source_title: Sr. Program Manager at Magna International, Inc. in Carrollton, Georgia | Disabled Persons

facts: Dekalb Tool & Die, Inc. operates as a metal stamping manufacturing source for the transportation industry.
The company manufactures galvanized steel roof caps for buses with eight different hole configuration variations.
The main manufacturing process is deep drawn metal stamping utilizing Dekalb Tool and Die’s 1,800 ton large double action press.
The press stamps a 12″ deep draw into a 52″ x 112″ galvanized steel blank with a .047″ (18 ga.) thickness.
DeKalb Tool and Die uses a precision Mitsubishi VZ-1 5-axis laser for the laser trimming and hole creation process.
The use of the laser eliminates the need for an additional trim die, creating lower tooling costs for the customer and a more efficient production run with the requirement for multiple variations.
Galvanized steel roof cap metal stampings are finished with a powder coat by one of Dekalb Tool and Die’s finishing production partners.
Finished metal stampings are shipped and delivered to Dekalb Tool & Die’s customer located in the Southeastern U.S. on a Dekalb Tool & Die truck.
The sheet metal stamped bus roof cap was custom manufactured in 8 different variations in a low volume part run.
Trim line tolerance is ± .03.
Hole position tolerance is ± .015.
Material specifications include 18 Ga. Galvanized Steel with an E-coat base/ Powder Coat Finish (Out sourced).
Each finished unit weighs 21 lbs.
Applications for the stamped components include Truck, Bus and Heavy Equipment.
The production run size is 5,000 units.
Customers supply prints and 2D CAD drawings for manufacturing.
source_title: Deep Drawn Metal Stamping Tool and Die Laser Cutting Manufacturing - Tucker Georgia

facts: DENSO announced it will expand its operations in Maryville, Tennessee.
DENSO will invest $1 billion to make its Blount County facility a primary manufacturing center in North America for electrification and safety systems.
The expansion will create approximately 1,000 new jobs in Maryville.
Governor Bill Haslam stated the investment creates 1,000 new jobs and invests $1 billion in Blount County.
DENSO has been a part of Tennessee for over 30 years.
Governor Bill Haslam aims to make Tennessee the No. 1 location in the Southeast for high quality jobs.
Commissioner Bob Rolfe stated Tennessee is No. 1 for growth in automotive employment in the Southeast since 2011, adding over 22,000 jobs.
DENSO has a presence in Tennessee for nearly three decades with two locations: Maryville and Athens.
DENSO currently employs roughly 4,500 people in Tennessee.
The Maryville campus is DENSO’s largest U.S. manufacturing facility.
The Maryville campus consists of four manufacturing plants on 194 acres.
The Maryville campus is Blount County’s largest employer.

Kenichiro Ito, chairman of DENSO’s North America Board of Directors and CEO of DENSO International America, stated the investment positions the company to meet dramatic shifts in the role of transportation in society.
DENSO produces starters, alternators, instrument clusters, safety products, and a wide range of electronic components and systems for the automotive industry at its Maryville operations.
Globally, DENSO is a leading developer of electrification systems for environmentally-friendly automobiles.
DENSO develops sophisticated functions involved in vehicle safety and security.
DENSO develops products that connect vehicles and society.
DENSO develops thermal systems to cool the cabin and under-the-hood components.
Jack Helmboldt, president of DENSO Manufacturing Tennessee, stated the investment enables new opportunities for top talent to advance the next generation of vehicles.
The expansion will expand multiple production lines to produce advanced safety, connectivity, and electrification products for hybrid and electric vehicles.
These new products will radically improve fuel efficiency and preserve electric power by recovering and recycling energy.
The new products will connect all systems and products inside the vehicles.
Prior to this expansion, DENSO had invested approximately $2 billion in Tennessee.
Japan is the leading source of foreign direct investment in Tennessee over the last 30 years.
Japanese businesses have invested more than $17 billion in Tennessee and employ more than 50,000 people.
Blount County Mayor Ed Mitchell thanked DENSO for its commitment to the community and noted the economic impact across all business sectors throughout Blount County cannot be underestimated.
Maryville Mayor Tom Taylor stated nearly 30 years ago the city made a commitment to DENSO and it has steadily grown into one of the major economic influencers for the economy.
Alcoa City Mayor Don Mull thanked DENSO for its diligence in being an active member of the community and appreciated the impact on expanding for the future workforce.
Blount County Industrial Development Board Chair Fred Lawson stated attracting and retaining good companies with above average wages is one of their tenets.

Fred Lawson noted the advancement shows the commitment of the Blount Partnership, the State of Tennessee economic development office, and Governor Bill Haslam in making the area a leader in economic development.
TVA Senior Vice President of Economic Development John Bradley congratulated DENSO on its plans to expand and create over a thousand new job opportunities in Blount County.
Maryville and Blount County are represented in the Tennessee General Assembly by Sen. Doug Overbey (R – Maryville), Rep. Bob Ramsey (R – Maryville), and Rep. Art Swann (R – Maryville).
DENSO’s North American headquarters is located in Southfield, Michigan.
DENSO employs more than 23,000 people at 28 consolidated subsidiaries and 4 affiliates across the North American region.
Of DENSO's North American facilities, 25 are manufacturing facilities located in the United States, Canada, and Mexico.

In the United States alone, DENSO employs more than 17,000 people in Alabama, Arkansas, California, Iowa, Georgia, Kentucky, Michigan, North Carolina, Ohio, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, Tennessee, and Texas.
DENSO’s North American consolidated sales totaled US$9.6 billion for the fiscal year ending March 31, 2017.
DENSO Corp. is headquartered in Kariya, Aichi prefecture, Japan.
DENSO has more than 200 subsidiaries and affiliates in 38 countries and regions worldwide.
DENSO employs more than 150,000 people worldwide.
DENSO’s consolidated global sales totaled US$40.4 billion for the fiscal year ending March 31, 2017.
Last fiscal year, DENSO spent 8.8 percent of its global consolidated sales on research and development.
DENSO common stock is traded on the Tokyo and Nagoya stock exchanges.
source_title: Governor Haslam, Commissioner Rolfe and DENSO Announce $1 Billion Investment, Expansion in Maryville - TNECD

facts: DENSO announced the opening of its Central Technical Training Center at its facility in Maryville, Tennessee.
The state-of-the-art technical training center was designed by DENSO to educate employees about the latest technologies and best practices in advanced manufacturing.
The initiative aims to ensure the company and its workforce remain leaders in manufacturing as it becomes more automated, connected, and data-rich.
The development is part of DENSO’s 2017 commitment to invest $1 billion in the Maryville location.
That investment has helped DENSO in Maryville expand its production of vehicle safety and electrification systems.
The investment has helped create 1,000 jobs at the Maryville location.
The investment established new employee training opportunities and reinforced the site’s ability to be a primary manufacturing center for DENSO in North America.
All of this supports DENSO’s Long-Term Policy 2030, which aims to create new value for advanced mobility.

Kenichiro Ito, senior executive officer of DENSO Corp. and CEO of DENSO’s North American headquarters, stated that DENSO’s manufacturing philosophy is to produce products in the region, for the region.
Ito noted that as a leading mobility supplier, DENSO manufactures products and systems for all major automakers in North America.
Ito emphasized it is crucial to remain at manufacturing’s leading edge to help customers realize new mobility solutions, with the technical training center helping accomplish that.
DENSO in Maryville plans to educate more than 1,500 employees annually at the training facility.
The facility features labs focused on electrical, robotic, and mechanical applications in manufacturing.
DENSO employees will have access to more than 50 hands-on courses in production-related areas, including electrical, mechanical, and mechatronic technologies, and automation fundamentals.
Future course programming will expand into rapidly evolving fields, including cybersecurity, artificial intelligence, and data analytics.
DENSO collaborates with local colleges and high schools to better prepare students interested in manufacturing for successful and rewarding careers in the industry.
Jack Helmboldt, president emeritus at DENSO in Maryville, stated they have manufactured technology in east Tennessee that makes cars safer and cleaner for more than 30 years.
Helmboldt noted that sustained production is impossible without the support and skill of the local community.

Helmboldt added that opening the Central Technical Training Center demonstrates commitment to employees and to maintaining DENSO and east Tennessee’s position as a manufacturing leader in North America.
Speakers at the training facility’s grand opening included Ito and Bob Booker, senior manager of Legal Services and Corporate Compliance at DENSO in Maryville.
Blount County Mayor Ed Mitchell and Commissioner of the Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development Bob Rolfe also spoke at the grand opening.

The Central Technical Training Center’s opening follows DENSO investing $1.95 million last year in its thermal manufacturing facility in Battle Creek, Michigan to open the North Technical Training Center.
Both training centers bolster DENSO’s manufacturing expertise across North America, according to the company.
DENSO’s Maryville location has nearly 5,000 employees and specializes in production of vehicle safety systems and electrification components.
DENSO has other Tennessee locations in Athens, Mount Juliet, and Jackson.
source_title: DENSO Opens Central Tech Training Center In TN

facts: DENSO Manufacturing Tennessee Inc. announced a four-year plan and $1 billion investment in 2017 to transform into a hub for electric vehicle parts and safety systems.
Within the last few months prior to publication, DENSO has invested $11 million in plant renovations.
DENSO spokesperson David Lazar stated that $10.7 million worth of construction was completed during June as part of the transformation.
In July, DENSO permitted another $319,375 worth of renovations.
Development activities span the Maryville campus, including Building 204, manufacturing areas, offices, and communal spaces.
The company has installed EV chargers in its parking lot.
Building 204 was opened in December 2018 with a ribbon cutting ceremony.
The renovation expanded the manufacturing campus with 335,900 square feet of indoor industrial space established for new technology.
Spokesperson David Lazar stated that despite pandemic and industry challenges, the company is making steady progress in developments and hiring.
The original $1 billion investment announcement included a goal of creating 1,000 new jobs in Maryville.
An October 2017 news release from DENSO stated that the new jobs would be in production and support, including roles for technicians and engineers.
The investments are intended to make the Maryville plant a primary manufacturing center in North America for electrification and safety systems.
New vehicle systems made in Maryville will play a crucial role in meeting increasing electric vehicle demand.
A July AAA survey found that 25% of people plan to purchase a fully electric vehicle (not a hybrid) the next time they shop for a car.
Of those planning to buy an EV, 77% want to save money by not having to visit the gas pump.
AAA spokesperson Megan Cooper stated that record-high gas prices have brought the cost of owning an EV more in line with a standard gasoline-powered vehicle.
Cooper noted that although EVs may cost more upfront, they cost less to charge, maintain, and are more efficient.
Industry predictions state that by 2050, 50% of vehicles on the road will be electric.
Ryan Stanton, senior project manager for EV Evolution at the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA), spoke during a statewide Tennessee Advanced Energy Business Council roundtable in July.
Stanton stated that growth in EVs will contribute to an increase in power grid demand over the next two decades.
TVA is confident it can meet increased EV demand through current and additional capacity.
Currently, TVA services about 27,000 EVs and has a goal to increase that service to 200,000 by 2028.
Stanton noted that servicing 200,000 EVs would still only make up about a half a percent of TVA’s load today.
source_title: DENSO in Maryville is $11 million closer to becoming an EV manufacturing hub | News | thedailytimes.com

facts: The State of Georgia has become a center of international business, with Atlanta and Savannah serving as primary global gateways for people and products through air, sea, and rail.

Fifty years ago in 1973, state leadership under then-Governor Jimmy Carter began establishing Georgia’s physical presence abroad to kickstart its trajectory to becoming a global hub for business and innovation.

Coryn Marsik, Director of International Investment at the Georgia Department of Economic Development, stated that having international representation for over 50 years has helped give Georgia's economy remarkable resiliency.
Georgia’s international offices work to recruit diverse investments that create jobs for Georgians and help local companies reach new global markets.
The State of Georgia’s office in Tokyo was first established in 1973, providing direct access to rapidly developing markets in East Asia.
Over the next few decades, Georgia expanded its international presence to include new offices dedicated to serving individual countries.
Japanese companies continue to be the top international employer in Georgia today.

Ties with Japan are supported by organizations such as the Japan-America Society of Georgia and the Japan External Trade Organization, alongside Georgia’s role as a charter member in the Southeast U.S.-Japan Alliance.
The State of Georgia’s Europe Office was established shortly after the Japan office, with professionals serving the European market from Munich, Germany.
Germany is one of Georgia’s top investor nations and has a robust mobility industry that anchors Georgia’s mission to attract innovative technologies from Europe.
In 2020, Governor Brian Kemp and a State of Georgia delegation met with automotive companies headquartered in Germany, where electrification and alternative fuels were repeatedly discussed.
Following the 2020 meetings, the state established a new goal to identify opportunities at every step of the mobility and battery supply chain.
Just three years later, the mobility and battery supply chain announced more than $25.5 billion in investments.
Just three years later, the mobility and battery supply chain announced an estimated 30,700 new jobs over the next few years.
European-based companies enhancing Georgia’s mobility ecosystem include GEDIA Automotive, Aurubis AG, and Heliox.
The success of Georgia’s mobility and clean tech industries led to representation at the 2023 World Economic Forum in Davos-Klosters, Switzerland.
Governor Brian Kemp served on a panel at the 2023 World Economic Forum, sharing that investors can count on stability and a great economy and business environment in Georgia.

Ellen Kraft, Managing Director of the Europe Office, noted that the European market offers opportunities for attracting innovative companies, helping Georgia businesses connect with new customers, and establishing new ties like the North Rhine-Westphalia office in Atlanta.
International businesses operating in Georgia tap into university-backed research, innovative ecosystems, and solutions-oriented partnerships to stay up-to-date on business trends.
Commissioner Pat Wilson explains how Georgia is reaping the seeds sown decades ago by visionary leaders regarding the state's industrial success.

links.context: Commissioner Pat Wilson explanation on Georgia's industrial success

source_title: GDEcD: 50 Years of International Presence - Tifton CEO

facts: At one time, Georgia was leading the country in incentives for EVs.
Approximately a year ago, Atlanta, Georgia was second in the nation in electric car use.
New registrations of electric vehicles have fallen ~90% since the summer of 2015.
State law provided those who bought or leased an EV a $5,000 tax credit.
A Georgian with an EV could divide that credit over a two-year lease and recoup about $200 a month.
In 2015, Georgia state lawmakers ended the tax incentive to find savings that offset a transportation spending bill.
Don Francis is the coordinator of the federal Clean Cities program in the state.
Tim Echols said the timing for approval of new tax incentives might be better in a few years.
Manufacturers like Chevy and Tesla promise to develop electric cars that can drive far longer distances on a single charge.
California's Charge Ahead program includes a $2,500 ZEV rebate and ZEV mandate.
California aims to be emissions free by 2025.
source_title: Georgia Electric Car Sales Drop ~90% In ~6 Months - CleanTechnica

facts: Dongwon Autopart Technology plans a $30 million investment for a new automotive project in the CSRA, Georgia.
Governor Brian P. Kemp’s office announced that the company will build a new production facility in Emanuel County.
Hiring for the new facility will draw workers from Burke, Jefferson, and Jenkins counties to fill management, system leads, and production positions.
Hiring for approximately 200 positions will begin this year.
Dongwon Autopart Technology is a 55-year-old company that supplies components such as doorframes, seat frames, and battery frames to automotive original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
The company currently supports more than 500 direct jobs in the Southeast.
Existing Dongwon Autopart Technology facilities in Hogansville, Georgia, will remain operational.

Heather Hollstein, Human Resources Manager at Dongwon Autopart Technology, stated the company has been operating in the United States since 2003 and remains committed to excellence and supplying customers with high-quality products and service.
The new manufacturing facility will be located at the Hwy 297 Industrial Park near Swainsboro.

Ken Warnock, CEO of the Emanuel County Development Authority, stated his organization assisted Dongwon Autopart Technology with site selection and that the project will be the single largest new economic development project for Emanuel County in over 30 years.
source_title: Automotive company to hire 200 people in the CSRA with a record investment - Augusta Business Daily
