facts: TDK Corporation is a Japanese multinational electronics corporation that manufactures electronic components, power supplies, and sensors.
Since the 2000s, TDK has focused on the development, manufacture and sales of electronic components, HDD heads and suspension, and power supplies.
Beginning in 2005, TDK has acquired many types of electronic device manufacturers including passive component developers, sensors manufacturers and power supply companies.
TDK's portfolio includes multi-axis MEMS motion tracking devices and MEMS microphones from InvenSense.
TDK acquired a point-of-load DC-DC converter technology from Faraday Semi.
TDK acquired MEMS-based ultrasonic Time-of-Flight sensors from Chirp Microsystems intended for consumer electronics, AR/VR, robotics, drones, IoT, automotive and industrial market segments.

Other areas of TDK's recent focus include power components for mobile devices, high-stability MEMS accelerometers from Tronics, miniaturized haptic actuators, and custom ASICs through its subsidiary ICsense.
In 2017, TDK and GenCell began collaborating to develop and produce an ammonia-based fuel solution.
In 2005, TDK acquired Amperex Technology Limited, a Lithium Polymer battery company based in Hong Kong.
In 2005, TDK acquired the Lambda Power Division, a group of power supply businesses of London-based Invensys PLC.
In 2017, TDK formed RF360 Holdings Singapore PTE Ltd., a joint venture with Qualcomm Inc.
In October 2024, McLaren announced TDK as the official technology partner for the McLaren Formula E Team and the McLaren Shadow F1 Sim Racing Team.
In 1980, TDK developed a multilayering technology to create chip capacitors and inductors used in personal computers, laptops, smartphones and other electronic devices.
source_title: TDK - Wikipedia

facts: TE Connectivity is a Tier 2 supplier to global automotive supply chains.
TE Connectivity operates a manufacturing plant in the North-Western Hungarian city of Esztergom.
The Hungarian facility employs more than a thousand people.
Passive electrical connectors and connector housings are manufactured at the facility using plastic injection moulding.
These products are used in vehicles from leading auto makers including the Volkswagen Group, PSA, and Mercedes.
In 2019, the Hungarian plant experienced material handling problems because existing systems reached their capability limits.
Labour shortages worsened the situation as interest in low-skilled warehouse operator positions declined sharply.
Industry 4.0-driven transformation is part of TE's corporate goals, prompting a decision to automate internal logistics using AMRs.
TE turned its Hungarian plant into an industrial test environment to experiment with multiple parallel AMR and MRE solutions simultaneously.
The company concluded that the MiR200 AMR combined with a TMC300 top module, cart, and docking station from ROEQ best suited its needs.
TE's fleet consists of six MiR200s, most carrying raw materials and finished products between the warehouse and value streams.
The plant operates as a semi-automatic system where employees launch missions and manually move carts connected to robots in production areas and warehouses.
Gábor Miszler serves as the Continuous Improvement Analyst at TE.
The ROEQ TMC300 top module, cart, and docking station provided a standardized solution easily integrated due to full compatibility with MiR robots and ROEQ Assist software.
ROEQ Assist software is fully integrated into the robots' fleet management program, eliminating the need for employees to learn separate programs.
Regularly occurring tasks can be pre-programmed, allowing missions to be launched quickly without special expertise.
Employees were initially averse to robots but accepted the technology after recognizing its ability to make jobs easier.
Two warehouse workers per shift learned to operate the robots at a basic level and correct simple errors like being stuck or lost.
The patented locking mechanism of the carts allows precise and safe connection to both the robot and the docking station.
ROEQ Assist software provides continuous tracking, allowing staff to check at any time which robot or dock a cart is connected to.
Staff can easily disconnect carts from robots at mission endpoints in the production area and warehouse, then move them manually.
ROEQ carts are remarkably stable and safe, maintaining a zero accident and zero property loss record for two years since deployment.
The ROEQ cart solution increases AMR payload, improving overall efficiency and throughput of the material handling process.
TE Connectivity purchased ROEQ's modules and carts after the outbreak of the Covid-19 epidemic.
Gábor Schvamberger serves as the Logistics Analyst at TE.
Travel restrictions prevented engineers from the Danish plant from traveling to Esztergom to assist with deployment.
TE set up and coordinated the devices and programs themselves using easy implementation, detailed manuals, and remote support from the manufacturer and distributor.
ROEQ top modules and equipment are extremely reliable, with quick support available for repairs or maintenance.
The distributor and ROEQ engineers provided immediate assistance during an initial challenge, discovering the cause and providing a permanent technical solution.
VFP Systems, the ROEQ partner in Hungary, supported TE during the acquisition of robotic solutions.
András Dobner serves as the Head of Internal Logistics at VFP Systems.
The TE Esztergom plant currently uses 3 ROEQ TMC300 top modules, 15 ROEQ 300E Easy-Pull-Out carts, and 10 easy locking docking stations for material handling tasks.
The company is building a new production hall optimized for AMR characteristics to leverage fleet capabilities more effectively and increase AMR utilization.
The payback time for the automation investment is estimated at around 2-3 years, requiring only half of the average payback time in logistics.
TE Connectivity is a USD 14 billion technology and manufacturing company.
TE Connectivity's products have supported the development of transport, industrial applications, and communication industries for more than 75 years.
source_title: TE Connectivity â€“ Hungary Case Study  | RoboticsTomorrow

facts: Georgian Economy Minister Mariam Kvrivishvili stated that the modernization of Georgia’s transport and logistics network aims to ensure uninterrupted transit of growing trade flows between Europe and Asia.
Georgia has named the development of the Middle Corridor an important priority to improve regional connectivity, develop transport systems, and accelerate logistics processes.
Major infrastructure projects being implemented include the active construction of the East-West Highway and the development of the Anaklia deep-sea port project.
The Anaklia Deep Sea Port is considered one of Georgia’s most important strategic projects to strengthen the country's economy and reinforce its strategic position.
Active construction is currently underway at the Anaklia Deep Sea Port, with progress open for public site visits.
The Anaklia Deep Sea Port is expected to receive its first vessel in 2029.

The Georgian Government decided to develop the Anaklia Deep-Sea Port under a landlord model, whereby the state will retain ownership of the core maritime and port infrastructure while overseeing its development and management.
The Anaklia Deep Sea Port will remain under state ownership, with international companies involved only in managing terminal infrastructure.

The development model ensures state leadership in strategic infrastructure while creating a competitive environment that accommodates the interests of multiple partner countries and international companies.
source_title: Georgian Economy Minister: Georgia modernizes transport network to ensure smooth transit between Europe, Asia | imedinews

entity_name: Georgia Electric Vehicle Market & Policy Landscape
canonical_name: Georgia EV Market and Policy Landscape
facts: Shelley Francis, a former medical school professor focused on environmental health who lives in Smyrna, co-founded EVHybridNoire to encourage Black and Latino drivers to adopt plug-in cars.
EVHybridNoire has a diverse membership of drivers owning vehicles ranging from used 10-year-old Nissan LEAFs to Ford Mustang Mach-E and Porsche Taycan models.
Georgia once led the nation in newly registered electric vehicles but now ranks 10th for the number of EVs on roadways.
There are approximately 24,400 electric vehicles in Georgia, with about one-third located in Fulton County.
Other top counties for EV adoption include DeKalb, Cobb, Gwinnett, and Forsyth, all within metro Atlanta.
Electric vehicles make up just over 1% of all newly registered automobiles in Georgia, a percentage that has remained steady for the last three years.
The state ended a generous $5,000 tax break for electric vehicles in 2015 and simultaneously created an annual EV fee.
The current annual EV fee is set at nearly $214, which advocates argue exceeds what many motorists pay in fuel taxes annually.
Georgia's gasoline tax is 28.7 cents per gallon, meaning a compact SUV owner would need to fill up weekly to match the annual fuel tax paid by an EV owner under the new fee structure.
The EV fee was included in a 2015 transportation bill intended to fund repairs for the state's roads and bridges.
Electric vehicle owners contributed $5 million in revenue to the state last year according to the Department of Revenue.
Georgia is one of more than two dozen states with a special electric car fee, which range from $50 to $225 across different states.
California charges a $100 EV fee while Florida does not have an annual fee for electric vehicles.

Anne Blair, director of energy efficient transportation for the Southeast Energy Efficiency Alliance, stated that lawmakers were too quick to impose the fee and called it punitive during the industry's early adoption stage.
Blair noted the fee acts as a deterrent particularly for lower-income individuals purchasing used EVs around $8,000.
Shelley Francis of EVHybridNoire believes the cumulative costs of new technology, charging logistics, and the additional fee deter potential buyers.
Legislative attempts to resurrect the tax credit or revisit the fee have failed over the years.
State Rep. Chuck Martin (Alpharetta Republican) successfully pushed to lower the fee for low-speed vehicles like golf carts this session.
Martin drives a hybrid car and agrees the state's EV fee is too high, advocating it should correlate more closely with gas taxes for comparable models.
Martin previously pushed to end the tax credit but now supports a smaller, revamped incentive to help people afford a range of EV models.
Some buyers previously used Georgia's tax credit to lease a Nissan LEAF for virtually nothing.
Martin introduced a bill this session to allow more electric vehicle manufacturers to sell directly to consumers in Georgia, similar to Tesla's model.
The direct-to-consumer bill has bipartisan co-signers and received a hearing but faced strong opposition from auto dealers concerned about undermining the franchise system.
Ben Jordan, lobbyist for the Georgia Automobile Dealers Association, told lawmakers that dealers thrive on competition rather than fearing it.
Steve Ewing, president and CEO of Wade Ford in Smyrna, argued that protecting dealership jobs is necessary to compete with manufacturer-owned stores.
Martin clarified his bill would only apply to zero-emission manufacturers without existing Georgia dealer connections, such as Rivian and Lucid, not established brands like Ford.
A Lucid representative told lawmakers that over 83% of electric vehicles purchased in Georgia last year were bought directly from manufacturers.
EV supporters argue the proposal would allow consumers to interact with specialists and avoid driving out of state to purchase from new manufacturers.
The bill remains active for next year, with Martin planning to limit it to metro Atlanta's seven-county nonattainment zone due to population density and air quality standards.
Sen. Elena Parent (Atlanta Democrat) unsuccessfully tried to eliminate the EV fee and restore the tax credit but believes interested buyers will switch anyway due to fuel and maintenance savings.
Parent cited last month's Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack as evidence of the benefits of reducing reliance on the gasoline network.
Range anxiety remains a deterrent for long trips, though newer models like the Ford F150 Lightning are expected to reach up to 300 miles.
Most day-to-day EV charging occurs at home, and Parent emphasized that charging infrastructure is critical until drivers feel confident on long trips.
Georgia Power included charging station costs in its recent rate case before the Public Service Commission.
The commission approved $24 million for two programs to reduce business charger installation costs and fund public fast-charging stations, with 50 already installed statewide.
Solar-powered charging stations were installed at Atlanta Technical College and Valdosta State University.
Georgia Power spokeswoman Allison Erdman Gregoire stated Georgia is already the sixth largest market in the nation for EV charging.
President Biden is pushing to include funding for EV charging infrastructure in his jobs plan, with Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg visiting Atlanta last month to champion it.
Buttigieg stated the infrastructure plan would trigger an electric vehicle revolution made in America.
U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff told reporters during Buttigieg's visit that EVs are coming in force and surface transportation infrastructure must be prepared with adequate charging station density.
A recent iteration of a federal infrastructure deal included about $10 billion for charging stations, funded by a fee on EV users, though some lawmakers have objected to the fee.
Anne Blair stated federal investment could significantly accelerate America's shift to electric vehicles from public health, climate, and economic perspectives.
A poll in Georgia's competitive 6th and 7th congressional districts showed bipartisan support for the American Jobs Plan among voters.
About two-thirds of respondents supported investment in EVs and charging stations to reduce pollution and help Americans buy clean cars.
Support for federal spending on roads, bridges, and public transit was higher at 83% in the 6th district and 82% in the 7th district.
Jennette Gayer, director of Environment Georgia, noted that support for EV adoption measures is higher among young voters who are more excited about new technology.
source_title: ‘Electric vehicle revolution’ in Georgia arrives at crossroads - The Current

entity_name: Flow Batteries vs Lithium-Ion Grid Storage Analysis
canonical_name: Flow Batteries vs Lithium-Ion 2026 Market Update
facts: The stationary storage market has shattered early-2026 forecasts.
Lithium-ion (dominated by LFP chemistry) has officially surpassed 400 GWh in cumulative global utility-scale installations.
Lithium-ion deployment is driven by an aggressive 150+ GWh build-out in China and IRA-backed deployment in the US.
Flow battery deployments have stabilized at roughly 8 GWh.
The flow battery pipeline has quadrupled as specific Long-Duration Energy Storage (LDES) tenders mandate non-lithium solutions capable of 8-12 hour discharge without degradation.
Lithium-ion (specifically LFP) utterly dominates the 1-4 hour duration market.
For 4-12 hour applications, flow batteries can cut lifetime Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS) by 10-25% compared with lithium.
Energy Solutions Intelligence benchmarked 80+ grid-scale projects using both chemistries across North America, Europe, and Asia updated to July 2026.
Vanadium Redox Flow Batteries (VRFB) offer 20+ year lifespans with virtually zero electrolyte degradation.
At durations beyond 6-8 hours, flow battery economics eclipse lithium-ion due to the ability to scale energy capacity simply by increasing tank size.
High upfront CAPEX for flow batteries ranges from $260-$360/kWh for an 8h system.
Flow batteries have lower round-trip efficiency (70-75%) compared with lithium-ion (88-92%).
Targeted long-duration energy storage (LDES) mandates in California, the UK, and China are providing revenue certainty to commercialize large flow projects.
Lithium-Ion stores energy in solid electrodes where power and energy are tightly coupled.
Adding duration to lithium-ion systems requires adding more complete battery packs, including cells, BMS, and enclosures, making long-duration scaling linear and expensive.
The solid lattice of lithium-ion degrades with every charge/discharge cycle.
Flow Batteries (VRFB/Zn-Br) store energy in liquid electrolytes in external tanks pumped through a central cell stack.
In flow batteries, power and energy are decoupled; sizing the cell stack determines power (kW) and tank/fluid volume determines energy (kWh).
Falling lithium carbonate prices in 2024-2025 brought 4h Li-ion systems down significantly.
Installed Cost (CAPEX) for Utility Lithium-Ion (LFP - 4h) is $220 - $320 / kWh.
Round-trip Efficiency for Utility Lithium-Ion is 88% - 92%.
Usable Lifetime Cycles for Utility Lithium-Ion are ~5,000 - 7,000 (Requires Augmentation).
O&M Costs for Utility Lithium-Ion are $5 - $9 per kW-year.
Installed Cost (CAPEX) for Vanadium Flow Battery (VRFB - 8h) is $260 - $360 / kWh.
Round-trip Efficiency for VRFB is 70% - 78%.
Usable Lifetime Cycles for VRFB are 15,000 - 20,000+ (No Cell Augmentation).
O&M Costs for VRFB are $10 - $18 per kW-year.
Lithium-ion has very high land footprint/energy density in compact containers.
Flow batteries have low energy density requiring large external tanks.
Lithium-ion carries high safety risk requiring HVAC and fire suppression.
Flow batteries carry zero fire risk due to aqueous electrolyte.
LCOS per Stored MWh for heavy cycle lithium-ion is baseline (1.0x).
LCOS per Stored MWh for heavy cycle VRFB is ~0.75x - 0.90x.
Invinity was formed by the merger of redT and Avalon.
Invinity is a VRFB provider with major deployments across the UK, North America, and Australia.
ESS pioneers an iron-based flow battery that eliminates vanadium cost volatility using abundant iron, salt, and water.
A Japanese industrial giant built some of the earliest and largest VRFB projects in the world, including a massive 15MW/60MWh system in Hokkaido.
CellCube is based in Austria and has over 130 deployments globally focusing on commercial and industrial microgrids.
Tesla (Megapack), Fluence, Wärtsilä, and Sungrow utterly dominate global lithium-ion market share.
Tesla's Megapack alone accounts for tens of GWh of capacity.
The processing and refining of Lithium, Nickel, and Cobalt remain overwhelmingly concentrated in China.
The US IRA (Inflation Reduction Act) has spurred domestic battery factories.
Vanadium is primarily produced as a co-product of steel manufacturing.
China, Russia, and South Africa control the majority of virgin vanadium production.
Vanadium electrolyte can be leased and retains full residual value at the end of a 20-year project for recovery or resale.
Iron-flow batteries rely on iron, salt, and water, offering supply chain sovereignty without reliance on critical mineral cartels.
Lithium megapacks dominate California and Texas stationary storage markets.
Flow battery pilot projects are scaling in China, the UK, and Australia.
source_title: Flow Batteries vs Lithium-Ion 2026: Cost, LCOS & Grid Storage Economics | Energy Solutions Intelligence

facts: Officials with Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG), Inc. celebrated the official grand opening of Kia’s first-ever U.S. automobile manufacturing plant in West Point, Georgia.
The plant is capable of producing 300,000 cars annually for the North American and global markets.
The ceremony was attended by approximately 500 VIPs from Korea and the U.S.

Attendees included Chairman of the Hyundai-Kia Automotive Group Mong-Koo Chung; Group President and CEO of KMMG and Kia Motors America (KMA) Byung-Mo Ahn; Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue; and other high-ranking Korean government and State of Georgia officials.
Byung-Mo Ahn stated that Kia is proud to call West Point, Georgia home and looks forward to many years of growing together.

Byung-Mo Ahn noted that the successful launch of the 2011 Kia Sorento CUV and establishment of their first U.S. manufacturing plant positions the Kia brand for further growth and to become a worldwide leader in quality and innovation.
The project represents a $1 billion investment by Kia Motors Corporation.
The plant is a sprawling, new 2.2 million-square-foot automobile manufacturing facility situated on more than 2,200 acres of land between Exit 2 and Exit 6 along Interstate 85 in West Point.
KMMG consists of four main shops: stamping, welding, paint, and assembly.
The site also includes a transmission shop, module shop, and a two-mile test track where every vehicle produced by KMMG will be thoroughly tested.

KMMG’s general assembly area is outfitted with more than a half mile of height-adjustable conveyors to achieve the most favorable installation position for each team member and reduce physical fatigue.
The assembly area is outfitted with wood flooring, which provides a more comfortable and forgiving work environment for team members.
Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue noted the event marks the continuation of a mutually beneficial partnership between the State of Georgia and Kia that will benefit the state in years to come.
Governor Perdue stated that Kia Motors has brought thousands of new jobs to the area and is truly delivering as a tremendous corporate citizen.
Governor Perdue pledged to continue providing specialized training through the Kia Georgia Training Center to guarantee a quality workforce and the future success of the plant in Georgia.
The massive Kia automobile manufacturing plant and its supplier companies have already provided thousands of jobs.
KMMG currently employs more than 1,200 team members.
KMMG is expected to nearly double its headcount by the end of the year.
Onsite and nearby suppliers will create up to 7,500 additional jobs in the region.
Randy Jackson, director of human resources and administration, expressed gladness that KMMG could help give back to the local community through job creation, community development, and tourism.
Randy Jackson stated that the Grand Opening marks a new milestone for Kia.
Beginning in spring, KMMG will open its doors to the community to see firsthand the quality and craftsmanship built into every Kia vehicle.
Mass production of the all-new 2011 Kia Sorento CUV began on Nov. 16, 2009.
The 2011 Sorento officially went on sale in the U.S. in January of this year.
The 2011 Sorento sold 7,398 units in its first month, making it Kia’s best-selling vehicle in January.
Byung-Mo Ahn stated that the outstanding early performance of the first vehicle produced from the West Point, Georgia plant is a significant achievement for KMMG, KMA and the Kia brand as a whole.
Kia plans to continue expanding its line of vehicles here and abroad while staying true to the Kia brand tenets of value, quality, safety, and technology.
KMMG will play a prominent role in achieving Kia's goals.
source_title: Kia Celebrates Grand Opening of Georgia Plant

facts: Redwood Materials laid off approximately 135 employees on April 21, 2026.
The layoffs affected multiple divisions including engineering and operations.
The cuts came three months after the company closed a $425 million funding round at a valuation above $6 billion.
This marks the second workforce reduction in five months, following a 5% cut in November 2025.
The restructuring triggered the departure of Chief Operating Officer Chris Lister and several other senior executives in supply chain and mechanical engineering.
Founder and CEO JB Straubel framed the layoffs as a deliberate realignment rather than a distress response.
Straubel noted that "parts of the company have expanded faster than needed to support the direction" of the business.
The company is reducing management layers to sharpen strategic focus.
Straubel stated Redwood "continues to dominate the U.S. battery recycling market" while touting "great momentum" in its newer energy storage business.
Cuts across engineering and operations aim to align headcount with the evolving priority of a second-life battery energy storage business growing faster than the core recycling vertical.
Ascend Elements, a Massachusetts-based battery recycler that raised more than $1.1 billion in equity and grants since its 2015 founding, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on April 9, 2026.
Ascend CEO Linh Austin described financial difficulties as "insurmountable," pointing to a canceled $316 million federal grant and broader softening of EV demand.
SK Battery America cut nearly 1,000 jobs in Georgia in 2026 amid slowing EV production by U.S. automakers.
Lithium carbonate peaked above $80,000 per metric ton in late 2022 and was trading near $12,000 per metric ton in early 2026, a decline of roughly 85%.
Nickel and cobalt prices have followed similar downward trajectories.
Federal grant cancellations and program reviews have removed funding that underpinned many recyclers' capital structures.
Redwood launched its dedicated energy storage unit, Redwood Energy, in June 2025.
Redwood Energy deployed North America's largest microgrid: a 12 MW / 63 MWh installation built from 805 retired EV battery packs to power a Crusoe AI modular data center in Sparks, Nevada.
The system was constructed in under four months and described as profitable by Straubel.
In April 2026, Redwood and Rivian announced the first repurposed battery energy storage system deployed at a U.S. automaker's manufacturing facility.
The installation is 10 MWh at Rivian's Normal, Illinois, plant using more than 100 second-life Rivian battery packs.
Redwood signed a battery repurposing MOU with General Motors.
Redwood holds partnerships spanning Volkswagen Group, BMW, Toyota, Ford, and Panasonic.
Redwood processes more than 20 GWh of batteries annually, equivalent to approximately 250,000 EVs.
This represents about 90% of all lithium-ion batteries recycled in North America.
Projections cited by Redwood indicate the U.S. will need more than 600 GWh of storage by 2030 to handle peak demand and AI-driven load growth.
Redwood's South Carolina facility carries a $2 billion conditional DOE loan commitment.
The South Carolina facility's first phase added an initial 20,000 metric tons of annual processing capacity in late 2025.
Ascend's bankruptcy leaves Redwood as the dominant independent domestic recycler with scale.

The absence of a meaningful competitor in high-purity cathode precursor production could affect pricing dynamics for automakers and cell manufacturers depending on recycled material inputs for domestic content compliance.
Redwood has raised over $2 billion in private capital before grant dependency became critical.
Companies can monetize packs before recycling by extracting storage value from batteries retaining 50% or more of usable capacity.
Redwood's restructuring is a strategic realignment, not a signal of financial distress.
The company's $6 billion-plus valuation and $425 million Series E position it among the best-capitalized players in the sector.
Feedstock control is a structural moat; Redwood's dominance in North American battery collection provides a durable competitive advantage that commodity-price headwinds cannot easily erode.
source_title: Redwood Materials' Restructuring Exposes Deep Cost Pressures in the U.S. Battery Recycling Supply Chain | Energy Tech News

facts: MWYN is expanding into high-margin battery recycling with a "black mass" strategy.
The planned development of a black mass production facility represents a major evolution of its EcoLoopX platform.
This move transitions the company from an asset-light logistics model to direct participation in the battery materials value chain.
Black mass is a critical intermediate material derived from recycled lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
Black mass contains concentrated quantities of high-value minerals including lithium, cobalt, nickel, and manganese.
These minerals serve as core inputs required for EV batteries and energy storage systems.
Moving into this segment positions MWYN to capture significantly greater economic value per transaction.
The initiative directly addresses global shortages of battery-grade materials driven by accelerating EV adoption.
This marks a strategic shift from coordination to participation, capturing value upstream while maintaining platform flexibility.
The black mass initiative reflects a deliberate move upstream in the value chain, transforming MWYN into a producer of battery-ready feedstock.
Rather than relying solely on logistics and trading, MWYN now aims to capture greater economic value per transaction.
This approach creates a closed-loop system that aligns directly with global sustainability mandates and industrial demand for critical minerals.
In parallel with its circular economy expansion, MWYN is pushing into AI infrastructure through its wholly owned subsidiary, NexaCore Technologies.
NexaCore is designed to address bottlenecks in AI by providing access to high-performance computing environments capable of supporting massive GPU workloads.
The platform focuses on delivering specialized infrastructure and services tailored for AI-driven enterprises.
Infrastructure offerings include high-density, liquid-cooled data centers and related infrastructure.
By combining computing infrastructure with resource recovery, MWYN is building an end-to-end ecosystem spanning digital and physical technology layers.
MWYN continues to expand its EcoLoopX platform as a global connector of sourcing, logistics, and commercial operations in the recyclable materials ecosystem.
The company's strategy remains rooted in building a cross-border network spanning the United States, Latin America, and Asia.
This network enables efficient aggregation and movement of electronic waste and recyclable inputs.
This hybrid approach allows MWYN to scale efficiently while selectively moving into higher-value segments like black mass production.
What differentiates MWYN is combining AI, recycling, and supply chain integration rather than just diversification.
MWYN is constructing a closed-loop ecosystem where materials can be sourced, processed, and redeployed into next-generation technology infrastructure.
This creates a scalable flywheel model: Source → Aggregate → Process → Redeploy → Compute → Scale.
Few companies are attempting to connect these layers into a unified platform.
$MWYN is evolving from a traditional supply chain operator into a multi-sector infrastructure platform with exposure to EV batteries, energy storage systems, and AI infrastructure.
With the addition of black mass production, the company is moving into higher-margin, value-added operations while maintaining scalability.

links.context: Company website for more information on $MWYN

source_title: Expanding Into High-Margin Battery Recycling With Black Mass Strategy plus Scaling AI Infrastructure & Global Supply Chain Platform: N A S D A Q: MWYN -- CorporateAds | PRLog

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) LLC
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America LLC
facts: Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung, Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp, company officials and state and local leaders officially broke ground Oct. 25 on Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) LLC's new manufacturing plant at the Bryan County Mega site.

The celebration included a morning groundbreaking on site, followed by a future technology expo at Enmarket Arena in Savannah, GA, where the company highlighted "advanced technology and extraordinary human achievement" with a wide array of exhibits for hundreds of attendees from the community in attendance.

The $5.54 billion investment by the company and its affiliate suppliers was announced five months ago and includes plans to produce a diverse range of innovative Hyundai, Genesis and Kia EVs and a new battery manufacturing facility.
HMGMA will create more than 8,100 jobs over the next few years as the company aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV battery and other EV components in the U.S. market.
HMGMA's new smart factory is expected to begin commercial production in the first half of 2025.

Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated that Hyundai and the people of Georgia share many qualities including respect for histories, ingenuity, creativity and determination to make the world better for the next generation.
Chung noted that Hyundai's EVs are recognized as best in class and with this partnership the company is determined to be the global leader in electrification, safety, quality and sustainability.
Chung added that with the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America they will continue to evolve beyond an automaker to the world's leading mobility solutions provider.

Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp stated that with more Georgians working than ever before, record jobs and investment coming to all parts of the state, and award-winning workforce development programs and infrastructure, the Peach State's economy is reaching new heights.
Kemp said their partnership with Hyundai Motor Group and the groundbreaking of this innovative facility exemplifies unprecedented success.
Kemp expressed looking forward to continuing the partnership and seeing its impact on both the community and the state as a whole due to a long-term commitment to improving lives and livelihoods.
Approximately $1 billion is anticipated to be invested by non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project in other locations.
Hyundai Motor Group will be a leader in the U.S. auto market's EV transition with the new facility.
The Group is accelerating its electrification efforts with the global target to sell 3.23 million full electric vehicles annually by 2030.
To realize this goal, the Group plans to establish a global EV production network that will ensure a stable supply of EVs around the globe.
With the additional EV and battery production capabilities in the U.S., the Group aims to maintain its position as one of the top three EV providers in the U.S.
The Group plans to lead the EV market not just in sales, but also in terms of design, technologies and mobility solutions optimized for the electric mobility era.
The investment is in line with the U.S. government's roadmap to accelerate electrification, as global OEMs are announcing plans to expand their local EV production in the U.S.
Last year, the U.S. government announced a new initiative under which sales of zero-emission vehicles should account for at least half of OEM's new vehicle sales by 2030.
The new plant will boast a highly connected, automated and flexible manufacturing system, which organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem to realize customer value.

The Georgia facility will become an intelligent manufacturing plant where all processes of production including order collection, procurement, logistics and production will be optimized using AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system will help create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.

As part of the Group's commitment to sustainability, the plant will primarily rely on renewable energy sources to power the facility and use emission-reduction technologies to meet the RE100 requirements.

HMG celebrated and announced new connections with the local economy that will enrich the health of local communities through their corporate social responsibility programs, with donations to date totaling $1.54 million.
Hyundai and Genesis, along with its various social responsibility initiatives and nonprofit organizations, have donated $940,000 to local organizations.
Programs through Hyundai Hope and Genesis Gives have previously collectively committed $120,000 to nearly 10 organizations supporting local communities in and around Bryan County.
Hyundai presented Georgia Tech, a leading technological research university, with a $120,000 donation to provide scholarships in STEM areas.
For the next four years, two $15,000 scholarships will be awarded to eligible students at Georgia Tech.
Hyundai Hope On Wheels presented $500,000 Hyundai Scholar Hope and Young Investigator Grants to Children's Healthcare of Atlanta to continue its long-standing fight against pediatric cancer.
Since 1998, Hyundai Hope On Wheels has pledged $200 million in support of more than 1,100 childhood cancer research grants.
Genesis Inspiration Foundation presented donations totaling $200,000 to two Georgia-based nonprofit organizations, drawchange and Paint Love.
Genesis Inspiration Foundation has donated nearly $5 million to fund youth arts programming since its inception in 2018.

Kia's Corporate Social Responsibility program, Accelerate the Good, aligns with the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals and seeks to inspire human potential, spark innovation and champion sustainability in U.S. communities.
As part of Kia's commitment to the Bryan County community, Kia's Accelerate the Good program will donate $600,000 to help local youth realize their hopes and dreams.
Family Promise of the Coastal Empire received $300,000 for housing homeless families that recently lost their homes and purchasing homes for them.
America's Second Harvest of GA received $100,000 to establish after-school programs where low-income children can eat and receive tutoring.
St. Jude Children's Research Hospital received $100,000 to pay travel expenses for pediatric cancer care at St. Jude in Memphis, TN.
Inspiredu received $100,000 to support low-income children with technology and resources to facilitate learning.
source_title: Hyundai Breaks Ground on Georgia EV and Battery Plant - Autobody News

facts: Ascend Elements received two grants from the DOE totaling $480m for the production of lithium-ion battery cathode materials made from recycled battery feedstock.
Grants will support the construction of the planned $1B Apex facility in Hopkinsville, Ky.
The Apex facility is expected to yield over $4.4B in positive economic impact for the region.

Apex will be the first facility of its kind, utilizing the company’s patented Hydro-to-Cathode™ technology to produce cathode precursors (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM) from spent lithium-ion batteries.
The awards were granted from the U.S. Department of Energy’s Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (BIL) Battery Materials Processing and Battery Manufacturing Initiative.
The awards total $480M in matching funds to support construction of the Ascend Elements ‘Apex’ manufacturing facility in southwest Kentucky.
The project aims to establish lithium-ion battery supply chain infrastructure that previously did not exist in the United States.
Mike O’Kronley, CEO of Ascend Elements, stated: “These awards are transformational for the U.S. lithium-ion battery industry.”

With Department of Energy support, the Apex facility will be the first and largest battery material production facility of its kind in the U.S., improving security of supply of critical materials for the domestic electric vehicle industry.
The facility will enhance the United States’ competitiveness in the lithium-ion battery industry on a global scale.
The grant accelerates achievement of a closed-loop lithium-ion battery supply chain.

The Apex facility will be a commercial-scale deployment of the company’s Hydro-to-Cathode direct precursor synthesis technology which had been running at pilot scale in Massachusetts for the last few years.
This technology enables manufacturing of lithium-ion battery cathode active material and precursors directly from a recycled battery feedstock known as black mass.
Construction at the 140-acre site, located in Hopkinsville Ky., will begin this month (October 2022).
Once completed, Apex will produce enough material annually to power over 250,000 electric vehicles.
The project will generate approximately $4.4B in total economic impact during its construction phase and the first ten years of operation.
The facility will create over 400 jobs in the Hopkinsville area.
The Apex project will additionally seek to establish community benefits programs including job training and workforce development, affordable childcare, and affordable transportation initiatives.
Ascend Elements is based in Westborough, Mass.
Ascend Elements is the leading provider of closed-loop battery material solutions.
Its Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis technology produces new CAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods.
The process results in reduced cost, improved performance, and lowered GHG emissions.
With fewer batteries going to landfill and a cleaner manufacturing process, Ascend Elements is taking the lithium-ion battery industry to a higher level of efficiency.
source_title: Ascend Elements | Ascend Elements Awarded $480M in Grants from U.S. Department of Energy to Manufacture Battery Cathode Active Materials

canonical_name: Georgia Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Deployment Plan (NEVI Program)
facts: Georgia released its Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Deployment Plan in August 2023.
The plan outlines an investment of over $135 million from the Federal National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) Program.
The strategy was developed by the Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT).
It is a comprehensive five-year strategy to build out a network of DC fast chargers across designated Alternative Fuel Corridors.

Input from regional planning commissions, local governments, utilities, site hosts, EV charging companies, labor organizations, freight carriers, and advocates for disadvantaged communities shaped the plan's approach to charger siting, procurement, operations, and equitable access.
Initial funds will focus on deploying NEVI-compliant stations spaced no more than 50 miles apart along approved corridors.
GDOT acknowledges that additional infrastructure beyond the current plan will be required to meet Georgia’s charging needs as EV adoption grows.
The agency plans to use lessons from similar programs in neighboring states to maximize the program's impact.
Multiple state agencies are aligned behind GDOT’s coordination efforts to support transportation electrification, economic development, and environmental goals.
Utilities are assessing grid capacity and strategies for “make-ready” infrastructure to energize charging sites.
Ongoing collaboration aims to optimize deployments and accelerate the energy transition.
Equity remains a top priority for the program.
Strategies to engage and benefit underserved communities were informed by discussions with advocates.
Performance measures will track progress toward increasing affordable mobility options for all Georgians.
Training initiatives aim to create green jobs through fair labor practices.
GDOT will keep stakeholders engaged annually through the implementation phase to adapt the plan as needs evolve.
The first chargers are scheduled to come online in 2023, positioning Georgia to lead the Southeast in developing the backbone for nationwide EV adoption.
source_title: Georgia NEVI Program - All You Need To Know

canonical_name: Georgia Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Program (GEVI) and Related Incentives
facts: All NEVI (National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure) funding has been frozen by the new administration until further notice.
Contracts for approved projects that have already been signed will still receive funding.
Projects that were approved but not yet signed are highly likely to not receive funding.
The NEVI funding freeze has created significant uncertainty for stakeholders in the electric vehicle infrastructure sector.
Many stakeholders had been anticipating the funds to move forward with their projects.
The future of these approvals remains unclear, depending on the administration’s review and potential changes to its priorities.
Georgia National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) Deployment Program is at the forefront of Georgia’s efforts to revolutionize its electric mobility landscape.
The GEVI Deployment Program was born out of the 2021 Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
Nearly $5 billion has been allocated nationally over a five-year span for NEVI, commencing in the fall of 2022.
Georgia’s dedicated share of the national NEVI allocation amounts to approximately $135 million.
The $135 million investment is earmarked to push forward the development of the state’s portion of the national EV charging network.
The Federal Highway Administration approved the Georgia NEVI Deployment Plan on September 14, 2022.
The Georgia Department of Transportation (DOT) is crafting a blueprint to actualize the approved NEVI plan.
In Round 1 of the GA NEVI Deployment Program, GDOT is actively seeking experienced developers to undertake EV charging infrastructure projects.
The NEVI program aims to design, install, finance, operate, and maintain EV charging infrastructure that aligns with the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Program.
The due date for responses in the first round of the GA NEVI Deployment Program was November 17th, 2023.

Cobb EMC’s EV Grant Program provides funding opportunities to businesses, property owners, multifamily unit owners, and government agencies interested in installing EV charging stations at their place of business.
Cobb EMC grant recipients can receive anywhere from $500 to $5,000.
Cobb EMC grants offset costs associated with purchasing and installing EV supply equipment (EVSE).
Cobb EMC grants are allocated on a first-come, first-served basis until the funds are exhausted.
Georgia Power supports the electrification of transportation through a series of programs including the Make Ready Electric Transportation Program.

The Make Ready Electric Transportation Program provides financial assistance for businesses and organizations to install infrastructure required to support electric transportation charging systems at various facilities.
Georgia Power's Level Up with Rebates program offers EV charger rebates for commercial customers who purchase or lease and install Level 2 EV chargers.
The Level Up rebate can be as high as $500 per charger.
The maximum rebate under the Level Up program is $2,500 for installing up to five chargers.
Georgia Power offers Commercial, Workplace, and Residential incentives for business owners, property owners, and government agencies.
Georgia’s comprehensive approach to EV adoption ensures that everyone can participate in the transition to cleaner transportation.
source_title: Georgia's EV charging incentives - AMPECO

facts: Georgia-based EnviroSpark announced that it will build DC fast chargers at a Shell station in Metter, Ga.
EnviroSpark will also build DC fast chargers at a Waffle House site in Tifton.
EnviroSpark is using National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) funding that was awarded by the Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT).
EnviroSpark won two of the five open Phase 1 eligible sites.
EnviroSpark was the only company selected to build more than one location in the first round of funding of Georgia’s NEVI deployment.
EnviroSpark will host all eight stations on its national EnviroSpark charging network.
The new Waffle House charging stations mark the second collaboration between EnviroSpark and Waffle House.
In February, the Tennessee Department of Transportation selected EnviroSpark to build stations at a Lakeland, Tenn., Waffle House in the state’s first round of NEVI funding awards.

EnviroSpark Co-Founder Stephanie Luque, who also leads the company’s federal initiatives, stated: “We are honored to once again partner with Waffle House — a fellow Georgia-based business committed to customer satisfaction and community engagement — on such a pivotal project.”

Stephanie Luque stated: “Collaborating with Waffle House, the Metter Shell station and GDOT exemplifies all parties’ shared commitment to driving innovation and sustainability in ways that benefit drivers and communities. NEVI-funded projects like these represent huge steps toward reducing drivers’ range anxiety and moving us closer to mass EV adoption.”
According to GDOT, Georgia is among the top 10 states for EV sales.
Three of the top five largest investments in the EV supply chain are in Georgia.
Georgia has approximately 1,193 publicly available EV charging stations.
Only 14 stations have DCFC ports that meet both the distance and power requirements under federal rules.

Andrew Heath, Georgia DOT Deputy Chief Engineer, stated: “The Georgia NEVI Deployment Program outlines the state’s approach to using our federal funding to address EV infrastructure gaps in our state’s Alternative Fuel Corridors.”

Andrew Heath stated: “In order for us to be successful, we are turning to the private sector to deliver this innovative infrastructure at strategic locations across our state through public-private partnerships.”
The Georgia NEVI Deployment Program is funded by the 2021 Bipartisan Infrastructure Law enacted as the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA).
The program provides nearly $5 billion nationally over five years, which started in the fall of 2022.
Georgia’s allotment from the formula program is approximately $135 million to develop its portion of the national network.
EnviroSpark will begin construction and installation of the eight NEVI-compliant DC fast chargers later this year.
EnviroSpark was founded in 2014.
EnviroSpark is a leader in the turnkey design, installation and operation of EV charging solutions.
EnviroSpark is responsible for more than 7,800 installations of charging plugs across North America to date.
source_title: EnviroSpark to Build DC Fast Chargers at Georgia Shell Station, Waffle House - CStore Decisions

facts: Bolstered by a stock offering last month that raised an estimated $13 billion, Rivian says it is moving ahead with plans to expand by building a new manufacturing plant on a site near Atlanta, GA.
The new $5 billion plant will be built on a 2,000-acre site spread across Morgan and Walton counties to the east of Atlanta.
The “carbon conscious” campus, when completed, will expand the company’s capacity to build electric vans, trucks and SUVs.
Construction on the new Rivian plant is expected to begin in summer 2022.
The plant will have capacity to build 400,000 vehicles annually.
The plant will employ 7,500 workers when fully operational.
Production at the new Georgia plant is slated to begin in 2024.

Rivian Chief People Officer Helen Russell stated the company is happy to partner with Georgia on its new manufacturing site, which will build next-generation products important for scaling the business.
Russell noted their work together is driven by a compelling purpose and rooted in building a highly collaborative environment that creates a true sense of belonging.
Both General Motors and Ford previously had plants in Atlanta.
The Ford plant in Atlanta closed in 2006.
The GM plant in Doraville closed in 2008.
Kia now operates in the Southwest corner of Georgia.
Helen Russell said they are excited for Georgians to bring their creativity and ideas to Rivian.
Russell stated that alongside Illinois, Georgia is the place for Rivian to continue to scale and thrive.
Rivian is scaling up capacity at its existing plant in Normal, IL, with a 623,000-sq.-ft. addition.
The addition will bring the total footprint of the Normal plant to approximately 4 million sq.-ft.
The Rivian plant in Normal already employs more than 3,000 people.
The automaker plans to add up to another 1,000 employees at the Normal plant by the second quarter of 2022.
Rivian considered settling in Texas before choosing Georgia.
The company is eager to hire employees in Georgia.
Town hall meetings are being planned for 2022 near the manufacturing site so area residents can learn more about jobs, the company and its plans.
It has launched a careers portal to scout for employment prospects with the company.
After a long ramp up, Rivian is producing and shipping its first product, the R1T electric pickup truck.
An all-electric SUV, the R1S, is expected to ship from the Normal plant in early 2022.
Rivian has contracts to build delivery vans for Amazon.
Amazon now owns about 20% of Rivian's shares.
Rivian’s market capitalization is now greater than both GM and Ford and second only to Tesla.
Rivian will deliver its first 1,000 vehicles to customers by the end of 2021.
According to SEC filings, Rivian remains a money-losing startup.
Ford is trimming its ties to Rivian.
The two companies confirmed they were canceling plans to develop an EV together for the Lincoln brand.
Due to earlier investments, Ford owns a 12% stake in Rivian.
Rivian was valued at nearly $10 billion after its initial public offering.
The IPO is financing Rivian’s expansion and continuing product development.
The IPO is making the company’s founder R.J. Scaringe a billionaire.
Scaringe began planning for Rivian while he was a graduate student in automotive engineering at MIT.
MIT became one of the sources of seed money for the EV company.
Scaringe founded the company in 2009 when GM and Chrysler were filing for bankruptcy.
In 2018, Scaringe told Wards that at the time of founding, investors had no interest in a car company.
source_title: Rivian Expanding to Georgia With 2nd BEV Plant | WardsAuto

facts: Rivian paused construction on its Georgia factory until "later" and will now build everything in its existing Illinois factory.
The paused Georgia factory received some of the most generous taxpayer-funded incentives in state history.
On Thursday, Rivian unveiled three new vehicles that will be available in the coming years.
Rivian currently offers the R1T luxury truck and R1S luxury SUV, which start at $70,000–$75,000 and can cost $100,000 or more.
CEO R.J. Scaringe announced the R2, a smaller and more modest SUV expected to be available in 2026 with prices starting at $45,000.
Rivian also announced the R3 and R3X crossovers, which are expected to be less expensive than the R1 series.
Rivian went public in November 2021, promising stylish and rugged luxury electric vehicles.
At the time of its IPO, Rivian operated only a single factory in Illinois.
In December 2021, Rivian struck a deal to build its second factory in Georgia.
Under the Georgia deal, Rivian committed to spending $5 billion on the factory.
In exchange for the factory investment, Georgia state and local governments authorized up to $1.5 billion in tax credits and incentives.
In May 2023, Bloomberg reported that Rivian had lost 93 percent of its share value.
At that time, Rivian's market cap reflected "almost no value beyond the company's cash hoard."
In the fourth quarter of 2023, Rivian lost $43,372 on each vehicle sold.
This per-vehicle loss increased from $30,648 in the third quarter of 2023.
Developing the more affordable R2 and R3 models is key to Rivian's long-term survival and aims to appeal to buyers who cannot pay over $75,000 for a luxury vehicle.
To enable an earlier R2 launch with reduced capital requirements, Rivian plans to start production of the R2 at its existing Normal, Illinois manufacturing facility.
Rivian stated that its Georgia plant remains an extremely important part of its strategy to scale production of the R2 and R3.
The timing for resuming construction in Georgia is expected to be later to focus teams on the capital-efficient launch of the R2 in Normal, Illinois.
The pivot to Illinois is expected to save Rivian $2.25 billion compared to the original forecast of launching the first line of R2 production at the Georgia site.
In October 2023, Rivian announced that the Georgia site was "95 percent graded" and "nearly ready for construction to begin."
Under the incentive agreement, Georgia officials paid over $32 million for "clearing and grading" the site.
One year ago, CEO R.J. Scaringe reaffirmed the company's dedication to the Georgia project, telling The Atlanta Journal-Constitution, "We're committed to this state and this project."

Scaringe added that "the future of our company in terms of scaling and growing really relies on the future of this project. There's not another option. We're not planning an alternative. This must work."
The electric vehicle market is currently experiencing flux due to softening consumer demand and persistently high interest rates.
Last month, Apple canceled its decade-long quest to develop an electric car.
General Motors and Ford have also rolled back pledged investments in electric vehicles.
Georgia officials committed significant taxpayer funds based on a plan that foresaw Rivian continuing on a financial path that is no longer feasible.
source_title: Rivian Pauses Construction at Factory That Costs Georgia Taxpayers $1.5 Billion

facts: A Morgan County tax assessors board has scheduled a special called meeting to consider a regional development authority’s request for a determination if land to be owned by the state and leased to Rivian will make the land tax-exempt.

The Morgan County-based group Our Communities Oppose Rivian Production Plant announced it has hired the Atlanta-based Schreeder, Wheeler & Flint law firm in its fight against the company’s plans for a $5 billion electric vehicle production and research facility on a 2,000-acre site partly in Social Circle.
Attorney John A. Christy said in a letter to the group that “we will do our best to accomplish your goals of preserving your beautiful community and lifestyle.”
Christy cautioned group members that judges “will only consider actual controversies and disputes” and not stop a government body from considering issuing a permit.

He wrote that if a permit or other governmental authorization is not properly issued, then a court will consider invalidating the action and stopping action from being taken on the improperly issued authorization.

Christy stated it is important to properly assert any objections to actions requested of governmental authorities in order to preserve appeal or litigation rights, noting those objections need to be made during any comment period or at the meeting of the governmental body considering the request.
He said a court will not stop the filing or the Army Corps of Engineers’ consideration of a “404” permit the state recently requested dealing with disturbance of land and water sources.

Christy wrote that during the public comment period, they will file any objections or comments to the application, and if the Corps does not adequately consider any objections and approves the application, they can appeal the decision to the United States District Court.
The law firm is reviewing the payment in lieu of taxes (PILOT) agreement presented to the Morgan County Board of Tax Assessors for approval.
Christy stated that if the Board approves the PILOT Agreement without properly considering objections, they have the right to pursue appeal and litigation remedies.

He added that similarly, if a land disturbance permit, zoning or other application for governmental approval is sought, they will review the requests, submit appropriate objections in a timely manner, and appear at any public hearing to urge them.

Unresolved legal issues include whether the state of Georgia is exempt from local zoning laws and whether the acquisition of land by the state for non-public purposes is lawful, which Christy said will likely need to be resolved by the courts in actions brought at the appropriate time.
The newly hired law firm is in addition to environmental law specialist Stack and Associates.

Our Communities' announcement followed the Morgan County Board of Tax Assessors scheduling a special called meeting for Thursday, May 12, at 9 a.m. to consider certification of a lease plan that was part of a payments in lieu of taxes (PILOT) agreement between Joint Development Authority (JDA) and Rivian.
The tax assessors board voted to delay action, or table, the JDA’s request April 27 over the objection of County Attorney Christian Henry.
Henry told board members they were exceeding the board’s authority by considering the merits of the entire Rivian deal rather than only checking if the rental agreement made the land tax-exempt.
The PILOT plan states the Georgia state government will acquire the land from the JDA and lease it to Rivian.
The agreement spelled out the amount the company will give in PILOT payments which could total $300 million over 25 years and be shared between the JDA’s four counties.
Newton will receive more than $90 million of the total PILOT payments.
Our Communities Oppose Rivian Production Plant will host a benefit concert May 21 at the Social Circle Theater in Social Circle to help pay legal fees for the group.
The Scott Little Band and Doug Deluxe & the Rodeo Clowns are set to perform at 7 p.m. at the benefit concert.
source_title: Rivian opponents hire additional law firm; board considers site’s tax exempt status - The Covington News

facts: SK On will lay off 958 employees at its battery cell production facility in Commerce, Georgia.
The layoffs reduce about 37% of the plant’s workforce.
The job cuts affect workers at the SK Battery America facility.
The plant currently employs 2,566 people.
The plant produces battery cells used in several electric vehicle models as well as energy storage systems.
The factory has supplied batteries for vehicles including the now-discontinued Ford F-150 Lightning pickup.
The factory has supplied batteries for the U.S. version of Volkswagen’s ID.4.
The factory has supplied batteries for models from Hyundai and Kia.
The site also manufactures battery cells used in stationary battery energy storage systems.
SK Battery America stated that the layoffs were part of an operational adjustment.
SK Battery America said it is aligning operations to market conditions.
SK Battery America remains committed to maintaining its presence in Georgia.
SK Battery America remains committed to continuing to develop a domestic battery supply chain in the United States.
The layoffs come amid broader uncertainty in the U.S. electric vehicle market.
Policy changes affecting EV incentives are contributing to the market uncertainty.
The $7,500 federal tax credit for electric vehicle purchases expired on Sept. 30, 2025.
Emissions regulations for internal combustion vehicles were relaxed.
Georgia U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff criticized the policy changes.
Ossoff said the policy changes had affected the state’s emerging battery manufacturing sector.
Ossoff stated: “Let’s be clear: these were battery manufacturing jobs and now they’re gone.”
Ossoff stated: “As predicted, Trump’s war on electric vehicles is hurting Georgia’s economy.”
Ossoff stated: “We were booming and building new plants. Now Georgians are losing their jobs.”
SK Innovation began constructing the battery plant in Commerce in 2019.
SK Innovation later added a second facility to the site.
The total investment brought to the site is about $2.6 billion.
The site was designed to produce up to 22 gigawatt-hours of battery capacity annually.
The annual capacity is enough to power roughly 300,000 electric vehicles.
Mass production at the first plant began in January 2022.
The first plant initially supplied batteries for Volkswagen’s ID.4.
Production at the second plant started in early 2023.
The second plant produces cells for the Ford F-150 Lightning.
SK Battery America became part of SK On after SK Innovation reorganized its battery business in 2021.
source_title: SK On to Lay Off Nearly 1,000 Workers at Georgia Battery Plant Amid EV Market Slowdown

facts: SK battery America is located at 1760 Steve Reynolds Industrial Pkwy, Commerce, Georgia, United States.
The company's official website is listed as skbatteryamerica.com.
source_title: Driving directions to SK battery America, 1760 Steve Reynolds Industrial Pkwy, Commerce - Waze

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is Hyundai Motor Group's third manufacturing plant in the US.
The facility serves as the key pillar of Hyundai Motor Group’s $12.6 billion investment in Georgia.
HMGMA will produce up to 500,000 electric and hybrid vehicles annually for the Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis brands.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America hosted a grand opening celebration on October 3, 2024.
The ceremony marks the completion of the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Groundbreaking for the facility occurred two and a half years prior to the October 3, 2024 opening.

Euisun Chung, Executive Chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated that HMGMA represents the Group’s advanced manufacturing capabilities, commitment to innovation, and investment in relationships with partners and communities in Georgia.
Euisun Chung also noted that together with the talented workforce at HMGMA, they are building the future of mobility with America, in America.
The first vehicle rolled off the assembly line on October 3, 2024, less than two years after breaking ground.
The first vehicle produced was a Hyundai Ioniq 5.
The Hyundai Ioniq 9 three-row electric SUV is also now in production at HMGMA.
The first Kia model to be built at the facility will join production in 2026.
In addition to EVs, the plant is equipped to produce hybrid vehicles.
HMGMA initially has the capacity to produce 300,000 vehicles annually.
Hyundai Motor Group's total investment in Georgia related to HMGMA and joint battery ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK On is $12.6 billion.
This $12.6 billion investment represents the largest single investment in Georgia's history.
The investment includes a total of 8,500 jobs at HMGMA by 2031.
Hyundai Motor Group’s total investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs.
These total investments are expected to generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year, according to the Center for Automotive Research.
As of July 2024, more than $2.5 billion in capital investment has been announced by HMGMA suppliers across 12 counties in Georgia.
Approximately 6,900 jobs have been announced by HMGMA suppliers as of July 2024.
The Korean auto major has invested more than $20.5 billion in the U.S. to date.
Earlier in the week of the article's publication, the Group announced a significant investment of $21 billion in the U.S. from 2025 to 2028.
Of the $21 billion announcement, $9 billion will be invested to establish an annual production capacity in the U.S. of 1.2 million vehicles.
The Group plans to expand HMGMA's annual production capacity to a total of 500,000 units, an increase of 200,000 units from the original plan.
Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama will see improvements in its production facilities.
Kia Autoland Georgia will also see improvements in its production facilities.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America opens | Autocar Professional

facts: August GEDA Monthly Meeting will be in downtown Columbus at the Convention and Trade Center, with the program focus on transformation of downtown redevelopment.

Panelists for the Columbus meeting include Richard Bishop (Chief of Staff, Office of the Mayor), Becca Zajac (Vice President of Marketing & Community Relations, Uptown Columbus), Peter Bowden (President of VisitColumbusGA), Pace Halter (President, W.C. Bradley Real Estate), and Betsy Covington (President, Community Foundation of the Chattahoochee Valley).
Coastal Georgia Good News Gathering will be held on Wednesday, August 28, hosted by Jason Coley (Community Development Manager, Georgia Power).
GEDA Annual Conference will be held at the Westin Savannah Harbor Golf Resort and Spa on September 18-20, 2019.

The Georgia Small Business ROCK STAR Awards Application is now open, with nominations teaming up the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) and GEDA to recognize outstanding small businesses.

Criteria for the ROCK STAR Awards include being a Georgia-based business with under 100 employees, with bonus points for those assisted by local or state resources, and peer-to-peer judging added for 2019.
Andrew Carnes has been promoted from Senior Director of Economic Development to Vice President, Economic Development at Partnership Gwinnett.
Deven Cason has been promoted from Project Manager to Director of Economic Development at Partnership Gwinnett.
Andrea Taylor (CEO, Hazlehurst-Jeff Davis Chamber & JDA) was recognized as a new Georgia Certified Chamber Executive (GCCE).
On August 1, 2019, Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Rivacold will create 140 jobs and invest $5 million in a new manufacturing facility in Buford to manufacture refrigeration systems.

Partners involved in the Rivacold project include Andrew Carnes and Deven Cason (Partnership Gwinnett), Nico Wijnberg (GDEcD Global Commerce Division), MaryBeth Flournoy (Georgia Power), and Rodger Brown (Quick Start).
On July 17, 2019, GDEcD announced that Variotech will create 20 new jobs at a new North American headquarters in Fulton County as a world leader in tray solutions.
Partners for the Variotech project include Fernanda Kirchner (GDEcD Global Commerce Division) and Bob Kosek (Invest Atlanta).
On July 15, 2019, GDEcD announced that Edifecs plans to hire up to 200 associates over the next three to five years in a new office location in Fulton County.
Partners for the Edifecs project include Randall Toussaint (GDEcD Global Commerce Division), Rebecca Jordan (Metro Atlanta Chamber), and MaryBeth Flournoy (Georgia Power).
On July 12, 2019, Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Stitch Fix will open a new distribution center in Lithia Springs, creating over 900 new jobs in the next few years.

Partners for the Stitch Fix project include Chris Pumphrey (Douglas County Economic Development Authority), Hank Evans (GDEcD Global Commerce Division), MaryBeth Flournoy (Georgia Power), and Josh Stephens (Metro Atlanta Chamber).
On July 11, 2019, Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Kroger and Ocado Solutions will join forces to build a new distribution facility in Forest Park, creating 410 jobs and investing $121 million.

Partners for the Kroger/Ocado project include Wylly Harrison (GDEcD Global Commerce Division), Charlie Moseley (Georgia Power), Rodger Brown (Quick Start), and local officials from the City of Forest Park.
On July 10, 2019, Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Amazon will open a new fulfillment center in Gwinnett and DeKalb counties, creating 1,000 new jobs.
Partners for the Amazon project include Andrew Carnes (Partnership Gwinnett), Hank Evans (GDEcD Global Commerce Division), and Bradley Harris (Georgia EMC).

On July 10, 2019, Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Swiss manufacturer Preci-Dip will open its first U.S. production facility in Bryan County to manufacture electronic connector pins, investing $13.6 million and creating 100 new jobs.

Partners for the Preci-Dip project include Anna Chafin (Development Authority of Bryan County), Joseph Huntemann (GDEcD Global Commerce Division), David Denison (Georgia EMC), and David Sink (Colliers International).
On August 1, 2019, GDEcD announced it is accepting nominations for the 2019 Georgia Automotive Awards through September 13, with winners to be announced in the fall.
The Vibrant Communities grant is accepting applications for arts projects in counties without FY20 grants, with a deadline of August 30, 2019.
GEDA is offering up to $600 in scholarship money as reimbursement for expenses associated with the AC19 conference, with three scholarships available.
